desc: Either a perfectly square (9:9) aspect ratio or the field:frame ratio of progressive scanning.
Either a perfectly square (9:9) aspect ratio or the field:frame ratio of progressive scanning.

name: 0-10V dimming
desc: A simple analog dimming protocol where a control voltage between 0V (off) and 10V (full brightness) is sent to LED drivers or ballasts. Simple but limited compared to digital protocols like DALI.

Method Used To Map The 24 Fps Of Film Onto The 30 Fps (60 Fields) Of 525-Line Tv, So That One Film Frame Occupies Three Tv Fields, The Next Two, Etc. It Means The Two Fields Of Every Other Tv Frame Come From Different Film Frames Making Operations Such As Rotoscoping Impossible, And Requiring Care In Editing. Some Sophisticated Equipment Can Unravel The 3:2 Sequences To Allow Frame-By-Frame Treatment And Subsequently Re-Compose 3:2. The 3:2 Sequence Repeats Every Five Tv Frames And Four Film Frames, The Latter Identified As A-D. Only Film Frame A Is Fully On A Tv Frame And So Exists At One Time Code Only, Making It The Editable Point Of The Video Sequence.

This Is A Set Of Sampling Frequencies In The Ratio 4:1:1, Used To Digitize The Luminance And Color Difference Components (Y, R-Y, B-Y) Of A Video Signal. The Four Represents 13.5 Mhz, The Sampling Frequency Of Y, And The Ones Each 3.75 Mhz For R-Y And B-Y.With The Color Information Sampled At Half The Rate Of The 4:2:2 System, This Is Generally Used As A More Economical Form Of Sampling For 525-Line Picture Formats. Both Luminance And Color Difference Are Still Sampled On Every Line. But The Latter Has Half The Horizontal Resolution Of 4:2:2, While The Vertical Resolution Of The Color Information Is Maintained. For 525-Line Pictures, This Means The Color Is Fairly Equally Resolved In Horizontal And Vertical Directions.

A Sampling System Used To Digitize The Luminance And Color Difference Components (Y, R- Y, B-Y) Of A Video Signal. The Four Represents The 13.5 Mhz Sampling Frequency Of Y, While The R-Y And B-Y Are Sampled At 6.75 Mhz--Effectively Between Every Other Line Only (One Line Is Sampled At 4:0:0, Luminance Only, And The Next At 4:2:2). This Is Generally Used As A More Economical System Than 4:2:2 Sampling For 625-Line Formats So That The Color Signals Have A Reasonably Even Resolution In The Vertical And Horizontal Directions For That Format.

A Sampling System Used To Digitize The Luminance And Color Difference Components (Y, R- Y, B-Y) Of A Video Signal. The Four Represents The 13.5 Mhz Sampling Frequency Of Y, While The R-Y And B-Y Are Sampled At 6.75 Mhz--Effectively Between Every Other Line Only (One Line Is Sampled At 4:0:0, Luminance Only, And The Next At 4:2:2). This Is Generally Used As A More Economical System Than 4:2:2 Sampling For 625-Line Formats So That The Color Signals Have A Reasonably Even Resolution In The Vertical And Horizontal Directions For That Format.

A Commonly Used Term For A Component Digital Video Format.A Ratio Of Sampling Frequencies Used To Digitize The Luminance And Color Difference Components (Y, R-Y, B-Y) Of A Video Signal. It Is Generally Used As Shorthand For Itu-R 601. The Term 4:2:2 Describes That For Every Four Samples Of Y, There Are Two Samples Each Of R-Y And B-Y, Giving More Chrominance Bandwidth In Relation To Luminance Compared To 4:1:1 Sampling.Itu-R 601, 4:2:2 Is The Standard For Digital Studio Equipment And The Terms "4:2:2" And "601" Are Commonly (But Technically Incorrectly) Used Synonymously. The Sampling Frequency Of Y Is 13.5 Mhz And That Of R-Y And B-Y Is Each 6.75 Mhz Providing A Maximum Color Bandwidth Of 3.37 Mhz--Enough For High-Quality Chromakeying. The Format Specifies Eight Bits Of Resolution.The Details Of The Format Are Specified In The Itu-R Bt.601-2 Standard Document. See Also: Itu- R Bt.601-2.
The aspect ratio of conventional video, television and computer screens.
The aspect ratio of conventional video, television and computer screens.
A sampling ratio that has equal amounts of the luminance and both chrominance channels.

name: 0H
desc: The reference point of horizontal sync. Synchronization at a video interface is achieved by associating a line sync datum, 0H, with every scan line. In analog video, sync is conveyed by voltage levels “blacker- than-black”. 0H is defined by the 50% point of the leading (or falling) edge of sync. In component digital video, sync is conveyed using digital codes 0 and 255 outside the range of the picture information.

name: 0V
desc: The reference point of vertical (field) sync. In both NTSC and PAL systems the normal sync pulse for a horizontal line is 4.7 µs. Vertical sync is identified by broad pulses, which are serrated in order for a receiver to maintain horizontal sync even during the vertical sync interval. The start  of the first broad pulse identifies the field sync datum, 0V.

Short form for color bar signal levels, usually describing four amplitude levels.
1st number: white amplitude 
2nd number: black amplitude
3rd number: white amplitude from which color bars are derived 
4th number: black amplitude from which color bars are derived
In this example: 75% color bars with 7.5% setup in which the white bar has been set to 100% and the black to 0%.

name: 1000BaseT / Gigabit Ethernet
desc: An Ethernet standard transmitting at 1 Gbps over twisted pair cable.

name: 100% Amplitude, 100% Saturation
desc: Common reference for 100/7.5/100/7.5 NTSC color bars.

name: 100BaseT
desc: An Ethernet standard (IEEE 802.3u) transmitting at 100 Mbps over twisted pair wire. Also called Fast Ethernet. Shared media at 100 Mbps with a hub; 100 Mbps duplex with a switch.

name: 100 Field Per Second
desc: Field rate of some European proposals for a world standard for ATV (Advanced Television).

name: 10/100Base-T
desc: The Ethernet protocol using UTP cable (Cat 5, etc.) transmitting at either 10 Mbps or 100 Mbps between two points.
An official ten-code referring to location. Example: 'What is your 20?' means 'What is your location?'
An official ten-code meaning message received and understood. Synonyms: affirmative, copy, roger.

name: 1080i
desc: 1080 lines of interlaced video (540 lines per field). Usually refers to 1920 x 1080 resolution in 1.78 aspect ratio.

name: 1080p
desc: 1080 lines of progressive video (1080 lines per frame). Usually refers to 1920 x 1080 resolution in 1.78 aspect ratio.

name: 10BaseT
desc: An Ethernet standard transmitting at 10 Mbps over twisted pair wire. Shared media with a hub; 10 Mbps duplex with a switch.

name: 10GbE
desc: 10 Gigabit Ethernet (IEEE 802.3ae). A full-duplex Ethernet standard transmitting at 10 Gbps over twisted pair cable. Does not use CSMA/CD.

name: 10GbE (AV)
desc: 10 Gigabit Ethernet. The minimum recommended network infrastructure for uncompressed AV-over-IP (SDVoE, SMPTE ST 2110). A single uncompressed 4K 60fps stream requires approximately 12 Gbps.

name: 125M
desc: SMPTE 125M. The SMPTE standard for a bit-parallel digital interface for 525-line component video signals.

name: 12.5T Sine-Squared Pulse with 3.579545 MHz Modulation
desc:
Conventional chrominance-to-luminance gain and delay measurements 
are based on analysis of the baseline of a modulated 12.5T pulse. This
pulse is made up of a sine-squared luminance pulse and a chrominance
packet with a sine-squared envelope as shown in the figure below. This
waveform has many advantages. First it allows for the evaluation of both
gain and delay differences with a single signal. It also eliminates the 
need to separately establish a low-frequency amplitude reference with 
a white bar. Since a low-frequency reference pulse is present along 
with the high-frequency information, the amplitude of the pulse itself 
can be normalized. The HAD of 12.5T was chosen in order to occupy 
the chrominance bandwidth of NTSC as fully as possible and to produce 
a pulse with sufficient sensitivity to delay distortion.

name: 12G-SDI
desc: Serial Digital Interface at 11.88 Gbps, defined by SMPTE ST 2082. Supports 4K/60 video over a single 75-ohm coaxial cable. Used in high-end broadcast and live event production.

name: 1410 NTSC Test Signal Generator
desc: Discontinued analog circuit based Tektronix test signal generator that is used to generate full field composite analog test signals. Has been replaced by the Tektronix TSG-170A.
A standardized screw/bolt with a 1/4-inch diameter and 20 threads per inch, used for mounting cameras and production equipment.

name: 1450 Demodulator
desc: Tektronix high quality demodulator that provides envelope and synchronous demodulation.

name: 1480 Waveform Monitor
desc: Discontinued Tektronix waveform monitor. It has been replaced by the 1780R.

name: 1/4” Phone
desc: A connector used in audio production that is characterized by its single shaft with locking tip.

name: 16 QAM
desc: (16 Quadrature Amplitude Modulation)

name: 16 VSB
desc: Vestigial sideband modulation with 16 discrete amplitude levels.

name: 16 x 9
desc: A widescreen television format in which the aspect ratio of the screen is 16 units wide by 9 high as opposed to the 4 x 3 of normal TV.

name: 1780R Waveform Monitor/Vectorscope
desc: Tektronix microprocessor controlled combination waveform monitor and vectorscope.

name: 180 degree rule
desc: a screen direction rule that camera operators must follow -an imaginary line on one side of the axis of action is made( e.g. , between two principal actors in a scene ) , and thecamera must not cross over that line - otherwise , there is adistressing visual discontinuity and disorientation ; similar tothe axis of action ( an imaginary line that separates thecamera from the action before it ) that should not be crossed

name: 180 Degree Video
desc: Video where a consumer is able to look around within the view of a half circle (i.e. 180 degrees). This type of video allows publishers and developers to keep the user’s attention to the front where the action of the video often is. It also creates a more seamless consumer experience where users don’t have to spin their head or mouse to view the content.

name: 1/8th Mini
desc: A small audio connector used frequently in consumer electronics.

name: 1910 Digital Generator/Inserter
desc: Tektronix VITS test signal generator.

name: 1-H
desc: Horizontal scan line interval, usually 64 µs for PAL or 63.5 µs for NTSC.

name: 1-Shot
desc: Camera framing of one subject, typically from the waist up.
Either an aspect ratio twice as wide as it is high (18:9) or the field:frame ratio of interlaced scanning.

name: 2-1/2D (Two and One-Half Dimensions)
desc: This term refers to the kind of dimensionality (i.e., 2D, 3D) that can be created using multiplane anima- tion. Since a layer in such animation can lie in front of one cel (or plane),  or in back of another layer, the resulting effect is of a 3 dimensional world. This is a limited 3D world, however, because the layers are fixed in relation to each other. For this reason, multiplane animation is referred to as 2-1/2 dimensions. It is a very useful technique, however, even for computer graphics, because by ordering the layers in the way a painter does, you can save the computer the need to compare objects that are in different layers (that is, compare them for purposes of hidden surface removal).

name: 2:2 film detection
desc: The ability to determine whether PAL video was converted from film using 2:2 pulldown. Allows video processing to optimize deinterlacing and eliminate artifacts.

name: 2:2 Pull-Down
desc: The process of transferring 24-frames/sec film format into video by repeating each frame as two video fields.

name: 2:3 Pull-Down
desc: See Pull-Down.

name: 24-Bit Color
desc: Color for which each red, green and blue component stores 8 bits of information. 24-bit color is capable of representing over one million different variations of color.

name: 24 frames per second
desc: refers to the standard frame rate or film speed - the numberof frames or images that are projected or displayed persecond ; in the silent era before a standard was set , manyfilms were projected at 16 or 18 frames per second , but thatrate proved to be too slow when attempting to record opticalfilm sound tracks ; aka 24fps or 24p

name: 24P
desc: 24 Frame Per Second, Progressive Scan.  This Has Been The Frame Rate Of Motion Picture Film Since Talkies Arrived. It Is Also One Of The Rates Allowed Fortransmission In The Dvb And Atsc Television Standards – So They Can Handle Film Without Needing Any Frame-Rate Change (3:2 Pull-Down For 60 Fields-Per-Second Systems Or Running Film At 25Fps For 50 Hz Systems). It Is Now Accepted As A Part Of Television Production Formats – Usually Associated With High Definition 1080 Lines, Progressive Scan. A Major Attraction Is A Relatively Easy Path From This To All Major Television Formats As Well As Offering Direct Electronic Support For Motion Picture Film And D-Cinema.

name: 24Psf
desc: 24 Frame Per Second, Progressive Segmented Frame.  A 24P System In Which Each Frame Is Segmented – Recorded As Odd Lines Followed By Even Lines. Unlike Normal Television, The Odd And Even Lines Are From The Same Snapshot In Time – Exactly As Film Is Shown Today On 625/50 Tv Systems. This Way The Signal Is More Compatible (Than Normal Progressive) For Use With Video Systems, E.G. Vtrs, Sdti Or Hd-Sdi Connections, Mixers/Switchers Etc., Which May Also Handle Interlaced Scans. It Can Also Easily Be Viewed Without The Need To Process The Pictures To Reduce 24-Frame Flicker.

name: 25 days, 1:00:00
desc: See: Itu-R Bt.601-2.

name: 25 Frames Per Second
desc: Frame rate of television in all countries not conforming to CCIR system M (NTSC). Also the frame rate of film shot for television in those countries.

name: 25 Hz HDTV Bitstream
desc: A bitstream which contains only Main Profile, High Level (or simpler) video at 25 Hz or 50 Hz frame rates.

name: 25 HZ HDTV IRD
desc: An IRD (Integrated Receiver Decoder) that is capable of decoding and displaying pictures based on a nominal video frame rate of 25 Hz or 50 Hz from MPEG-2 Main Profile, High Level bitstreams, in addition to providing the functionality of a 25 Hz SDTV IRD.

name: 25 Hz SDTV Bitstream
desc: A bitstream which contains only Main Profile, Main Level video at 25 Hz frame rate.

name: 25 Hz SDTV IRD
desc: An IRD (Integrated Receiver Decoder) which is capable of decoding and displaying pictures based on a nominal video frame rate of 25 Hz from MPEG-2 Main Profile, Main Level bitstreams.

name: 29.97 Frames Per Second
desc: Frame rate of NTSC color television, changed from 30 so that the color subcarrier could be interleaved between both the horizontal line frequency and the sound carrier.

name: 2K
desc: A film image scanned into a computer file at a resolution of 2048 horizontal pixels per line.

name: 2-Shot
desc: Camera framing of two subjects within the same shot.

name: 2T Pulse
desc: See the discussion on Sine-Squared Pulses.

name: 30 Degree Rule
desc: A guideline that two consecutive camera shots should be at least 30° apart relative to the subject to prevent a jump cut.

name: 30 Frames Per Second
desc: Frame rate of NTSC prior to color. Frame rate of the ATSC/SMPTE HDEP standard. A potential new film standard.

name: 30 Hz HDTV Bitstream
desc: A bitstream which contains only Main Profile, High Level (or simpler) video at 24000/1001, 24, 30000/1001, 30, 60/1001 or 60 Hz frame rates.

name: 30 Hz HDTV IRD
desc: An IRD (Integrated Receiver Decoder) that is capable of decoding and displaying pictures based on nominal video frame rates of 24000/1001, 24, 30000/1001, 30, 60/1001 or 60 Hz from MPEG-2 Main Profile, High Level bitstreams, in addition to providing the functionality of a 30 Hz SDTV IRD.

name: 30 Hz SDTV Bitstream
desc: A bitstream which contains only Main Profile, Main Level video at 24000/1001, 24, 30000/1001 or 30 Hz frame rate.

name: 30 Hz SDTV IRD
desc: An IRD (Integrated Receiver Decoder) which is capable of decoding and displaying pictures based on a nominal video frame rate of 24000/1001 (approximately 23,98), 24, 3000/1001 (approximately 29,97) or 30 Hz from MPEG-2 Main Profile at Main Level bitstreams.

name: 3:2 Pull-Down
desc: a) The technique used to convert 24 frames per second film to 30 frames per second video. Every other film frame is held for 3 video fields resulting in a sequence of 3 fields, 2 fields, 3 fields, 2 fields, etc. b) A frame cadence found in video that has been telecined or convert- ed from film to video. This cadence is produced because the frame rates for film and video are different. During the process of compression, some compression hardware recognizes this cadence and can further compress video because of it. Material which is video to start with gains no extra compression advantage. Material edited after being telecined may not gain a compression advantage.

name: 3:2 pulldown detection
desc: Technology in scalers detecting the presence of 3:2 pulldown to maximize image detail and sharpness, applying video processing algorithms that optimize image reproduction and avoid jaggies.

name: 3/4 Shot
desc: A loosely defined term for a camera shot at 45° off-center of the subject, or framing subjects from around the knees up.

name: 3.579545 MHz
desc: This is the frequency of the NTSC color subcarrier.

name: 360 Camera
desc: A Device Designed To Capture 360  Photography  And/Or 360  Video. See Also Insta360 Pro.

name: 360 Degree Video
desc: 360 Degree Video is an immersive video format consisting of a video – or series of images – mapped to a portion of a sphere that allows viewing in multiple directions from a fixed central point.The mapping is usually carried out using equirectangular   projection,   where the horizontal coordinate is simply longitude, and the vertical coordinate is simply latitude, with no transformation or scaling applied. Other possible projections are Cube Map (that uses the six faces of a cube as the map shape), Equi-Angular Cubemap – EAC (detailed by Google in 2017 to distribute pixels as evenly as possible across the sphere so that the density of information is consistent, regardless of which direction the viewer is looking), and Pyramid (defined by Facebook in 2016).This type of video content is typically viewable through a head-mounted display, mobile device, or personal computer and allows for three degrees of freedom (see section 4.2 for an explanation of the concept of degrees of freedom).

name: 360 Photography, VR Photography
desc: A Type Of Photography That Uses Image Stitching Methods To Represent A Full 360- Degree View Of A Scene. Unlike A Conventional Panorama, 360 Photography Captures Points Above And Below The Camera, Creating A Spherical Viewing Space. 360 Photographs Can Be Used In Virtual  Reality  Applications.

name: 360 Video
desc: Video Recordings Where Every Direction Is Captured Simultaneously, Utilizing A Single Omnidirectional Camera Or An Array Of Cameras.
A standardized screw/bolt with a 3/8-inch diameter and 16 threads per inch, used for mounting medium to large cameras and equipment.

name: 3 - D
desc: a film that has a three - dimensional , stereoscopic form orappearance , giving the life - like illusion of depth ; oftenachieved by viewers donning special red / blue ( or green ) orpolarized lens glasses ; when 3 - D images are madeinteractive so that users feel involved with the scene , theexperience is called virtual reality ; 3 - D experienced aheyday in the early 1950s ; aka 3D , three - D , Stereoscopic3D , Natural Vision 3D , or three - dimensional

name: 3D Axis (Menu)
desc: The 3D function that moves the image away from the center of rotation. The image can be moved along, or off any of the three axes.

name: 3D Model, 3D Object
desc: A 3D Model Is A Digital Object That Consists Of A Collection Of Points Plotted In Three- Dimensional Space. 3D Models Are Created To Represent Real Or Imagined Physical Objects.

name: 3D Printer
desc: A Device Capable Of Manufacturing A Physical Three-Dimensional Object Based On A Digital 3D  Model.

name: 3D Space
desc: Three dimensional space is easily imagined by looking at a corner of a rectangular room. The corner is called the origin. Each edge leaving from the origin (there are three of them) is called an axis. Each axis extends infinitely in two directions (up/down, left/right, and front/back). Imagine laying long measuring sticks on each axis. These are used to locate specific points in space. On the Cubicomp, or any other graphics systems, the yardsticks are not infinitely long, and 3D space on these devices is not infinite; it is more like an aquarium.

name: 3D Terrain Map, 3D Digital Elevation Model
desc: A 3D  Model Representing The Topography Of An Area. See Also Terrain  2Stl.

name: 3D (Three Dimensional)
desc: Either as in stereoscopic television (NHK has suggested alternating 3DTV transmissions with HDTV), or more often, when referring to ATV, relating to the three dimensions of the spatio-temporal spectrum: horizontal, vertical, and time.

name: 3GP
desc: A media container format used for many mobile phones.

name: 3G-SDI
desc: Serial Digital Interface at 2.97 Gbps, defined by SMPTE ST 424. Supports 1080p/60 and 1080p/50 video over 75-ohm coaxial cable. Widely used in broadcast and live production.

name: 3-Perf
desc: A concept for saving money on film stock by shooting each 35 mm frame in an area covered by three perforations rather than four. The savings is more than enough to compensate for switching from 24 frames per second to 30. Three-perf naturally accommodates a 1.78:1 (16:9) aspect ratio and can be easily masked to the 1.85:1 common in U.S. movie theaters. It changes the shoot-and-protect concept of using theatrical film on television, however, from one in which the protected area is extended vertically to one in which the shooting area is reduced horizontally.

name: 3 Point Lighting
desc: A widely used lighting setup consisting of three lights: a key light, a fill light, and a hair light. 4-point adds a backlight.

name: 3-Shot
desc: Camera framing of three subjects within the same shot.

name: 3XNTSC
desc: A Zenith proposal for an HDEP scheme that would use three times as many scanning lines as NTSC (1575), but would otherwise retain NTS characteristics. It is said to allow easy standards conversion to 525- or 625-scanning line systems and to accept material shot in 1125 scan- ning lines in a 16:9 aspect ratio without difficulty. 3XNTSC would have 1449 active scanning lines, 2:1 interlace, a 4:3 aspect ratio, and a band- width of 37.8 MHz.

4:1:1 indicates that Y’ has been sampled at 13.5 MHz, while Cb
and Cr were each sampled at 3.375 MHz. Thus, for every four samples of
Y’, there is one sample each of Cb and Cr

name: 4:1:1 Chroma Subsampling
desc: Color information sampled at one-fourth the horizontal resolution of the luminance information.

a) A sampling system used to digitize the luminance and color difference components (Y, R-Y, B-Y) of a video signal. The four represents the 13.5 MHz sampling frequency of Y, while the R-Y and B-Y are sampled at 6.75 MHz : effectively between every other line only. b) The component digital video format used by DVD, where there is one Cb sample and one Cr sample for every four Y samples (i.e., 1 pixel in a 2 x 2 grid). 2:1 horizontal downsampling and 2:1 vertical downsampling. Cb and Cr are sampled on every other line, in between the scan lines, with one set of chroma samples for each two luma samples on a line. This amounts to a subsampling of chroma by a factor of two compared to luma (and by a factor of four for a single Cb or Cr component).

name: 4:2:0 Chroma Subsampling
desc: Color information sampled at half the vertical and half the horizontal resolution of the luminance information.

name: 4:2:0 Macroblock
desc: A 4:2:0 macroblock has four 8 x 8 blocks of lumi- nance (Y) and two 8 x 8 blocks of chrominance (one block of Cb and one block, of Cr).

a) A commonly used term for a component digital video format. The details of the format are specified in the ITU-R BT.601 standard document. The numerals 4:2:2 denote the ratio of the sampling frequen- cies of the single luminance channel to the two color difference channels. For every four luminance samples, there are two samples of each color difference channel. b) ITU-R BT.601 digital component waveform sampling standard where the luminance signal is sampled at the rate of 13.5 MHz, and each of the color difference signals, (Cr and Cb) are sampled at the rate of 6.25 MHz each. This results in four samples of the luminance signal for each two samples of the color difference signals. See ITU-R BT.601-2.
Same As 4:2:2, But With The Addition Of A Key Channel That Is Sampled Four Times For Every Four Samples Of The Luminance Channel.

name: 4:2:2 Chroma Subsampling
desc: Color information sampled at half the horizontal resolution of luminance. Popular in high-quality broadcast video systems.

name: 4:2:2p (Professional Profile)
desc: 4:2:2p refers to a higher quality, higher bitrate encoding designed for professional video usage. It allows multiple encodings/decodings before transmission or distribution.

name: 4:2:2 Profile at Main Level
desc: An MPEG-2 profile that benefits the needs of video contribution applications. Features include high-chrominance reso- lution.

name: 4:3 Safe
desc: A method of shooting widescreen footage while retaining a safe area that can be cropped for 4:3 aspect ratio use.
Component digital sampling where for every four luminance samples, the color channels are also sampled four times. Provides full color resolution equal to luminance.
Same as 4:2:2 with the addition of a key channel, and all chan- nels are sampled at the same frequency as the luminance.

name: 4:4:4 Chroma Sampling
desc: Color information sampled at the same rate as luminance. Required for computer graphics with sharp pixel transitions to avoid information loss.

name: 45 Mbps
desc: Nominal data rate of the third level of the hierarchy of ISDN in North America. See also DS3.

name: 480i
desc: 480 lines of interlaced video (240 lines per field). Usually refers to 720 x 480 (or 704 x 480) resolution.

name: 480p
desc: 480 lines of progressive video (480 lines per frame). 480p60 refers to 60 frames per second; 480p30 refers to 30 frames per second; and 480p24 refers to 24 frames per second (film source). Usually refers to 720 x 480 (or 704 x 480) resolution.

name: 48sF
desc: 48 Segmented Frames. The Process Of Taking 24-Frame Progressive Images And Deconstructing Them To Produce 48 Interlaced Frames Each With Half Of The Number Of Lines Of Resolution To Allow Some Hdtv Processors To Pass The Signal And For Viewing On An Interlaced Monitor Without Flicker.

name: 4C
desc: The four-company entity: IBM, Intel, Matsushita, Toshiba.

name: 4D Movies
desc: A Style Of Movie That Introduces Tangibility And Movement To The Audience.

name: 4fsc
desc: Four Times The Frequency Of Sc (Sub-Carrier). The Sampling Rate Of A D2 Digital Video Signal With Respect To The Sub-Carrier Frequency Of An Ntsc Or Pal Analog Video Signal. The 4Fsc Frequency Is 14.3 Mhz In Ntsc And 17.7 Mhz In Pal.

name: 4K
desc: A film image scanned into a computer file at a resolution of 4096 horizontal pixels per line. 4K is considered to be a full-resolution scan of 35 mm film.

name: 4K UHD
desc: A resolution of 3840×2160 pixels. Specified by SMPTE ST 2036-1.

name: 50 Fields Per Second
desc: Field rate of 25 frame-per-second interlaced television.

A Type Of Surround Sound. Six Discrete Audio Channels Are Used: Left, Center, Right, Left Rear (Or Side) Surround, Right Rear (Or Side) Surround, And A Subwoofer (Considered The ".1" As It Is Limited In Bandwidth).

name: 5.1 Channel Audio
desc: An arrangement of five audio channels (left, center, right, left-surround and right-surround) and one subwoofer channel.

name: 520A Vectorscope
desc: Discontinued Tektronix vectorscope. It has been replaced by the 1780R.
Another expression for NTSC television standard using 525 lines/frame and 60 fields/sec.

name: 576i
desc: A video format with 720×576 pixels at an interlaced frame rate. Used in countries with 50 Hz power distribution. Also called PAL.

name: 59.94 Fields Per Second
desc: Field rate of NTSC color television.

name: 5C
desc: The five-company entity: IBM, Intel, Matsushita, Toshiba, Sony.

name: 5Play
desc: A feature set of HDBaseT enabling simultaneous delivery of uncompressed UHD video, audio, 100BaseT Ethernet, control signals, and PoE power over a single CAT5e/6 cable up to 100 meters.
See ITU-R BT.601-2. The international standard for encoding parameters of component digital television for studios, defining 4:2:2 sampling at 13.5 MHz.

name: 60 Fields Per Second
desc: Field rate of the ATSC/SMPTE HDEP standard.

name: 60 Frames Per Second
desc: Frame rate of Showscan and some progressive- ly scanned ATV schemes.
Another expression for PAL television standard using 625 lines/frame and 50 fields/sec.

name: 6G-SDI
desc: SMPTE signal standard for serial digital UHD video at 3840×2160 at 30 Hz progressive. Carries up to 32 audio channels. 6G = 6 Gbps, twice the bit rate of 3G-SDI. See SMPTE ST-2081.

name: 6P Laser Projection
desc: A 3D projection technology using two sets of RGB lasers — one set per eye — with six primary colors total. 3D glasses filter the wavelengths to the correct eye. Provides higher brightness, wider color range, and reduces viewer fatigue versus sequential 3D.

name: 70 V/100 V
desc: High impedance audio distribution standard. Audio signals distributed over long distances at adequate levels, connecting multiple speakers to a power amplifier. 70V used in the US; 100V in Europe.

name: 720p
desc: 720 lines of progressive video (720 lines per frame). Higher definition than standard DVD (480i or 480p). 720p60 refers to 60 frames per second; 720p30 refers to 30 frames per second; and 720p24 refers to 24 frames per second (film source). Usually refers to 1280 x 720 resolution in 1.78 aspect ratio.

name: 75%/100% Bars
desc: See Vectorscope.

name: 75% Amplitude, 100% Saturation
desc: Common reference for 75/7.5/75/7.5 NTSC/EIA color bars.
The IEEE family of wireless networking standards (Wi-Fi). Key variants include 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) and 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E). Used for wireless AV control and increasingly for wireless AV streaming.

name: 802.1p
desc: An IEEE standard for Layer 2 traffic prioritization (Class of Service) using a 3-bit priority field in the 802.1Q VLAN tag. Allows AV traffic to be marked with higher priority than general data traffic on a network.

name: 802.1Q
desc: The IEEE standard for VLAN tagging on Ethernet networks, adding a 4-byte tag to Ethernet frames to identify VLAN membership and 802.1p Class of Service priority. Foundational to VLAN-based AV network segmentation.

name: 8/16 Modulation
desc: The form of modulation block code used by DVD to store channel data on the disc. See Modulation.

name: 8K
desc: Video resolution standard of 7680×4320 pixels, four times the resolution of 4K UHD and sixteen times Full HD. Specified in ITU-R BT.2020 and increasingly supported in professional AV displays and processors.

name: 8K UHD
desc: A resolution of 7680×4320 pixels.

name: 8 mm
desc: A compact videocassette record/playback tape format which uses eight millimeter wide magnetic tape. A worldwide standard established in 1983 allowing high quality video and audio recording. Flexibility, lightweight cameras and reduced tape storage requirements are among the format’s advantages.

name: 8 ohms
desc: Low impedance speaker designation. Low impedance speakers (approximately 4–16 ohms) are used in stereo and program audio applications.

name: 8 PSK (8 Phase Shift Keying)
desc: A variant of QPSK used for satellite links to provide greater data capacity under low-noise conditions.

name: 8 VSB
desc: Vestigial sideband modulation with 8 discrete amplitude levels, used in the ATSC digital television transmission standard.

name: A
desc: Abbreviation for Advanced.

name: A1
desc: The Lead Audio Engineer Or Head Of Audio On A Particular Gig, Generally The Engineer Mixing Foh

name: A2
desc: The Assistant Audio Engineer/Technician Who Follows The Guidance Of The A1. Specific Responsibilities May Vary Depending On The Size Of The Show, But Could Include Mixing Monitors, Coordinating Rf, Patching The Stage/Stage Managing, Among Other Important Tasks.

name: A3
desc: The Assistant Audio Engineer Who Follows The Guidance Of The A1 And A2. Common Duties Involve Pinning The Stage And Running Audio Or Power Cables Where Necessary.

name: A/A (A/X/A) Roll Editing
desc: Editing from a single source using effects to transition from the source to itself (source “A” to “A”) using a picture freeze at the end of one scene to transition the start of the next scene.

name: AAC
desc: Advanced Audio Coding. A digital audio compression standard providing very high sound quality at relatively low bit rates.

name: AAC (Advanced Audio Coding)
desc: Part 7 of the MPEG-2 standard. It is a multichannel coding standard that defines the highest quality multichannel audio known today. It also has modes that perform extremely well for audio, speech and music at <16 kbps.

name: AACS
desc: Advanced Access Content System. A DRM standard for Blu-ray Disc and HD DVD that uses sets of encrypted keys to prevent unauthorized copying of protected content.

name: AAF (Advanced Authoring Format)
desc: Used to describe the standardized metadata definitions that are used to exchange metadata between creative content workstations. This metadata format can contain much more information than the description implies. Nevertheless, this open standard “format” has been created primarily for post-production use. It is worth noting that the definition of AAF does provide for essence exchange as well as metadata exchange.

name: AAL5 (ATM Adaption or Adaptation Layer 5)
desc: Connection-oriented, Unspecified Bit Rate (UBR). Least amount of error checking and retransmission.

name: AAL (ATM Adaption or Adaptation Layer)
desc: ATM protocols that map large data packets into ATM cells are defined by segmentation and reassembly protocols.

name: A and B Cutting
desc: A method of assembling original material in two sepa- rate rolls, allowing optical effects to be made by double printing.

name: A and B Rolls, Tape
desc: Separation of material into two groups of reels (A rolls and B rolls), with alternate scenes on each reel pair (A reel and B reel) to allow transitions between reels.

name: AAP AV Connectivity Modules
desc: Mountable metal plates offering pass-through audio, video, phone, data, power, and control connectors. Interchangeable components creating a customizable AV connectivity solution.

name: AAU (Audio Access Unit)
desc: See Access Unit.

name: ABC
desc: Television network financially supporting development of ACTV and pioneering the use of digital video transmission.

name: A:B:C Notation
desc: The a:b:c notation for sampling ratios, as found in the ITU-R BT.601 specifications, has the following meaning: a) 4:2:2 means 2:1 horizontal downsampling, no vertical downsampling. Think 4 Y samples for every 2 Cb and 2 Cr samples in a scan line. b) 4:1:1 ought to mean 4:1 horizontal downsampling, no vertical. Think 4 Y samples for every 1 Cb and 1 Cr samples in a scan line. It is often misused to mean the same as 4:2:0. c) 4:2:0 means 2:1 horizontal and 2:1 vertical downsampling. Think 4 Y samples for every Cb and Cr samples in a scan line. Not only is this notation not internally consistent, but it is incapable of being extended to represent any unusual sampling ratios, that is different ratios for the Cb and Cr channels.

name: Aberration
desc: A term from optics that refers to anything affecting the fidelity of the image in regards to the original scene.

name: ABKW
desc: See Audio Breakaway.

name: Ableton
desc: A Digital Audio Workstation (Daw) Used To Record, Sequence And Play Back Music. Commonly Used For Live Performance Playback.

name: Abort
desc: Halts the program and returns control to the operator or operating system.

name: above the line
desc: usually refers to that part of a film's budget that covers thecosts associated with major creative talent : the stars , thedirector , the producer ( s ) and the writer ( s ) , although films withexpensive special effects ( and few stars ) have more ' abovethe line ' budget costs for technical aspects ; the term'sopposite is below the line

name: Above The Title
desc: In Advertisements, When The Performer'S Name Appears Before The Title Of The Show Or Play. Reserved For The Big Stars!

name: A/B Roll
desc: a) Creating fades, wipes and other transitions from one video source to another. b) Typically, A/B roll is an editing technique where scenes or sounds on two source reels (called Roll A and Roll B) are played simultaneously to create dissolves, wipes and other effects. On nonlinear editing systems, A/B roll refers to using two source streams (.avi,.wav,.tga and so on) to create an effect.

name: A/B Roll Editing
desc: Editing from two source VCRs (“A” and “B”) to a third (recording) VCR. Typically a switcher or mixer, such as the Digital Video Mixer, is used to provide transition effects between sources. Control over the machines and process can be done manually or automatically using an edit controller.

name: A/B Roll Linear Editing
desc: Recording edits from two video sources, such as two VCRs to a third, to achieve transition effects. See also, B-Roll.

name: A-B Rolls
desc: Duplicate rolls of videotape information having identical time code; required to achieve effects of dissolves.

name: Absolute Time Code
desc: Absolute time code (ATC) is generally recorded in the subcode or control track region of any digital tape. This is the code that digital tape machines use to locate specific points on a tape for autolocation or other functions. In some machines it is even used to synchronize the tape to other equipment. ATC is precisely accurate and usually conforms to the IEC standard which is easily converted to the more commercially used SMPTE time code. Unlike SMPTE, ATC always begins at zero at the beginning of a digital tape and increments one frame at a time until recording stops. Some DAT machines have the ability to function without ATC on a tape while others simply will not play a tape without it. These days almost all machines record it automatically so it will always be on every tape.

name: Absolute Values
desc: Position And Orientation Within A Virtual Space As Measured From A Single, Constant Point Of Origin. If A Real Or Virtual Object Is Moved, Its Previous Coordinates Are Ignored, And New Position And Orientation Measurements Are Taken.

name: Absorption
desc: In acoustics, the opposite of reflection. Sound waves are “absorbed” or soaked up by soft materials they encounter. Studio designers put this fact to work to control the problem of reflections coming back to the engineer’s ear and interfering with the primary audio coming from the monitors. The absorptive capabilities of various materials are rated with an “Absorption Coefficient”.

name: Absorption Coefficient
desc: The Fraction Of Sound Energy That Is Absorbed At Any Surface. It Has A Value Between 0 And 1 And Varies With The Frequency And Angle Of Incidence Of The Sound.

name: abstract ( form )
desc: a type of film that rejects traditional narrative in favor of usingpoetic form ( color , motion , sound , irrational images , etc. ) toconvey its meaning or feeling ; aka non - linear ; see alsoavantgarde

name: Abstraction layer
desc: In Hog 4 OS, the software layer that separates the user from raw DMX values, presenting fixture parameters in real-world units (e.g., degrees of rotation rather than 0–255 DMX values) for more intuitive programming.

name: Abstract setting
desc: A stage setting that is stylized rather than photorealistic or representational — one that does not attempt to present a realistic stage picture.

name: absurd ( absurdism )
desc: a stage , philosophical and literary term originally , adopted byfilm - makers , in which ordinary settings become bizarre ,illogical , irrational , unrealistic , meaningless , and incoherent

name: A-B Test
desc: A Test Between Two Components Or Rooms. For Example, A Test Between Two Different Pre-Amplifiers. For The Test To Be Scientifically Valid The Levels Should Be Matched.

name: ABU (Asia-Pacific Broadcasting Union)
desc: The Asia-Pacific Broadcasting Union (ABU) is a professional association of television and radio broadcast- ers. It has over 100 members in 52 countries. The ABU was established in 1964 to promote the development of broadcasting in the Asia-Pacific region and to organize cooperative activities amongst its members.

name: A Bus
desc: The top row of the two rows of video source select buttons asso- ciated with a given M/E.

name: A Bus Keyer
desc: A keyer that appears only on top of an “A” bus background video on an M/E.

name: AC
desc: Alternating Current. Electron flow that changes direction alternately.

name: AC-3
desc: See: Dolby Digital.

name: AC’97, AC’98
desc: These are definitions by Intel for the audio I/O implemen- tation for PCs. Two chips are defined: an analog audio I/O chip and a digi- tal controller chip. The digital chip will eventually be replaced by a software solution. The goal is to increase the audio performance of PCs and lower cost.

name: Academy
desc: Pertaining to specifications that meet the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences standards, such as academy leader, academy format (for film stock), academy countdown, and so forth.

name: Academy Awards
desc: the name given to the prestigious film awards presented eachyear by AMPAS ( the Academy of Motion Picture Arts andSciences , or simply ' The Academy ' ) , a professional honoraryorganization within the industry , since 1927. The annualawards show , in slang , is sometimes referred to as a kudocast , see also Oscars

name: ACATS (Advisory Committee on Advanced Television Service)
desc: A group comprised almost exclusively of presidents, chief executive officers, and chairs of the boards of major broadcasting, CATV, consumer electronics, and entertainment production companies. It is currently supported by a planning subcommittee (with two advisory groups and six working parties), a systems subcommittee (with four working parties), and an implementation subcommittee (with two working parties). ACATS is an entity under the FCC, and is the approving body of advanced TV in the USA. ACTS recommended the ATSC digital TV system to the FCC in November 1995.

name: AC Bias
desc: The alternating current, usually of frequency several times high- er than the highest signal frequency, that is fed to a record head in addi- tion to the signal current. AC bias serves to linearize the recoding process and is universally used in analog recording. Generally, a large AC bias is necessary to achieve maximum long wavelength output and linearity, but a lower value of bias is required to obtain maximum short-wavelength output. The mechanism of AC bias can best be explained in terms of anhysteresis.

name: ACC
desc: See Automatic Color Correction.

name: Accelerance
desc: The Frequency Response Function Of Acceleration/Force. Also Known As Inertance

name: Acceleration
desc: Graphic accelerators function like application-specific microprocessors whose purpose is to work in conjunction with a PC’s host microprocessor to display graphics. In general, graphic accelerators control frame memory, color processing, resolution, and display speed. with the advent of the high-speed local buses and higher clock rates, accelerators operate on 32-, 64-, and 128-bit pixel data.

name: Accelerator
desc: Specialized Hardware That Increases The Speed Of Graphics Calculations.

name: Accent Light
desc: 1) Illumination Used To Make Something Stand Out. It May Be Done With Intensity And/Or Color. 2) A Luminaire That Provides Such Illumination.

name: Accent Lighting
desc: A Technique That Emphasizes A Particular  Object Or Draws Attention To A Particular Area. Accent Lighting Usually Utilizes The Tight Beam Control Of Par-Lamps And Mr- Lamps. Also Called Highlighting.

name: Accent Luminaire
desc: A Type Of Luminaire That Includes Ceiling-Mounted Track And Directional Luminaires And Recessed Accent Luminaires.Accent Luminaires Provide Directional Lighting To Accent An Object Or An Area Within A Space.

name: Acceptance Angle
desc: In fiber optics, the critical angle measured from the center axis of the fiber below which incoming light must be directed to enter the core and propagate through total internal reflection.

name: Access Channels
desc: Channels set aside by a cable operator for use by third parties, including the public, educational institutions, local govern- ments, and commercial interests unaffiliated with the operator.

name: Access Time
desc: a) The time required to receive valid data from a memory device following a read signal. b) This is the time it takes from when a disk command is sent, until the disk reaches the data sector requested. Access time is a combination of latency, seek time, and the time it takes for the command to be issued. Access time is important in data intensive situa- tions like hard disk recording, multimedia playback, and digital video appli- cations. Lower access times are better. Keeping your drives in good shape with periodic de-fragging, etc. will ensure that your drive is providing the fastest access times it can.

name: Access Unit (AU)
desc: a) The coded data for a picture or block of sound and any stuffing (null values) that follows it. b) A coded representation of a presentation unit. In the case of audio, an access unit is the coded repre- sentation of an audio frame. In the case of video, an access unit includes all the coded data for a picture, and any stuffing that follows it, up to but not including the start of the next access unit. If a picture is not preceded by a group_start_code or a sequence_header_code, the access unit begins with a picture_start_code. If a picture is preceded by a group_start_code and/or a sequence_header_code, the access unit begins with the first byte of the first of these start codes. If it is the last picture preceding a sequence_end_code in the bit stream, all bytes between the last byte of the coded picture and the sequence_end_code (including the sequence_end_code) belong to the access unit.

name: Access Unit Header (AU Header)
desc: Optional information preceding an Access Unit Payload. This information consists of decoding and/or presen- tation time stamps. This information may be defaulted, resulting in an empty AU header. The format of the AU header is determined in the ES header.

name: Access Unit Payload (AU Payload)
desc: The data field of an access unit.

name: AC Coefficient
desc: Any discrete cosine transform (DCT) coefficient for which the frequency in one or both dimensions is non-zero.

name: Accommodation
desc: Change In The Focal Length Of The Eye'S Lens To Maintain Focus On A Moving Close Object.

name: Account
desc: See Login Account.

name: AC Coupled
desc: a) AC coupling is a method of inputting a video signal to a circuit to remove any DC offset, or the overall voltage level that the video signal “rides” on. One way to find the signal is to remove the DC offset by AC coupling, and then do DC restoration to add a known DC offset (one that we selected). Another reason AC coupling is important is that it can remove harmful DC offsets. b) A connection that removes the constant voltage (DC component) on which the signal (AC component) is riding.Implemented by passing the signal through a capacitor.

name: AccuFrame
desc: A Christie technology nullifying image artifacts (smearing or double-image) in high-speed simulation by accurately managing frame display at various frame rates.

name: Accumulator
desc: One or more registers associated with the Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU), which temporarily store sums and other arithmetical and logical results of the ALU.

name: Accuracy
desc: The closeness of the indicated value to the true value

name: AC Erasure
desc: See Erasure.

name: ACLE (Analog Component Link Equipment)
desc: A form of MAC optimized for remote broadcasting links.

name: A Column Array
desc: Generally A Single Cabinet With The Elements Of The Array Oriented In A Straight Line To Provide Some Level Of Directivity At A Range Of Frequencies.

name: A Constant Curvature Array
desc: An Array Of Multiple Boxes, Each At The Same Angle Of Seperation Between Each Array Element.

name: Ac Or Alternating Current
desc: Often Abbreviated As Ac, An Electrical Current That Is Common Worldwide In The Distribution Of Electricity. This Current Has A Periodical Reversal Of Direction Which Is Measured In Cycles Per Second, Or Hz. In The Us, All Power That Comes Into Standard Outlets Is Alternating Current At 120 Volts. We Abbreviate This As 120Vac. The Uk, Europe, Australia, And Many Other Parts Of The World Utilize 240Vac, Which Requires A Transformer For Use With Audio Equipment That Is Made For Use In The Us. The Other Common Form Of Current Is Dc (Direct Current) Which Maintains A Single Direction.

name: Acoustical reflector panel
desc: A reflective panel hung above the audience in an auditorium to direct sound into desired zones. Often decorative in nature. Also called a cloud or acoustic cloud.

name: Acoustical Reflector Panel (Cloud)
desc: A Reflective Panel Hung In The Auditorium, Generally Above The Audience, That Is Used To Direct Sound Into Desired Zones. Often Decorative In Nature.

name: Acoustic Echo Canceller
desc: Full-duplex audio technology; used for the elimination of acoustically-coupled return echoes within a teleconference room. Note that all microphones connected to an AEC are active at all times. Consequently, as more microphones are added, the total transmitted noise level (caused by picking up room ambient noise) increases. See also Tail Time, Echo Suppresser and Echo Return Loss Enhancement.

name: Acoustic Emission
desc: The Detected Energy That Is Generated When Materials Are Deformed Or Break. For Rolling Element Bearing Analysis, It Is The Periodic Energy Generated By The Over Rolling Of Particles Or Flaws And Detected By The Display Of The Bearing Flaw Frequencies.

name: Acoustic Impedance
desc: Z=R+Jx

name: Acoustic Material
desc: Any Material Considered In Terms Of Its Acoustical Properties. Commonly And Especially, A Material Designed To Absorb Sound.

name: Acoustics
desc: Technically, Acoustics Refers To The Study Of Sound And Human Hearing But We Often Use The Term To Describe The Sonic Characteristics Of A Performance Space. Larger Spaces Tend To Have More Reverberation, Or “Wet” Acoustics, While Smaller Spaces Tend To Have A Tighter Sound, Or “Dry” Acoustics.

name: Acoustic Shadow
desc: An area in which sound waves are attenuated due to the presence of an acoustic absorber or reflector in the path of the sound waves.

name: Acoustic Suspension
desc: A type of speaker design using a sealed cabinet. Primarily used for low frequency enclosures, acoustic suspension designs use the air mass within the cabinet as a “spring” to help return the relatively massive speaker to the rest position. This allows heavier, longer throw drivers to be used, but results in a less efficient design requiring more amplifier power.

name: Acquisition Team
desc: The Vad Team Responsible For The World / Reality Capture Of Real - Life Locations And Textures Viaphotogrammetry , Lidar , Photography And Other Methods . See Also Building Team .

name: Acronym
desc: An Acronym For A Contrived Reduction Of Nomenclature Yielding Mnemonics

name: act
desc: a main division within the plot of a film ; a film is often dividedby ' plot points ' ( places of dramatic change ) rather than acts ;long films are divided mid - way with an intermission

name: ACT (Anti-Comet-Tail)
desc: This is a complex technique of preventing picture highlights from “comet-tailing” due to lack of beam current in the camera tube. (The usually colored trail behind a moving, very bright light/reflection in a picture is called a “comet-tail” since the effect looks similar to an astronomical comet.) The technique involves a special tube and circuitry to drive it. Basically, the charge due to a very bright object is never allowed to build up to an unmanageable level by discharging the target above a preset level during horizontal retrace time when the ACT action is turned on, with an increased beam current.

name: Act Curtain
desc: A Curtain (Sometimes Designed For A Specific Show) That Is Opened To Signal The Beginning Of A Performance. The Front Curtain Is Often Used For This Purpose.

name: Acting area
desc: The area within a performance space within which actors may move in full view of the audience; a specific portion used for acting during all or part of a performance.

name: Acting edition
desc: The published text of a play approved by the author for public performance. Used by production companies for licensing.

name: action
desc: ( 1 ) any movement or series of events ( usually rehearsed ) thattake place before the camera and propel the story forwardtoward its conclusion ; ( 2 ) the word called out ( by amegaphone ) at the start of the current take during filming toalert actors to begin performing ; ( 3 ) also refers to the maincomponent of action films - that often contain significantamounts of violence

name: Action Design
desc: A Form Of Visualization Which Uses 3D Assets And Physical Simulation To Plan Live - Action Stunts .Also Called Safetyvis And Stuntvis .

name: Action-safe Area
desc: The area of a video frame ensuring important action is seen by most viewers. Defined as 93% of the width and height of the production aperture.

name: Active
desc: Describes Any Audio Device That Requires A Power Source. This Can Range From Small Devices Such An Active Di (Powered By Internal Battery Or By Phantom Power Through An Xlr Cable) To An Active (Powered) Speaker, Which Usually Needs A Full 120V Ac Due To The Power Needs Of The Built-In Amplification Circuit.

name: Active crossover
desc: A circuit separating the audio signal into appropriate frequency bands for woofer, midrange, and tweeter, placed in the signal path ahead of the amplifier.

name: Active Di
desc: See Di Box. A Type Of Di That, Unlike A Passive Di, Requires A Power Source, Often Phantom Power Or Batteries And Uses A Preamplifier To Provide A Stronger Signal.

name: Active HDMI Cable
desc: An HDMI cable augmented by signal equalization electronics, drawing power through the HDMI connector or external supply, enabling longer cable lengths than passive cables.

name: Active Line (PAL)
desc: The part of the video waveform (usually 64 µs), which occupies the visible part of the signal (without sync, blanking or burst).The active line time is usually 52 µs. Also called Active Line Time or Active Video.

name: Active Lines
desc: The total number of scanning lines minus those scanning lines devoted to the vertical blanking interval.

name: Active Line Time
desc: The duration of a scanning line minus that period devoted to the horizontal blanking interval.

name: Active Marker
desc: A Physical Reference For Tracking Purposes With A Unique I.D. Such As A Strobing Led Bulb .

name: ActiveMovie
desc: Microsoft’s architecture for the control and processing of streams of multimedia data and software that uses this architecture to play digital video and sound. It is intended to supersede Video for Windows®.

name: Active Optical Cable (AOC)
desc: A hybrid HDMI cable using optical fiber strands and copper wire to transmit AV, auxiliary data, and power. Supports longer lengths than all-copper active cables.

name: Active Picture
desc: That portion of the ITU-R BT.601 digital picture signal between the SAV and EAV data words.

name: Active Picture Area
desc: The part of a TV picture that contains actual picture as opposed to sync or other data. Vertically the active picture area is 487 lines for NTSC and 576 lines for PAL. The inactive area is called blanking.

name: Active Pixel Region
desc: On a computer display, the area of the screen used for actual display of pixel information.

name: Active Video
desc: The part of the video waveform that is not specified to be blanking, burst, or sync information. Most of the active video, if not all of it, is visible on the display screen.

name: Active Video Lines
desc: All video lines that are not in the horizontal and vertical blanking intervals.

name: Active Window
desc: On A PC, the only window that recognizes input (activi- ty) from the keyboard and mouse; only one window is active at a time.

name: Activity Detection
desc: Refers to a method built into some multiplexers for detecting movement within the camera’s field of view (connected to the multiplexer), which is then used to improve camera recording update rate.

name: actor
desc: refers either to a male performer , or to any male or femalewho plays a character role in an on - screen film ; alternategender - neutral terms : player , artist , or performer

name: actress
desc: refers to any female who portrays a role in a film

name: Actuator
desc: Usually Mechanical (Hydraulic) Or Electric Means Used To Provide Force Or Tactile Feedback To A User.

name: ACTV (Advanced Compatible Television)
desc: Techniques for ATV trans- mission developed by the DSRC, with support initially from NBC and RCA/GE Consumer Electronics (now Thomson Consumer Electronics) and with later support from such organizations as ABC and HBO. There are two ACTVs. a) ACTV I is a channel-compatible, receiver-compatible system utilizing many different techniques to add widescreen panels and increase horizontal and vertical resolution. Among the techniques are the filling of a Fukinuki hole, time compression, seam-elimination, spatio-temporal filtering, and quadrature modulation of the picture carrier. The last prevents direct compatibility with videotape recorders and with ordinary satellite transmission techniques. b) ACTV II is ACTV I plus an augmentation chan- nel to improve resolution and sound.

name: Acuity
desc: See Visual Acuity.

name: A/D
desc: See A-to-D Converter.

name: ADA
desc: Americans with Disabilities Act. Requires access to public spaces by people with disabilities. Governs theatre venue design including accessible seating, ramps, and restrooms.

name: adaptation
desc: the presentation of one art form through another medium ; afilm based upon , derived from ( or adapted from ) a stage play( or from another medium such as a short story , book , article ,history , novel , video game , comic strip / book , etc. ) whichbasically preserves both the setting and dialogue of theoriginal ; can be in the form of a script ( screenplay ) or aproposal treatment

name: Adaptation Field
desc: Ancillary program data (especially PCR) which are uncoded and are transmitted at least every 100 ms after the TS header of a data stream (PID) belonging to a program.

name: Adaptation Layer Entity (AL Entity)
desc: An instance of an MPEG-4 systems resource that processes AL PDUs associated to a single FlexMux channel.

name: Adaptation Layer Protocol Data Unit (AL PDU)
desc: The smallest protocol unit exchanged between peer AL entities. It consists of AL PDU header  and AL PDU payload. One or more AL PDUs with data from one or more elementary streams form the payload of a FlexMux PDU.

name: Adaptation Layer Protocol Data Unit Header (AL PDU Header)
desc: Optional information preceding the AL PDU payload. It is mainly used for error detection and framing of the AL PDU payload. The format of the AL PDU header is determined when opening/configuring the associated FlexMux channel.

name: Adaptation Layer Protocol Data Unit Payload (AL PDU Payload)
desc: The data field of an AL PDU.

name: Adaptation Layer Service Data Unit (AL-SDU)
desc: An information unit whose integrity is preserved in transfer from one AL user to the peer AL user.

name: Adaptation Layer User (AL User)
desc: A system entity that makes use of the services of the adaptation layer, typically an elementary stream entity.

name: Adapter
desc: A device used to achieve compatibility between two items of audio/video equipment.

name: Adaptive
desc: Changing according to conditions.

name: Adaptive Bit Allocation
desc: The allocation of more bits to image areas of high activity which does not lend itself to all types of video compression techniques, especially when interframe sampling is used.

name: Adaptive bitrate streaming
desc: A streaming technique dynamically adjusting video quality based on available bandwidth, switching between multiple pre-encoded renditions to minimize buffering. Used by HLS, DASH, and OTT platforms.

name: Adaptive Compression
desc: Data compression software that continually analyzes and compensates its algorithm, depending on the type and content of the data and the storage medium.

name: Adaptive Differential Pulse Code Modulation
desc: a) A compression tech- nique that encodes the predictive residual instead of the original waveform signal so that the compression efficiency is improved by a predictive gain. Rather than transmitting PCM samples directly, the difference between the estimate of the next sample and the actual sample is transmitted. This difference is usually small and can thus be encoded in fewer bits than the sample itself. b) Differential pulse code modulation that also uses adaptive quantizing; an audio coding algorithm which provides a modest degree of compression together with good quality. c) A technique for compressing  the transmission requirements of digital signals. ADPCM has been used by ABC between New York and Washington to allow NTSC transmission on a 45 Mbps (DS3) telephone company data transmission circuit. d) A pulse code modulation system typically operating at a high sampling rate where- by coding is based on a prior knowledge of the signal to be processed (i.e., greater than, equal to, or less than the previous sample). The system is adaptive in that digital bits of code signify different sizes of signal change depending on the magnitude of the signal.

name: Adaptive Emphasis
desc: An ATV technique for improving detail of dark parts of the picture by increasing their level. If a complementary de-emphasis is performed at the receiver, noise can be reduced. Dolby B noise reduction (the form of Dolby noise reduction most common in consumer cassette recorders) is a classic example of complementary adaptive emphasis.

name: Adaptive Filter
desc: A filter which changes its parameters on a continual basis to guarantee a constant or desired output value.

name: Adaptive Multichannel Prediction
desc: Multichannel data reduction exploit- ing statistical inter-channel dependencies in audio.

name: Adaptive Noise Allocation
desc: Variable assignment of coding noise in audio frequency bands based on a psychoacoustic model.

name: Adaptive Quantization
desc: Varying quantization values are applied based on some model analysis of the data characteristics.

name: Adaptor
desc: A device that allows an ordinary NTSC television to receive pic- tures from a non-receiver-compatible ATV system.

name: ADC
desc: Analog to Digital Converter. A device that converts continuous analog signals into discrete digital values for processing, storage, or transmission.

name: ADC (A-D, A/D, A-to-D)
desc: Analog To Digital Conversion. Also Referred To As Digitization Or Quantization. The Conversion Of An Analog Signal Into The Digital Data Representation Of That Signal--Normally For Subsequent Use In A Digital Machine. For Tv, Samples Of Audio And Video Are Taken, The Accuracy Of The Process Depending On Both The Sampling Frequency And The Resolution Of The Analog Amplitude Information--How Many Bits Are Used To Describe The Analog Levels. For Tv Pictures Eight Or 10-Bits Are Normally Used; For Sound, 16 Or 20-Bits Are Common, And 24-Bits Are Being Introduced. The Itu-R 601 Standard Defines The Sampling Of Video Components Based On 13.5 Mhz, And Aes/Ebu Defines Sampling Of 44.1 And 48 Khz For Audio.For Pictures, The Samples Are Called Pixels, Each Containing Data For Brightness And Color. See Also: Binary, Bit.

name: A/D Conversion
desc: Short For Analog To Digital Conversion, This Is The Process By Which An Analog Signal Is Sampled Into Digital Information For Use By Digital Audio Equipment. This Can Often Be The Weakest Link In A Signal Chain, As High-Quality A/D Converters Are Very Expensive.

name: A/D Converter
desc: A Device That Converts An Analog Audio Signal To Digital Information. Can Operate With Varying Sample Rates And Bit-Depth.

name: A/D Converter (Adc)
desc: Analog-To-Digital Converter, A Device That Trans Forms Incoming Analog Signals Into Digital Form

name: Added Calibrator
desc: This is a feature of some waveform monitors which allows an internal 1 volt calibrator signal to be used as a reference for amplitude measurements.

name: Add Edit
desc: An edit added between consecutive frames in a sequence segment with the timeline. An add edit separates segment sections so the user can modify or add effects to a subsection of the segment.

name: Adder
desc: Device that forms, as output, the sum of two or more numbers presented as inputs.

name: Additive
desc: Any material in the coating of magnetic tape other than the oxide and the binder resins; for example, plasticizers (materials used to soften an otherwise hard or brittle binder), lubricants (materials used to lower the coefficient of friction of an otherwise high-friction binder), fungicides (materials used to prevent fungus growth), dispersants (to uniformly distribute the oxide particles) or dyes.

name: Additive Color
desc: Color produced by “adding” colors, usually the combina- tion of red, green and blue.

name: Additive color model
desc: A projector model using red, green, and blue light as additive primaries. Equal combinations produce cyan, magenta, and yellow; all three produce white.

name: Additive color process
desc: Also called RGB. A color generation process combining red, green, and blue to make all colors. All three at 100% produce white; all at 0% produce black.

name: Additive Color System
desc: Color specification system in which primary colors are added together to create a desired color. An example is the red/green/blue (RGB) system. Additive systems are generally associated with light emitting devices (CRTs).

name: Additive Mix
desc: A mix wherein the instantaneous video output signal is equal to the weighted sum of the input video signals. Unless otherwise specified, “mix” is taken to mean “additive mix”.

name: Address
desc: Number that indicates the position of a word in the memory.

name: Addressability
desc: The capability to selectively and remotely activate, disconnect or descramble television signals in individual subscribers’ homes. A functionality of pay-per-view systems.

name: Addressable
desc: Capable of being activated or accessed remotely by sig- nals sent from a cable system’s headend (usually refers to descramblers and other set-top boxes.)

name: Address Bus
desc: Set of wires (typically 32) used to transmit addresses, usually from the microprocessor to a memory or I/O device.

name: Address Decoding
desc: Process of selecting a specific address or field of addresses to enable unique devices.

name: Address Dial
desc: See SCSI Address Dial.

name: Addressing Modes
desc: Various methods of specifying an address as part of an instruction. See Direct Addressing, Indirect Addressing, Immediate Addressing and Indexed Addressing.

name: Adhesion
desc: The degree to which the coating adheres to the base film. Anchorage may be checked by measuring the force required to separate the coating from the base film by means of a specially designed plow blade or, more simply, by determining whether the coating can be peeled from  the base film by means of ordinary pressure-sensitive adhesive tape.

name: Adiabatic
desc: Literally, It Means "Not To Pass Through." In Describing The High-Density Foam Used Inside The Hr Series Studio Monitors, It Means That Internal Reflections Within The Cabinet Are Absorbed By The Foam. In Physical Terms, It Means The Mechanical Energy Of The Sound Wave Is Converted Into Heat Energy.

name: ADIF (Audio Data Interchange Format)
desc: ADIF is just one header at the beginning of the AAC file. The rest of the data is just the same as a raw Advanced Audio Coding (AAC) file.

name: Adjacent Channel
desc:
A television transmission channel immediately adja cent to an existing channel. For example, channel 3 is adjacent to channels
2 and 4. There are three exceptions to what might otherwise be considered
adjacent channels: there is a small gap between channels 4 and 5, there 
is a large gap between channels 6 and 7, and there is an enormous gap
between channels 13 and 14. Adjacent channels figure into ATV in two
ways. a) First, it is currently illegal to broadcast on adjacent channels in 
a single location. Some ATV proponents feel that augmentation channels
might someday be allowed to be placed in adjacent channels. If half-size 
(3 MHz) or smaller augmentation channels are used, all current broadcast ers could then be allowed an augmentation channel. Some proponents feel 
the use of a low power digital augmentation channel will allow adjacent
channels to be used without interference. b) Second, some ATV proposals
require that the augmentation channel be adjacent to the transmission
channel or require a larger than normal transmission channel, thus occupy ing a channel and one of its adjacent channels.
Adjust input video timing to match a reference video input. Eliminates the
need for manual timing adjustments

name: Adjustable Head
desc: An Adjustable Luminaire That Is Surface-Mounted, Or That Inserts Into A Linear Track And Provides Directional Lighting.

name: ad lib
desc: a line of dialogue improvised by an actor during aperformance ; can be either unscripted ordeliberate ; improvisation consists of ad - libbed dialogue ( andaction ) that is invented or created by the performer

name: Administrator
desc: See System Administrator and Network Administrator.

name: ADO (Ampex Digital Optics)
desc: Trade name for digital effects system manufactured and sold by Ampex.

name: Adobe RGB
desc: A color space specification by Adobe Systems offering a wider color gamut than sRGB. Supported in Photoshop and other Adobe software.

name: ADPCM
desc: See Adaptive Differential Pulse Code Modulation.

name: ADR
desc: Additional Dialogue Recording. Recording audio to be added to a project during editing or post-production.

name: ADR (Automatic Display Replacement)
desc: The process of looping play- back of a selected region in a sequence and automatically recording multi- ple replacement takes.

name: ADSL
desc: Asymmetrical Digital Subscriber Line. The most common DSL technology, designed to deliver more bandwidth downstream (from ISP to customer) than upstream.

name: ADSP
desc: Advanced Digital Sync Processing. Extron's method of restoring original sync timing relationship for a stable sync signal while allowing H-shift or V-shift centering control.

name: Adsr
desc: Acronym For Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release, Referring To The Amplitude Envelope Of A Sound

name: ADSR (Attack, Decay, Sustain and Release)
desc: These are the four parameters found on a basic synthesizer envelope generator. An envelope generator is sometimes called a transient generator and is traditionally used to control the loudness envelope of sounds, through some modern designs allow for far greater flexibility. The Attack, Decay, and Release parameters are rate or time controls. Sustain is a level control. When a key is pressed, the envelope generator will begin to rise to its full level at the rate set by the attack parameter, upon reaching peak level it will begin to fall at the rate set by the decay parameters to the level set by the sustain control. The envelope will remain at the sustain level as long a the key is held down. Whenever a key is released, it will return to zero at the rate set by the release parameters.

name: ADTS (Audio Data Transport Stream)
desc: ADTS headers are present before each Advanced Audio Coding (AAC) raw_data_block or block of 2 to 4 raw_data_blocks. Until the MPEG revision from December 2002 for MPEG-4 AAC ADTS headers, this was basically the same as a MP3 header, except that the emphasis field was not present for MPEG-2 AAC, only for MPEG-4 AAC.

name: ADTV
desc: Advanced Definition Television. An early HDTV system proposed by the Advanced Television Research Consortium in 1992, now superseded by US HDTV standards.

name: ADTV (Advanced Definition Television)
desc: A term sometimes used for both EDTV and HDTV.

name: Advance
desc: The separation between a point on the sound track of a film and the corresponding picture image.

name: Advance Contact
desc: The Person In Charge Of Negotiating The Specifics Of An Artist Engagement At A Venue. This Is Usually The Venue Production Manager (Pm), A Promoter Rep, Or A Full-Time Venue Staff Member. This Person Is Usually Listed In The Deal Memo, And Often An Introduction Is Made Over Email From The Artist'S Tour Manager.

name: Advanced Coding Efficiency (ACE)
desc: The ACE profile supports coding efficiency for both rectangular and arbitrary shaped objects. It is suitable for applications such as mobile broadcast reception, acquisition of image sequences, and other applications where high coding efficiency is request- ed and a small footprint isn’t the prime concern.

name: Advanced Encoder
desc: A device that changes RGB or DAV into NTSE utilizing some form or forms of pre-filtering to reduce or eliminate NTSC artifacts. Some advanced encoders also offer image enhancement, gamma correction, and the like.

name: Advanced Real-Time Simple (ARTS)
desc: The ARTS profile provides advanced error resilient coding techniques of rectangular video objects using a back channel and improved temporal resolution stability with the low buffering delay. Use it for real-time coding applications, such as the videophone, teleconferencing and remote observation.

name: Advanced Television Systems Committee (ATSC)
desc: The US-based organization that is defining the high definition television standard for the U.S.. A sort of NTSE for ATV. It is comprised of three technology groups and a number of smaller committees. T1 Group is studying receiver-com- patible improved NTSC. T2 Group is studying non-receiver-compatible 525 scanning line production, distribution, and display systems. T3 Group is studying HDTV.

name: Advanced TV
desc: Although sometimes used interchangeably, advanced and high-definition television (HDTV) are not one and the same. Advanced tele- vision (ATV) would distribute wide-screen television signals with resolution substantially better than current systems. It requires changes to current emission regulations, including transmission standards. In addition, ATV would offer at least two-channel, CD-quality audio.

name: AEA (American Electronics Association)
desc: An organization of manufac- turers more associated with computers and communications than is the EIA. The AEA has established an ATV Task Force, the members of which include

name: AEC
desc: Acoustic Echo Cancellation. A DSP technique that removes the echo created when audio from a far-end speaker is picked up by the near-end microphone and sent back.

name: Aerial Cables
desc: Optical fiber cables designed for outdoor aerial installations on poles, withstanding wind, ice loading, UV radiation, thermal cycling, stress, and aging.

name: aerial shot
desc: a camera shot filmed in an exterior location from far overhead( from a bird's eye view ) , as from a helicopter ( mostcommon ) , blimp , balloon , plane , or kite ; a variation onthecrane shot ; if the aerial shot is at the opening of a film , akaan establishing shot

name: Aes
desc: Audio Engineering Society.

name: AES3
desc: See AES/EBU Digital Audio.

name: AES67
desc: An AES standard for audio-over-IP interoperability. Defines a common transport layer (RTP over UDP/IP) allowing Dante, Ravenna, LIVEWIRE, and Q-LAN systems to exchange audio streams. Uses PTP (IEEE 1588) for synchronization.

name: AES (Audio Engineering Society)
desc: The official association of technical personnel, scientists, engineers and executives in the audio field. Of potential interest in electronic production are the following

name: AES/EBU
desc: Informal Name For A Digital Audio Standard Established Jointly By The Aes (Audio Engineering Society) And Ebu (European Broadcasting Union) Organizations. The Sampling Frequencies For This Standard Vary Depending On The Format Being Used; The Sampling Frequency For D1 And D2 Audio Tracks Is 48 Khz.

name: AES/EBU Digital Audio
desc: Specification titled “AES recommended practice for digital audio engineering, Serial transmission format for two channel linearity represented digital audio data”. AES/EBU digital audio standard that is the result of cooperation between the US based AES and the European based EBU.

name: AF
desc: See Adaptation Field.

name: AFC
desc: See Automatic Frequency Control.

name: AFC/Direct
desc: See Waveform Monitors.

name: AFI (Authority and Format Identifier)
desc: Part of network level address header.

name: a final look to a finished sequence, for example, by fine-tuning the color values to enhance the mood of a dramatic program. See also Source side.Program Side
desc: In color correction, the second of two available levels of color adjustment. Corrections made on the program side typically apply

name: Afl
desc: An Acronym For After Fade Listen, Which Is Another Way Of Saying Post-Fader Solo Function.

name: AFL (After Fade Listen)
desc: Used in mixing boards to override the normal monitoring path in order to monitor a specific signal at a predefined point in the mixer. Unlike PFL, the AFL signal definition is taken after the fader  of a channel or group buss such that the level of the fader will affect the level heard in the AFL monitor circuit. AFL is sometimes also taken after the pan pot which also allows the engineer to monitor the signal with the pan position as it is in the mix. AFL is a handy way to monitor a small group of related instruments by themselves with all of their eq, level, and pan information reproduced as it is in the overall mix. An AFL circuit that includes pan information is often called “solo” or “solo in place” depending upon who builds the mixer.

name: AFM (Audio Frequency Modulation)
desc: The most common form of audio recording found in most consumer and professional video recording decks, especially in VHS and 8 mm recorders. AFM audio is limited to dynamic range and frequency response, and can include stereo and multitrack audio.

name: AFNOR (Association Francaise de Normalisation)
desc: French standards body.

name: A-Frame Edit
desc: A video edit which starts on the first frame of the 5 video frame (4 film frame) sequence created when 24 frame film is transferred to 30 frame. The A-frame is the only frame in the sequence where a film frame is completely reproduced on one complete video frame. Here is the full sequence. (The letters correspond to the film frames.) A-frame = video fields 1&2, B-frame = video fields 1&2&1, C-frame = video fields 2&1,D-frame = video fields 2&1&2.

name: Aftertouch
desc: MIDI data sent when pressure is applied to a keyboard after the key has been struck, and while it is being held down or sustained.Aftertouch is often routed to control vibrato, volume, and other parameters.
