There are two types the most common is Channel Aftertouch which looks at the keys being held, and transmits only the highest aftertouch value among them. Less common is Polyphonic Aftertouch, which allows each key being held to transmit a separate, independent aftertouch value. While polyphonic aftertouch can be extremely expressive, it can also be difficult for the unskilled to control, and can result in the transmission a great deal of unnecessary MIDI data, eating bandwidth and slowing MIDI response time.

name: AFV
desc: See Audio Follow Video.

name: AFX (Animation Framework Extension)
desc: AFX is an integrated toolbox that uses existing MPEG-4 tools to create powerful synthetic MPEG-4 environments. This collection of interoperable tool categories (with each tool providing a functionality, such as an audiovisual stream) works togeth- er to produce a reusable architecture for interactive animated content.

name: AGC
desc: See Automatic Gain Control.

name: A-Guide
desc: A-Shaped Aluminum Members Fixed In Parallel Rows For The Purpose Of Guiding Arbors Or Clews. They Are Intended For Use On Counter Weighted Systems Employing Compensating Chains And In Zones Of High Seismic Activity. This Is A Clancy Product.

name: AI (Amplitude Imbalance)
desc: The purpose of the AI measurement is to assess the QAM distortions resulting from amplitude imbalance of I and Q signals.

name: AIF
desc: Audio Interchange File. An audio file format developed by Apple Computer to store high-quality sampled sound and musical instrument information. Popular for transferring between Mac and PC.

name: AIF (Audio Interchange File)
desc: An Audio File Format Developed By Apple Computer To Store High Quality Sampled Sound And Musical Instrument Information. The Aif Files Are A Popular Format For Transferring Between The Macintosh And The Pc.See Also: Au, Wav.

name: AIFF (Audio Interchange File Format)
desc: This is the format for both compressed and uncompressed audio data.

name: AIFF-C (Audio Interchange File Format-Condensed)
desc: A sampled- sound file format that allows for the storage of audio data. This format is primarily used as data interchange format but can be used as a storage format as well. OMF Interchange includes AIFF-C as a common interchange format for non-compressed audio data.

name: AIMS
desc: Alliance for IP Media Solutions. An industry alliance promoting open, interoperable standards for IP-based media networks. Key specifications include IPMX.

name: Air Blown Fiber (ABF)
desc: Optical fiber installed through special tube cables using pressurized air or nitrogen to blow fiber bundles through individual tubes within pre-installed tube cables.

name: Airborne Sound
desc: Sound That Arrives At The Point Of Interest, Such As One Side Of A Partition, By Propagation Through Air.

name: Airflow Resistance, R
desc: [Ml-4T-1] *(Pa·  S/M3) ---The Quotient Of The Air Pressure Difference Across A Specimen Divided By The Volume Velocity Of Airflow Through The Specimen. The Pressure Difference And The Volume Velocity May Be Either Steady Or Alternating.

name: AirPlay
desc: A proprietary protocol developed by Apple Inc. for streaming audio and video media between devices over a Wi-Fi connection.

name: Air Polish
desc: In fiber optics, the first step in polishing a connector using special fine grit film, after the fiber has been cleaved.

name: Air Tally
desc: The ability of a switcher console to indicate to an operator which video sources and keys are on air at any given time. Ampex switch- ers have “true” air tally in that they sense actual presence of sources.

name: Aisle
desc: A passage through seating areas in an auditorium.

name: AIT (Application Information Table)
desc: Provides information about the activation state of service bound applications.

name: A-Lamp
desc: Common Incandescent “Light Bulb” Used Throughout Most Homes In North America.  An A-Lamp Can Have A Clear Glass Bulb Or A White Coat- Ing Or An Etched Frost On The Inside Of The Glass Bulb.

name: Alan Smithee film
desc: the pseudonym used by directors who refuse to put theirname on a film and want to disassociate themselves , usuallywhen they believe their control or vision has been co - opted bythe studio ( i.e. , the film could have been recut , mutilated andaltered against their wishes ) ; aka Alan Smithee Jr. , AllanSmithee , or Allen Smithee

name: A-Law
desc: A pulse code modulation (PCM) coding and companding standard that is used in Europe for digital voice communications.

name: ALC
desc: See Automatic Level Control.

name: ALC (Automatic Light Control)
desc: A part of the electronics of an automatic iris lens that has a function similar to backlight compensation in photography.

name: A - Level ( or A - List )
desc: usually refers to top - tier actors / actresses who are paidupwards of $ 20 million per feature film ; can also refer toproducers , directors and writers who can be guaranteed tohave a film made and released

name: Algorithm
desc: A Formula Or Set Of Steps Used To Simplify, Modify, Or Predict Data. Complex Algorithms Are Used To Selectively Reduce The High Digital Audio And Video Data Rates. These Algorithms Utilize Physiologists' Knowledge Of Hearing And Eyesight. For Example, We Can Resolve Fine Detail In A Still Scene, But Our Eye Cannot Resolve The Same Detail In A Moving Scene. Using Knowledge Of These Limitations, Algorithms Are Formulated To Selectively Reduce The Data Rate Without Affecting The Viewing Experience.See Also: Compression, Mpeg.

name: Alias, Aliasing
desc: Something other that what it appears to be. Stairsteps

name: Aliasing
desc: This Is A Type Of Distortion Caused During The Analog-To-Digital Conversion Process. If The Fre Quency Of The Analog Signal Exceeds One-Half The Sampling Rate, Spurious Signals And Harmon Ics Not Present On The Original Signal May Be Created (See Nyquist Theorem). Careful Design And Filtering Before The Sampling Stage Can Reduce This Aliasing To A Minimum.

name: Alignment
desc: Most commonly, Head Alignment, but also used to describe the process of adjusting a recorder’s Bias and Equalization for optimum results from a specific tape.

name: Alignment Jitter
desc: The variation in time of the significant instants (such as zero crossings) of a digital signal relative to a hypothetical clock recovered from the signal itself. This recovered clock will track in the  signal up to its upper clock recovery bandwidth, typically 1 kHz to 100 kHz. Measured alignment jitter includes those terms above this frequency.Alignment jitter shows signal-to-latch clock timing margin degradation. The allowed specification for SMPTE 292 is 0.2 unit intervals.

name: Align (Titan)
desc: An Avolites Titan function that copies attribute values from the first selected fixture to all other selected fixtures, quickly equalizing their parameters.

name: A Line Array
desc: Is A Loudspeaker System Made Up Of A Number Of Loud- Speaker Elements Joined In A Line To Create A Single Sound Source. The Loudspeakers Must Be Close Enough Together To Send Sound Waves Farther Than Individual Loudspeakers, And With A More Evenly Distributed Sound Output Pattern.

name: All Dielectric
desc: In fiber optics, denotes the presence of only dielectric (non-metal) elements.

name: allegory
desc: mostly a literary term , but taken in film terms to mean asuggestive resemblance or correspondence between a visibleevent or character in a film with other more significant orabstract levels of meaning outside of the film ; an extendedmetaphor

name: Allen & Heath Avantis
desc: Allen & Heath's high-end digital console below the dLive series. Features a large touchscreen surface, 96kHz processing, Dante and WAVES integration, and XCVI processing core.

name: Allen & Heath dLive
desc: Allen & Heath's flagship digital mixing system (S-class and C-class surfaces with DM-Rack). Features 96kHz processing, floating-point XCVI core, Dante, WAVES, and full network redundancy.

name: Allen & Heath GLD
desc: Allen & Heath's modular digital mixing system designed for flexible touring and installation. Features a control surface and audio rack connected via Gigabit Ethernet.

name: Allen & Heath QU series
desc: Allen & Heath's compact digital console range (QU-16, QU-24, QU-32). Touchscreen-based operation with iPad remote control. Popular for small venues and installations.

name: Allen & Heath SQ series
desc: Allen & Heath's mid-range digital console range (SQ-5, SQ-6, SQ-7). Features 96kHz processing, built-in DEEP plug-in processing, Waves integration, and XCVI core DSP. Popular in touring, theatre, and houses of worship.

name: AllFade
desc: An Eos cue flag that causes all channels with non-zero intensity to fade to zero when the cue executes, regardless of what is tracked or recorded. Used to create complete blackouts that override tracking.

name: Allow Rate
desc: The percentage of users that allow camera access when they encounter an AR ad through an app or on the mobile web.

name: allusion
desc: a direct or indirect reference - through an image or throughdialogue - to the Bible , a classic , a person , a place , anexternal and / or real - life event , another film , or a well - knowncultural idea

name: Alpha
desc: See Alpha Channel and Alpha Mix.

name: Alpha channel
desc: A Relative Transparency Value. Alpha Values Facilitate The Layering Of Media Object On Top Of Each Other. In A Four Digit Digital Sampling Structure (4:2:2:4) The Alpha Channel Is Represented By The Last Digit.

name: Alpha Compositing
desc: The process of combining an image with a background using the alpha channel to create partial transparency and composite multiple elements into a single image.

name: Alpha Key
desc: Also known as a Linear Key. Uses two outputs from a device — a cut (greyscale) and a fill — to layer video elements on a switcher.

name: Alpha Keying
desc: The process of keying a video signal over another using transparency information from an alpha channel.

name: Alpha Map
desc: The representation of the transparency parameters associat- ed to a texture map.

name: Alpha Mix
desc: This is a way of combining two images. How the mixing is performed is provided by the alpha channel. The little box that appears over the left-hand shoulder of a news anchor is put there by an alpha mixer. Wherever the pixels of the little box appear in the frame buffer, an alpha number of “1” is put in the alpha channel. Wherever they don’t appear, an alpha number of “0” is placed. When the alpha mixer sees a “1” coming from the alpha channel, it displays the little box. Whenever it sees a “0”, it displays the news anchor. Of course, it doesn’t matter if a “1” or a “0” is used, but you get the point.

name: Alphanumeric
desc: Set of all alphabetic and numeric characters.

name: Alpha Plane
desc: Image component providing transparency information.

name: Alternate
desc: An actor who shares a specific role with another, alternating performances; an understudy scheduled to perform a set number of performances each week, typically matinees.

name: alternate ending
desc: the shooting ( or re - shooting ) of a film's ending for its theatrical release , usually enforced by the studio for any number ofreasons ( because of test audience preview results ,controversial or unpopular subject matter , to provide a ' happy 'ending , etc. ) . See also director's cut

name: Alternate Mode
desc: A USB-C operation type that dedicates some buses for direct device-to-host transmission of alternate data protocols such as video (DisplayPort, HDMI, or MHL). Also referred to as USB Type-C Alternate Mode.

name: Alternate Reality Game (ARG)
desc: A Type Of Game That Consists Of Storytelling And Puzzle-Solving Elements Delivered Over Heterogeneous Media, Which May Include Social Media, Phone Calls, And Live Performances. Because Args Attempt To Insert Themselves Into Reality, They Are Sometimes Compared To Virtual  Reality  And Other Forms Of Immersive  Technology.

name: Alternating Current (AC)
desc: An Electric Current That Repeatedly Reverses Direction.

name: Alternative content
desc: Non-cinema program material such as concerts, plays, sporting events, or corporate training presented in theaters.

name: Altspace VR
desc: A Multipurpose, Free-To-Use Virtual  Reality  Application That Allows Users To Interact With Each Other Within Custom Virtual “Worlds.” The Platform Can Be Used To Host Live Virtual Events, Which May Include Classes, Conferences, And Meetups.

name: ALU
desc: See Arithmetic and Logic Unit.

name: AM
desc: A form of modulation where the level of the baseband information affects the level of the carrier. See Amplitude Modulation.

name: A-MAC
desc: A MAC (Multiplexed Analog Component) with audio and data frequency multiplexed before modulation. See also MAC.

name: ambiance
desc: the feeling or mood of a particular scene or setting

name: Ambience
desc: The Acoustic Characteristics Of A Space With Regard To Reverberation. A Room With A Lot Of Reverb Is Said To Be "Live"

name: Ambient
desc: Natural, or surrounding light in a clip.

name: Ambient  Light
desc: Naturally Occurring Illumination Arising From Outside The Apparatus.

name: Ambient Lighting
desc: Lighting That Is Designed To Provide A Substantially  Uniform Light Level Throughout An Area, Exclusive Of Any Provision For Special Local Requirements.

name: Ambient light rejection
desc: The ability of a screen to reflect ambient light away from the viewer's line of sight.

name: Ambient Microphone
desc: A Microphone That Is Not Pointed At A Specific Audio Source. Ambient Microphones Are Positioned To Pick Up The Ambient Sound In A Room Or Venue.

name: Ambient Noise
desc: The Composite Of Airborne Sound From Many Sources Near And Far Associated With A Given Environment. No Particular Sound Is Singled Out For Interest.

name: Ambient Sound
desc: Ambient Sound (Also Known As “Room Tone” Or “Natural Sound”) ­ The Natural Sounds Recorded On Location. For Example, The Sound Of A Fan In A Room Or Wind.

name: Ambient Sound Level
desc: Any environmental or background sound that exists before a new sound source is added. Must be considered when designing a sound support system.

name: ambiguity
desc: a situation , story - line , scene , or character , etc. in which thereare apparent contradictions ; an event ( and its outcome ) isdeliberately left unclear , and there may exist more than onemeaning or interpretation ; can be either intentional orunintentional , to deliberately provoke imaginative thinking orconfusion

name: American Shot
desc: A camera shot framing a subject from mid-thigh up. Also called Cowboy Shot or Plan Américain.

name: American Television and Communications
desc: See ATC.

name: A-Mode
desc: A linear method of assembling edited footage. In A-mode, the editing system performs edits in the order in which they will appear on the master, stopping whenever the edit decision list (EDL) calls for a tape that is not presently in the deck. See also B-Mode, C-Mode, D-Mode, E-Mode, Source Mode.

name: A-Mode Edit
desc: An editing method where the footage is assembled in the final scene order. Scene 1, scene 2, etc.

name: Amp
desc: An Abridged Version Of Ampere.

name: Amperage (A)
desc: The Measure Of Electrical Current In Amperes.

name: Ampere
desc: Often Abbreviated As Amp, The Standardized Unit Of Electrical Current.

name: Ampere(A)
desc: A Unit Of Measurement For Electrical Current.

name: Amphitheatre
desc: An oval or round building with tiers of seats arranged around a central open area used for performance. The original form of the Greek theatre.

name: Amplification Factor (Q)
desc: The Amount Of Mechanical Gain Of A Structure When Excited At A Resonant Frequency. The Ratio Of The Amplitude Of The Steady State Solution (Amplitude At Resonance) To The Static Deflection For The Same Force F. The Amplification Factor Is A Function Of The System Damping. For A Damping Ratio =0 (No Damping) The Amplification Factor Is Infinite, For =1 (Critically Damped) There Is No Amplification.

name: Amplifier
desc: An Electronic Device Used To Increase The Amplitude Of The Audio Signal Fed Into It.

name: Amplifier classifications
desc: Audio amplifiers are described by class. Four primary classes: A, B, A/B, and D.

name: Amplitude
desc: Volume, Or Electronic Level Of An Audio Signal.

name: Amplitude Modulation (AM)
desc: a) The process used for some radio (AM broadcast, in North American audio service broadcast over 535 kHz-1705 kHz) and television video transmission. A low frequency (program) signal modulates (changes) the amplitude of a high frequency RF carrier signal (causing it to deviate from its nominal base amplitude). The original pro- gram signal is recovered (demodulated) at the receiver. This system is extensively used in broadcast radio transmission because it is less prone to signal interference and retains most of the original signal quality. In video, FM is used in order to record high quality signals on videotape. b) The process by which the amplitude of a high-frequency carrier is varied in proportion to the signal of interest. In the PAL television system, AM is  used to encode the color information and to transmit the picture. Several different forms of AM are differentiated by various methods of sideband fil- tering and carrier suppression. Double sideband suppressed carrier is used to encode the PAL color information, while the signal is transmitted with a large-carrier vestigial sideband scheme.

name: Amplitude Non-Uniformity
desc: A term used in connection with magnetic tape testing and refers to the reproduced peak-to-peak voltage and its variation from what was recorded.

name: Amplitude Versus Frequency Response
desc: Refer to the Frequency Response discussion.

name: AM-VSB (Amplitude Modulation with Vestigial Sideband)
desc: The form of modulation used in broadcast and cable television transmission. It is more efficient than dual-sideband amplitude modulation and is easier to implement than single-sideband amplitude modulation.

name: AMWA
desc: Advanced Media Workflow Association. A non-profit developing open specifications for media workflow including the NMOS family of APIs for networked media devices.

name: Amx
desc: Abbreviation For Analog Multiplex.

name: AMX Enova
desc: AMX's family of AV matrix switchers and signal processors supporting HDMI, HDBaseT, and other formats, integrated with NetLinx control for fully managed AV distribution.

name: anachronism
desc: an element or artifact in a film that belongs to another time orplace ; often anachronistic elements are called film flubs

name: Anaerobic
desc: In fiber optics, a method of bonding optical fibers via a non-heat, intrinsic chemical reaction. An anaerobic adhesive does not require air to cure.

name: Analog
desc: An Electrical Signal Whose Frequency And Level Vary Continuously In Direct Relationship To The Original Electrical Or Acoustical Signal.

name: Analog: 1
desc: An Adjective Describing Any Signal That Varies Continuously As Opposed To A Digital Signal, Which Contains Discrete Levels. 2. A System Or Device That Operates Primarily On Analog Signals.

name: Analog Components
desc: Video signals in which a continuously variable voltage or current (rather than a set of digital numbers) represents a pixel.

name: Analog control
desc: A method using continuously varying voltage levels to provide control of equipment.

name: Analog Interface
desc: An interface between a display controller and a display in which pixel colors are determined by the voltage levels on three output lines (RGB). Theoretically, an unlimited number of colors can be supported by this method (24 bits per pixel allows 16,777,216 colors).The voltage level on any line varies between zero volts (for black) to about 700 millivolts (for maximum brightness).

name: Analog mixer
desc: A mixing console using analog circuitry and signal paths throughout. All signal processing (EQ, dynamics, effects) is performed using physical electronic components. Known for warm sound character and tactile control.

name: Analog Monitor
desc: A video monitor which accepts analog signals. Several types of inputs are accepted by analog monitors composite video, RGB & sync, Y/C, YUV and any combination of these formats. The signals transmit- ted to an analog monitor are usually between 0 and 1 V and use 75 ohm coaxial cables.

name: Analog Multiplex (Amx)
desc: A System That Simultaneously Transmits More Than One Analog Signal.

name: Analog Recording
desc: The common form of magnetic recording where the recorded waveform signal maintains the shape of the original waveform signal.

name: Analog Signal
desc: A Continuous Communication Signal Where The Amplitude Or Frequency Of The Voltage And/Or Current Takes Any Value Within A Range Of Values.

name: Analog Sunset
desc: The trend of digital video displacing analog. In a legal sense, AACS restrictions requiring Blu-ray players after 2010 to limit analog output to SD, and after 2013 to output no analog video when playing protected content.

name: Analog Video
desc: a) A video signal represented by a smooth and infinite number of video levels. b) A video signal made of a continuous electrical signal. A television and VCR can be analog video devices. To be stored and manipulated on a computer, analog video must be converted to digital video.

name: Analysis-By-Synthesis Coding
desc: A method of coding in which the analysis procedure (encoder) has embedded in it the synthesis procedure (decoder). The reconstructed and original signals are compared and the difference is minimized. Used in many recent speech coding standards.

name: Analysis Filterbank
desc: Filterbank that transforms a broadband signal into a set of subsampled sub-band samples. An audio encoder function.

name: Anamorphic
desc: a) Unequally scaled in vertical and horizontal dimensions. Applies to lenses used for widescreen movies. b) Distortion in viewing of images or geometry related to the difference between computer monitor screen aspect ratio (in which pixels are square) and broadcast, projected or frame aspect ratio (in which image pixels are wider than they are high).

name: Anamorphic DVD
desc: A DVD with a horizontally squeezed widescreen image designed for optimal display on 16:9 widescreen displays or video scalers with an anamorphic squeeze mode.

name: Anamorphic Squeeze
desc: A change in picture geometry to compress one direction (usually horizontal) more than the other. Anamorphic squeeze lenses made CinemaScope possible. Occasionally, when widescreen movies are transferred to video, an anamorphic squeeze will be used (usually only in credits) to allow the smaller aspect ratio of television to accommo- date the larger movie aspect ratio. Some ATV proponents have suggested a gentle anamorphic squeeze as a technique to assist in aspect ratio accommodation.

name: Anamorphic Video
desc: Found on a large number of DVDs, anamorphic video squeezes a 1.78

name: Anchorage
desc: For recording tape, the degree to which the magnetic tape oxide coating adheres to the base film.

name: Anchor Frame
desc: A video frame that is used for prediction. I-frames and P-frames are generally used as anchor frames, but B-frames are never anchor frames.

name: Anchor Point
desc: A bit stream location that serves as a random access point. MPEG I-frames are the most common anchor points.

name: ancillary rights
desc: contractual agreement in which a percentage of the profits arereceived and derived from the sale of action figures , posters ,CDs , books , T - shirts , etc.

name: Ancillary Timecode (ATC)
desc: BT.1366 defines how to transfer VITC and LTC as ancillary data in digital component interfaces.

name: Anechoic
desc: Without Echo.

name: Anechoic Chamber
desc: A Room Designed To Suppress Internal Sound Reflections. Used For Acoustical Measurements.

name: Angel
desc: An investor in a theatrical production. An 'angel' provides financial backing for a show in exchange for a share of profits.

name: Angle
desc: An angle is a scene recorded from different viewpoints. Each angle is equal in time length and an Angle Block may contain up to nine angles.

name: Angled Physical Contact (APC)
desc: A singlemode fiber technique where the fiber endface is cut and polished at an 8-degree angle to minimize return loss. APC connectors are green and not compatible with UPC/SPC/PC connectors.

name: Angle Menu
desc: Menu used to select the Angle number.

name: Angle of Coverage
desc: The angular measurement defining the extents of a scene visible through a lens.

name: Angle of Incidence
desc: The angle between a ray incident on a surface and the perpendicular normal at the point of incidence.

name: Angle of View
desc: The angular measurement defining the extents of a scene visible through a lens and sensor pair. Also called AOV.

name: Anhysteresis
desc: The process whereby a material is magnetized by applying a unidirectional field upon which is superimposed an alternating field of gradually decreasing amplitude. One form of this process is analogous to the recoding process using AC Bias.

name: Animatic
desc: Limited animation consisting of art work shot and edited to serve as a videotape storyboard. Commonly used for test commercials.

name: Animation
desc: a) Animation is the process of fooling the human eye into perceiving a moving object by presenting the eye with a rapid succession of still pictures. Each still is called a frame. On the Cubicomp, animation consists of moving objects which, in themselves stay unchanged. b) The recording of a sequence of still artwork or objects in a way that makes them appear to move on film or video. 24 fps is considered the appropriate speed for animation.

name: animation ( and animator ,animated films )
desc: a form or process of filmmaking in which inanimate , staticobjects or individual drawings ( hand - drawn or CGI ) are filmed" frame by frame " or one frame at a time ( opposed to beingshot " live " ) , each one differing slightly from the previousframe , to create the illusion of motion in a sequence , asopposed to filming naturally - occurring action or live objects ata regular frame rate . Often used as a synonymfor cartoons ( or toons for short ) , although animation includesother media such as claymation , computer animation ; seealso CGI , claymation , stop - motion , time lapse .

name: Animation Curve
desc: A curve depicting the interpolation between the various keyframes.

name: Animation Path
desc: The motion of an object as it flies through space is called its animation or motion path.

name: Animations
desc: Motion image sequences produced synthetically on video processing or computing systems.

name: Animatronic
desc: Originally Audio-Animatronic (Disney trademark). Describes a robotic figure able to perform pre-programmed functions, typically synchronized with a pre-recorded soundtrack. Used in theme parks, film sets, and large-scale theatre.

name: anime
desc: a distinctive style of animated film that has its roots inJapanese comic books ( known as manga ) , yet covers a widerange of genres , such as romance , action / adventure , drama ,gothic , historical , horror , mystery , erotica ( hentai ) , children'sstories , although most notably sci - fi and fantasy themes ;originally called ' Japanimation ' but this term is not usedanymore ; anime is found in a wide variety of storylines andsettings , but usually recognizable and often characterized byheavily - stylized backgrounds , colorful images and graphics ,highly exaggerated facial expressions with limited facialmovement , simulation of motion through varying thebackground behind a static character or other foreground element , and frequently , big - headed characters with child - like ,large eyes

name: Anisotropy
desc: Directional dependence of magnetic properties, leading to the existence of easy or preferred directions of magnetization. Anisotropy of a particle may be related to its shape, to its crystalline structure or to the existence of strains within it. Shape anisotropy is the dominant form in acicular particles.

name: Annotation
desc: A hardware-based processor feature allowing a presenter to draw, point, or type over live presentations using a touch display, graphics tablet, or keyboard and mouse.

name: Annual Energy Savings
desc: A  Term Used In The Economics Chapter To Refer To The Difference Per Year In Kwh Between The Energy Used For Lighting Design 1 And Design 2.

name: Annual Energy Use
desc: A  Term Used In The Economics Chapter To Refer To The Energy Used Per Year In Kwh By A Lighting System.

name: Annual Lamp Replacement  Costs
desc: A Term Used In The Economics Chapter To Refer To The Cost Per Year Of Replacement  Lamps, Excluding Labor.

name: Annual Operating Cost
desc: A Term Used In The Economics Chapter To Refer To The Cost Per Year Of Electricity  And Replacement Lamps.

name: Annual Operating Cost Savings
desc: A Term Used In The Economics Chapter To Refer To The Difference Between The Annual Operating Cost Of Design 1 And That Of Design 2.

name: ANRS, Super ANRS
desc: A noise reduction system used to JVC. ANRS operates on principles similar to those used by the Dolby system. Therefore, there is a degree of compatibility between recordings made with either system.

name: Ansi
desc: Abbreviation For American National Standards Institute.

name: ANSI 4.40
desc: See AES/EBU Digital Audio.

name: ANSI (American National Standards Institute)
desc: ANSI is a voluntary and privately funded business standards group in the USA. ANSI seeks  to promote and to facilitate consensus standards nationally, and is internationally engaged as the sole US member of the ISO. The members of ANSI consist of about 1,300 American and international companies, 30 government agencies and some 250 organizations of trade, labor, professionals, consumers, etc.

name: Ansi Code
desc: A Three Letter System That Has Been Devised To Describe Lamps Of Different Manufacture But The Same Application. The Letters Have No Relationship To Lamp Description, But The Same Letters Always Designate The Same Type Of Lamp. Some Of The Application Parameters They Define Are Wattage, Base Type, Envelope Size, And Light Center Length.

name: ANSI lumen
desc: The standardized unit of projector brightness as defined by ANSI/PIMA 7228. Higher lumen ratings indicate brighter projectors. A 30% difference in ANSI lumen ratings is approximately the human perception threshold.

name: Answer
desc: Smoothing, removing, or reducing jagged edges along the lines and curves in test, images, or geometry.

name: Answer Print
desc: The first print combining picture and sound submitted by the laboratory for the customers’ approval.

name: antagonist
desc: the main character , person , group , society , nature , force ,spirit world , bad guy , or villain of a film or script who is inadversarial conflict with the film's hero , lead characterorprotagonist ; also sometimes termed the heavy .

name: anthology film
desc: a multi - part or multi - segmented film with a collection or seriesof various tales or short stories sometimes linked together bysome theme or by a ' wrap - around ' tale ; often the stories aredirected by different directors or scripted by variousscreenwriters , and are in the horror film genre ; also known asan episode film or omnibus film ; this term may also refer to afull - length , compilation - documentary film of excerptedsegments or clips from other films ( i.e. , That's Entertainment( 1974 ) ) .

name: anthropomorphism
desc: the tendency in animated films to give creatures or objectshuman qualities , abilities , and characteristics .

name: Anti-Aliased Fonts
desc: Computer generated fonts that have been digitally rounded for smooth edges.

name: Anti-Alias Filter
desc: A filter (typically a lowpass filter) used to bandwidth- limit the signal to less than half the sampling rate before sampling.

name: Anti-aliasing
desc: The Smoothing And Removing Of Aliasing Effects By Filtering And Other Techniques. Most, But Not All, Dves And Character Generators Contain Anti-Aliasing Facilities.

name: anti - climax
desc: anything in a film , usually following the film's high point ,zenith , apex , crescendo , or climax , in which there is anunsatisfying and disappointing let - down of emotion , or what isexpected doesn't occur .

name: anti - hero
desc: the principal protagonist of a film who lacks the attributes orcharacteristics of a typical hero archetype , but with whom theaudience identifies . The character is often confused orconflicted with ambiguous morals , or character defects andeccentricities , and lacks courage , honesty , or grace . The antihero can be tough yet sympathetic , or display vulnerable andweak traits . Specifically , the anti - hero often functions outsidethe mainstream and challenges it .

name: AP
desc: See Active Picture.

name: Aperture
desc: The Space Through Which Light Enters A Camera That Affects Image Brightness And Depth Of ﬁeld

name: Aperture Delay
desc: In ADCs, aperture delay is the time from an edge of the input clock of the ADC until the time the part actually takes the sample.The smaller this number, the better.

name: Aperture grill
desc: A Sony Trinitron CRT monitor feature controlling the number of electrons hitting the phosphor coating on the screen.

name: Aperture Jitter
desc: The uncertainty in the aperture delay. This means the aperture delay time changes a little bit over time, and that little bit of change is the aperture jitter.

name: APL (Average Picture Level)
desc: The average signal level (with respect to blanking) during active picture time, expressed as a percentage of the difference between the blanking and reference white levels.

name: Apostilb
desc: A photometric unit for measuring luminance where, instead of candelas, lumens are used to measure the luminous flux of a source.

name: Application
desc: An application runs in a module, communicating with the host, and provides facilities to the user over and above those provided directly by the host. An application may process the transport stream.

name: Application Format
desc: A specification for storing information in a particu- lar way to enable a particular use.

name: Application Window
desc: The workspace (window) available to an applica- tion. The size can be adjusted by the user and limited only by the size of the monitor’s display.

name: Apron
desc: In A Traditional Theatre, The Part Of The Stage Which Projects In Front Of The Curtain.In Many Theatres This Can Be Extended, Sometimes By Building Out Over The Pit (Qv).

name: APS (Advanced Photo System)
desc: A new photographic system conceived by Kodak and developed jointly with Canon, Fuji, Minolta, and Nikon. The APS was launched in April 1996. APS also represents the file format used to store data on the new film’s magnetic coating.

name: APS-C
desc: A camera sensor format with an active surface area approximately 25×16.7mm, similar to an Advanced Photo System Classic film frame.

name: Apt-X100
desc: The apt-X100 is a proprietary audio compression algorithm from APT, Ltd., which features an adaptive differential PCM (ADPCM) algorithm in four sub-bands. The algorithm provides a fixed 4:1 compres- sion with low delay and bandwidths ranging from 7.5 kHz to 22.5 kHz and output bit rates from 64 to 384 kbit/s, depending on the sampling rate.

name: APU (Audio Presentation Unit 13818-1)
desc: A 13818-1 audio frame.

name: AR
desc: AR Is An Overlay Of Computer Generated Content On The Real World. For Example, Google Translate Has An Ar Feature That Uses Your Camera To Translate Signs, Menus, And Similar Items In Real-Time From One Language To Another.

name: Aramid Yarn
desc: A woven strength member (Kevlar is a common brand) incorporated into fiber optic cable providing tensile strength and protection.

name: Arbor
desc: A Carriage Or Rack That Contains Weights, Usually Flame Cut Steel Or Cast Iron, In Sufficient Quantity To Balance A Load.

name: Arbor Pit
desc: An Area Located Below An Opening In The Stage Floor That Permits Greater Travel For Counter Weight Arbors And Pipe Battens.

name: Arbor Release
desc: A Part Of The Fire Curtain Rigging That Permits The Fire Safety Curtain Arbor To Move And The Fire Safety Curtain To Close. The Release Is Usually Controlled By The Fire Line System.

name: Arc
desc: The Light Caused By An Electrical Discharge Between Two Electrodes In A Gas Such As Xenon, Argon, Or Air. The First Usable Arc As A Practical Light Source Was Developed In 1809 By Sir Humphrey Davy.

name: archetype
desc: a character , place , or thing , that is repeatedly presented infilms with a particular style or characterization ; an archetypeusually applies to a specific genre or type classification .

name: Architectural Lines
desc: Design Indications Within A Set Plan Or Digital Asset Which Indicate Perspective . Whencombined With Parallax On An Led Volume , Helps To Create The Illusion Of Depth .

name: Architectural  Luminaire
desc: A Luminaire That Is Integrated Into The Structure Of The Room.Architectural Luminaires Are Mounted Horizontally  On A Wall Or Ceiling With A Shield To Hide The Lamp(S) From View. See Also Cove, Soffit, And Valance Luminaires.

name: Architecture
desc: a) Logical structure of a computer system. b) In digital video, architecture (also known as format) refers to the structure of the software responsible for creating, storing and displaying video content. An architecture may include such things as compression support, system extensions and browser plug-ins. Different multimedia architectures offer different features and compression options and store video data in different file formats. QuickTime, RealVideo and MPEG are examples of video archi- tectures (though MPEG is also a type of compression).

name: Archive
desc: Off-Line Storage Of Video/Audio Onto Backup Tapes, Floppy Disks, Optical Disks, Etc.

name: Arc Minutes
desc: A unit of angular measurement describing how much of a viewer's vision is occupied by an object. An arc minute equals 1/60th of a degree.

name: arc shot
desc: a shot in which the subject ( s ) is photographed by anencircling or moving camera .

name: Arena
desc: A Performance Space With Seating All Round The Performers. Examples Include Theaters, Basketball Courts, And Indoor Rodeos.

name: Arena (theatre)
desc: A stage configuration in which the audience is seated on two or more sides of the acting area. See also 'In the round'.

name: AR Engagement
desc: When a user interacts with the AR experience in some manner (i.e. clicking an object, swiping up for more information, etc.).

name: AR Head Mounted Display (HMD)
desc: A device with clear glasses or goggles that attaches to a user’s head and shows virtual images projected on, or in, the real world (i.e. Google Glass).

name: ARIB (Association of Radio Industries and Businesses)
desc: ARIB con- ducts studies and R&D, provides consultation services for radio spectrum coordination, cooperates with other organizations around the world and provides frequency change support services for the smooth introduction of digital terrestrial television broadcasting.

name: Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)
desc: One of three essential components of a microprocessor. The other two are the registers and the control block. The ALU performs various forms of addition, subtraction, and logic opera- tions, such as ANDing the contents of two registers or masking the con- tents of a register.

name: Arithmetic Coding
desc: Perhaps the major drawback to each of the Huffman encoding techniques is their poor performance when processing texts where one symbol has a probability of occurrence approaching unity.

name: Arithmetic Mean Sound Pressure Level
desc: Of Several Related Sound Pressure Levels Measured At Different Positions Or Sound Pressure Levels Measured At Different Positions Or Different Times, Or Both, In A Specified Frequency Band, The Sum Of The Sound Pressure Levels Divided By The Number Of Levels.

name: Armored Cable
desc: Cable protected with metal sheathing or rods against adverse outdoor factors such as rodent attack.

name: A-Roll
desc: A method of conforming that requires the compositing of all multilayer effects into a single layer (including laboratory-standard dissolves and fades) before assembly. Also called Single-Strand Editing.Although the entropy associated with such symbols is extremely low, each symbol must still be encoded as a discrete value. Arithmetic coding removes this restriction by representing messages as intervals of the real numbers between 0 and 1. Initially, the range of values for coding a text is the entire interval (0, 1). As encoding proceeds, this range narrows while the number of bits required to represent it expands. Frequently occurring characters reduce the range less than characters occurring infrequently, and thus add fewer bits to the length of an encoded message.

name: ARP
desc: Address Resolution Protocol. A network protocol that maps 32-bit IP addresses to 48-bit MAC addresses on a local network. Devices broadcast ARP requests to discover the MAC address associated with a known IP address.

name: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)
desc: A TCP/IP protocol used to obtain a node’s physical address. A client station broadcasts an ARP request onto the network with the IP address of the target node it wishes to communi- cate with, and the node with that address responds by sending back its physical address so that packets can be transmitted. ARP returns the layer 2 address for a layer 3 address. Since an ARP gets the message to the target machine, one might wonder why bother with IP addresses in the first place. The reason is that ARP requests are broadcast onto the network, requiring every station in the subnet to process the request.

name: ARQ
desc: See Application Programming Interface.

name: Array
desc: A Loudspeaker System Made Up Of A Number Of Loudspeaker Elements Joined Together.

name: Array Processor
desc: A compute engine that efficiently performs operations on large amounts of data with a regular structure (array).

name: ArraySync
desc: A Christie content management system enabling automatic color and brightness management across multiple tiled screens.

name: arret
desc: French word meaning ' halt ' or ' stop ' ; refers to the in - cameratrick technique of stopping the camera , then removing orinserting an object , then restarting the camera to have anobject magically disappear or appear ; one of the earliesttechniques of silent film

name: ARS
desc: See Automatic Route Selection.

name: AR Session Time
desc: The amount of time someone was in the AR experience with their camera open

name: art director
desc: refers to the individual responsible for the design , look , andfeel of a film's set , including the number and type of props ,furniture , windows , floors , ceilings dressings , and all other setmaterials ; a member of the film's art department ( responsiblefor set construction , interior design , and prop placement ) .

name: arthouse
desc: a motion picture theater that shows foreign or nonmainstream independent films , often considered high - brow or' art ' films .

name: art - house film
desc: films , often low budget or ' art ' films , that are acknowledged ashaving artistic merit or aesthetic pretensions , and are shownin an arthouse theatre ; films shown usually include foreignlanguage films , independent films , non - mainstream( sometimes anti - Hollywood ) films , shorts , documentaries ,explicitly - erotic films , and other under - appreciated cinema oflow mass appeal ; began to appear in the 1950s and provideda distinct contrast to commercial films .

name: Articulation
desc: A Quantitative Measure Of The Intelligibility Of Speech

name: Artifacts
desc:
a) Artifacts can range from noise and snow, to spots. Anything that is visually wrong with the picture is an artifact. Artifacts however do not include picture errors caused by improperly adjusted displays. Artifacts are visual errors caused by the signal being sent to the display. b) A defect or distortion of the image, introduced along the sequence from origination and image capture to final display. Artifacts may arise from the overload of channel capacity by excess signal bandwidth. Artifacts may also result from: sampling effects in temporal, spatial, or frequency domains; process- ing by the transfer functions; compromises and inadequacies in the system employed; cascading of minor defects; basically any other departure of the total system from “complete transparency”. c) Visible (or audible) 
consequences of various television processes. Artifacts are usually referred
to only when they are considered defects. Artifact elimination is often 
more apparent than quality increases such as resolution enhancement.
d) Interference or other unwanted “noise” in video such as flickering,
changes in color and macroblocking. Some artifacts, such as macroblock ing, can be remedied in video compression and some cannot. The quality of the finished product is, in large part, no better than the source material.
See also Filter Artifacts, Impairments, and NTSC Artifacts

name: Artificial Intelligence( AI )
desc: The Usage Of Computers And Algorithms To Mimic The Problem - Solving And Decision - Makingprocesses Of Humans . Artificial Intelligence And Machine Learning Techniques Can Be Used Toprocess Performance Capture Data And Apply It To A Digital Character With Markedly Differentanatomy .

name: Artificial   Reality
desc: Simulated Spaces Created From A Combination Of Computer And Video Systems.

name: Artificial Reverberation
desc: Reverberation Generated By Electrical Or Acoustical Means To Simulate That Of Concert Halls, Etc., Added To A Signal To Make It Sound More Lifelike.

name: Art-Net
desc: A royalty-free protocol developed by Artistic Licence for transmitting DMX512 data over Ethernet UDP/IP networks. Allows hundreds of DMX universes to be carried over a single network connection. Widely supported by lighting consoles, fixtures, and media servers.

name: Asa
desc: Acoustical Society Of America.

name: As Built Drawings (Final Drawings)
desc: Equipment Or Layout Drawings That Show Equipment As It Was Actually Built And Intended To Be Installed. They May Not Reflect Actual, As Installed, Conditions.

name: ASCII
desc: American Standard Code For Information Interchange. A Standard Code For Transmitting Data, Consisting Of 128 Letters, Numerals, Symbols, And Special Codes Each Of Which Is Represented By A Unique Binary Number.

name: ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange)
desc:
a) Character code used for representing information as binary data in most
computer systems. b) A standard code for transmitting data, consisting 
of 128 letters, numerals, symbols and special codes each of which is 
represented by a unique binary number.

name: ASF (Active Streaming Format)
desc: a) A Microsoft file format for digital video playback over the Internet, or on a standalone computer. Kind of a wrapper around any of a number of compression types, including MPEG. b) Part of a NetShow, a proprietary streaming media solution from Microsoft. Biggest competitor is Real Networks. While this ‘wrapper’ supports many standard formats, ASF files are themselves proprietary.

name: Ashrae
desc: American Society Of Heating, Refrigerating, And Air-Conditioning Engineers.

name: ASI (Asynchronous Serial Interface)
desc: Transmission standard defined by the digital video broadcast (DVB) used to connect video delivery equip- ment within a cable, satellite or terrestrial plant.

name: ASIC
desc: Application Specific Integrated Circuit. An Integrated Circuit Designed For Special Rather Than General Applications.

name: ASIC (Application Specific Integrated Circuit)
desc: An integrated circuit designed for special rather than general applications.

name: aside
desc: occurs when a character in a film breaks the ' fourth wall ' anddirectly addresses the audience with a comment .

name: ASM
desc: Assistant Stage Manager. The most junior member of the Stage Management team, responsible for sourcing and running properties (props) during a show, setting props, and carrying out pre-show checklists.

name: ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation 1)
desc: OSI language for describing data types independent of particular computer structures and representation techniques. Described by ISO International Standard 8824.

name: ASPEC (Adaptive Spectral Perceptual Entrophy Coding)
desc: An algorithm developed by Fraunhofer Institut, AT&T, Thomas Brandt, and the CNET. The ASPEC algorithm was later used for developing the MPEG audio Layer 3 specification.

name: Aspect ratio
desc: The Ratio Of Television Picture Width To Height. In Ntsc And Pal Video, The Present Standard Is 4:3. In Widescreen Video, It Is Typically 16:9, However, 14:9 Has Been Used As A Transition.

name: Aspect Ratio Accommodation
desc: Techniques by means of which something shot in one aspect ratio can be presented in another. The five currently used or proposed techniques are compared in the following table. It is also possible to combine techniques. Current ATV aspect ratio debates concentrate on the problems of presenting widescreen images to existing TV sets; the same problems (in an opposite direction) will occur when current aspect ratio images are presented on widescreen TV sets.  In movie theaters these problems are usually solved with movable drapes.

name: Asperities
desc: Small projecting imperfections on the surface of the tape costing that limit and cause variations in head-to-tape contact.

name: Aspherical Lens
desc: A lens that has an aspherical surface. It is harder and more expensive to manufacture, but it offers certain advantages over a normal spherical lens.

name: Assemble
desc: One of the two editing modes that are possible with video tapes. All tracks on the tape are added free of disturbances at the cutting point, but all tracks are newly written. The other editing method is known as Insert Edit.

name: Assembled Edit
desc: a) Electronic edit that replaces all previously recorded material with new audio and video and a new control track, starting at the edit point. Inserting a new control track allows for a constant speed reference throughout the entire tape. b) Adding material that has a differ- ent signal to the end of a pre-recorded section of a videotape. Adding an assemble edit to the middle of an existing segment causes an abrupt and undesirable change in the sync of the video signal. Contrast with Insert Edit.

name: Assembler Program
desc: Translates assembly language statements (mnemonics) into machine language.

name: assembly
desc: the first stage of editing , in which all the shots are arranged inscript order .

name: Assembly Language
desc: Machine-oriented language. A program is normally written as a series of statements using mnemonic symbols that suggest  the definition of the instruction. It is then translated into machine language by an assembler program.

name: Assert
desc: An Eos cue flag that forces all parameter values in a cue to play back as move instructions, overriding tracking and collecting any channels with unfinished fades from previous cues. Critical for ensuring correct blackout cues play at the intended time.

name: Asset
desc: In The Context Of Game Design, An Asset Is A Discrete Element That Can Be Used At Multiple Points In A Game Or Across Multiple Games. Types Of Discrete Elements Include 3D  Models, Graphics, Background, Special Effects, Or Sound Clips. Game Engines Like Unity  And Unreal  May Have ‘Asset Stores’ Or ‘Marketplaces’ Where User-Created Assets Are Hosted For Free Or Paid Download. See Also Sketchfab.

name: Asset Management
desc: Enterprise tools giving at-a-glance equipment status, event scheduling support, and usage reports for inventory management, maintenance scheduling, and future system planning.

name: Assign
desc: In Sound Mixers, Assign Means To Switch Or Route A Signal To A Particular Signal Path Or Com Bination Of Signal Paths

name: Assistant Director
desc: Assists The Director (Qv) By Taking Notes On All Moves And Other Decisions And Keeping Them Together In One Copy Of The Script (The Prompt Copy (Qv)). In Some Companies This Is Done By The Stage Manager (Qv), Because There Is No Assistant.

name: Assistant Stage Manager
desc: (Asm) Another Name For Stage Crew (Usually, In The Professional Theatre, Also An Understudy For One Of The Minor Roles Who Is, In Turn, Also Understudying A Major Role). The Lowest Rung On The Professional Theatre Ladder.

name: Assistive Agents
desc: Artificial Intelligence Algorithms Developed To Guide Users Through A Vr World, And To Coach The User On Available Choices Within The World.

name: ASTA
desc: Active Sync Termination Adapter. A VGA-style adapter providing active circuits to shape horizontal and vertical sync signals, eliminating jitter and intermittent tearing.

name: Astigmatism
desc: The uneven foreground and background blur that is in an image.

name: ASV (Audio Still Video)
desc: A still picture on a DVD-Audio disc.

name: Asymmetrical Digital Subscriber Line
desc:
Bellcore’s term for one-way 
T-1 to the home over the plain old, single twisted pair wiring already going
to homes. ADSL is designed to carry video to the home. ADSL, like ISDN,
uses adaptive digital filtering, which is a way of adjusting itself to over come noise and other problems on the line. According to Northern Telecom,
initial ADSL field trails and business cases have focused on ADSL’s poten tial for video on Demand service, in competition with cable pay-per-view
and neighborhood video rental stores. But ADSL offers a wide range of
other applications, including education and health care. Once telephone
companies are able to deliver megabits to the home, Northern Telecom
expects an explosion in potential applications including work-at-home
access to corporate LANs, interactive services such as home shopping and
home banking and even multi-party video gaming, interactive travelogues,
and remote medical diagnosis. Multimedia retrieval will also become 
possible, enabling the home user to browse through libraries of text, audio,
and image data – or simply subscribe to CD-quality music services. In 
the field of education, ADSL could make it possible to provide a low-cost
“scholar’s workstation” – little more than a keyboard, mouse and screen –
to every student, providing access to unlimited computer processing
resources from their home. For a more modern version of ADSL, see DMT,
which stands for Discrete Multi-Tone.

name: Asymmetric Compression
desc: Compression in which the encoding and decoding require different processing power (the encoding is normally more demanding).

name: Asynchronous
desc: Lacking Synchronization. In Video, A Signal Is Asynchronous When Its Timing Differs From That Of The System Reference Signal. A Foreign Video Signal Is Asynchronous Before It Is Treated By A Local Frame Synchronizer.

name: Asynchronous Data Streaming
desc: Streaming of only data without any timing requirements. See Asynchronous Data Streaming, Synchronous Data Streaming.

name: Asynchronous, Nonsynchronous
desc: Frequencies In A Vibration Spectrum That Exceed Shaft Turning Speed (Ts), But Are Not Integer Or Harmonic Multiples Of Ts. Also Commonly Referred To As Non-Synchronous.

name: Asynchronous Signals
desc: Data communication transmission of signals with no timing relationship between the signals. Stop and start bits may be used to avoid the need for timing clocks.

name: asynchronous ( sound )
desc: refers to audio - track sounds that are mismatched or out ofconjunction or unison with the images in the visual frame ( orscreen ) ; sometimes accidental , but sometimes intentional ;aka non - synchronized

name: Asynchronous Transfer Mode (ATM)
desc: a) A digital transmission system using packets of 53 bytes for transmission. ATM may be used for LANs and WANs. ATM is a switching/ transmission technique where data is transmitted in small, 53 byte fixed sized cells (5 byte header, 48 byte payload). The cells lend themselves both to the time-division-multiplexing characteristics of the transmission media, and the packet switching characteristics desired of data networks. At each switching node, the ATM header identifies a virtual path or virtual circuit that the cell contains data for, enabling the switch to forward the cell to the correct next-hop trunk. The virtual path is set up through the involved switches when two end- points wish to communicate. This type of switching can be implemented in hardware, almost essential when trunk speed range from 45 Mbps to 1 Gbps. The ATM Forum, a worldwide organization, aimed at promoting ATM within the industry and the end user community was formed in October 1991 and currently includes more than 500 companies representing all sectors of the communications and computer industries, as well as a number of government agencies, research organizations and users. b) A digital signal protocol for efficient transport of both constant-rate and bursty information in broadband digital networks.

name: ATAPI  (Advanced Technology Attachment Packet Interface)
desc: An interface between a computer and its internal peripherals such as DVD-ROM drives. ATAPI provides the command set for controlling devices connected via an IDE interface. ATAPI is part of the Enhanced IDE (E-IDE) interface, also known as ATA-2. ATAPI was extended for use in DVD-ROM drives by the SFF 8090 specification.

name: ATC
desc: See Ancillary Timecode.

name: ATC (Adaptive Transform Coding)
desc: A method used to encode voice transmissions using only 16 kpbs.

name: ATC (American Television and Communications)
desc: Time Inc.’s CATV multiple system operator (MSO), a co-proposer with HBO of C-HDTV and a supporter of ACTV.

name: ATEL (Advanced Television Evaluation Laboratory)
desc: World-unique facility for conducting subjective assessments of picture quality for advanced television, digital video and multimedia services delivered using a wide range of formats, from low resolution to high-definition television (HDTV) and three-dimensional television (3D-TV).

name: A-Time (Absolute Time)
desc: Elapsed time, referenced to the program start (00:00:00), on a DVD. A-time is measured in minutes, seconds and frames.

name: ATM
desc: Asynchronous Transfer Mode. A Data Transmission Scheme Using Self-Routing Packets Of 53 Bytes, 48 Of Which Are Available For User Data. Typically 25, 155, And 622 Mbps--The Latter Of Which Could Be Used To Carry Non-Compressed Itu-R 601 Video As A Data File.

name: ATM Cell
desc: An ATM packet of 53 bytes, 5 bytes for the header, 48 bytes payload.

name: ATM Forum
desc: An international body of technical representatives defining ATM as a delivery mechanism, including ATM-based transfer, switching and management.

name: atmosphere
desc: refers to any concrete or nebulous quality or feeling thatcontributes a dimensional tone to a film's action .

name: Atmospheric
desc: Fog , Smoke , Fire , And Other Physically - Based Visual Effects Rendered By A Real - Time Engine .

name: ATMS
desc: Advanced Traffic Management Systems. Systems integrating technology to improve safety and traffic flow on public roadways, reducing fuel consumption and environmental cost. Typically integrated into a Traffic Management Center (TMC).

name: A-to-D Converter
desc: a) A circuit that uses digital sampling to convert an analog signal into a digital representation of that signal. An ADC for digitizing video must be capable of sampling at 10 to 150 million samples per second (MSPS). b) Converts analog voltages and currents to the digital representation used by computer systems. This enables the computer to sense real-world signals.

name: ATRAC (Adaptive Transform Acoustic Coding)
desc: An algorithm that splits an audio signal into three non-uniform sub-bands.

name: ATR (Audiotape Recorder)
desc: A device for recoding and reproducing sound on magnetic recording tape.

name: ATRP (Advanced Television Research Program)
desc: ATRP was estab- lished at MIT in 1983 by a consortium of U.S. companies. The major objectives of the ATRP are: to develop the theoretical and empirical basis for the improvement of existing television systems, as well as the design  of future television systems; to educate students through television-related research and development and to motivate them to undertake careers in television-related industries; to facilitate continuing education of scientists and engineers already working in the industry; to establish a resource center to which problems and proposals can be brought for discussion and detailed study; to transfer the technology developed from this program to the industries.

name: ATSC
desc: See Advanced Television Systems Committee.

name: ATSC A/49
desc: Defines the ghost cancellation reference signal for NTSC.

name: ATSC A/52
desc: Defines the (Dolby Digital) audio compression for ATSC HDTV.

name: ATSC A/53, A/54
desc: Defines ATSC HDTV for the USA.

name: ATSC A/57
desc: Defines the program, episode, and version ID for ATSC HDTV.

name: ATSC A/63
desc: Defines the method for handling 25 and 50 Hz video for ATSC HDTV.

name: ATSC A/65
desc: Defines the program and system information protocol (PSIP) and ATSC HDTV.

name: ATSC A/70
desc: Defines the conditional access system for ATSC HDTV.

name: ATSC A/90
desc: Defines the data broadcast standard for ATSC HDTV

name: ATSC A/92
desc: Defines the data broadcast standard for ATSC HDTV

name: AT&T
desc: Consumer electronics manufacturer and long distance telephone, television, and data carrier. Its Bell Labs has worked on the development of ATV systems.

name: Attached
desc: A Physical Channel Of A Digital Picture Manipulator Is Attached To A Logical Channel Of A Controller If The Physical Channel Is Successfully Acquired By The Controller. A Physical Channel May Be Attached To Only One Logical Channel Of One Controller At A Time.

name: Attack
desc: The Beginning Of A Sound

name: Attack Time
desc: The Amount Of Time It Takes (Often Measured In Milliseconds) For A Dynamic Processor Such As A Compressor Or Limiter To Engage (Start Compressing Or Limiting A Signal) After The Audio Signal Has Crossed The Threshold.

name: ATTC (Advanced Television Test Center)
desc: Created by seven broadcast- ing organizations to test different broadcast ATV systems. See also Cable Labs.

name: ATT-C (AT&T Communications)
desc: The Long distance arm of AT&T.

name: Attenuate
desc: To Reduce The Level Of An Electrical Or Acoustical Signal.

name: Attenuate/Attenuation
desc: To reduce the amplitude (strength) or current of a signal.

name: Attenuation
desc: A decrease in the level of a signal is referred to as attenua- tion. In some cases this is unintentional, as in the attenuation caused by using wire for signal transmission. Attenuators (circuits which attenuate a signal) may also be used to lower the level of a signal in an audio system to prevent overload and distortion.

name: Attenuation (fiber optics)
desc: Loss of optical power as light passes through a fiber optic path due to absorption, scattering, or excessive bending. Usually expressed in dB/km.

name: Attenuator
desc: A circuit that provides reduction of the amplitude of an elec- trical signal without introducing appreciable phase or frequency distortion.

name: Attic Folder
desc: The folder containing backups of your files or bins. Every time you save or the system automatically saves your work, copies of your files or bins are placed in the attic folder, until the folder reaches the specified maximum. The attic folder copies have the file name exten- sion.bak and a number added to the file name. The number of backup files for one project can be changed (increased or decreased) in the Bin Settings dialog box.

name: Attribute Clip
desc: A mechanism that applications can use to store supple- mental information in a special track that is synchronized to the other track in a track group.

name: Attribute wheel
desc: The rotary encoders on a lighting console used to adjust fixture parameter values (pan, tilt, color, gobo, zoom) for selected fixtures. Also called parameter wheels or encoder wheels.

name: ATV
desc: Advanced Television. Digital Television, Including Standard, Enhanced And High-Definition Versions.

name: AU
desc: See Access Unit.

name: AU (also SND)
desc: Interchangeable Audio File Formats Used In The Sun Sparc Station, Nest And Silicon Graphics (Sgi) Computers. Essentially A Raw Audio Data Format Proceeded By An Identifying Header. The .Au File Is Cross-Platform Compatible.See Also: Aif, Wav.

name: Audible Frequency Range
desc: The Range Of Sound Frequencies Normally Heard By The Human Ear. The Audible Range Spans From 20Hz To 20,000Hz

name: audience
desc: refers to spectators , viewers , participants - those who serveas a measure of a film's success ; although usually audiencesare viewed in universal terms , they can also be segmented orcategorized ( e.g. , ' art - film ' audiences , ' chick film ' audiences ,etc. ) .

name: audio
desc: refers to the sound portion of a film .

name: Audio Amplifier
desc: Electronic Equipment That Increases The Level Of A Sound Signal Synonym: Amp, Amplifier, Power Amplifier

name: Audio Assistant
desc: Person Who Works Under The Direction Of The Audio Department Head Synonym A2

name: Audio Balanced Signals
desc: These are signals with two components, equal in amplitude but opposite in polarity. The impedance characteristics of the conductors are matched. Current practices designate these as non- inverted and inverted, + and or positive and return. Interconnect cables usually have three conductors. Two arranged as a twisted pair, carry the non-inverted and inverted. By employing a twisted pair of conductors for the signal leads, the loop area responsible for magnetic interference is minimized. The third conductor is a shield.

name: Audio Bandwidth
desc: The range of audio frequencies which directly influ- ence the fidelity of the audio. The higher the audio bandwidth, the better the audio fidelity. The highest practical frequency the human ear can normally hear is 20 kHz. An audio amplifier that processes all frequencies equally (flat response to 20 kHz) and a reasonably high signal to noise ratio, will accurately amplify the audio signal.

name: Audio Breakaway (ABKW)
desc: The ability to independently select audio sources regardless of which video source is selected, even though the audio is normally associated with a particular video (as opposed to follow).

name: audio bridge
desc: refers to an outgoing sound ( either dialogue or sound effects ) in one scene that continues over into a new image or shot - in this case , the soundtrack , not a visual image , connects the two shots or scenes ; aka lightning mix

name: Audio Buffer
desc: A decoder buffer for storage of compressed audio data.

name: Audio Channel Number
desc: These are consecutive numbers assigned to the Audio channel of the audio stream. They range from “0” to “7” in the description are of the video title set manager area. ACH0 and ACH1 are assigned to left channel and right channel respectively for two-channel stereo audio signals.

name: Audio Coding Mode
desc: In general this is often used to show an audio coding method such as linear PCM, AC-3 or MPEG audio, etc., but in some contexts it refers to the channel constitution in AC-3 tracks and the speaker layout.

name: Audio Control Packet
desc: Transmitted once per field in the second horizon- tal ancillary data space after the vertical interval switch point. It contains information on audio frame number, sampling frequency, active channels, and relative audio-to-video delay of each channel. Transmission of audio control packets is optional for 48 kHz synchronous operation and required for all other modes of operation.

name: Audio Delay
desc: See Lip Sync.

name: Audio Department Head
desc: Person In Charge Of The Audio Crew Synonym: A1

name: Audio Dub
desc: Process which allows for the replacement of an audio signals on a previously recorded tape without disturbing the video signal.

name: Audio Editing
desc: Portions of the audio material are combined and recorded onto the videotape. Examples include creating a sound track that includes signals such as background music, voice narration or sound effects.

name: Audio Effects Board
desc: Similar to a switcher, an audio effects board is the primary router and mixer for source audio, and for adjusting, mixing and filtering audio. Usually, a digital audio workstation is used to perform more complex audio work.

name: Audio follow
desc: When audio is tied to other signals such as video and switched together. The opposite of breakaway.

name: Audio-Follow-Video
desc: During video recording or editing, the video signal is usually accompanied by its associated audio signal. While editing video, it is sometimes necessary to separate the audio and video signals. Audio-follow-video mixers allow the audio to, or not to follow the video when switching video signals.

name: Audio Follow Video (AFV)
desc: Audio selections made simultaneously upon selection of associated video sources (as opposed to audio breakaway).

name: Audio frequency
desc: Frequencies within the range of human hearing, about 20 Hz to 20 kHz.

name: Audio Level Measurements
desc: Typically within audio measurements a dBm value is specified. This means that a reference power of 1 mW was used with a 600 W termination. Therefore using the equations 0 dBm is equivalent to a voltage of 0.775 V into a 600 W load. You may encounter several different types of dB measurements used within audio. The follow- ing list indicates the typically used equations. dBm = 10 logP1/.001W dBV = 20 logV2/1V rms dBu = 20 log V2/775mV rms dBSPL = 20 logP1/P2

name: Audio Matrix
desc: That portion of the switcher electronics used to switch audio sources. Usually this matrix is controlled by AFV selections on the primary matrix, ABKW selections on an aux audio bus, or by an external editor or computer control.

name: Audio Menu
desc: Menu used to select the audio stream.

name: Audio Mixer
desc: A component that combines more than one sound input for composite output.

name: Audio Mixer Console
desc: That Receives Audio Inputs For Selecting, Processing And Blending The Output To Other Audio Equipment Synonym: Audio Board, Audio Desk, Board, Console, Mixer, Mixing Board

name: Audio Mixing
desc: The blending of two or more audio signals to generate a combined signal which is often used for audio dub. During video process- ing, audio mixing may be used to insert narration or background music.

name: Audio Modulation
desc: A carrier is modified with audio information and is mixed with the video information for transmission.

name: Audio Modulation Decoders
desc: Converts sound carrier elements of the video waveform into left and right audio channels for stereo monitoring.

name: Audio Modulation Monitors
desc: Displays sound carrier elements of the video waveform.

name: Audio On ISDN
desc: Through use of the MPEG audio specification, the ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network) may be tuned into an audio transmis- sion media. Data compression techniques like MPEG Layer II allow a tailored mix of cost and quality, and are now thought of implicitly when talking audio on ISDN.

name: Audiophile
desc: A Person Who Is Interested In Sound Reproduction.

name: Audio Scrub
desc: See Scrubbing

name: Audio Sequence
desc: A series of audio frames with the same global parameters.

name: Audio Signals
desc: XLR connectors provide dual-channel audio signals. The left channel can be set to click as a means of easily distinguishing the left channel from the right channel in audio tests.

name: Audio Stream Number
desc: These are consecutive numbers assigned to the Audio streams for a Title in a VTS. These range from `0' to `7' in the order described in the video title set manager area. For menus the number of audio streams is limited to 0 or 1.

name: Audio Subcarrier
desc: A specific frequency that is modulated with audio data before being mixed with the video data and transmitted.

name: Audio Subframe
desc: There are 100 subframes of audio for every frame of video.

name: Audio summing amplifier
desc: A device converting two-channel stereo audio signals into balanced/unbalanced one-channel mono audio signals.

name: Audio Sweetening
desc: The mixing of sound effects, music and announcer audio tracks with the audio track of the edited master tape, usually during the mixing stages of a production. Also called Audio Post-Production for Video.

name: Audio Timecode
desc: Longitudinal timecode (LTC) recorded on an audio track.

name: Audiotonix
desc: An audio technology group owning multiple professional audio brands including Allen & Heath, DiGiCo, SSL (Live), Calrec, and Fourier Audio. Formed to consolidate leading live and studio audio console manufacturers.

name: Audio/Video Mixer
desc: A single electronic component that consists of an audio mixer and a video mixer, switcher, or special effects generator. Also called an A/V Mixer.

name: AudioVision
desc: A registered trademark of Avid Technology, Inc. A digital, nonlinear audio editing system that locks digital video in sync with audio for audio editing and sweetening.

name: Audio Visual Objects (AV Objects)
desc: An AV object is a representation of a real or virtual object that can be manifested aurally and/or visually. AV objects are generally hierarchical, in that they may be defined as compos- ites of other AV objects, which are called sub-objects. AV objects that are composites of sub-objects are called compound AV objects. All other AV objects are called primitive AV objects.

name: Audio Visual Scene (AV Scene)
desc: A set of media objects together with scene description information that defines their spatial and temporal attributes including behavior resulting from object and user interaction.

name: audition
desc: the process whereby an actor - performer seeks a role bypresenting to a director or casting director a prepared readingor by ' reading cold ' from the film script , or performing achoreographed dance ; after the initial audition , a performermay be called back for additional readings or run - throughs .

name: Auditorium
desc: A Hall Or Seating Area Within The Hall Where The Audience Views A Performance.

name: Auditory Area
desc: The Sensory Area Lying Between The Threshold Of Hearing And The Threshold Of Feeling Or Pain.

name: Auditory Masking
desc: Auditory masking is used in MPEG and Dolby Digital compression, and is coded based on the range of frequency that human ears can detect. A phenomenon that occurs when two sounds of similar frequencies occur at the same time. Because of auditory masking, the louder sound drowns out the softer sound and makes it inaudible to the human ear.

name: Augmentation Channel
desc: A transmission channel carrying information that can augment that being transmitted in an ordinary transmission channel such that a special television set that can receive both channels can get a better picture than those available from the main channel alone. Some ATV schemes require the augmentation channel to be adjacent to the main channel. Others can theoretically accept a non-adjacent augmen- tation channel, though, at the time of this writing, the acceptability of non-adjacent channels has not been proven to everyone’s satisfaction.

name: Augmented  Reality
desc: The Use Of Transparent Glasses On Which A Computer Displays Data So That The Viewer Can Simultaneously View Computer Generated And Real World Scenes.

name: Augmented Reality (AR)
desc: Augmented    Reality    (AR)    overlays digitally-created content on top of the user’s real-world environment, viewed through a device (such as a smartphone) that incorporates real-time inputs to create an enhanced version of reality. Digital and virtual objects (e.g., graphics, sounds) are superimposed on an existing environment to create an AR experience.

name: Aural
desc: Having To Do With The Auditory Mechanism.

name: Austrian Curtain
desc: A Curtain That Is Raised (Opened) With Brailed Lifting Lines And Is Sewn With Both Vertical And Horizontal Fullness.

name: auteur ( or auteur theory )
desc: literally the French word for " author " ; in film criticism , used inthe terms auteurism or auteur theory , denoting a criticaltheory ( originally known as la politique des auteurs or " thepolicy of authors " ) popular in France in the late 1940s andearly 1950s that was introduced by Francois Truffaut and theeditors ( including legendary film critic and theorist AndreBazin ) of the celebrated French film journal Cahiers duCinÃ©ma ( literally ' cinema notebooks ' ) , arguably the mostinfluential film magazine in film history ; their ideas weresubsequently enlarged upon in the 1960s by American criticAndrew Sarris , among others ; the theory ascribed overallresponsibility for the creation of a film and its personal vision ,identifiable style , thematic aspects and techniques to its filmmaker or director , rather than to the collaborative efforts of allinvolved ( actors , producer , production designer , specialeffects supervisor , etc ) ; the theory posited that directorsshould be considered the ' true ' authors of film ( rather than thescreenwriters ) because they exercise a great deal of controlover all facets of film making and impart a distinctive ,personal style to their films ; simply stated , an auteur can referto a director with a recognizable or signature style .

name: Authoring
desc: The encoding of material from various sources, all the con- version processes of the encoded data, incorporating the required control structures and other signals for playback sequences in the DVD-video format. The final product of authoring is a DLT tape with DVD image files in DDP format.

name: Authoring Platform
desc: Computer hardware and software used to create material for use on a multimedia system. The video quality of the authoring platform has to be high enough that the playback equipment is the limiting factor.

name: Authoring System
desc: Software, which helps developers design interactive courseware easily, without the painstaking detail of computer program- ming.

name: Auto Assembly
desc: a) Process of assembling an edited videotape on a computerized editing system under the control of an edit decision list (EDL). A computer automatically conforms source footage into an edited video program under the direction of a list of preprogrammed edit instruc- tions. b) An edit in which an off-line edit decision list is loaded into an on-line edit computer and all the edits are assembled automatically with little or no human intervention. c) The automatic assembling of an edited video tape on a computerized editing system (controller), based on an edit decision list (EDL).

name: Autoblock
desc: An Eos feature that automatically places a block on a channel's parameter when a redundant level change occurs (i.e., the upstream edit would track through to create the same value that is already stored). Displayed as a lowercase 'b' in the PSD.

name: Auto-focus
desc: Automatic focus. A device in a projector or camera using light reflected from a surface to focus the image.

name: Auto-input switching
desc: The feature enabling a product to detect which input has an active sync signal and switch to that input.

name: Auto Iris (AI)
desc: An automatic method of varying the size of a lens aperture in response to changes in scene illumination.

name: Autoload (Titan)
