desc: A system file that you change to customize the way your system behaves. Such files are sometimes referred to as customiza- tion files.

name: Conform
desc: To prepare a complete version of your project for viewing. The version produced might be an intermediate working version or the final cut.

name: Conforming
desc: The process wherein an offline edited master is used as a guide for performing final edits.

name: Conforming a Film Negative
desc: The mathematical process that the edit- ing system uses to ensure that the edits made on a videotape version of a film project (30 fps) are frame accurate when they are made to the final film version (24 fps).

name: Congestion
desc: Occurs when a network link or node carries so much data that quality of service deteriorates, causing queuing delay, packet loss, or blocked connections.

name: Connection-Oriented Protocol
desc: In a packet switching network, a virtual circuit can be formed to emulate a fixed bandwidth switched circuit, for example, ATM. This benefits transmission of media requiring constant delays and bandwidth.

name: Connector
desc: Hardware at the end of a cable that lets you fasten the cable to an outlet, port, or another connector.

name: Connector Box
desc: See Plug-In Box

name: Connector Strip
desc: A Piece Of Power Distribution Equipment Comprising An Elongated Metal Housing, And A Plurality Of Female Flush Mount Connectors Or Female Pigtail Connectors For The Purpose Of Supplying Electricity To Luminaires. It Usually Hangs From A Batten And Has Many Circuits, The Line Side Is Usually Hard-Wired, And It Gets Its Electrical Supply From Dimmers.

name: Console
desc: Another Term For A Sound Mixer, Usually A Large Desk-Like Mixer.

name: Console surface
desc: The physical hardware control surface of a digital mixer (faders, encoders, screens) which may be connected to a separate engine/rack unit. Allows flexible installation where the engine is in a rack and the surface is at the operator position.

name: Console Window
desc: The window that appears each time you log in. IRIX reports all status and error messages to this window.

name: Consolidate
desc: To make copies of media files or portions of media files, and then save them on a drive. The consolidate feature operates differently for master clips, subclips and sequences.

name: Constant
desc: a) A fixed value. b) An option for the interpolation and/or extrapolation of an animation curve that produces a square or stepped curve.

name: Constant Alpha
desc: A gray scale alpha plane that consists of a constant non-zero value.

name: Constant Bit Rate (CBR)
desc: a) An operation where the bit rate is constant from start to finish of the compressed bit stream. b) A variety of MPEG video compression where the amount of compression does not change. c) Traffic that requires guaranteed levels of service and throughput in delay-sensitive applications such as audio and video that are digitized and represented by a continuous bit stream.

name: Constant Bit Rate Coded Media
desc: A compressed media bitstream with a constant average bit rate. For example, some MPEG video bitstreams.

name: Constant Bit Rate Coded Video
desc: A compressed video bit stream with a constant average bit rate.

name: Constant Luminance Principle
desc: A rule of composite color television that any change in color not accompanied by a change in brightness should not have any effect on the brightness of the image displayed on a picture tube. The constant luminance principle is generally violated by existing NTSC encoders and decoders. See also Gamma.

name: Constant Quality
desc: Video encoding where quality remains constant while bit rate varies based on the nature of the video material.

name: Constant Shading
desc: The simplest shading type is constant. The color of a constant shaded polygon’s interior pixels is always the same, regardless of the polygon’s orientation with respect to the viewer and light sources. Constant shading is useful for creating light sources, for example. With all other shading types, a polygon changes its shade as it moves.

name: Constant voltage system
desc: A distributed audio standard where amplifier output voltage is fixed (70V USA, 100V Europe) regardless of load. Loudspeakers connect via matching transformers, allowing many speakers across long cable runs.

name: Constellation Diagram
desc: A display used within digital modulation to determine the health of the system. It consists of a plot of symbol values onto an X-Y display, similar to a vectorscope display. The horizontal axis is known as the In-Phase (I) and the vertical axis is known as the Quadrature Phase (Q) axis. The position of the symbols within the constellation diagram provides information about distortions in the QAM or QPSK modulator as well as about distortions after the transmission of digitally coded signals.

name: Constrained Parameters
desc: MPEG-1 video term that specifies the values of the set of coding parameters in order to assure a baseline interoperability.

name: Constrained System Parameter Stream (CSPS)
desc: An MPEG-1 multi- plexed system stream to which the constrained parameters are applied.

name: Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG)
desc: This way of modeling builds a world by combining “primitive” solids such as cubes, spheres, and cones. The operations that combine these primitives are typically union, intersec- tion, and difference. These are called Boolean operations. A CSG database is called a CSG tree. In the tree, branch points indicate the operations that take place on the solids that flow into the branch point.

name: Contact closure
desc: A simple switch interface providing binary on/off control by completing or opening an electrical circuit. Used to trigger AV events: raise/lower screens, switch inputs, mute audio, start recording.

name: Container
desc: A type of file (also called a wrapper or envelope) that can hold different CODECs. Examples: AVI, FLV, MKV, MP4, QuickTime, RM, WMV.

name: Content
desc: The program content will consist of the sum total of the essence (video, audio, data, graphics, etc.) and the metadata. Content can include television programming, data and executable software.

name: Content-Based Image Coding
desc: The analysis of an image to recognize the objects of the scene (e.g., a house, a person, a car, a face,...). The objects, once recognized are coded as parameters to a general object model (of the house, person, car, face,...) which is then synthesized (i.e., rendered) by the decoder using computer graphic techniques.

name: Contention
desc: Competition for overused network media among network devices.

name: Content Object
desc: The object encapsulation of the MPEG-4 decoded representation of audiovisual data.

name: Continuation Indicator (CI)
desc: Indicates the end of an object in the current packet (or continuation).

name: Continuity
desc: This Is An Important Concept To Keep In Mind During Recording Of Video/Audio And Later In Post-Production. Continuity Means That If Something Is In One Position Or State-Of-Being In One Shot, It Needs To Be The Same Way In The Next Shot Unless It Has Purposely Been Changed For Storytelling Purposes. Some Examples Of Lack Of Continuity Are Changes In A Subject’S Clothing, Hair Style, Body Position, Or Position Of Objects On The Set Between Two Shots That Are Supposed To Be Occurring Within The Same Time Frame. Another Example Is When In One Shot A Subject Is Traveling In One Direction, But In The Next Shot The Movement Is In The Opposite Direction. Continuity Changes Can Also Occur With Audio If Scenes Are Shot In Different Locations Or At Different Times But Are Supposed To Be Occurring In The Same Location.

name: continuity( editing or cutting )
desc: the system of editing that developed in the early 20th centuryto provide a continuous and clear movement ofevents / images in a film ; refers to the final edited structure of acompleted film , with the events or scenes / sequencesarranged as if they had occurred continuously , when , in fact ,they were shot out of sequence ; continuity also refers to thedegree to which a film is self - consistent without errors , jumpcuts , or mis - matched shots and details ; a continuity cut refersto a editing cut that takes the viewer seamlessly ,unobtrusively , and logically from one sequence or scene toanother , to propel the narrative along ; a blooper orflub isa continuity error

name: Continuous Monitoring
desc: The monitoring method that provides continu- ous real-time monitoring of all transport streams in a network.

name: Continuous power
desc: Amplifier output specification stated as watts (rms) into ohms from 20 Hz to 20 kHz at a specified THD percentage.

name: Continuous presence
desc: A videoconferencing feature allowing participants to view multiple sites on the same screen simultaneously.

name: Continuous Tone
desc: An image that has all the values (0 to 100%) of gray (black and white) or color in it. A photograph is a continuous tone image.

name: Contour curtain
desc: A brail or Austrian curtain rigged so each lift line can be operated separately, creating different shaped openings.

name: Contour Enhancement
desc: A general term usually intended to include both aperture correction and edge enhancement.

name: Contouring
desc: a) Video picture defect due to quantizing at too coarse a level. The visual effect of this defect is that pictures take on a layered look somewhat like a geographical contoured map. b) This is an image artifact caused by not having enough bits to represent the image. The reason the effect is called “contouring” is because the image develops vertical bands of brightness.

name: contract player
desc: an actor ( both stars and bit players ) who has a contractualcommitment or agreement to a studio / producer / company

name: Contrast
desc: Contrast describes the difference between the white and black levels in a video waveform. If there is a large difference between the white and black picture levels, the image has high contrast. If there is a small difference between the white and black portions of the picture, then the picture has low contrast and takes on a gray appearance.

name: Contrast range
desc: The range of grays in a video image.

name: Contrast Ratio
desc: a) Related to gamma law and is a measurement of the maximum range of light to dark objects that a television system can reproduce. b) The comparison of the brightest part of the screen to the darkest part of the screen, expressed as a ratio. The maximum contrast ratio for television production is 30 x 1.

name: Contribution
desc: A form of signal transmission where the destination is not the ultimate viewer and where processing (such as electronic matting) is likely to be applied to the signal before it reaches the ultimate viewer. Contribution demands higher signal quality than does distribution because of the processing.

name: Contribution quality
desc: The Level Of Quality Of A Television Signal From The Network To Its Affiliates. For Digital Television This Is Approximately 45 Mbps.

name: Control .
desc: A Mechanism To Turn Lamps On And Off, Or Dim Lamps. Controls Include Switches, Dimmers, Timing Devices, Motion Detectors, Photosensors,  And Central Control Systems

name: Control Block
desc: Circuits that perform the control functions of the CPU. They are responsible for decoding instructions and then generating the internal control signals that perform the operations requested.

name: Control Board
desc: See Control Console.

name: Control Bus
desc: Set of control lines in a computer system. Provides the synchronization and control information necessary to run the system.

name: Control Cable
desc: A Cable Used To Transmit Digital Or Analog Signals From A Control Console To The Apparatus To Be Controlled.

name: Control Card
desc: 1) Specifically, A Circuit Board That Receives The Control Signal From The Control Console And, In Turn, Individually Controls The Independent Outputs Of A Bank Of Dimmer Modules. 2) Generally, Any Circuit Board That Performs Many Of The Control Functions Of An Electronic Apparatus, E.G., A Ballast Or Automated Light.

name: Control Channel
desc: A logical channel which carries control messages.

name: Control Console
desc: An Electronic Apparatus, Run By An Operator, That Converts The Settings Of Various Items, Such As Sliders, Switches, Buttons, Or Some Form Of Data Input, Into A Digital Or Analog Signal That Is Thereby Transmitted To A Control Card, Dimmer Bank, Or Some Other Electronic Apparatus. Some Control Consoles Are Also Equipped With Monitors.

name: Control group
desc: DiGiCo's term for a VCA/DCA group. Multiple channels assigned to a control group so their levels can be adjusted together with a single fader while maintaining individual channel routing and processing.

name: Control Layer
desc: The MPEG-4 Systems Layer that maintains and updates the state of the MPEG-4 Systems Layers according to control messages or user interaction.

name: Controller
desc: A Type Of Play  Peripheral, Usually Held In The Hands, That Accompanies Many Headsets  And Video  Game  Platforms. In Virtual  Reality, Controllers Commonly Allow Users To Grab Or Manipulate Objects Within The Virtual Environment.

name: Controllers
desc: A piece of equipment that users hold in their hands and enables them to interact with virtual environments. Controllers allow the user to maneuver more easily and can lead to greater sense of presence. Some companies are experimenting with controllers that have haptic links that enable the user to feel movement, texture, etc.

name: Control-L (LANC)
desc: Sony’s wired edit control protocol, also called LANC (Local Application Control), which allows two-way communication between a camcorder or VCR and an edit controller such as the Thumbs Up.Control-L allows the controller to control the deck (fast forward, play, etc.) and also allows the controller to read the tape position (tape counter) information from the deck.

name: Control-M
desc: Panasonic’s wired edit control protocol. Similar to Control-L in function but not compatible. Also called Panasonic 5-pin edit control. See Control-L.

name: Control Menu Box
desc: Located on the upper left corner of all application windows, document windows, and dialog boxes, it sizes (maximize, mini- mize, or restore) or exits the window.

name: Control Message
desc: An information unit exchanged to configure or modify the state of the MPEG-4 systems.

name: Control Point
desc: A location on a Bézier curve that controls its direction. Each control point has two direction handles that can extend from it.

name: Control Processor
desc: The central device in a control system that runs the program or configuration file and tells control ports how to interface to controlled devices.

name: Control Processor Unit/Central Processing Unit (CPU)
desc: a) Circuits used to generate or alter control signals. b) A card in the frame which controls overall switcher operation.

name: Control Program
desc: Sequence of instructions that guide the CPU through the various operations it must perform. This program is stored permanently in ROM where it can be accessed by the CPU during operation. Usually this ROM is located within the microprocessor chip. Same as Microprogram or Microcode.

name: Control Room
desc: The enclosed room where the electronic control system for radio and television are located and where the director and technical director sit.

name: Control-S
desc: Sony wired transport control protocol that duplicates a VCR’s infra-red remote transport control (play, stop, pause, fast forward and rewind). Unlike Control-L, Control-S does not allow the controller to read tape counter information.

name: Control Signal
desc: A signal used to cause an alteration or transition of video signals.

name: Control-T
desc: Similar to Control-L but allows multiple units to be controlled. Not used in current equipment.

name: Control Track
desc: a) The magnetized portion along the length of a video- tape on which sync control information is placed. The control track contains a pulse for each video field and is used to synchronize the tape and the video signal. b) A synchronizing signal on the edge of the tape which provides a reference for tracking control and tape speed. Control tracks that have heavy dropouts are improperly recorded and may cause tracking defects or picture jumps. c) A signal recorded on videotape to allow the tape to play back at a precise speed in any VTR. Analogous to the sprocket holes on film. d) A linear track, consisting of 30-or 60-Hz pulses, placed on the bottom of videotape that aids in the proper playback of the video signal.

name: Control Track Editing
desc: The linear editing of videotape with equipment that reads the control track information to synchronize the editing between two decks. Contrast with Timecode Editing.

name: Control Track Editor
desc: Type of editing system that uses frame pulses on the videotape control track for reference.

name: Conventional
desc: Stage lighting instruments other than moving lights, LEDs, or special effects units. Includes fresnels, profiles, PARs, and floods. Also called generics.

name: Conventional Control Gear
desc: Control Gear Used To Run A Lamp, Typically Consisting Of A Ballast, Ignitor And Capacitor.

name: Conventional Definition Television (CDTV)
desc: This term is used to signify the analog NTSC television system as defined in ITU-R Recommendation 470. See also Standard Definition Television and ITU-R Recommendation 1125.

name: conventions
desc: the expected elements in a type of film , without question ,thought , or judgment

name: Convergence
desc: The act of adjusting or the state of having adjusted, the Red, Green and Blue color gun deflection such that the electron beams are all hitting the same color triad at the same time.

name: Conversion curve
desc: On Hog consoles, a curve defining the relationship between a programmed parameter value and the DMX value sent to the fixture. Similar to dimmer curves on other consoles. A property of the fixture, not the cue.

name: Conversion, Frame-Rate
desc: Standardized image systems now exist in the following frame rates per second: 24, 25, 29.97, 30, and 60. In transcod- ing from one system to another, frame rate conversion algorithms perform this conversion. The algorithm may be as simple as to drop or add frames or fields, or it may process the information to generate predictive frames employing information from the original sequence. In interlace systems, the algorithm may be applied independently to each field.

name: Conversion Ratio
desc: The size conversion ratio for the purpose of rate control of shape.

name: Converter
desc: Equipment for changing the frequency of a television signal such as at a cable head-end or at the subscriber’s receiver.

name: Convex
desc: A Term Used To Describe A Lens Side That Is Outwardly And Usually Spherically Curved.

name: Convex Lens
desc: A convex lens has a positive focal length, i.e., the focus is real. It is usually called magnifying glass, since it magnifies the objects.

name: Convolutional Coding
desc: The data stream to be transmitted via satellite (DVB-S) which is loaded bit by bit into shift registers. The data which is split and delayed as it is shifted through different registers is combined in several paths. This means that double the data rate (two paths) is usually obtained. Puncturing follows to reduce the data rate: the time sequence of the bits is predefined by this coding and is represented by the trellis diagram.

name: Convolution reverb
desc: A reverb effect using impulse responses (IRs) — recordings of real spaces — to recreate the exact acoustic characteristics of specific rooms, halls, or hardware reverb units with high accuracy.

name: Convolve
desc: To Filter And Intertwine Signals (E.G., Sounds) And Render Them Three-Dimensional. Used In Vr Applications To Recreate Sounds That Give Directional Cues.

name: Convolvotron
desc: An Output System For Controlling Binaural Sound Production In A Vr World.

name: Coogan's Law
desc: refers to landmark legislation in the late 30s designed toprotect a child actor's earnings , by depositing some of theminor's earnings in court - administered trust funds that thechild receives when he / she reaches the age of majority ;named after child actor Jackie Coogan

name: Cookie
desc: Cookie Is A Foam Core Board With Holes Cut Out To Create A Splash Of Light And Shadow On The Background.

name: Cool Color
desc: Generally, A Color That Is In The Green-Blue-Violet Range.

name: Cool Light
desc: Light Having A Color Temperature Of Approximately 3600°K To 4900°K, I.E., Bright-White To Blue-White.

name: Coordinates
desc: A Set Of Data Values That Determine The Location Of A Point In A Space. The Number Of Coordinates Corresponds To The Dimensionality Of The Space.

name: Coordination System
desc: See Reference.

name: COP-3
desc: A Code of Practices for MPEG-2 transport stream transmission applying Forward Error Correction to protect enclosed data.

name: COP-4
desc: A Code of Practices for transmission of uncompressed standard video at up to 270 Mbps and HD video at up to 1.485 Gbps, applying Forward Error Correction.

name: “Copy”
desc: A Response Intended To Convey Something Is Heard And Understood; Also Expressed As “Copy That”.

name: Copy and paste
desc: A digital console workflow function for copying all or selected parameters from one channel to another, speeding up configuration of similar inputs.

name: CORBA (Common Object Request Broker Architecture)
desc: A standard defined by the Common Object Group. It is a framework that provides interoperability between objects built in different programming languages, running on different physical machines perhaps on different networks.CORBA specifies an Interface Definition Language, and API (Application Programming Interface) that allows client / server interaction with the ORB (Object Request Broker).

name: Cord Wrap
desc: 1) A Loop Made Of Rope Attached To A Yoke For The Purpose Of Supporting Excess Coils Of Electric Cable.

name: Core
desc: In Fiber Optic Cable, The Core Is The Light-Transmitting Material At The Center Of The Fiber.

name: Core Experiment
desc: Core experiments verify the inclusion of a new tech- nique or set of techniques. At the heart of the core experiment process are multiple, independent, directly comparable experiments, performed to determine whether or not proposed algorithmic techniques have merits. A core experiment must be completely and uniquely defined, so that the results are unambiguous. In addition to the specification of the algorithmic technique(s) to be evaluated, a core experiment also specifies the parame- ters to be used (for example, audio sample rate or video resolution), so that the results can be compared. A core experiment is proposed by one or more MPEG experts, and it is approved by consensus, provided that two or more independent experts carry out the experiment.

name: Core Visual Profile
desc: Adds support for coding of arbitrary-shaped and temporally scalable objects to the Simple Visual Profile. It is useful for applications such as those providing relatively simple content interactivity (Internet multimedia applications).

name: Coring
desc: A system for reducing the noise content of circuits by removing low-amplitude noise riding on the baseline of the signals. Both aperture correction and enhancement can be cored. It involves preventing any boosting of very low level edge transitions. The threshold point is the coring control. The more the coring is increased, the more the extra noise added by the enhanced (or aperture corrector) high frequency boosting is reduced. Of course, the fine detail enhancement is also reduced or eliminated. Too high levels of coring can cause a “plastic picture” effect.

name: Corner
desc: Short For The "Prompt Corner"; The Place From Which The Stage Manager Controls The Show. From Here He Has Communication Links To All Parts Of The The Theatre And Gives Cues (Qv) To All Departments. The Corner Can Be On Either Side Of The Stage But Traditionally It Is On The Left (I.E. The Prompt) Side. Perversely Some Theatres Have The Prompt Corner On The "Opposite Prompt" (Op) Side Of The Stage! The Person Who Is Operating The Corner Is Sometimes Said To Be "In The Corner" And Sometimes "On The Book".

name: Cornice Luminaire
desc: See Soffit Luminaire.

name: Corpse
desc: Not A Dead Body In A Thriller! An Actor Who Gets An Unintended And Uncontrollable Fit Of Laughter On Stage Is Said To "Corpse".

name: Correlated Color Temperature (Cct)
desc: Describes The Color Appearance Of The Light That Is Produced, In Terms Of Its Warmth Or Coolness. The Cct  Relates The Color Appearance Of The Lamp To The Color Appearance Of A Reference Source When The Reference Source Is Heated To A Particular  Temperature,  Measured On The Kelvin (K) Temperature Scale. A  Low Color Temperature (3000 K  And Lower) Describes A Warm Source, Such As A Typical Incandescent  Lamp And A Warm Fluorescent  Lamp. A High Color Temperature (4000 K  And Higher) Describes A Cool Source, Such As A Cool White Fluorescent  Lamp.

name: Correlation
desc: A comparison of data which is used to find signals in noise or for pattern recognition. It uses a best-match algorithm which compares the data to the reference.

name: CoS
desc: Class of Service. IEEE 802.1p Layer 2 QoS marking using a 3-bit priority field in the VLAN tag. Priority 7 (highest) to 0 (lowest). AV traffic typically marked 5-6, voice 6, best-effort data 0.

name: Co-Sited Sampling
desc: Co-sited sampling ensures that the luminance and the chrominance digital information is simultaneous, minimizing chroma/luma delay. This sampling technique is applied to color difference component video signals: Y, Cr, and Cb. The color difference signals, Cr and Cb, are sampled at a sub-multiple of Y, the luminance frequency – 4:2:2, for example. With co-sited sampling, the two color difference signals are sampled at the same instant, as well as one of the luminance samples.

name: Co-siting
desc: Relates To Smpte 125M Component Digital Video, In Which The Luminance Component

name: Costume designer
desc: The person responsible for designing and overseeing the creation of all costumes worn by performers in a production.

name: costume( or wardrobe )and costume design
desc: refers to the garments or clothing worn by actors / performersin a film ; a costume ( or wardrobe ) designer researches ,designs , and selects the costumes to be appropriate to thefilm's time period , the characters , their location , and theiroccupations , whereas thecostumer ( or stylist ) is responsiblefor acquiring , selecting , manufacturing , and / or handling theclothing and accessories ; acostume drama is a film set in aparticular historical time period , often with elaboratecostuming

name: Counterweight
desc: A standard weight (typically 30 or 60 lb.) used in a counterweight flying system to balance the weight of flown scenery.

name: Counterweight Fly System
desc: A fly system that uses an arbor loaded with stage weights to counterbalance the load on the batten, allowing one operator to raise and lower heavy loads safely. See Fly System.

name: Counterweight system
desc: A fly system that uses counterweights on an arbor to balance the weight of the flown item, requiring minimal operator effort to fly scenery up and down.

name: Coupler
desc: A passive device used to connect two terminated cables for signal extension.

name: Coupling Loss
desc: Loss of optical power at a junction (such as a coupler), expressed as the ratio of power measured at the junction to total power entering the system.

name: courtroom drama
desc: a drama and / or mystery story , in which the main protagonistis a lawyer , and a majority of the drama and dramatic actiontakes place in a courtroom setting ; the plot revolves aroundthe preparation of a trial and its result of guilt or innocence

name: Cove
desc: A lighting position. A vertical pipe hidden behind a notch in the wall downstage of the proscenium. Similar to a box boom but concealed from the audience.

name: Cove Luminaire
desc: An Architectural Luminaire That Directs Light From Sources That Are Mounted In A Cove To The Ceiling Or Upper Wall. A Cove Is A Ledge Or Shelf  On The Wall, Or A Recess In The Wall.

name: coverage
desc: refers to all the shots , including closeups and reverse angles ,that a director takes in addition to the master shot , to make upthe final product ; to have proper coverage means having allthe proper scenes , angles , lightings , close - ups , and directions

name: Coverage area
desc: Coverage Area Is The Area Within An Ntsc Station'S Grade B Contour Without Regard To Interference From Other Television Stations Which May Be Present. For An Atv Station, Coverage Area Is The Area Contained Within The Station'S Noise-Limited Contour Without Regard To Interference Which May Be Present.

name: Coverage Pattern
desc: A Loudspeaker System’S Directional Pattern, Which Can Vary By Frequency And Tone.

name: CPE (Common Phase Error)
desc: Signal distortions that are common to all carriers. This error can (partly) be suppressed by channel estimation using the continual pilots.

name: CPM (Copyrighted Material)
desc: In DVD-Video, a 1-bit value stored in the CPR_MAI that indicates if the corresponding sector includes any copyrighted material.

name: CPPM (Content Protection for Prerecorded Media)
desc: Copy protection for DVD-Audio.

name: CPR_MAI (Copyright Management Information)
desc: In DVD-Video, an extra 6 bytes per sector that includes the Copyright Protection System Type (CPS_TY) and Region Management information (RMA) in the Contents provider section of the Control data block; and Copyrighted Material flag (CPM), Copyright Protection System flag (CP_SEC) and Copy Guard Management System (CGMS) flags in the Data Area.

name: CPRM (Content Protection for Recordable Media)
desc: Copy protection for writable DVD formats.

name: CPS
desc: Abbreviation for Characters Per Second.

name: CPSA (Content Protection System Architecture)
desc: An overall copy protection design for DVD.

name: CP_SEC (Copyright Protection System)
desc: In DVD-Video, a 1-bit value stored in the CPR_MAI that indicates if the corresponding sector has implemented a copyright protection system. See Content Scrambling System (CSS).

name: CPS_TY (Copyright Protection System Type)
desc: In DVD-Video, an 8-bit (1 byte) value stored in the CPR_MAI that defines the type of copyright protection system implemented on a disc.

name: CPTWG (Copy Protection Technical Working Group)
desc: The industry body responsible for developing or approving DVD copy protection systems.

name: CPU
desc: See Central Processing Unit.

name: CPU Board
desc: The printed circuit board within a workstation chassis that contains the central processing unit(s). When you open the front metal panel of the Indigo chassis, it is the board on the left.

name: CPV
desc: This is a proprietary and relatively old format designed for 30 fps video over packet based networks. It is still being used in closed video systems where 30 fps is required, such as in security applications.

name: CR
desc: Scaled version of the R-Y signal.

name: crane shot
desc: a camera shot taken from a large camera dolly or electronicdevice ( an apparatus , such as a crane ) , resembling aextendable mechanical arm ( or boom ) , that can raise thecamera up in the air above the ground 20 feet or more ; thecrane allows the camera to fluidly move in virtually anydirection ( with vertical and horizontal movement ) , providingshifts in levels and angles ; crane shots usually provide somekind of overhead view of a scene

name: Crash Edit
desc: An edit that is electronically unstable, such as one made using the pause control on a deck, or using a non-capstan served deck.

name: Crash rail
desc: A member mounted to counterweight guides that limits the travel of arbors at their top and bottom trim positions. Often fitted with rubber or wood bumpers.

name: Crash Recording
desc: See Hard Recording.

name: Crawl
desc: a) Titles that move slowly up the screen, mounted on a revolving drum. b) Sideways movement of text across a screen. c) An appearance of motion in an image where there should be none. See also Chroma Crawl and Line Crawl.

name: Crawling Text
desc: Text that moves horizontally over time. Examples include stock and sports score tickers that appear along the bottom of a television screen.

name: CRC
desc: See Cyclic Redundancy Check.

name: Crease
desc: A tape deformity which may cause horizontal or vertical lines in the playback picture. See Wrinkle.

name: Creative Commons
desc: Creative Commons Is  Media That The Copyright Owner Has Put Out For Us To Use, Provided We Follow Their Copyright Requirements. These Requirements Will Vary But Can Including Giving The Copyright Holder Credit

name: Credits
desc: Listing of actors, singers, directors, etc., in title preceding or directly following the program.

name: Creepy-Crawlies
desc: Yes, this is a real video term! Creepy-crawlies refers to a specific image artifact that is a result of the NTSC system. When the nightly news is on, and a little box containing a picture appears over the anchorperson’s shoulder, or when some computer-generated text shows up on top of the video clip being shown, get up close to the TV and check it out. Along the edges of the box, or along the edges of the text, you’ll notice some jaggies “rolling” up (or down) the picture. That is the creepy-crawlies. Some people refer to this as zipper because it looks like one.

name: Crescent wrench
desc: An adjustable wrench named for its curved shape. Also called a C-wrench. Used for tightening scaffold couplers and rigging hardware.

name: Crest Factor
desc: The Ratio Of The Peak Value To The Rms Value. Musical Signals Can Have Peaks Many Times Higher Than The Rms Value. The Larger The Tran_x0002_Sient Peaks, The Larger The Crest Factor.

name: Crestron
desc: A leading manufacturer of control systems, room automation, AV distribution, and collaboration technology for commercial and government applications. Known for CP4, SIMPL+, Crestron DM, and NVX product lines.

name: Crestron DM
desc: Crestron DigitalMedia. A proprietary AV-over-HDBaseT matrix distribution platform supporting 4K HDR video, audio, control, Ethernet, and power over Cat cable.

name: Crestron Home
desc: Crestron's smart home automation platform for residential installations, offering simplified guided setup wizards rather than full SIMPL programming.

name: Crestron NVX
desc: Crestron's AV-over-IP platform based on the SDVoE standard, allowing scalable matrix switching over standard 1G or 10G Ethernet infrastructure.

name: crew
desc: refers to those involved in the technical production of a filmwho are not actual performers

name: Cri
desc: Color Rendering Index

name: crisis
desc: the period of highest tension just before the climax of a film( there can be more than one ) ; the point at which events reachtheir highest level of tension

name: Crispening
desc: A means of increasing picture sharpness by generating and applying a second time derivative of the original signal.

name: Critical Angle
desc: The angle of incidence below which total internal reflection occurs at the boundary between two refractive materials. In optical fiber, light always strikes the core-cladding boundary below this angle.

name: Critical Band
desc: Frequency band of selectivity of the human ear which is a psychoacoustic measure in the spectral domain. Units of the critical band rate scale are expressed as Barks.

name: critic( or film critic ,film reviewer )
desc: an individual who writes and / or publishes a review of a filmfrom either an artistic or entertainment point of view . Filmreviews often analyze and discuss a film's details , its contentand characters , a critique of the performances , camera work ,directing , editing , production , and script ; film critics are usuallymore philosophical and theoretical than film reviewers orcommentators ; film criticism refers to the analysis of thenarrative , historical and stylistic characteristics of film ; ' critics 'is sometimes abbreviated as crix .

name: Crop
desc: Term used for the action of moving left, right, top and bottom boundaries of a key. See Trim.

name: Crop Box
desc: A box that is superimposed over frames, either automatically or manually, to limit color corrections, key setups, etc., to the area inside the box.

name: Cropping
desc: A digital process which removes areas of a picture (frame) by replacing video pixels with opaque pixels of background colors. Cropping may be used to eliminate unwanted picture areas such as edges or as quasi-masking in preparation for keying.

name: Cross-Assembler
desc: Assembler that runs on a processor whose assembly language is different from the language being assembled.

name: Cross Bar
desc: 1) In The Theater Industry, A Bar Mounted Horizontally On Top Of A Stand. It Contains Two Or More Sliding Tees For Mounting Luminaires, And A Fixed Tee For Mounting The Bar To The Stand. 2) In The Film And Video Industries, A Bar Mounted Horizontally Between Two Stands For The Purpose Of Hanging Luminaires Or Grip Equipment.

name: Cross Color
desc: Spurious signal resulting from high-frequency luminance information being interpreted as color information in decoding a composite signal. Typical video examples are “rainbow” on venetian blinds and striped shirts.

name: Cross Connecting Panel
desc: See Patch Panel.

name: Cross Conversion
desc: The process of converting video from one standard to another, usually at the same resolution. Example: 1080i59.94 to 1080p25.

name: cross - cutting
desc: the editing technique of alternating , interweaving , orinterspersing one narrative action ( scene , sequence , or event )with another - usually in different locations or places , thuscombining the two ; this editing method suggests parallelaction ( that takes place simultaneously ) ; often used todramatically build tension and suspense in chase scenes , orto compare two different scenes ; also known as intercutting or parallel editing .

name: Cross Fade
desc: A Relatively Slow Change From One Control Console Setting To Another.

name: Cross Fader
desc: A Slider On A Control Console That Enables A Cross Fade.

name: Cross Hairs
desc: The plus-sign pattern in a camera viewfinder showing the center of frame.

name: Crosshatch
desc: A test pattern consisting of vertical and horizontal lines used for converging color monitors and cameras.

name: Cross Light
desc: A Luminaire Used For Crosslighting.

name: Crosslighting
desc: Illumination From Two Sources On Opposite Sides Of The Subject.

name: Cross Luma
desc: This occurs when the video decoder incorrectly interprets chroma information (color) to be high-frequency luma information (bright- ness).

name: Cross Luminance
desc: Spurious signals occurring in the Y channel as a result of composite chroma signals being interpreted as luminance, such as “dot crawl” or “busy edges” on colored areas.

name: Cross-Luminance Artifacts
desc: Introduced in the S-VHS concept for a better luminance resolution.

name: Cross Mod
desc: A test method for determining the optimum print require- ments for a variable area sound track.

name: Cross Modulation
desc: See Chrominance-to-Luminance Intermodulation.

name: Crossover
desc: A Device Used To Split A Full-Range Audio Signal Into Multiple, Smaller Frequency Ranges By Running The Audio Through A Set Of Filters.

name: cross - overandcross - over appeal
desc: a film or production that is made for one audience , but mayeasily ' cross - over ' to another unexpected audience ; alsorefers to a film , actor , or production that appeals to differentdemographic groups or age groups and can move betweentwo or more distinct franchises ; see also hybrid

name: Crossover distortion
desc: Distortion in push-pull class AB or class B amplifiers occurring when one output stage shuts off and the other turns on.

name: Crossover frequency
desc: The frequency at which an audio signal is divided between two loudspeaker components — for example, between a subwoofer and mid-high cabinet. Set in a DSP processor or system controller.

name: Crossover Network
desc: A device which divides a signal into two or more frequency bands before low frequency outputs of a crossover network. The level of each output at this frequency is 3 dB down from the flat section of the crossover’s frequency response curve.

name: Crossover Volumes
desc: Different Configurations Of The Same Volume Load Which Encompass Overlapping Physicalareas . See Also Volume Variants .

name: Cross-Play
desc: By cross-play capability is meant the ability to record and reproduce on the same or a different machine; record at one speed and reproduce at the same or a different speed; accomplish the foregoing singly or in any combination without readjustment for tape or transport type.

name: Crosspoint
desc: a) The electronic circuit used to switch video, usually on a bus. b) An electronic switch, usually controlled by a push-button on the panel, or remotely by computer that allows video or audio to pass when the switch is closed.

name: Cross Reality (XR)
desc: See Extended  Reality.

name: Cross-Sectional Modeling
desc: This type of modeling is also a boundary representation method available in PictureMaker. The artist can define an object’s cross-section, and then extrude in the longitudinal direction after selecting an outline to define the cross-section’s changes in scale as it traverses the longitudinal axis.

name: Crosstalk
desc: The interference between two audio or two video signals caused by unwanted stray signals. a) In video, crosstalk between input channels can be classified into two basic categories: luminance/sync crosstalk; and color (chroma) crosstalk. When video crosstalk is too high, ghost images from one source appear over the other. b) In audio, signal leakage, typically between left and right channels or between different inputs, can be caused by poor grounding connections or improperly shield- ed cables. See Chrominance-to-Luminance Intermodulation.

name: Crosstalk Noise
desc: The signal-to-crosstalk noise ratio is the ratio, in decibels, of the nominal amplitude of the luminance signal (100 IRE units) to the peak-to-peak amplitude of the interfering waveform.

name: crowd shot
desc: a shot or image of a large group of people ( often extras ) in afilm ; CGI is now often used to film large crowd shots , to avoidhuge costs associated with hiring extras

name: CRT (Cathode Ray Tube)
desc: There are three forms of display CRTs in color television: tri-color (a color picture tube), monochrome (black and white), and single color (red, green, or blue, used in projection television systems). Many widescreen ATV schemes would require a different shape CRT, par- ticularly for direct-view systems.

name: CRT Terminal
desc: Computer terminal using a CRT display and a keyboard, usually connected to the computer by a serial link.

name: Crushing the Blacks
desc: The reduction of detail in the black regions of a film or video image by compressing the lower end of the contrast range.

name: CSA (Common Scrambling Algorithm)
desc: Scrambling algorithm specified by DVB. The Common Scrambling Algorithm was designed to minimize the likelihood of piracy attack over a long period of time. By using the Common Scrambling Algorithm system in conjunction with the standard MPEG2 Transport Stream and selection mechanisms, it is possible to incorporate in a transmission the means to carry multiple messages which all enable control of the same scrambled broadcast but are generated by a number of Conditional Access Systems.

name: CS (Carrier Suppression)
desc: This is the result of an unwanted coherent signal added to the center carrier of the COFDM signal. It could be produced from the DC offset voltages or crosstalk.

name: CSC (Computer Support Collaboration)
desc: Describes computers that enhance productivity for people working in groups. Application examples include video conferencing, video mail, and shared workspaces.

name: CSDI
desc: See: Compressed Serial Digital Interface.

name: CSELT (Centro Studi e Laboratori Telecomunicazioni S.p.A.)
desc: CSELT situated in Torino, Italy, is the research company owned by STET

name: CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry)
desc: In CSG, solid objects are represented as Boolean combinations (union, intersection and difference) of solids.

name: Csi
desc: Compact Source Iodide: A Type Of Discharge Lamp, Usually Used In Follow-Spots, Which, Although Very Small (Often No Bigger Than A Finger Nail), Gives A Very Bright, White Light. A 2K Csi Lamp Will Give The Same Power As A 5K Tungsten. These Are, Needless To Say, Very Expensive.

name: CSMA/CD
desc: Carrier Sense Multiple Access/Collision Detection. The Ethernet Media Access Control method where devices check if the network is quiet before transmitting and back off randomly after a collision.

name: CS-Mount
desc: A newer standard for lens mounting. It uses the same physi- cal thread as the C-mount, but the back flange-to-CCD distance is reduced to 12.5 mm in order to have the lenses made smaller, more compact and less expensive. CS-mount lenses can only be used on CS-mount cameras.

name: CSPS
desc: See Constrained System Parameter Stream.

name: CSS (Content Scrambling System)
desc: A type of digital copy protection sanctioned by the DVD forum.

name: C-Stand
desc: See Century Stand

name: CS-to-C-Mount Adaptor
desc: An adaptor used to convert a CS-mount cam- era to C-mount to accommodate a C-mount lens. It looks like a ring 5 mm thick, with a male thread on one side and a female on the other, with 1” diameter and 32 threads/inch. It usually comes packaged with the newer type (CS-mount) of cameras.

name: CSV (Comma Separated Variables)
desc: Commonly used no-frills text file format used for import from and import to spreadsheets and SQL databases.

name: CTA (Cordless Terminal Adapter)
desc: Provides the interface between the subscriber line on a hook-up site and the DBS (Direct Broadcast Satellite). The CTA offers subscribers a range of services equivalent or better quality than a wired connection. The CTA offers the option of more advanced services, such as high-speed V.90 Internet access, and thus provide a supplementary income source.

name: CTS
desc: Certified Technology Specialist. An AV professional with verified expertise through practical experience and ICIA industry training programs.

name: Cucoloris
desc: Also called a cookie, cookaloris, or coo-koo. A board or screen with cut-out patterns placed in front of a light to create shadow patterns on a surface.

name: Cue
desc: An Instruction Given By The Stage Manager To One Of The Technical Departments To Take Some Action; E.G. Lx Cue 7 Is The Seventh Instruction In The Play To The Lighting Department. Also Used In The Sense Of The Point At Which An Actor Must Enter Or Speak.

name: Cue bus
desc: On a mixing console, the cue bus routes soloed signals to the engineer's headphones for monitoring. PFL and AFL signals are routed to the cue bus.

name: cue cards
desc: a device ( cards , scrolling screen , teleprompter , or othermechanism ) printed with dialogue provided to help an actorrecite his / her lines ; an electronic cue card is called a ( tele )prompter ; derogatively calledidiot cards or idiot sheets .

name: Cue Channel
desc: A dedicated track for sync pulses or timecode.

name: Cue Conflict
desc: A Theory To Explain A Kind Of Motion Sickness Caused When The Body Tries To Interpret Conflicting Clues Being Received By Different Senses. Frequent Causes Are Faulty Calibration Of Eye Devices Or Delay Between The Sensory Inputs And Output Display.

name: Cue Control
desc: A switch that temporarily disables a recorder’s Tape Lifters during fast forward and rewind so the operator can judge what portion of the recording is passing the heads.

name: Cue flag
desc: In Eos, a marker applied to a cue that modifies its behavior. Flags include: M (Mark), B (Block), A (Assert), P (Preheat), AF (AllFade), L (Live move), D (Dark move). Displayed in the PSD flags column.

name: Cueing
desc: In Broadcast, Stage And Post-Production Work, To “Cue Up” A Sound Source (A Record, A Sound Effect On A Cd, A Song On A Tape) Means To Get It Ready For Playback By Making Sure You Are In The Right Position On The “Cue,” Making Sure The Level And Eq Are All Set Properly. This Requires A Special Monitoring Circuit That Only The Mixing Engineer Hears. It Does Not Go Out On The Air Or To The Main Mixing Buses. This “Cueing” Circuit Is The Same As Pre-Fader (Pfl) Solo On A Mackie Mixer, And Often The Terms Are Interchangeable.

name: Cue Light
desc: A Light Used To Signal A Cue. Red Usually Means Stand By And Green Usually Means Execute The Cue.

name: Cue list (Eos)
desc: In Eos Family consoles, a cue list is the primary playback object containing a sequential list of numbered cues. Multiple cue lists can run simultaneously on different playback faders.

name: Cue list (general)
desc: A linked sequence of cues programmed onto a playback, used to store and replay an entire show or element of a show from a go button. Also called a cue stack, stack, or sequence depending on the console brand.

name: Cuelist (Hog)
desc: On Hog consoles, a cuelist is a group of cues that run in a specific order, either automatically linked as a chase or manually triggered. Cuelists are run from masters.

name: Cue Mark
desc: Marks used to indicate frames of interest on a clip.

name: Cue number
desc: A numerical identifier for each cue in a cue list or cue stack. Most consoles support whole numbers (Cue 1, 2, 3) and point cues (Cue 2.5) inserted between existing cues.

name: Cue Only
desc: An Eos recording mode where changes to a cue are applied only to that cue and do not track forward into subsequent cues. The opposite of the default tracking behavior. Activated with the [Cue Only/Track] button.

name: Cue Only/Track
desc: A toggle on Eos Family consoles allowing the operator to switch between recording in Cue Only mode (changes don't track forward) and Track mode (changes track forward through the cue list).

name: Cue (Q)
desc: 1) An Event In A Production That Is The Signal For A Specific Action. 2) The Signal Given In Order To Cause Such An Action. 3) The Response To Such A Signal, Which May Include A Change In Intensity Settings For A Luminaire(S), Or A Change In Action By An Apparatus(Es).

name: Cue (show)
desc: 1) A command given to a technical department to carry out a specific operation (Lighting Cue, Sound Cue, Fly Cue). 2) Any signal (spoken line or action) indicating another action should follow.

name: Cue Stack (MagicQ)
desc: In MagicQ, a cue stack is a sequence of cues managed by a playback. Cue stacks can run as a theatre stack (manually triggered), a chase (auto-stepping), or a variety of other modes.

name: Cue view
desc: A display showing all parameter values stored within a specific cue, allowing the operator to inspect and edit individual channel or parameter values directly.

name: Culling
desc: Removing Invisible Pieces Of Geometry And Only Sending Potentially Visible Geometry To The Graphics Subsystem. Simple Culling Rejects Entire Objects Not In The View. More Complex Systems Take Into Account Occlusion Of Some Objects By Others, E.G. A Building Hiding Trees Behind It.

name: cult film ( s )
desc: usually a non - mainstream film that attracts a small , butloyally - obsessed group of fans , and remains popular andworshipped over many years ; cult films have limited butspecial appeal , and often have unusual or subversiveelements or subject matter ; they are often replayed for repeatviewings and audience participation ( and group identification )as midnight movies ; not to be confused withB - films ( not allcult films are B - films )

name: Cupping
desc: Curvature of a tape in the lateral direction. Cupping may occur because of improper drying or curing of the coating or because of differ- ences between the coefficients of thermal or hygroscopic expansion of coating and base film.

name: Curfew
desc: When The Show Must Be Done By. This Is Often Dictated By Local Noise Ordinances Or Labor Concerns. A "Hard Curfew" Is One Where There Is Some Penalty For Exceeding The Time, And A "Soft Curfew" Isn'T As Strict.

name: Curing Oven
desc: A specialized oven used to thermally cure epoxy for adhering a fiber optic connector ferrule onto an optical fiber.

name: Curl
desc: A defect of a photographic film consisting of unflatness in a plane cutting across the width of the film. Curl may result from improper drying conditions, and the direction and amount of curl may vary with the humidity of the air to which the film is exposed.

name: Current
desc: The flow of electrons.

name: Current Tracer
desc: Handheld troubleshooting tool used to detect current flow in logic circuits.

name: Current Working Directory
desc: The directory within the file system in which you are currently located when you are working in a shell window.

name: Cursor
desc: a) The small arrow on the screen that echoes the movements of the mouse. It changes shape depending on its location on the screen. b) An indicator on a screen that can be moved to highlight a particular function or control which is the current parameter now under adjustment or selected.

name: Curtain
desc: A movable drape used to conceal all or part of the stage. Short for the main curtain which rises at the start of a performance. By extension, the start of a performance ('What time is curtain?').

name: Curtain Call
desc: Taking A Bow In Front Of The Audience At The End Of A Show. Usually Abbreviated To "Curtain".

name: Curvature Error
desc: A change in track shape that results in a bowed or S-shaped track. This becomes a problem if the playback head is not able to follow the track closely enough to capture the information.

name: Curve
desc: A single continuous line with continuity of tangent vector and of curvature. It is defined by its type, degree, and rational feature.

name: Curved screen
desc: A slightly concave projection screen for improved screen gain. Typically has gain greater than 1 but narrower viewing angles than flat screens.

name: Curve (Eos)
desc: In Eos, a curve modifies how a fade happens over time — for example, a square law curve, a logarithmic curve, or a custom S-curve. Curves can be applied to intensity or non-intensity parameters to change their fade behavior.

name: Curves Graph
desc: An X, Y graph that plots input color values on the hori- zontal axis and output color values on the vertical axis. Used in the Color Correction Tool as a control for changing the relationship between input and output color values.

name: Cusp
desc: Breakpoints on curves.

name: Cut
desc: 1) To Remove Illumination From A Scene Or Subject. 2) To Block A Portion Of A Light Beam.

name: Cutaway
desc: Shot Is The Interruption Of A Continuously Filmed Action By Inserting A View Of Something Else. It Is Usually, Although Not Always, Followed By A Cut Back To The First Shot, When The Cutaway Avoids A Jump Cut. Coverage Is The Amount Of Footage Shot And Different Camera Angles Used To Capture A Scene. When In The Post­Production Process, The More Camera Coverage Means That There Is More Footage For The Film Editor To Work With In Assembling The Final Cut.

name: cutaway shot
desc: a brief shot that momentarily interrupts a continuously - filmedaction , by briefly inserting another related action , object , orperson ( sometimes not part of the principal scene or mainaction ) , followed by a cutback to the original shot ; often filmedfrom the POV of the character and used to break up asequence and provide some visual relief , or to ease thetransition from one shot to the next , or to provide additionalinformation , or to hint at an impending change ; reactionshots are usually cutaways ; cross - cutting is a series ofcutaways and cutbacks indicating concurrent action ; acutaway is different from an insert shot .

name: Cut List
desc: A series of output lists containing specifications used to conform the film work print or negative. See also Dupe List.

name: Cutoff
desc: A General Term For Anything Used To Block A Portion Of A Light Beam, E.G., Flags, Cutters, Shutters, Barn Doors, Etc.

name: Cut-Off Frequency
desc: That frequency beyond which no appreciable energy is transmitted. It may refer to either an upper or lower limit of a frequency band.

name: Cutoff Wavelength
desc: In singlemode optical fiber, the wavelength below which the fiber transmits as multimode instead of singlemode.

name: cut( or cutting )
desc: an abrupt or sudden change or jump in camera angle ,location , placement , or time , from one shot to another ;consists of a transition from one scene to another ( a visualcut ) or from one soundtrack to another ( a soundcut ) ; cutting refers to the selection , splicing and assembly bythe film editor of the various shots or sequences for a reel offilm , and the process of shortening a scene ; also refers to theinstructional word ' cut ' said at the end of a take by the directorto stop the action in front of the camera ; cut to refers to thepoint at which one shot or scene is changed immediately toanother ; also refers to a complete edited version of a film( e.g. , rough cut ) ; also see director's cut ; various types of cutsinclude invisible cut , smooth cut , jump cut ( an abrupt cut fromone scene or shot to the next ) , shock cut ( the abruptreplacement of one image by another ) , etc.

name: Cut-Out
desc: A Free-Standing Piece Of Scenery, E.G.A Tree, Cut Out Of Board Into The Correct Shape And Painted.

name: Cutscene
desc: A Non - Interactive Animation Sequence In An Otherwise Interactive Experience . Also Called Acinematic .

name: Cut Sheet
desc: Also Known As Data Sheet; A Paper, Pamphlet Or Leaflet That Has Detailed Information About A Lamp, Luminaire, Piece Of Equipment, Etc., Usually Supplied By The Manufacturer.

name: Cuts Only
desc: Transition limited to on/off or instantaneous transition-type edits; a basic editing process with limited capabilities.

name: Cutting
desc: The selection and assembly of the various scenes or sequences of a reel of film.

name: Cutting Head
desc: A transducer used to convert electrical signals into hills and valleys in the sides of record grooves.

name: Cutting plane
desc: An imaginary plane in technical drawings indicating what an object would look like if cut in half at that location. Establishes which elements are shown or hidden in ground plans and section drawings.

name: CVBS (Color Video Blanking and Sync)
desc: Another term for Composite Video.

name: CVBS (Composite Video Bar Signal)
desc: In broadcast television, this refers to the video signal, including the color information and syncs.

name: CVC
desc: See Compatible Video Consortium.

name: CVCT
desc: See Cable Virtual Channel Table.

name: CVT
desc: Coordinated Video Timings. A VESA standard defining signal timing information for computer-video signals using a formula based on blanking intervals, frequencies, and sync polarity. Superseded GTF.

name: CW (Continuous Wave)
desc: Refers to a separate subcarrier sine wave used for synchronization of the chrominance information.

name: CWDM
desc: Coarse Wavelength Division Multiplexing. Combining several wavelengths into a single optical signal with 20 nm separation between wavelengths. Distinguished from DWDM by its wider wavelength spacing.

name: C-Weighting - A Frequency
desc: Curve Designed To Mimic The Relative Sensitivity Of The Human Ear To Different Frequencies At High Sound Pressure Levels. In Practice, The Filter Rolls-Off The Low Frequencies Below About 20Hz And The Highs Above About 10Khz. This Filtering Is Often Used When Making Measurements Of High-Level Sounds, Such As When Calibrating Loudspeaker Reference Levels.

name: CX Noise Reduction
desc: This is a level sensitive audio noise reduction scheme that involves compression, on the encode side, and expansion, on the decode side. It was originally developed for CBS for noise reduction on LP records and is a trademark of CBS, Inc. The noise reduction obtained by CX was to be better than Dolby B3 for tape, but remain unnoticeable in playback if decoding didn’t take place. A modified CX system was applied to the analog audio tracks for the laserdisc to compensate for interference between the audio and video carriers. The original CX system for LP records was never implemented.

name: cyberpunk
desc: a sub - genre of science fiction , derived from combining thetermscybernetics and punk , and related to the digital orinformation technology society ( referring to the proliferation ofcomputers , the online world , cyberspace , and ' hacking ' ) ; thissub - genre also incorporates classic film - noirish characteristicsinto its style - traits include alienation , dehumanization , thepresence of counter - cultural anti - heroes , darkness , dystopia ,and corruption ; heavily influenced by the novels of RaymondChandler ; also associated with the work of writer WilliamGibson and his 1984 novel Neuromancer

name: Cyber Space
desc: A Computer Synthesized Reality. Often A Computer Synthesized 3-D Space. See Also Virtual Reality.

name: Cyborg
desc: A Robotic Humanoid Modeled Directly From Digital Readings Of A Real Human And Transformed Into A Photo Realistic, Animated Character Produced Via Illusionary Metamorphosis.

name: Cyc
desc: See Cyclorama

name: Cycle
desc: An alternation of a waveform which begins at a point, passes through the zero line and ends at a point with the same value and moving in the same direction as the starting point.

name: Cycle Per Second
desc: A measure of frequency, equivalent to Hertz.

name: Cycles Per Second
desc: Or Cps, The Number Of Times A Sound Wave Completes A Full Positive/Negative Cycle Within One Second, Synonymous With Hertz.

name: Cycles Per Second (Cps)
desc: One Cycle Is The Transition Of A Sine Wave From 0 To Positive Crest Down Through 0 To The Negative Crst And Back To 0. One Full Cycle Is Shown In This Graph. Cycles Per Second Refers To The Number Of Times A Full Cycle Is Repeated In The Period Of One Second. Cycles Per Second Is Usually Expressed As Hertz (Hz). One Hertz Is Equal To One Cycle Per Second.

name: Cycle Time
desc: Total time required by a memory device to complete a read or write cycle and become available again.

name: Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)
desc: a) Used to generate check informa- tion on blocks of data. Similar to a checksum, but is harder to generate and more reliable. b) Used in data transfer to check if the data has been corrupted. It is a check value calculated for a data stream by feeding it through a shifter with feedback terms “EXORed” back in. A CRC can detect errors but not repair them, unlike an ECC, which is attached to almost any burst of data that might possibly be corrupted. CRCs are used on disks, ITU-R 601 data, Ethernet packets, etc. c) Error detection using a parity check.

name: Cyc Light
desc: See Cyclorama Light

name: cyclorama
desc: the curved backdrop used to represent the sky when outdoorscenes are shot in the studio

name: Cyclorama (Cyc)
desc: A Vertical Surface Which Is Used To Form The Background For A Theatrical Type Setting, Usually Made Of Heavy Cloth Drawn Tight To Achieve A Smooth, Flat Surface. It Usually Represents The Sky Or Suggests Limitless Space. Traditionally, Cycloramas Were Dome Shaped Or Horizontally Curved, But May Now Also Be Flat Or Vertically Curved As Well.

name: Cyclorama Light (Cyc Light)
desc: A Luminaire Mounted At The Top And/Or Bottom Of A Cyclorama In Order To Light It In A Smooth, Uniform Manner.

name: D1
desc: A non-compressed component digital video recording format that uses data conforming to the ITU-R BT.601-2 standard. Records on high end 19 mm (3/4”) magnetic tape recorders. Systems manufactured by Sony and BTS. Most models can record 525, 625, ITU-R BT.601-2 and SMPTE 125M. The D1 designation is often used in-correctly to indicate component digital video.

name: D16
desc: A Recording Format For Digital Film Images Making Use Of Standard D1 Recorders. The Scheme Was Developed Specifically To Handle Quantel'S Domino (Digital Opticals For Movies) Pictures And Record Them Over The Space That Sixteen 625 Line Digital Pictures Would Occupy. This Way Three Film Frames Can Be Recorded Or Played Every Two Seconds. Playing The Recorder Allows The Film Images To Be Viewed On A Standard Monitor; Running At 16X Speed Shows Full Motion Direct From The Tape.

name: D2
desc: A non-compressed composite digital video record-ing format original- ly developed by Ampex that uses data conforming to SMPTE 244M and four 20 bit audio channels. Records on high end 19 mm (3/4”) magnetic tape recorders. It uses the same tape cassette cartridge but the tape itself is metal particle tape like Beta SP and MII. The D2 designation is often used incorrectly to indicate composite digital video.

name: D2-MAC
desc: Similar to D-MAC, the form preferred by manufacturers for European DBS. See also MAC.

name: D3
desc: A Composite Digital Video Recording Format That Uses Data Conforming To Smpte 244M. Uses 1/2-Inch Tape Cassettes For Recording Digitized Composite (Coded) Pal Or Ntsc Signals Sampled At 8 Bits. Cassettes Are Available For 50 To 245 Minutes. Since This Uses A Composite Signal The Characteristics Are Generally As For D2 Except That The 1/2-Inch Cassette Size Has Allowed A Full Family Of Vtr Equipment To Be Realized In One Format, Including A Camcorder.

name: D4
desc: A Format Designation Never Utilized Due To The Fact That The Number Four Is Considered Unlucky (Being Synonymous With Death In Some Asian Languages).

name: D5
desc: A Vtr Format Using The Same Cassette As D3 But Recording Component Signals Conforming To The Itu-R Bt.601-2 (Ccir 601) Recommendations At 10-Bit Resolution. With Internal Decoding D5 Vtrs Can Play Back D3 Tapes And Provide Component Outputs. Being A Non-Compressed Component Digital Video Recorder Means D5 Enjoys All The Performance Benefits Of D1, Making It Suitable For High-End Post Production As Well As More General Studio Use. Besides Servicing The Current 625 And 525 Line Tv Standards The Format Also Has Provision For Hdtv Recording By Use Of About 4:1 Compression (Hd D5). See Also: Hd D5.

name: D6
desc: A digital tape format which uses a 19 mm helical-scan cassette tape to record uncompressed high definition television material at 1.88 GBps (1.2 Gbps).

name: D65
desc: Reference white point for BT.709, DCI-P3, and BT.2020 color gamuts. Corresponds to 6500K color temperature with CIE chromaticity coordinates xW=0.3127, yW=0.329.

name: D7
desc: Dvcpro. Panasonic'S Development Of Native Dv Component Format Which Records A 18 Micron (18X10-6M, Eighteen Thousandths Of A Millimeter) Track On 6.35 Mm (0.25-Inch) Metal Particle Tape. Dvcpro Uses Native Dct-Based Dv Compression At  5:1 From A 4:1:1 8-Bit Sampled Source. It Uses 10 Tracks Per Frame For 525/60 Sources And 12 Tracks Per Frame For 625/50 Sources, Both Use 4:1:1 Sampling. Tape Speed Is 33.813Mm/S. It Includes Two 16-Bit Digital Audio Channels Sampled At 48 Khz And An Analog Cue Track. Both Linear (Ltc) And Vertical Interval Time Code (Vitc) Are Supported. There Is A 4:2:2 (Dvcpro50) And Progressive Scan 4:2:0 (Dvcpro P) Version Of The Format, As Well As A High Definition Version (Dvcprohd). See Also: Dvcpro50, Dvcprohd, Dvcpro P.

name: D8
desc: There Is No D8. The Television Recording And Reproduction Technology Committee Of Smpte Decided To Skip D8 Because Of The Possibility Of Confusion With Similarly Named Digital Audio Or Data Recorders (Da-88).

name: D9
desc: Digital-S. A 1/2-inch digital tape format developed by JVC which uses a high-density metal particle tape running at 57.8 mm/s to record a video data rate of 50 Mbps.

name: D9 (Formerly Digital-S)
desc: A 1/2-Inch Digital Tape Format Developed By Jvc Which Uses A High- Density Metal Particle Tape Running At 57.8Mm/S To Record A Video Data Rate Of 50 Mbps. The Tape Can Be Shuttled And Search Up To 32X Speed. Video Sampled At  4:2:2 Is Compressed At 3.3:1 Using Dct-Based Intra-Frame Compression (Dv). Two Or Four Audio Channels Are Recorded At 16-Bit, 48 Khz Sampling; Each Is Individually Editable. The Format Also Includes Two Cue Tracks. Some Machines Can Play Back Analog S-Vhs. D9 Hd Is The High Definition Version Recording At 100 Mbps.

name: D9 HD
desc: A High Definition Digital Component Format Based On D9. Records On 1/2-Inch Tape With 100 Mbps Video.

name: DA
desc: Distribution Amplifier. Takes one signal input and drives multiple buffered outputs simultaneously while maintaining signal integrity. Used to split video, audio, or control signals to multiple destinations.

name: DA-88
desc: A Tascam-Brand Eight Track Digital Audio Tape Machine Using The 8 Mm Video Format Of Sony. It Has Become The De Facto Standard For Audio Post Production Though There Are Numerous Other Formats, Ranging From Swappable Hard Drives To Analog Tape Formats And Everything In Between.

name: DAB
desc: See Digital Audio Broadcasting.

name: DAC
desc: Digital to analog converter.

name: DAC (D-A, D/A, D-to-A)
desc: Digital-To-Analog Converter.

name: DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)
desc: A device in which signals having a few (usually two) defined levels or states (digital) are converted into signals having a theoretically infinite number of states (analog).

name: D/A Converter
desc: Digital-To-Analog Converter

name: D/A Converter (Dac)
desc: Digital-To-Analog Converter, A Device That Trans_x0002_Forms Incoming Digital Signals Into Analog Form.

name: DAC to DAC Skew
desc: The difference in a full scale transition between R, B and B DAC outputs measured at the 50% transition point. Skew is meas- ured in tenths of nanoseconds.

name: DAE (Digidesign Audio Engine)
desc: A trademark of Avid Technology, Inc. The application that manages the AudioSuite plug-ins.

name: DAE (Digital Audio Extraction)
desc: Reading digital audio data directly from a CD audio disc.

name: DAI (DMIF Application Interface)
desc: The bridge between DMIF (delivery multimedia integration framework) and MPEG-4 systems.

name: dailies
desc: the immediately processed , rough cuts , exposed film , or firstprints of a film ( w / o special effects or edits ) for the director( producer , cinematographer , or editor ) to review , to see howthe film came out after the day's ( or previous day's ) shooting ;more commonly in the form of videotape or digital dailiesnowadays ; aka rushes ( referring to the haste taken to makethem available ) ; used to determine ifcontinuity is correct , ifprops are missing or out of place , or if sound is poor , etc. , tohelp decide whether to re - shoot

name: Daisy Chain
desc: Bus line that is interconnected with units so that the signal passes from one unit to the next in serial fashion.

name: DALI
desc: Digital Addressable Lighting Interface. IEC 62386. A two-wire digital control protocol for architectural and building lighting. Each DALI device has a unique address (up to 64 per loop) allowing individual or group control of dimmers, ballasts, and LED drivers.

name: DALI-2
desc: The second generation of the DALI standard (IEC 62386 Parts 200+). Adds mandatory interoperability testing, support for additional device types (LED drivers, sensors, occupancy detectors), and improved multi-manufacturer compatibility.

name: DAM (DECT Authentication Module)
desc: a) An IC card used for cordless telecommunications. b) A smart card that makes billing more secure and prevents fraud. The DAM is reminiscent of the subscriber identity module (SIM) card in the GSM standard.

name: Damped Oscillation
desc: Oscillation which, because the driving force has been removed, gradually dies out, each swing being smaller than the preceding in smooth regular decay.

name: Damping
desc: Damping Factor Is A Number That Represents The Ratio Of The Impedance Of The Load To The Out_x0002_Put Impedance Of The Amplifier. In Practical Terms, It Is A Measure Of How Well The Amplifier Can Control The Movement Of A Speaker'S Cone. The Greater The Damping Factor, The Better Its Ability To Control The Cone'S Movement. A Low Damping Factor (High Amplifier Output Imped_x0002_Ance) Allows A Woofer To Continue To Move After The Signal Stops, Resulting In An Indistinct And Mushy Low Frequency Response. A High Damp_x0002_Ing Factor (200 Or Above) Provides Excellent Control Over Low Frequency Woofers And Pro_x0002_Duces A Tight, Clean Bas

name: Damping factor
desc: The measurement of a power amplifier's ability to control the motion of a speaker's cone after a signal disappears. Higher number = better damping.

name: Dance captain
desc: The member of the performing company responsible for maintaining the choreography standards during a run, leading warm-ups, and rehearsing replacements.

name: Dante
desc: Audio-over-IP protocol by Audinate for distributing uncompressed, low-latency digital audio over standard Ethernet. Supports up to 512 bidirectional audio channels at 48kHz/24-bit. Widely used in installed AV and live sound.

name: Dante Controller
desc: Free software from Audinate for discovering, routing, and managing audio connections between Dante-enabled devices on a local network.

name: Dante Domain Manager
desc: A server-based application from Audinate for controlling, monitoring, and securing Dante audio networks at scale across multiple subnets and locations.

name: DAR
desc: Data at Rest. All data in storage excluding data that frequently traverses the network or resides in temporary memory.

name: Dark
desc: A theatre or show that is not currently in production. 'The theatre is dark on Mondays'.

name: Dark Current
desc: Leakage signal from a CCD sensor in the absence of incident light.

name: Dark Fiber
desc: In fiber optics, fiber that is installed at a facility but reserved for future use.

name: dark horse
desc: in film terms , a little - known , unlikely movie ( often a sleeper , alow - budget film , indie , or a foreign film ) that is , surprisingly ,nominated for a major award ( i.e. , Academy Award or GoldenGlobe )

name: Dark move
desc: In Eos, a non-intensity parameter change (color, position, gobo) that occurs on a fixture while it is at zero intensity. Indicated by a 'D' flag in the PSD. Used to pre-position fixtures invisibly.

name: Dark Noise
desc: Noise caused by the random (quantum) nature of the dark current.

name: DASH
desc: Dynamic Adaptive Streaming over HTTP. Also called MPEG-DASH. An ISO/IEC international standard for adaptive bitrate streaming. Codec-agnostic and royalty-free. Used by YouTube, Netflix, and many OTT platforms.

name: Dat
desc: Sound Recording Term: Digital Audio Tape. A Very High Quality Audio Recording Method In Which Sound Is Digitally Recorded On Tape (Often Video S-Vhs Tapes Are Used).

name: Data
desc: General term denoting any or all facts, numbers, letters, and symbols or facts that refer to or describe an object, idea, condition, situa- tion or other factors. Connotes basic elements of information that can be processed or produced by a computer. Sometimes data is considered to be expressible only in numerical form, but information is not so limited.

name: Data Acquisition
desc: Collection of data from external sensors usually in analog form.

name: Data Area
desc: The physical area of a DVD disc between the lead in and the lead out (or middle area) which contains the stored data content of the disc.

name: Data Base
desc: Systematic organization of data files for easy access, retrieval, and updating.

name: Data Bus
desc: Set of lines carrying data. The data bus is usually bidirectional and three-state.

name: Data Capture
desc: Data Capture Is A Vfx Department Which Exists To Capture Important Details During Principle Photography.

name: Data Carousels
desc: The data broadcast specification for data carousels supports data broadcast services that require the periodic transmission of data modules through DVB compliant broadcast networks. The modules are of known sizes and may be updated, added to, or removed from the data carousel in time. Modules can be clustered into a group of modules if required by the service. Likewise, groups can in turn be clustered into SuperGroups. Data broadcast according to the data carousel specification is transmitted in a DSM-CC data carousel which is defined in MPEG-2 DSM-CC. This specification defines additional structures and descriptors to be used in DV compliant networks. The method is such that no explicit references are made to PIDs and timing parameters enabling preparation of the content off-line.

name: Datacasting
desc: Digital television allows for the transmission of not only digital sound and images, but also digital data (text, graphics, maps, services, etc.). This aspect of DTV is the least developed; but in the near future, applications will likely include interactive program guides, sports statistics, stock quotes, retail ordering information, and the like.Datacasting is not two-way, although most industry experts expect that set-top box manufacturers will create methods for interaction. By integrating dial-up Internet connections with the technology, simple responses will be possible using a modem and either an add-on keyboard or the set-tops remote control.

name: Data Circuit-Terminating Equipment (DCE)
desc: Equipment at a node or access point of a network that interfaces between the data terminal equip- ment (DTE) and the channel. For example, a modem.

name: Data compression
desc: A Technique That Provides For The Transmission Or Storage, Without Noticeable Information Loss, Of Fewer Data Bits Than Were Originally Used When The Data Was Created.

name: Data Compression Ratio
desc: The ratio of data output from a compression system relative to the original data, quantifying the reduction in data-representation size.

name: Data Conferencing
desc: Sharing of computer data by remote participants by application sharing or shared white board technologies.

name: Data Domain
desc: Analysis or display of signals in which only their digital value is considered and not their precise voltage or timing. A logic state analyzer displays information in the data domain.

name: Data Element
desc: An item of data as represented before encoding and after decoding.

name: Data Encryption Standard (DES)
desc: A national standard used in the U.S. for the encryption of digital information using keys. It provides privacy protection but not security protection.

name: Data Essence
desc: a) Essence that is distinguished as different from video or audio essence. Digital data that may stand alone or may be associated with video or audio essence or metadata. b) Refers to the bits and bytes of new forms of content, such as interactive TV-specific content, Advanced Television Enhancement Forum (ATVEF) content (SMPTE 363M), closed captions.

name: Data Glove
desc: A Story-Driven Virtual Reality Experience.

name: Data Link
desc: A fiber optic system comprising the cable, transmitter, and receiver for data transmission between two locations.

name: Data Manager
desc: A Crewmember Who Manages On - Set Data , Which Can Include Camera Telemetry , Footage ,Tracking Telemetry , Real - Time Scene , Metadata , Etc. Also Referred To As Data Wrangler .

name: Data Partitioning
desc: A method for dividing a bit stream into two separate bit streams for error resilience purposes. The two bit streams have to be recombined before decoding.

name: Data Piping
