desc: A Device That Enables A Musical Instrument To Be Connected Directly To A Mic- Or Line-Level Mixer Input.

name: Direct Broadcast Satellite (DBS)
desc: a) A distribution scheme involving transmission of signals directly from satellites to homes. It does not carry the burden of terrestrial broadcasting’s restricted bandwidth and regula- tions and so is thought by many to be an ideal mechanism for the intro- duction of high base bandwidth ATV. DBS is the most effective delivery mechanism for reaching most rural areas; it is relatively poor in urban areas and in mountainous terrain, particularly in the north. Depending on frequency band used, it can be affected by factors such as rain. b) Multiple television channel programming service that is transmitted direct from high powered satellites, directly to a home receiving dish.

name: Direct Color
desc: An SVGA mode for which each pixel color value is specified directly by the contents of a bit field.

name: Direct Current (Dc)
desc: An Electrical Current That Maintains Constant Direction.

name: Direct Current Voltage
desc: A Voltage That Maintains Constant Polarity.

name: Direct Digital Interface
desc: The interconnection of compatible pieces of digital audio or video equipment without conversion of the signal to an ana- log form.

name: Direct Draw Overlay
desc: This is a feature that lets you see the video full screen and full motion on your computer screen while editing. Most new 3D graphics cards support this. If yours does not, it simply means you will need an external monitor to view the video. Direct Draw Overlay has absolutely nothing to do with your final video quality.

name: Direct Glare
desc: Glare Resulting From Very Bright Sources Of Light In The Field Of View. It Usually Is Associated  With Bright Light From Luminaires And Windows.  A  Direct Glare Source May Also Affect Performance By Reducing The Apparent  Contrast Of Objects In The Field Of View, Especially  Those Near The Source Of Light.

name: directing the eye
desc: in cinematographic terms , using light and dark lighting andframe composition to emphasize what is important

name: Direct Input Box (Di)
desc: An Electronic Device Used To Convert High Impedance Unbalanced Signal To Low Impedance Balanced Microphone Level.

name: Directional
desc: Sound Reflected From Surfaces Behind A Directional Micro- Phone’S Diaphragm Is Permitted To Be Incident On The Rear Side Of The Diaphragm. Since The Sound Reaching The Rear Of The Diaphragm Travels Slightly Farther Than The Sound At The Front, It Is Slightly Out Of Phase. The Greater This Phase Difference, The Greater The Pressure Difference And The Greater The Diaphragm Movement. As The Sound Source Moves Off The Diaphragm Axis, This Phase Difference Decreases Due To Decreasing Path Length Difference. This Is What Gives A Directional Microphone Its Directivity.

name: Directional Antenna
desc: An antenna that directs most of its signal strength in a specific direction rather than at equal strength in all directions.

name: Directional Audio
desc: Create Fields Of Sound Using An Array Of Small Speakers To Control The Direction Of Audio Output.

name: Directional  Lighting
desc: The Lighting Produced By Luminaires That Distribute All, Or Nearly All, Of The Light In One Direction.

name: Directional Luminaire
desc: A Luminaire That Provides Directional Lighting, Including Downlights, Accent Luminaires, And The Like.

name: Directional Microphone
desc: One whose sensitivity to sound varies with direction. Such microphones can be aimed so their most sensitive sides face the sound source, while their least sensitive sides face sources of noise or other undesired sound.

name: Directional Source
desc: Light that emanates from a constant direction with a constant intensity. This is called the infinite light source.

name: Direction Handle
desc: A line extending from a control point that controls the direction of a Bézier curve. Each control point has two direction handles. These two handles together affect how the curve passes through the con- trol point, with one handle controlling how the curve appears before the control point, and the other handle controlling how the curve appears after the control point.

name: Direct Lighting
desc: Illumination On A Subject Or Area That Goes Directly From The Front Of The Luminaire In A Straight Line To The Subject Or Area.

name: Direct Memory Access (DMA)
desc: Method of gaining direct access to main storage in order to perform data transfers without involving the CPU.

name: Director
desc: In Control Of All Aspects Of The Production.(S)He Develops The Concept Of The Production, Briefs The Designer And Lighting Designer, Plots The Actor'S Moves, Rehearses The Actors, Etc. Etc. Etc..

name: director( and directing )
desc: the creative artist responsible for complete artistic control ofall phases of a film's production ( such as making day - to - daydeterminations about sound , lighting , action , casting , evenediting ) , for translating / interpreting a script into a film , forguiding the performances of the actors in a particular roleand / or scene , and for supervising the cinematography andfilm crew . The director is usually the single person mostresponsible for the finished product , although he / she couldn'tmake a film without support from many other artists andtechnicians ; often the director is called a helmer ( at - the - helm ) ;the assistant director is known as the a.d .; the director ofphotography ( or cinematographer ) , responsible for themechanics of camera placement , movements , and lighting , isknown as the d.p.

name: director's cut
desc: a rough cut ( the first completely - edited version ) of a filmwithout studio interference as the director would like it to beviewed , before the final cut ( the last version of the film that isreleased ) is made by the studio .

name: Directory
desc: a) A container in the file system in which you store other directories and files. b) A logical or physical portion of a hard disk drive where the operating system stores files.

name: Direct out
desc: An output on a console channel strip that taps the signal directly from the channel for recording, providing the channel signal post-preamp (and sometimes post-EQ or post-fader) to a separate recording system.

name: Direct Recording
desc: A type of analog recording which records and repro- duces data in the electrical form of its source.

name: Direct selects
desc: On Eos Family consoles, a bank of touchscreen buttons providing one-touch access to channels, groups, palettes, presets, or macros without requiring command line entry. Fully customizable and labeled by the operator.

name: DirectShow
desc: The application programming interface (API) for client-side playback, transformation, and capture of a wide variety of data formats. DirectShow is the successor to Microsoft Video for Windows® and Microsoft ActiveMovie, significantly improving on these older technologies.

name: direct sound
desc: the technique of recording sound simultaneously with theimage

name: Direct to Disk
desc: A method of recording directly to the cutting head of the audio disk cutter, eliminating the magnetic recorder in the sequence, typified by no tape hiss.

name: Direct-View
desc: A CRT watched directly, as opposed to one projecting its image on a screen.

name: Dirty List (Dirty EDL)
desc: An edit decision list (EDL) containing overlapping or redundant edits. Contrast with Clean List (Clean EDL).

name: Disability Glare
desc: Glare Which Impairs The Vision Of Objects But May Not Cause Discomfort.

name: Disable
desc: Process of inhibiting a device function.

name: Disappearing Footlight
desc: A Footlight Mounted Into A Stage Floor, That When Closed, Has Its Lid Completely Flush With The Floor. The Lid Is Usually Made From The Same Material As The Stage Floor.

name: Disc Array
desc: Multiple hard disks formatted to work together as if they were part of a single hard drive. Disc arrays are typically used for high data rate video storage.

name: Discomfort Glare
desc: Glare Causing Discomfort Which May Not Impair The Ability To See Objects.

name: discovery shot
desc: in a film scene , when the moving or panning cameraunexpectedly comes upon or ' discovers ' an object or personpreviously undisclosed to the viewer

name: Discrete
desc: Having an individual identity. An individual circuit component.

name: Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)
desc: a) Used in JPEG and the MPEG, H.261, and H.263 video compression algorithms, DCT techniques allow images to be represented in the frequency rather than time domain. Images can be represented in the frequency domain using less information than in the time domain. b) A mathematical transform that can be perfectly undone and which is useful in image compression. c) Many encoders per- form a DCT on an eight-by-eight block of image data as the first step in the image compression process. The DCT converts the video data from the time domain into the frequency domain. The DCT takes each block, which is a 64-point discrete signal, and breaks it into 64 basis signals. The output of the operation is a set of 64 basis-signal amplitudes, called DCT coefficients. These coefficients are unique for each input signal. The DCT provides a basis for compression because most of the coefficients for a block will be zero (or close to zero) and do not need to be encoded.

name: Discrete Signals
desc: The sampling of a continuous signal for which the sample values are equidistant in time.

name: Discrete Surround Sound
desc: Audio in which each channel is stored and transmitted separate from and independent of other channels. Multiple independent channels directed to loudspeakers in front of and behind the listener allow precise control of the sound field in order to generate localized sounds and simulate moving sound sources.

name: Discrete Time Oscillator (DTO)
desc: Digital implementation of the voltage controlled oscillator.

name: Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)
desc: A transform converting an image from a spatial domain to a wavelet domain using high-pass and low-pass filters. Provides more efficient compression than DCT for signals with sharp discontinuities.

name: DIS (Draft International Standard)
desc: The last step before a fast-track document is approved as an International Standard. Note: The fast-track process is a different process than the normal development process. DIS documents are balloted and approved at the TC-level.

name: Dish
desc: A parabolic antenna used to receive satellite transmissions at home. The older “C band” dishes measure 7-12 feet in diameter, while the newer “Ku band” dishes used to receive high-powered DBS services can be as small as 18 inches in diameter.

name: Disk, Disc
desc: a) An information/digital data storage medium. b) A flat circular plate, coated with a magnetic material, on which data may be stored by selective magnetization of portions of the surface. May be a flexible, floppy disk or rigid hard disk. It could also be a plastic compact disc (CD) or digital video disc (DVD).

name: Disk Drive
desc: The machine used to record and retrieve digital information on disk.

name: Disk (Menus)
desc: Recall and Store enable effects to be stored, renamed and recalled on 3-1/2” disks in the disk drive provided with the system.

name: Disk Resource
desc: Any disk (hard, CD-ROM, or floppy) that you can access either because it is physically attached to your workstation with a cable, or it is available over the network.

name: Disk Use
desc: The percentage of space on your disk that contains informa- tion.

name: Disney - ficationorDisney - fied
desc: refers to the making of an adapted , sanitized , ' family - friendly 'version of a book or play , by removing objectionable elements( such as crude language , sexuality , or violence ) andmodifying plot elements to make the tale more acceptable ,entertaining , predictable and popular for mass consumptionby audiences , as first exercised by the Disney studios in the50s ; now used as a derogatory term for how popular culturehas been homogenized and cultural diversity has beenminimized ; see also bowdlerize ( d )

name: Dispersion
desc: Distribution of the oxide particles within the binder. A good dispersion can be defined as one in which equal numbers of particles would be found in equal, vanishingly small volumes sampled from different points within the coating.

name: Dispersion Shifted Fiber (DSF)
desc: A singlemode optical fiber with its optimal dispersion wavelength shifted (through dopants) to a wavelength delivering optimal attenuation.

name: Displacement Mapping
desc: The adding of a 3D effect to a 2D image.

name: Displacement of Porches
desc: Refers to any difference between the level of the front porch and the level of the back porch.

name: Display
desc: a) The ultimate image presented to a viewer; the process of presenting that image. b) CRT, LCD, LED or other photo luminescent panel upon which numbers, characters, graphics or other data is presented.

name: Display device
desc: Any output device for presenting information visually, including CRT, LED, or LCD panel. A general term for a projector or monitor.

name: DisplayID
desc: A second-generation VESA EDID standard released December 2007. A 256-byte data structure conveying display information to source devices, encompassing PC displays, consumer TVs, and embedded displays.

name: Display Order
desc: The order in which the decoded pictures are displayed. Normally this is the same order in which they were presented at the input of the encoder.

name: DisplayPort
desc: Digital display interface by VESA. Current version 2.1 supports up to 80 Gbps, enabling 16K resolution or multiple 4K displays. Supports daisy-chaining (MST), USB Power Delivery, and embedded audio.

name: Display Rate
desc: The number of times/sec the image in a video system is refreshed. Progressive scan systems such as film or HDTV change the image once per frame. Interlace scan systems such as standard TV change the image twice per frame, with two fields in each frame. Film has a frame rate of 24 fps but each frame is shown twice by the projector for a display rate of 48 fps. NTSC TV has a rate of 29.97 fps, PAL 25 fps.

name: Display setting
desc: An adjustment affecting the display of an image, including contrast, brightness, tint, blanking, size, and offsets.

name: Display Signal Processing
desc: An efficient, widely compatible system required that distribution be free of detailed requirements specific to dis- play, and that necessary additional display processing unique to that dis- play class be conducted only at the display. The variety of display systems, already numerous, continues to increase. Each system or variant has its own set of specifications, performance characteristics, and requirements, including electro-optic transfer function, color gamut, scanning sequence, etc. Display signal processing might include transformation at the display to the appropriate luminance range and chrominance, to display primaries and reference white, matrixing to achieve metameric color match, adapta- tion to surround, plus conversion to scanning progressive or scanning interlaced, etc. Display processing may not be required for transmission if there is unique point-to-point routing clearly identified and appropriate processing has been provided in distribution. But it is frequently required for emission to a diffuse population of display system.

name: Dissolve
desc: a) A process whereby one video signal is gradually faded out while a second image simultaneously replaces the original one. b) A video or audio transition in which an image from one source gradually becomes less distinct as an image from a second source replaces it. An audio dissolve is also called a segue. See also Crossfade, Fade.

name: dissolve( or lap dissolve )
desc: a transitional editing technique between two sequences , shotsor scenes , in which the visible image of one shot or scene isgradually replaced , superimposed or blended ( by anoverlapping fade out orfade in and dissolve ) with the imagefrom another shot or scene ; often used to suggest thepassage of time and to transform one scene to the next ; lapdissolve is shorthand for ' over'lap dissolve ; also known asa soft transition or dissolve to

name: Distance Learning
desc: Technologies that allow interactive remote site classes or training by use of multipoint or point-to-point connections.

name: Distant Miking
desc: Placing a mike far from a sound source so that a high proportion of reflected sound is picked up.

name: Distant Signal
desc: TV signals which originate at a point too far away to be picked up by ordinary home reception equipment; also signals defined by the FCC as outside a broadcaster’s license area. Cable systems are limited by FCC rules in the number of distant signals they can offer subscribers.

name: Distortion
desc: The Alteration Of The Original Shape (Or Other Characteristic) Of A Sound Wave Which Changes The Sound. Distortion Is Usually Unwanted, But It May Be Stylistically Desirable For Certain Instruments, Such As An Electric Guitar. The Three Principal Types Of Intentional Distortion Effects Are Overdrive, Distortion, And Fuzz.

name: Distributed Rendering
desc: Multiple Instances Of An Engine Processing The Same Scene In Parallel To Achieve A Muchhigher Total Resolution .

name: Distribution
desc: See Light Distribution.

name: Distribution Amplifier
desc: Device used to multiply (fan-out) a video signal. Typically, distribution amplifiers are used in duplication studios where many tape copies must be generated from one source or in multiple display setups where many monitors must carry the same picture, etc. May also include cable equalization and/or delay.

name: Distribution Cable
desc: Fiber optic cable comprising a bundle of jacketed fibers encased within an outside jacket.

name: Distribution Equipment
desc: Any Electrical Apparatus That Routes Electrical Current To Another Apparatus, Usually Luminaires.

name: Distribution Panel
desc: For fiber optics, a combined patch panel and splice panel usually installed at a hub or entrance facility.

name: Distribution quality
desc: The Level Of Quality Of A Television Signal From The Station To Its Viewers. For Digital Television This Is Approximately 19.39 Mbps.

name: DITEC
desc: See Digital Television Communications System.

name: Dither
desc: A Form Of Smart Conversion From A Higher Bit Depth To A Lower Bit Depth, Used In The Conversion Of Audio And Graphic Files. In The Conversion From 24-Bit Color To 8-Bit Color (Millions Of Colors Reduced To 256), The Process Attempts To Improve On The Quality Of On-Screen Graphics With Reduced Color Palettes By Adding Patterns Of Different Colored Pixels To Simulate The Original Color. The Technique Is Also Known As "Error Diffusion," And Is Applied To Audio Bit Rate Reduction And Graphic Resolution.

name: Dither Component Encoding
desc: A slight expansion of the analog signal levels so that the signal comes in contact with more quantizing levels. The results are smoother transitions. This is done by adding white noise (which is at the amplitude of one quantizing level) to the analog signal prior to sampling.

name: Dithering
desc: Giving the illusion of new color and shades by combining dots in various patterns. This is a common way of gaining gray scales and is commonly used in newspapers. The effects of dithering would not be optimal in the video produced during a videoconference.

name: Dither Pattern
desc: The matrix of color or gray-scale values used to repre- sent colors or gray shades in a display system with a limited color palette.

name: Diversity
desc: A Radio Reception Technique In Which Signal Is Obtained By Selecting The Stronger Of Two Independent Sources At Any Given Time.

name: DIVX
desc: A commercial and non-commercial video codec that enables high quality video at high compression rates.

name: DLP
desc: Digital Light Processing.  Texas Instrument’S™ Micro-Mirror Display Solution Technology That Uses A Reflective Optical Semiconductor To Manipulate Light Digitally.

name: DLP Cinema
desc: A Version Of Dlp Technology Specifically Developed For Digital Movie Presentation. It Contains Extended Color Management And Control, And Enhanced Contrast Performance.

name: DLT (Digital Linear Tape)
desc: a) A high capacity data tape format.b) A high-density tape storage medium (usually 10-20 gigabytes) used to transport and input data to master a DVD. Media is designated as “Type III” or “Type IV” for tapes used for DVD.

name: DMA
desc: See Direct Memory Access.

name: D-MAC
desc: Originally, a MAC (Multiplexed Analog Component) with audio and data frequency multiplexed after modulation, currently a term used in Europe to describe a family of B-MAC-like signals, one of which is the British choice for DBS. See also MAC.

name: DMD
desc: Digital Micro-Mirror Device.  Texas Instrument’S™ Micro-Mirror Display Solution Technology That Uses An Optical Semiconductor To Manipulate Light Digitally.

name: DMD (Digital Micro-Mirror Device)
desc: A new video projection technology that uses chips with a large number of miniature mirrors, whose projection angle can be controlled with digital precision.

name: DMI
desc: Dynamic Motion Interpolation. An Extron video processing technique using advanced motion prediction and compensation, treating motion and still content with different algorithms for high-fidelity images.

name: DMIF (Digital Storage Media-Command and Control Multimedia Integration Framework)
desc: In November 1996, a work item on DMIF (DSM-CC Multimedia Integration Framework) was accepted as part 6 of the MPEG-4 ISO/IEC 14496 work activity. DMIF extends the concepts in DSM-CC to symmetric conversational applications and the addition of Internet as a core network. These extensions are required to satisfy the needs of MPEG-4 applications.

name: DMK (Downstream Mixer-Keyer)
desc: See DSK.

name: DMM
desc: Digital Multimeter. A handheld test and measurement device combining tools for voltage, amperage, resistance, and other electrical measurements.

name: DM-M (Delayed Modulation Mark)
desc: Also called Miller Code.

name: D-Mode
desc: An edit decision list (EDL) in which all effects (dissolves, wipes, graphic overlays) are performed at the end. See also A-Mode, B-Mode,C-Mode, E-Mode, Source Mode.

name: DMT
desc: Display Monitor Timing. A VESA standard defining signal timing information for computer-video signals. Superseded by GTF.

name: Dmx
desc: Abbreviation For Digital Multiplex.

name: Dmx 512
desc: A Somewhat Unique Digital Multiplex Signal With Specific Characteristics That Is Commonly Used In The Stage And Studio Lighting Industries. Control Consoles Designed To Generate This Signal Were Originally Designed To Control A Maximum Of 512 Apparatuses, Usually Dimmers, But Now Can Control Many More.

name: DMX address
desc: A number between 1 and 512 that identifies a controllable parameter channel of a fixture within a DMX universe. Each fixture has a start address — the first of the range it occupies.

name: DMX Universe
desc: A single DMX512 data stream carrying up to 512 channels. Large lighting rigs require multiple universes, each carried on its own cable or distributed over Ethernet using Art-Net or sACN.

name: DNG
desc: Digital News Gathering. Electronic News Gathering (Eng) Using Digital Equipment And/Or Transmission.

name: DNG (Digital News Gathering)
desc: Electronic News Gathering (ENG) using digital equipment and/or transmission.

name: DNL
desc: Noise reduction system produced by Philips.

name: DNR (Dynamic Noise Reduction)
desc: This filter reduces changes across frames by eliminating dynamic noise without blurring. This helps MPEG compression without damaging image quality.

name: DNS
desc: Domain Name System. The way Internet domain names are located and translated into IP addresses.

name: DOC
desc: Declaration of Conformity. A document stating the EU directives and standards to which equipment should comply.

name: documentary
desc: a non - fiction ( factual ) , narrative film with real people ( notperformers or actors ) ; typically , a documentary is a lowbudget , journalistic record of an event , person , or place ; adocumentary film - maker should be an unobtrusive observer -like a fly - on - the - wall , capturing reality as it happens ;aka doc or docu ; also called direct cinema ; one type istermed docudrama ; contrast with cinemaverite andmockumentary

name: Document Window
desc: A sub-window inside an application. The size is user adjustable but limited by the size of its application window.

name: Dogme 95
desc: a collective of film directors founded in Denmark in 1995 ledby Lars von Trier , with a distinctive democratizing philosophyand set of rules ( termed " the vow of chastity " ) that rejectedspecial effects and contrived lighting / staging and camerawork , and espoused returning to more " truthful " and honest ," non - Hollywood " forms of cinema ; the ten rules includedshooting on location , use of hand - held cameras , naturallighting only , no props , use of digital - video ( DV ) , lack ofcredits for the director , etc.

name: Dolby™
desc: A compression/expansion (companding) noise reduction system developed by Ray Dolby, widely used in consumer, professional and broad- cast audio applications. Signal-to-noise ratio improvement is accomplished by processing a signal before recording and reverse-processing the signal upon playback.

name: Dolby AC-2 and AC-3
desc: These are compression algorithms from the Dolby Laboratories. The AC-2 coding is an adaptive transform coding that includes a filterbank based on time domain alias cancellation (TDAS). The AC-3 is a dedicated multichannel coding, which like AC-2 uses adaptive transform coding with a TDAS filterbank. In addition, AC-3 employs a bit- allocation routine that distributes bits to channels and frequencies depend- ing on the signals, and this improves the coding efficiency compared to AC-2. The AC-3 algorithm is adopted for the 5.1-channel audio surround system in the American HDTV system.

name: Dolby Atmos
desc: An immersive audio format from Dolby allowing individual sounds to be precisely placed in 3D space. Cinema: up to 64 discrete speaker feeds. Home/Pro AV: up to 24.1.10 configuration.

name: Dolby Digital
desc: Formerly AC-3, a perceptual audio coding system based upon transform coding techniques and psycho-acoustic principles.Frequency-domain processing takes full advantage of noise masking by confining quantization noise to narrow spectral regions where it will be masked by the audio signal. Designed as an emissions (delivery) system, Dolby Digital provides flexible coding of up to 5.1 audio channels at a variety of data rates. In addition, Dolby Digital bit streams carry informa- tional data about the associated audio.

name: Dolby Digital (formerly Dolby AC-3)
desc: The Approved 5.1 Channel (Surround-Sound) Audio Standard For Atsc Digital Television, Using Approximately 13:1 Compression Six Discrete Audio Channels Are Used: Left, Center, Right, Left Rear (Or Side) Surround, Right Rear (Or Side) Surround, And A Subwoofer (Considered The ".1" As It Is Limited In Bandwidth). The Bit Rate Can Range From 56 Kbps To 640 Kbps, Typically 64 Kbps Mono, 192 Kbps Two-Channel, 320 Kbps 35Mm Cinema 5.1, 384 Kbps Laserdisc/Dvd 5.1 And Atsc, 448 Kbps 5.1. When Moving From Analog Recording To A Digital Recording Medium, The Digital Audio Coding Used Yields An Amount Of Data Often Too Immense To Store Or Transmit Economically, Especially When Multiple Channels Are Required. As A Result, New Forms Of Digital Audio Coding--Often Known As "Perceptual Coding"--Have Been Developed To Allow The Use Of Lower Data Rates With A Minimum Of Perceived Degradation Of Sound Quality. Dolby'S Third Generation Audio Coding Algorithm (Originally Called Ac-3) Is Such A Coder. This Coder Has Been Designed To Take Maximum Advantage Of Human Auditory Masking In That It Divides The Audio Spectrum Of Each Channel Into Narrow Frequency Bands Of Different Sizes, Optimized With Respect To The Frequency Selectivity Of Human Hearing. This Makes It Possible To Sharply Filter Coding Noise So That It Is Forced To Stay Very Close In Frequency To The Frequency Components Of The Audio Signal Being Coded. By Reducing Or Eliminating Coding Noise Wherever There Are No Audio Signals To Mask It, The Sound Quality Of The Original Signal Can Be Subjectively Preserved. In This Key Respect, A Coding System Like Dolby Digital Is Essentially A Form Of Very Selective And Powerful Noise Reduction.

name: Dolby Digital Plus
desc: An extension of Dolby Digital supporting up to 13 audio channels (Blu-ray limited to 8). Optional codec for Blu-ray Disc.

name: Dolby E
desc: A New Coding System Designed Specifically For Use With Video Available From Dolby Laboratories. The Audio Framing Is Matched To The Video Framing, Which Allows Synchronous And Seamless Switching Or Editing Of Audio And Video Without The Introduction Of Gaps Or A/V Sync Slips. All Of The Common Video Frame Rates, Including 30/29.97, 25, And 24/23.976, Can Be Supported With Matched Dolby E Audio Frame Sizes. The Dolby E Coding Technology Is Intended To Provide Approximately 4:1 Reduction In Bit Rate. The Reduction Ratio Is Intentionally Limited So That The Quality Of The Audio May Be Kept Very High Even After A Number Of Encode-Decode Generations. The Fact That Operations Such As Editing And Switching Can Be Performed Seamlessly In The Coded Domain Allows Many Coding Generations To Be Avoided, Further Increasing Quality. A Primary Carrier For The Dolby E Data Will Be The Aes/Ebu Signal. The Dolby E Coding Will Allow The Two Pcm Audio Channels To Be Replaced With Eight Encoded Audio Channels. A Vtr Pcm Track Pair Will Become Capable Of Carrying Eight Independent Audio Channels, Plus The Accompanying Metadata. The System Is Also Intended To Be Applied On Servers And Satellite Links. A Time Delay When Encoding Or Decoding Dolby E Is Unavoidable. In Order To Facilitate The Provision Of A Compensating Video Delay, The Audio Encoding And Decoding Delay Have Been Fixed At Exactly One Frame. When Applied With Video Recording Formats Which Incorporate Frame Based Video Encoding, It Can Be Relatively Easy To Provide For Equal Video And Audio Coding Delays. When Applied With Uncoded Video, It May Be Necessary To Provide A Compensating One Frame Video Delay.

name: Dolby Laboratories
desc: Founded in 1965, Dolby Laboratories is well known for the technologies it has developed for improving audio sound reproduc- tion, including their noise reduction systems (e.g., Dolby A, B, and C), Dolby Digital (AC-3), Dolby Surround, and more. For more information, visit the Dolby Laboratories website.

name: Dolby MAT
desc: Dolby Metadata-enhanced Audio Transmission. An audio format for transmission and distribution of multichannel audio and accompanying metadata over high-resolution interfaces.

name: Dolby noise reduction
desc: A patented noise reduction technique from Dolby Labs raising volume of elements likely to be affected by inherent noise during recording, then lowering them during playback.

name: Dolby Pro Logic
desc: The technique (or the circuit which applies the tech- nique) of extracting surround audio channels from a matrix-encoded audio signal. Dolby Pro Logic is a decoding technique only, but is often mistaken- ly used to refer to Dolby Surround audio encoding.

name: Dolby stereo
desc: a stereo - sound process for motion pictures created by DolbyLaboratories , Inc. , used to improve sound quality ; 35mmprints have two optical sound tracks ( Dolby can decode andplayback on four channels ) , while 70mm prints have sixmagnetic tracks for multi - channel playback ; by the 1990s ,Dolby Stereo was superceded by advanced digitally - recordedsound

name: Dolby Surround
desc: A passive system that matrix encodes four channels of audio into a standard two-channel format (Lt/Rt). When the signal is decod- ed using a Dolby Surround Pro Logic decoder, the left, center and right sig- nals are recovered for playback over three front speakers and the surround signal is distributed over the rear speakers.

name: Dolby Surround (Dolby Stereo, & Dolby 4:2:4):
desc: Matrix Analog Coding Of Four Audio Channels-- Left, Center, Right, Surround (Lcrs), Into Two Channels Referred To As Right-Total And Left-Total (Rt, Lt). On Playback, A Dolby Surround Pro Logic Decoder Converts The Two Channels To Lcrs And, Optionally, A Subwoofer Channel. The Pro Logic Circuits Are Used To Steer The Audio And Increase Channel Separation. The Dolby Surround System, Originally Developed For The Cinema, Is A Method Of Getting More Audio Channels But Suffers From Poor Channel Separation, A Mono Limited Bandwidth Surround Channel And Other Limitations. A Dolby Surround Track Can Be Carried By Analog Audio Or Linear Pcm, Dolby Digital And Mpeg Compression Systems.

name: Dolby Surround Pro Logic (DSPL)
desc: An active decoding process designed to enhance the sound localization of Dolby Surround encoded programs through the use of high-separation techniques. Dolby Surround Pro Logic decoders continuously monitor the encoded audio program and evaluate the inherent sound field dominance, applying enhancement in the same direction and in proportion to that dominance.

name: Dolby TrueHD
desc: A lossless multi-channel audio encoder/decoder for HD content, optional for Blu-ray Disc and HDMI 1.3. Supports up to 8 channels at 96 kHz or 6 channels at 192 kHz.

name: Dolby Vision
desc: A proprietary HDR standard from Dolby using dynamic metadata on a frame-by-frame basis. Supports 12-bit color depth and 68.7 billion colors.

name: Dolly
desc: a) A set of casters attached to the legs of a tripod to allow the tri- pod to roll b) A forward/backward rolling movement of the camera on top of the tripod dolly.

name: Dolly  Shot
desc: Display Of A Scene While Moving Forward Or Backward. (See Also Pan Shot And Track Shot.)

name: Dolly (Tracking)
desc: This Refers To A Move Where The Camera Is Mounted On A Platform Called A Dolly Which Is Pushed On Rails Toward Or Away From The Subject Or Can Be Used To Follow Alongside A Subject In Which Case It Is Called A “Dolly With”. This Move Can Be Done Inexpensively By Safely Placing The Camera Operator In A Desk Chair, Little Red Wagon, Or Even A Grocery Cart To Create The Effect.

name: Dolly Wall
desc: A Section Of Led Panels Built Onto A Mobile Platform For Easy Repositioning And Use As Areflection Source And Other Purposes . Also Called Roaming Panels Or Roaming Walls .

name: Domain
desc: a) The smallest known permanent magnet. b) Program Chains (PGC) are classified into four types of domains, including First Play Domain, Video Manager Menu Domain, VTS Menu Domain and Title Domain.

name: Dome Tweeter
desc: See Compression Driver

name: Dongle
desc: A hardware device used as a key to control the use of licensed software. The software can be installed on any system but will run only on the system that has a dongle installed. The dongle connects to the Apple Desktop Bus on Macintosh systems or to the parallel (printer) port on PC systems.

name: Donut
desc: A Flat Metal Apparatus With A Circular Hole In The Center Used To Reduce Halation And Sharpen The Image When Using Patterns.

name: Door
desc: 1) A Single Flap On A Barndoor. 2) A Cover To An Access Opening In The Housing Of A Luminaire Or Other Apparatus.

name: Doors
desc: When The Venue Opens To The Ticketed Public.

name: doppelganger
desc: a German word literally meaning : " doublewalker , " a referenceto the fact that a shadow - self , duplicate , counterpart or double( spiritual , ghostly , or real ) accompanies every individual

name: Doppler Effect
desc: An Apparent Increase In The Frequency Of Sound Or Light As Its Source Approaches An Observer Or A Decrease If It Moves Away.

name: DOS (Disk Operating System)
desc: a) A single-user operating system from Microsoft for the PC. It was the first operating system for the PC and is the underlying control program for Windows 3.1, 95, 98 and ME. Windows NT, 2000 and XP emulate DOS in order to support existing DOS applications.b) A software package that makes a computer work with its hardware devices such as hard drive, floppy drive, screen, keyboard, etc.

name: dot2
desc: MA Lighting's entry-level lighting control console and software. Affordable, full-featured DMX console suitable for smaller venues, schools, clubs, and touring support acts. Runs MA dot2 OS, separate from the grandMA3 ecosystem.

name: dot2 on PC
desc: The free MA Lighting software version of the dot2 platform running on Windows. Provides the complete dot2 feature set without dedicated hardware, using Art-Net or USB interfaces for DMX output.

name: Dot clock
desc: Also called pixel clock. The timing device in a graphics card determining pixel resolution. In a digital projector, samples analog video at a rate producing the resultant pixel resolution.

name: Dot Crawl
desc: See Chroma Crawl.

name: Dot Matrix
desc: Method of forming characters by using many small dots.

name: Dot Pitch
desc: a) This is the density measurement of screen pixels specified in pixels/mm. The more dense the pixel count, the better the screen resolu- tion. b) The distance between phosphor dots in a tri-color, direct-view CRT. It can be the ultimate determinant of resolution.

name: double
desc: refers to the person who temporarily takes the leadingplayer's place for a dangerous or difficult stunt , or tophotographically stand in for the actor ( when the latter is notavailable or when the actor wants a body double for a nudescene , etc. )

name: Double Buffering
desc: As the name implies, you are using two buffers, for video, this means two frame buffers. While buffer 1 is being read, buffer 2 is being written to. When finished, buffer 2 is read out while buffer 1 is being written to.

name: Double-Click
desc: To hold the mouse still, then press and release a mouse button twice, very rapidly. When you double-click an icon it opens into a window; when you double-click the Window menu button the window closes.

name: Double Ended Lamp
desc: A Somewhat Elongated Lamp That Has A Base And Contact On Each End.

name: double exposure
desc: to expose a single frame twice so that elements of bothimages are visible in the finished product ; produces an effectsimilar to superimposition and is often used to produce' ghostly ' effects

name: Double Header
desc: A Tee Bar With Two Individual, Or Sets Of, Studs Or Receivers.

name: Double-Perf Film
desc: Film stock with perforations along both edges of the film.

name: Double Pipe Clamp
desc: Two Pipe Clamps Connected Together Via A Short Stud With Their Serrated Jaws On Opposite Ends. It Is Used To Connect Two Pipes Together.

name: Double Pipe Clamp Extension
desc: Two Pipe Clamps Connected Together Via A Narrow Pipe, Usually 24" Or More, With Their Serrated Jaws On Opposite Ends. It Is Used To Connect Two Pipes Together.

name: Double Precision Arithmetic
desc: Uses two words to represent each number.

name: Double Purchase
desc: A Rope Or Cable That Passes From A Lifting Device (Arbor, Winch, Or Person) Over A Block, To A Block Attached To The Load, And Tied Off At The Previous Block, Is Double Purchased. The System Allows Twice As Much Load To Be Raised For A Given Effort, But The Rope Or Cable Must Be Pulled Twice As Far, So Total Work Done Remains The Same.

name: Double purchase (rigging)
desc: A rope or cable system where the line passes from the lifting device over a block to a block attached to the load and back — allowing twice the load to be raised for a given force, at twice the rope travel.

name: Double Stack
desc: Placing two or more projectors on top of each other and aligning them to make one brighter image on a single screen. Also provides redundancy.

name: Double-Strand Editing
desc: See A/B Roll.

name: Double System
desc: Any film system in which picture and sound are record- ed on separate media. A double system requires the resyncing of picture and sound during post-production.

name: double take
desc: a comedic convention that refers to the way in which an actorfirst looks at an object ( subject , event , scene , etc. ) , then looksaway , and then snaps his head back to the situation for asecond look - with surprise , disgust , sexual longing , etc .; avariation is termed a spit - take ( the double - take causes thecharacter to spit out whatever he is drinking )

name: Doubling
desc: A Delay Effect, Where The Original Signal Is Mixed With A Medium (20 To 50 Ms) Delayed Copy Of Itself. When Used Carefully, This Effect Can Simulate Double-Tracking (Recording A Voice Or Instrument Twice)

name: Down Beat
desc: The Start Of The Show.

name: Down Converter
desc: This device accepts modulated high frequency televi- sion signals and down converts the signal to an intermediate frequency.

name: Down converting
desc: The Process Which Changes The Number Of Pixels And/Or Frame Rate And/Or Scanning Format Used To Represent An Image By Removing Pixels. Down Converting Is Done From High Definition To Standard Definition. See Also: Side Converting, Up Converting.

name: Down Light
desc: 1) Downward Illumination, Almost Perpendicular With The Floor. 2) A Luminaire That Provides Such Illumination.

name: Down Link
desc: a) The frequency satellites use to transmit data to earth stations. b) Hardware used to transmit data to earth stations.

name: Download
desc: The process of having an effect moved from disk storage into the ADO control panel.

name: Downloadability
desc: Ability of a decoder to load data or necessary decod- ing tools via Internet or ATM.

name: Downloader
desc: A computer program allowing you to save a web video to a local computer for storage and playback.

name: Downmix
desc: A process wherein multiple channels are summed to a lesser number of channels. In the audio portion of a DVD there can be as many as 8 channels of audio in any single stream and it is required that all DVD players produce a stereo version of those channels provided on the disc. This capacity is provided as legacy support for older audio systems.

name: Downscaling
desc: The process of decimating or interpolating data from an incoming video signal to decease the size of the image before placing it into memory.

name: Down Stage
desc: The Area Of The Stage That Is Closest To The Audience.See “Raked Stage.”

name: Downstream
desc: A term describing the precedence of an effect or key. The “stream” of video through a switcher allows multiple layers of effects to be accomplished, with each successive layer appearing on top of the previous one. The most downstream effect is that video which appears as the top- most layer.

name: Downstream Keyer
desc: The last keyer on the switcher. A key on the DSK will appear in front of all other video. Ampex DSKs are actually DMKs, that is they also allow mixes and fades with the switcher output.

name: Downstream Keyer (DSK)
desc: A term used for a keyer that inserts the key “downstream” (last layer of video within switcher) of the effects system video output. This enables the key to remain on-air while the backgrounds and effects keys are changed behind it.

name: DPCM
desc: See Differential Pulse Code Modulation.

name: DPCP
desc: DisplayPort Content Protection. A DisplayPort content-protection scheme from AMD using AES-128 encryption. Not yet implemented by any manufacturer; all current devices use HDCP.

name: D-Pictures
desc: Pictures for which only DC coefficients are transmitted.D-pictures are not part of MPEG-2 but only of MPEG-1. MPEG-2 decoders must be able to decode D-pictures.

name: D-Plug
desc: See Iec Cable

name: DQPSK
desc: Differential Quadrature Phase Shift Keying. Dqpsk Is A Digital Modulation Technique Commonly Used With Cellular Systems. Motorola'S Cybersurfr Cable Modem Uses Dqpsk To Carry Data Upstream From The Subscriber'S Computer To The Internet On A Narrower Frequency Band Than Standard Qpsk. Narrower Bands Allow More Upstream Channels, So The Cybersurfr Has Additional Noise-Free Channels To Choose From When It'S Installed.

name: Drafting Template
desc: A Translucent Mask With Traceable Patterned Cutouts Of Luminaires And Other Items Used To Draw A Light Plot.

name: Drag
desc: To press and hold down a mouse button, then move the mouse. This drags the cursor to move icons, to highlight menu items, or to perform other functions.

name: Drain wire
desc: Non-insulated wire used in cable termination as a ground connection.

name: DRAM
desc: Dynamic Ram (Random Access Memory). High Density, Cost-Effective Memory Chips (Integrated Circuits). Their Importance Is Such That The Japanese Call Them The "Rice Of Electronics." Drams Are Used Extensively In Computers And Generally In Digital Circuit Design, But Also For Building Framestores And Animation Stores. Being Solid State, There Are No Moving Parts And They Offer The Densest Available Method For Accessing Or Storing Data. Each Bit Is Stored On A Single Transistor, And The Chip Must Be Powered And Clocked To Retain Data.

name: Dramatic irony
desc: A theatrical device where the audience is aware of something that the character on stage does not know.

name: Dramaturg
desc: A literary advisor and researcher working with a production team on the script, historical context, and artistic vision of a play.

name: DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory)
desc: An integrated circuit device that stores data bits as charges in thousands of tiny capacitors. Since the capacitors are very small, DRAM must be constantly refreshed to restore charges in appropriate cells. DRAM is used for short-term memory such as frame and screen memory and memory which contains operating programs which are loaded from ROM or disk.

name: DRC (Dynamic Range Control)
desc: A feature of Dolby Digital that allows the end user to retain or modify the dynamic range of a Dolby Digital Encoded program upon playback. The amount of control is dictated by encoder parameter settings and decoder user options.

name: Dress
desc: To Arrange Electric Cables In A Neat And Orderly Fashion.

name: Dresser
desc: One Whose Job It Is To Help An Actor (Or Actors) In Quick Changes Of Costume.

name: Dressing
desc: Properties that are not handled or referred to by performers but complete the stage picture — posters, knick-knacks, plants, rugs, etc. Differs from props which are actively used by performers.

name: Dressing room
desc: A backstage room where performers change costumes and apply makeup. Typically numbered with principal performers in lower-numbered rooms.

name: Dress rehearsal
desc: A full rehearsal of a show as close as possible to a live performance, with complete technical elements, costumes, makeup, and full run conditions.

name: Drift
desc: Gradual shift or change in the output over a period of time due to change or aging of circuit components. Change is often caused by thermal instability of components.

name: Drive
desc: A hardware device that lets you access information on various forms of media, such as hard, floppy, and CD-ROM disks, and magnetic tapes.

name: Drive Address
desc: See SCSI Address.

name: drive - in
desc: an outdoor movie theatre in which the patrons viewed a filmfrom their automobile ; films projected were often B - films orlow - budget films ; reached their peak in terms of popularityand numbers in the 1970s ; also called a passion pit , ozoner ;contrast with a hard top ( or indoor movie theatre ) .

name: Drive Lines
desc: Audio Signals Sent From A Mixing Console To Feed A Pa System. Typically For Live Music Production, These Are A Stereo Signal For The Mains, A Mono Signal For The Subs, And A Mono Signal For The Front Fills, With Further Processing For Specific Zones Happening In The System Processor.

name: Drive Pulse
desc: A term commonly used to describe a set of signals needed by source equipment such as a camera. This signal set may be composed of any of the following: sync, blanking, subcarrier, horizontal drive, vertical drive, and burst flag. Also called pulse drive.

name: Driver
desc: Another Name For An Individual Transducer In A Speaker Enclosure, Or The Element That Vibrates Back And Forth To Produce Sound.

name: Drive rack
desc: A rackmount signal processing unit (crossover, EQ, limiter, delay) used to process and optimize the signal from the mixing console to the amplifiers and speakers. Examples include dbx DriveRack, Lake LM series, and Lab.gruppen PLM.

name: Drive Shaft
desc: A Rotating Component That Conducts Power From An Engine Or Other Power Source To A Drum, Pulley, Or Gear.

name: Driving Signals
desc: Signals that time the scanning at the pickup device.

name: DRM
desc: Digital Rights Management. Technologies controlling access to or reproduction of copyrighted content (e.g., HDCP, DPCP). Used to prevent piracy and govern how content can be used.

name: Drop
desc: A piece of scenic canvas, painted or plain, flown or fixed to hang vertically. Types include backdrop, front drop, cut drop, star drop, and floor drop.

name: Drop Box
desc: See Plug-In Box.

name: Drop Curtain
desc: A Curtain That Is Painted Or Constructed In A Manner That Makes It A Part Of The Scenic Environment.

name: Drop-Down List Box
desc: Displays a list of possible options only when the list box is selected.

name: Drop Field Scrambling
desc: This method is identical to the sync suppres- sion technique for scrambling analog TV channels, except there is no suppression of the horizontal blanking intervals. Sync pulse suppression only takes place during the vertical blanking interval. The descrambling pulses still go out for the horizontal blanking intervals (to fool unauthorized descrambling devices). If a descrambling device is triggering on descram- bling pulses only, and does not know that the scrambler is using the drop field scrambling technique, it will try to reinsert the horizontal intervals (which were never suppressed). This is known as double reinsertion, which causes compression of the active video signal. An unauthorized descram- bling device creates a washed-out picture and loss of neutral sync during drop field scrambling.

name: Drop Frame
desc: a) System of modifying the frame counting sequence (dropping two frames every minute except on every tenth minute) to allow time code to match a real-time clock. b) The timecode adjustment made to handle the 29.97 per second frame rate of color video by dropping certain, agreed-upon frames to compensate for the 0.03 fps discrepancy. Drop-frame timecode is critical in broadcast applications. Contrast with Non-Drop Frame.

name: Drop Frame Time Code
desc: a) SMPTE time code format that skips (drops) two frames per minute except on the tenth minute, so that the time code stays coincident with real time. b) The television broadcast standard for time code. c) The NTSC color coding system uses a 525/60 line/field format, it actually runs at 59.94 fields per second, or 29.97 frames per second (a difference of 1:1000). Time code identifies 30 frames per second, whereas drop frame time code compensates by dropping two frames in every minute except the tenth. Note that the 625/50 PAL system is exact and does not require drop frame.

name: Drop-In Iris
desc: An Iris Mounted To A Plate That Can Be Installed, I.E., Dropped Into Or Removed From A Luminaire.

name: Dropout
desc: Loss Of Transmission Signal Used By A Wireless Mic.

name: Dropout Compensator
desc: Technology that replaces dropped video with the video from the previous image’s scan line. High-end time base correctors usually included a dropout compensator.

name: Dropout Count
desc: The number of dropouts detected in a given length of magnetic tape.

name: Drop Outs
desc: Small bit of missing picture information usually caused by physical imperfections in the surface of the video tape.

name: Dropped Frames
desc: Missing frames lost during the process of digitizing or capturing video. Dropped frames can be caused by a hard drive incapable of the necessary data transfer rate.

name: Drop Shadow
desc: a) A type of key border where a key is made to look three dimensional and as if it were illuminated by a light coming from the upper left by creating a border to the right and bottom. b) A key border mode which places a black, white or gray border to the right and below the title key insert, giving a shadow effect.

name: Drum Winch
desc: A Winch With A Drum For Wrapping Cable As It Is Taken Up. One End Of The Drum Is Typically Supported By A Bearing While The Other End Is Connected To The Output Shaft Of The Gear Box.

name: Dry
desc: Usually Means Without Reverberation, Or With_x0002_Out Some Other Applied Effect Like Delay Or Cho_x0002_Rusing. Dry Is Not Wet, I.E., Totally Unaffected.

name: Dry contact closure
desc: A pair of electrical contacts that carry no live voltage.

name: Dry Signal
desc: A signal without any added effects, especially without reverb.

name: Dry tech
desc: A technical rehearsal without actors to rehearse the integration of lighting, scenic changes, and other technical cues. Also called a Dry Run.

name: DS0
desc: Digital Signal Level Zero, 64 Kbps.

name: DS0 (Digital Service Level 0)
desc: 64 kbps.

name: DS1
desc: A Telephone Company Format For Transmitting Information Digitally. Ds1 Has A Capacity Of 24 Voice Circuits At A Transmission Speed Of 1.544 Megabits Per Second. See Also: T1.

name: DS1 (Digital Service Level 1)
desc: A telephone company format for trans- mitting information digitally. DS1 has a capacity of 24 voice circuits at a transmission speed of 1.544 megabits per second.

name: DS3
desc: A Terrestrial And Satellite Format For Transmitting Information Digitally. Ds3 Has A Capacity Of 672 Voice Circuits At A Transmission Speed Of 44.736 Mbps (Commonly Referred To As 45 Mbps). Ds3 Is Used For Digital Television Distribution Using Mezzanine Level Compression--Typically

name: DS3 (Digital Service Level 3)
desc: One of a hierarchy of North American data transmission rates associated with ISDN and B-ISDN, 44.736 Mbps. The terrestrial and satellite format for transmitting information digitally. DS3 has a capacity of 672 voice circuits at a transmission speed of 44.736 Mbps (commonly referred to as 45 Mbps). DS3 is used for digital television distribution using mezzanine level compression – typically MPEG-2 in nature, decompressed at the local station to full bandwidth signals (such as HDTV) and then re-compressed to the ATSC’s 19.39 Mbps transmission standard

name: DSC
desc: Display Stream Compression. A VESA industry-wide compression standard for video interfaces featuring low latency and visually lossless performance supporting higher color depth and HDR.

name: DS (Dansk Standard)
desc: Danish standarding body.

name: DSK (Downstream Keying)
desc: An effect available in some special effects generators and video mixers in which one video signal is keyed on top of another video signal. The lightest portions of the DSK signal replace the source video leaving the dark areas showing the original video image.Optionally, the DSK signal can be inverted so the dark portions are keyed rather than the lightest portions allowing a solid color to be added to the keyed portions. The DSK input is most commonly a video camera or char- acter generator. The DSK signal must be genlocked to the other signals.

name: DSK Monitor
desc: A video output showing program video with the DSK key over full time.

name: Dsl
desc: Down Stage Left: Towards The Front Of The Stage On The Left-Hand Side As You Look At The Audience.

name: Dslr Camera
desc: A Digital Camera That Combines The Optics And The Mechanisms Of A Single-Lens Reﬂex Camera With A Digital Imaging Sensor (The Canon 5D And 7D, For Example)

name: DSM
desc: See Digital Storage Media.

name: DSM-CC (Digital Storage Media-Command and Control)
desc: A syntax defined in the Mpeg-2 Standard, Part 6.DSM-CC IS U-N (DSM-CC International Standard User-to-Network) DSM-CC U-N (DSM-CC User-to-Network)

name: DSNG (Digital Satellite News Gathering)
desc: The use of mobile communi- cations equipment for the purpose of worldwide newscasting. Mobile units are usually vans equipped with advanced, two-way audio and video transmitters and receivers, using dish antennas that can be aimed at geostationary satellites.

name: Dsp
desc: Digital Signal Processing Can Accomplish The Same Functions Found In Analog Signal Proces_x0002_Sors, But Performs Them Mathematically In The Digital Domain, With More Precision And Accu_x0002_Racy Than Its Analog Counterpart. Since Dsp Is A Software-Based Process, Parameters And Process_x0002_Ing Functions Are Easily Changed And Updated By Revising The Software, Rather Than Redesigning The Hardware. Dsp Can Be Found In An Out_x0002_Board Effects Device, Such As A Reverb Or Delay Unit, Or It Can Be Integrated Into A Daw Or Dig_x0002_Ital Mixing Console.

name: DSP (Digital Signal Processing)
desc: a) A DSP segments the voice signal into frames and stores them in voice packets. It usually refers to the electronic circuit section of a device capable of processing digital signals. b) When applied to video cameras, DSP means that the analog signal from the CCD sensors is converted to a digital signal. It is then processed for signal separation, bandwidth settings and signal adjustments. After pro- cessing, the video signal either remains in the digital domain for recording by a digital VTR or is converted back into an analog signal for recording or transmission. DSP is also being used in other parts of the video chain, including VTRs, and switching and routing devices.

name: Dsr
desc: Down Stage Right.

name: DSRC (David Sarnoff Research Center)
desc: Formerly RCA Laboratories (now part of SRI International), home of the ACTV research.

name: DSS
desc: Digital Satellite System. Due To Trademark Issues, The Abbreviation Is No Longer Used. See Dbs.

name: DSSB (Dual Single Sideband)
desc: A modulation technique that might be applied to two of the components of ACTV.

name: DSS (Direct Satellite System)
desc: An alternative to cable and analog satellite reception initially utilizing a fixed 18-inch dish focused on one or more geostationary satellites. DSS units are able to receive multiple chan-nels of multiplexed video and audio signals as well as programming information, email, and related data. DSS typically used MPEG-2 video and audio encoding.

name: DSVP
desc: Digital Sync Validation Processing. An Extron technology confirming input sources are active by scanning all sync inputs. Provides instantaneous frequency feedback via RS-232/422 port.

name: DTE
desc: See Data Terminal Equipment.

name: DTG (Digital Terrestrial Group)
desc: Over 80 companies that are working together for the implementation of digital television around the world, but most importantly in the UK.

name: DTIM
desc: Digital Theatre Interim Mastering Format.  An Interchange Format That Allows One Digital Master To Be Played On Projectors From Different Manufacturers.

name: DTMF (Dual Tone Multi-Frequency)
desc: The type of audio signals that are generated when you press the buttons on a touch-tone telephone.

name: D-to-A Converter (Digital to Analog Converter)
desc: A device that con- verts digital signals to analog signals.

name: DTS (Decoding Time Stamp)
desc: Part of PES header indicating when an access unit is to be decoded.

name: DTS Digital Sound
desc: A proprietary surround sound format from DTS, Inc. Supports up to 5.1 speaker configuration with a maximum bandwidth of 1.5 Mbps.

name: DTS Digital Surround
desc: A digital audio encoding/decoding technology from DTS delivering 5.1 channels of surround sound. Optional for DVDs; mandatory for Blu-ray Disc.

name: DTS (Digital Theater Sound)
desc: A perceptual audio-coding system devel- oped for theaters. A competitor to Dolby Digital and an optional audio track format for DVD-Video and DVD-Audio.

name: DTS (Digital Theater Systems)
desc: – One of a hierarchy of North American data transmission rates associated with ISDN and B-ISDN, 44.736 Mbps. The terrestrial and satellite format for transmitting information digitally. DS3 has a capacity of 672 voice circuits at a transmission speed of 44.736 Mbps (commonly referred to as 45 Mbps). DS3 is used for digital television distribution using mezzanine level compression – typically MPEG-2 in nature, decompressed at the local station to full bandwidth signals (such as HDTV) and then re-compressed to the ATSC’s 19.39 Mbps transmission standard

name: DTS-ES
desc: A version of DTS decoding that is compatible with 6.1-channel Dolby Surround EX. DTS-ES Discrete is a variation of DTS encoding and decoding that carries a discrete rear center channel instead of a matrixed channel.

name: DTS-HD High Resolution Audio
desc: An extension of DTS Digital Surround offering up to 7.1 channels at 24-bit/96 kHz. Optional surround format for Blu-ray Disc.

name: DTS-HD Master Audio
desc: A lossless audio encoder/decoder from DTS providing bit-for-bit studio master soundtrack reproduction. Supports up to 8 channels. Optional for Blu-ray and HDMI 1.3.

name: DTS-X
desc: An immersive audio codec supporting up to 32 speaker locations and 11.2 channels. Layered on top of DTS-HD Master Audio on Blu-ray discs.

name: DTT
desc: Digital Terrestrial Television. The Term Used In Europe To Describe The Broadcast Of Digital Television Services Using Terrestrial Frequencies.

name: DTT (Digital Terrestrial Television)
desc: The term used in Europe to describe the broadcast of digital television services using terrestrial frequencies.

name: DTTV (Digital Terrestrial Television)
desc: DTTV (digital terrestrial televi- sion, sometimes also abbreviated DTT) is digital television (DTV) broadcast entirely over earthbound circuits. A satellite is not used for any part of the link between the broadcaster and the end user. DTTV signals are broadcast over essentially the same media as the older analog terrestrial TV signals. The most common circuits use coaxial cable at the subscriber end to connect the network to the TV receiver. Fiber optic and/or microwave links may be used between the studio and the broadcast station, or between the broadcast station and local community networks. DTTV provides a clearer picture and superior sound quality when compared to analog TV, with less interference. DTTV offers far more channels, thus providing the viewer with a greater variety of programs to choose from. DTTV can be viewed on personal computers. Using a split-screen format, a computer user can surf the Web while watching TV.

name: DTTV-SA (Digital Terrestrial Television
desc: System Aspects)

name: DTV
desc: Digital Television. Often used to describe new forms of digital terrestrial transmission of video program material.

name: DTV (Digital Television)
desc: a) A term used for all types of digital television including High Definition Television and Standard Definition Television. b) Another acronym for the new digital television standards. c) The tech- nology enabling the terrestrial transmission of television programs as data. See HDTV.

name: DTV Team
desc: Originally Compaq, Microsoft and Intel, later joined by Lucent Technology. The DTV Team promotes the computer industry’s views on digi- tal television, namely, that DTV should not have interlace scanning formats but progressive scanning formats only. (Intel, however, now supports all the ATSC Table 3 formats, including those that are interlace, such as 1080i.)

name: DTV Team, The
desc: Originally Compaq, Microsoft And Intel, Later Joined By Lucent Technologies. The Dtv Team Promotes The Computer Industry'S Views On Digital Television--Namely, That Dtv Should Not Have Interlace Scanning Formats But Progressive Scanning Formats Only. (Intel, However, Now Supports All The Atsc Table 3 Formats, Including Those That Are Interlace, Such As 1080I.) Internet :  Www.Dtv.Org.

name: Dual Capstan
desc: Refers to a transport system in which a capstan and pinchroller are used on both sides of the recording and playback head system.

name: Dual Channel Audio
desc: A mode, where two audio channels are encoded within one bit stream. They may be played simultaneously (stereo) or independently (two languages).

name: Dual-Link DVI
desc: A DVI output with two TMDS links and twice the bandwidth of single-link DVI, supporting much higher resolutions such as 2560×1600.

name: Dual-Link HD-SDI
desc: A method applying two HD-SDI signals for 1920×1080 video at 50 or 60 Hz progressive, at 12-bit depth or with 4:4:4 color quantization.

name: Dual-Mode DisplayPort
desc: Allows DisplayPort sources to connect to HDMI, DVI, or VGA displays via a passive adapter by outputting a TMDS signal. Also depicted as DP++.

name: Dual Mono
desc: A Mode Of Operation For A Stereo Amplifier That Routes A Single Input To Both Channels, But Still Allows Independent Level Control Over Each Amplifier Output.

name: Dub
desc: a) A duplicate copy made from one recording medium to another. b) To record or mix pre-recorded audio or video from one or more sources to a another source to create a single recording. See also, Bump-Up.

name: Dubbing
desc: a) In videotape production, the process of copying video or audio from one tape to another. b) In film production, the process of replacing dialog on a sound track. See also ADR, Foley.

name: Dubmaster
desc: A second-generation copy of a program master used for making additional preview or distribution copies, thereby protecting the master from overuse.

name: dub( or dubbing )
desc: the act of putting a new soundtrack on a film or adding asoundtrack ( of dialogue , sound effects , or music ) afterproduction , to match the action and / or lip movements ofalready - filmed shots ; commonly used when films are shot onlocation in noisy environments ; also refers to addingtranslated dialogue to a foreign - language film ; as opposedtodirect sound - which is sound recorded when filming ascene ; contrast to looping .

name: Dubs
desc: Copies of videotape.

name: Dugan automixer
desc: An automatic microphone mixer algorithm that continuously adjusts the gain of multiple open microphones to maintain consistent overall system gain. Essential for conference, broadcast, and panel discussion applications. Available built-in on Yamaha and other consoles.

name: Dummy Cab
desc: A Guitar Cabinet That Isn'T Making Sound, Used As A Prop.

name: dunning
desc: the process or technique of combining shots filmed in a studiowith background footage shot elsewhere

name: Duologue
desc: A dialogue scene between exactly two performers. Also called a duet scene.

name: Dupe
desc: To duplicate. A section of film or video source footage that has been repeated (duplicated) one or more times in an edited program.

name: Dupe List
desc: A sublist of duplicated clips of film requiring additional prints or copies of negative for film finishing. See also Cut List.

name: Dupe Reel
desc: A reel designated for the recording and playback of dupes (duplicate shots) during videotape editing.

name: Duplex
desc: A communication system that carries information in both direc- tion is called a duplex system. In CCTV, duplex is often used to describe the type of multiplexer that can perform two functions simultaneously, recording in multiplex mode and playback in multiplex mode. It can also refer to duplex communication between a matrix switcher and a PTZ site driver, for example.

name: Duplication
desc: The reproduction of media. Generally refers to producing discs in small quantities, as opposed to large-scale replication.

name: Durability
desc: Usually expressed as a number of passes that can be made before a significant degradation of output occurs; divided by the correspon- ding number that can be made using a reference tape.

name: Duration
desc: Length of time (in hours, minutes, seconds and frames) that a particular effect or section of audio or video material lasts.

name: Dust Cap
desc: A plastic cap covering the connector ferrule, plug, or sleeve to protect the connector endface.

name: Dutch Angle
desc: Combines A Low Angle Shot With The Camera Tilted Sideways. For Example, A Shot Of The Haunted House Where The House Not Only Looms Over The Viewer But Seems Off-Kilter So As To Make The Viewer Feel Disoriented Or Off- Balance, Like On The Deck Of A Ship In Rough Seas.

name: dutch tilt( or canted angle )
desc: a shot made with the camera leaned to one side and filmingat a diagonal angle ; see also camera angle .

name: Duveteen
desc: A heavy black cloth used for blacking out windows, making set pieces, and hiding cables. Also called duvey.

name: Duvetyn (Duvatyne, Duvetine, Duvetyne, Duvyteen)
desc: An Opaque Material Used For Butterflies, Cutters, Flags, Gobos, And Overheads In The Film And Video Industries.

name: DV
desc: This Digital Vcr Format Is A Cooperation Between Hitachi, Jvc, Sony, Matsushita, Mitsubishi, Philips, Sanyo, Sharp, Thomson And Toshiba. It Uses 6.35 Mm (0.25-Inch) Wide Tape In A Range Of Products To Record 525/60 Or 625/50 Video For The Consumer (Dv) And Professional Markets (Panasonic'S Dvcpro, Sony'S Dvcam And Digital-8). All Models Use Digital Intra-Field Dct-Based "Dv" Compression (About 5:1) To Record 8-Bit Component Digital Video Based On 13.5 Mhz Luminance Sampling. The Consumer Versions, Dvcam, And Digital-8 Sample Video At 4:1:1 (525/60) Or 4:2:0 (625/50) Video (Dvcpro Is 4:1:1 In Both 525/60 And 625/25) And Provide Two 16-Bit/48 Or 44.1 Khz, Or Four 12-Bit/32 Khz Audio Channels Onto A 4 Hour 30 Minutes Standard Cassette Or Smaller 1 Hour "Mini" Cassette. The Video Recording Rate Is 25 Mbps.

name: DV25
desc: The most common form of DV compression. DV25 uses a fixed data rate of 25 megabits per second.

name: DVB
desc: Digital Video Broadcasting. The Group, With Over 200 Members In 25 Countries, Which Developed The Preferred Scheme For Digital Broadcasting In Europe. The Dvb Group Has Put Together A Satellite System--Dvb-S--That Can Be Used With Any Transponder, Current Or Planned, A Matching Cable System--Dvb-C, And A Digital Terrestrial System--Dvb-T. Internet: Www.Dvb.Org. See Also: Dvb-T.

name: DVB/ASI
desc: Digital Video Broadcasting/Asynchronous Serial Interface. A standard for broadcasting digital TV. DVB-T for terrestrial (mainly Europe); ATSC used for terrestrial in North America.

name: DVB-CA
desc: Support for use of scrambling and conditional access (CA) within digital broadcasting systems (ETR 289).

name: DVB-C (Digital Video Broadcasting
desc: Cable) – Broadcasting TV signals that comply with a digital standard by cable (ETS 300 429). DVB-CA – Support for use of scrambling and conditional access (CA) within digital broadcasting systems (ETR 289)

name: DVB-CI
desc: Common interface specification for conditional access and other digital video broadcasting decoder applications (EN 50221).

name: DVB-Cook
desc: A guideline for the use of DVB specifications and standards (TR 101 200).

name: DVB-CS
desc: Digital video broadcasting baseline system for SMATV distribu- tion systems (ETS 300 473).

name: DVB-Data
desc: Specification for Data Broadcasting (EN 301 192).

name: DVB (Digital Video Broadcasting)
desc: Broadcasting TV signals that comply with a digital standard.

name: DVB-DSNG
desc: Digital satellite news gathering (DSNG) specification (EN 301 210).

name: DVB-IRD (Digital Video Broadcasting Integrated Receiver Decoder)
desc: A receiving decoder that can automatically configure itself using the MPEG-2 Program Specific Information (PSI).

name: DVB-IRDI
desc: Interface for DVB-IRDs (EN 50201).

name: DVB-M
desc: Measurement guidelines for DVB systems (ETR 290).

name: DVB-MC
desc: Digital video broadcasting baseline system for multi-point video distribution systems below 10 GHz (EN 300 749).

name: DVB-MPEG
desc: Implementation guidelines for the use of MPEG-2 systems, video and audio in satellite, cable and terrestrial broadcasting applications (ETR 154).

name: DVB-MS
desc: Digital video broadcasting baseline system for multi-point video distribution systems at 10 MHz and above (EN 300 748).

name: DVB-NIP
desc: Network-independent protocols for DVB interactive services (ETS 300 802).

name: DVB-PDH
desc: DVB interfaces to plesiochronous digital hierarchy (PDH) networks (ETS 300 813).

name: DVB-PI
desc: DVB-PI (EN 50083-9) describes the electrical, mechanical and some protocol specification for the interface (cable/wiring) between two devices. DVB-PI includes interfaces for CATV/SMATV headends and similar professional equipment. Common interface types such as LVDS/SPI, ASI and SSI are addressed.

name: DVB-RCC
desc: Interaction channel for cable TV distribution system (CATV) (ETS 300 800).

name: DVB-RCCL (Return Channel for Cable and LMDS Digital Television Platform)
desc: An older cable standard that used to compete with DOCSIS.

name: DVB-RCCS
desc: Interaction channel for satellite master antenna TV (SMATV) distribution systems. Guidelines for versions based on satellite and coaxial sections (TR 101 201).

name: DVB-RCDECT
desc: Interaction channel through the digital enhanced cordless telecommunications (DECT) (EN 301 193).

name: DVB-RCL
desc: Interaction channel for local multi-point distribution system (LMDS) distribution systems (EN 301 199)

name: DVB-RCS (Return Channel for Satellite Digital Television Platform)
desc: DVB-RCS is a satellite standard.

name: DVB-RCT (Return Channel for Terrestrial Digital Television Platform)
desc: Interaction channel through public switched telecommunica- tions network (PSTN)/integrated services digital networks (ISDN)(ETS 300 801).

name: DVB-SDH
desc: Interfaces to synchronous digital hierarchy (SDH) networks (ETS 300 814).

name: DVB-S (Digital Video Broadcasting
desc: Satellite) – For broadcasting TV signals to a digital standard by satellite (ETS 300 421).

name: DVB-SFN
desc: Mega-frame for single frequency network (SFN) synchroniza- tion (TS 101 191).

name: DVB-SI (Digital Video Broadcasting
desc: Satellite) – For broadcasting TV signals to a digital standard by satellite (ETS 300 421).

name: DVB-SIM
desc: DVB SimulCrypt. Part 1: headend architecture and synchro- nization (TS 101 197).

name: DVB-SMATV
desc: DVB satellite master antenna television (SMATV) distribu- tion systems (EN 300 473).

name: DVB-SUB
desc: DVB subtitling systems (ETS 300 743).

name: DVB-T
desc: The Dvb-T Is A Transmission Scheme For Terrestrial Digital Television. Its Specification Was Approved By Etsi In February 1997 And Dvb-T Services Began In 1998. As With The Other Dvb Standards, Mpeg-2 Sound And Picture Coding Form The Basis Of Dvb-T. It Uses A Transmission Scheme Based On Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (Cofdm), Which Spreads The Signals Over A Large Number Of Carriers To Enable It To Operate Effectively In Very Strong Multipath Environments. The Multipath Immunity Of This Approach Means That Dvb-T Can Operate An Overlapping Network Of Transmitting Stations With A Single Frequency. In The Areas Of Overlap, The Weaker Of The Two Received Signals Is Rejected. See Also: Cofdm, Dvb.

name: DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting
desc: – Terrestrial) – Terrestrial broad_x0002_casting of TV signals to a digital standard (ETS 300 744).

name: DVB-TXT
desc: Specification for conveying ITU-R system B teletext in DVB bitstreams (ETS 300 472).

name: DVC
desc: See Digital Video Cassette.

name: DVCAM
desc: Sony'S Development Of Native Dv Which Records A 15 Micron (15X10-6M, Fifteen Thousandths Of A Millimeter) Track On A Metal Evaporated (Me) Tape. Dvcam Uses Dv Compression Of  A 4:1:1 Signal For 525/60 (Ntsc) Sources And 4:2:0 For 625/50 (Pal). Audio Is Recorded In One Of Two Forms--Four 12-Bit Channels Sampled At 32 Khz, Or Two 16-Bit Channels Sampled At 48 Khz.

name: DVCPRO
desc: See: D7

name: DVCPRO50
desc: This variant of DV uses a video data rate of 50 Mbps – double that of other DV systems – and is aimed at the higher quality end of the market. Sampling is 4:2:2 to give enhanced chroma resolution, useful in post-production processes (such as chroma-keying). Four 16-bit audio tracks are provided. The format is similar to Digital-S (D9).

name: DVCPRO HD
desc: This Variant Of Dv Uses A Video Data Rate Of 100 Mbps--Four Times That Of Other Dv Systems--And Is Aimed At The High Definition Efp End Of The Market. Eight Audio Channels Are Supported. The Format Is Similar To D9 Hd.

name: DVCPRO P
desc: This Variant Of Dv Uses A Video Data Rate Of 50 Mbps--Double That Of Other Dv Systems--To Produce A 480 Progressive Picture.  Sampling Is 4:2:0.

name: DVCR
desc: See Digital Video Cassette Recorder.

name: DVD
desc: Digital Versatile Disk:A High Density Development Of The Compact Disk. It Is The Same Size As A Cd But Stores From 4.38 Gb (Seven Times Cd Capacity) On A Single Sided, Single Layer Disk. Dvds Can Also Be Double Sided Or Dual Layer-Storing Even More Data. The Capacities Commonly Available At Present.

name: DVD-10
desc: 9.4 Gb (2 Sides, 1 Layer Each)

name: DVD-18
desc: 17.0 Gb (2 Sides, 2 Layers)

name: DVD-5
desc: A DVD format in which 4.7 gigabytes of data can be stored on one side of a disc in one layer.

name: DVD-9
desc: A DVD format in which 8.5 gigabytes of data can be stored on one side of a two-layer disc.

name: DVDA (DVD Association)
desc: A non-profit industry trade association repre- senting DVD authors, producers, and vendors throughout the world.

name: DVD-A (DVD Audio)
desc: DVDs that contain linear PCM audio data in any combination of 44.1, 48.0, 88.2, 96.0, 176.4, or 192 kHz sample rates,16, 20, or 24 bits per sample, and 1 to 6 channels, subject to a maximum bit rate of 9.6 Mbps. With a 176.4 or 192 kHz sample rate, only two chan- nels are allowed. Meridian Lossless Packing (MLP) is a lossless compres- sion method that has an approximate 2:1 compression ratio. The use of MLP is optional, but the decoding capability is mandatory on all DVD-Audio players. Dolby Digital compressed audio is required for any video portion of a DVD-Audio disc.

name: DVD-Audio
desc: A digital format for high-fidelity audio content on DVD. A standalone audio format similar to SACD. Added to HDMI 1.1 in 2004.

name: DVD (Digital Video Disc)
desc: A new format for putting full length movies on a 5” CD using MPEG-2 compression for “much better than VHS” quality. Also known as Digital Versatile Disc.

name: DVD Forum
desc: An international association of hardware and media manu- facturers, software firms and other users of digital versatile discs, created for the purpose of exchanging and disseminating ideas and information about the DVD Format.

name: DV (Digital Video)
desc: This digital VCR format is a cooperation between Hitachi, JVC, Sony, Matsushita, Mitsubishi, Philips, Sanyo, Sharp, Thomson and Toshiba. It uses 6.35 mm (0.25-inch) wide tape in a range of products to record 525/60 or 625/50 video for the consumer (DV) and professional markets (Panasonic’s DVCPRO, Sony’s DVCAM and Digital-8). All models use digital intra-field DCT-based “DV” compression (about 5:1) to record 8-bit component digital video based on 13.5 MHz luminance sampling.

name: DVD-Interactive
desc: DVD-Interactive is intended to provide additional capa- bility for users to do interactive operation with content on DVDs or at Web sites on the Internet. It will probably be based on one of three technolo- gies: MPEG-4, Java/HTML, or software from InterActual.

name: DVD Multi
