ETSI has now become a leading body on all telecommunications standards, however, and provides a strong input to international bodies. This being so, the ETSI focuses on standards that involve interactions between public and private networks, and specifies the framework of activities that form the telecommunications infrastructure. ETSI produces standards through a number of technical committees, and utilizes project teams composed of paid experts to pro- duce drafts of standards. The standards produced are called European Telecommunications Standards (ETS) or Interim European Telecommunications Standards (I-ETS).

name: ETT
desc: See Extended Text Table.

name: ETV (Educational Television)
desc: A term applied to any television program or equipment related to some form of education or instruction.

name: Euphonic
desc: Pleasing. As A Descriptive Audio Term, Usually Refers To A Coloration Or Inaccuracy That None-The-Less May Be Sonically Pleasing.

name: Eureka
desc: A massive European research effort, sometimes called the European version of Star Wars, embracing many separate R&D projects, including semiconductors, telecommunications, and computers. The Eureka EU-95 project is about ATV systems for 625 scanning line/50 field per second countries.

name: Eurocable
desc: See Iec Cable

name: EuroDAB
desc: This is an organization formed through the EBU with the pur- pose of paving the way for DAB in Europe. The group, which holds more than 100 broadcasters, manufacturers, regulators, etc., looks into services to be offered, identified features and applications, it researches data services and receiver implementation, and monitors national regulations. Finally, the group is analyzing satellite DAB projects.

name: Europe
desc: A geographic region that led the opposition to the ATSC proposal when it was presented to the CCIR as a proposed worldwide standard and is developing its own ATV systems. European television currently has 625 scanning lines and 50 field per second as opposed to NTSC’s 525/59.94.

name: Evaluator
desc: Equipment that evaluates physical and magnetic quality of tape, usually provided as an adjunct to a winder/cleaner. In contrast to a certifier, it does not stop when it detects an error.

name: E-Value
desc: The difference in inches between the radii of the outside layer of tape in a roll and the outside edge of the reel flange.

name: Even Field
desc: In a 2:1 interlaced system, the field that begins with a broad pulse halfway between two line syncs. For NTSC that is line 262-1/2 – 525, for PAL that is line 312-1/2 – 625.

name: Even Number
desc: The number of scanning lines per frame possible in a progressively scanned television system. An interlaced scan system must use an odd number of lines so that sequential fields will be displaced by one scanning line.

name: Event
desc: a) An event is defined as a collection of elementary streams with a common time base, an associated start time, and an associated end time. b) A grouping of elementary broadcast data streams with a defined start and end time belonging to a common service, e.g., first half of a football match, News Flash, first part of an entertainment show.

name: Event Number
desc: Number assigned by the system (or editor) to each edit that is recorded in the EDL.

name: Exabyte
desc: An 8 mm data tape format. Popular for storing graphics files due to its low cost and high capacity (commonly 8 GB, but new models hold up to 40 GB). Exabyte is also the number of bytes that comes after petabyte.

name: Excursion
desc: The amplitude difference between two levels.

name: exec or exex
desc: abbreviations for ' executive ' or ' executives '

name: Execute (Cycle)
desc: Last cycle of instruction execution. During this time, the instruction operation is performed.

name: Execution Time
desc: Time required for the execution of an instruction.

name: executive producer
desc: the person who is responsible for a film's financing , or forarranging the film's production elements ( stars , screenwriter ,etc. )

name: Executor
desc: In MA Lighting grandMA consoles, an executor is a physical or virtual button, fader, or encoder that can be assigned a sequence, cue stack, special, or macro to control a lighting state or function. Equivalent to a master on other consoles.

name: exhibitor
desc: term meaning ' movie theatre owner ' ; aka knownas exhib ( shortened term )

name: Exif (Exchangeable Image Format)
desc: A file format used in digital cameras.

name: Exit
desc: The point at which an edit will end (normally displayed by time code).

name: Exoskeleton
desc: Mechanically Linked Structure For Control Of And Feedback From An Application. {{Photo}}

name: Expander
desc: A device which increases the dynamic range of a signal by either reducing the level of soft signals or increasing the level of loud signals when the input is above or below a certain threshold level.

name: Expansion
desc: An undesired increase in amplitude of a portion of the com- posite video signal relative to that of another portion. Also, a greater than proportional change in the output of a circuit for a change in input level.For example, expansion of the sync pulse means an increase in the per- centage of sync during transmission.

name: Expansion Slot
desc: Electrical connection slot mounted on a computer’s motherboard (main circuit board). It allows several peripheral devices to be connected inside a computer.

name: Expansion slots
desc: Slots inside a cabinet used to connect additional circuit modules or cards.

name: experimental film
desc: refers to a film , usually a low - budget or indie film not orientedtoward profit - making , that challenges conventional filmmakingby using camera techniques , imagery , sound , editing , and / oracting in unusual or never - before - seen ways ; sometimesakaavante - garde , art films

name: Explicit Scene Description
desc: The representation of the composition information based on a parametric description (syntax and semantic) of the spatio-temporal relationships between audiovisual objects, as opposed to Implicit Scene Description.

name: exploitation film
desc: a sensational , often trashy B - film aimed at a particularaudience and designed to succeed commercially andprofitably by appealing to specific psychological traits orneeds in that audience without any fuller analysis orexposition ; often refers to films with extremely violent orsexual scenes ; not necessarily a derogatory term ; varioustypes include blaxploitation , sexploitation , splatter films .

name: Exponent
desc: Power of ten by which a number is multiplied, used in floating point representation. For example, the exponent in the decimal number 0.9873 x 107 is 7.

name: Export
desc: To use NFS software to make all or part of your file system available to other users and systems on the network.

name: exposition
desc: the conveyance ( usually by dialogue or action ) of importantbackground information for the events of a story ; or the setupof a film's story , including what's at stake for the characters ,the initial problem , and other main problems .

name: Exposure
desc: The Amount Of Time Light Is Allowed To Hit The Sensor.

name: Exposure Sheet
desc: In a piece of animation there are hundreds of frames. Typically, they are organized on an exposure sheet. The sheet describes, for each piece of artwork used, on which frame the art is first used, what happens to it (on a frame by frame basis) while it is used, and on which frame it disappears. Also noted on the sheet, for each frame, are any changes in the animation system (animation table, camera, lights, etc.). Exposure sheets on the PictureMaker are created using the SEQ program, and are organized somewhat differently than traditional sheets, in order to best use the computer. Each level (or layer, or plane) can be one of three types: Image (a file of pixel values), object (a 3D database and animation path), and explicit command (a PictureMaker command mode command). Each level specifies a beginning from and duration (ending frame), and the computer keeps track of all levels with respect to their overlaps in both time and space.

name: expressionism( and expressionist )
desc: refers to the distortion of reality through lighting , editing , andcostumes , to reflect the inner feelings and emotions of thecharacters and / or the filmmaker ; a cinematic style of fantasyfilm common in post - WWI Germany in the 1920s and 1930s ,characterized by dramatic lighting , dark visual images andshadows , grotesque and fantastic shots , distorted sets andangles , heavy makeup , highly stylized acting , and symbolicmime - like action and characters ; opposed to realism .

name: Expressionism (theatre)
desc: A theatrical style emphasizing emotional content over realism, characterized by heavy symbolism, abstract sets and costumes, and manipulation of the pace of action.

name: Extended End Prong Base
desc: A Lamp Base, Approximately 1-1/4" Deep, With Two Flat, Parallel Contacts Protruding From The Bottom.

name: Extended/Enhanced Definition Television (EDTV)
desc: a) Extended (or Enhanced) Definition Television is a proposed intermediate television system for evolution to full HDTV that offers picture quality substantially improved over conventional 525-line or 625-line receivers, by employing techniques at the transmitter and at the receiver that are transparent to (and cause no visible quality degradation to) existing 525-line or 625-line receivers. One example of EDTV is the improved separation of luminance and color components by pre-combing the signals prior to transmission. Also see Improved Definition Television. b) Specifically a video format with sampling frequencies 18 MHz (Y), 4.5 MHz (C), and resolution 960 pixels by 576 lines (Y), 480 pixels by 288 lines (C).

name: Extended Reality, Cross Reality (XR)
desc: An Umbrella Term For Virtual  Reality, Augmented  Reality, And Mixed  Reality.

name: Extended   Reality   (XR)
desc: Extended   Reality   (XR)   is   an   evolving paradigm. The XRSI definitions characterize important aspects of XR and are intended to serve as a means for a broad comparison of XR technologies and environments. This document also provides a baseline on what Extended Reality (XR) is and how to best use it. The definitions and taxonomies defined in this document are not intended to prescribe or constrain any particular dimension, timeline, or environment of immersive technologies.

name: Extended Studio PAL
desc: A 625-Line Video Standard That Allows Processing Of Component Video Quality Digital Signals By Composite Pal Equipment. The Signal Can Be Distributed And Recorded In A Composite Digital Form Using D2 Or D3 Vtrs.

name: Extended Text Table (ETT)
desc: The optional ATSC PSIP table that carries long descriptions of events and channels. There are two types of ETTs: Channel ETTs, which carry channel descriptions, and Event ETTs, which carry event descriptions.

name: Extensibility
desc: A property of a system, format, or standard that allows changes in performance or format within a common framework, while retaining partial or complete compatibility among system that belong to the common framework.

name: Extension
desc: A Catch-All Term Used To Describe Any Item That Stretches The Reach Of, Or Increase The Length Of Something, E.G., Side Arms, Extension Arms, Stage Cables, Etc.

name: Extension Hanger
desc: A Telescoping Grip Hanger.

name: Exterior Lighting
desc: Lighting For The Outside Of A Building, Including  Decorative And Functional Lighting.

name: External Device
desc: In computer systems, any piece of hardware that is attached to the workstation with a cable.

name: External Key Input
desc: Extra key inputs that may be accessed by keyboard that do not appear on the bus rows. Traditionally these inputs are used only for luminance keys, such as simple character generators or titling cameras, however, they are not limited to this on Ampex switchers. These are sources 9 and 0 on 4100 series switchers, and 31 and 32 on AVC switchers.

name: External Key Processor
desc: See Processed External Keys.

name: External Synchronization
desc: A means of ensuring that all equipment is synchronized to the one source.

name: extra ( s )
desc: a person who appears in a movie in a non - specific , nonspeaking , unnoticed , or unrecognized character role , such aspart of a crowd or background , e.g. , a patron in a restaurant ,a soldier on a battlefield ; usually without any screen credit ;also termed atmosphere people ; contrast with walkon and non - speaking role , bit players , or principals ; alsosee cast of thousands

name: Extreme Close-Up Shot (Ecu)
desc: A Shot That Shows Only Part Of A Person Or A Part Of An Object, For Example, A Person’S Eye With A Tear Falling From It Or A Detail Of A Machine.

name: Extrinsic Joint Loss
desc: Optical signal loss at a joint not intrinsic to the fibers — caused by misalignment, end separation, or imperfections in the end finish.

name: Eye
desc: The Fresnel Lens Of A Fresnel Spotlight.

name: Eyeball  In  The  Hand
desc: A Metaphor For Visualized Tracking Where The Tracker Is Held In The Hand And Is Connected To Motion Of The Projection Point Of The Display.

name: “Eyeball” Luminaire
desc: A Recessed Luminaire With A Partially Recessed Sphere That Can Be Rotated To Provide Adjustable, Directional Lighting.

name: EYEBOLTS
desc: EYEBOLTS There are two types (22): 0 Shouldered - used for vertical and angular lifts; when used for angular lifts the Safe Working Load (SWL) is to be down rated as shown in Table 1. Angles less than 45 degrees are prohibited. Shoulder must be flush with the surface and screw. 0 Unshouldered - for vertical lies only, angular lifts will bend threaded shaft.

name: Eye Clearance
desc: The Most Accurate Figure Of Merit Used To Describe The Hmo Positioning Relative To The Eye.

name: Eye Diagram
desc:
A means to display the health of the Physical Layer of the
digital data. It is formed by overlaying segments of the sampled digital 
signal in much the same way as a waveform monitor overlays lines of a
video signal to produce the familiar line display. By providing enough of 
the sample digital segments the eye display is produced and should ideally
conform to the digital standards for the appropriate format.

name: Eyedropper
desc: A tool for taking a color from a screen image and using that color for text or graphics. 88 www.tektronix.com/video_audio

name: Eyeline
desc: Where An Actor Looks During A Scene ; Must Match To Preserve Spatial Continuity Shot To Shot .Eyelines Can Present A Particular Challenge In Virtual Production When Characters May Befilmed Asynchronously Or At Varying Scales .

name: eyeline match
desc: a cut between two shots that creates the illusion of thecharacter ( in the first shot ) looking at an object ( in the secondshot ) .

name: Eye Pattern
desc: The representation of a digital signal on an oscilloscope by repetitively sampling a digital data signal. Distortion appears as closure of the eye. Used to evaluate digital signal quality through cables, switchers, and other electronics.

name: Eye Tracking
desc: Measurement Of The Direction Of Gaze.

name: Fabry-Perot (FP)
desc: A standard laser diode using a laser oscillator comprised of two mirrors with an amplifying medium between them.

name: Facade Lighting
desc: Floodlighting  The Exterior Of A Structure For Security Or For Illuminating Architectural Features.

name: FACECAP
desc: Refers To The Capturing Of The Physical Movements Of A Person’S Face And Translating It Into A Digital Model. A Head Mounted Camera Records Facial Expressions And / Or Dialogue To Fully Capture The Intricacies Of A Performance For A Digital Character, From Blinks To Smiles And Frowns.

name: Facepanel
desc: The physical front panel of an Eos Family console containing the keypad, encoder wheels, playback faders, and hardkeys. Remote facepanels (RFP) allow additional control surfaces to be connected over the network.

name: Facial Capture
desc: The Capture Of Facial Expression Using A Head Mounted Camera Or Other Methods ; A Part Ofperformance Capture .

name: Facial PerformanceReplacement ( FPR )
desc: A Form Of Facial Capture Intended To Replace An Original Facial Performance While Retaining Theoriginal Body Performance .

name: Factor of Safety
desc: The Ratio Between The Rated Working Load Of A Component Or System And Its Minimum Ultimate Breaking Strength.

name: Fade
desc: To Gradually Increase Or Decrease The Intensity Of Light.

name: Fade In
desc: The Gradual Increase In Intensity Of Light.

name: Fade Out
desc: The Gradual Decrease In Intensity Of Light.

name: Fader
desc: The console control which allows an operator to perform manual dissolves, fades and wipes.

name: Fader Bar
desc: A vertical slide controller on audio and video equipment.

name: Fader layer
desc: On digital consoles, a bank of physical faders that can be switched to control different sets of input or output channels. For example, a 24-fader console may control 48 inputs by switching between Layer 1 and Layer 2.

name: Fader pair
desc: On Eos Family consoles, the main playback faders typically function as a pair — one controlling fade-up intensity (A fader) and one controlling fade-down intensity (B fader) for smooth theatrical crossfades. The pair must be raised for cue playback.

name: Fader wing
desc: An ETC accessory adding additional playback faders to an Eos Family system. Connects via USB or Ethernet and runs in tandem with the main console to provide more simultaneous playback masters.

name: Fade to Black
desc: a) This is a video editing term that describes switching from one video source to a black level or from black to a video signal. This is commonly called a “fade to black” or “fade from black”. b) The picture luminance is reduced until the screen is black.

name: Fail Safe Brake
desc: A Brake That Will Fully Engage And Resist Motion Of The Device If Power Or Control Signal Is Lost. For Example: A Spring Applied, Electrically Released Brake.

name: Fair Use
desc: Fair Use Is The Limited Use Of Copyrighted Material Based On The Purpose And Character, Nature Of The Copied Work, Amount And Substantiality And The Effect Upon The Work'S Value

name: Fall Arrest System
desc: A Device That Engages To Halt A Person Or Other Load That Has Exceeded A Predetermined Speed, Indicating A Falling Condition.

name: Falling Edge
desc: High-to-low logic or analog transition.

name: Fall Time
desc: Usually measured from the 10% to the 90% amplitude points of a negative going transition. See Rise Time.

name: False perspective
desc: Scenic technique where pieces of scenery are built in unrealistically decreasing sizes from downstage to upstage, creating an illusion of greater depth than actually exists.

name: Family Of Curves
desc: A Composite Graph Showing On One Chart Sev_x0002_Eral Examples Of Possible Eq Curves For A Given Equalizer Or Equalizer Section.

name: Fan-In
desc: Electrical load presented by an input. Usually expressed as the number of equivalent standard input loads.

name: Fanning
desc: A console technique for quickly assigning an evenly spread range of values across multiple selected fixtures in a single operation. For example, fanning intensity across 10 fixtures from 10% to 100%.

name: Fan-Out
desc: Electrical load that an output can drive. Usually expressed as the number of inputs that can be driven.

name: Fan-Out Kit
desc: In fiber optics, a kit for use with loose tube cable allowing termination and protection of bare fiber bundles in each buffer tube.

name: FAP (Face Animation Parameters)
desc: Represents a complete set of facial actions; allows representation of most of the natural facial expressions.

name: FAPU (Facial Animation Parameter Units)
desc: The amount of displace- ment described by a FAP is expressed in specific measurement units, called Facial Animation Parameter Units (FAPU), which represent fractions of key facial distances. Rotations are instead described as fractions of a radian.

name: Farad
desc: Unit of measurement for capacitance. More commonly stated in picofarads (10⁻¹² of a farad).

name: farce
desc: refers to a light - hearted , gleeful , often fast - paced , crudelyhumorous , contrived and ' over - the - top ' comedy that broadlysatirizes , pokes fun , exaggerates , or gleefully presents anunlikely or improbable stock situation ( e.g. , a tale of mistakenidentity , cross - dressing , etc. ) often characterized by slapstick ,pratfalls , and other physical antics ; types of farcesinclude screwball comedy , bedroom / sex farce / comedy ;contrast to parody and satire .

name: Far Cyc
desc: A Cyclorama Light Placed At A Distance From The Cyclorama, Generally 8' Or More.

name: Far end
desc: In videoconferencing, the party or group at the distant site you are connecting to.

name: Far Field
desc: That Part Of The Sound Field In Which Sound Pressure Decreases Inversely With Distance From The Source. This Corresponds To A Reduction Of Approximately 6 Db In Level For Each Doubling Distance.

name: Faroudja
desc: Yves Faroudja and Faroudja Laboratories. First to market an advanced NTSC encoder with pre-combing; proponent of the Super-NTSC ATV system and of a 1050 scanning line (900 active line), progressive scan, 29.97 frame per second, 1.61:1 aspect ratio HDEP system.

name: FAS (Frame Alignment Signal)
desc: The distinctive signal inserted in every frame or once in frames that always occupies the same relative position within the frame and is used to establish and maintain frame alignment, i.e. synchronization.

name: Fast Forward
desc: The provision on a tape recorder permitting tape to be run rapidly through it in normal play direction, usually for search purposes.

name: Fast Forward Playback
desc: The process of displaying a sequence, or parts of a sequence, of pictures in display-order faster than real-time.

name: Fast Fourier Transform (Fft)
desc: The Fft Is An Algorithm, Or Digital Calculation Routine, That Efficiently Calculates The Diusing An Fft Analyzer.  The Application Of The Fourier Transform To A Signal To Determine Its Spectrum.Screte Fourier Transform From The Sampled Time Waveform. In Other Words It Converts, Or "Transforms" A Signal From The Time Domain Into The Frequency Domain.

name: fast motion( or acceleratedmotion )
desc: a camera device or effect to compress reality and highlight ascene or cause a dramatic effect , created by filming a scenewith the film running at a rate less than the normal 24 framesper second and then projecting it back at standard speed ,thereby creating the effect of moving faster than normal ;generally used for comic effect ; contrast to slowmotion ortime - compression .

name: Fast-Page Mode
desc: A read or write mode of DRAMs characterized by a decrease in cycle time of about 2-3 times and a corresponding increase in performance. The data accessed in Fast-Page Mode cycles must be adjacent in memory. See EDO.

name: Fast Reverse Playback
desc: The process of displaying the picture sequence in the reverse of display order faster than real-time.

name: FAT (File Allocation Table)
desc: A file system used on MS-DOS and Windows computers.

name: Father
desc: The metal master disc formed by electroplating the glass master. The father disc is used to make mother discs, from which multiple stam- pers (sons) can be made.

name: Fault
desc: In fiber optics, any part of an optical fiber that deviates from normal performance.

name: Fault Finder
desc: A simplified OTDR used to detect breaks within an optical fiber run. Also known as a Fiber Break Locator.

name: FBA (Face and Body Animation)
desc: A collection of nodes in a scene graph which are animated by the FAB (Face and Body Animation) object bitstream.

name: FC-AL (Fiber Channel-Arbitrated Loop)
desc: Architecture used to maintain high data transfer rates over long distances. With FC-AL storage arrays can be separated by as much as 20 kilometers, connected by only one non-amplified Fibre Channel fiber optic link. In the dual-loop architecture, data transfer rates can reach 200 Mbps. Another advantage is increased fault tolerance. In the unlikely event of a drive failure, port bypass circuits single out each failed drive and quickly route around it, with no limitation on the number of drives that can be bypassed.

name: FCC
desc: Federal Communications Commission. The US government agency controlling all policy for broadcast airwave use.

name: FCC 73.699
desc: Federal Communications Commission (FCC) NTSC video signal specifications standard.

name: FCC (Federal Communications Commission)
desc: a) The government agency responsible for (among other things) the regulation of the electro- magnetic spectrum utilization in the U.S., and the body that licenses radio and television broadcast stations. The FCC is an independent government agency, which answers directly to Congress. b) The FCC rules and regulations constitute mandatory standards for broadcasters, CATV operators, transmission organizations, and others. See also ACATS.

name: F-Connector
desc: A video connector characterized by a single metal wire. F-connectors may be either push-on or screw-post.

name: FDC (Final Committee Draft)
desc: This is the final public form of the Committee Draft of a proposed international standard, and must be identified as such before being submitted for a four-month approval ballot amongst the Participating Member Bodies of the Subcommittee.

name: FDDI
desc: Fiber Distributed Data Interface. Standards For A 100 Mbps Local Area Network, Based Upon Fiber Optic Or Wired Media Configured As Dual Counter Rotating Token Rings. This Configuration Provides A High Level Of Fault Tolerance By Creating Multiple Connection Paths Between Nodes--Connections Can Be Established Even If A Ring Is Broken.

name: FDDI (Fiber Distributed Data Interface)
desc: Standards for a 100 Mbps local area network, based upon fiber optic or wired media configured as dual counter rotating token rings. This configuration provides a high level of fault tolerance by creating multiple connection paths between nodes, connections can be established even if a ring is broken.

name: FDIS (Final Draft International Standard)
desc: This is the final form of a proposed standard before it is adopted as an International Standard. An approved Final Committee Draft, modified as necessary to accommodate comments submitted by National Bodies during, or after, the approval ballot, must first be registered as a Final Draft International Standard, and then submitted to a two-month letter ballot amongst Participating Member Bodies of JTC1.

name: FDM (Frequency Division Multiplex)
desc: A technology that transmits multiple signals simultaneously over a single transmission path, such as a cable or wireless system. Each signal travels within its own unique frequency range (carrier), which is modulated by the data (text, voice, video, etc.).

name: Feather
desc: Placing an object in front of a light so that the edge of the light output is blocked, resulting in a blurred, soft edge.

name: Feathering
desc: A tool that tapers the values around edges of binary alpha mask for composition with the background.

name: Feature Connector
desc: An expansion connector on the VGA that can accept or drive video signals to or from the VGA. This is used in applications involving video overlay. This is also called VESA Pass-Through Connector.

name: feature ( film )
desc: a " full - length " motion picture , one greater than 60 minutes inlength - but usually about 90-120 minutes on one particulartopic ; also known as a theatrical ; contrast to shorts .

name: featurette
desc: a term often used before the 1970s to refer to a 20 to 45minute film ( longer than a short subject but shorter thanafeature film ) , usually a " making of " or " behind the scenes "mini - documentary , or an extended trailer , which was usuallydisplayed by theater owners to " sell " a film for exhibition intheir movie house -- nowadays , featurettes are commonly runon premium cable stations , or offered as a ' bonus feature ' aspart of a DVD's extras ; see also " Making of ... "

name: FEC
desc: Forward Error Correction. A system of error control where the sender adds redundant data to messages, allowing the receiver to detect and correct errors without requesting retransmission.

name: FEC (Forward Error Correction)
desc: a) A system in which redundancy is added to the message so that errors can be corrected dynamically at the receiver. b) Error control bits added to useful data in the QAM/QPSK modulator.

name: FED
desc: Field Emissive Display. Similar to CRTs — phosphor is excited by electrons through a vacuum. Emissive display requiring no backlight, providing equal brightness at all viewing angles.

name: Feed
desc: The Live Or Nearly Live Footage Coming From Cameras , Real - Time Engines , Or Other Live - Actionsources . Often Used For Remote Collaboration To Provide Camera Feeds Remotely To Editorsand Other Crew Members To Assist With Rapid Feedback .

name: Feedback
desc: a) Information from one or more outputs to be used as inputs in a control loop. b) A loop caused by audio or video signal being fed back into itself. In video the effect is caused when a camera is directed at its receiving monitor. In audio the effect, manifested as an echo or squeal,is caused when a microphone is aimed at a speaker. c) A loud squeal or howl caused when the sound from a loudspeaker is picked up by a nearby microphone and reamplified. Also caused when the output of a tape recorder is fed back into the record circuit.

name: Feeder Cables
desc: A Set Of Electric Cables, Usually Individually Insulated Conductors With A High Ampacity, Used To Remotely Connect Portable Dimmer Racks, Power Distribution Racks, And The Like, To The Electrical Supply. They Are Usually Of The Wire Types W Or Sc, And Are Often Provided With Cam-Lok Connectors.

name: Feeders
desc: See Leads, Short For Feeder Cables.

name: Feed Reel
desc: Also called “stock”, “supply” or “storage” reel. The reel on a tape recorder from which tape unwinds as the machine records or plays.

name: " feel good " film ( ormovie )
desc: usually a light - hearted , upbeat comedy or romance that endswith an audience - pleasing conclusion ; sometimes usedderogatively ; compare to tearjerker

name: Female
desc: A Term Applied To A Connector That Contains The Holes And/Or Slots For Receiving The Pins, Prongs, Blades And/Or Tabs Of A Male Connector. The Female Connector Should Always Be Attached To The Line Side Of A Circuit.

name: Female Connector
desc: A connector that has indentations or holes into which you plug a male connector. An example of a female connector is an electrical wall outlet that accepts and electrical plug.

name: FEP
desc: Fluoro Ethylene Polymer. Teflon FEP used as foaming dielectric in plenum-rated coax products.

name: Ferrichrome
desc: A relatively recent word describing the technique of dual coating with both a layer of gamma ferric oxide and a layer of chromium dioxide. An intermediate level bias position used only for ferrichrome tapes.

name: Ferrule
desc: A precision tube that centers an optical fiber for stabilization and precise alignment. May be part of a connector or mechanical splice.

name: Ferrule Connector (FC)
desc: A screw-type optical fiber connector with a keying mechanism. Designated as FC/PC, FC/SPC, or FC/APC based on polishing finish.

name: festival
desc: an event at which films can often be premiered , exhibited ,awarded , and engaged in distribution deals , such as Cannes ,Toronto , Sundance , etc .; also known as fest

name: Fetch
desc: Reading an instruction from memory.

name: FF
desc: See Full Field.

name: FFT
desc: Fast Fourier Transform. Converts analog waveforms into a form easily analyzed for DSP applications.

name: FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)
desc: A mathematical means of converting time domain information to frequency domain information.

name: FGS (Fine Grain Scalability)
desc: A tool that allows small quality steps by adding or deleting layers of extra information. It is useful in a number of environments, notably for streaming purposes but also for dynamic(statistical) multiplexing of pre-encoded content in broadcast environments.

name: FH
desc: Line frequency (horizontal) 15,734 lines/sec Hz for NTSC (525 lines x 29.97 Hz).

name: Fiber
desc: The basic optical transmission element consisting of a core, cladding, and protective coating. Optical properties of the core and cladding contain light within the core as it travels.

name: Fiber Break Locator
desc: An instrument used as a simplified method to locate breaks within an optical fiber. Also known as a Fault Finder.

name: Fiber bundle
desc: A Group Of Parallel Optical Fibers Contained Within A Common Jacket.

name: Fiber Channel
desc: See Fibre Channel.

name: Fiber Coating
desc: A coating surrounding the cladding of an optical fiber to protect it from handling and the environment during manufacturing.

name: Fiber Distribution Unit (FDU)
desc: An enclosure that houses and organizes groups of optical fibers.

name: Fiber optic
desc: A transmission medium using glass or plastic fibers to carry modulated light signals. Immune to EMI, supports very high bandwidth (Tbps) over long distances. Single-mode: 9μm core, long distance. Multimode: 50/62.5μm core, shorter distance.

name: Fiber-Optic Cable
desc: “Wires” made of glass fiber used to transmit video, audio, voice or data providing vastly wider bandwidth than standard coaxial cable.

name: Fiber optics
desc: Thin Glass Filaments Within A Jacket That Optically Transmits Images Or Signals In The Form Of Light Around Corners And Over Distances With Extremely Low Losses.

name: Fiber Surface Finish
desc: A term describing the quality of polishing at the end of a fiber.

name: Fiber to the Building (FTTB)
desc: Fiber optic service to a business or building.

name: Fiber to the Curb (FTTC)
desc: Fiber optic service to a neighborhood node that then feeds homes via copper wiring.

name: Fiber to the Desk (FTTD)
desc: Fiber optic runs to individual desktop workstations.

name: Fiber to the Home (FTTH)
desc: Fiber optic service to individual homes.

name: Fibre Channel
desc: A high speed data link planned to run up to 2 Gbps on a fiber optic cable. A number of manufacturers are developing products to utilize the Fiber Channel-Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL) serial storage interface at 1 Gbps so that storage devices such as hard disks can be connected. Supports signaling rates from 132.8 Mbps to 1,062.5 Mbps, over a mix- ture of physical media including optical fiber, video coax, miniature coax, and shielded twisted pair wiring. The standard supports data transmission and framing protocols for the most popular channel and network standards including SCSI, HIPPI, Ethernet, Internet Protocol, and ATM.

name: Fibre Channel (also Fiber Channel)
desc: A High Speed Data Link Planned To Run Up To 2 Gbps On A Fiber Optic Cable. A Number Of Manufacturers Are Developing Products To Utilize The Fiber Channel--Arbitrated Loop (Fc-Al) Serial Storage Interface At 1 Gbps So That Storage Devices Such As Hard Disks Can Be Connected. Supports Signaling Rates From 132.8 Mbps To 1,062.5 Mbps, Over A Mixture Of Physical Media Including Optical Fiber, Video Coax, Miniature Coax, And Shielded Twisted Pair Wiring. The Standard Supports Data Transmission And Framing Protocols For The Most Popular Channel And Network Standards Including Scsi, Hippi, Ethernet, Internet Protocol, And Atm.

name: Fiducial Marker
desc: A Reference Object Visible To An Imaging System Such As A Camera , For Tracking Purposes , Suchas Motion Capture .

name: Field
desc: a) In interlaced scan systems, the information for one picture is divided up into two fields. Each field contains one-half of the lines required to produce the entire picture. Adjacent lines in the picture are in alternate fields. b) Half of the horizontal lines (262.5 in NTSC and 312.5 in PAL) needed to create a complete picture. c) One complete vertical scan of an image. In a progressive scanning system, all of the scanning lines compris- ing a frame also comprise a field. d) An area in a window in which you can type text. e) A television picture is produced by scanning the TV screen with an electron beam. One complete scan of the screen is called a field. Two fields are required to make a complete picture, which is called a frame. The duration of a field is approximately 1/60 of a second in NTSC and 1/50 or 1/60 of a second in PAL. f) One half of a complete interlaced video picture (frame), containing all the odd or even scanning lines of the picture.

name: Field Alias
desc: An alias caused by interlaced scanning. See also Interlace Artifacts.

name: Field Angle
desc: The Angle Of The Vertex Of A Cone Shaped Beam Where The Perimeter Of The Base Is Defined By Where The Intensity Is 10% Of The Maximum Intensity.

name: Field Blanking
desc: Refers to the part of the signal at the end of each field that make the vertical retrace invisible. Also called vertical blanking.

name: Field Check
desc: A Visit Made To An Installation Project For The Purpose Of Obtaining Project Measurements, Checking Its Status, And Finding Potential Conflicts.

name: Field DCT Coding
desc: Discrete cosine transform coding is where every block consists of lines from one field. The chrominance blocks in the 4:2:0 format must never be coded by using field DCT coding, but it is allowed to use field based prediction for this type of block.

name: Field, Depth of
desc: a) The range of distance in subject space within which a lens (or a system) provides an image that reproduces detail with an acceptably small circle of confusion (acceptable focus) usually small enough for subjective evaluation as a “point”, defines the depth of field. Tables are calculated for lenses as a function of optical aperture and the subject distance at which they are focused. Regrettably, these calculations are strictly geometric (ignoring the possibility of diffraction effects, of all optical aberrations, and of possible differing contributions to focal length from different annuli of the optical system). Thus, the tables are at times overly optimistic. b) Depth of field for a given imaging system decreases with increasing optical aperture of that system, and decreases as the distance to the subject decreases. A “maximum acceptable” diameter for the “circle of confusion” may depend upon the resolution capabilities of the light-sensitive receptor (electronic or photographic) and of the system within which it is functioning. Quantitative measurements for actual imaging systems may be made on an optical bench. Practical determina- tions are made from subjective examination of the actual images in the system of interest.

name: Field Diameter
desc: The Diameter Of The Base Of A Cone Shaped Beam Where The Perimeter Of The Base Is Defined By Where The Intensity Is 10% Of The Maximum Intensity.

name: Field Dominance
desc: When a CAV laserdisc is placed in the still frame mode, it continuously plays back two adjacent fields of information. There are no rules in the NTSC system stating that a complete video picture has to start on field 1 or field 2. Most of the video in this program is field 1 dominant. There are two sections of the disc that are field 2 dominant. In the case of film translated to video, the start of a complete film picture changes from field 1 to field 2 about 6 times a second. There is a code in the vertical interval of the disc that tells the player on which field it can start displaying each of the disc’s still frames.

name: Field Frequency
desc: The rate at which one complete field is scanned, normally 59.94 time a second in NTSC or 50 times a second in PAL.

name: Field  Of  View  (Fov)
desc: The Angle In Degrees Of The Visual Field. Since A Human'S Two Eyes Have Overlapping 140 Degree Fov, Binocular Or Total Fov Is Roughly 180 Degrees In Most People. A Feeling Of Immersion Arises With Fov Greater Than Roughly 60 To 90 Degrees.

name: Field Period
desc: The reciprocal of twice the frame rate.

name: Field Picture
desc: A picture in which the two fields in a frame are coded independently. Field pictures always come in sets of two fields, which are called top field and bottom field, respectively. When the first field is coded as a P- or a B-picture, the second picture must be coded in the same manner; however, if the first field is coded as an I-picture, the second field may be coded as either an I-picture or a P-picture (that is predicted from the first field).

name: Field Rate
desc: Number of fields per second.

name: Field Time Linear Distortions
desc: Distortions involve signals in the 64 µsec to 16 msec range. Field time distortions cause field-rate tilt in video signals. The error is expressed in IRE or as a percentage of a reference amplitude which is generally the amplitude at the center of the line bar.These distortions will cause top to bottom brightness inaccuracies in large objects in the picture. These distortions can be measured with either a window signal or a field square wave. See Linear Distortions.

name: Field Time Waveform Distortions
desc: See Field Time Linear Distortions.

name: FIFO (First-In-First-Out)
desc: a) A memory structure in which data is entered at one end and removed from the other. A FIFO is used as a buffer to connect two devices that operate asynchronously. b) A storage device (parallel shift register) which operates as a Turing machine to buffer asynchronous data where the first data stored is the first data read out.FIFOs are used to store video and act as “rubber-band” type buffers to keep a steady video stream where memory and system clock speeds do not match. FIFOs have less delays than standard shift registers as input and output are controlled by separate clocks.

name: fifteen minutes offame '
desc: a cliched term popularized by pop artist / painter Andy Warholin the late 60s , who predicted that everyone could be famousfor 15 minutes and experience a moment of ' crowning glory ' ;aka one - hit wonders ; due to today's increasing demands forpseudo - celebrities or ' personalities ' , headline - grabbers , andthe widespread dissemination of information by cable TV , talkradio , and the WWW , it may be possible for everyone to ' baskin the limelight ' for a fleeting moment ( a flash in the pan ) .

name: Fight choreographer
desc: A specialist who designs and directs stage combat sequences to look realistic while keeping performers safe. Also called a fight director or fight captain.

name: Figure 8
desc: A Type Of Microphone Polar Pattern That Is Equally Sensitive To Sound In Front Of And Behind The Diaphragm, But Rejects Sound From The Sides.

name: Figure-8 Microphone
desc: A microphone (usually a ribbon type) whose sensitivity is greatest to front and rear, and weakest to both sides.

name: Filament
desc: The Wire Inside An Incandescent Lamp Envelope That Glows And Emits Light When Heated, I.E., When Electricity Passes Through It.

name: File
desc: A container in which you store information such as text, programs, or images.

name: File Format
desc: A general term used interchangeably for the container format. Common formats include AVI, MP4, WMV, 3GP, QuickTime, MKV, and FLV.

name: File Set
desc: A collection of files and directories.

name: File System
desc: A hierarchy of directories and files. Directories contain other directories and files; files cannot contain directories. The root (/) directory is at the top of the hierarchy. See also Format.

name: Fill
desc: To Create The Illumination Needed To Reduce Shadows In An Area Or On A Subject.

name: Fill Bus
desc: A separate bus or buses from which fill videos can be selected independently from the key source cutting the hole.

name: Filled Clip
desc: A segment of a sequence that contains no audio or video information. Filler can be added to the source monitor (or pop-up monitor) and edited into a sequence. See also Filler Proxy.

name: Filled Key
desc: A key effect in which the key source image is different from the foreground image. Areas not keyed (that is, not made transparent) in the key source image are filled with the corresponding areas of the fore- ground image.

name: Filler Proxy
desc: The result of a composition specifying media to be played for the filler clips in each track.

name: Fillers
desc: Non-conducting materials in fiber optic cable construction adding roundness, flexibility, and tensile strength.

name: Fill (Insert) Video
desc: A video signal which replaces a “hole” (video infor- mation) cut in background video by a key source.

name: Fill Light
desc: Light That Is Used To “Fill” In The Shadows

name: film
desc: ( 1 ) as a verb , to record a scene or make ( or lense ) a motionpicture ; ( 2 ) as a noun , refers to a motion picture , or ( 3 ) thethin strip of material on the film negative ( with a base andlight - sensitive coating of emulsion ) that is used to createimages through light exposure .

name: film aesthetics
desc: the examination or study of film as an art form

name: film artifact
desc: unwanted film damage that could be a defect or error - dust ,hair , specks , emulsion scratches , splices , reel - change marks ,a hiss , crackle or pop on the soundtrack , mottling of theimage , scratches on the negative being printed positive , etc .;film preservation , restoration , and archival efforts help to keepolder , decomposing , and endangered films from deterioratingand acquiring artifacts , through painstaking processes( oftendigital restoration )

name: Film Chain
desc: a) Projectors, multiplexers and cameras, connected for the purpose of transferring film to video. b) A device that transfers a film image to a video image. It is also know as a Telecine chain.

name: film clip
desc: a short section of film removed from a movie and oftenexhibited ; a part of a film , and sometimes a complete sceneor sequence , taken from a film ; similar to an excerpt .

name: film d'art
desc: an early movement in French cinema to film more respectablestage productions

name: film form
desc: refers to various technical or logistical aspects which makeup , compose , or produce a finished film , includingCinematography ( Camera Movement ) , Sound and Editing ,Lighting , Framing , Acting , and the Narrative itself

name: film gauge
desc: refers to the measurement of a width of a film strip ( inmillimeters ) used in a camera ; see 35mm , film stock ,Cinerama , Cinemascope , etc .; see also digital video

name: film grain
desc: the amount of light - sensitive material in the film's coatingoremulsion ; results can either be fine - grained ( or sharp ) - that requires more light for filming , or excessively grainy ( orcoarse ) - best for low - light situations .

name: film ( ic ) codes ( orconventions )
desc: many elements within a film ( the use of music , audio ,costuming , scripting , camera angles , framing , shot duration , acharacter's actions , etc. ) speak a ' language , ' ' grammar , ' orcode that when used by the filmmaker help the viewer tounderstand more about the plot and its characters

name: Film Loop
desc: A piece of file, quite short, which is to be played repeatedly.

name: filmmaker ( s )
desc: a collective term used to refer to a person ( s ) who have asignificant degree of control over the creation of afilm : directors , producers , screenwriters , and editors .

name: film noir
desc: a French phrase literally meaning " black film " that developedin the early 40s ; refers to a genre of mostly black / white filmsthat blossomed in the post - war era in American cinema , withbleak subject matter and a somber , downbeat tone ; the plot( often a quest ) , low - key lighting ( harsh shadowsandchiaroscuro ) often in night scenes , camera angles( oftencanted or high angle shots ) , the setting ( the gloomyunderworld of crime and corruption ) , iconography ( guns ,urban settings ) , characters ( disillusioned , jaded ) , and otherelements ( voice - overs and flashbacks ) combined to present adark atmosphere of pessimism , tension , cynicism , oroppression . Film noirs , often crime films , were usually set ingrim and seedy cities , with characters includingcriminals , anti - heroes , private detectives , andduplicitous femme fatales ; see alsotech - noir

name: filmography
desc: a comprehensive ( often chronological by year ) listing of filmsfeaturing the work of an actor / actress , director , or other crewmember ; may also be a list of films for a specific genre ortopic ; a filmographer is another term for a film - maker or aperson who studies film

name: Film Recorder
desc: A device for converting digital data into film output. Continuous tone recorders produce color photographs as transparencies, prints or negatives.

name: film review
desc: an evaluative oral or written judgment about the quality of amovie , based upon various assumptions , facts , biases , etc ;professional film reviewers are known as critics ; a film reviewusually includes a brief synopsis ( avoiding spoilers , usually ) , abalanced notation of both the film's plusses and minuses ,quotable wording , and some judgments ; more extensive , indepth film evaluations are called analytical essays .

name: ( film ) score
desc: the musical component of a movie's soundtrack , usuallycomposed specifically for the film by a film composer ; thebackground music in a film , usually specially composed forthe film ; may be orchestral , synthesized , or performed by asmall group of musicians ; also refers to the act of writingmusic for a film

name: film stock
desc: refers to film size or gauge ( 8mm , 16mm , 35mm , 70mm ,105mm , for example ) , and film speed , among other things ;also refers to raw unused , unexposed film ; various kinds offilm stock include tungsten ( for use with artificial light , usuallyindoors ) and daylight film stock ( for use with natural light ,usually outdoors )

name: Film Timecode
desc: Timecode added to the film negative during the film shoot via a film timecode generator. Film timecode numbers are synced to the film key numbers on the dailies during the telecine transfer process. A special key link reader is required for viewing the film timecode.

name: film within a film
desc: a particular story - telling approach , literally , to have one filmwithin another ; in some cases , the characters are aware ofthe ' film - within - a - film , ' and break the fourth wall and enter intoor interact with it ; aka subset film or picture within a picture

name: Filter
desc: A Frequency-Dependent Amplifier Circuit Designed To Amplify, Pass, Or Attenuate Certain Frequency Ranges.

name: Filter Artifacts
desc: Distortions introduced by filters. The most common visual artifacts introduced by filters are reduced resolution and ringing.

name: Filterbank
desc: A set of bandpass filters covering the entire media frequency range.

name: Filter, Brick Wall
desc: A low-pass filter with a steep cut-off (such as 20 dB/octave or greater), such that a negligible amount of higher frequency information passes. The filter typically has uniform group delay.

name: Filter Frame
desc: See Color Frame.

name: Filter, Gaussian
desc: A low-pass filter providing a gradual attenuation of the higher frequencies. Strictly the attenuation should follow the curve V=e^(-af^2). But the term is also applied to attenuation functions that only qualitatively resemble the precise power function.

name: Filtering
desc: A process used in both analog and digital image processing to reduce bandwidth. Filters can be designed to remove information con- tent such as high or low frequencies, for example, or to average adjacent pixels, creating a new value from two or more pixels.

name: Filter, Optical
desc: In addition to the familiar optical filters for modifying spectral energy distribution, and thereby color rendition, optical filters are also produced as low-pass filters for spatial detail in an optical image, eliminating high-frequency information that would exceed the Nyquist limit of the system and produce excessive aliasing. Many of these filters are cut from optically birefringent crystals and function by providing multiple images slightly displaced one form another so that fine detail is blurred (i.e., low-pass filtered).

name: Final Cut
desc: When The Cutting And Timing Of The Footage Is Finalized.

name: Final Pixel
desc: The Goal Of Achieving Final Image Quality Live , In - Camera , Without The Need For Additionalmajor Visual Effects Work .

name: Finite Impulse Response Filter (FIR)
desc: A digital filter that is in general, better than analog filters but also more complex and expensive. Some specialized filter functions can only be accomplished using a FIR.

name: FIR
desc: See Finite Impulse Response Filter.

name: Fire Curtain
desc: A permanent safety feature of proscenium theaters required by fire and life safety codes. Located immediately upstage of the proscenium arch, it automatically closes to seal the stage from the house in the event of a fire.

name: Fireline
desc: Firelines Are Installed Around The Perimeter Of A Stage-Proscenium Arch To Hold The Fire Safety Curtain Open. Firelines Connect The Curtain To All Manual Electrical And Heat Activated Devices That Release The Safety Curtain.

name: Fireproof curtain
desc: Also called the safety curtain or iron curtain. A fire-resistant curtain that descends to seal off the stage from the auditorium in an emergency. Required by fire regulations in most proscenium theatres.

name: Fire Safety Curtain
desc: A Curtain That Closes Automatically In Event Of A Fire To Prevent Heat, Smoke And Flames On The Stage From Reaching The Audience. The Curtain May Be Closed When The Space Is Not Occupied To Prevent Unauthorized Access And To Prevent Falls From The Edge Of The Stage.

name: Fire Up
desc: To Switch On A Luminaire Or Some Other Electrical Apparatus.

name: Firewall
desc: Network security device filtering traffic based on rules. Inspects packets by IP address, port, protocol. In enterprise AV: separates AV network from corporate network and internet.

name: FireWire
desc: Apple Computer'S Trademark For Ieee 1394. See: Ieee 1394.

name: FireWire (IEEE P1394)
desc: FireWire is a special high-speed bus standard capable of over 100 megabits/sec sustained data rate.

name: Firing Card
desc: See #1 Of Control Card.

name: Firmware
desc: Program stored in ROM. Normally, firmware designates any ROM-implemented program.

name: First Assembly
desc: The Process Of Assembling Your Video Files Into Order Using The Storyboard (Often Including Multiple Takes Of The Same Shot)

name: First Border
desc: 1) The Borderlight And/Or Its Position On The First Electric. 2) The First Teaser Curtain Upstage Of The Proscenium Arch.

name: First Electric
desc: The Electrics Pipe And/Or Its Position, Which Is Located Immediately Upstage Of The Proscenium Arch.

name: First-Frame Analysis
desc: A transparency technique wherein the first frame of the video file is a dummy frame that supplies the color or range of col- ors to be rendered as transparent: the color of the chroma-key back- ground, for example. See Transparency, Transparency Frame.

name: First Party AR AD Delivery
desc: A branded AR experience that is delivered through a brand’s proprietary app.

name: First Play PGC
desc: This Program Chain (PGC) is described in the Video Manager Information table, and has no corresponding video objects (VOB). The First Play PGC is executed at initial access, e.g. just after disc loading.

name: First surface mirror
desc: A mirror where the front surface is coated with reflective material to prevent double images (ghosting) in A/V applications.

name: fish - eye( lens )
desc: an extreme type of super wide - angle lens with a veryshortfocal point ( and nearly infinite depth - of - field ) , thatexaggerates and distorts the linear dimensions of the image ,giving it a sense of curvature

name: ' fish - out - of - water ' tale
desc: a film ( usually humorous ) in which the main character ( s ) faces' culture shock ' by being placed in unfamiliar or newsurroundings or situations

name: Fish  Tank  Vr
desc: The Egocenter Of An Observer Looking "Through" A Computer Monitor To A Virtual Outside World Using A Stereoscopic Display System. That Is, To A Person Looking Through A Stereo "Window" To A Virtual "Outside", The Person Imagines Him/Herself To Be In A Fish Tank.

name: FITS (Functional Interpolating Transformation System)
desc: A format that contains all data used to design and assemble extremely large files in a small, efficient mathematical structure.

name: Fitting
desc: See Luminaire.

name: Fit to Fill
desc: An insert edit where an incoming source clip replaces an existing segment (or gap) in the record clip. A fit to fill edit functions like a swap shot edit except that the edit sequence does not ripple. If the source clip has a different length than the segment it replaces, the source clip is shortened or lengthened proportionally to fit the duration of the replaced segment.

name: Fit Up
desc: Not Something Done By The Police (Allegedly!), But Actually Building Up The Set (Qv) On-Stage.

name: Five-Step Staircase
desc: Test signal commonly used to check luminance gain linearity.

name: Fixed data rate compression
desc: Techniques Designed To Produce A Data Stream With A Constant Data Rate. Such Techniques May Vary The Quality Of Quantization To Match The Allocated Bandwidth.

name: Fixed Focal Length Lens
desc: A lens with a predetermined fixed focal length, a focusing control and a choice of iris functions.

name: Fixed Focus
desc: A Term Used To Describe An Optical System Whereby The Lenses In A Luminaire Remain At A Fixed Distance From One Another, Although They May Move As A Group Within The System.

name: Fixed Lens System
desc: An Optical System Where The Lens Or Lenses In A Luminaire Remain Stationary, I.E., They Lack The Ability To Move.

name: Fixed-Point Representation
desc: Number representation in which the decimal point is assumed to be in a fixed position.

name: Fixed Rate
desc: Information flow at a constant volume over time. See CBR.

name: Fixed Speed
desc: A Winch That Operates At A Single Speed With No Ability To Modify The Speed. Fixed Speed Winches Are Typically Used For Low Speed Setup Or Heavy Load Applications.

name: Fix It In Pre
desc: A Philosophical Tenet Of Icvfx Referring To The Preparation Of Assets To Be Camera - Readyduring Pre - Production ; As Opposed To The Traditional Visual Effects Paradigm Of Fix It In Post .

name: Fixture
desc: A Term That Is Often Used Interchangeably With Luminaire.

name: Fixture exchange
desc: A console feature allowing one fixture type to be swapped for a different make or model while retaining as much of the existing programming as possible. Available in Titan (Avolites), grandMA3, and other professional consoles.

name: Fixture Extension Clamp
desc: A Pipe Clamp Connected By Its Base To A Narrow Pipe, Usually 24" Or More, With A Bolt And Washer On The Other End For The Purpose Of Extending The Mounting Position Of A Luminaire.

name: Fixture library (console)
desc: A database installed on a lighting console containing DMX personality files for thousands of fixture makes and models, defining how the console maps each controllable parameter to DMX channels.

name: Fixture overlap
desc: A console parameter that staggers when changes are applied across multiple selected fixtures. At 100%, all fixtures update simultaneously; at 0%, each fixture updates one after the other in selection order.

name: Fixture personality
desc: Also called a fixture profile or fixture type. A DMX mapping file in a lighting console's fixture library that tells the console how each channel of a specific fixture model maps to controllable parameters.

name: Fiz ( Focus , Iris , Zoom )
desc: A Control System Which Enables The Remote Control Of Focus , Iris , And Zoom Settings On Acamera Lens Simultaneously .

name: FL
desc: Focal Length. The distance between the center of a lens and the point where the image comes into focus. Shorter focal length = larger projected image.

name: Flag
desc: a) A variable which can take one of only two values. b) Information bit that indicates some form of demarcation has been reached, such as overflow or carry. Also an indicator of special conditions such as interrupts.

name: Flags
desc: Menu functions other than the X, Y or Z parameters which turn on/off or enable a selection of one or more system conditions.

name: Flanger
desc: A modulation effect created by mixing a signal with a slightly delayed copy of itself where the delay time varies slowly. Creates a sweeping, jet-like sound characteristic of classic rock guitar tones.

name: Flanging
desc: A Term For An Effect Similar In Sound To Phas_x0002_Ing. Before We Had Electronic Delay Units, Flang_x0002_Ing Was Accomplished By Playing Two Tape Machines In Synchronization, Then Delaying One Slightly By Rubbing A Finger On The Reel Flange. Get It?

name: Flash
desc: Momentary interference to the picture of a duration of approxi- mately one field or less, and of sufficient magnitude to totally distort the picture information. In general, this term is used alone when the impair- ment is of such short duration that the basic impairment cannot be recog- nized. Sometimes called “Hit”.

name: Flash Analog to Digital Converter
desc: A high speed digitizing device based on a bank of analog comparators. The analog value to be digitized is the input to one side of the comparators bank. The other comparators input is tied to a tap of a resistor ladder, with each comparator tied to its own tap. The input voltage at each comparators will be somewhere between the top and bottom voltages of the resistor ladder. The comparators output a high or a low based on the comparison of the input voltage to the resistor ladder voltage. This string of 1s and 0s are converted to the binary number.

name: flashback
desc: a filmic technique that alters the natural order of the narrative ;a flashback may often be the entire film ; it takes the storyorder back chronologically in time to a previous or past event ,scene , or sequence that took place prior to the present timeframe of the film ; the flashbacked story that providesbackground on action and events is often called thebackstory ;contrast to flash - forward

name: Flash button
desc: A button on a playback that adds the playback's output into the existing console output while held, without affecting other active playbacks. Used for bump effects and live event specials.

name: flash - forward( or flash - ahead )
desc: simply put , the opposite of flashback ; a filmic technique thatdepicts a scene , event or shot taking place ( or imagined ) orexpected that is projected into a future time beyond thepresent time of the film , or it can be a flashforward from thepast to the present

name: Flash Frame
desc: After a long, complex piece is edited, small bits of video might be accidentally left in a sequence. When the Timeline is zoomed to 100 percent, these small, unwanted, pieces might not be visible. An editor can find these bits using the Find Flash Frame command.

name: flash frame( or shot )
desc: a single clear frame that is inserted between two shots thatcan barely be perceived , giving the appearance of a flash ofwhite when viewed , and for the intention of producing a shockor sudden dramatic effect

name: flash in the pan
desc: transitory , impermanent success or recognition ; derived frompanning for gold experience ; see fifteen minutes of fame

name: Flashmaster
desc: In ONYX, a special fader that sets the maximum intensity level output when any flash button is pressed on any cuelist. Acts as a global flash level master across all playbacks.

name: Flash Memory
desc: Nonvolatile, digital storage. Flash memory has slower access than SRAM or DRAM.

name: FlashPix
desc: A multi-resolution image format in which the image is stored as a series of independent arrays. Developed by Kodak, Hewlett-Packard, Live Picture, Inc. and Microsoft and introduced in June 1996.

name: Flash pot
desc: A pyrotechnic device consisting of a small container loaded with pyrotechnic powder, ignited electrically to produce a flash, bang, and smoke effect on stage.

name: Flat
desc: An Oblong Frame Of Timber, Covered With Either Canvas Or Hardboard And Painted, Which Forms Part Of The Set. There Are Also Door Flats, Window Flates, Even Fireplace Flats. Canvas Flats, Being Lighter And Easier To Move Around, Are The Preferred Option, But Schools Often Go For Hardboard-Covered Flats Which Are More Hardwearing.

name: Flat Eq
desc: Describes Eq That Has No Boosted Or Reduced Frequencies.

name: Flat Field
desc: As used herein, the entire area viewed by a television camera with the viewed area being uniformly white or any single specified color or any shade of gray.

name: Flat Frequency Response
desc: An Accurate, Uncolored Reproduction Of An Audio Source Is This The Same As Equal Amplitude/Linear? Are We Talking About Sounds Flat Or Measures Flat? How Flat? A Razor Blade Is Flat Until You Look At Under An Electron Microscope.

name: Flat polish
desc: In fiber optics, a condition where the fiber and ferrule tip endfaces are polished flat.

name: Flat (response)
desc: A theoretical audio ideal where frequency response does not deviate over the audible spectrum. Speakers within ±2–3 dB of flat are considered very linear.

name: Flat (scenery)
desc: A lightweight framed scenic unit covered with canvas, muslin, or plywood, painted to represent walls or surfaces. The basic building block of theatrical scenery construction.

name: Flat Shading
desc: A polygon rendered so that its interior pixels are all the same color has been rendered with “flat” shading. An object represented by polygons that is rendered with flat shading will look distinctly faceted. No highlights or reflections are visible.

name: Flatten
desc: The process of converting a Macintosh file into a self-contained, single-forked file so that it is compatible with Windows environment. See Self-Contained, Single-Forked.

name: Flat (theatre)
desc: A relatively thin piece of scenery joined with others to create walls and vertical scenic elements. Can be soft-covered (fabric on a wooden frame) or hard-covered (plywood on a wooden frame). Hard-covered flats are sometimes called Hollywoods.

name: Fleet Angle
desc: The Angle Formed Between The Centerline Of A Sheave Or Drum And Another Sheave Or Fixed Point.

name: Fletcher-Munson effect
desc: Also called equal loudness contours. Human hearing sensitivity varies with frequency and volume — most sensitive at 3–4 kHz. The loudness control on audio systems compensates for this.

name: Flexibility Layer
desc: The MPEG-4 Systems Layer that specifies how some parts of the MPEG-4 terminal can be configured or downloaded. Two modes are identified in this layer, the non-flexible mode and the flexible mode.

name: Flexible Mode
desc: The configuration of an MPEG-4 terminal in which the capability to alter parameters or algorithms for the processing of audio- visual objects is achieved by the transmission of new classes or scripts.

name: Flexi mode
desc: An Eos display mode that shows only patched channels in the live display, hiding unpatched channel numbers for a cleaner workspace. Especially useful in larger systems with many unused channel numbers.

name: FlexMux Channel (FMC)
desc: A label to differentiate between data belonging to different constituent streams within one FlexMux stream.

name: FlexMux Entity
desc: An instance of the MPEG-4 system resource that processes FlexMux Protocol Data Units (PDUs) associated to one FlexMux stream.

name: FlexMux Layer (FML)
desc: A logical MPEG-4 Systems Layer between the Elementary Stream Layer and the TransMux Layer used to interleave one or more elementary streams, packetized in Adaption Layer protocol data unit, into one FlexMux stream.

name: FlexMux Packet
desc: The smallest data entity managed by the FlexMux tool consisting of a header and a payload

name: FlexMux Protocol Data Unit (FlexMux-PDU)
desc: The smallest protocol unit of a FlexMux stream exchanged between peer FlexMux entities. It con- sists of FlexMux-PDU Header and FlexMux-PDU Payload. It carries data from one or more FlexMux channel(s).

name: FlexMux Protocol Data Unit Header (FlexMux-PDU Header)
desc: Information preceding the FlexMux-PDU payload. It identifies the FlexMux channel(s) to which the payload of this FlexMux-PDU belongs.

name: FlexMux Stream
desc: A sequence of FlexMux packets associated with one or more FlexMux channels flowing through one TransMux channel.

name: Flex Scrim
desc: A Small Fabric Scrim That Is Not Intended For Use On An Open End Frame, I.E., They Are Intended To Dim The Full Beam.

name: flick
desc: the flickering image in early films gave rise to the generictermflicks when referring to the movies ; often used in a condescending way , such as stating that a film is a ' horrorflick ' or ' chick - flick '

name: Flicker
desc: The Strobing Of Some Luminaires That Cannot Be Visually Detected Because Of The Frequency Of Its Output Voltage, But Can Adversely Affect The Way Motion Picture Film Records Light.

name: Flicker Filter
desc: Video data from a VGA is not interlaced. This data must be converted into interlaced format for display on a TV. If every second line is discarded of the non-interlaced data, flicker may occur if, for example, video information is contained in just one noninterlaced line. Flicker will also be perceptible at the top and bottom of multilane objects. A flicker filter overcomes these problems in computing a weighted average of two or three adjacent lines (noninterlaced) for each line of output (interlaced).

name: Flicker-Free
desc: A Term Used To Describe Electronic Ballasts That Electronically Alter The Electrical Frequency That Causes Flicker.

name: Flicker Frequency
desc: The minimum rate of change of brightness at which flicker is no longer visible. The flicker frequency increases with brightness and with the amount of the visual field being stimulated. In a recent study, a still image flashed on and off for equal amounts of time was found to have a flicker frequency of 60 flashes per second at a brightness of 40 foot lamberts (fL) and 70 at 500. Television sets generally range around 100 fL in peak brightness (though some new ones claim over 700). The SMPTE recommends 16 fL for movie theater screens (though this is meas- ured without film, which reduces the actual scene brightness by at least 50 percent). One reason for interlaced scanning is to increase television’s flashing pictures to the flicker frequency, without increasing bandwidth.

name: Flies
desc: The Space Above A Stage Where Scenery, Luminaires, Etc. Are Hoisted Above Horizontal Sight Lines.

name: Flightcase
desc: A heavy-duty road case with a metal frame, reinforced corners, and removable lid used for transporting production equipment between venues. Designed to survive aircraft loading. Also called a Touring Case.

name: Flip
desc: Special effect in which the picture is either horizontally or vertically reversed.

name: Floating
desc: Logic node that has no active outputs. Three-state bus lines, such as data bus lines, float when no devices are enabled.

name: Floating-Point Representation
desc: Technique used to represent a large range of numbers, using a mantissa and an exponent. The precision of the representation is limited by the number of bits allocated to the mantissa. See Mantissa and Exponent.

name: Float mic
desc: A microphone placed at the front edge of the stage (on the stage lip or footlight position) to capture ambient stage sound and help reinforce quiet passages.

name: Floats
desc: A Rather Old-Fashioned Term For Footlights (Qv).

name: Flood
desc: 1) The Position Of A Movable Lamp, Lens, Or Pair Of Lenses On A Spotlight That Produces The Widest Field Angle.

name: Flood Lamp
desc: A Lamp That Produces A Relatively Wide Beam Of Light.

name: Flood Light
desc: A Luminaire Consisting Of A Reflector, Lamp, And Sometimes A Single Lens, Used To Direct A Large Amount Of Light On A Relatively Large Area.

name: Floor
desc: Typically Refers To The Dance Floor, Where The Audience Stands.

name: Floor Block
desc: Pulley Mounted At The Floor To Hold A Rope Or Cable In Position And To Reverse Its Direction. Floor Blocks Meant For Rope Often Incorporate A Means Of Adjustment To Accommodate Changes In Length Due To Loads Or Environmental Conditions.

name: Floor Box
desc: See Floor Pocket.

name: Floor Bracket
desc: See Floor Trunnion.

name: Floor Pocket
desc: A Stage Pocket Whose Cover Is Flush Mounted With The Floor To Which It Is Mounted.

name: Floor Surface Microphone
desc: Microphone Designed To Be Placed Directly On The Floor Synonym: Dance Mic, Floor Mic, Pmz

name: Floor Trunnion (Floor Trunion)
desc: A Metal Bracket With A Base Used To Support A Striplight. Always Used In Pairs, A Trunnion Attaches To Each End And Can Sit On A Floor Or Can Be Attached To Pipe Clamp For Hanging. They Can Also Be Provided With Casters.

name: flop
desc: a film that is a failure at the box - office ; also knownasfloppola , bomb , turkey . See Greatest All - Time Film Flops .

name: Floppy Disk
desc: Mass-storage device that uses a flexible (floppy) diskette to record information. See Disk.

name: Flowchart or Flow Diagram
desc: Graphical representation of program logic. Flowcharts enable the designer to visualize a procedure. A complete flow- chart leads directly to the final code.

name: Flown
desc: Suspended In A Manner That Permits The Equipment To Be Raised And Lowered.

name: FLSD (Fixed Linear Spline Data)
desc: The different modes used to animate a value, for example, position, color, or rotation.

name: Fluid Head
desc: Refers to a tripod mount that contains lubricating fluid which decreases friction and enables smooth camera movement.

name: Fluorescence
desc: The Property Of Certain Materials To Absorb Radiation Of Certain Wavelengths, Usually Ultraviolet, And Re-Emit The Radiation As Light.

name: Fluorescent Lamp
desc: A Lamp Consisting Of A Tube Filled With Argon, Along With Krypton Or Other Inert Gas.

name: Fluoropolymer
desc: Generic term for Teflon FEP (Fluorinated Ethylene Propylene). Used in cable jacketing and insulation for chemical resistance, broad temperature range, and excellent electrical properties.

name: Flush Mount
desc: A Term Used To Describe Anything Whose Upper Surface, When Installed, Is Flush With The Surface To Which It Was Installed. This Term Is Used To Describe Floor And Wall Pockets, Disappearing Footlights, Ceiling Ports, And Certain Types Of Connectors.

name: Flutter
desc: Distortion which occurs in sound reproduction as a result of undesired speed variations during recording or reproducing. Flutter occurring at frequencies below approximately 6 Hz is termed “wow”.

name: Flux
desc: Magnetic field generated by a record head, stored on magnetic tape, and picked up by the playback head. Also the magnetic field that exists between the poles of a magnet.

name: Flux Transition
desc: A 180 degree change in the flux pattern of a magnetic medium brought about by the reversal of the magnetic poles within the medium.

name: Flux Transition Density
desc: Number of flux transitions per track length unit.

name: Fly
desc: To Lift Scenery, Truss, Luminaires, Etc., Into The Air By Support Cables, Chain, Or Ropes, With The Aid Of Motors, Pulleys, Winches, And The Like.

name: Fly-Back
desc: See Horizontal Retrace.

name: Fly floor
desc: The working platform at the side of the fly tower from which fly operators work the flying lines to raise and lower scenery and equipment.

name: Fly Gallery
desc: A Gallery Or Catwalk Above The Stage Floor From Which Counter Weight And Hemp (Rope) Rigging Is Operated.

name: Flying
desc: The process of raising and lowering scenic pieces, lighting bars, or performers using the fly system. Safety announcements are given: 'Flying in' or 'Going out' with 'Heads' warnings.

name: Flying Erase Head
desc: The erase head mounted on the spinning (flying) video head drum. Facilitates smooth, seamless edits whenever the camcorder recording begins. Without a flying erase head, a video “glitch” may occur at scene transitions.

name: Flying Head
desc: A video head that engages when the video deck is on “pause”, providing a clear still-frame image.

name: Fly Loft
desc: The Space Between The Roof And The Performance Area That Is Not Visible To The Audience.

name: Fly Over
desc: A camera move where the operator tilts up and pans over the subject's head during a push or pull to add a transition effect.

name: Fly Pa
desc: Hanging A Speaker System In The Air, Typically With Chain Motors. This Term Can Also Be Applied To Other Production Elements, Like Lights Or Video Walls. We Say "Take It Up/Out" When Raising Items Up, And "Take It In/Down" When Lowering.

name: Flyrail
desc: 1) In Modern Theaters, It Is A Sturdy, Steel Structure With An Assortment Of Pulleys And Counterweights, And Cam-Like Clamps Or Clutch Mechanisms Which Secure The Ropes That Support The Battens And Electrics Pipes. These Apparatuses Are Often Motorized. 2) In Older Theaters, The Flyrail Is A Pinrail.

name: Flys
desc: The area above the performance space upstage of the proscenium arch where fly system equipment lives and scenery or curtains are stored when raised out of sight. Spelled flys, not flies.

name: Fly system
desc: A mechanical system above the stage consisting of battens, ropes or cables, counterweights, and pulleys used to raise and lower scenery, lighting bars, curtains, and actors.

name: Fly-Through
desc: A fly-through is a type of animation where a moving observer flies through a seemingly stationary world.

name: Fly Tower
desc: The Support Structure Mounted To The Stage Wall That Contains The Ropes Or Cables And Pulleys That Go Between The Flies And The Flyrail Or Pinrail.

name: FM
desc: See Frequency Modulation.

name: FM-FM
desc: Dual carrier FM coded discrete stereo transmissions, analogue. Can be used for bi-lingual operation under user selection, but no auto- selection is available. Audio characteristics better than standard mono soundtrack.

name: FM Recording
desc: The data signal is used to modulate the frequency of a “carrier” having a frequency much higher than any spectral component of the data signal. Permits the recording of DC or very low signal frequencies.

name: FMV
desc: See Full Motion Video.

name: F-Number
desc: In lenses with adjustable irises, the maximum iris opening is expressed as a ratio (focal length of the lens)/(maximum diameter of aperture). This maximum iris will be engraved on the front ring of the lens.

name: Foamcore
desc: A Polystyrene, Styrofoam Material Used As A Substrate For Some Reflector Boards, Effective Because Of Its Light Weight And Ease Of Mounting Via Reflector Forks.

name: Focal Length
desc: The distance between the secondary principal point in the lens and the plane of the imaging device. The longer the focal length, the narrower is the angle of view.

name: Focal Plane
desc: The Plane That Is Perpendicular To The Axis Of An Optical System And Also Contains The Focal Point.

name: Focal Point
desc: The Small Region Where A Lens Or Reflector Concentrates The Light From A Light Source.

name: focus
desc: refers to the degree of sharpness or distinctness of an image( or an element of an image such as a person , object , etc. ) ; asa verb , it refers to the manipulation or adjustment of the lensto create a sharper image ; terms related are deepfocus , shallow focus ( very common in close - ups ) , soft focus ,andrack focusing
