desc: We think of a normal key as, for example, letters superim- posed over a background. When this key is inverted, the background appears inside the key; it appears we are looking through the cut-out key and seeing the background. The key insert video appears outside the key.

name: I/O
desc: See Input/Output.

name: I/O Device
desc: Input/output equipment used to send information or data signals to and from an editing computer.

name: IO (Image Orthicon)
desc: The picture forming tube in a TV camera.

name: I/O Mapped I/O
desc: I/O devices that are accessed by using instructions and control signals that differ from those of the memory devices in a system. Assigns I/O devices to a separate address space.

name: Ion
desc: A charged atom, usually an atom of residual gas in an electron tube.

name: Ion Spot
desc: A spot on the fluorescent surface of a cathode ray tube, which is somewhat darker than the surrounding area because of bombardment by negative ions which reduce the phosphor sensitivity.

name: Ion Trap
desc: An arrangement of magnetic fields and apertures which will allow an electron beam to pass through but will obstruct the passage of ions.

name: Ion Xe
desc: ETC's mid-range Eos Family console featuring a fader wing and full Eos OS. Popular in regional theatres, broadcast, and touring for its balance of capability and portability. The current-generation successor to the Ion Classic.

name: I/O rack
desc: A remote input/output unit in a digital audio system that provides mic preamps, line inputs, and outputs at a distance from the console. Connected to the console via AES, MADI, Dante, or proprietary protocols.

name: IP
desc: See:  Tcp/Ip.

name: IP Address
desc: The number that uniquely identifies each host (system) on the network.

name: IPCGBESFX
desc: Avolites' attribute grouping system — Intensity, Position, Colour, Gobo, Beam, Effect, Special, FX (shapes). Used by Titan to group similar fixture parameters together for efficient programming, palette recording, and mask selection.

name: IPCP (Internet Protocol Control Protocol)
desc: Protocol that establishes and configures IP over PPP.

name: IP Datagram
desc: Basic unit of information that passes across a connection- less TCP/IP Internet. It contains routing source and destination addresses with the data.

name: I-Picture (Intra-Coded Picture)
desc: One of three types of digital pictures in an MPEG data stream. An I-picture is not predictive and is essentially a snapshot picture. This type of picture generally has the most data of any of the picture types. A picture coded using information only from itself. For that reason, an I-picture can be decoded separately.

name: IPI (Intellectual Property Identification)
desc: The IPI descriptor is a vehicle to convey standardized identifiers for content like international standard book number, international standard music number, or digital object identifier if so desired by the content author. If multiple media objects within one MPEG-4 session are identified by the same IPI informa- tion, the IPI descriptor may consist just of a pointer to another elementary stream, using its ES ID, that carries the IPI information.

name: IP (Index of Protection)
desc: A numbering system that describes the quality of protection of an enclosure from outside influences, such as moisture, dust and impact.

name: IP (Internet Protocol)
desc: a) IP is the basic language of the Internet. It was developed by the government for use in internetworking multiple computer networks together. b) The Network Layer protocol for the Internet protocol suite.

name: IP Link
desc: Extron's high-performance IP integration technology engineered for professional A/V environments.

name: IPMP (Intellectual Property Management and Protection)
desc: The Intellectual Property Management and Protection (IPMP) identifies carriers of creative works. The tool was developed as a complement of MPEG-4, the ISO compression standard for digital audio-visual material. Involved experts, notably those representing authors’ societies, felt that MPEG-4 needed extra rules designed to protect intellectual property. To this end, IPMP was constructed as a supplementary layer on the standard.

name: IP Multicast
desc: A system for sending IP transmissions out only one time, but allowing for multiple users to receive it. This would reduce the band- width required for audio and video broadcasting over the Internet, but it is not widely used yet.

name: IPMX
desc: IP Media Experience. AIMS open standard combining SMPTE ST 2110 (uncompressed) and compressed video transport with discovery/connection management for interoperable AVoIP systems.

name: IP netmask
desc: A 32-bit binary number used on subnets to help routers determine which traffic routes internally and which goes out to the Internet.

name: IPR (Intellectual Property Rights)
desc: The conditions under which the information created by one party may be appreciated by another party

name: IPS (Inches Per Second)
desc: The measurement of the speed of tape passing by a read/write head or paper passing through a pen plotter.

name: IPv4
desc: Internet Protocol version 4. The legacy IP version on which the Internet is based. Connectionless, best-effort delivery over packet-switched networks.

name: IPv6
desc: Internet Protocol version 6. Designed to replace IPv4 with 128-bit addressing, auto-configuration, new security features, and support for real-time communications and multicasting.

name: IPX
desc: Internetwork Packet Exchange Protocol. Commonly used over Novell Netware and Microsoft Windows networks.

name: I/Q
desc: In Phase/Quadrature Phase.

name: IQ (In-Phase/Quadrature Components)
desc: Color difference signals used in NTSC systems.U = 0.492 (B_-Y) V = 0.877 (R_-Y) I = V cos 33º _-U sin 33º Q = V sin 33º _-U cos 33º

name: IQTV (Improved Quality Television)
desc: A rarely used term for IDTV and INTSC.

name: IR
desc: Infrared. Electromagnetic radiation (700nm-1mm wavelength) used for line-of-sight remote control of AV equipment. Extended via IR blasters over CAT cable, fiber, or IP for control of remotely located equipment.

name: IR Control
desc: Infrared remote control. Sends signals via infrared light pulses with a carrier frequency between 36 kHz and 60 kHz. Restricted to line-of-sight or reflections.

name: IrDA (Infrared Data Association)
desc: A membership organization founded in 1993 and dedicated to developing standards for wireless, infrared trans- mission systems between computers.

name: IRD (Integrated Receiver Decoder)
desc: a) A combined RF receiver and MPEG decoder that is used to adapt a TV set to digital transmissions. b) An IRD with digital interface has the ability to decode partial transport streams (TS) received from a digital interface connected to digital bitstream storage device such as a digital VCR, in addition to providing the function- ality of a baseline IRD.

name: IRE
desc: Institute of Radio Engineers unit for video signal levels. In NTSC: 0 IRE = blanking level, 100 IRE = peak white, -40 IRE = sync tip. 7.5 IRE pedestal used in NTSC USA.

name: Iredale, Richard
desc: Creator of the HD-NTSC ATV scheme and the HD-PRO HDEP scheme.

name: IRE (Institute of Radio Engineers)
desc: a) The composite analog television signal’s amplitude can be described in volts or IRE units with 140 IRE rep- resenting a full amplitude composite analog signal. The 0 IRE point is at blanking level, with sync tip at -40 IRE and white extending to +100 IRE  In the studio, the composite analog video signal is typically 1 volt in ampli- tude. Thus in the studio, 1 IRE is equal to 1/140 of a volt or 7.14 mV. IRE stands for Institute of Radio Engineers, the organization which defined the unit. b) Unit of video measurement. 140 IRE measures the peak-to-peak amplitude of the video signal (including sync) and is typically 1 volt.

name: IR emitter
desc: A small infrared LED device attached to a control processor's IR output, placed over a device's IR receiver window to send control commands without network or serial connections.

name: IRE Roll-Off
desc: The IRE standard oscilloscope frequency response charac- teristic for measurement of level. This characteristic is such that at 2 MHz the response is approximately 3.5 dB below that in the flat (low frequency) portion of the spectrum, and cuts off slowly.

name: IRE Scale
desc: An oscilloscope or waveform monitor scale conforming to IRE Standard 50, IRE 23.S1 and the recommendations of the Joint Committee of TV Broadcasters and Manufacturers for Coordination of Video Levels.

name: IRE Units
desc: a) A linear scale for measuring the relative amplitudes of the various components of a television signal. Reference white is assigned a value of 100, blanking a value of 0. b) The values for NTSC composite  and for SMPTE 240M are shown in the following table. One IRE unit corre- sponds to 7-1/7 mV in CCIR System M/NTSC and to 7.0 mV in all other systems. Measurement procedure developed by the Institute of Radio Engineers, the predecessor to the IEEE.

name: IRIG (Inter-Range Instrumentation Group)
desc: Has recently been renamed “Range Control Council”.

name: IR (Infrared)
desc: An invisible band of radiation at the upper end of the elec- tromagnetic spectrum. It starts at the middle of the microwave spectrum and goes up to the beginning of visible light. Infrared transmission requires an unobstructed line of sight between transmitter and receiver. It is used for wireless transmission between computer devices as well as most remote controls for TVs and stereo equipment.

name: Iris
desc: a) The video camera’s lens opening which regulates the amount of light entering a camera. b) A means of controlling the size of a lens aperture and therefore the amount of light passing through the lens.

name: Iris Diaphragm
desc: An Arrangement Of Thin, Movable, Heat-Resistant Metal Plates, I.E., Leaves, That Form An Adjustable Circular Opening. They Are Usually Placed Within An Ellipsoidal Spotlight Or Follow Spot In Order To Adjust The Diameter Of The Beam, Or In Some Cases, To Mechanically Dim The Beam.

name: Iris (lighting)
desc: An adjustable aperture inside a lighting fixture that reduces the beam size to create a hard circular spot, controlled manually or via DMX. Used in ellipsoidals and moving heads.

name: iris( or irising )
desc: an earlier cinematographic technique or wipe effect , in theform of an expanding or diminishing circle , in which a part ofthe screen is blacked out so that only a portion of the imagecan be seen by the viewer ; usually the lens aperture iscircular or oval shaped and is often expanded or contractedas the film rolls , often from one scene to the next ; known alsoasdiaphragm . The camera movment is often termed iriswipe , circle - in / circle - out , or iris - in / iris - out ; also refers to theadjustable opening in the lens that allows light to passthrough the measurement for the iris opening is f - stop

name: Iris Slot
desc: A Narrow Opening In Some Luminaires For The Purpose Of Inserting A Drop-In Iris.

name: IRIX
desc: Silicon Graphics, Inc.’s version of the UNIX operating system. See also System Software.

name: Ir-Lamp
desc: See Infrared-Reflecting Lamp.

name: IR learning
desc: The ability of a device to receive and store infrared commands for other devices, then transmit the learned commands via an IR emitter when a specific operation is executed.

name: IR library
desc: Sets of infrared commands for video projectors, available from device manufacturers such as Extron.

name: IR Light
desc: Infrared light, invisible to the human eye. It usually refers to wavelengths longer than 700 nm. Monochrome (B/W) cameras have extremely high sensitivity in the infrared region of the light spectrum.

name: Iron
desc: The Safety Curtain (Qv).

name: Iron Oxide/Gamma Ferric Oxide
desc: The most popular oxide particle used as a magnetic recording medium produced from an oxide of pure iron.

name: Ir Par-Lamp
desc: An Infrared-Reflecting Par-Lamp. See Infrared-Reflecting Lamp.

name: IRT (Institut für Rundfunktechnik)
desc: IRT is the research and develop- ment branch of the public broadcasters in Germany (the ARD and ZDF), Austria (the ORF) and in Switzerland (the SRG). Situated in Munich, Germany, the IRT participates in both national and international research projects, and is highly involved in broadcasting system development.

name: IS-04
desc: NMOS IS-04. The AMWA specification defining how media devices advertise their capabilities to a registry for discovery and connection management in IP media networks.

name: IS-05
desc: NMOS IS-05. The AMWA specification defining how to make and break connections between media senders and receivers in an IP production network via RESTful API.

name: ISA (Industry Standard Architecture)
desc: Originally designed around the 16-bit 286 microprocessor and called the AT bus, the ISA bus has 24 address and 16 data lines, sufficient to handle 16 megabyte memory I/O addresses. The ISA bus is limited to a slow 8 MHz clock speed and for this reason, faster peripherals and memory left the ISA bus behind soon after its development. Unlike the earlier 8-bit PC/XT bus, the ISA bus includes two connectors. In addition to the single, 62-pin, 8-bit PC/XT bus connec- tor, the ISA bus includes a second connector with four additional address and eight additional data lines, interrupt, and DMA control lines. Although IBM documented every pin on the ISA bus, they never published strict timing specifications to signals on the bus. As a result, ISA bus system developers designing products for many platforms had to guess at timing. Problems developed as a result of holding the ISA bus to 8 MHz for back- ward compatibility. Some anxious manufacturers pushed the system speed causing products with marginal operations characteristics, especially when extra memory was added to high-speed PCs. Since the IEEE ISA standard of 1987, the bus signals have remained unchanged. In 1993, Intel and Microsoft announced a joint development, Plug and Play ISA, a method for making expansion boards work with the ISA bus, eliminating the need for DIP switch settings, jumpers, interrupts, DMA channels, ports, and ROM ranges. The Plug and Play card tells the host computer what resources it requires. This requires a large software-based isolation protocol which keeps an expansion board switched off until it can be addressed, allowing one card to be polled at a time because slot-specific-address enable signals for expansion cards are not part of the ISA specification. In 1987, the ISA bus made way for the IBM PS/2 “clone-killer” computer “Micro Channel” bus however, the clone makers initially ignored the PS/2 and Micro Channel.

name: ISA Slot
desc: Connection slot to a type of computer expansion bus formerly found in most computers. It is larger in size than the PCI slots found on most Pentium based computers and provides connections to the slower ISA bus.

name: ISA Transfer
desc: One of the advantages of an ISA transfer is that it allows the user to process images as they go through the processor. However, its utility is limited by its low bandwidth, Even under ideal conditions, the ISA transfer requires three to five BCLK cycles at 8 MHz to transfer a single pixel. This represents a severe system throughput penalty; a large percentage of the available (and already limited) bandwidth is consumed by the transfer.

name: ISDB
desc: Integrated Services Digital Broadcasting. Japan'S Transmission Specification For Digital Broadcasting. Isdb Uses A New Transmission Scheme Called Bst-Ofdm That Ensures The Flexible Use Of Transmission Capacity And Service Expandability In Addition To The Benefits Of Ofdm. Since Ofdm Uses A Large Number Of Carriers That Are Digitally Modulated. It Provides Sufficient Transmission Quality Under Multipath Interference. The Basic Approach Of Bst-Ofdm Is That A Transmitting Signal Consists Of The Required Number Of Narrow Band Ofdm Blocks Called Bst- Segments, Each With A Bandwidth Of 100 Khz.

name: ISDB (Integrated Services Digital Broadcasting)
desc: Japan's transmis- sion specification for digital broadcasting. ISDB uses a new transmission scheme called BST-OFDM that ensures the flexible use of transmission capacity and service expandability in addition to the benefits of OFDM. Since OFDM uses a large number of carriers that are digitally modulated. It provides sufficient transmission quality under multipath interference. The basic approach of BST-OFDM is that a transmitting signal consists of the required number of narrow band OFDM blocks called BST-segments, each with a bandwidth of 100 kHz.

name: ISDN
desc: Integrated Services Digital Network. Allows Data To Be Transmitted At High Speed Over The Public Telephone Network. Isdn Operates From The Basic Rate Of 64 Kbits/Sec To The Primary Rate Of 2 Mbps (Usually Called Isdn-30 As It Comprises 30 Basic Rate Channels). Most Of The Western World Currently Has The Capability To Install Isdn-2 With 128 Kbps And Very Rapid Growth Is Predicted For Isdn Generally. In The Television And Film Industries, Audio Facilities Are Already Using It. The Cost Of A Call Is Usually Similar To Using A Normal Telephone. Nominally Isdn Operates Internationally, But There Are Variations In Standards, Service And Isdn Adapter Technologies. Some Operators In The Usa Use A Similar System, Switch 56 (56 Kbits/Sec And Upwards), Although The Availability Of Isdn Is Becoming Wider.

name: ISI (Inter Symbol Interference)
desc: Inter Symbol Interference is the interference between adjacent pulses of a transmitted code.

name: IS (International Standard)
desc: The series of standards from ISO and its subcommittees.

name: ISMA (Internet Streaming Media Alliance)
desc: ISMA is a group of indus- try leaders in content management, distribution infrastructure and media streaming working together to promote open standards for developing

name: ISO
desc: See International Organization for Standardization.

name: ISO 2202
desc: Information Processing: ISO 7-bit and 8-bit coded character sets – Code extension techniques

name: ISO 3166
desc: Codes for the representation of names of countries.

name: ISO 3901
desc: Documentation: International Standard Recording Code (ISRC).

name: ISO 639
desc: Codes for the representation of names of languages.

name: ISO 8859-1
desc: Information Processing: 8-bit single-byte coded graphic character sets.

name: ISO 9660
desc: The international standard for the file system used by CD- ROM. Allows file names of only 8 characters plus a 3-character extension.

name: Isochronous
desc: For digital transmission, events occur with known constant periods. “Equal-time”. The synchronization signal is derived from the signal bearing the data.

name: ISO/IEC 11172
desc: Information Technology: Coding of moving pictures and associated audio for digital storage media up to about 1.5 Mbit/s. (MPEG-1)

name: ISO/IEC 13818
desc: Information Technology: Generic coding of moving pictures and associated audio. (MPEG-2)

name: ISO/IEC DIS 13818-3
desc: Information technology: Generic coding of moving pictures and associated audio.

name: Isokey
desc: See External Key.

name: Isolated Key
desc: A key where the “hole cutting” or key video is different from the “key filling” or insert video. This is most commonly used with character generators that provide these two outputs, and allows the character generator to create a key border that is wider and cleaner than internally bordered keys. Such signals may also come from a color camera that provides its own keying output or even a monochrome camera looking at an art card. An isolated key is always a luminance key, although com- posite chroma keys may be done with an isolated key source, ignoring the isolated input. AVC series switchers can defeat isolated inputs to standard type keys by turning key borders on. Also referred to as a Processed External Key.

name: ISO OSI Model
desc: The ISO open systems interconnection model representing communication functions as seven layers: 1) Physical, 2) Data Link, 3) Network, 4) Transport, 5) Session, 6) Presentation, 7) Application.

name: Isoparameters
desc: The curves along a surface resulting from setting u or v to a constant value.

name: ISO Reel
desc: Multiple reels of tape of the same subject recorded simultane- ously from different cameras on different VTRs.

name: ISP (Internet Service Provider)
desc: An organization that provides access to the Internet.

name: ISR
desc: Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance. Information gathering activities supporting military or law enforcement operations.

name: IS&T (Society for Imaging Science and Technology)
desc: An international non-profit organization whose goal is to keep members aware of the latest scientific and technological developments in the field of imaging through conferences, journals and other publications. We focus on imaging in all its aspects, with particular emphasis on silver halide, digital printing, elec- tronic imaging, photo finishing, image preservation, image assessment, pre-press technologies and hybrid imaging systems.

name: ISV (Independent Software Vendor)
desc: Company which develops and sells application tools and/or software titles.

name: ISVR Pro
desc: See Smart Video Recorder Pro.

name: ISVYUV9
desc: Recording format for decompressed Indeo video technology using VidCap under Microsoft’s Video for Windows®.

name: Itd
desc: Initial Time-Delay Gap.

name: Iterative
desc: Procedure or process that repeatedly executes a series of operations until some condition is satisfied. Usually implemented by a loop in a program.

name: ITFS (Instructional Television Fixed Service)
desc: A method of broadcast- ing TV programs throughout school systems using low-power high-frequen- cy transmitters.

name: IT (Information Technology)
desc: Processing information by computer. The latest title for the information processing industry.

name: " It " List
desc: refers to the tendency in show business to prioritizeindividuals ( stars , writers , would - be celebrities , or up - andcomers ) as ' hot ' or ' watchable ' - highlighting those who havesuddenly ' burst onto the scene ' and are either notable andbankable ; those who had some transient success or ' brushwith greatness , but then were demoted from the list arecalled forgotten , has - beens , shooting stars , or flashes in thepan ( after " fifteen minutes of fame " - an Andy Warholexpression ) ; aka " A " List

name: ITS
desc: Intelligent Transportation Systems. Technology-based systems enabling improved services in transportation modes and traffic management.

name: ITS (Insertion Test Signal)
desc: A test signal that is inserted in one line of the vertical interval to facilitate in-service testing.

name: ITSTC (Information Technology Steering Committee)
desc: Established by the July 2002 to provide advice and recommendations to the Vice Chancellor on the overall priorities and funding level for information technology and communications for the University of Pittsburgh.

name: ITTF (Information Technology Task Force)
desc: The United World Colleges (UWC) International Board of Directors created the UWC IT Task Force (ITTF) to coordinate IT development projects for the UWC movement as a whole.

name: iTTi (International Telecom Union
desc: – Telecommunication Sector) – Started in 1998 as part of the ACTS (Advanced Communication Technologies and Services). The project goal was the specification and practical demonstration of a wireless return channel for the terrestrial digital television.

name: ITU
desc: International Telecommunications Union. An International Broadcast Standards Committee That Replaced The Ccir. Place Des Nations, Ch-1211 Geneva 20, Switzerland. Tel: 011-41-22-730-5111. Fax: 011-41-22-733-7256. Email: Itumail@Itu.Int. Internet: Www.Itu.Int.

name: ITU (International Telecommunications Union)
desc: This is the United Nations specialized agency dealing with telecommunications. At present there are 164 member countries. One of its bodies is the International Telegraph and Telephone Consultative Committee, CCITT. A Plenary Assembly of the CCITT, which takes place every few years, draws up a list of ‘Questions’ about possible improvements in international electronic communication. In Study Groups, experts from different countries develop ‘Recommendations’ which are published after they have been adopted. Especially relevant to computing are the V series of recommendations on modems (e.g. V.32, V.42), the X series on data networks and OSI (e.g., X.25, X.400), the I and Q series that define ISDN, the Z series that defines specification and programming languages (SDL, CHILL), the T series on text communication (teletext, fax, videotext, ODA) and the H series on digital sound and video encoding.

name: ITU-R 601
desc: See:  Itu-R Bt.601-2.

name: ITU-R.624
desc: ITU standard that defines PAL, NTSC and SECAM.

name: ITU-R BS.775
desc: An International Recommendation For Multichannel Stereophonic Sound Systems With And Without Accompanying Picture. This Recommendation Gives Speaker Placements For Various Types Of Sound Systems.

name: ITU-R BT.601-2
desc: Formerly Known As Ccir 601. This International Standard Defines The Encoding Parameters Of Digital Television For Studios. It Is The International Standard For Digitizing Component Television Video In Both 525 And 625 Line Systems And Is Derived From The Smpte Rp125. Itu-R 601 Deals With Both Color Difference (Y, R-Y, B-Y) And Rgb Video, And Defines Sampling Systems, Rgb/Y, R-Y, B-Y Matrix Values And Filter Characteristics. It Does Not Actually Define The Electro-Mechanical Interface--See Itu-R Bt.656. Itu-R 601 Is Normally Taken To Refer To Color Difference Component Digital Video (Rather Than Rgb), For Which It Defines 4:2:2 Sampling At 13.5 Mhz With 720 Luminance Samples Per Active Line And 8 Or 10-Bit Digitizing. Some Headroom Is Allowed With Black At Level 16 (Not 0) And White At Level 235 (Not 255)--To Minimize Clipping Of Noise And Overshoots. Using 8-Bit Digitizing Approximately 16 Million Unique Colors Are Possible: 28 Each For Y (Luminance), Cr And Cb (The Digitized Color Difference Signals) = 224 = 16,777,216 Possible Combinations. The Sampling Frequency Of 13.5 Mhz Was Chosen To Provide A Politically Acceptable Common Sampling Standard Between 525/60 And 625/50 Systems, Being A Multiple Of 2.25 Mhz, The Lowest Common Frequency To Provide A Static Sampling Pattern For Both.

name: ITU-R BT.653
desc: Standard that defines teletext systems used around the world.

name: ITU-R BT.656
desc: The physical parallel and serial interconnect scheme for ITU-R BT.601-2. ITU-R BT.656 defines the parallel connector pinouts as well as the blanking, sync, and multiplexing schemes used in both parallel and serial interfaces.

name: ITU-R BT.709-3
desc: Ratified By The International Telecommunications Union (Itu) In June 1999, The 1920X1080 Digital Sampling Structure Is A World Format. All Supporting Technical Parameters Relating To Scanning, Colorimetry, Transfer Characteristics, Etc. Are Universal. The Cif Can Be Used With A Variety Of Picture Capture Rates: 60P, 50P, 30P, 25P, 24P, As Well As 60I And 50I.

name: ITU-R (International Telecommunication Union, Radiocommunication Sector)
desc: Replaces the CCIR.

name: ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union, Telecommunication Standardization Sector)
desc: International body that develops worldwide standards for telecommunications technologies. The ITU-T carries out the functions of the former CCITT.

name: ITVA (International Television Association)
desc: An association for media, film, video, and television professionals.

name: I-vop (Intra-coded VOP)
desc: A vop coded using information only from itself.

name: IVUE
desc: A file format associated with FITS technology that enables images to be opened and displayed in seconds by showing only as much data on the screen as is implied by the screen size and zoom factor.

name: I, W, Q, B
desc: An NTSC test signal used to check television broadcast equip- ment. It consists of an I signal followed by a white bar, then a Q signal and a black level on each line.

name: IWU (Inter-Working Unit)
desc: The network “modem” where all the digital to analogue (and visa versa) conversions take place within the digital GSM networks

name: J.41
desc: This is a recommendation from the ITU-T covering high-quality coding of audio material at 384 kbit/s. In the same family we find the J.42, the J.43 and the J.44 recommendations that define the coding of analog “medium quality” sound at 384 kbit/s, “high quality” sound at 320 kbit/s, and “medium quality” sound at 320 kbit/s, respectively.

name: J.81
desc: This ITU-T recommendation is identical to the ETSI standard ETS 300 174 for video broadcast transmission at 34 Mbit/s.

name: Jack
desc: Receptacle for a plug connector leading to the input or output circuit of a tape recorder or other piece of equipment. A jack matches a specific plug.

name: Jacket
desc: Outer protective covering of a wire or cable.

name: Jack Lead
desc: See Jack To Jack

name: Jack (scenery)
desc: A device mounted out of sight of the audience used to keep flats from falling over. Can be homemade or purchased from theatrical manufacturers.

name: Jack To Jack
desc: International Term For A ¼" Instrument Cable, But Not Generally Used In The Usa.

name: Jaes
desc: Journal Of The Audio Engineering Society.

name: Jaggies
desc: a) Slang for the stair-step aliasing that appears on diagonal lines. Caused by insufficient filtering, violation of the Nyquist Theory, and/or poor interpolation. b) A term for the jagged visual appearance of lines and shapes in raster pictures that results from producing graphics on a grid format. This effect can be reduced by increasing the sample rate in scan conversion.

name: Jam Sync
desc: a) Process of locking a time-code generator to existing recorded time code on a tape in order to recreate or extend the time code. This may be necessary because, beyond a given point on tape, time code may be non-existent or of poor quality. b) Process of synchronizing a secondary time code generator with a selected master time code, i.e., synchronizing the smart slate and the audio time code to the same clock.

name: Jam Syncing
desc: The process of synchronizing a secondary timecode generator with a selected master timecode.

name: Japan Broadcasting Corporation
desc: See NHK.

name: Jasa
desc: Journal Of The Acoustical Society Of America.

name: Java
desc: A highly portable, object-oriented programming language developed by Sun Microsystems. Not to be confused with JavaScript.

name: JavaScript
desc: A programming language originally created by Netscape with specific features designed for use with the Internet and HTML, and syntax resembling that of Java and C++. Now standardized as ECMA-262.

name: JBIG
desc: See Joint Bi-Level Image Experts Group.

name: JBOD (Just a Bunch of Disks)
desc: A collection of optical/magnetic disks used for storing data.

name: JCIC (Joint Committee for Inter-Society Coordination)
desc: A group comprised of the EIA, the IEEE, the NAB, the NCTA, and the SMPTE. The JCIC created the ATSC in 1982 to handle all of the new advances in TV, including HDTV. The

name: JCTEA (Japan Cable Television Engineering Association) JEC
desc: Joint Engineering Committee of EIA and NCTA.

name: J - cut
desc: see L - cut ( below ) ; aka split edit

name: Jerk And Jounce
desc: Derivatives Of Acceleration And Sometimes Important Issues To Filter Out When Doing Motioncontrol .

name: Jewel Box
desc: The plastic clamshell case that holds a CD or DVD.

name: J-Guide
desc: J-Shaped Aluminum Members Fixed In Parallel Rows For The Purpose Of Guiding Arbors Or Clews.

name: Jitter
desc: a) The variation of a digital signal’s significant instants (such as transition points) from their ideal positions in time. Jitter can cause the recovered clock and the data to become momentarily misaligned in time. In some cases the data can be misinterpreted if this misalignment becomes too great. b) An undesirable random signal variation with respect to time. A tendency toward lack of synchronization of the picture. It

name: Jitter Amplitude
desc: The variation in phase of the bit rate clock expressed as a percent of the bit period.

name: Jitter Rate
desc: The rate of change of the jitter amplitude expressed as a frequency in Hertz.

name: JND (Just Noticeable Difference)
desc: A measure of the minimum percep- tible change in quality. A one JND change is accurately detected 75 per- cent of the time; a three JND change is accurately detected 99 percent of the time. There is a large number of JNDs of difference between NTSC as it is now received in U.S. homes and high definition electronic production (HDEP). This difference decreases in ATV systems in a hierarchical order.

name: Jogging
desc: Single-frame forward or backward movement of video tape. See Stepping.

name: Jog/Shuttle Wheel
desc: A dial on many video decks, VCRs and editing control units that controls jog and shuttle functions.

name: Joint Bi-Level Image Experts Group (JBIG)
desc: This is a lossless bi-level (black and white) image compression technique. JBIG is intended to replace G3 fax algorithms. The JBIG technique can be used on either gray- scaled or color images. Some of the applied techniques have a strong resemblance with the JPEG standard. Commercially available implementa- tions of JBIT have been scarce, but some find use in remote printing of newspapers.

name: Joint Photographic Expert Group (JPEG)
desc: Compression technique

name: Joint Stereo Coding
desc: Exploitation of interchannel stereophonic redundancies in audio coding resulting in the left and right stereo pair being coded in a single bitstream.

name: Jot
desc: The text editor that comes as a standard utility on every IRIS.

name: Joystick
desc: Affecting control over X, Y and Z parameters. Typical uses are switcher pattern positioner, ADO positioner/controller, ACE switcher preview controller. See Positioner.

name: JPEG
desc: See Joint Photographic Experts Group.

name: JPEG-1
desc: ISO/IEC DIS 10918-1 begins with a digital image in the format Y, CB, CR (such as defined in CCIR 601-2) and provides several levels of compression. Predictive coding and transforms are employed, with the higher compression ratios selectively recognizing the decrease in human visual acuity with increasing spatial frequencies. It is optimized for about 15:1 compression. As increased data storage and increased processing capabilities are becoming available, there is exploration of adapting JPEG-1 for application to successive frames in real time; i.e., full-motion JPEG.

name: JPEG-2
desc: ISO/IEC CD 11172 describes procedures for compliance testing in applications of JPEG-1.

name: JPEG2000
desc: A wavelet-based image compression standard. Modest improvement over JPEG in compression performance, with significant flexibility in the codestream allowing representation at various resolutions.

name: JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group)
desc: Commonly used lossy compression for photographic images using a discrete cosine transfer function. Typically achieves 10:1 compression with little perceptible loss. Produces blocking artifacts.

name: JPEG XS
desc: A visually lossless, low-latency video compression standard (ISO/IEC 21122) achieving 10:1–20:1 compression with sub-millisecond latency. Enables 4K transport over 10GbE and lower-bandwidth links. Used in IPMX.

name: .Jpg
desc: Best File Format For Photographs Because It Contains Millions Of Colors

name: JScript
desc: A proprietary Microsoft variant of JavaScript.

name: JTC1 (Joint Technical Committee)
desc: JTC1 is a joint committee of ISO and IEC. The scope of JTC1 is information technology standardization.

name: Judder
desc: Shaking Or Stuttering Experienced Inside Of A Vr Headset.

name: jukebox musical
desc: a filmed musical ( drama , or animation , etc. ) that uses preexisting popular songs ( usually from a variety of artisticsources ) as its song score ; the songs are often re - imaginedwith different song styles ; aka karaoke musical

name: Jumbo Frame
desc: Ethernet frames with more than 1500 bytes of payload. Use of jumbo packets can increase transmission efficiency by reducing network transmission overhead for the Ethernet datagram wrapper.

name: Jumbo frames
desc: Ethernet frames with a payload larger than the standard 1500-byte MTU, typically 9000 bytes. Required by many AV-over-IP protocols (SDVoE, NDI, ST 2110) to efficiently transport large video payloads.

name: Jump
desc: Instruction that results in a change of sequence.

name: jump cut
desc: an abrupt , disorienting transitional device in the middle of acontinuous shot in which the action is noticeably advanced intime and / or cut between two similar scenes , either doneaccidentally ( a technical flaw or the result of bad editing ) orpurposefully ( to create discontinuity for artistic effect ) ; alsocontrast with an ellipsis and match cut

name: Jumper
desc: Slang for a short extension cord, typically made in 5-foot increments starting at 1 foot.

name: Juve
desc: Abbreviation For Juvenile Lead: The Young Male Main Part.

name: juvenile
desc: the role of a young , teenaged male character ; the femalecounterpart is known as an ingenue .

name: juxtaposition
desc: in a film , the contiguous positioning of either two images ,characters , objects , or two scenes in sequence , in order tocompare and contrast them , or establish a relationshipbetween them ; see also sequence , symmetry ,andcomposition .

name: K
desc: Symbol for 1000 (103). When referring to bits or words, K=1024 (210).

name: K60Hz
desc: A field-rate square wave is used to measure this parameter. Locate the center of the field bar time, normalize the point to 100% and measure the maximum amplitude deviation for each half. Ignore the first and last 2.5% (about 200 µsec). The largest of the two tilt measurements divided by two is the K60Hz rating. of the bar time, normalize that point to 100% and measure the maximum amplitude deviation for each half. Ignore the first and last 2.5% (0.45 µsec) of the bar. The largest of the two is the Kbar rating.

name: Kbar
desc: A line bar (18 µsecs) is used to measure Kbar. Locate the center of the bar time, normalize that point to 100% and measure the maximum amplitude deviation for each half. Ignore the first and last 2.5% (0.45 µsec) of the bar. The largest of the two is the Kbar rating.

name: Keeper
desc: Term used to indicate the effect, edit was good enough to keep, but could possibly be improved on, however, the effect or edit should be stored as is in case it cannot be improved upon.

name: Kell Effect
desc: Vertical resolution of a scanned image subjectively evaluated is consistently shown to be less than the geometrically-predicted resolu- tion. Observations are usually stated in terms of the ratio of perceived television lines to active lines present in the display. From the time that R. Kelt published his studies (conducted on a progressive scanned image), there have been numerous numerical values and substantiating theories proposed for this effect. The range of results suggests that many details

name: Kell Factor
desc: The Vertical Definition Of A Scanned Image Is Only Around 70% (The Kell Factor) Of The Line Count Due To A Scan'S Inability To Show Detail Occurring Between The Lines. Note That, For Interlaced Scans, Vertical Definition Is Further Reduced By The Interlace Factor To 50% Or Less Overall During Most Vertical Image Movement.

name: Kelvin
desc: This is a system or scale used for measuring temperature. Absolute zero is 0° Kelvin or -273°C. The “color” of white light is expressed in terms of degrees Kelvin, the color of light emitted when an ideal object is heated to a particular temperature.

name: Kelvin (K)
desc: The Standard Unit Of Temperature That Is Used In The Système Internationale D’Unités (Si) System Of Measurements.  The Kelvin Temperature Scale Is Used To Describe The Correlated Color Temperature Of A Light Source.

name: KEM Roll
desc: The roll of film used on a KEM flatbed editing system. A KEM roll combines multiple takes onto a single roll (a work print, not a negative). The maximum length of a KEM roll is 1000 feet.

name: Kerberos
desc: Kerberos is a network authentication protocol developed by MIT. It is designed to provide strong authentication for client/server applications by using secret-key cryptography.

name: Kernel
desc: Minimum circuitry required to allow the microprocessor to function. Usually consists of the microprocessor, clock circuit, interrupt and DMA control lines, and power supply.

name: Kerning
desc: The spacing between text characters in print media, such as titles.

name: Kettle Lead
desc: See Iec Cable

name: Kevlar
desc: A DuPont brand name for aramid yarn used in cable construction to provide strength and strain relief.

name: Key
desc: a) A signal that can electronically “cut a hole” in the video picture to allow for insertion of other elements such as text or a smaller video picture. b) A video that has been overlaid on top of another video. Keys may be either determined by the luminance or brightness of the key video, or determined by the chroma or hue of the key video. c) A push-button.

name: Keyboard
desc: a) Group of push-buttons used for inputting information to a system. b) The human interface portion of a computer, typewriter with alpha numeric keys or push-buttons.

name: Key Channel
desc: See Alpha Channel.

name: Key Color
desc: The solid color used to key.

name: Keyer
desc: a) The electronics and panel controls that create keys. There are many types of keyers, some limited to titles only, and some capable of  any type of key. All Ampex keyers are full capability. b) A tool that you use to create a composite from a clip from a background and foreground clip by using an input key-in clip to determine how the clips are combined.

name: Key Fill
desc: Line key effects, the video signal which is said to “fill the hole” cut in background video by the key source.

name: Key Frame
desc: A frame containing all the data representing an image, rather than just the data that has changed since the last frame. The first frame of every video file is a key frame; in addition, they occur throughout the file to refresh image quality and permit certain operations, such as random user access. Compare Delta Frame.

name: Keyframe Duration
desc: The length of the keyframe; the time from keyframe to the start of the next.

name: Key Frame Shape
desc: An Avolites Titan shape type where the programmer creates custom steps that can then be spread and synchronized across a selection of fixtures, combining the control of a chase with the flexibility of a shape.

name: Keyframing
desc: The process of creating an animated clip wherein by select- ing a beginning image and an ending image the software automatically generates the frames in between. See also Tweening.

name: Key Gain
desc: An adjustment for keys that determines the sharpness of the key edges. As key gain is reduced, keys become softer at the edges and may be adjusted to be more transparent.

name: Keying
desc: The process of replacing part of one television image with video from anther image; that is chroma keying and insert keying.

name: Key Insert
desc: The video that fills a key.

name: Key Invert
desc: a) A luminance key mode which inverts the polarity of the key source to allow dark areas of the source video to cut holes in back- ground instead of bright areas. b) A chroma key mode which inverts the foreground and background positions.

name: Keykode
desc: A trademark of Eastman Kodak Company. A barcode on the edge of motion picture film which allows the film edge numbers to be electronically read and inserted into an edit list. Very useful for generating a negative cut list from a video off-line EDL.

name: Keykode Numbers Reader
desc: Device attached to a telecine or part of a bench logger which read Keykode number bar code from motion picture film and provides electronic output to a decoder.

name: Key-Length-Value (KLV)
desc: The grouping of information concerning a single metadata element that combines three pieces of information: its UL Data Key; the Length of its instantiation Value in the next field; its instantiated Value in the

name: Key Light
desc: The term used to describe a subject’s main source of illumination. When shooting outdoors, the key light is usually the sun.

name: Key Mask
desc: A key mode which allows use of independent key mask generators to create a pattern to prevent some undesirable portions of the key source from cutting a hole in the background. This is also possible using externally generated masks on the Vista.

name: Key Matrix
desc: The electronic crosspoints which switch and route key signals and key insert signals to appropriate key processing electronics. On Ampex switchers, these matrices are controlled by keypads and keyer insert selector push-button controls and form the Phantom matrix portion of the switcher.

name: Key Memory
desc: An AVC series feature that allows a key to be fully adjusted as soon as it is selected. This is accomplished by a “store” button on the key adjust panel that may be pressed when an operator is satisfied with  the adjustment of a key. From that point on, whenever that key is selected, regardless of which keyer it is on, all adjustments and features of that key are automatically recalled.

name: Key Minder
desc: An Extron technology for managing HDCP authentication keys in a switching and distribution system. Authenticates and maintains continuous HDCP encryption between input and output devices for reliable switching.

name: Key Numbers
desc: The original frame identification numbers applied by the film manufacturers to the film stock. Key numbers are used by the negative cutter to conform the film negative. Film composer cut lists and change lists reference key numbers.

name: Keypad
desc: The numbered push-buttons used to entered numerical data, i.e., pattern numbers, transition rates, key source numbers, etc.

name: Key profile
desc: A user-customizable setting on Avolites Titan and other consoles that changes the function of buttons (blue, grey) on playback handles. Allows the same button to perform different functions (GO, flash, preload) depending on the profile.

name: Key Region
desc: See Slice.

name: Keys
desc: See KSV – Key Selection Vector.

name: Key Signal
desc: A hole cutting signal.

name: Key Source
desc: a) A hole cutter. The signal which is said to “cut a hole” in the background scene for a key effect. In actuality, this signal controls a video mixer which switches between the background scene and the

name: Keystone effect
desc: A distorted picture where one edge is not the same dimension as the opposite, producing a tapered or wedge shape. Typically results when an image is projected to a screen at an angle.

name: Key Type
desc: There are three key types on Ampex switchers; luminance keys, RGB chroma keys and composite chroma keys.

name: K Factor
desc: A specification rating method that gives a higher factor to video disturbances that cause the most observable picture degradation.

name: K Factor Ratings
desc: K Factor ratings are a system that maps linear distortions of 2T pulses and line time bars onto subjectively determined scales of picture quality. The various distortions are weighted in terms of impairment to the picture. The usual K Factor measurements are Kpulse/bar, K2T  or Kpulse  (2T pulse response), Kbar and sometimes K60Hz. The overall K Factor rating is the largest value obtained from all of these measurements. Special graticules can be used to obtain the K Factor number or it can be calculated from the appropriate formula. All types of linear distortions affect the K Factor rating. Picture effects may include any of the short time, line time, field time and long time picture distortions. Any signal containing the 2T pulse and an 18 µsec bar can be used to measure Kpulse/bar, K2T (Kpulse), or Kbar.

name: KF Flags (Menu)
desc: Miscellaneous keyframe flags, currently used to turn Globals off and on.

name: kHz (Kilohertz)
desc: One thousand cycles per second.

name: Kicker
desc: A light placed low and to the side of a subject to add dimension and separation from the background.

name: kick - off
desc: a term denoting the start of production or principalphotography

name: Kill
desc: To Disconnect Electrical Current To One, Some, Or All Luminaires, Motors, Or Other Electrical Equipment.

name: Kilobaud
desc: A unit of measurement of data transmission speed equaling 1000 baud.

name: Kilobyte (KB)
desc: One thousand bytes. Actually 1024 bytes because of the way computer math works out.

name: Kilohertz
desc: A Unit Of Frequency Equal To One Thousand Cycles Per Second. Synonym: Khz

name: Kilo (K)
desc: A Numerical Prefix Denoting 1000.

name: Kilovolt-Ampere (Kva)
desc: 1000 Volt-Amperes.

name: Kilovolt (Kv)
desc: 1000 Volts.

name: Kilowatt-Hour (Kwh)
desc: Measure Of Electrical Energy Consumed; 1 Kilowatt-Hour Is Equal To 1000 Watts Used For 1 Hour. See Also Watt And Watt-Hour.

name: Kilowatt (Kw)
desc: One Thousand Watts. See Also Watt And Watt-Hour.

name: Kinds (Hog)
desc: On Hog 4 OS, kinds are collections of fixture functions organized into wheelsets. Fixed kinds (Intensity, Position, Colour, Beam, Effects, Time, Control) are pre-defined; user kinds are custom collections.

name: Kinescope
desc: a) Frequently used to mean picture tubes in general. However, this name has been copyrighted. b) A film recording of a video image displayed on a specially designed television monitor. Only means of recording TV programs before video recorders and tape were invented.

name: Kinescope Recording
desc: Motion pictures taken of a program pho- tographed directly from images on the face of a kinescope tube. A television transcription.

name: Kinesthesis
desc: Sensations Derived From Muscles, Tendons And Joints And Stimulated By Movement And Tension.

name: Kinesthetic Dissonance
desc: The Sensory Disconnect Experienced When Touch Or Motion Feedback From A Virtual Environment Is Delayed Or Absent.

name: Klieglight
desc: a type of powerful carbon - arc lamp that produces an intenselight , often used in film - making ; also used for promotionalpurposes at film premieres

name: KLV (Key, Length, and Value)
desc: A data-encoding protocol (SMPTE 336M) that complies with International Standards Organization rules for Object Identifier data and SMPTE Universal Label (SMPTE 298M). This is the “header” information in a metadata stream that will identify the data and which metadata dictionary of definitions should be used for the metadata that follows. KLV and UMIDs (Unique Material Identifiers) are the basic engineering building blocks that have been designed to make metadata easier to exchange between different media (such as tapes or files) and metadata standards.

name: Knee
desc: A Knee Is A Sharp Bend In A Curve (An Eq Fre_x0002_Quency Response Or Compressor Gain Curve) Not Unlike The Sharp Bend In Your Leg.

name: Kpulse/bar or K-PB
desc: Calculation of this parameter requires the measurement of the pulse and bar amplitudes. Kpulse/bar is equal to:

name: KSV
desc: Key Selection Vector. A unique numerical key used in content protection or DRM schemes such as HDCP to authenticate devices connected to one another, ensuring a source is connected to a display and not a recording device.

name: Ku-band
desc: Ku-band satellites use the band of satellite downlink frequen- cies from 11.7 to 12.2 GHz. Also the group of microwave frequencies from 12 to 18 GHz.

name: kudocast
desc: another term for an awards show ; see Academy Awards

name: KVM
desc: Keyboard, Video, Mouse. A switch or extender allowing control of multiple computers from one keyboard/monitor/mouse set. AV-grade KVM systems extend signals over fiber, CAT, or IP. Used in control rooms and broadcast.

name: KVM over IP
desc: Keyboard, Video, Mouse extension over IP networks allowing remote control of computers as if locally connected. AV-grade KVM-over-IP supports 4K video with low latency for control room and broadcast applications.

name: K-Weighting - A Frequency
desc: Curve Designed To Mimic The Relative Sensitivity Of The Human Ear To Different Frequencies In Terms Of Perceived Loudness. It Is Similar To The A Weighting Curve, But Adds A Shelf Boost Above 2Khz. We Use These Units Of Measure To Determine And Monitor The Audio Energy In A Room For Human Health. See Also A Weighting And C-Weighting.

name: L8
desc: Lighting previsualization shorthand. Refers to a full 3D lighting visualization session, typically using software like Capture, Wysiwyg, or MA 3D for offline console programming before arriving at the venue.

name: Label
desc: Name assigned to a memory location. When an assembly language program is written, a label is assigned to an instruction or memory location that must be referred to by another instruction. Then when the program is converted to machine code, an actual address is assigned to the label.

name: Ladders
desc: High Stands For Hanging Lanterns At The Side Of The Stage. Not For Climbing!

name: LAeq
desc: An Leq measurement using A weighting. Refer to Leq and Weighting.

name: Lake processing
desc: Touring-grade speaker system optimization and processing from Lab.gruppen (a Crown International brand). Lake processors provide advanced crossover, EQ, limiting, and delay for professional PA systems.

name: Lambert
desc: A unit of measure for light reflected off an object. 1 lambert = 0.318 foot-candles per square centimeter.

name: Lambertian Source/Surface
desc: A surface is called a Lambert radiator or reflector (depending whether the surface is a primary or a secondary source of light) if it is a perfectly diffusing surface.

name: Lamp
desc: A  Manufactured Light Source. For Electric Lamps, It Includes The Bulb, The Base, And The Internal Structure  That Produces Light, Either A Filament Or An Arc Tube. Lamps Are Often Referred To As Light Bulbs. The Term Lamp Also Is Commonly  Used To Refer To Plug-In Luminaires (See Desk, Floor, And Table Lamps).

name: Lamp Bar
desc: A Pipe, Usually Aluminum, That Has A Plurality Of Luminaires Attached At Even Intervals. The Power Cords For The Luminaires Enter The Pipe At Even Intervals. The Power Cords For The Luminaires Enter The Pipe Via A Strain Relief, And Are Electrically Connected To Wires Within The Pipe. The Internal Wires Usually Terminate Into A Multiconnector.

name: Lamp Dip
desc: A Colored Transparent Or Translucent Lacquer Used On Low-Wattage Incandescent Lamps In Those Instances When It Is Not Practical Or Possible To Use Standard Color Media.

name: Lampholder
desc: The Electrical Device That Supports A Lamp In A Luminaire, And Generally Contains The Contacts That Make The Electrical Connection To The Contacts Of The Lamp Base.

name: Lamp Life
desc: See Average Rated Lamp Life And Service Life Of A Lamp.

name: Lamp Life Multiplier
desc: A Factor Used In The Economic Analyses In This Book To Adjust The Average Rated Lamp Life To Reflect The Effects Of Hours Per Start And Dimming Of Lamps.

name: Lamp on/off
desc: A DMX macro command sent from the console to switch a fixture's lamp on or off without interrupting power to the fixture electronics. Critical for HMI discharge lamps that require cooling cycles.

name: Lampy
desc: Slang For A Lighting Director.

name: LAN
desc: Local Area Network. Network connecting devices within a limited area (building, campus) using Ethernet or Wi-Fi. Most installed AV systems operate on the facility LAN. Contrast with WAN (wide area network).

name: LANC
desc: See Control-L.

name: Land
desc: The raised area of an optical disc.

name: landmark film
desc: a revolutionary film , due to either its technical or performanceartistry ; those films recognized by the National Film Registry

name: Landscape
desc: Orientation of a display device so that it is wider than it is tall. The common orientation for a display.

name: LAN (Local Area Network)
desc: A communications network that serves users within a confined geographical area. It is made up of servers, workstations, a network operating system and a communications link.

name: Lantern
desc: What In Normal English We Call A Light, In The Theatre Is Called A Lantern. Actually, It'S A Slightly Old-Fashioned Expression: Luminaire Is Used More Often Nowadays, Whereas Some Prefer The American Term Instrument.

name: Lap Dissolve
desc: A slow dissolve in which both pictures are actually overlapped for a very brief period of time. Same as Dissolve.

name: Lapel Mic
desc: Small Body Microphone Often Clips To The Talents Lapel, Tie Or Collar.

name: Lapel Microphone
desc: See Lavalier Microphone

name: LAP (Link Access Procedure)
desc: An ITU family of error correction protocols originally derived from the HDLC standard.

name: Lapping film
desc: Sheets of film used for polishing ferrule endfaces, comprising a film backing with mineral particles at various grit ratings.

name: Large-Area Flicker
desc: Flicker of the overall image or large parts of it. See also Flicker Frequency and Twitter.

name: Large Scale Integration (LSI)
desc: Technology by which thousands of semiconductor devices are fabricated on a single chip.

name: LAR (Logarithmic Area Ratio)
desc: Describes spectral envelope in speech coding.

name: Laser
desc: Light amplification by stimulated emission of radiation. A laser produces a very strong and coherent light of a single frequency.

name: Laser Beam Recording
desc: A technique for recording video on film.

name: Laser chirp
desc: A sudden change in the center wavelength of a laser, caused by reflected or crosstalk optical energy entering the lasing chamber.

name: Laser Disc
desc: A 12-inch (or 8-inch) optical disc that holds analog video (using an FM signal) and both analog and digital (PCM) audio. A precursor to DVD.

name: Laser-Optimized Multimode Fiber
desc: A multimode fiber with higher bandwidth than legacy multimode fiber, designed for transmission with laser-based sources such as VCSEL.

name: Latch
desc: a) Hardware device that captures information and holds sit (e.g., group of flip-flops). b) An electronic circuit that holds a signal on once it has been selected. To latch a signal means to hold it on or off.

name: Latency
desc: a) The length of time it takes a packet to move from source to destination. b) A factor of data access time due to disk rotation. The faster a disk spins the quicker it will be at the position where the required data can start to be read. As disk diameters have decreased so rotational speeds have tended to increase but there is still much variation. Modern 3-1/2-inch drives typically have spindle speeds of between 3,600 and 7,200 revolutions per minute, so one revolution is completed in 16 or 8 milliseconds (ms) respectively. This is represented in the disk specification as average latency of 8 or 4 ms.

name: Latency budget
desc: The total acceptable end-to-end delay in an AV system, broken down across each component (encoder, network, decoder, display). Critical for live production and videoconferencing.

name: Latent Image
desc: The invisible image formed in a camera or printer by the action of light on a photographic emulsion.

name: Lateral Direction
desc: Across the width of the tape.

name: Latitude
desc: In a photographic process, the range of exposure over which substantially correct reproduction is obtained. When the process is repre- sented by and H and D curve, the latitude is the projection on the exposure axis of that part of the curve which approximates a straight line within the tolerance permitted for the purpose at hand.

name: LATM (Low-Overhead MPEG-4 Audio Transport Multiplex)
desc: MPEG-4 audio is an audio standard that integrates many different types of audio coding tools. Low-overhead MPEG-4 Audio Transport Multiplex (LATM) manages the sequences of audio data with relatively small overhead. In audio-only applications, then, it is desirable for LATM-based MPEG-4 audio bitstreams to be directly mapped onto the RTP packets without using MPEG-4 systems.

name: Lattice Track
desc: A Parallel Pair Of Angles Or Other Structural Members That Guide An Arbor Or Clew. Low Friction Slides Or Roller Guides Are Placed On Both Sides  Of The Device To Be Guided.

name: Launch
desc: To start up an application, often by double-clicking an icon.

name: Lavaliere
desc: A microphone designed to hang from the performer’s neck.

name: Lavaliere Microphone
desc: Small Microphone Worn On A Performer Synonym: Body Mic, Lapel, Lapel Mic, Lav, Lavaliere

name: lavalier ( microphone )
desc: a miniature type of microphone , usually omni - directional andwireless , and small enough to be taped or clipped to an actor ,to record dialogue ; aka lav , lapel or lap microphones

name: Lav Mic
desc: A Small Microphone That Attaches To The Talent Which Allows For Hands-Free Operation

name: Lawo
desc: A German manufacturer of high-end broadcast audio consoles and networks. Known for the mc² series consoles used in major broadcast facilities, OB trucks, and live broadcast productions. Early adopter of IP audio (AES67, RAVENNA).

name: Law Of The First Wave Front
desc: The First Wave Front Falling On The Ear Determines The Perceived Direction Of The Sound.

name: Layback
desc: Transferring the finished audio track back to the master video tape.

name: Layer
desc: a) A term used to describe which video is on top of which back- ground versus foreground and subsequent keys superimposed. b) One of the levels in the data hierarchy of the video and system specification.

name: Layer 0
desc: In a dual-layer disc, this is the layer closest to the optical pick- up beam and surface of the disc, and the first to be read when scanning from the beginning of the disc’s data. Dual-layer discs are 10% less dense than single layer discs due to crosstalk between the layers.

name: Layer 1
desc: In a dual-layer disc, this is the deeper of the two layers, and the second one to be read when scanning from the beginning of the disc’s data.

name: Layer 2 Switch
desc: A network switch supporting ISO model Layer 2 functions. Switches packets between devices using a MAC address table. Does not examine IP packets.

name: Layer 3 Switch
desc: A network switch supporting ISO model Layer 3 functions. Examines network packets and makes routing decisions based on IP information. Supports VLANs, IGMP snooping, and multicast delivery.

name: Layered Bitstream
desc: A single bitstream associated to a specific layer (always used in conjunction with layer qualifiers).

name: Layered embedded encoding
desc: The Process Of Compressing Data In Layers Such That Successive Layers Provide More Information And Thus Higher Quality Reconstruction Of The Original. That Is, A Single Stream Of Data Can Supply A Range Of Compression And Thus, In The Case Of Video, A Scalable Range Of Video Resolution And Picture Quality. This Is Particularly Useful For A Multicast Where A Single Stream Is Sent Out And People Are Connecting Over Varying Bandwidths. The Low Bandwidth Connection Can Take Just The Lower Layers While The High-Bandwidth Connection Can Take All Of The Layers For The Highest Quality.

name: Layered Tracks
desc: The elements of an effect created by combining two or more tracks in a specified way, such as nesting one track as a layer within another.

name: Layer-to-Layer Adhesion
desc: The tendency for adjacent layers of tape in a roll to adhere to each other.

name: Layer-to-Layer Signal Transfer
desc: The magnetization of a layer of tape in a roll by the field from a nearby recorded layer, sometimes referred to as “print-thru”.

name: LBC
desc: Laptop Breakout Cable. An Extron cable connecting a laptop computer to an Extron computer-video interface.

name: LBR (Laser Beam Recorder)
desc: It creates the DVD master file.

name: LCD
desc: Liquid Crystal Display. Flat panel technology using liquid crystals to modulate backlight transmission. Used in professional monitors, digital signage, videowall panels, and LCD projectors. Types: IPS, VA, TN.

name: LCD (Liquid Crystal Display)
desc: A screen for displaying text/graphics based on a technology called liquid crystal, where minute currents change the reflectiveness or transparency of selected parts of

name: LCD projector
desc: A projector separating red, green, and blue information to three LCD panels. Appropriate colored light passes through each panel and combines to exit through the lens onto the screen.

name: LC (Low Complexity)
desc: The most used profile (MPEG-2) or object type (MPEG-4) in AAC (advanced audio coding) encoders and decoders nowa- days because of its low system requirements, i.e., CPU and memory resources.

name: LCoS
desc: Liquid Crystal on Silicon. A reflective display technology where a glass substrate is attached to a silicon chip coated with crystals. The chip contains the control circuitry.

name: LCP (Link Control Protocol)
desc: See PPP.

name: L - cut
desc: a digital film editing term , also known as a split edit , Jcut ordelayed edit ; it refers to a transitional edit in which theaudio and video edit do not start at the same time ; the audiostarts before ( or after ) the picture cut

name: Ld
desc: Abbreviation For Lighting Director (Or Lighting Designer, Which Can Be The Same Person). Typically, A Lighting Designer Will Develop The Look Of A Show And Put A Design In Place, Then The Ld Will Go On Tour And Manage The Implementation Of That Design In Each Venue. This Includes Directing Both Touring Crew And Stagehands To Set Up And Take Down The Lighting System In Each Venue.

name: Leader
desc: a) Special non-magnetic tape that can be spliced to either end  of a magnetic tape to prevent damage and possible loss of recorded mate- rial and to indicate visually where the recorded portion of the tape begins and ends. b) Any film or strip of material used for threading a motion picture machine. Leader may consist of short lengths of blank film attached to the ends of a print to protect the print from damage during the threading of a projector, or it may be a long length of any kind of film which is used to establish the film path in a processing machine before the use of the machine for processing film.

name: Lead In
desc: On a compact disc, the lead-in contains a table of contents for the track layout.

name: Leading Blacks
desc: A term used to describe a picture condition in which the edge preceding a white object is overshaded toward black. The object appears to have a preceding or leading black border.

name: Leading Whites
desc: A term used to describe a picture condition in which the edge preceding a black object is overshaded toward white. The object appears to have a preceding or leading white border.

name: Lead Line
desc: See “Lift Line.”

name: Lead Out
desc: On a compact disc, the lead-out indicates the end of data.

name: lead role
desc: refers to the most important , main character in a film , oftendistinguished by gender ; usually there is at least one maleand female lead role ; also usually known as protagonist ;contrasted to supporting roles or characters .

name: Lead Room
desc: The Space In Front Of A Moving Object Or Person.

name: Leads
desc: The Electric Cable(S) Or Sleeved, Insulated Wires, Attached To A Luminaire Or Piece Of Power Distribution Equipment Via A Strain Relief, That Terminate In A Connector For The Purpose Of Providing An Electrical Connection To The Electrical Supply Or To Another Luminaire.

name: Leaf
desc: 1) A Single Thin, Heat-Resistant Metal Plate From An Iris Or Some Mechanical Dimmers. 2) See Definition #1 For Door.

name: Leakage
desc: A term describing the signal picked up by a mike which is intended to pick up other signals only.

name: Learn
desc: The act of storing switcher control panel data into memory in a real-time mode (learning as they happen).

name: Learning Curve
desc: An algebraic metaphor for the amount of time a learner needs to learn a new task (such as operating an item of television produc- tion equipment).

name: Leased Access
desc: Commercial channels made available by a cable operator to third parties for a fee, as required by the Cable Acts of 1984 and 1992.

name: Least Significant Bit (LSB)
desc: The bit that has the least value in a binary number or data byte. In written form, this would be the bit on the right.

name: Lechner Distance
desc: Named for Bernard Lechner, researcher at RCA Laboratories. The Lechner distance is nine feet, the typical distance Americans sit from television sets, regardless of screen size. The Jackson distance, three meters, named for Richard Jackson, a researcher at Philips in Britain, is similar. There is reason to believe that the Lechner and Jackson distances are why HDTV research was undertaken sooner

name: Led
desc: See Light-Emitting Diode.

name: LED driver
desc: An electronic circuit or device providing the correct voltage and current to power LED light sources. In architectural DALI systems, DALI-2 LED drivers can be individually addressed and monitored.

name: Lede
desc: Live End Dead End.

name: Led Engineer
desc: The Crewmember Responsible For The Operation And Maintenance Of The Led Walls On Avolume . Can Also Be Called Creative Supervisor Or Screen Manager .

name: LED (Light Emitting Diode)
desc: A light on a piece of hardware that indicates status or error conditions.

name: Led Panel
desc: A Modular Array Of Led Lights Designed To Display Video Content . Originally Designed Forindoor / Outdoor Advertising , Entertainment Venues , And Broadcast Use , Led Panels Are Nowused To Create Volumes For Motion Picture Cinematography .

name: Led Volume
desc: Virtual Productions That Use Led Volumes Have Grown In Popularity In Recent Years, With The Mandalorian The Most Famous Example. Actors Perform On A Physical Set In A Studio In Front Of A Giant Array Of Led Screens Which Feature Images Of Real-World Or Computer-Generated Environments. The Set-Up Is Not Unlike The Projected Backgrounds Filmmakers Have Been Using Since The Silent Era. The Difference Is That It’S Smart. Harnessing A Real-Time Engine, It Responds To The Movement Of The On-Set Camera By Adjusting The Perspective, Lighting, And Other Elements Within The Panels. The Technology Allows Filmmakers To Capture Effects In-Camera And Real-Time And Lends Itself Best To Single Camera Shoots – Such As Film, Drama And Commercials.

name: Left
desc: Stage Left, Or The Lhs As You Face The Audience. Also Called The Prompt Side Or Ps.

name: Left Thirds
desc: Graphics that take up the left third of the screen, typically for lower thirds or name keys.

name: Leg
desc: Also known as a side curtain or wing. A tall curtain hanging on the sides of the stage to prevent the audience from seeing backstage.

name: Legacy
desc: A term used to describe a hybrid disc that can be played in both a DVD player and a CD player.

name: Legal Signal
desc: – A signal in which each component remains within the limits specified for the video signal format; that is, it does not exceed the specified gamut for the current format. For instance, the gamut limits for an R’, G’, B’ signal are 0 mV to 700 mV and Y’ is 0 mV to 700 mV and P’b/P’r are +/-350 mV. If the signal remains within these limits the value is legal.

name: Leg Curtain
desc: A Curtain Used To Define The Side Limit Of The Stage And To Mask Or Hide Actors, Lights, And Unused Scenery In The Off Stage Area (Wings).

name: Leg (drape)
desc: A drape set as a masking piece at the side of the acting area. Usually set in pairs across the stage and used with borders to frame the audience's view of the stage.

name: Legend (Avolites)
desc: The name or image label applied to a handle (button, fader, or touchscreen button) in Titan to identify what is stored on it. Can be text or a custom image.

name: ' legs '
desc: a film that has ' legs ' has strong and profitable box - office ,stamina and audience drawing power far beyond the openingweekend ; the term usually applies to films that last manymonths

name: leitmotif
desc: an intentionally - repeated , recurring elementor themeassociated with a particular person , idea , milieu , oraction ; the element presents itself as a repeated sound , shot ,bit of dialogue , piece of music , etc. , that helps unify a film byreminding the viewer of its earlier appearance ; sometimespresented along with a film's tag line on a film poster .

name: Leko
desc: American Term For A Profile Spot. Originally A Brand Name.

name: Lekolight
desc: See The Definition For Leko.

name: Lempel-Ziv Welch (LZW) Compression
desc: Algorithm used by the UNIX compress command to reduce the size of files, e.g., for archival or trans- mission. The algorithm relies on repetition of byte sequences(strings) in its input. It maintains a table mapping input strings to their associated output codes. The table initially contains mappings for all possible strings of length one. Input is taken one byte at a time to find the longest initial string present in the table. The code for that string is output and then the

name: Length
desc: a) The physical length of the tape wound on a reel or on a hub, varying from 213 feet in a C45 cassette to 9200 feet in a roll of instru- mentation tape. b) The number of bytes represented by the items whose Length is being described.

name: Leno filled scrim
desc: A scrim similar to sharkstooth but with the openings in the weave filled in, diffusing and softening reflected light. Available seamless in various colors.

name: lens
desc: a piece of glass in a camera through which light passesbefore hitting the film stock inside ; various types include wideangle lens , telephoto lens , normal , etc .; to lense means to filma motion picture

name: Lens Barrel
desc: 1) The Movable, Inner Tube Of A Focusing Lens System In An Ellipsoidal Spotlight. 2) The Complete Tubular Front Section Of An Ellipsoidal Spotlight That Contains The Lenses.

name: Lens Element
desc: A single lens component within a compound lens system.

name: Lens Holder
desc: 1) Any Apparatus Used To Retain A Lens. 2) See Definitions #1 And #2 For Lens Barrel.

name: Lens Mapping
desc: The Process Of Quantifying The Distortion Of A Lens By Shooting A Lens Chart And Then Analyzingit . Used For Matching Visual Effects With Real - World Cinematography . See Also Cameracalibration And Alignment .

name: Lens Size
desc: 1) A Term Used In The Theater Industry To Describe Plano-Convex Or Bi-Convex Lenses In Terms Of Diameter And Focal Length (In Inches), E.G., 6X9, 4.5X12. 2) Generally, The Diameter Of A Lens.

name: Lenticular screen
desc: A screen with silvered or aluminized embossing reflecting maximum light over wide horizontal and narrow vertical angles. Has parallel lenticulations cut vertically to improve horizontal dispersion.

name: Leq
desc: Leq represents the continuous noise level, equivalent in loudness and energy, to the fluctuating sound signal under consideration. Refer to LAeq.

name: Letterbox
desc: Image Of A Widescreen Picture On A Standard 4:3 Aspect Ratio Television Screen, Typically With Black Bars Above And Below. Used To Maintain The Original Aspect Ratio Of The Source Material.See Also: Side Panels And Pillarbox.

name: Letterbox Filter
desc: Circuitry in a DVD player that reduces the vertical size of anamorphic widescreen video (combining every 4 lines into 3) and adds black mattes at the top and bottom.

name: Letterboxing
desc: A technique that maintains the original wide aspect ratio of film when displayed as video. The top and bottom of the video screen are blackened and the total scene content is maintained.

name: letterboxing( or letterboxed )
desc: the technique of shrinking the film image just enough so thatits entire width appears on TV screen , with black areas aboveand below the image ; refers to the way that videos emulatethe widescreen format on television screens ; if a widescreenfilm is not in the letterbox format it is often in pan - andscanformat .

name: Level
desc: a) A defined set of constraints on the values which may be taken by some parameters within a particular profile. A profile may contain one or more levels. b) In MPEG-2, a range of picture parameters. c) Defines the bounds of the coding parameters, such as resolution, bit rate, etc. within each profile. The variation of performance is inherently wide in a profile.

name: Level 0
desc: No Redundancy--Benefits Only Of Speed And Capacity--Generated By Combining A Number Of Disks. Also Known As "Striping."

name: Level 2
desc: An Array Of Nine Disks. Each Byte Is Recorded With One Bit On Each Of Eight Disks And A Parity Bit Recorded To The Ninth. This Level Is Rarely, If Ever, Used.

name: Level 3
desc: An Array Of N+1 Disks Recording 512 Byte Sectors On Each Of The N Disks To Create N X 512 "Super Sectors" + 1 X 512 Parity Sector On The Additional Disk Which Is Used To Check The Data.The Minimum Unit Of Transfer Is A Whole Superblock. This Is Most Suitable For Systems In Which Large Amounts Of Sequential Data Are Transferred--Such As For Audio And Video. For These It Is The Most Efficient Raid Level Since It Is Never Necessary To Read/Modify/Write The Parity Block. It Is Less Suitable For Database Types Of Access In Which Small Amounts Of Data Need To Be Transferred At Random.

name: Level 4
desc: The Same As Level 3 But Individual Blocks Can Be Transferred. When Data Is Written It Is Necessary To Read The Old Data And Parity Blocks Before Writing The New Data As Well As The Updated Parity Block, Which Reduces Performance.

name: Level 5
desc: The Same As Level 4, But The Role Of Parity The Disk Is Rotated For Each Block. In Level 4 The Parity Disk Receives Excessive Load For Writes And No Load For Reads. In Level 5 The Load Is Balanced Across The Disks.

name: Level control
desc: A control on interface products similar to a contrast control. Increases or decreases the output signal level to a monitor or projector.

name: Level, L
desc: Ten Times The Common Logarithm Of The Ratio Of A Quantity Proportional To Power Or Energy To A Reference Quantity Of The Same Kind. (See Sound Power Level, Sound Pressure Level.) The Quantity So Obtained Is Expressed In Decibels.

name: Level Of Detail ( LOD )
desc: The Representation Of A 3D Asset , With A Specific Image Quality And Render Performance .Multiple Levels Of Detail May Be Produced For Various Applications .

name: Level Reduction, Lr
desc: In A Specified Frequency Band, The Decrease In Sound Pressure Level, Measured At The Location Of The Receiver, When A Barrier Or Other Sound-Reducing Element Is Placed Between The Source And The Receiver.

name: Levels
desc: The Volume Of Sound, Usually Associated With Audio

name: LFE (Low Frequency Effects)
desc: The optional LFE channel (also referred to as the “boom” channel) carries a separate, limited, frequency bandwidth signal that complements the main channels. It delivers bass energy specifi- cally created for subwoofer effects or low-frequency information derived from the other channels. The LFE channel is the “.1” in 5.1-channel audio.

name: Library
desc: As in a book library, it is somewhere one might keep effects, i.e., on a disk or collection of disks hence a library of canned effects.

name: library shot
desc: a stock shot , often unimaginative or commonplace

name: Libretto
desc: The text of a musical or opera, including both spoken dialogue and song lyrics.

name: Lidar ( Light Imaging ,Detection , AndRanging )
