desc: A Survey Method That Illuminates A Target With Laser And Measures The Reflected Light Viainfrared Sensors To Derive A Point Cloud ; Useful As Part Of Asset Creation And To Capture Realworld Locations .

name: LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)
desc: A buffer. Same as Push-Down Stack. See Stack.

name: Lift
desc: To remove selected frames from a sequence and leave black or silence in the place of the frames.

name: Lift Line
desc: Any Rope Or Cable Located Between A Load And A Winch Or Counter Weight Arbor.

name: Lift lines
desc: Part of a fly system. The wire, manila, or synthetic ropes from which a batten hangs, running from the batten up through loft blocks to the head block and down to the arbor.

name: Light
desc: Radiant Energy That Is Capable Of Producing A Visual Sensation. The Visible Portion Of  The Electromagnetic Spectrum Extends From About 380 To 770 Nanometers.

name: Light board
desc: The electronic console used to control the stage lighting for a theatre. Also called the lighting board, lighting desk, or dimmer board.

name: Light Card
desc: A Virtual Light Placed On The Surface Of The Volume To Assist With Lighting The Subject , Can Beany Shape , Hue , Intensity , And Opacity .

name: Light Console
desc: See The Definition For Control Console.

name: Light Contamination
desc: The Presence Of Unintentional Light Effects On A Given Surface , Such As The Spill From Movielights Onto An Led Wall .

name: Light Distribution
desc: The Way In Which Illumination Of Any Color Or Quantity Is Spread Over A Particular Background.

name: Light-Emitting Diode (Led)
desc: A Semiconductor Diode That Radiates In The Visible Region Of The Spectrum. Leds Are Used As Indicator Lamps On Some Lighting Controls,  And Are Used In Some Emergency Exit Signs.

name: Lightguide
desc: Also known as an optical waveguide or optical fiber. A glass or plastic fiber guiding light along its axis via total internal reflection between a core and lower-refractive-index cladding.

name: lighting
desc: refers to the illumination of a scene , and the manipulation oflight and shadows by the cinematographer .

name: Lighting Bridge
desc: A Walkway Across The Stage (Fixed Or Flown) Where Lights Are Hung And Where They May Be Adjusted And Maintained.

name: Lighting Console
desc: A board used to control all of the lighting effects used on stage. Usually placed at FOH.

name: Lighting Crew
desc: A Group Of Individuals Trained In Lighting Skills And Techniques, And Collectively Assembled To Work On A Stage, Film, Or Video Production. The Group May Include Any Or All Of The Following: Stagehands, Electrics, Electricians, Roadies, Gaffers, Grips, Operators And Lighting

name: Lighting Design
desc: The Planned Application Of Lighting Systems To An Indoor Or Outdoor Space.

name: Lighting Designer
desc: One Who Plans Lighting Compositions, Lays Out Light Plots, Directs The Focusing Of Luminaires, And Determines The Various Intensities, Colors, Looks, And Cues For A Lighting Production.

name: Lighting Director
desc: One Who Is Responsible For The Execution Of The Lighting Design For A Production, And, In Some Instances, May Also Be The Lighting Designer.

name: Lighting Gel
desc: A translucent colored material placed in front of a light source to alter its color or diffuse its light output.

name: Lighting Grid
desc: See The Definition For Grid.

name: Lighting plan
desc: A scale drawing of the stage and auditorium showing the position, type, circuit, color, and focus direction of every lighting instrument in the rig. The key documentation for the lighting design.

name: Lighting System
desc: The Set Of Equipment That Is Used To Produce Light, Including A Luminaire And Control.

name: Lighting Technician
desc: 1) One Trained In The Lighting Skills And Techniques Necessary For The Implementation Of The Lighting Design For A Particular Production. 2) See Definition #2 For Electrician.

name: Lighting Technique
desc: A Way To Light A Space To Achieve A Desired Effect.

name: Lighting visualizer
desc: Software used to pre-program and preview lighting designs in 3D before arriving on site. Examples include Capture, Wysiwyg, and MA 3D. Allows designers to program offline without the physical rig.

name: Light Ladder
desc: A Steel Or Iron, Ladder-Like Apparatus Used To Hang A Plurality Of Luminaires.

name: Light Leak
desc: Unwanted Light That Escapes A Luminaire From A Location Other Than Its Intended Opening.

name: Light Level
desc: The Average Illumination On A Subject, Performing Area, Or Part Thereof.

name: Light Meter
desc: Any Apparatus Used To Measure Various Quantities Of Light, I.E., Color Temperature, Footcandles, Lux, Etc.

name: Lightness
desc: The brightness of an area (subjectively) judged relative to the brightness of a similarly illuminated area that appears to be white or highly transmitting.

name: Lightning Connector
desc: A proprietary Apple connector for mobile devices (iPhones, iPads, iPods) connecting to host computers, external monitors, cameras, USB chargers, and peripherals.

name: Lightning Measurement Method
desc: A measurement method that allows for the evaluation of the luma signal gain and for making chroma/luma  gain comparisons. It can also provide simple indication of inter-channel timing errors indicated by a bowing in the trace between the green-magen- ta transition. Tektronix developed this two-dimensional Lightning display, named because of the zigzag trace pattern it produces. This display is created by plotting luminance versus B-Y in the upper half of the display and inverted luminance versus R-Y in the lower half of the display. The bright dot in the center of the screen is the luminance blanking level.

name: Light Output
desc: Luminous Flux, Measured In Lumens. The Light Output Rating Of A Lamp Is A Measure Of Its Total Integrated  Light Output. See Also Lumen.

name: Light Plot
desc: The Diagrammatic Layout Of Luminaires And Related Equipment, And Their Application(S) For A Lighting Production.

name: Light Pollution
desc: The Spillage Of Light Into Areas Where It Is Not Required.

name: Light Source
desc: The Object That Produces The Light. For Electric Lighting, A Lamp; For Daylighting,  The Sun.

name: Light Spill
desc: This Is The Unwanted Spillage Of Light Onto Adjacent Areas And May Affect Sensitive Receptors Particularly Residential Properties And Ecological Sites.

name: Light Tree
desc: A Stand With Arms Attached.

name: Light Trespass
desc: Light That Impacts On A Surface Outside Of The Area Designed To Be Lit By A Lighting Installation.

name: Light Valve Technology
desc: A light valve projector uses a bulb as the source of light. The valve technology changes the color and intensity of the source to form the picture. Film or slide projectors are examples of light valve technology. The Digital Micro-Mirror Device (DMD); also known as the Digital Light Processor (DLP), the Image Light Amplifier (ILA), and LCD are all examples of electronic light valve technology. Obtaining black in a pic- ture produced by a light valve projector requires an ability to shut the light off in particular areas of the picture. Shutting light off in a small area is actually rather difficult. Consequently, the real picture contrast ratio of a number of these projectors is rather poor.

name: Lime
desc: See Follow Spot. Lime Is An Old-Fashioned Word, Going Back To The Earliest Days Of Stage Lighting.

name: Limited run
desc: A production scheduled for a specific, finite number of performances, as opposed to an open-ended run.

name: Limiter
desc: A Circuit That Allows Signals Below A Specified Input Power To Pass Unaffected While Attenuating The Peaks Of Stronger Signals That Exceed This Input Power. A Limiter Is  A Compressor With A High Ratio And, Generally, A Fast Attack Time.

name: Limiting
desc: Special circuitry is sometimes included in equipment to limit bandwidth or amplitude, i.e., white amplitude in cameras is generally limited. Saturation of matte generators in switchers are generally limited to stop illegal colors.

name: Limit Switch
desc: An Electro-Mechanical Switch That Trips (Changes State) When Contacted By A Moving Device. They Are Used To Halt The Motion Of A Winch Or Other Electro-Mechanical Device.

name: Line
desc: Level-The Standard Operating Level For Professional Audio Equipment, Including Mixing Consoles.'

name: Linear
desc: A Device Or Circuit With A Linear Characteristic Means That A Signal Passing Through It Is Not Distorted.

name: Linear Addressing
desc: This is a modern method of addressing the display memory. The display memory (in the IBM PC world) was originally located in a 128-Kbyte area from A000:0 through BFFF:F, too small for today’s display systems with multi-megabyte memories. Linear addressing allows the display memory to be addressed in upper memory, where a large contiguous area is set aside for it.

name: Linear (Assembly) Editing
desc: Editing using media like tape, in which material must be accessed in order (e.g., to access scene 5 from the beginning of the tape, one must proceed from scene 1 through scene 4). See Nonlinear Editing.

name: Linear Distortion
desc: Distortion that is independent of signal amplitude. These distortions occur as a result of the system’s inability to uniformly transfer amplitude and phase characteristics at all frequencies. When fast signal components such as transitions and high frequency chrominance are affected differently than slower line-rate or field-rate information, linear distortions are probably present. These distortions are more commonly caused by imperfect transfer characteristics in the signal path. However linear distortions can also be externally introduced. Signals such as power line hum can couple into the video signal and manifest themselves as dis- tortions.

name: Linear Editing
desc: A type of tape editing in which you assemble the pro- gram from beginning to end. If you require changes, you must re-record everything downstream of the change. The physical nature of the medium (for example, analog videotape) dictates how you place material on the medium. See Nonlinear Editing.

name: Linear Fluorescent Lamp
desc: Any Of The Family Of Straight Tubular Fluorescent Lamps. Lamps Are Available In 6-Inch To 8-Foot Lengths, With The Most-Common Length Being 4 Feet.

name: Linearity
desc: a) This is the basic measurement of how well analog to digital and digital to analog conversion are performed. To test for linearity, a mathematically perfect diagonal line is converted and then compared to a copy of itself. The difference between the two lines is calculated to show linearity of the system and is given as a percentage or range of Least Significant Bits. b) The uniformity of scanning speed which primarily affects the accuracy of geometry along a horizontal or vertical line through the picture center. c) The measurement of how accurately a piece of electronic equipment processes a signal, (a measure of its transparency).

name: Linear Key
desc: a) A term given to a key which contains soft edges and information at many different luminance levels. This is the ability of the keyer to key many levels linearly and means the keyer has a gain close to one. b) A process for the selective overlay of one video image upon

name: Linear PCM
desc: One of the allowed types of audio formats for DVD. It may have up to 8 channels and provide very high sample rates and sample depths. Unfortunately, these very high data rates consume so much DVD capacity that meaningful quantities of both audio and video become prob- lematic.

name: Linear Predictive Coding (LPC)
desc: LPC is a speech coding technique. It models the human vocal tract by producing a time varying filter that predicts the current speech sample from past speech samples.

name: Linear Pulse Distribution Amplifier (Linear Pulse DA)
desc: A linear pulse distribution amplifier will handle 4 Vp-p signals (pulses) but is limited to amplifying and fanning out the signal. Also see Regenerative Pulse DA.

name: Line array
desc: A PA speaker system consisting of multiple identical speaker elements arranged vertically and coupled acoustically, producing a column of sound with controlled vertical dispersion and long throw. Standard for large venue concert sound.

name: Linear Select Decoding
desc: Address decoding technique that uses the most significant address bits to directly enable devices in the system.

name: Linear Time Code (LTC)
desc: Time code recorded on a linear analog track on a videotape. This type of time code can be read only while the tape is moving.

name: Line Blanking
desc: The blanking signal at the end of each horizontal scanning line. Used to make the horizontal retrace invisible. Also called horizontal blanking.

name: Line Check
desc: Process Of Testing Every Input Signal Device On Stage, Usually Without Performers Present

name: Line Compensation
desc: Use of a video line amplifier to pre-compensate for high frequency video signal transmission losses resulting from long distance cable runs (several hundred meters) by boosting those signal frequencies most effected. Without such compensation, deterioration is manifested as loss of fine details and color distortion.

name: Line Count
desc: The total number of horizontal lines in the picture.

name: Line Crawl
desc: Tendency of the eyes to follow the sequentially flashing scanning lines of interlaced scanning up or down the screen in the same way that the eyes follow the sequentially flashing light bulbs on a movie theater marquee. Line crawl tends to reduce vertical resolution.

name: Line Doubler
desc: A video processor that doubles the number of lines in the scanning system in order to create a display with scan lines that are less visible. Some line doublers convert from interlaced to progressive scan.

name: Line Doubling
desc: Any number of schemes to convert interlaced scanning to progressive scanning at the display, the simplest of which simply doubles each scanning line. More elaborate schemes use line interpolation and motion compensation or median filtering.

name: Line driver
desc: A signal amplifier extending video over longer distances. Similar to a distribution amplifier (DA) with one input and one output.

name: Line Frequency
desc: The number of horizontal scans per second, normally 15,734.26 times per second for NTSC color systems and 15,625 in PAL.

name: Line Input
desc: An Input On A Mixing Console That Is Designed To Accept Line-Level Signal.

name: Line Interpolation
desc: An advanced mechanism used in some line doublers that calculates the value of scanning lines to be inserted between existing ones.

name: Line Level
desc: A Signal Whose Level Falls Between -10 Dbu And +30 Dbu.

name: Line Locked
desc: a) The sync pulses of cameras are locked to the AC mains frequency. b) In CCTV, this usually refers to multiple cameras being pow- ered by a common alternating current (AC) source (either 24 VAC, 110 VAC or 240 VAC) and consequently have field frequencies locked to the same AC source frequency (50 Hz in CCIR systems and 60 Hz in EIA systems).

name: Line-Locked Clock
desc: A design that ensures that there is always a constant number of samples per scan line, even if the timing of the line changes.

name: Line Mode
desc: A Dolby Digital decoder operational mode. The dialnorm reference playback level is -31 dBFS and dynamic range words are used in dynamic range compression. Refer to Dynamic Range Compression.

name: Line of Sight
desc: Refers to what the audience can see in a direct line from where they are sitting.

name: Line out
desc: Audio output. In consumer systems typically 10,000–50,000 ohms at -10 dB or -20 dB.

name: Line-Out Monitor
desc: A monitor connected to a recording device that displays the finished product. A line-out monitor may be a video monitor (video product), an audio speaker (audio product), or a television (both audio and video).

name: Line Pair
desc: A measure of resolution often used in film and print media. In television, lines are used instead, creating confusion when comparing film and video.

name: Line Pairing
desc: A reduction in vertical resolution caused when a display (or camera) does not correctly space fields, resulting in an overlap of odd and even numbered scanning lines. See also Random Interlace.

name: Line Pair, Optical
desc: In optical measurements and specifications, resolu- tion is specified in terms of line-pairs per unit distance or unit angle, a line pair consisting of one “black” plus one “white” line. Thus one line pair corresponds to two television lines.

name: Line Powered
desc: A camera in which the power is supplied along the same coaxial cable that carries the video signal.

name: Line Rate
desc: The rate at which scanning lines appear per second (the number of scanning lines per frame times the frame rate); sometimes used (non-quantitatively) as an indication of the number of scanning lines per frame (e.g., a high line rate camera).

name: Line Rate Conversion
desc: Standardized video systems currently exist employing the following number of total lines per frame: 525, 625, 1125. Furthermore, each of these operates in a 2:1 interlace mode, with 262.5, 312.5, 562.5 lines per field (with concurrent temporal differences at field rates of 50.00, 59.94, or 60.00 fields per second). Additional systems are being proposed. While simple transcoding by deletion or repetition can be applied, it is more commonly done by applying an algorithm to stored infor- mation in order to generate predictive line structures in the target system.

name: Liners/Friction Plates
desc: Friction controlling plastic sheets used inside a Philips cassette to control winding uniformity and torque level.

name: Lines
desc: Scanning lines or lines of resolution. The latter are hypothetical lines alternating between white and black (or, in the case of chroma resolu- tion, between complementary colors). The combined maximum number

name: Lines, Active Horizontal
desc: In the scanning of a video image, the line number associated with the format is the total number of lines assigned to one frame. It is in fact a timing specification defining the conjunction with the field frequency the time interval allocated to each horizontal line (com- monly measured in number of samples at the specified sampling rate or in microseconds). Some of these lines and intervals carry image information, some from the total assigned are dedicated to operational and control functions, including returning the scanning beam back to the upper left corner to begin the next field. Those allotted time intervals (lines) actually carrying image information or image-associated information such as cap- tioning, image test signals, etc., are the active lines. In further reduction of time allocated to image information, some of each active line is dedicated to the horizontal interval to get the scanning beam to return to the left- edge starting point for the next line and to reaffirm color subcarrier, etc.

name: Lines, Active Vertical
desc: In a scanning standard, the number of raster lines per frame that are not required to contain blanking. The active vertical lines may include signals containing non-image information.

name: Line Set
desc: A System Consisting Of One Or More Lift Lines And Related Components Operating Together To Lift, Lower, Or Suspend A Load.

name: Line Shaft Winch
desc: Winch With A Series Of Cable Drums Connected To A Gearbox By A Common Shaft.

name: Lines, Television
desc: Television images are scanned in a sequence of horizontal lines, beginning at the upper left corner, and reaching the bottom right corner at the end of the field. Thereupon the scan is returned to the upper left corner to begin the next field. As a consequence of the line structure, all television images are sampled vertically. Within a line, the signal may remain analog or be sampled digitally. A television line is also a measure of time, representing the interval allocated to one line.

name: Line Store
desc: A memory buffer which stores a single digital video line. One application for line stores is use with video filtering algorithms or video compression applications.

name: Line Structure Visibility
desc: The ability to see scanning lines. Seeing them makes it harder to see the image (like looking out a window through Venetian blinds or not being able to see the forest for the trees). Some ATV schemes propose blurring the boundary between scanning lines for this reason.

name: Line Sync
desc: The sync signal pulse transition that defines the start of a scan line. Line sync may be the start of a normal sync or the start of an equalization or broad pulse.

name: Line Sync Frequency
desc: See Line Frequency.

name: Line Time
desc: The time interval between OH data or the time taken for a complete scan line. Example: In a PAL system the line time is 64 µs.

name: Line Time Linear Distortions
desc: Causes tilt in line-rate signal compo- nents such as white bars. The amount of distortion is expressed in as  a percentage of the amplitude at the center of the line bar amplitude. Distortions involving signals in the 1 µsec to 64 µsec range. Line Time distortions can also be quantified in Kbar units. In large pictures details, this distortion produces brightness variations between the left and right sides of the screen. Horizontal streaking and smearing may also be

name: Line Time Waveform Distortion
desc: See Line Time Linear Distortions.

name: Line voltage
desc: Alternating current (AC) at the level typically found in the home.

name: Link
desc: A Physical Layer communication path.

name: LinkLicense
desc: A license key that unlocks additional or enhanced features within an Extron product.

name: lip sync
desc: refers to synchronization between mouth movement and thewords on the film's soundtrack

name: Lip Synchronization
desc: The absence of noticeable lag or lead between the video and the audio playback.

name: Liquid Crystal Display  (Lcd)
desc: Display Devices That Use Bipolar Films Sandwiched Between Thin Panes Of Glass. They Are Lightweight And Transmissive Or Reflective, And Are Often Used In Hmos.

name: Liquid Dimmer
desc: See The Definition For Salt Water Dimmer.

name: Liquid Gate
desc: A printing system in which the original is immersed in a suitable liquid at the moment of exposure in order to reduce the effect of surface scratches and abrasions.

name: List Box
desc: Used to make a selection from a list of options. If the list is too long to fit inside the given area, a vertical scroll bar moves the list up and down.

name: Listener
desc: Device that inputs data from a data bus.

name: Listening plane
desc: In acoustics, the intersection of the audio field (horizontal plane) with the listener's ear level.

name: Little Endian
desc: A process which starts with the low-order byte and ends with the high-order byte. Intel processors use the little endian format.

name: Live
desc: Having Any Voltage Potential In Reference To Neutral Or Ground.

name: Live 360(Also referred to as live VR)
desc: Virtual reality live video (often 360 degree video) that can be viewed as the event or experience is actually happening through apps such as Facebook, YouTube and other compatible 360 players.

name: Live End
desc: The End Of A Rope Or Part Of A Device That Is Active Or Load Carrying.

name: Live End Dead End
desc: An Acoustical Treatment Plan For Rooms In Which One End Is Highly Absorbent And The Other End Reflective And Diffusive.

name: Live Load
desc: That Part Of A System Load That May Be Added Or Deleted (I.E. Lights Hung From A Pipe Batten).

name: Live move
desc: In Eos, a non-intensity parameter change (color, position, gobo) that occurs while a fixture is at a visible intensity level, meaning the audience can see the movement. Indicated by an 'L' flag in the PSD.

name: Live programming
desc: Programming lighting cues directly on a console while the output is live, making changes that are immediately visible on stage.

name: Live-streaming
desc: Streaming Media That Is Broadcast To Many People At A Set Time.See Also: On-Demand Streaming.P> Lossless Compression: Reducing The Bandwidth Required For Transmission Of A Given Data Rate Without Loss Of Any Data.

name: LLC (Logical Link Control)
desc: In the Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model of communication, the Logical Link Control Layer is one of two sub- layers of the Data-Link Layer and is concerned with managing traffic (flow and error control) over the physical medium. The Logical Link Control Layer identifies a line protocol, such as SDLC, NetBIOS, or NetWare, and may  also assign sequence numbers to frames and track acknowledgements.)

name: LL-HLS
desc: Low-Latency HLS. An Apple extension to the HLS protocol enabling 2–5 second streaming latency using partial segments and playlist delta updates, while maintaining compatibility with standard HLS players.

name: LLME (Lower Layer Management Entity)
desc: Contains the management functions and functions that concern more than one layer.

name: LMDS (Local Multi-Point Distribution System)
desc: A digital wireless transmission system that works in the 28 GHz range in the U.S. and 24-40 GHz overseas. It requires line of sight between transmitter and receiving antenna, which can be from one to four miles apart depending on weather conditions. LMDS provides bandwidth in the OC-1 to OC-12 range, which is considerably greater than other broadband wireless services. LMDS can be deployed in asymmetric and symmetric configurations. It is designed to provide the “last mile” from a carrier of data services to a large building

name: L-Member (Liaison Member)
desc: A term used within ISO/IEC JTC1 committees. A Liaison Organization does not vote.

name: LNB (Low-Noise Block Converter)
desc: A device hooked to a satellite dish’s feedhorn that receives the signal at ~4 or 12 GHz and converts it to a lower frequency for input into a receiver.

name: Load
desc: An Assembly File That Includes Elements Such As Virtual Sets , Characters , And Performancesprepared For Shooting With Virtual Camera , Simulcam , In A Led Volume , Etc.

name: Load-in
desc: North American term for Get-in. The process of moving all production equipment into a venue and setting up for a show.

name: Loading gallery
desc: A gallery above the stage floor where technicians add and remove counterweights from arbors, usually positioned for access when battens are at their lowest positions.

name: Loading Gallery (Loading Bridge)
desc: A Gallery Above The Stage Floor Where Technicians Add And Remove Counter Weights From The Arbors.Usually Located So Technicians Have Access To Arbors When Battens Are At Their Lowest Positions.

name: Load-out
desc: North American term for Get-out. The process of striking and removing all production equipment from a venue after the final performance.

name: Load Rating
desc: 1) The Maximum Electrical Load That Something, Such As Wire, Fuses, Electrical Connectors, Etc., Can Safely Accommodate. 2) The Maximum Weight That Something Can Safely Accommodate.

name: Load Resistance
desc: The impedance or resistance (load) that a cable places on a signal being transmitted through it. In the case of a high frequency signal, signal-to-cable matching is essential to prevent signal deterioration. The cable should be terminated by a specific load resistance, usually 50 or 75 ohms. Improper cable loading results in signal distortion, ghost images, color loss and other adverse phenomena. Most video inputs have the proper termination built in.

name: Load Sensing
desc: A Mechanical Or Electrical Device That Senses The Load In A Cable Or Block And Produce A Signal That Can Be Read By A Controlling Device

name: Load Side Brake (Load Brake)
desc: A Brake In The Power Train Of The Winch That Is Attached To The Same Shaft As The Load, At The Output Side Of The Gearbox.

name: LOAS (Low Overhead Audio Stream)
desc: This is an audio-only transport format for applications where an MPEG-4 audio object needs to be trans- mitted and additional transport overhead is an issue.

name: Local Bus Transfer
desc: The host/local bus transfer consumes a smaller percentage of available bandwidth during video/graphics transfers than earlier bus standards but the still-noticeable performance penalty may be objectionable for some users, especially when compared to systems that circumvent it.

name: Local Decode
desc: A feature of Indeo video interactive allowing the playback application to tell the codec to decode only a rectangular subregion of the source video image: the viewport. See Viewport.

name: Local Tally
desc: A tally of which bus on an M/E is active regardless of whether or not it is on air.

name: Local Workstation, Drive, Disk, File System, or Printer
desc: The physical workstation whose keyboard and mouse you are using, all hardware that  is connected to that workstation, and all software that resides on that hardware or its removable media.

name: Locate (fixture)
desc: A console command that sets all parameters of a selected fixture to its default 'home' position — typically full white, beam open, gobo open, and centered pan/tilt. Used as a starting point for programming.

name: Locate (Menu)
desc: The 3D function used to move or relocate an image. Locate moves the image as if it were in three-dimensional space, even though the image is seen on a two-dimensional video screen.

name: Location
desc: Shooting locale.

name: Location Based VR (Also referred to as Experintial Marketing OR Site Activations)
desc: A VR experience that takes place outside the home—in a movie theater, mall, amusement park, etc. In location-based VR, a user is wearing a head mounted display and the location itself is designed in a way to heighten the experience further (i.e. if the user walks past a virtual air conditioner, they will feel cold air). One of the benefits of location-based VR is that it provides the user the opportunity to interact with other people. While these experiences have less scale, they can be a value add for advertisers in terms of additional foot traffic and word of mouth from visitors.

name: Location Fresnel
desc: A Fresnel Spotlight Used Primarily In Non-Standard Production Settings, I.E., Locations Other Than Stages Or Studios. Because Portability Is Generally A Concern, They Tend To Be Smaller In Size When Compared To Studio Fresnels Of The Same Wattage.

name: location( or on location )
desc: the properties or places ( interior or exterior ) used for filmingaway from the studio , set , or ( back ) lot , often to increase theauthenticity and realism of the film's appearance ; exteriors areabbreviated as ext . , and interiors as int .

name: location sound
desc: refers to recording background sound on location , to improvethe film's realism ; see also buzz track

name: Locator
desc: A mark added to a selected frame to qualify a particular loca- tion within a sequence. User-defined comments can be added to locators.

name: Locked
desc: a) A video system is considered to be locked when the

name: locked - down shot
desc: refers to a camera shot in which the camera remainsimmobile , while something happens off - screen ( e.g. , an offscreen death ) - a technique to create suspense

name: Locking Collar
desc: A Fastening Device Located On The Counter Weight Arbor Rods Above The Upper Spreader Plate And Counter Weights And Intended To Help Keep The Weights In The Arbor During A Hard Impact.

name: Locking rail
desc: A structural railing designed to support rope locks for easy operation and to hold out-of-balance loads in the rigging system. Also serves as a safety railing.

name: Locking Rail (Loading Rail)
desc: A Structural Railing Designed To Support Rope Locks In A Way That Allows Them To Be Easily Operated. It Holds The Out-Of- Balance Loads From The Rigging System Held By Rope Locks. It Also Serves As A Safety Railing For Operators And Other Personnel.

name: Locking Range
desc: The time range measured in micro- or nano-seconds over which a video decoder can “lock” or stabilize a signal. Digital out of range signals may require “rubber-band” buffering using a parallel shift register (FIFO) to reduce the locking range.

name: Lock rail
desc: The part of the flying system where brakes and rope locks are applied to ensure a fly bar cannot move once set at a desired height.

name: Lock-Up Time
desc: The time before a machine is activated and the time it is ready for use.

name: LOD (Level of Detail)
desc: An important mechanism for achieving a high level of performance in a 3D virtual world. It balances the quantity (extent) of an object with its quality (detail). As some measure of the distance between the viewer and the object change, a related change is made in the quantity and quality of the rendering of an object.

name: LOF (Loss of Frame)
desc: LOF is a generic term with various meanings depending on the signal standards domain in which it is being used. A SONET port status indicator that activates when an LOF defect occurs and does not clear for an interval of time equal to the alarm integration period, which is typically 2.5 seconds.

name: Loft Block
desc: A Pulley Mounted To The Gridiron Or Support Steel That Supports And Changes The Direction Of A Lift Line Cable Between The Load And The Head Block.

name: Lofting
desc: The ability to stretch a “skin” over shapes that are in fact cross- sectional ribs.

name: Loft Well
desc: (1) An Opening In The Gridiron Designed For The Attachment Of Loft Blocks So That Lift Lines Can Pass Through It.(2) An Opening In The Gridiron Designed So Cables Can Pass Through From Blocks Mounted Above Without Rubbing Against The Opening.

name: Log
desc: To enter information about your media into bins at the beginning of the editing process. Logging can be done automatically or manually. See Shot Log.

name: Logarithm
desc: An Exponent Of 10 In The Common Logarithms To The Base 10. For Example, 10 To The Exponent 2=100; The Log Of 100=2.

name: Logarithmic Scale
desc: A mathematical function which spreads out low values and squeezes together higher values.

name: Loge
desc: The part of a theatre mezzanine closest to the stage.

name: Logging
desc: Going Through All Your Video Clips To Weed Out The Bad Ones And Rename/Organize The Good Ones To Make Finding The Right Files Easier During The Editing Process

name: Logical
desc: An artificial structure or organization of information created for convenience of access or reference, usually different from the physical structure or organization. For example, the application specifications of DVD (the way information is organized and stored) are logical formats.

name: Logical Channel
desc: A virtual connection between peer Multiplex Layer (FlexMux or TransMux) entities. It has associated parameters relating to its priority or error resilience tools applied to the Adaption Layer packets to be transported in this logical channel.

name: Logical Unit
desc: A physical or virtual peripheral device, such as a DVD-ROM drive.

name: Logical Value
desc: A description of the memory blocks disks used for the frame store.

name: Logic Analyzer
desc: Test system capable of displaying 0s and 1s, as well as performing complex test functions. Logic analyzers typically have 16 to 32 input lines and can store sequences of sixteen or more bits on each of the input lines.

name: Logic Comparator
desc: Test product that compares pin-for-pin operation of an IC operating in-circuit with a known good reference IC.

name: Logic Probe
desc: Handheld troubleshooting tool that detects logic state and activity on digital circuit nodes.

name: Logic Pulser
desc: Handheld troubleshooting tool that injects controlled digital signals into logic nodes.

name: Login
desc: To log in to a workstation is to establish a connection to the workstation and to identify yourself as an authorized user.

name: Login Account
desc: A database of information about each user that, at the minimum, consists of login name, user ID, and a home directory.

name: Login Name
desc: A name that uniquely identifies a user to the system.

name: Login Screen
desc: The window that you see after powering on the system, before you can access files and directories.

name: logline
desc: a short , introductory summary of a film , usually found on thefirst page of the screenplay , to be read by executives , judges ,agents , producers and script - readers ; all screenwriters useloglines to sell their scripts ; also known as premise ; seealsohigh concept hook

name: Logout
desc: To log out from a workstation is to finish a connection to the workstation.

name: LO (Local Origination Channel)
desc: A channel on a cable system (exclu- sive of broadcast signals) which is programmed by the cable operator and subject to his exclusive control.

name: Longitudinal Curvature
desc: Any deviation from straightness of a length of tape.

name: Longitudinal Direction
desc: Along the length of the tape.

name: Longitudinal Time Code (LTC)
desc: Audio rate time code information that is stored on its own audio track. This audio rate signal allows the editing system to track the position of the tape even at high shuttle speeds where VITC data could not be used.

name: Longitudinal Uniformity
desc: Ratio Of The Lowest To Highest Road Surface Luminance On A Set Of Grid Points In A Line Along The Centre Of A Driving Lane.

name: Long Shot
desc: Camera view of a subject or scene, usually from a distance, showing a broad perspective.

name: Long Shot (Establishing)
desc: Typically Used To Establish The Subject In Their Environment

name: long - shot( LS )
desc: a camera view of an object or character from a considerabledistance so that it appears relatively small in the frame , e.g. , aperson standing in a crowd of people or a horse in a vastlandscape ; variations are the medium long - shot ( or midshot ) ( MS ) and the extreme long - shot ( ELS or XLS ) ; alsocalled a wide shot ; a long shot often serves as anestablishingshot ; contrast to close - up ( CU ) ; a full - shot is a type of longshot that includes a subject's entire body ( head to feet ) .

name: long take( or lengthy take )
desc: a shot of lengthy duration ; see also mise - en - scene

name: Long Throw
desc: A Speaker That Is Designed To Transmit Sound Over A Long Distance Colloquial Term For Narrow Coverage (High Forward Aspect Ratio) Loudspeakers Use To Reach The Back Of The Room.

name: Long Time Distortion
desc: The low frequency transient resulting from a change in APL. This distortion usually appears as a very low frequency damped oscillation. The peak overshoot, in IRE, is generally quoted as the amount of distortion. Setting time is also sometimes measured.

name: Long Time Linear Distortions
desc: Distortions involving signals in the greater-than-16 msec range. Long time distortions affect slowly varying aspects of the signal such as changes in APL which occur at intervals of a few seconds. The affected signal components range in duration from

name: Look Ahead Preview
desc: See Preview.

name: Look Up Table ( LUT )
desc: A Mathematical Formula Or Matrix That Acts As A Color Correction , Such As Transformingbetween A Raw Camera Image And The Desired Display Appearance Such As An Sdr Or Hdrmonitor .

name: Loom
desc: The Protective Bundle Wrapped Around Video And Networking Cables On A Volume To Tetherbetween Equipment Carts And Other Connection Points .

name: Loop
desc: Piece of tape spliced beginning (head) to end (tail) for continuous playback or recording. To fold around. A loop/slack section of film with the necessary “play” to allow film which had been previously and continuously moving from a reel to be intermittently moved through a grate/projection head/optical lens arrangement. Proper loop size is important in threading a film projector, i.e., in telecine for film to videotape transfer.

name: Loop Filter
desc: Used in a PLL design to smooth out tiny inaccuracies in the output of the phase comparator that might drive the loop out of lock. The loop filter helps to determine how well the loop locks, how long it takes to lock and how easy it is to cause the loop out of lock.

name: Loop Frame Store
desc: The principal is that a series of video frames is com- pressed and stored in a continuous loop. This records a certain number of frames and then records over them again until an alarm signal is received. When this happens it carries on recording for a dozen frames or so and then stops. This means that frames before and after the incident are recorded. This eliminates the boring searching through hours of videotape and concentrates on the period of activity.

name: Looping
desc: a) A term used to describe the chaining of a video signal through several video devices (distribution amplifiers, VCRs, monitors, etc.). A VCR may be hooked up to a distribution amplifier which is supplied with a video input connector and a loop output connector. When a signal is fed to the distribution amplifier, it is also fed unprocessed to the loop output connector (parallel connection) on the distribution amplifier. In turn, the same signal is fed to another device which is attached to the first one and so on. Thus a very large number of VCRs or other video devices can be looped together for multiple processing. b) An input that includes two con- nectors. One connector accepts the input signal, and the other connector  is used as an output for connecting the input signal to another piece of equipment or to a monitor.

name: Loop Through
desc: A video signal entering a piece of equipment is returned to the outside world for further use. Loop through circuitry requires careful design to prevent signal degradation.

name: Loose buffer cable
desc: A fiber optic cable type where the fiber is encased in a loosely surrounding buffer tube. Used typically for outdoor installations.

name: Loosen Up
desc: A camera director's instruction to zoom out slightly for a wider shot of the subject.

name: Loose Tube Gel Filled (LTGF)
desc: A loose buffer cable filled with insulating gel material.

name: Lo/Ro (Left Only, Right Only)
desc: A type of two-channel downmix for multichannel audio programs. Lo/Ro downmixes are intended for applica- tions where surround playback is neither desired nor required.

name: LORT
desc: League of Resident Theatres. The largest professional theatre association in the US, representing member regional theatres and establishing standard Equity contracts for regional productions.

name: Loss
desc: Reduction in signal strength or level.

name: Loss Budget
desc: A specified maximum tolerable attenuation of optical power as light passes through a fiber optic system.

name: Lossless
desc: Compression techniques that reduce file size without losing any data.

name: Lossless (Compression)
desc: a) Reducing the bandwidth required for transmission of a given data rate without loss of any data. b) Image compression where the recovered image is identical to the original.

name: Lossy
desc: Compression techniques that discard data in the process. More data loss = smaller file but lower quality. Includes JPEG and MPEG.

name: Lossy compression
desc: Reducing The Total Data Rate By Discarding Data That Is Not Critical. Both The Video And Audio For Dtv Transmission Will Use Lossy Compression.

name: Loudness
desc: A Subjective Term For The Sensation Of The Magnitude Of Sound.

name: Loudspeaker
desc: An Electroacoustic Transducer That Produces Sound In Response To An Electrical Audio Signal Input. May Refer To Individual Transducers (Or “Drivers”) Or To Complete Speaker Systems Consisting Of More Than One. To Reproduce A Wide Range Of Frequencies, Most Loudspeaker Systems Use More Than One Driver. Individual Drivers Reproduce Different Frequency Ranges.

name: Louver
desc: A  Series Of Baffles Or Reflectors That Is Used To Shield A Light Source From View At Certain Angles,  Absorb Unwanted Light, Or Reflect Light.

name: Louvre
desc: Assembly Used To Control Light Distribution From A Luminaire.

name: Low Angle
desc: Makes Your Subject Look Larger And More Powerful High Angle ­ Makes Your Subject Look Smaller And Weaker Worms Eye ­ Placing The Camera Very Low (Often On The Ground)

name: low - angle shot
desc: a shot in which the subject is filmed directly from below andthe camera tilts up at the action or character , to make thesubject appear larger than life , more formidable , taller andmore menacing ; contrast to a high - angle shot

name: Low Band Color
desc: The old, original professional videotape color recording.

name: Lowboy (Loboy)
desc: A Heavy-Duty Stand Designed To Hold Luminaires Or Heavy Grip Equipment. The Stand Is Equipped With Wheels And Short Risers, And A 1-1/8" Receiver And A Grip Head.

name: Low Delay
desc: A video sequence does not include B-pictures when the low delay flag is set; consequently, the pictures follow in chronological order, and low delay is obtained. Normally,

name: Low End
desc: The lowest frequency of a signal. See High End.

name: LOWER
desc: LOWER With arm extended downward, forefinger pointing down, move hand in small horizontal circles.

name: Lower Layer
desc: A relative reference to the layer immediately below a given Enhancement Layer (implicitly including decoding of all layers below this Enhancement Layer).

name: Lower Third
desc: The lower portion of a video frame containing graphical information such as a name, title, social media handle, or scripture reference.

name: LowFER
desc: One who experiments with radio communication at unusually low frequencies (typically 1750 meters, which is 160-90 kHz and can be used under FCC Part 15).

name: Low-Frequency Amplitude Distortion
desc: A variation in amplitude level that occurs as a function of frequencies below 1 MHz.

name: Low Impedance
desc: Electronic Input/Output Circuits With An Impedance Of A Few Hundred Ohms Synonym: Low Z, Lo Z

name: Low Impedance Mike
desc: A mike designed to be fed into an amplifier or transformer with input impedance of 150 to 250 ohms.

name: Low Key
desc: A scene is reproduced in a low key if the tone range of the reproduction is largely in the high density portion of the H and D scale of the process.

name: Low latency streaming
desc: Streaming configurations minimizing delay between capture and viewing. Standard HLS latency is 10–30 seconds; LL-HLS targets 2–5 seconds; WebRTC and SRT can achieve sub-second latency.

name: Low Noise Triangular Waveform Generator
desc: A triangle generator at the heart of a Class D amplifier, using closed-loop feedback to reduce audio band noise content.

name: Low-Order
desc: Pertaining to the weight or significance assigned to the digits of a number. In the number 123456, the lower order digit is six. The three low-order bits of the binary word 11100101 are 101.

name: Low-Pass Filter
desc: A Low-Pass Filter Allows Frequencies Below Its Cutoff To Pass And Progressively Attenuates Frequencies Above The Cutoff.

name: Low-Pressure Sodium
desc: A Discharge Lamp In Which Light Is Produced By Radiation From Low- Pressure Sodium Vapour.

name: Low-Voltage Lamp
desc: A Lamp That Nominally Operates At 6, 12, Or 24 Volts. A  Transformer Must Be Used To Convert The 120-Volt Line Voltage To The Lower Voltage.

name: LPAC
desc: Lossless Predictive Audio Compression. A lossless audio compression algorithm developed at TU Berlin. Superseded by MPEG-4 Audio Lossless Coding.

name: LPC (Linear Predictive Coding)
desc: An encoding technique used to aid in the prediction of the next sample. This technique can be found in many analogue to digital conversion processes.

name: LPCM
desc: Linear PCM. A pulse code modulation method representing an analog waveform as amplitude values. Used in DVD, Blu-ray, and HDMI standards.

name: LPCM (Linear Pulse Code Modulation)
desc: A pulse code modulation system in which the signal is converted directly to a PCM word without companding, or other processing. Refer to PCM.

name: LPTV (Low Power TV)
desc: LPTV stations provide their communities with local programming, covering events and issues in a smaller area than most TV stations. There were licensed in the United States, 2,190 LPTV stations as of July 1, 1999. As LPTV signals are comparatively weak, LPTV stations don’t generally interfere with larger TV stations using the same frequency.

name: LSB
desc: See Least Significant Bit.

name: LSFOH
desc: Low Smoke and Fumes Zero Halogen. IEC designation for cable jacket passing IEC 332-3 flame, IEC 61034 smoke, and IEC 754-1 corrosivity tests.

name: LSI
desc: See Large Scale Integration.

name: LSP (Line Spectral Pairs)
desc: An alternative representation of linear predictor coefficients. LSPs have very good quantization properties for use in speech coding systems.

name: LS/RS (Left Surround, Right Surround)
desc: The actual channels or speakers delivering discrete surround program material.

name: LSTTL (Low Power Schottky TTL)
desc: Digital integrated circuits that employ Schottky diodes for improved speed/power performance over standard TTL.

name: LTC
desc: See Linear Time Code or Longitudinal Time Code.

name: LTP
desc: Latest Takes Precedence. A channel merging rule where the most recently executed instruction for a parameter takes priority. Used for color, position, and beam attributes on most consoles, so the latest cue's values override previous ones.

name: LTP (Long Term Prediction)
desc: A method to detect the innovation in the voice signal. Since the voice signal contains many redundant voice seg- ments, we can detect these redundancies and only send information about the changes in the signal from one segment to the next. This is accom- plished by comparing the speech samples of the current segment on a sample by sample basis to the reconstructed speech samples from the previous segments to obtain the innovation information and an indicator of the error in the prediction.

name: Lt/Rt (Left Total, Right Total)
desc: Two-channel delivery format for Dolby Surround. Four channels of audio, Left, Center, Right and Surround (LCRS) are matrix encoded for two-channel delivery (Lt/Rt). Lt/Rt encoded pro- grams are decoded using Dolby Surround and Dolby Surround Pro Logic decoders. Refer to Dolby Surround and Dolby Surround Pro Logic.

name: LTS (Lifetime Time Stamp)
desc: Gives the duration (in milliseconds) an object should be displayed in a scene. LTS is implicit in some cases such as a video sequence where a frame is displayed for 1/frame-rate or until the next frame is available, whichever is larger. An explicit LTS is necessary when displaying graphics and text. An audiovisual object should be decod- ed only once for use during its life time.

name: Lua scripting
desc: A lightweight scripting language used in Q-SYS and other AV control platforms for custom control logic, third-party device integration, and automation tasks.

name: Lucent Connector (LC)
desc: A high-density fiber optic connector replacing the SC due to smaller size. Used on Extron fiber optic products.

name: LUFS
desc: Loudness Units Full Scale. A loudness measurement standard (EBU R128, ITU-R BS.1770) used in broadcast and streaming to measure perceived loudness rather than peak levels. Streaming platforms normalize content to target LUFS levels (-14 LUFS for Spotify, -16 LUFS for YouTube).

name: Luma
desc: See the definition for Luminance.

name: Luma Bandpass
desc: A filter used to pass luma information only. It is used for the same purpose as a chroma bandpass filter. See Chroma Bandpass.

name: Luma (Component)
desc: A matrix, block or single pel representing a mono- chrome representation of the signal and related to the primary colors in the manner defined in the bit stream. The symbol used for luma is Y.

name: Luma Delay
desc: Luma delay is used in PAL/NTSC encoding and color decoding in TV systems and processing of luminance in VTRs. The Y signal occupies a greater bandwidth than the low definition, narrowband chroma. This also means that the signal is delayed less as the bandwidth of a cir- cuit increases. Without a delay, the chroma would be printed slightly later than the corresponding luminance signal.

name: Lumakey
desc: When keying one image onto another, if the composition is based on a combination of luminance and brightness values, it constitutes a lumakey.

name: LumaLift™
desc: The Lumalift Line Of Lighting Hoists Is Available From Clancy In Self-Climbing And Grid Mounted Versions. The Hoists Are Compact And Feature Built-In Plugging Strips And Electric Cable Handling.

name: Lumen
desc: The Unit Of Luminous Flux. The Lumen Is The Time Rate Of Flow Of Light.

name: Lumen (lu)
desc: A light intensity produced by the luminosity of one candela in one radian of a solid angle.

name: Lumen maintenance
desc: The percentage of initial lumens a light source produces at a specified point in its life (e.g., L70 = 70% of initial lumens at rated hours). A key metric for LED fixture comparisons.

name: Lumens Per Watt (Lpw)
desc: See Efficacy.

name: Luminaire
desc: A Complete Lighting Unit Consisting Of A Lamp Or Lamps, Together With The Parts Designed To Distribute The Light, To Position And Protect  The Lamps, And To Connect The Lamps To The Power Supply. Also Referred To As A Light Fixture, Fitting, Or Unit.

name: Luminance
desc: The Component Of A Video Signal That Includes Information About Its Brightness.

name: Luminance, Constant (Video)
desc: In an image coding system that derives  a luminance signal and two bandwidth-limited color-difference signals, constant luminance prevails if all of the luminance information is encoded into one signal that is supplemented by but totally independent of two color signals carrying only chrominance information, e.g., hue and saturation.

name: Luminance Factor b
desc: At a surface element of a non self-radiating medi- um, in a given direction, under specified conditions of illumination, ratio

name: Luminance Key
desc: A key wherein the keying signal is derived from the instantaneous luminance of a video signal after chroma has been filtered out. That is, for a particular clip level, all parts of a scene that are brighter than that level will appear keyed in, leaving background video everywhere else.

name: Luminance Noise
desc: Noise which manifests itself in a video picture as white snow, typically caused by one of the following situations: low signal level due to poor lighting conditions, poor video signal processing, low quality videotapes, excessively long video cables used without pre-compen- sation, dirt on the video recorder heads which interferes with reading and writing, over-enhancement of the video signal.

name: Luminance Nonlinearity
desc: Present if luminance gain is affected by luminance levels. This amplitude distortion is a result of the system’s inability to uniformly process luminance information over the entire amplitude range. This distortion is also called differential luminance.

name: Luminance, Physics (Generic Usage)
desc: a) Luminance has technical as well as colloquial definitions. The generic flux from a light-emitting or

name: Luminance Range
desc: The range in measured luminance between the lightest and the darkest element of a luminous scene or its display.

name: Luminance Range, Display CRT
desc: The luminance range that can be displayed on a CRT is the ratio of maximum to minimum luminance on the tube face. The maximum practical output is determined by beam current, phosphor efficiency, shadow-mask distortion, etc. The minimum is the luminance of that portion of the tube face being scanned with beam cur- rent set to cut-off. The contributions from room illumination, external and internal reflections, etc., must be recognized.

name: Luminance Range, Display Theater
desc: The luminance range that can be displayed on a theater projection screen is the ratio of maximum to mini- mum luminance achievable during projection of film. The maximum achiev- able highlight is determined by light-source output capacity, projection optical efficiency, the transmission of minimum film densities, screen gain, etc. The minimum is the luminance contribution from house illumination and other stray light, plus optical flare raising black levels, and the trans- mission of maximum film densities. Measured values in typical first-run theaters show luminance ranges of 500:1 to 300:1 (usually limited by house illumination).

name: Luminance Range, Recorded
desc: The luminance range, recorded may be reduced from the luminance range, scene intentionally and/or by the limitations of the recording system. Most systems have a maximum effective signal level limiting the high end, and noise limiting the low end. All of the scene that is of interest must be placed within these two limits by the choice of an appropriate transfer function. Some analog functions permit gradual transitions to overload and/or noise. Digital functions have inflexible limits imposed by the number of levels permitted by the bit assignments.

name: Luminance Range, Scene
desc: The luminance range of original scenes varies from outdoor scenes in sunlight with a range possibly exceeding 10000:1, to indoor scenes with controlled lighting, where the range may be reduced to 10:1 or even less. Adjustment of or accommodation to the luminance range, scene is one of the conditions to be evaluated in deter- mining how the scene is to be recorded. It is a test of artistic judgment  to place the relative luminances for the objects of interest on a suitable portion of the opto-electronic or opto-photographic transfer function in order to produce the desired subjective quality.

name: Luminance Ratio
desc: See Brighmess Ratio.

name: Luminance, Relative, Scene
desc: A convenient linear scale for measuring  in arbitrary units the relative luminance amplitudes within the scene, to be recorded in a video or photographic image, as shown below. The relative luminance scale is one factor affecting the choice of suitably artistic scene

name: Luminance Signal
desc: The black and white signal (the brightness signal) in color TV. The luminance signal is formed by combining a proportion of 30% red, 50% green and 11% blue from the color signal. This combined output becomes the luminance (brightness/monochrome) signal.

name: Luminance, Television
desc: a) When television was monochrome and sensors were in approximate conformance to CIE Photopic Spectral Luminous Efficiency Function, it became common to think of the video signal as the luminance signal. With the introduction of color, a matrix  was designed to develop a luminance function by weighting the R, G, B signals in accordance with the CIE Photopic Spectral Luminance Efficiency

name: Luminance (Y)
desc: Video originates with linear-light (tristimulus) RGB primary components, conventionally contained in the range 0 (black) to

name: Luminescence
desc: The absorption of energy by matter and its following emission as light. If the light follows and then completes itself quickly after absorption of the energy, the term fluorescence is used. If the process is of a longer and more persistent length, the term phosphorescence is applied.

name: Luminosity Curve
desc: A function that expresses the apparent brightness of the spectral colors. It is used in video systems to calculate the luminance signal.

name: Luminous Ceiling
desc: A Dropped Ceiling Containing Lamps Above Translucent Panels. Lumìnous Ceilings Provide Bright, Diffuse Lighting.

name: Luminous Flux
desc: a) The time rate of flow of light. b) The time rate of flow of radiant energy evaluated in terms of a standardized visual response.

name: Luminous Intensity
desc: Total Luminous Flux Within A Given Solid Angle, In Units Of Candelas, Or Lumens / Steradian.

name: Luminous Intensity  Distribution  Data
desc: Curve, Generally Plotted On Polar Or Rectilinear Coordinates,  Which Represents Variation In Luminous Intensity (In Candelas) From A Bare Lamp Or From A Luminaire. Distribution Data Can Also Be Presented In Tabular Format.

name: Luminous Intensity (I)
desc: A Measure Of The Strength Of A Light Source In A Particular Direction, In Candles Or Candelas. It Is Independent Of The Distance From The Source.

name: Luminous ßux
desc: The Time Rate Of Flow Of Light, Measured In Lumens.

name: LUT (Look-Up Table)
desc: A cross-reference table in the computer memory that transforms raw information from the scanner or computer and corrects values to compensate for weakness in equipment or for differences in emulsion types.

name: Lux
desc: Standard International Unit Of Illuminance Equal To 1 Lumen Per Square Meter. One Lux Equals 0.0929 Footcandles.

name: Lux (lx)
desc: a) The metric unit for illumination is 1 lumen per square meter. 1 foot candle = 10.76 Lux. b) A measurement of light. Lux is used in television production to determine the minimum amount of light (lux rating) needed for camera operation. Hence, a “2 lux” camcorder requires less light than a “4 lux” camcorder.

name: LVDS
desc: Low Voltage Differential Signal. A signal transmission standard developed for connecting laptop computers to their local LCD displays.

name: LVDS (Low Voltage Differential Signal)
desc: A transmission method defined by DVB for sending digital information in parallel mode The specification within EN50083-9 describes a 25-pin type D connector using differential lines. The lines consist of a clock, eight data lines, packet sync, and a data-valid line. LVDS has been widely used in laptops to send signals from the motherboard to the flat panel display, because  it uses fewer wires. The technology is also used between the image scaler and the panel in some stand-alone flat panel displays such as SGI’s popular 1600SW flat panel.

name: LV (LaserVision)
desc: Technology used in optical video disk.

name: Lx
desc: Electrics. The Title Is Given To The Lighting Department, And The Chief Electrician Is Known As The Chief Lx.

name: Lyrics
desc: The words of a song in a musical or opera.

name: M
desc: The CCIR designation for 525 scanning-line/30 frame-per-second television. U.S. color television is internationally designated NTSC-M. The M standard is the world’s second oldest (the oldest was a 405-line/25 frame British standard, no longer broadcast).

name: M2
desc: See Miller Squared Code.

name: M4IF (MPEG-4 Industry Forum)
desc: The MPEG-4 Industry Forum starts where the MPEG ends, i.e., dealing with all issues related to practical implementations of the theoretical standards set by the MPEG in commer- cial applications.

name: MAAP
desc: Mini AV Connectivity Modules. Compact mountable metal plates offering pass-through audio, video, phone, data, power, and control connectors in hundreds of models.

name: MAC
desc: Media Access Control. A data communication protocol sub-layer providing addressing and channel access control mechanisms for multi-point networks like LANs.

name: MAC-60
desc: An early version of the HDMAC-60.

name: MAC address
desc: Media Access Control address. A unique 48-bit hardware identifier (e.g., 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E) assigned to each network interface. Used for Layer 2 Ethernet frame delivery within a local network segment.

name: Machine Code
desc: See Machine Language.

name: Machine Cycle
desc: Basic period of time required to manipulate data in a system.

name: Machine Learning
desc: The Application Of Artificial Intelligence Which Enables A Computer To Automatically Learn Andimprove Without Being Specifically Programmed .

name: Machine Room
desc: The Enclosed Room On Or Near A Volume Where The Main Computer Networking , Video Server ,Render Nodes , And Other Critical Infrastructure Are Housed . Also Called Server Closet .

name: Mackie
desc: A professional audio equipment manufacturer known for accessible mixers, powered speakers, and studio monitors. The Mackie Onyx and ProFX series are popular analog and digital mixers for small venues, podcasting, and home studios.

name: MAC (Multiplexed Analog Components)
desc: a) A system in which the components are time multiplexed into one channel using time domain techniques; that is the components are kept separate by being sent at different times through the same channel. There are many different MAC formats and standards. b) A means of time multiplexing component analog video down a single transmission channel such as coax, fiber or a satellite channel. Usually involves digital processes to achieve the time compres- sion. c) A large family of television signal formats sharing the following “two characteristics: color remains in a component rather than composite form, and luminance and chrominance components are time compressed so that active line time remains constant, with chrominance following luminance. Most of the MACs also include digital audio/data channels.

name: Macrobending
desc: A macroscopic deviation of an optical fiber's axis from a straight line due to bending, causing optical loss.

name: Macroblock
desc: In The Typical 4:2:0 Picture Representation Used By Mpeg-2, A Macroblock Consists Of Four Eight By Eight Blocks Of Luminance Data (Arranged In A 16 By 16 Sample Array) And Two Eight By Eight Blocks Of Color Difference Data Which Correspond To The Area Covered By The 16 By 16 Section Luminance Component Of The Picture. The Macroblock Is The Basic Unit Used For Motion Compensated Prediction.

name: Macro (lighting)
desc: A recorded sequence of console button presses or commands that can be triggered with a single button press, used to automate repetitive console operations.

name: madcap comedy
desc: a fast - paced , wild , and reckless humorous work , usually withplenty of slapstick humor , goofy and farcical action , and crazycharacters ; also see screwball comedy

name: made - fors
desc: short for movies filmed or made - for - television , often mid - wayin style between a short drama and a cinematic release

name: MADI
desc: Multichannel Audio Digital Interface. AES10. A digital audio protocol transmitting up to 64 channels of 24-bit audio over a single coaxial or fiber optic cable at standard sample rates (48kHz). Widely used for studio and broadcast multitrack audio.

name: MagicHD
desc: ChamSys's media player software integrated with MagicQ. Supports HD media playback on up to 8 simultaneous layers, controlled directly from the MagicQ console.

name: magic hour
desc: the optimum time for filming romantic or magical scenes dueto ' warm ' and ' soft ' lighting conditions , characterized by a golden - orange hue color ; occurs for about 30 minutes aroundthe time of sunset and sunrise ; aka golden hour

name: MagicQ
desc: ChamSys's professional lighting control software and console platform. Available as free PC/Mac software and as dedicated consoles (MQ50, MQ70, MQ80, MQ250M, MQ500M). Supports up to 400 universes, pixel mapping, and media server integration.

name: Magic Sheet
desc: A custom, user-created graphic display in Eos showing a visual representation of the lighting rig (a stage layout, truss map, or any image) with live channel data overlaid. Used for quick visual feedback and channel selection.

name: MagicVis
desc: ChamSys's free 3D lighting visualizer software integrated with MagicQ. Allows lighting designers to pre-visualize their show, program offline, and generate rigging paperwork including plots and patch lists.

name: Magic Wand
desc: A Three-Dimensional Input Device Used For Pointing And Interaction. A Kind Of Three-Dimensional Mouse.

name: Magnetic Ballast
desc: A Ballast That Uses A Magnetic Core And Coil To Provide The Voltage And Current That Are Needed To Start The Lamp(S) And To Maintain Its Operation. Magnetic Ballasts Are Heavier Than Electronic Ballasts. See Also Ballast.

name: Magnetic deflection
desc: Using a magnetic field to alter the path of an electron beam in a CRT. Magnetic coils carry currents creating fields that control the beam path.

name: Magnetic Recording
desc: The technology and process of recording audio/video information using magnetism as the medium for storage of information. The term is often used to mean the process/capability of both recording and reproduction/playback.

name: Magnetic Tape
desc: With a few exceptions, magnetic tape consists of a base film coated with magnetic particles held in a binder. The magnetic particles are usually of a circular shape and approach single domain size. See Gamma Ferric Oxide, Chromium Dioxide and Cobalt Doped Oxide.

name: Magnetic Track
desc: A sound-track recorded on magnetic film or tape.

name: Magnetism
desc: The property of certain physical materials to exert a force  on other physical materials, and to cause voltage to be induced in conduct- ing bodies moving relative to the magnetized body.

name: Magnetizing Field Strength, H
desc: The instantaneous strength of the magnetic field applied to a sample of magnetic material.

name: Magneto-Optical
desc: Recordable disc technology using a laser to heat spots that are altered by a magnetic field. Other formats include dye-subli- mation and phase-change.

name: Magnitude
desc: The Value Of A Voltage Or Current Waveform.

name: Main Channel
desc: The basic transmission channel of an ATV channel utilizing an augmentation channel.

name: Main Cross-Connect (MC)
desc: The central portion of a facility's backbone cabling providing connectivity between equipment rooms, entrance facilities, and cross-connects. Usually a patch panel distribution.

name: Main data
desc: User data portion of each sector. 2048 bytes.

name: Main Distribution Frame (MDF)
desc: A signal distribution frame connecting outside lines to inside lines.

name: Main (House) Speakers
desc: The Main Loudspeakers For A Sound Reinforce_x0002_Ment System. These Are Usually The Largest And Loudest Loudspeakers, And Are Usually Posi_x0002_Tioned So That Their Sound Seems To Come From The Area Of The Main Stage.

name: Main Light
desc: See The Definition For Key Light.

name: Main Mix
desc: An Audio Signal Found On The Main Output Buss Of A Mixing Console. This Is Typically Used To Either Directly Feed The Mains, Or To Feed Matrices That, In Turn, Feed Various Parts Of The Speaker System.

name: Main Profile
desc: A subset of the syntax of the MPEG-2 video coding specifi- cation that is expected to be supported over a large range of applications. MPEG-2 standard uses four levels which define picture resolution and five profiles which define the compression tools used.

name: Mains
desc: Short For Main Or House Speakers In A Sound Rein_x0002_Forcement System

name: mainstream
desc: a Hollywood - made film with major stars , big budgets , andbighype ; compare to independents ; its extreme opposite istermed counter - cinema ( forms of alternative cinema , suchasavant - garde , art films , Third World cinema , etc. )

name: Maintenance state
desc: Hog 4 OS's feature that ensures correct lighting states when cues are played back out of sequence, compensating for the inherent ambiguity of tracking-based cue programming.

name: Main Visual Profile
desc: Adds support for coding of interlaced, semitrans- parent, and sprite objects to the Core Visual Profile. It is useful for interac- tive and entertainment-quality broadcast and DVD applications.

name: majors
desc: refers to the major Hollywood motion pictureproducer / distributor studios at the present time ( i.e. ,DreamWorks SKG , MGM / UA , Paramount Pictures , 20thCentury Fox , Sony ( Columbia / TriStar ) , Warner Bros ,Universal , and Disney ) ; contrast to the smaller , minimajorproduction - distribution companies ( i.e. , Miramax , NewLine Cinema , and Polygram ) that compete directly with thebigger studios

name: makeup
desc: refers to the materials that are used to prepare the performerfor his / her respective role ( s ) before the camera , anywherefrom facial pancake to elaborate costuming , latex masks , andother ghastly transformations ; the makeup department isheaded by a makeup artist

name: making of ...
desc: a specialized documentary that focuses on the production of aspecific film ; most " making of ... " documentaries are extendedpromotional advertisements before the release of the film , andalmost all of them are shot while the film is in production ;some specialized documentaries of classic films( called retrospectives ) , made years after the film wasreleased , gather interviews and behind the scenes clips , etc.

name: Male
desc: A Term Applied To A Connector That Contains The Pins, Prongs, Blades, And/Or Tabs For Insertion Into The Holes Or Slots Of A Female Connector. The Male Connector Should Never Be Attached To The Line Side Of A Circuit.

name: Male Connector
desc: A connector that has raised edges, pins, or other protruding parts that you plug into a female connector. An example of a male connector is an electrical plug that you plug into a wall outlet.

name: MAMA
desc: The Media Asset Management Association. Mama Serves As A Advanced User-Group And Independent International Industry Consortium, Created By And For Media Producers, Content Publishers, Technology Providers, And Value-Chain Partners To Develop Open Content And Metadata Exchange Protocols For Digital Media Creation And Asset Management. Internet :  Www.Mamgroup.Org.

name: Managed Switch
desc: A network switch that can be configured and managed using software tools, allowing monitoring and traffic control.

name: M and E Tracks
desc: a) Stands for music and effects audio tracks. b) The common designation for a single sound track containing music and sound effects but not dialog.

name: MA-Net
desc: A proprietary networking protocol used by MA Lighting grandMA consoles to distribute DMX data across a network to MA NPU (Network Processing Units) and other MA nodes at high speed.

name: MAN (Metropolitan Area Network)
desc: Network that spans a metropolitan area. Generally, a MAN spans a larger geographic area than a LAN, but a smaller geographic area than a WAN.

name: Man Page
desc: An on-line document that describes how to use a particular IRIX or UNIX command.

name: MA NPU
desc: MA Network Processing Unit. A rackmount MA Lighting device that distributes DMX universes from the grandMA network to physical DMX outputs, allowing a grandMA system to control many universes without overloading the console.

name: Mantissa
desc: Fractional value used as part of a floating point number. For example, the mantissa in the number 0.9873 x 107 is 0.9873.

name: Manual Iris
desc: A manual method of varying the size of a lens’s aperture.

name: ManualMaker™
desc: This Software Program From Clancy Facilitates The Creation Custom Operation And Maintenance Manuals For Stages Using Clancy Hardware. Sections Are Written In Microsoft Word For Most Standard Clancy Products.

name: Map
desc: Refers To A Set Environment Within A Real - Time Engine .

name: Mapping
desc: a) A technique for taking a 2D image and applying (mapping) it as a surface onto a 3D object. b) Conversion of bytes (8 bits) to 2n-bit wide symbols. Thus n is the bit width for the I and Q quantization; e.g., at 64 QAM the symbol width is 2n=6 bit, n=3, i.e., I and Q are subdivided into 23=8 amplitude values each. c) Refers to the definition of memory  for storing data used by a particular display mode. The range of addresses reserved for graphics information in IBM-compatible systems is from A000:0 to BFFF:F.

name: mark
desc: ( 1 ) the name for the clapping of the sticks to sync up thesound and the picture ; and ( 2 ) something on the ground( tape , a stick , chalk , etc. ) that lets the talent know where theyshould be for the shot

name: Mark cue
desc: A pre-position cue in MagicQ that automatically moves LTP (non-intensity) parameters of fixtures to their values for the following cue while the fixtures are at zero intensity, ensuring they are in position before being faded up.

name: Mark (Eos)
desc: An Eos cue flag that pre-positions non-intensity parameters of fixtures while they are at zero intensity, before they are needed in the following cue. Ensures moving lights are in the correct position before being faded up, avoiding visible moves through light.

name: Marker
desc: A Physical Reference For Tracking Purposes . See Also Active Marker And Passive Marker .

name: Marker Based
desc:
A type of AR experience that uses a specific marker 
in the real world, such as a QR code or an AI learned 
concept (recognized object) like a “dog”, to trigger the 
display of AR content (Source: IAB New Standard Ad Unit Portfolio, 
page 34). It does require a pre-determined image, pattern, 
or physical object that is recognized by the camera and 
image recognition software to launch the AR experience. 
For example, a consumer could point their device camera
at a fast food logo and have one experience, then point 
their camera in the same app at a retail store logo and 
experience different AR content that was triggered by 
the appearance of a different logo.

name: Markerless
desc: Markerless AR technology is used to recognize patterns or features in an environment that were not previously provided to the application, enabling, for instance, a consumer to scan a real world environment like a tabletop or a room in their apartment using their smartphone camera and virtually place a product there to see how it would look.

name: Mark IN
desc: To select the first frame of a clip.

name: Mark IN/OUT
desc: a) The process of entering the start and end time codes for a clip to be edited into a sequence. b) The process of marking or logging timecode numbers to define clips during a logging, recording or digitizing session. See also IN Point, OUT Point.

name: Mark OUT
desc: To select the last frame of a clip.

name: Maroon
desc: An Electrically Fired Thunderflash, Set Off In A Steel Tank Fitted With A Wire Mesh Top, To Simulate An Explosion. These Are So Loud That It Is As Well To Inform The Police In Advance If One Is To Be Set Off (And At What Time), So That They Can Be Ready To Deal With Hundeds Of Reports Of Bombs Going Off!

name: Mask
desc: To Hide: An Actors Masks Another When He Stands In Front Of Him And Prevents The Audience From Seeing Him Properly. Also A Noun: Fabric Hiding A Row Of Lanterns Hung Above The Stage.

name: Masking
desc: Masking is one way of partial compensation for photo-receptor dot sensitivity, non-optimum color filters, non-ideal display phosphors, unwanted dye absorption. Audio: The phenomenon by which loud sounds prevent the ear from hearing softer sounds of similar frequency. The process of blocking out portions of a picture area/signal. A psychoacoustic phenomenon whereby certain sounds cannot be heard in the presence of others. Video: A process to alter color rendition in which the appropriate color signals are used to modify each other. Note: The process is usually accomplished by suitable cross coupling between primary color-signal channels. Photography: Comparable control of color rendition is accom- plished by the simultaneous optimization of image dyes, masking dyes,  and spectral sensitivities.

name: Masking Threshold
desc: A measure of a function below which an audio signal cannot be perceived by the human auditory system.

name: Mask Key
desc: A key that is selectively limited in what portions of the key source will be allowed to cut the hole. Masks are usually square, however, on Ampex switchers mask keys are done by utilizing the pattern system with any pattern shape on the switcher. See Preset Pattern.

name: mask( or blackout )
desc: refers to covering up or blocking out a portion of the framewith blackness or opaqueness ; most masks are black , butthey could be white or some other color

name: Mask Programmed
desc: An IC that is programmed by generating a unique photomask used in the fabrication of the IC.

name: Mass Law
