name: Moto DV Playback
desc: An edit mode in Premiere, specifically for Moto DV studio users, that allows video to be streamed out of a Moto DV captured card.

name: Motorized fader
desc: A fader with a motor that automatically repositions itself when recalling scenes/snapshots, enabling fully automated mix recall on digital consoles. Standard on most professional digital mixing consoles.

name: Motorized Fly System
desc: A fly system using electric motors to raise and lower battens, enabling precise remote-controlled movement. See Fly System.

name: Motorized Rigging
desc: A Theatrical Rigging System Using Powered Winches And Other Devices To Move Equipment Rather Than Muscle Power.

name: Motor (Primary) Brake
desc: A Brake That Is Mounted At The Motor. It Has A Low Torque Capacity And Fast Response.Used For Normal Stopping And Holding Duty On A Motorized Hoist.

name: Motor (rigging)
desc: An electric chain hoist used in entertainment rigging. Controlled via a hoist controller, motor bars can lift and position truss, scenic pieces, and lighting rigs with precision.

name: Mount
desc: To make a file system that is stored on a local or remote disk resource accessible from a specific directory on a workstation.

name: Mounting Height
desc: The Distance From The Floor To The Lamp Center Of The Luminaire Or To The Plane Of The Ceiling For Recessed Equipment. Motion Detectors Also Have A Recommended Mounting Height.

name: Mount Point
desc: The directory on a workstation from which you access information that is stored on a local or remote disk resource.

name: Mouse
desc: To Wrap The End Of A Rope, Cable Or Turnbuckle To Prevent It From Unwinding.

name: Mouse ( House )
desc: a slang term for the Walt Disney Co. or any division thereof --refers to the company's most famous animated character :Mickey Mouse

name: Mouse Pad
desc: For an optical mouse, this is the rectangular, metallic surface that reads the movements of the mouse. For a mechanical mouse, this is a clean, soft rectangular surface that makes the mouse’s track ball roll efficiently.

name: Mouse (rigging)
desc: To wrap the end of a rope, cable, or turnbuckle to prevent it from unwinding — usually with bailing wire. A safety precaution in rigging.

name: MOV
desc: The File Extension Used By Moov Format Video Files On Windows. These Mov Files Are Generated With Apple Computer'S Quicktime And Played On Windows Systems Via Quicktime For Windows.

name: Move in Dark (MID)
desc: Avolites Titan's term for automatically pre-positioning fixtures while they are at zero intensity before they are needed in the next cue. Called 'Mark' on ETC Eos and other consoles.

name: Move instruction
desc: In Eos tracking, a move instruction is a parameter value that is explicitly recorded into a cue and represents a change from the previous cue. Move instructions are color-coded in the PSD and form the basis of the Eos tracking engine.

name: Movie-2 Bus (or Movie-2 Bus Connector)
desc: Over the top connector used for high-speed data transfer. These two terms refer to the assembled component, which consists of a printed circuit board (backplane) with attached connectors.

name: Moving-Coil
desc: A microphone whose generating element is a coil which moves within a magnetic gap in response to sound pressure on the diaphragm attached to it, rather like a small loudspeaker in reverse. The most common type of Dynamic Microphone.

name: Moving Dots
desc: See Chroma Crawl.

name: Moving head
desc: Also called an intelligent fixture or automated luminaire. A motorized fixture capable of pan, tilt, color, gobo, zoom, and other functions controlled via DMX. Available in spot, wash, and beam varieties.

name: Moving Light
desc: See The Definition For Automated Light.

name: Moving Picture Experts Group 1 (MPEG-1)
desc: ISO/IEC CD 11172 is the first of the standards designed for handling highly compressed moving images in real-time. It accepts periodically chosen frames to be com- pressed as in JPEG-1, predicts the content of intervening frames, and encodes only the difference between the actual and the prediction. Audio is compressed synchronously. The encoder includes a decoder section in order to generate and verify the predictions. At the display, a much simpler decoder becomes possible. MPEG-1 is optimized for a data rate of up

name: Moving Picture Experts Group 2 (MPEG-2)
desc: MPEG-2 expands the MPEG-1 standard to cover a wider range of applications.

name: Moving Picture Experts Group 3 (MPEG-3)
desc: MPEG 3 was originally intended for HDTV applications but has since been incorporated into MPEG 2.

name: Moving Picture Experts Group 4 (MPEG-4)
desc: The goal of MPEG-4 is to establish a universal and efficient coding for different forms of audio-visual data, called audio-visual objects. Coding tools for audio-visual objects are being developed to support various functionality’s, such as object-based interactivity and scalability. The syntax of the audio-visual objects is being developed to allow for description of coded objects and to describe how they were coded. This information can then be downloaded into a decoder.

name: Moving Picture Experts Group (MPEG)
desc: An international group of industry ex-perts set up to standardize compressed moving pictures and audio. The first release of the MPEG standard was called MPEG-1 (ISO/IEC 11172-1).

name: .Mp3
desc: This Is A Common File Format For Audio That Uses Compress To Scale The File Size

name: MP4
desc: A popular media container format capable of carrying the H.264 codec for high-quality video at low bitrates.

name: .Mp4 (H.264)
desc: Newer Video/Audio Format With Better Compression So It Has Higher Quality In A Smaller File Size

name: MPAA
desc: acronym - initials meaning ' Motion Picture Association ofAmerica ' - an organization that represents the interests of themajor motion picture studios

name: MPCD (Minimum Perceptible Color Difference)
desc: This is a unit of measure, developed by the CIE, to define the change in light and color required to be just noticeable to the human eye. The human being in this MPCD unit is defined as “a trained observer” because there are differences in the way each of us perceive light.

name: MPC (Multimedia PC)
desc: A specification developed by the Multimedia Council. It defines the minimum platform capable of running multimedia software. PCs carrying the MPC logo will be able to run any software that also displays the MPC logo.

name: MPE
desc: See Multiprotocol Encapsulation.

name: MPEG
desc: Compression Standards For Moving Images Conceived By The Motion Pictures Expert Group, An International Group Of Industry Experts Set Up To Standardize Compressed Moving Pictures And Audio. Mpeg-2 Is The Basis For Atsc Digital Television Transmission. Its Work Follows On From That Of Jpeg To Add Interfield Compression, The Extra Compression Potentially Available Through Similarities Between Successive Frames Of Moving Pictures. Four Mpeg Standards Were Originally Planned, But The Accommodation Of Hdtv Within Mpeg-2 Has Meant That Mpeg-3 Is Now Redundant. Mpeg-4 Is Intended For Unrelated Applications, However, Can Be Used To Display Atsc Formats On A Pc. The Main Interest For The Television Industry Is In Mpeg-1 And Mpeg-2. A Group Of Picture Blocks, Usually Four, Which Are Analyzed During Mpeg Coding To Give An Estimate Of The Movement Between Frames. This Generates The Motion Vectors That Are Then Used To Place The Macroblocks In Decoded Pictures. See Also: B Frames, Gop, I Frames, P Frames.

name: MPEG-1
desc: A Group Of Picture Blocks, Usually Four, Which Are Analyzed During Mpeg Coding To Give An Estimate Of The Movement Between Frames. This Generates The Motion Vectors That Are Then Used To Place The Macroblocks In Decoded Pictures. This Was Designed To Work At 1.2 Mbps, The Data Rate Of Cd-Rom, So That Video Could Be Played From Cds. However The Quality Is Not Sufficient For Tv Broadcast. A Recent Addition To Mpeg-2 Is The Studio Profile. Designed For Studio Work Its Sampling Is 4:2:2. The Studio Profile Is Written As 422P@Ml. To Improve The Picture Quality, Higher Bit Rates Are Used. The First Applications For This Appear To Be In Electronic News Gathering (Eng), And With Some Video Servers. See Also: B Frames, Compression, Gop, I Frames, Jpeg, P Frames.

name: MPEG-2
desc: This Has Been Designed To Cover A Wide Range Of Requirements From "Vhs Quality" All The Way To Hdtv Through A Series Of Algorithm "Profiles" And Image Resolution "Levels." With Data Rates Of Between 1.2 And 15 Mbps, There Is Intense Interest In The Use Of Mpeg-2 For The Digital Transmission Of Television--Including Hdtv--Applications For Which The System Was Conceived. Coding The Video Is Very Complex, Especially As It Is Required To Keep The Decoding At The Reception End As Simple And Inexpensive As Possible. Mpeg-2 Is The Compression Used By The Atsc And Dvb Standards.Mpeg Can Offer Better Quality Pictures At High Compression Ratios Than Pure Jpeg Compression, But With The Complexity Of Decoding And Especially Coding And The 12-Long Group Of Pictures (Gop), It Is Not An Ideal Compression System For Editing. If Any P Or B Frames Are Used Then Even A Cut Will Require The Re-Use Of Complex, And Not Perfect, Mpeg Coding. However, Mpeg Splicers Are Beginning To Appear To Alleviate This Difficulty.Of The Six Profiles And Four Levels Creating A Grid Of 24 Possible Combinations, 12 Have Already Been Implemented. The Variations These Define Are So Wide That It Would Not Be Practical To Build A Universal Coder Or Decoder. Interest Is Now Focused On The Main Profile, Main Level, Sometimes Written As Mp@Ml, Which Covers Broadcast Television Formats Up To 720 Pixels X 576 Lines At 30 Frames Per Second. These Figures Are Quoted As Maximums So 720 X 486 At 30 Frames Are Included, As Are 720 X 576 At 25 Frames. As The Coding Is Intended For Transmission The Economy Of  4:2:0 Sampling Is Used.

name: MPEG-21
desc: The Motion Picture Experts Group'S Attempt To Get A Handle On The Overall Topic Of Content Delivery. By Defining A Multimedia Framework From The Viewpoint Of The Consumer, They Hope To Understand How Various Components Relate To Each Other And Where Gaps In The Infrastructure Might Benefit From New Standards. A Technical Report On The Mpeg-21 Framework Is Scheduled For Mid-2000.

name: MPEG-4
desc: The Third Standard Developed By Mpeg. Started In July 1993 Mpeg-4 Has Benefited From The Huge R&D Investments Made By Participating Companies And Provides A Harmonized Range Of Responses To The Diverse Needs Of The Digital Audio-Visual Industry, Including Compatibility With Other Major Standards Such As H.263 And Vrml.

name: MPEG 4:2:2
desc: Also referred to as Studio MPEG, Professional MPEG and 442P@ML. Sony’s Betacam SX is based on MPEG 4:2:2.

name: MPEG-4 Class
desc: MPEG-4 standardizes a number of pre-defined classes. This set of classes is called the MPEG-4 Standard Class Library. The root  of MPEG-4 classes is called MPEG-4 Object. In Flexible Mode, an MPEG-4 Terminal, based on this library, will be able to produce or use new encoder- defined classes and instantiate objects according to these class definitions. Graphical methods to represent this hierarchy are commonly used. the  OMT notation has been chosen within the context of MPEG-4 Systems.

name: MPEG-4 Object
desc: The root of MPEG-4 classes.

name: MPEG-4 Systems
desc: The “Systems” part of the MPEG-4 standard in charge of the Multiplex Layer, the Composition Layer and the Flexibility Layer.

name: MPEG-4 Systems Description Language (MSDL)
desc: The language(s) defined by MPEG-4 Systems for the purpose of the Flexibility Layer.

name: MPEG-4 Terminal
desc: An MPEG-4 Terminal is a system that allows presentation of an interactive audiovisual scene from coded audiovisual information. It can be either a standalone application, or part of a multime- dia terminal that needs to deal with MPEG-4 coded audiovisual information, among others.

name: MPEG-7
desc: MPEG-7 is a multimedia content (images, graphics, 3D models, audio, speech, video) representation standard for information searching.

name: MPEG Audio
desc: Audio compressed according to the MPEG perceptual encoding system. MPEG-1 audio provides two channels, which can be in Dolby Surround format. MPEG-2 audio adds data to provide discrete multi- channel audio. Stereo MPEG audio is the mandatory audio compression system for 625/50 (PAL/SECAM) DVD-Video.

name: MPEG IMX
desc: Sony'S Trademark For A Family Of Devices, Such As Dvtrs, That Are I Frame-Only 50 Mbps Mpeg-2 Streams Using Betacam Style Cassettes. Plays Digital Betacam, Betacam Sx, Betacam Sp, Betacam, And, Mpeg Imx, Outputting 50 Mbps Mpeg I-Frame On Sdti-Cp Regardless Of The Tape Being Played. It Can Also Handle Other (Lower) Input And Output Data Rates, But The Recordings Are 50 Mbps I-Frame In Any Case.

name: MPEG-J
desc: A set of Java application program interfaces. It also sets the rules for delivering Java into a bitstream and it specifies what happens at the receiving end.

name: MPEG splicing
desc: The Ability To Cut Into An Mpeg Bit Stream For Switching And Editing, Regardless Of Type Of Frames (I, B, P).

name: MPEG TS (MPEG Transport Stream)
desc: The MPEG transport stream is an extremely complex structure using interlinked tables and coded identifiers to separate the programs and the elementary streams within the programs. Within each elementary stream, there is a complex structure, allowing a decoder to distinguish between, for example, vectors, coefficients and quantization tables.

name: MPEG Video
desc: Video compressed according to the MPEG encoding system. MPEG-1 is typically used for low data rate video such as on a Video CD. MPEG-2 is used for higher-quality video, especially interlaced video, such as on DVD or HDTV.

name: MP@HL
desc: Main Profile At High Level For Hdtv And Is Implemented In Dvb And Atsc Systems With Bitstreams Running Up To 19.4 Mb/S.

name: MP@HL (Main Profile at High Level)
desc: Widely used shorthand notation for a specific quality and resolution of MPEG: Main Profile (4:2:0 quality), High Level (HD resolution).

name: MPLS
desc: Multiprotocol Label Switching. A high-performance telecommunications mechanism directing data between network nodes by creating virtual links between distant nodes.

name: MP@ML
desc: Main Profile At Main Level Covers Broadcast Television Formats Up To 720 Pixels X 576 Lines And 30 Fps So Includes 720 X 486 At 30 Fps And 720 X 576 At 25 Fps. The Economy Of  4:2:0 Sampling Is Used And Bit Rates Vary From As Low As 2 Mb/S On Multiplexed Transmissions, Up To 9 Mb/S On Dvd-Video.

name: MP@ML (Main Profile at Main Level)
desc: MPEG-2 specifies different degrees of compression vs. quality. Of these, Main Profile at Main Level is the most commonly used.

name: MPP (Mix to Preset Pattern)
desc: See Preset Pattern.

name: MPU (Microprocessing Unit)
desc: See Microprocessor.

name: MR
desc: Mr Removes The Boundaries Between Real And Virtual Interaction - Like A Virtual Robot Scurrying Under Your Coffee Table Or Planning A New Piece Of Furniture In An Existing Room Of Your House.

name: Mr-Lamp
desc: A Multi-Faceted Reflector Lamp.

name: Mr Sands
desc: Theatrical Code To Warn Theatre Employees Of A Fire Without Frightening The Audience. "Mr Sands Is In The Foyer" Means That Fire Has Broken Out In The Foyer.

name: MSB
desc: Most Significant Bit. The Bit That Has The Most Value In A Binary Number Or Data Byte. In Written Form, This Would Be The Bit On The Left. For Example: Binary 1110 = Decimal 14 In This Example, The Left-Most Binary Digit, 1, Is The Most Significant Bit--Here Representing 8. If The Msb In This Example Were Corrupt, The Decimal Would Not Be 14 But 6. See Also: Lsb.

name: MSDL (MPEG-4 Syntactic or Systems Description Language)
desc: An extensible description language defined in MPEG-4 that allows for selection, description and downloading of tools, algorithms and profiles.

name: MSE
desc: Mean Squared Error. The per-channel mean square of the residual error for a modulated signal.

name: MSI (Medium Scale Integration)
desc: Between 100 and 3,000 transistors on a chip.

name: MSO (Multiple System Operator)
desc: A major cable TV organization that has franchises in multiple locations.

name: MS Stereo
desc: Exploitation of stereo redundancy in audio programs based on coding the sum and difference signal instead of the left and right channels.

name: MST
desc: Multi-Stream Transport. A DisplayPort transport mode allowing daisy-chaining of monitors to support multiple downstream display devices.

name: MTBF
desc: Mean Time Between Failures. A basic reliability measure describing the number of hours before a component or system fails.

name: MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure)
desc: The average time a component works without failure. It is the number of failures divided by the hours under observation.channel. One channel provides stereo sound by providing left/right channel difference signals relative to transmitted mono audio track. The second carrier carries the Secondary Audio Program (SAP) which is used for a second language or a descriptive commentary for the blind. Uses a technique based on the dBx noise reduction to improve the fre- quency response of the audio channel.

name: MTP
desc: Multiple Termination Plug. A small form factor plug for multiple fibers. Also Extron's acronym for the Mini Twisted Pair product line.

name: MTR Touchscreen Console
desc: Microsoft's term for a Microsoft Teams Rooms controller (IP or USB enabled).

name: MTS
desc: Multichannel Television Sound. One of the first stereo TV sound systems. Provides left/right stereo plus a Secondary Audio Program (SAP) channel for a second language or descriptive video.

name: MTTR (Mean Time to Repair)
desc: The average time it takes to repair a failed component.

name: MTU
desc: Maximum Transfer Unit. The maximum size for an Ethernet frame payload, typically 1500 bytes. Routers break up larger segments to fit within the MTU.

name: MTV style editing
desc: refers to the style of filming and editing first found on the MTVcable channel in the 1980s and its music videos , consisting ofrapidly - cut shots , fast - paced action , jump - cuts , fast - edits ,numerous camera angles

name: Muddy
desc: A Descriptor For An Audio Signal That Has An Overabundance Of Low Or Low-Mid Frequency Information, Usually ~100-400Hz. This Can Often Be Rectified With Eq.

name: Mu-Law
desc: The PCM coding and companding standard for digital voice communications that is used in North America and Japan for analog-to- digital conversion.

name: Mule Block
desc: A Pulley That Supports And Changes The Direction Of One Or More Cables Traveling Between Loft Blocks And Head Block.

name: Mule Winch
desc: See “Capstan Winch.”

name: Mullion
desc: The physical bezel surrounding a display screen. In videowalls, stacked displays create a crisscross pattern of mullion space between active screen areas.

name: Mult
desc: Short For Multiple. In Audio Work, A Mult Is A Parallel Connection (In A Patch Bay Or With Spe_x0002_Cially Built Cables Or Wiring) Used To Feed An Output To More Than One Input. A “Y” Cable Is A Type Of Mult Connection. Also Used A Verb, As In “Why Did You Mult The Flanger Into Every Input In The Board?”

name: Multiangle
desc: A DVD-video program containing multiple angles allowing different views of a scene to be selected during playback.

name: Multiband compressor
desc: A compressor that splits the audio signal into two or more frequency bands and compresses each independently. Offers much more precise dynamic control than a broadband compressor.

name: Multiburst
desc: Useful for quick approximations of the system’s frequency response and can be used as an in-service VIT signal. The multiburst waveform is shown in the figure below.

name: Multi Cable
desc: An Abridged Version Of Multiconductor Cable.

name: Multicamera
desc: A production or scene that is shot and recorded from more than one camera simultaneously.

name: Multicast
desc: 1. Data Flow From Single Source To Multiple Destinations; A Multicast May Be Distinguished From A Broadcast In That Number Of Destinations May Be Limited. 2. A Term Often Used Incorrectly To Describe Digital Television Program Multiplexing.

name: Multicast DNS
desc: A protocol resolving hostnames to IP addresses in small networks without a local name server. Related to Apple's Bonjour zero-configuration networking for AirPlay discovery.

name: Multichannel
desc: Multiple channels of audio, usually containing different signals for different speakers in order to create a surround-sound effect.

name: Multiconductor Cable
desc: An Electrical Cable That Generally Has More Than Three Conductors.

name: Multiconnector
desc: A Connector That Generally Has More Than Three Contacts.

name: Multicore
desc: A Cable That Can Transfer More Than One Analog Audio Signal At A Time, Also Called A Snake.

name: MultiCrypt
desc: Is used to describe the simultaneous operation of several conditional access systems.

name: Multi-Faceted Reflector Lamp (Mr-Lamp}
desc: A Low- Voltage Halogen Reflector Lamp That Is Used In Lighting Applications Where Precise Beam Control Is Required, Such As Accent Lighting. Some Mr- Lamps, Such As Projection Lamps, Are Designed For Line-Voltage Operation.

name: Multifrequency Monitor
desc: A monitor that accommodates a variety of horizontal and vertical synchronization frequencies. This monitor type accepts inputs from many different display adapters, and is typically capable of either analog or digital input.

name: Multi-Language Support
desc: A DVD has the ability to store 8 audio streams. This is different than the number of channels each stream might have. Thus, each of the streams might contain a multi-channel audio program in a separate language.

name: Multi-Line Block
desc: Any Block That Can Support More Than One Line. See “Incremental Block” And “Multi-Sheave Block”.

name: Multilingual
desc: A presentation of dialog in more than one language.

name: Multimedia
desc: A somewhat ambiguous term that describes the ability to combine audio, video and other information with graphics, control, storage and other features of computer-based systems. Applications

name: Multimedia Computing
desc: Refers to the delivery of multimedia information delivered via computers.

name: Multimedia content description interface
desc: See:Mpeg-7.

name: Multimedia Hypermedia Expert Group (MHEG)
desc: MHEG is another working group under the same ISO/IEC subcommittee that feature the MPEG. The MHEG is the Working Group 12 (WG 12) of Subcommittee 29 (SC 29) of the joint ISO and IEC Technical Committee 1 (JTC 1). The ISO/IEC standards produced have number 13522. MHEG targets coding of multimedia and hypermedia information, and defines an interchange format for composite multimedia contents. The defined MHEG format encapsulates a multimedia document, so to speak, as communication takes place in a specific data structure. Despite the talk about multimedia, there is no very much said and written about MHEG, which seems odd given the realm of MHEG. The present market significance of MHEG is very low, probably due to the high number of proprietary standards for audio visual representation in multimedia PC environments.

name: Multimode fiber
desc: Optical fiber with larger core (50μm or 62.5μm) supporting multiple light propagation modes. Distances: up to 300m (OM3) or 550m (OM4) at 10 Gbps. Lower cost than single-mode. Used for intra-building AV.

name: Multimode Fiber (MMF)
desc: An optical fiber allowing propagation of more than one mode or light path. Commonly used with LED sources for shorter distance links.

name: Multipart cue
desc: An Eos cue containing multiple independently timed parts (Part 1, Part 2, etc.) that execute simultaneously. Allows different channels or parameter types within the same cue to have different fade times.

name: Multipass Encoding
desc: True multipass encoding is currently available only for WM8 and MPEG-2. An encoder supporting multipass will, in a first pass, analyze the video stream to be encoded and write down a log about everything it encounters. Let’s assume there is a short clip that starts out in a dialog scene where there are few cuts and the camera remains static. Then it leads over to a karate fight with lots of fast cuts and a lot of action (people flying through the air, kicking, punching, etc.). In regular CBR, encoding every second gets more or less bitrate (it is hard to stay 100% CBR) whereas in multipass VBR mode the encoder will use the bitrate according to its knowledge about the video stream, i.e. the dialog part gets the available bitrate and the fighting scene gets allotted more bitrate. The more passes, the more refined the bitrate distribution will be. In single pass VBR, the encoder has to base its knowledge on what it previously has encoded.

name: Multi-Pass Transform
desc: A compression process returning to a data set multiple times. Supports greater compression ratios but requires more processing time.

name: Multipath
desc: In Wireless Systems, It Is Interference Due To Multiple Arrivals Of The Same Rf Signal Due To Reflections Off Nearby Objects. The Difference In Path Lengths Creates Different Arrival Times, Thus Causing Signal Cancellation And Degradation.

name: Multipath Distortion
desc: A form of interference caused by signal reflec- tions. Signals that are reflected more take a longer path to reach the receiver than those that are reflected less. The receiver will synchronize to the strongest signal, with the weaker signals traveling via different paths causing ghostly images superimposed on the main image. Since many ATV schemes offer increased horizontal resolution, ghosts can have a more deleterious effect on them than on ordinary NTSC signals. There have been many demonstrations of ghost canceling/ eliminating systems and robust transmission systems over the years. It is probable that these will have to be used for HDTV.

name: Multipath interference
desc: The Signal Variation Caused When Two Rf Signals Take Multiple Paths From Transmitter To Receiver. In Analog Television, This Creates Ghosting. In Digital Television, This Can Cause The Receiver Not To Output A Signal As It Can Not Differentiate Between Signals.

name: Multi-Pin -
desc: A Connector That Has Multiple Connection Points, Also Called A Mult

name: Multiplane Animation
desc: Multiplane animation refers to a type of cel animation where individual cels are superimposed using the painters algorithm, and their motion relative to each other is controlled. Here, the word “plane” and cel are interchangeable.

name: Multiple Blanking Lines
desc: Evidenced by a thickening of the blanking line trace or by several distinct blanking lines as viewed on an oscilloscope.

name: Multiple B-Roll
desc: A duplicate of the original source tape, created so that overlays can be merged onto one source tape.

name: Multiple-FIFO Architecture
desc: A display controller architecture character- ized by having multiple FIFOs or write buffers. There is typically one FIFO or write buffer at the CPU interface, and one or more FIFOs in the display pipeline.

name: Multiple-Level Switching
desc: A Switching  Technique Wherein The Individual Lamps, Or Groups Of Lamps, In A Luminaire Are Switched Independently  To Achieve Multiple Light Outputs.For Example, An Architectural Luminaire That Contains Two Rows Of Lamps May Have Each Row Controlled By A Separate Switch, So That The Two Rows Of Lamps May Be Turned On And Off Independently.

name: Multiple System Operator (MSO)
desc: A cable TV service provider that operates more than one cable television system.

name: Multiple Termination Plug (MTP)
desc: A small form factor plug for multiple fibers.

name: Multiplex
desc: 1. To Transmit Two Or More Signals At The Same Time Or On The Same Carrier Frequency. 2. To Combine Two Or More Electrical Signals Into A Single, Composite Signal, Such As Atsc Multicasting.

name: Multiplex Code Field (MC Field)
desc: A field in the TransMux/FlexMux-PDU header which specifies, by reference to a Multiplex Table Entry, the logical channel where each byte in the information field belongs.

name: Multiplexed Analog Component
desc: See MAC.

name: Multiplexer
desc: Device For Combining Two Or More Electrical Signals Into A Single, Composite Signal.

name: Multiplexer (MUX)
desc: Device for combining two or more electrical signals into a single, composite signal.

name: Multiplexing
desc: Process of transmitting more than one signal via a single link. The most common technique used in microprocessor systems is  time division multiplexing, in which one signal line is used for different information at different times.

name: Multiplex Layer (MUX Layer)
desc: In its broad sense, the combination

name: Multiplex Layer Protocol Data Unit (MUX-PDU)
desc: An information unit exchanged between peer Multiplex Layer entities.

name: Multiplex Layer Service Data Unit (MUX-SDU)
desc: A logical information unit whose integrity is preserved in transfer from one Multiplex Layer User to the peer Multiplex Layer User.

name: Multiplex Layer User (MUX-User)
desc: An entity which makes use of the services of the MUX Layer.

name: Multiplex Table
desc: A table which specifies the multiplexing pattern for the information field of a MUX-PDU).

name: Multiplier
desc: A control circuit in which a non-video control signal is faded down as the other is faded up.

name: Multipoint
desc: A Term Used By Network Designers To Describe Network Links That Have Many Possible Endpoints.

name: Multipoint Conferencing Server (MCS)
desc: A hardware or software H.323 device that allows multiple video conferencing (or audio or data) users to connect together. Without an MCS typically only point-to-point conferences can take place. Commonly supports voice activated switching, where whoever is talking is broadcast to all users, but new systems support “Hollywood Squares”, where multiple windows show each participant. ITU-T standard H.231 describes the standard way of doing this. Many current systems only support H.320 (ISDN) but many vendors are working to upgrade their products to support H.323 (LAN, Internet) as well. In the

name: Multipoint Controller (MC)
desc: Used for conference control of three or more terminals. It allocates bandwidth.

name: Multipoint Control Unit (MCU)
desc: A switching device commonly used to switch and control a video conferencing network allowing multiple sites to conference simultaneously.

name: Multiprotocol Encapsulation (MPE)
desc: The data broadcast specification profile for multiprotocol encapsulation supports data broadcast services that require the transmission of datagrams of communication protocols via DVB compliant broadcast networks. The transmission of datagrams accord- ing to the multiprotocol encapsulation specification is done by encapsulat- ing the datagrams in DSM-CC sections., which are compliant with the MPEG-2 private sector format.

name: Multi-Rate SDI
desc: Capability to support multiple SMPTE SDI standards including SMPTE 424M (3G-SDI at 2.97 Gbps), SMPTE 292M (HD-SDI at 1.485 Gbps), and SMPTE 259M (SDI at 270 Mbps).

name: Multi-Scan Monitor
desc: A monitor (also referred to as multi-sync or

name: Multi-Sheave Block
desc: A Block With More Than One Sheave, Each Of Which Can Support And Change The Direction Of A Rope Or Cable. Sheaves Can Be Held By A Common Shaft Or By Multiple Shafts Within A Common Housing.

name: Multistream
desc: The ability to simultaneously stream to multiple platforms or destinations from a single encoder. Also called simultaneous multi-destination streaming or simulcasting.

name: Munsell Chroma
desc: a) Illuminating Engineering: The index of perceived (Y)

name: MUSE-6
desc: A family of three versions of an ATV transmission scheme said to be both receiver-compatible and channel-compatible. Since the original MUSE schemes are neither, there is little similarity between them, other than the use of sub-sampling. The differences between the three versions relate to how the wide aspect ratio is handled and what techniques are used for augmentation in an ATV set. Two versions of MUSE-6 use the letterbox technique for aspect ratio accommodation and both of these use blanking stuffing in the expanded VBI area for vertical resolution enhance- ment. The differences between the two versions relate to the duration of the sub-sampling sequence (one frame or two). The third uses the trunca- tion technique for aspect ratio accommodation, sending the side panels stuffed into the existing VBI and HBI. Additional horizontal detail is trans- mitted via two-frame sub-sampling.

name: MUSE-9
desc: A family of three versions of an ATV transmission scheme said to be receiver-compatible and utilizing a 3 MHz augmentation channel.

name: MUSE-E
desc: MUSE optimized for emission (i.e., broadcasting) rather than transmission (i.e., satellite distribution). It is a non-receiver-compatible, non-channel-compatible scheme occupying 8.1 MHz of base bandwidth and requiring four fields to build up a full-resolution picture. Thus, it requires motion compensation (and retains some motion artifacts). It offers four channels of high-quality digital audio. It has been tested in the Washington, DC area.

name: MUSE-II
desc: See MUSE-E.

name: MUSE (Multiple Sub-Nyquist Sampling Encoding)
desc: a) 16:9 aspect ratio, high definition, widescreen television being proposed in Japan.

name: MUSE-T
desc: MUSE optimized for transmission (via satellite) rather than emission (via terrestrial broadcasting). It occupies twice the bandwidth of MUSE-E (16.2 MHz), but is otherwise quite similar.

name: Musical Director
desc: (Md) In Complete Control Of The Music In The Production, Under The Overall Control Of The Director. Rehearses The Singers And Musicians, Conducts The Orchestra Or Band, And Usually Arranges The Music Too.

name: musical ( film )
desc: a major film genre category denoting a film that emphasizessegments of song and dance interspersed within the actionand dialogue ; known for its distinctive artists , stars , singers ,and dancers ; two major types are ' backstage ' musicals and' music - integrated ' musicals .

name: MUSICAM (Masking Pattern Adapted Universal Sub-Band Integrated Coding and Multiplexing)
desc: Compression method for audio coding.

name: Music and Effects Track(s)
desc: Music and effects audio without video. Can be on one track, on different tracks on one piece of film or tape, or on different tapes, which are combined during an audio “track mix” session. Sometimes abbreviated M&E.

name: Muslin
desc: A Material Used For Fabric Scrims Used In The Theater, Film, And Video Industries.

name: Muslin (scenic)
desc: A solid, non-transparent fabric used as a backdrop that can be painted and lit with colored lights. Available seamed or seamless.

name: Must Carry
desc: Legal requirement that cable operators carry local broadcast signals. Cable systems with 12 or fewer channels must carry at least three broadcast signals; systems with 12 or more channels must carry up to

name: mute
desc: a print with only the picture image ( minus the sound track )

name: Mute group
desc: A function that allows multiple channels to be muted and unmuted simultaneously with a single button press. Essential for live sound to quickly silence a drum kit, backing vocals, or any group of channels between songs.

name: Muting
desc: To Greatly Decrease The Volume Level. Many Receivers And Pre-Amplifiers Have A Muting Control Which Allows The Volume Level To Be Cut Way Down Without Changing The Master Volume Control. Great For When The Phone Rings.

name: Mux
desc: See: Multiplex.

name: Mux Rate
desc: Defined by MPEG-2 as the combined rate of all video and audio elementary stream packets common to one program or multi-pro- gram stream. The rate of a stream is set based upon a user selection, by the quality of the program (i.e., constant quality variable rate), or by the symbol rate required from an RF transponder. This rate also includes the VBI and sub-picture private stream data, which MPEG treats as a private stream type. Mux rate is always specified as 10.08 Mbps because this is the rate at which user data arrives into the track buffer.

name: Mvn Animate
desc: Xsens’S Motion Capture Software Which Exports All The Real-Time Tracking Data From Its Sensors To A Users’ 3D Package. Mvn Animate Offers Real-Time 3D Animation, Graphs, Data Streaming And Video.

name: MVR
desc: My Virtual Rig. An open file format for exchanging lighting rig data between lighting design software, visualization tools, and consoles. Accompanies GDTF to describe the physical layout of a rig.

name: MXF
desc: The Material Exchange Format Is Aimed At The Exchange Of Program Material Between File Servers, Tape Streamers And Digital Archives. It Usually Contains One Complete Sequence But This May Comprise A Sequence Of Clips And Program Segments. There Are Six Operational Patterns: Simple, Compiled, Compound, Uncompiled Simple, Uncompiled Compound And Metadata-Only. As Mxf Is Derived From The Aaf Data Model It Integrates Closely With Aaf Files As Well As Stream Formats. Bridging File And Streaming Transfers, Mxf Helps Move Material Between Aaf File-Based Post Production And Streaming Program Replay Using Standard Networks. This Set Up Extends The Reliable Essence And Metadata Pathways Of Both Formats To Reach From Content Creation To Playout. The Mxf Body Carries The Content. It Can Include Compressed Formats Such As Mpeg And Dv As Well As Uncompressed Video And Contains An Interleaved Sequence Of Picture Frames, Each With Audio And Data Essence Plus Frame-Based Metadata. Mxf Has Been Submitted To The Smpte As A Proposed Standard.

name: MXF DMS-1
desc: The MXF development community has been working on a specific dialect for Descriptive Metadata, called MXF DMS-1, which is being designed to describe people, places, times, production billing.

name: MXF (Material Exchange Format)
desc: An object subset of AAF and is on the verge of becoming a SMPTE standard. MXF was designed for less complex (less vertically rich) metadata applications, such as news editing and video streaming from servers. Because of its flatter metadata struc- ture, it is better suited to be used as a metadata wrapper within a video signal or a TCP/IP stream. It offers performance benefits over the more complex AAF file structure because of its streamable nature.

name: Mylar
desc: A registered trademark of E.I. duPont de Nemours & Co., designating their polyester film.

name: Nab
desc: National Association Of Broadcasters.

name: NAB Curves, NAB Equalization
desc: Standard playback equalization curves for various tape speeds, developed by the National Association of Broadcasters.

name: NABET (National Association of Broadcast Employees and Technicians)
desc: NABET is a union of technicians that supplies members for many videotape, live and film productions.

name: NAB (National Association of Broadcasters)
desc: An association which has standardized the equalization used in recording and reproducing. This is a station owner and/or operator’s trade association. NAB is also a partic- ipant in ATV testing and standardization work, and a charter member of ATSC. Though not a proponent of any particular ATV system, NAB lobbies for the interests of broadcasting as a delivery mechanism and has pub- lished some of the least biased information on the subject.

name: NAB Reel, NAB Hub
desc: Reels and hubs used in professional recording, having a large center hole and usually an outer diameter of 10-1/2”.

name: NABTS
desc: See North American Broadcast Teletext Specification.

name: Nagra
desc: A brand of audio tape recorder using 1/4” wide audio tape extensively used for studio and location separate audio recording.

name: NAM
desc: See Non-Additive Mix.

name: Nanometer (nm)
desc: A nanometer, or one billionth (10⁻⁹) of a meter.

name: Nanosecond
desc: One billionth of a second: 1 x 10-9 or 0.000000001 second.

name: NAP (North American Philips)
desc: Philips Laboratories developed the HDS-NA ATV scheme and was among the first to suggest advanced pre-combing. See also PCEC.

name: narration
desc: the telling of a story , and the supplemental information givento the film audience by an off - screen voice ; sometimes thenarrator is a character in the film , who provides information ina flashback ; see also voice - over .

name: narrative film
desc: a structured series of events , linked by cause and effect , thatprovide the plot of a film ; a film that tells a chronological orlinear story ( with a beginning , middle , and end ) , as opposedto non - narrative films , such as poetic or abstractfilms .

name: Narrowband
desc: Relatively restricted in bandwidth.

name: Narrowband ISDN (N-ISDN)
desc: Telecommunications at 1.5 Mbps on copper wire.

name: Narrowcasting
desc: Broadcasting to a small audience.

name: Narrow MUSE
desc: An NHK-proposed ATV scheme very similar to MUSE (and potentially able to use the same decoder) but fitting within a single, 6 MHz transmission channel. Unlike MUSE-6 and MUSE-9, narrow MUSE is not receiver-compatible.

name: NAS
desc: Network Attached Storage. One or more storage devices associated with a single server existing as a node on a LAN.

name: NAT
desc: Network Address Translation. Router function translating between private internal IP addresses and public external addresses. All devices behind a NAT router share one public IP. Can complicate AV control system connectivity.

name: National Television System Committee (NTSC)
desc: a) The organization that formulated the “NTSC” system. Usually taken to mean the NTSC color television system itself, or its interconnect standards. NTSC is the television standard currently in use in the U.S., Canada and Japan. NTSC image for- mat is 4:3 aspect ratio, 525 lines, 60 Hz and 4 MHz video bandwidth with  a total 6 MHz of video channel width. NTSC uses YIQ. NTSC-1 was set in 1948. It increased the number of scanning lines from 441 to 525, and replaced AM sound with FM. b) The name of two standardization groups, the first of which established the 525 scanning-line-per-frame/30 frame- per-second standard and the second of which established the color televi- sion system currently used in the U.S.; also the common name of the

name: Native BIFS Node
desc: A Binary Format for Scenes (BIFS) node which is introduced and specified within the Final Committee Draft of International Standard as opposed to non-native BIFS node, which is a node referenced from ISO/IEC 14772-1.

name: Native Resolution
desc: The resolution at which the video file was captured.

name: naturalism( naturalistic )
desc: a stage , artistic , philosophical , or literary term as well as a filmterm , signifying an extreme form of realism in which life isdepicted in a stoic , unbiased way ; see also Neo - Realism .

name: Naturalism (theatre)
desc: A dramatic form attempting to show life and society as they actually are, using believable behavior and scientifically observed human interaction. Closely linked to Realism.

name: NAVA (National Audio-Visual Association)
desc: A trade association for audio-visual dealers, manufacturers and producers.

name: Navigation
desc: Purposeful Motion Through Virtual Space.

name: Navigation Data
desc: In DVD-Video there are five types of navigation data: Video Manager Information (VMGI), Video Title Set Information (VTSI), Program Chain Information (PGCI), Presentation Control Information (PCI) and Data Search Information (DSI).

name: Navigation Timer
desc: In DVD-Video a system timer used during navigation operations.

name: NBC
desc: Television network that was an original proponent of the ACTV ATV schemes. NBC was also the first network to announce its intention to shift from NTSC entirely to CAV recording equipment.

name: NCTA (National Cable Television Association)
desc: This is the primary cable TV owner and/or operator’s trade association. NCTA is performing similar roles to NAB in ATV research and lobbying, with an emphasis

name: NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement)
desc: An agreement signed between two parties that have to disclose confidential information to each other in order to do business. In general, the NDA states why the information is

name: NDI
desc: Network Device Interface. NewTek/Vizrt's software-defined standard for high-quality, low-latency IP video production over Gigabit Ethernet. Enables live video sharing between applications on a LAN. Widely used in production and broadcast.

name: NDI Bridge
desc: An NDI tool allowing NDI streams to be sent across different network segments or over the internet by tunneling streams between NDI Bridge instances.

name: NDI HX
desc: NDI High Efficiency. A compressed version of NDI using H.264 or H.265, designed for cameras with limited processing power. Reduces bandwidth requirements at the cost of slightly higher latency than full NDI.

name: Near Cyc
desc: A Cyclorama Light Placed Close To The Cyclorama, General Less Than 8'.

name: Near end
desc: In videoconferencing, the party or group at the local end of the connection.

name: Near Field
desc: Locations Close To The Sound Source Between The Source And The Far Field. The Near Field Is Typically Characterized By Large Sound Pressure Level Variations With Small Changes In Measurement Position From The Source.

name: NEC
desc: National Electrical Code. Produced by NFPA, it covers installation of electric conductors and equipment in buildings and other premises. Used in every US state.

name: negative
desc: refers to film that has an inverted record of the light and darkareas of the photographed scene

name: Negative Effect
desc: Special effect in which either blacks and whites are reversed or colors are inverted. For example, red becomes a blue-green, green becomes purple, etc. The Video Equalizer and Digital Video Mixer includes a negative effect which can be used to generate electronic color slides from color negatives. An electronic color filter can be used for fine adjustment of the hues.

name: Negative Image
desc: Refers to a picture signal having a polarity which is opposite to normal polarity and which results in a picture in which the white areas appear as black and vice versa.

name: Negative Logic
desc: The logic false state is represented by the more positive voltage in the system, and the logic true state is represented by the more negative voltage in the system. For TTL, 0 becomes +2.4 volts or greater, and 1 becomes +0.4 volts or less.

name: Nema
desc: Abbreviation For National Electrical Manufacturers' Association.

name: Nema Configuration
desc: An Alpha-Numeric Code Applied To Connectors To Guarantee Consistency And Interchangeability Among Manufacturers.

name: NE (Network Element)
desc: In general, an NE is a combination hardware and software system that is designed primarily to perform a telecommuni- cations service function. For example, an NE is the part of the network equipment where a transport entity (such as a line, a path, or a section)  is terminated and monitored. As defined by wavelength routing, an NE is the originating, transient, or terminating node of a wavelength path.

name: NEO (console)
desc: Vari-Lite's professional lighting control software and console platform (NEO X15 console). NEO uses a cue-based, tracking architecture and is designed for both theatrical and live event applications.

name: Neo - Realism
desc: an influential movement of the late 1940s and 1950s thatoriginated in Italy ; inaugurated by Jean Renoir , but associatedwith Italian post - war directors ( Rossellini , Visconti , and DeSica ) ; refers to films made outside the studio , with shootingon real locations , sometimes the absence of a script and / ornon - professional casts and actors - all designedsimultaneously to cut costs and increase the impression ofspontaneity ; neo - realistic films often deal with contemporarysocial and political issues ; see alsonaturalism .

name: Nested
desc: Subroutine that is called by another subroutine or a loop within a larger loop is said to be nested.

name: Net3
desc: ETC's proprietary Ethernet-based lighting network protocol and hardware ecosystem for distributing sACN, EDMX, and console data across a facility. Net3 devices include gateways, hubs, and RFR wireless remotes.

name: NetLinx
desc: AMX's control processor platform and programming language. NetLinx Studio is the IDE for writing control programs using a C-like syntax for AMX processors.

name: NET (National Educational Television)
desc: A public TV Network of stations.

name: NETRON
desc: Obsidian's professional EtherDMX node family. Converts Art-Net and sACN signals to physical DMX outputs for distributing universe data from an ONYX console across a venue or touring rig.

name: Netshow
desc: Microsoft Netshow Is A Service That Runs On Windows Nt Servers, Delivering The High- Quality Streaming Multimedia To Users On Corporate Intranets And The Internet. It Consists Of Server And Tools Components For Delivering Audio, Video, Illustrated Audio, And Other Multimedia Types Over The Network. Netshow Provides The Foundation For Building Rich, Interactive Multimedia Applications For Commerce, Distance Learning, News And Entertainment Delivery, And Corporate Communications.

name: Network
desc: a) A group of stations connected together for common broad- cast or common business purposes; multiple circuits. b) A group of computers and other devices (such as printers) that can all communicate with each other electronically to transfer and share information. c) A col- lection of MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS) multiplexes transmitted on a single delivery system, e.g., all digital channels on a specific cable system.

name: Network Administrator
desc: The individual responsible for setting up, maintaining, and troubleshooting the network, and for supplying setup information to system administrators of each system.

name: Networking
desc: Two or more devices communicating with each other and sharing resources.

name: Network Interface Card (NIC)
desc: A device that connects a terminal to a network.

name: Network Router
desc: A network device forwarding packets from one network to another (Layer 3). Examines each incoming packet and forwards based on routing tables and destination address.

name: Network switch
desc: A device connecting multiple network devices on a LAN, forwarding frames only to the destination port. Managed switches support VLANs, QoS, IGMP snooping, and STP — all essential for professional AVoIP networks.

name: network TV
desc: originally referred to the " Big Three " ( ABC , NBC and CBS ) ,but now with additional competitors , including Fox Channel ,often known as ' free - TV '

name: Neutral
desc: Normal; without power; not in working position; without much color or brightness purposes; multiple circuits.

name: Neutral Colors
desc: The range of gray levels, from black to white, but with- out color. For neutral areas in the image, the RGB signals will all be equal; in color difference formats, the color difference signals will be zero.

name: Neutral Conductor
desc: A Current Carrying Conductor That Is Electrically Connected To Neutral.

name: Neutral Density Filter
desc: A Filter That Reduces The Intensity Of Light Without Affecting Its Color.

name: newsreel
desc: refers to a filmed cinema news report

name: New Wave
desc: also known as Nouvelle Vague ; originally referred to a groupof individualistic , innovative , and non - traditional Frenchfilmmakers , directors and producers in the late 1950s andearly 1960s , including Francois Truffaut , Jean - Luc Godard ,Claude Chabrol , Eric Rohmer , and Alain Resnais , who beganas critics on Cahiers du Cinema and espoused the principlesof auteur theory ; the New Wave film style was characterizedby a cinema verite style with the use of the jump cut , thehand - held camera , non - linear storytelling , and loose ,improvised direction ; now used to generally refer to any newmovement in a national cinema .

name: New York Institute of Technology
desc: Private engineering school head- quartered in Old Westbury, NY, noted for its advanced computer graphics. Its Science and Technology Research Center, in Dania, FL, has been researching ATV for years. NYIT is a proponent of the VISTA ATV scheme.

name: NEXT
desc: Near-End Crosstalk. A measure of unwanted signal coupling from a transmitter at the near end into neighboring pairs measured at the near end. Expressed in dB.

name: NexTView
desc: An electronic program guide (EPG) based on ETSI ETS 300 707.

name: Nfg
desc: Abbreviation For No F###Ing Good. Usually Written On A Piece Of Tape And Attached To A Cable Or Production Device That Is Broken.

name: NFPA 101
desc: The Life Safety Code. A US standard establishing minimum requirements for safe evacuation from buildings, including theatres.

name: NFS
desc: Network File System protocol. Enables users to access remote data and files over a shared network as if accessed locally.

name: NFS™  (Network File System)
desc: A distributed file system developed by Sun that enables a set of computers to cooperatively access each other’s files transparently.

name: NG
desc: An often-used term meaning “no good”.

name: NHK
desc: See Nippon Hoso Kyokai.

name: Nibble
desc: Four bits or half a byte. A group of four contiguous bits. A nibble can take any of 16 (24) values.

name: NIC
desc: Network Interface Card. Hardware, usually a peripheral card, that interfaces a computer to a network.

name: NiCad (Nickel Cadmium)
desc: Common Rechargeable video camera battery type.

name: NICAM
desc: See Near Instantaneous Companded Audio Multiplexer.

name: NICAM 728
desc: A technique of implementing digital stereo audio for PAL video using another audio subcarrier. The bit rate is 728 kbps. It is discussed in BS.707 and ETSI EN 300 163. NICAM 728 is also used to transmit non-audio digital data in China.

name: nickelodeon
desc: the term for a makeshift motion picture theater , often aconverted store , which proliferated all over the US , mostly inworking - class areas of metropolitan centers , during the firstdecade of the 20th century . The name was derived from the 5cents / nickel charged to patrons .

name: Nicopress®
desc: A Registered Trademark Of The National Telephone Supply Company, Used To Describe Compression Type Sleeves Placed At The End Of A Wire Rope To Interconnect Two Ropes Or To Form An Eye At The End.

name: Nighttime Mode
desc: Name for Dolby Digital dynamic range compression feature to allow low-volume nighttime listening without losing legibility of dialog.

name: nihilistic ( nihilism )
desc: a dark and brooding film that features a downbeat ,depressing , dreary , cynical , gloomy or bleak tone ; oftendoom - laden and concerned with the subjects of death ,suffering , tragedy , unhappiness , and existential despair ; theprotagonist often meets with death or tragedy in a film'sconclusion ; see also dystopia .

name: Nippon Hoso Kyokai (NHK)
desc: The Japan Broadcasting Corporation, principal researchers of HDTV through the 1970s, developers of the 1125 scanning-line system for HDEP and of all the MUSE systems for transmission.

name: Nippon Television
desc: See NTV.

name: NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)
desc: This is the North American regional forum at which OSI implementation agreements are decided. It is equivalent to EWOS in Europe and AOW in the Pacific.

name: Nit
desc: A Measurement Of The Light Intensity Of A Display Screen . One Nit Is Equal To One Candela ( Onecandlepower ) Per Square Meter .

name: NIT (Network Information Table)
desc: The NIT conveys information relating to the physical organization of the multiplex, transport streams carried via a given network, and the characteristics of the network itself. Transport streams are identified by the combination of an original network ID and a transport stream ID in the NIT.

name: nitrate film base
desc: a highly - flammable kind of film base , composed of cellulosenitrate , used up until the late 1940s when it was thensupplanted by acetate base .

name: Nits
desc: The metric unit for brightness. 1 foot lambert = 3.425 nits.

name: NIU (Network Interface Unit)
desc: A device that serves as a common inter- face for various other devices within a local area network (LAN), or as an interface to allow networked computers to connect to an outside network. The NIU enables communication between devices that use different proto- cols by supplying a common transmission protocol, which may be used instead of the device’s own protocols, or may be used to convert the spe- cific device protocol to the common one. To enable an interface between

name: NLM (Network Loadable Module)
desc: Software that runs in a NetWare server. Although NetWare servers store DOS and Windows applications, they do not execute them. All programs that run in a NetWare server must be compiled into the NLM format.

name: NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt)
desc: A hardware interrupt request to the CPU which cannot be masked internally in the processor by a bit, but must be serviced immediately.

name: NMOS
desc: Networked Media Open Specifications. Open API specifications from AMWA for discovery, registration, and connection management of networked media devices in IP production environments. Complements SMPTE ST 2110.

name: NNI (Nederlands Normalisatie-Instituut)
desc: Standards body in the Netherlands.

name: NOC
desc: Network Operations Center. A facility used to monitor and manage system operations for telecommunications and network providers.

name: Node
desc: a) A list of calculations that you can apply to materials as part of the rendering tree language. The node can in turn serve as input to other nodes. b) Any signal line connected to two or more circuit elements. All logic inputs and outputs electrically connected together are part of the same node.

name: Nodules
desc: Clusters of materials, i.e., a large nodule of iron oxide on magnetic tape would be a tape defect.

name: noir
desc: see film noir , tech - noir

name: Noise
desc: Any unwanted electrical disturbances, other than crosstalk or dis- tortion components, that occur at the output of the reproduce amplifier.

name: Noise/A-Weighted
desc: Unwanted electrical signals produced by electronic equipment or by magnetic tape. Mostly confined to the extremes of the audible frequency spectrum where it occurs as hum and/or hiss. A-weight- ed noise is noise measured within the audio frequency band using a measuring instrument that has a frequency selective characteristic. The frequency sensitivity of the measuring instrument is adjusted to correspond to that of the average human hearing response.

name: Noise Bars
desc: White streaks in a picture, usually caused when video heads trace parts of the tape that have no recorded signal.

name: Noise-canceling
desc: A microphone designed to cancel ambient noise. Housing allows noise to reach both sides of the diaphragm simultaneously, canceling it out. Close speech strikes only one side.

name: Noise Criteria
desc: Standard Spectrum Curves By Which A Given Measured Noise May Be Described By A Single Nc Number.

name: Noise Floor
desc: The level of background noise in a signal or the level of noise introduced by equipment or storage media below which the signal can't be isolated from the noise.

name: Noise Gate
desc: A Device Used To Control Signal Level By Attenuating The Signal When It’S Under A Set Threshold. The Amount Of Attenuation Is Set By The “Range” Control. Once The Signal Crosses The Threshold, The Gate Is “Opened,” Allowing The Signal To Pass And Be Fully Heard.

name: Noise Isolation Class, Nic
desc: A Single-Number Rating Calculated In Accordance With Classification E 413 Using Measured Values Of Noise Reduction. It Provides An Estimate Of The Sound Isolation Between Two Enclosed Spaces That Are Acoustically Connected By One Or More Paths.

name: Noise Pulse
desc: A spurious signal of short duration that occurs during reproduction of a tape and is of magnitude considerably in excess of the average peak value of the ordinary system noise.

name: Noise Reduction
desc: The amount in dB that the noise added to a signal by transmission or storage chain, especially a tape recorder, is reduced from the level at which it would be if no noise reduction devices were used.

name: Noise Reduction Coefficient (Nrc)
desc: The Arithmetic Average, To The Nearest Multiple Of .05, Of The Sound Absorption Coefficients In The 1/3 Octave Bands Centered At 250 Hz, 500Hz, 1000 Hz, And 2000 Hz..Normal Incidence Sound Absorption: *; [Dimensionless]---Of A Surface, At A Specified Frequency, The Fraction Of The Perpendicularly Incident Sound Power Absorbed Or Otherwise Not Reflected.

name: Noise Reduction (Nr)
desc: The Difference In Sound Pressure Level Between Any Two Points Along The Path Of Sound Propagation. As An Example, Noise Reduction Is The Term Used To Describe The Difference In Sound Pressure Levels Between The Inside And Outside Of An Enclosure.

name: Noise Reduction Systems
desc: Refers to electronic circuits designed to minimize hiss level in magnetic recording.

name: Noise Weighting
desc: An adjustment used in the electrical measurement of television signal noise values to take into account the difference between the observable effect of noise in a television picture and the actual electri- cal value of noise.

name: Noisy
desc: A description of a picture with abnormal or spurious pixel values. The picture’s noise is a random variation in signal interfering with the information content.

name: Noisy Video
desc: Noisy video (e.g., video from low quality VTRs) is more difficult to code than the cleaner version of the same sequence. The reason is that the video encoder spends many bits trying to represent the noise as if it were part of the image. Because noise lacks the spatial coherence of the image, it is not coded efficiently.

name: Nomad (ETC)
desc: ETC's software-only Eos Family solution running on a standard Windows PC or Mac. Requires a licensed USB key (ETCnomad Key) or hardware interface to enable full output. Used for offline programming, touring supplements, and installations.

name: Nominal level
desc: The signal strength at which a product is designed to operate optimally. Common levels: video 1Vp-p; pro audio +4dBu; consumer audio -10dBV; mic level -60dBV.

name: Nomograph
desc: This is a table that allows for the determination of Chrominance to Luminance Gain and Delay errors. Refer to the discussion on Chrominance to Luminance Gain and Delay.

name: Non-Additive Mix (NAM)
desc: The process of combining two video signals such that the resultant video signal is instant-by-instant the same as the brighter of the two weighted input signals. For example, at 50% fader, the brighter of the two videos predominates. The net effect of this type

name: Non-blocking matrix switchers
desc: True matrix switchers allowing any input to switch to any or all outputs with no switching limitations.

name: Non-Blocking Network Switches
desc: A network switch capable of handling the theoretical total of all ports without interfering with other traffic.

name: Non-Compatible
desc: Incapable of working together.

name: Noncomposite Video
desc: A video which does not contain a synchronizing pulse.

name: Non-composite video signal
desc: A video signal containing picture and blanking information only, with no sync information.

name: Non-Dim
desc: 1) A Term Used To Describe A Circuit That Does Not Pass Through A Dimmer. 2) A Term Used To Describe A Load That Is Not Intended To Be Connected To A Dimmer.

name: Nondirectional
desc: A pickup pattern which is equally sensitive to sounds from all directions.

name: Non-Drop Frame
desc: System of time code that retains all frame numbers in chronological order, resulting in a slight deviation from real clock time.

name: Non-Drop Frame Time Code
desc: SMPTE time code format that continuous- ly counts a full 30 frames per second. Because NTSC video does not operate at exactly 30 frames per second, non-drop frame time code will count 108 more frames in one hour than actually occur in the NTSC video in one hour. The result is incorrect synchronization of time code with clock time. Drop frame time code solves this problem by skipping or dropping 2 frame numbers per minute, except at the tens of the minute count.

name: Non-Equity
desc: A production or performer not operating under an Actors' Equity Association contract. Common in regional theatre, community theatre, and small touring productions.

name: Non-Intra Coding
desc: Coding of a macroblock or picture that uses informa- tion both from itself and from macroblocks and pictures occurring at other times.

name: Nonlinear
desc: A Term Used For Editing And The Storage Of Audio, Video And Data. Information (Footage) Is Available Anywhere On The Media (Computer Disk Or Laser Disc) Almost Immediately Without Having To Locate The Desired Information In A Time Linear Format.

name: Nonlinear Distortion
desc: Amplitude-dependent waveform distortion. This includes APL and instantaneous signal level changes. Analog amplifiers are linear over a limited portion of their operating range. Signals which fall outside of the linear range of operation are distorted. Nonlinear distortions include crosstalk and intermodulation effects between the luminance and chrominance portions of the signal.

name: Nonlinear editing
desc: Nonlinear Distinguishes Editing Operation From The "Linear" Methods Used With Tape. Nonlinear Refers To Not Having To Edit Material In The Sequence Of The Final Program And Does Not Involve Copying To Make Edits. It Allows Any Part Of The Edit To Be Accessed And Modified Without Having To Re-Edit Or Re-Copy The Material That Is Already Edited And Follows That Point. Nonlinear Editing Is Also Non-Destructive--The Video Is Not Changed But The List Of How That Video Is Played Back Is Modified During Editing.

name: Nonlinear Editing (NLE)
desc: a) The process of editing using rapid retrieval (random access) computer controlled media such as hard disks, CD-ROMs and laser discs. Its main advantages are: allows you to reorganize clips or make changes to sections without having to redo the entire production and very fast random access to any point on the hard disk (typically 20-40 ms).

name: Nonlinear Editor
desc: An editing system based on storage of video and audio on computer disk, where the order or lengths of scenes can be changed without the necessity of reassembling or copying the program.

name: Nonlinear Encoding
desc: Relatively more levels of quantization are assigned to small amplitude signals, relatively fewer to the large signal peaks.

name: Nonlinearity
desc: The amount by which a measured video signal output dif- fers from a standard video signal output. The greater this deviation, the greater the video signal distortion and possibility of luminance and chromi- nance problems. Having gain vary as a function of signal amplitude.

name: Non-plenum
desc: A cable that does not meet CMP or CL2P flame test requirements.

name: Non-Return-to-Zero Inverse (NRZI)
desc: A video data scrambling scheme that is polarity insensitive. 0 = no change in logic; 1 = a transition from one logic level to the other.

name: Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ)
desc: A coding scheme that is polarity sensitive.

name: Non-segmented
desc: A video recording system using one head to scan one field of video onto the tape.

name: non - speaking role
desc: a small role in a film , usually a brief appearance on screen ,that has no dialogue but where the individual is clearly identifiable and usually appears in the credits ; seealsoextra , cameo , bit , and walk - on .

name: Non-Synchronous
desc: Separate things not operating together properly, i.e., audio and video or the inability to properly operate together with another specific piece of equipment or signal. See Synchronous.

name: Non-Synchronous Source
desc: A video signal whose timing information differs from the reference video by more than 800 ns.

name: non - sync( non - synchronized )
desc: refers to a scene shot without synchronized sound - andsounds must be added later during the editing stage ; syncsound is its opposite ; also refers to a mis - matchedsoundtrack ; aka asynchronous

name: non - traditional casting
desc: a movement , now officially headed by the Non - TraditionalCasting Project ( NTCP ) to " promote inclusive hiring practicesand standards , diversity in leadership and balanced portrayalsof persons of color and persons with disabilities " ; not to beconfused with cast against type ormiscast

name: Non-Uniform B-Splines (NURBS)
desc: A superset of both Bézier and Uniform B-Splines. NURBS introduces the feature of non-uniformity. Thus it is possible to subdivide a spline, for example, to locally increase the number of control points without changing the shape of the spline. This is a powerful feature which enables you to insert more control points on a spline without altering its shape; cut anywhere on a spline to generate two parts; and creates cusps in splines.

name: Non-Useful DC Component
desc: Produced by the transmission equipment and not related to picture content. The non-useful DC component present across the interface point, with or without the load impedance connected, shall be zero +/-50 µV.

name: Normal
desc: a) Relating to the orientation of a surface or a solid, a normal specifies the direction in which the outside of the surface or the solid faces. b) The normal to a plane is the direction perpendicular to the surface.

name: Normalized Noise Isolation Class, Nnic
desc: A Single-Number Rating Calculated In Accordance With Classification E413 Using Measured Values Of Normalized Noise Reduction. (See Normalized Noise Reduction.)

name: Normalized Noise Reduction, Nnr
desc: Between Two Rooms, In A Specified Frequency Band, The Value That The Noise Reduction In A Given Field Test Would Have If The Reverberation Time In The Receiving Room Were .5 S.

name: Normal Key
desc: On the 4100 series, an RGB chroma key or a luminance key, as distinct from a composite (encoded) chroma key.

name: Normally closed contact
desc: Relay or switch contacts that are closed when the relay is not activated.

name: Normally open contact
desc: Relay or switch contacts that are open when the relay is not activated.

name: Normal Mode
desc: A Room Resonance. See Mode.

name: Normal/Reverse
desc: The specification of the direction a pattern moves as the fader is pulled. A normal pattern starts small at the center and grows  to the outside while a reverse pattern starts from the edge of the screen and shrinks. Normal/Reverse specifies that the pattern will grow as the fader is pulled down, and shrink as it is pushed up. This definition loses some meaning for wipes that do not have a size per-se such as a vertical bar, however, this feature still will select the direction of pattern movement.

name: North American Broadcast Teletext Specification
desc: Provisions for 525-line system C teletext as described in EIA-516 and ITU-R BT.653.

name: NOS (Network Operating System)
desc: Generic term used to refer to what are really distributed file systems. Examples of NOSs include LAN Manager, NetWare, NFS, and VINES.

name: nostalgia film
desc: A film that wistfully looks back at an earlier past time , oftendepicting it as more innocent and uncomplicated than itactually was , historically ; nostalgia films usually look back onthe protagonist's or narrator's childhood . See alsocoming ofage film .

name: Notch Filter
desc: A device which attenuates a particular frequency greatly, but has little effect on frequencies above or below the notch frequency.

name: Notes
desc: At The End Of Each Rehearsal, The Director Will Give His Notes, Which Are His Comments On The Performance.

name: Notifier
desc: A form that appears when the system requires you to confirm an operation that you just requested, or when an error occurs.

name: novelization
desc: refers to making a novel from a film or screenplay

name: NRZ
desc: See Non-Return-to-Zero.

name: NRZI
desc: See Non-Return-to-Zero Inverse.

name: NSAP (Network Service Access Point)
desc: Network addresses, as specified by ISO. An NSAP is the point at which OSI network service is made available to a Transport Layer (Layer 4) entity.

name: NSCA
desc: National Systems Contractors Association. International trade association of companies specializing in audio, video, security, presentation, lighting, and low-voltage systems.

name: NSF (Norges Standardiseringsforbund)
desc: Standards body of Norway.

name: NST (Network Status Table)
desc: The network status table shows the network name, the protocol, the interface over which the network runs (eth:1 for LAN, atm:1 or hdlc:1 for WAN), how the network was created (static for LAN, dynamic for WAN) and the network address assigned to the connection.

name: NTFS (New Technology File System)
desc: A file system used on Windows computers.

name: NTP
desc: Network Time Protocol. A protocol synchronizing computer clocks over IP networks to within milliseconds of UTC. Used for logging, scheduling, and general system time in AV control systems. For AV sync, see PTP.

name: NTSC
desc: an abbreviation , refers specificallyto National Television System Committee that sets TV andvideo standards ; also refers to the US and Japanese videosystems that have 525 horizontal scan lines , 16 milliondifferent colors , and 30 frames per second ( or 60 half - frames( interlaced ) per second ) ; competing systems in Europe andworldwide arePAL ( Phase Alternating Line )and SECAM ( Sequential Color with Memory )

name: NTSC 4.43
desc: This is a NTSC video signal that uses the PAL color subcarri- er frequency (about 4.43 MHz). It was developed by Sony in the 1970s to more easily adapt European receivers to accept NTSC signals.

name: NTSC Artifacts
desc: Defects associated with NTSC.

name: NTSC Color
desc: The color signal TV standard set by the National Television Standards Committee of the USA.

name: NTSC Color Bars
desc: The pattern comprising eight equal-width color bars generated by an NTSC generator. The color bars are used for calibration and as a reference to check transmission paths, signal phase, recording and playback quality, and monitor alignment.

name: NTSC Composite
desc: The video signal standard proposed by the NTSC and adopted by the FCC for broadcast television in the U.S. The signal is an interlaced composite video signal of 525 lines and 60 fields per second (30 frames per second), with a bandwidth limited to 4 MHz to fit into a 6 MHz broadcast television channel without interfering with adjacent channels.

name: NTSC Decoder
desc: An electronic circuit that breaks down the composite NTSC video signal into its components.

name: NTSC Format
desc: A color television format having 525 scan lines (rows) of resolution at 30 frames per second (30 Hz). See NTSC. Compare PAL Format.

name: NTSC-M
desc: The U.S. standard of color television transmissions. See also NTSC and M.

name: NTSC MUSE
desc: Term sometimes used for MUSE-6 and MUSE-9.

name: NTSC RGB
desc: Interlaced red, green, and blue video signals timed to NTSC standards. Refers to the three monochrome signals that represent the primary colors of an image. Contrast with Component Video.

name: NTSC Standard
desc: Documentation of the characteristics of NTSC. NTSC is defined primarily in FCC Part 73 technical specifications. Many of its characteristics are defined in EIA-170A. NTSC is also defined by the CCIR. NTSC is a living standard; as problems with it are discovered, they are corrected. For example, a former EIA standard, RS-170, omitted any phase relationship between luminance and chrominance timing, resulting in blanking problems. EIA-170A defines that relationship (called SC/H for subcarrier to horizontal phase relationship). See also True NTSC.

name: NTU (Network Termination Unit)
desc: An Network Termination Unit is a device located at the final interconnect point between the PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network) and the customers own equipment.

name: NTV (Nippon Television Network)
desc: A Japanese broadcaster that is a proponent of ATV schemes similar to Faroudja’s SuperNTSC. NTV’s first generation EDTV system would use high line-rate and/or progressive scan cameras with prefiltering, adaptive emphasis, gamma correction, ghost cancellation, a progressive scan display, and advanced decoding at the receiver. The second generation would add more resolution, a widescreen aspect ratio, and better sound. The first generation is scheduled to be broadcast beginning in 1988.

name: nudie( or nudie flick )
desc: an old term for a pornographic movie , often used during theage of the Hayes Code when nudity was forbidden by censorsin mainstream films on the silver screen ; an era of nudie filmswas generated by filmmaker Russ Meyer in the late 50s ; alsosee porn .

name: Null
desc: A Low Or Minimum Point On A Graph. A Minimum Pressure Region In A Room.

name: Null Packets
desc: Packets of “stuffing” that carry no data but are necessary to maintain a constant bit rate with a variable payload. Null packets always have a PID of 8191.

name: Number 1 Bar
desc: The Lighting Bar Immediately Behind The Proscenium Arch (Qv) Or The Front Bar Which Hangs Over The Stage In A Non-Proscenium Arch Theatre.

name: Number Crunching
desc: Action of performing complex numerical operations.

name: Numerical Aperture
desc: A number that defines the light gathering ability of a specific fiber. The numerical aperture is equal to the sine of the maximum acceptance angle.

name: Numerical Aperture (NA)
desc: In fiber optics, the sine of the acceptance angle. Incoming light must be directed below this angle to enter the fiber core and propagate through total internal reflection.

name: nut
desc: in the movie - theatre business , refers to operating expensesassociated with a film ( the exhibitor's calculation of what ittakes to lease his theater , to staff and run it , etc. ) ; akahousenut

name: NVOD
desc: Near Video On Demand. Rapid Access To Program Material On Demand Achieved By Providing The Same Program On A Number Of Channels With Staggered Start Times. Many Of The Hundreds Of Tv Channels Soon To Be On Offer Will Be Made Up Of Nvod Services. These Are Delivered By A Disk-Based Transmission Server.

name: NVOD (Near Video On Demand)
desc: This service allows for a single TV program to be rebroadcast consecutively with a few minutes of difference in starting time. For example, a movie could be transmitted at 9:00, 9:15 and 9:30.

name: NWK
desc: See Network.

name: NX1
desc: Obsidian Control Systems' compact ONYX console. A portable, full-featured console with a 10.1" touchscreen, 10 motorized faders, and 8 DMX universes of integrated processing.

name: NX2
desc: Obsidian Control Systems' mid-range ONYX console. Features a large HD touchscreen, 10 motorized playbacks, full keypad, 64 universes of integrated processing, and support for 2 external 4K touch monitors.

name: NX4
desc: Obsidian Control Systems' flagship professional lighting console running ONYX. Features 4 touchscreens, 30 motorized faders, full command section, and 64 DMX universes of integrated processing.

name: NYIT
desc: See New York Institute of Technology.

name: Nyquist
desc: Nyquist Filter, Nyquist Limit, Nyquist Rule, and Harry Nyquist, for whom they are named.

name: Nyquist Filter
desc: Commonly used in the IF stage of a television receiver to separate the desired television channel from potential interference.

name: Nyquist Frequency
