desc: The lowest sampling frequency that can be used for analog-to-digital conversion of a signal without resulting in significant aliasing. Normally, this frequency is twice the rate of the highest frequency contained in the signal being sampled.

name: Nyquist frequency (Nyquist rate)
desc: The Lowest Sampling Frequency That Can Be Used For Analog- To-Digital Conversion Of A Signal Without Resulting In Significant Aliasing. Normally, This Frequency Is Twice The Rate Of The Highest Frequency Contained In The Signal Being Sampled.

name: Nyquist Interval
desc: The maximum separation in time which can be given to regularly spaced instantaneous samples of a wave of bandwidth W for complete determination of the waveform of the signal. Numerically, it is equal to 1/2 W seconds.

name: Nyquist Limit
desc: When time-varying information is sampled at a rate R, the highest frequency that can be recovered without alias is limited to R/2. Aliasing may be generated by under sampling temporally in frame rate, or vertically in lines allocated to image height, or horizontally in analog band- width or in pixel allocation. Intermodulations prior to band limiting may “preserve” some distracting effects of aliasing in the final display. Note: Sampling at a rate below the Nyquist limit permits mathematical confirma- tion of the frequencies present (as for example in a Fourier analysis of recorded motion). If the sampling window is very small (as in synchronized flash exposure), however, it may become a subjective judgment whether strobing is perceived in the image for motion approaching the limiting velocity (frequency).

name: Nyquist Plot
desc: 1)  A Plot Of The Real Part Versus The Imaginary Part Of The Frequency Response Function. For A Single-Degree- Of-Freedom System, The Nyquist Plot Is A Circle. 2) The Nyquist Plot Is Representation Of A Frequency Response Function By Graphing The "Real" Part Versus The "Imaginary" Part. In The Nyquist Plot, A Resonance Shows Up As A Circle, But There Is No Indication What Its Frequency Is -- The Nyquist Plot Is Like Sighting Down The Frequency Axis At The Real And Imaginary Parts Of The Function.

name: Nyquist Rate Limit
desc: Maximum rate of transmitting pulse signals through a channel of given bandwidth. If B is the effective bandwidth in Hertz, then 2B is the maximum number of code elements per second that can be received with certainty. The definition is often inverted, in effect, to read “the theoretical minimum rate at which an analog signal can be sampled for transmitting digitally”.

name: Nyquist Rule
desc: States that in order to be able to reconstruct a sampled signal without aliases, the sampling must occur at a rate of more than twice the highest desired frequency. The Nyquist Rule is usually observed in digital systems. For example, CDs have a sampling frequency of 44.1 kHz to allow signals up to 20 kHz to be recorded. It is, however, frequently violated in the vertical and temporal sampling of television, resulting in aliases. See also Alias.

name: Nyquist Sampling
desc: Sampling at or above twice the maximum bandwidth of a signal. This allows the original signal to be recovered without distor- tion.

name: Nyquist Sampling Theorem
desc: Intervals between successive samples must be equal to or less than one-half the period of highest frequency.

name: NZDS
desc: Non-Zero Dispersion Shifted fiber. A singlemode fiber with the zero-dispersion wavelength slightly beyond the spectral transmission region to improve performance.

name: OAM (Operation, Administration and Maintenance)
desc: ATM Forum specification for cells used to monitor virtual circuits. OAM cells provide a virtual circuit level loopback in which a router responds to the cells, demonstrating that the circuit is up and the router is operational.

name: Object Based Coding (OBC)
desc: A technique that codes arbitrarily shaped objects within a scene. Transmitted parameters are shape, color and motion.

name: Object Carousels
desc: The object carousel specification has been added in order to support data broadcast services that require the periodic broadcasting of DSM-CC user-user (U-U) objects through DVB compliant broadcast networks, specifically as defined by DVB systems for interactive services (SIS). Data broadcast according to the DVB object carousel specification is transmitted according to the DSM-CC object carousel and DSM-CC data carousel specification which are defined in MPEG-2 DSM-CC.

name: Object Clock Reference (OCR)
desc: A clock reference that is used by a media object hierarchy. This notation has been chosen within the context of the MPEG-4 Systems.

name: Object Content Information (OCI)
desc: Additional information about content conveyed through one or more elementary streams. It is either attached to individual elementary stream descriptors or conveyed itself as an elemen- tary stream.

name: Object Descriptor Message
desc: A message that identifies the action to be taken on a list of object descriptors or object descriptor Ids, for example, update or remove.

name: Object Descriptor (OD)
desc: A descriptor that associates one or more elementary streams by means of their elementary stream descriptors and defines their logical dependencies.

name: Object Descriptor Stream
desc: An elementary stream that conveys object descriptors encapsulated in object descriptor messages.

name: Objective
desc: The very first optical element at the front of a lens.

name: Objective (acting)
desc: In acting, the thing that a character wants — their goal or motivation in a scene or throughout the play.

name: Object Modeling Technique (OMT)
desc: A graphical method to represent the class hierarchy. This notation has been chosen within the context of the MPEG-4 Systems.

name: Object Program
desc: End result of the source language program (assembly or high-level) after it has been translated into machine language.

name: Objects
desc: Discrete 3-D Shapes Within The Virtual World That A User Can Interact With.

name: Object Time Base (OTB)
desc: a) The OTB defines the notation of time of a given encoder. All time stamps that the encoder inserts in a coded audiovi- sual object data stream refer to this time base. b) A time base valid for a given object, and hence for its media object decoder. The OTB is conveyed to the media object decoder via object clock references. All time stamps relating to this object’s decoding process refer to this time base.

name: obligatory scene
desc: a cliched and expected scene for a particular genre , e.g. , alove scene in a romance or dramatic film , a shoot - out in aWestern , the solving of a crime in a mystery , a rescue in anaction film , etc.

name: Oblique Mode
desc: See Mode.

name: OBO (Output Back-Off)
desc: The ratio of the signal power measured at the output of a high power amplifier to the maximum output signal power. The output back-off is expressed in decibels as either a positive or negative quantity. It can be applied to a single carrier at the output to the HPA (carrier OBO), or to the ensemble of output signals (total OBO).

name: Obsidian Control Systems
desc: A professional lighting console manufacturer (formerly part of Martin Professional/Harman) producing the ONYX platform. Known for NX1, NX2, NX4 consoles. Sister platform to the Martin M-Series consoles.

name: OBS Studio
desc: Open Broadcaster Software. Free, open-source software for video recording and live streaming. Used for creating streaming productions with scenes, sources, audio mixing, and output to RTMP, SRT, and other protocols.

name: OC12 (Optical Carrier Level 12)
desc: A signal with a bitrate of 622 Mbps.

name: OC1 (Optical Carrier Level 1)
desc: A signal with a bitrate of 51.8 Mbps. Fundamental transmission rate for SONET.

name: OC3
desc: Optical Carrier Level 3. A 155 Mbps Atm Sonet Signal Stream That Can Carry Three Ds3 Signals.

name: OC3 (Optical Carrier Level 3)
desc: A 155 Mbps ATM SONET signal stream that can carry three DS-3 signals. Equivalent to SDH STM-1.

name: OC48 (Optical Carrier Level 48)
desc: A signal with a bitrate of 2.4 Gbps.

name: Occipital   Cortex
desc: The Back Of The Brain Receiving Retinotopic Projections Of Visual Displays.

name: Occlusion
desc: The process whereby an area of the video raster is blocked or made non-transparent by controlling selected bits. Occlusion is used when more than one picture is displayed or windowed simultaneously.

name: Occupancy Sensor
desc: See Motion Detector. Operating Cost: See Annual Operating Cost.

name: OCR
desc: Optical Character Reader/Recognition. Hardware and software reading characters as images and converting them into text for use in a computer.

name: Octal
desc: Base 8 number system. Often used to represent binary numbers, since each octal digit corresponds directly to three binary digits.

name: Octave
desc: An Octave Is A Doubling Or Halving Of Frequency. 20Hz-40Hz Is Often Considered The Bottom Octave. Each Octave You Add On The Bottom Requires That Your Speakers Move Four Times As Much Air!

name: Octave Bands
desc: Frequency Ranges In Which The Upper Limit Of Each Band Is Twice The Lower Limit. Octave Bands Are Identified By Their Geometric Mean Frequency, Or Center Frequency.

name: OCT (Octal Notation)
desc: Any mathematical notation that uses 8 different characters (usually the digits 0 to 7).

name: Ocular
desc: The very last optical element at the back of a lens (the one closer to the CCD chip).

name: Oculus
desc: A Line Of Virtual  Reality  Devices. The First Commercial Oculus Model, Oculus Rift, Was Released In 2016. The Most Recent Model Is The Oculus Quest 2. Oculus Is Owned By Meta, Formerly Known As Facebook, Inc. The Oculus Brand Will Be Phased Out In 2022, And Future Headsets  Will Be Released Under The Meta Brand. Oculus Play  Peripherals Include Controllers  And Tracking  Devices.

name: Odd Number
desc: The number of scanning lines per frame necessary in an interlaced scanning system. One line is split between fields to ensure proper spacing between scanning lines from different fields. A progressive- ly scanned system may use an even number of scanning lines.

name: OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer)
desc: A company which develops, produces and sells computer and consumer hardware to other companies.

name: Oersted
desc: A unit of magnetic field strength.

name: OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplex)
desc: First promoted in the early 1990s as a wireless LAN technology. OFDM’s spread spectrum technique distributes the data over a large number of carriers that are spaced apart at precise frequencies. This spacing provides the “orthogonal- ity” in this technique which prevents the demodulators from seeing other frequencies than their own. Coded OFDM (COFDM) adds forward error correction to the OFDM method.

name: Off-Axis -
desc: The Position Of A Microphone'S Polar Pattern That Rejects Sound.

name: Off-Broadway
desc: Professional theatre produced in New York City in venues with 100–499 seats, distinct from Broadway's larger houses. Known for experimental and developmental work.

name: Off-line
desc: A file, media, or object that is out of action or not currently usable.

name: Off-Line Edit
desc: Rough cut editing used to produce an Edit Decision List.

name: Off-line (editing)
desc: A Decision-Making Process Using Low-Cost Equipment Usually To Produce An Edl Or A Rough Cut Which Can Then Be Conformed Or Referred To In A High Quality On-Line Suite-- So Reducing Decision-Making Time In The More Expensive On-Line Environment. While Most Off- Line Suites Enable Shot Selection And The Defining Of Transitions Such As Cuts And Dissolves, Very Few Allow Settings For The Dves, Color Correctors, Keyers And Layering That Are Increasingly A Part Of The On-Line Editing Process.

name: Off-Line Editor
desc: A low resolution, usually computer and disk based edit system in which the creative editing decisions can be made at lower cost and often with greater flexibility than in an expensive fully equipped on-line bay.

name: Offline Encoder
desc: The Indeo video codec’s normal mode of operation, in which it takes as long as necessary to encode a video file so that it displays the best image quality and the lowest and most consistent data rate. Compare Quick Compressor.

name: Off-Line, Offline
desc: Preliminary editing done on relatively low-cost editing systems, usually to provide an EDL for final on-line editing and assembly of the finished show.

name: Off-off-Broadway
desc: The smallest professional theatre sector in New York City, typically in venues under 100 seats. Known for avant-garde, experimental, and developmental work.

name: off or offstage( or off - camera )
desc: refers to action or dialogue off the visible stage , or beyond theboundaries of the camera's field of vision or depicted frame ;aka off - screen

name: Offset
desc: a) The horizontal and vertical displacement of a clip. b) Reference numbers that indicate the change, in terms of frames, that take place when you trim.

name: Off Stage
desc: The Stage Floor Area That Is Not A Part Of The Acting Area And Is Not Visible To The Audience.

name: Oh, $%&!
desc: Sometimes When Things Go Wrong At A Show, Cursing Happens. It'S Advisable To Not Do This Too Loudly, Especially When Working Corporate Events.

name: Ohm
desc: A Unit Of Measurement For Resistance, Reactance, Or Impedance.

name: Ohm Meter
desc: An Apparatus That Measures Resistance.

name: Ohm'S Law
desc: A Basic Electrical Formula That Simply States That Voltage Is Equal To Electrical Current Multiplied By Resistance, I.E., V=Ir.

name: Oil Spotted
desc: When Someone Is Late To A Bus Call, And Left Behind.

name: OIRT (Organisation Internationale de Radiodiffusion-Television)
desc: The OIRT was dissolved in 1992 and integrated into the Union of the European Broadcast Organizations (UER).

name: OLED
desc: Organic Light Emitting Diode. A flat panel display where each pixel emits its own light and can be turned off completely. Inherently advantageous for HDR.

name: OLE (Object Linking and Embedding)
desc: A standard for combining data from different applications that updates automatically.

name: Olio Curtain
desc: A Curtain Located Between The “Front” Or “Act” Curtains And The “Rear” Curtain That Closes Off A Portion Of The Acting Area For More Intimate Presentations. It Is Often Colored Or Decorative.

name: O-Member (Observing Member)
desc: A term used within ISO/IEC JTC1 committees. A National Body that does not vote.

name: OMF, OMFI, OMF Interchange (Open Media Framework Interchange)
desc: A media and metadata exchange solution developed by Avid Technology. A standard format for the interchange of digital media data among heterogeneous platforms. The format is designed to encapsulate all the information required to interchange a variety of digital media, such as audio, video, graphics, and still images as well as the rules for combining and presenting the media. The format includes rules for identifying the original sources of the digital media, and it can encapsulate both com- pressed and uncompressed digital media data.

name: Omni
desc: Abbreviation For Omnidirectional; A Microphone Polar Pattern That Picks Up Sound Evenly In All Directions.

name: Omni Directional
desc: Also Called Nondirectional, This Type Of Microphone’S Response Is Generally Considered To Be A Sphere In Three Dimensions.

name: omniscientpoint - of - view( POV )
desc: a film in which the narrator knows ( and sees ) everythingoccurring in a story , including character thoughts , action ,places , conversations , and events ; contrast to subjectivepoint - of - view

name: OMWF (Open MPEG Windows Forum)
desc: OMWF is a Japanese industry consortium aiming at compatibility in MPEG-based multimedia applications. The group, that includes various hardware and software vendors and content providers in Japan, ahs its offspring in the popularity in Japan of CD movies and Karaoke. Through cooperation with the Open MPEG Consortium in the USA, the OMWF cleared up details in the MCI standard, that impeded compatibility. The new specification, called the Video CD specification, allows Windows machines to play MPEG-1 video CDs and allows Windows data and applications to be stored on the same CD along with the video contents.

name: On-Air Output
desc: Ready to use for transmission or videotaping, this is the PGM output.

name: On-demand streaming
desc: Streaming Media Content That Is Transmitted To The Client Upon Request.See Also: Live Streaming.

name: One Light
desc: A telecine transfer or film print produced with a single setting of color correction values. One light is the simplest, fastest, and least costly type of transfer.

name: one - liner
desc: a term for a short , one - line joke ( that contains itsown punchline ) ; also the term may refer to the ' highconcept ' description of a film - a few words used to describe a script , storyline or a film's premise that a person can easilyunderstand with a simple one - liner

name: one man ( or woman )show
desc: a scripted or filmed narrative ( or an avantgarde or experimentalfilm ) featuring a solo performance piecewith only one actor or actress who sometimes plays multipleroles or characters ; often presented by a stand - up comedian ;contrast with concert film

name: One_Random_PGC Title
desc: In DVD-Video, a Title within a Video Title Set (VTS) that contains a single Program Chain (PGC), but does not meet the requirements of a One_Sequential_PGC Title. Contrast with to One_Sequential_PGC Title and Multi_PGC Title.

name: one - reeler
desc: refers to a film 10-12 minutes long

name: One’s Complement
desc: Number representation system used for signed binary integers in which the negative of a number is obtained by comple- menting it. The leftmost bit becomes the sign bit, with 0 for plus, 1 for minus.

name: One_Sequential_PGC Title
desc: In DVD-Video, a Title within a Video Title  Set (VTS) that contains a single Program Chain (PGC) with the following attributes: 1) PG Playback mode is Sequential, 2) no Next PGC, Previous PGC or Go Up PGCs are defined, and 3) the Navigation Timer is neither set, nor referred to. Contrast with One_Random_PGC Title and Multi_PGC Title.

name: one - sheet
desc: refers to the typical size of a movie poster

name: One Shot
desc: A Shot With One Person In It Usually Framed As A Medium Close-Up. Examples Include Shots Of Someone Eating Alone At A Table Or Running In A Marathon.

name: One-Third Octave Bands
desc: Frequency Ranges Where Each Octave Is Divided Into One-Third Octaves With The Upper Frequency Limit Being 2* (1.26) Times The Lower Frequency. Identified By The Geometric Mean Frequency Of Each Band.

name: One Wire Interconnect
desc: Interconnect consists of a single wire transport- ing an encoded, composite analog video signal.

name: On-line (editing)
desc: Production Of The Complete, Final Edit Performed At Full Program Quality--The Buck Stops Here! Being Higher Quality Than Off-Line, Time Costs More But The Difference Is Reducing. Preparation In An Off-Line Suite Will Help Save Time And Money In The On-Line. To Produce The Finished Edit On-Line Has To Include A Wide Range Of Tools, Offer Flexibility To Try Ideas And Accommodate Late Changes, And To Work Fast To Maintain The Creative Flow And To Handle Pressured Situations.

name: On-Line Editor
desc: An editing system where the actual video master is created. An on-line bay usually consists of an editing computer, video switcher, audio mixer, one or more channels of DVE, character generator, and several video tape machines.

name: On-Line, Online
desc: Final editing or assembly using master tapes to pro- duce a finished program ready for distribution. Often preceded by off-line editing, but in some cases programs go directly to the on-line editing suite. Usually associated with high-quality computer editing and digital effects.

name: on or onstage( or on - camera )
desc: on the visible stage , or within the boundaries of the camera'sfield of vision

name: On-Screen
desc: Something That’S Shown In Your Video

name: On-Screen Display
desc: A function on many VCRs and televisions in which operational functions (tint, brightness, VCR function, programming, etc.) are displayed graphically on the television screen.

name: On - Set Operations
desc: The Team Responsible For Managing Any Technical Difficulties Related To The Volume Duringproduction Operations And Maintain The Key Creatives .

name: On - Set Virtual Production ( OSVP )
desc: Use Of Virtual Production Techniques To Capture Final Imagery In - Camera . See Also Icvfx .

name: On Stage
desc: The Portion Of The Stage Area Visible To The Audience, Usually Defined By Masking Curtains, Scenery, An Orchestra Shell, Or By Lighting.

name: On the book
desc: The DSM or Stage Manager who is following and calling all cues from the prompt script during a performance. They give STANDBY (UK) or WARN (US) cues first, then GO cues to all departments.

name: On the Fly
desc: a) Depressing a button causing some change while a switch- er is transitioning. b) Selecting a tape edit point while VTR is moving.

name: ONYX
desc: Obsidian Control Systems' lighting control software platform. Runs on NX-series consoles and as free PC software. Features multiple cuelist types, DYLOS pixel mapping, and supports Art-Net, sACN, and ONYX X-Net protocols.

name: ONYX X-Net
desc: Obsidian Control Systems' proprietary Ethernet networking protocol for communication between ONYX consoles and NX-series hardware, providing low-latency, reliable data transfer for show-critical applications.

name: OOB (Out-of-Band)
desc: Out-of-band is any frequency outside the band used for voice frequencies.

name: Op
desc: Opposite Prompt: The Right Hand Side Of The Stage As You Face The Audience.

name: Op Amp
desc: Short For Operational Amplifier; An Integrated Circuit Used In Many Different Kinds Of Electronic Equipment That Can Amplify Small Signals.

name: Opaque Macroblock
desc: A macroblock with shape mask of all 255’s.

name: Opcode
desc: See Operation Code.

name: Open
desc: To double-click an icon, or to select an icon then choose “Open” from a menu in order to display a window that contains the information that the icon represents.

name: Open Architecture
desc: A concept for television receivers that acknowledges an absence of ATV transmission/distribution standards and allows a receiver to deal with a multiplicity of standards and delivery mechanisms.

name: Open Cable
desc: A Project Aimed At Obtaining A New Generation Of Set-Top Boxes That Are Interoperable. These New Devices Will Enable A New Range Of Interactive Services To Be Provided To Cable Customers.

name: Open Circuit
desc: A Circuit That Has A Physical Break Or Disconnection, Whether Intentional Or Accidental, In Its Electrical Path.

name: Opencolorlo ( OCIO )
desc: A System That Enables Color Transforms And Image Display To Be Handled In A Consistentmanner Across Multiple Graphics Applications .

name: Open End
desc: A Term Used To Describe A U Shaped Mounting Frame For A Scrim That Supports The Scrim On Three Sides, Allowing The Fourth Side To Remain Free From A Frame Section. When This Side Is Partially Introduced Into The Beam, The Straight Edge Of The Dimmed Portion Of The Beam Casts No Dark Shadow Line.

name: Open-Ended Edit
desc: a) Assemble mode. b) Edit that has a start time but no designated stop time.

name: Open Face
desc: A Term Used To Describe Luminaires That Use No Lenses.

name: opening credits or title( sequence )
desc: the presentation of the ' opening credits ' ( as an introduction tothe audience about the film and including selected importantmembers of the production ) is known as the opening creditssequence ; sometimes it is superimposed on the action , butoften exists as static letters on a solid background ; sincethe closing or end credits usually list the entire cast andproduction crew , the opening credits sequence is usuallypositioned to set the mood of the film , and sometimes evenlacks any credits except the film's title ; akafrontcredits or beginning titles

name: Opening night
desc: The first official public performance of a production, typically attended by press critics and industry guests. The reviews from opening night establish a show's critical reputation.

name: Open-Loop
desc: Circuit or other system operating without feedback.

name: Open MPEG Consortium
desc: The goal of the Open MPEG Consortium is  to “create a single API for the playback of MPEG-1 titles under Windows and DOS”. The consortium has developed the MPEG Multimedia Control Interface (MCI) which defines how MPEG boards operate under Windows. Due to some undefined topics, the MCI specification has not been able to curb incompatibility, but the consortium has later cooperated with the Japanese OMWF group on an enhanced specification.

name: Open run
desc: A production with no scheduled closing date, running as long as ticket sales remain commercially viable.

name: Open Sound Control ( OSC )
desc: A Protocol For Networking Sound Synthesizers , Computers , And Other Multimedia Devices Forpurposes Such As Musical Performance Or Show Control . Osc'S Advantages Includeinteroperability , Accuracy , Flexibility And Enhanced Organization And Documentation .

name: Open Subtitles
desc: See Subtitles.

name: Open White
desc: A Lantern (Qv) Is Said To Be "In Open White" If No Filter Or Gel Is Used To Colour Its Light.

name: Operating Level
desc: A certain level of flux recorded on magnetic tape.

name: Operating Light
desc: A Work Light Used By The Operator Of A Control Console.

name: Operating Pole
desc: A Long, Lightweight Rod With A Handle On One End And An Attachment On The Other For The Purpose Of Adjusting Or Switching On Pole-Operated Yokes, Luminaires, Pantographs, Etc.

name: Operating Program
desc: Computer software program which controls all functions of related computers and hardware devices.

name: Operating system
desc: The Base Program That Manages A Computer And Gives Control Of The Functions Designed For General Purpose Usage--Not For Specific Applications. Common Examples Are Ms-Dos And Windows For Pcs, Mac Os8 For Apple Macintosh, And Unix (And Its Variations Irix And Linux). For Actual Use, For Example, As A Word Processor, Specific Applications Software Is Run On Top Of The Operating System.

name: Operation Code (Opcode)
desc: Segment of the machine-language instruc- tion that specifies the operation to be performed. The other segments specify the data, address, or port. For the 8085, the first byte of each instruction is the opcode.

name: Operator
desc: A Person Designated To Operate A Control Console, Follow Spot, Generator, Or Some Other Apparatus That Requires Some Degree Of Training And/Or Skill To Use.

name: OPEX
desc: Operating Expense. An ongoing cost for running a product, business, or system.

name: Opposite Track Path (OTP)
desc: Dual-layer disc where Layer 0 and Layer 1 have opposite track directions. Layer 0 reads from the inside to the outside of the disc, whereas Layer 1 reads from the outside to the inside. The disc always spins clockwise, regardless of track structure or layers. This mode facilitates movie playback by allowing seamless (or near-seamless) transition from one layer to another. In computer applications (DVD-ROM), it usually makes more sense to use the Parallel Track Path (PTP) format where random access time is more important.

name: Optic
desc: The Components Of A Luminaire Such As Reflectors, Refractors, Protectors Which Make Up The Light Emitting Section.

name: Optical disks
desc: Disks Using Optical Techniques For Recording And Replay Of Material. These Offer Large Storage Capacities On A Small Area, The Most Common Being The 5-1/4-Inch Compact Disk, Being Removable And Having Rather Slower Data Rates Than Fixed Magnetic Disks--But Faster Than Floppies. Write Once, Read Many Or "Worm" Optical Disks First Appeared With 2 Gb Capacity On Each Side Of A 12-Inch Platter--Useful For Archiving Images. In 1989 The Read/Write Magneto Optical (Mo) Disk Was Introduced Which Can Be Re-Written Around A Million Times. With Its Modest Size, Just 5-1/4-Inches In Diameter, The Iso Standard Cartridge Can Store 325 Mb Per Side--Offering Low Priced Removable Storage For Over 700 Tv Pictures Per Disk. A Variant On The Technology Is The Phase Change Disk But This Is Not Compatible With The Iso Standard. An Up-Rated Mo Disk System Introduced In 1994 Has A Capacity Of 650 Mb Per Side, 1.3 Gb Per Disk. In 1996 A Second Doubling Of Capacity Was Introduced Offering 2.6 Gb On A Removable Disk. Besides The Obvious Advantages For Storing Tv Pictures This Is Particularly Useful Where Large Format Images Are Used, In Print And In Film For Example. The Nec Diskcam System Uses Optical Disks For Storage.

name: Optical Effects
desc: Trick shots prepared by the use of an optical printer in the laboratory, especially fades and dissolves.

name: Optical Ethernet
desc: An optical connection for delivering Ethernet packets over fiber, used to preserve signal quality over greater distances or for security concerns.

name: Optical Fiber
desc: A glass strand designed to carry light in a fashion similar to the manner in which wires carry electrical signals. Since light is electro- magnetic radiation of tremendously high frequency, optical fibers can carry much more information than can wires, though multiple paths through the fiber place an upper limit on transmission over long distances due to a characteristic called pulse dispersion. Many feel that the wide bandwidth of an optical fiber eliminates the transmission problems associated with the high base bandwidth of HDEP schemes. CATV and telephone companies propose connecting optical fibers directly to homes.

name: Optical Link
desc: A single fiber optic signal path or point-to-point connection between a transmitter and receiver, including connectors, fiber, splices, and other components.

name: Optical Loss Test Set (OLTS)
desc: Test equipment for singlemode or multimode fiber comprising a light source and power meter, used to measure optical signal loss along the fiber and connectors.

name: Optical Return Loss (ORL)
desc: A measure in dB of optical power reflected within a fiber optic pathway due to the fiber and optical components.

name: Opticals
desc: The effects created in a film lab through a process called A-roll and B-roll printing. This process involves a specified manipulation of the film negative to create a new negative containing an effect. The most common opticals used in film editing are fades, dissolves, and superimpositions.

name: optical ( s )( or optical effects )
desc: in film - making , refers to a visual device , e.g. ,a fade , wipe , dissolve , superimposition , freeze - frame , splitscreen , composite ( a train reflection in a car window ) , oranother effect , some of which can be created in the camera ,and others that have to be achieved in post - production bymixers or other specialized techniques

name: Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)
desc: An instrument in fiber optics measuring backscattered light to detect defects along a span of optical fiber.

name: Optimization
desc: A Stage In The Process Of Asset Development Where Assets Are Optimized For Real - Timeperformance While Maintaining High Visual Quality . See Also Level Of Detail And Performance .

name: Option Button
desc: Used to select from a list of related items. The selected option box has a black dot. (One item in the group must be selected.)

name: Option Drive
desc: Any internal drive other than the system disk. Option  drives include floppy disk drives, secondary hard disk drives, or DAT drives.

name: Orange Book
desc: The document begun in 1990 which specifies the format of recordable CD. Three parts define magneto-optical erasable (MO) and write-once (WO), dye-sublimation write-once (CD-R), and phase-change rewritable (CD-RW) discs. Orange Book added multisession capabilities to the CD-ROM XA format.

name: Orbit
desc: The rotation of the camera eye around the point of interest.

name: Orchestra
desc: (1) A Group Of Musicians Who Play Instrumental Selections.(2) The Portion Of The Auditorium On The Main Floor That Is Closest To The Musicians And The Acting Area.

name: Orchestra enclosure
desc: An on-stage enclosure of walls and ceiling that reflects sound into the auditorium for acoustic concerts. Usually decorative in appearance.

name: Orchestra Lift
desc: A Moving Platform That Is Used To Adjust The Elevation Of The Musicians In Relation To The Stage And Auditorium. Usually Operates Within The Confines Of An Orchestra Pit.

name: Orchestra Pit
desc: A Depressed Area Between The Stage And Audience Seating Area Where Musicians Sit, So The Audience Can Hear The Music And See The Performance Over The Heads Of The Musicians.

name: Orchestra Pit Filler
desc: Removable Platforms That Are Used To Close Off The Orchestra Pit At The Level Of The Stage  Or Auditorium.

name: Orchestra Pit Lift
desc: A Section Of The Orchestra Pit Floor That May Be Raised And Lowered By Some Mechanical (Typically Motorized) Means.

name: Orchestra pit net
desc: A detachable safety net placed over the orchestra pit to catch anyone or anything that might fall in.

name: Orchestra Shell
desc: An Enclosure On Stage, Consisting Of Walls And A Ceiling That Reflects Sound Into The Auditorium. Usually Decorative In Nature.

name: Orientation
desc: a) For animation, many 3D systems fix the viewer’s location at a specified distance from the viewing screen. Currently, PictureMaker is one of these. In such systems, the database is moved relative to the viewer. The set of motions that accomplish any particular view of the world is called its “orientation”. Using the three coordinate axes as references, we can translate (shuffle on a plane) and rotate objects to create new views. During animation, we change the amounts of these motions. A set of numbers describes orientation: x-trans, y-trans, z-trans, x-rot, y-rot, z-rot. b) A direction of presentation affecting resolution requirements. Horizontal lines become vertical lines when their orientation is rotated by 90 degrees; a pattern of dots appearing to be in horizontal and vertical rows may not appear to be diagonally aligned when its orientation is rotated 45 degrees due to characteristics of the human visual system.

name: Orientation Animation
desc: We can also use splines to calculate orienta- tions for objects in between their orientations at keyframe positions. This allows the motions of an object to be smooth rather than robot-like. In traditional animation, orientation animation required an artist to redraw  the object when it rotated out of the plane of the platen (on the animation stand) and path animation was limited to repositioning the cells in X and Y (although the whole scene could be zoomed). In computer graphics, it is easy to rotate and reposition objects anywhere in three dimensions. That is why you see so much of it!

name: Orientation Direction
desc: The arrangement of magnetic particles on recording tape. In tapes designed for quadraplex recording applications, the orientation direction is transverse. For helical and longitudinal recording, it is longitudinal.

name: Orientation Ratio
desc: In a material composed of oriented particles, the orientation ratio is the ratio of the residual flux density in the orientation direction to the residual flux density perpendicular to the orientation direction. The orientation ratio of conventional tapes is typically about 1.7.

name: Origin
desc: A reference point for measuring sections of recorded or digitized sample data. A file mob value for the start position in the media is expressed in relation to the origin. Although the same sample data can be re-recorded or re-digitized, and more sample data might be added, the origin remains the same so that composition source clips referencing it remain valid.

name: Original Negative
desc: The actual film stock used in the camera to photograph a scene.

name: original_network_id
desc: A unique identifier of a network.

name: Origination
desc: The production cycle begins with the introduction of images in photographic, electronic imaging, or computational media. Image capture in real-time is usually essential for recording live subjects and maintaining the impact of realism. Image generation, normally achieved in non real-time, provides additional subject matter that can be edited into and combined with recorded live subjects to achieve programs that are more artistic, or more instructional, or both.

name: Orthicon (Conventional)
desc: A camera tube in which a low-velocity electron beam scans a photoemissive mosaic on which the image is focused optically and which has electrical storage capability.

name: Orthicon Effect
desc: One or more of several image orthicon impairments that have been referred to as “Orthicon Effect” as follows: edge effect, meshbeat or Moiré, ghost, halo, burned in image. It is obviously necessary to indicate specifically the effects experienced and, therefore, it is recommended that use of this term be discontinued.

name: Orthicon (Image)
desc: A camera tube in which the optical image falls on a photo-emissive cathode which emits electrons that are focused on a target at high velocity. The target is canned from the rear by a low-velocity elec- tron beam. Return beam modulation is amplified by an electron multiplier to form an overall light-sensitive device.

name: Orthogonal Projection
desc: With orthogonal projection, parallel receding lines do not converge. The process of projecting from 3D to 2D is particularly simple, simply throw away the Z-value of each coordinate.

name: Orthogonal Sampling
desc: a) Sampling of a line of repetitive video signal in such a way that samples in each line are in the same horizontal position. b) Picture sampling arranged in horizontal rows and vertical columns.

name: Osborne, Joseph
desc: An ATV proponent issued a patent for a data compression transmission scheme for HD signals. The Osborne compression system is said to allow channel-compatible but not receiver-compatible HDTV.

name: OSC
desc: Open Sound Control. A network communication protocol used in the entertainment industry for communication between lighting consoles, sound systems, media servers, and other devices. Used for cue triggering and remote control.

name: Oscar bait
desc: Often used in a derogatory way to describe studio - inventedpre - release PR buzz that a film ( usually an epic or seriousbiopic released late in the year ) is worthy , meaningful , anddeserving of Oscar awards ; the term was reportedly first usedby Hedda Hopper in a " Looking at Hollywood " column onJune 1 , 1948 ; the term either refers to ( 1 ) a self - proclaimed ," important " , often over - produced film , undercut by its attemptto appeal to all demographics , or ( 2 ) a showy actingperformance designed to draw attention to itself ; these kindsof films and performances were the sort that used toguarantee an Oscar from Academy voters during the film industry's adolescent years of the 1950's and early 1960's ,but are now considered either pretentious and / or cheesy inthe modern age , and ironically often hurt the film's or actor'schances at winning an Oscar , though some films stillsucceed ; aka Oscarbation

name: Oscar ( s )
desc: the name given to the awards of AMPAS ( the Academy ofMotion Picture Arts and Sciences ) given each year to variousperformers and others in the film industry ; officially known asthe " Academy Award of Merit "

name: Oscillator
desc: A Device Used To Generate Pure Sound Waves At Specified Frequencies.

name: Oscilloscope
desc: An Instrument That Displays Graphic Representations Of Audio Signals.

name: OSC trigger
desc: A method of triggering lighting console events (cue go, playback activation, etc.) using OSC messages from external devices such as QLab, sound consoles, or show control systems.

name: OSD
desc: On-Screen Display. A menu system overlaid on a video output for device configuration and status. Found on displays, projectors, matrices, and AV processors. Navigated via front panel buttons or RS-232/IP commands.

name: OSI Model
desc: The model is similar in structure to that of SNA. It consists of seven architectural layers: the Physical Layer and Data Link Layer, the Network Layer; the Transport Layer; the Session Layer; the Presentation Layer; the Application Layer.

name: OSI (Open Systems Interconnection)
desc: The OSI Reference Model was formally initiated by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) in March, 1977, in response to the international need for an open set of communications standards. OSI’s objectives are: to provide an architectural reference point for developing standardized procedures; to allow inter-net- working between networks of the same type; to serve as a common frame- work for the development of services and protocols consistent with the OSI model; to expedite the offering of interoperable, multi-vendor products and services.

name: OTP
desc: See Opposite Track Path.

name: OTT
desc: Over-The-Top. Video content delivered directly to viewers over the internet, bypassing traditional cable or satellite TV distribution. Examples: Netflix, YouTube, Hulu.

name: OUI (Organizational Unique Identifier)
desc: The part of the MAC address that identifies the vendor of the network adapter. The OUI is the first three bytes of the six-byte field and is administered by the IEEE.

name: Outboard Equipment
desc: Audio Processing Devices That Live In Racks, Separate From The Console.

name: Outer Diameter
desc: Width of the disc. This is 12 cm for “normal” CDs and DVDs, and 8 cm for small CDs and DVDs.

name: Outlet
desc: A Female Connector.

name: Outlets
desc: Openings in the hardware to which you attach connectors to make an electrical connection.

name: Outline
desc: A type of key border effect. An outline key with a character generator appears as if the letters have been traced; the background video is visible all around the letter as well as inside it.

name: Out-of-Balance
desc: A Condition That Exists When The Weight Of A Batten, Fittings, And Attached Loads Do Not Equal That Of Counterbalancing Equipment Such As Counter Weight And An Arbor. For Safe And Efficient Use, Manually Operated Sets Should Be Balanced To Within 50 Pounds Of Neutral.

name: Out-of-Band Signaling
desc: A channel that is separate from the data chan- nel carries the signaling.

name: Out of Order Packet
desc: In computer networking, the delivery of data packets in a different order from which they were sent. Video decoders must account for out-of-order packets.

name: Out Of Phase
desc: Typically Referring To Two Coherent Signals Whose Frequency Dependent Delay Does Not Match In Some Region. Often Incorrectly Used To Indicate A Polarity Reversal, Even Though It Is Common To Hear It Used This Way In Production.

name: OUT Point
desc: The end point of an edit, or a mark on a clip indicating a transition point. Also called a Mark OUT. See also IN Point, Mark IN/OUT.

name: Output
desc: The magnitude of the reproduced signal voltage, usually meas- ured at the output of the reproduce amplifier. The output of an audio or instrumentation tape is normally specified in terms of the maximum output that can be obtained for a given amount of harmonic distortion, and is expressed in dB relative to the output that can be obtained from a reference tape under the same conditions.

name: Output Format
desc: The form in which video is presented by a video chip to monitoring or recording systems is called the output format. This can be RGB, YUV, YCRCB, etc.

name: Output Impedance
desc: The impedance a device presents to its load. The impedance measured at the output terminals of a transducer with the load disconnected and all impressed driving forces taken as zero.

name: Output Port
desc: Circuit that allows the microprocessor system to output signals to other devices.

name: Output Power
desc: In fiber optics, the radiant power expressed in watts.

name: Outrigger
desc: A Barrier Device That  Protects Counter Weight Arbors From Scenery, Etc. That May Be Leaned Against Them. Often Also Supports Index Lights.

name: Outriggers
desc: Sturdy Support Legs That Assist In Stabilizing Some Stands And Lifts. They Are Generally Removable Or Easily Folded Away To Assist In Transporting Or Maneuvering The Stand Or Lift.

name: Outside - In Tracking
desc: A Method Of Camera Tracking Which Uses Cameras Or Sensors Mounted On The Perimeter Of Avolume To Analyze The Position Of The Camera And Other Objects . See Also Inside - Out Tracking .

name: Outside Plant (OSP)
desc: In telecommunications, the portion of a network situated mainly outdoors, including patch panels, closures, pedestals, cabling, poles, towers, and repeaters.

name: Out-Take
desc: A take of a scene which is not used for printing or final assembly in editing.

name: out - takes
desc: refers to camera shots that are not included ( literally , they are' taken out ' ) in the final cut or print of a film , often retrievedfrom thecutting room floor , and shown during the closingcredits ; also seeblooper

name: Ovenized Crystal Oscillator
desc: A crystal oscillator that is surrounded by a temperature regulated heater (oven) to maintain a stable frequency in spite of external temperature variations.

name: overacting
desc: poor , overly - broad , or ' over - the - top ' acting by a ' ham ' actor ;aka " hamming it up " or ' chewing up the scenery ' ; sometimesconsidered in a positive light as ' campy ' ; contrastwith underacting

name: Overall Uniformity
desc: Ratio Of The Lowest To Highest Road Surface Luminance On A Set Of Grid Points.

name: Over Balance Bar System
desc: In This System The Curtain And  Counter Weight Are Balanced So That The Curtain Can Be Raised And Lowered Manually With A Minimum Of Effort.A Weighted Bar Is Held Above The Upper Batten Of The Fire Safety Curtain By A Hinged Mechanism. The Hinged Mechanism Opens When The Fire Line Is Released, So That The Weighted Bar Slides Down The Center Lift Lines To Rest On The Upper Batten. This Makes The Curtain Heavier Than The Counter Weight And Causes It To Close.

name: Overbooking
desc: In telecommunications, when a provider sells access to more customers than the network can support, causing inability for some customers to use the service.

name: Overcoat
desc: A thin layer of clear or dyed gelatin sometimes applied on top of the emulsion surface of a film to act as a filter layer or to protect the emulsion from abrasion during exposure and processing.

name: overcrank
desc: to speed up a camera - to shoot at more than the normal 24fps , so that the resulting image appears in slow - motion ; thistechnique is often used to shoot miniatures ; the term" cranking ' refers to the old technique of having to turn orcrank a camera by hand

name: Overcranking
desc: Shooting at a frame rate that exceeds the playback rate, resulting in slow motion when played back at standard speed.

name: overexposed
desc: refers to a film shot that has more light than normal , causing ablinding , washed - out , whitish , glaring effect ; deliberately usedfor flashbacked or dream scenes ; aka flared or bleached ; theopposite of underexposed

name: Overexposed Shot
desc: The Picture Appears Washed-Out Because Of Too Much Lighting. A Desert Scene In Which Light Shines Directly Into The Camera, Or When Something Explodes, Causing The Screen To Go Completely White Are Examples Of Overexposed Shots.

name: Overfilled Launch Condition (OFL)
desc: In fiber optics, a condition where incoming light has a spot size and numerical aperture larger than acceptable by the fiber. Associated with LED transmission and multimode cable.

name: Overflow
desc: Results when an arithmetic operation generates a quantity beyond the capacity of the register. An overflow status bit in the flag register is set if an operation causes an overflow.

name: Overhead
desc: Any data transferred on a communication link that is in addition to the content data. In IP networks: addressing, control, routing, redundant, error-checking, and error concealment data.

name: Overhead Bits
desc: Bits added to the binary message for the purpose of facilitating the transmission and recovery of the message (e.g., frame synchronization words, check bits, etc.)

name: overlap
desc: the carry - over of dialogue , sounds , or music from one sceneto another ; occurs when the cut in the soundtrack is not at thesame time as the cut in the image ; can also refer to two ormore characters speaking at the same time ; aka overlapsound

name: Overlap Edit
desc: An edit in which the audio and video signals are given separate IN points or OUT points, so the edit takes place with one signal preceding the other. This does not affect the audio and video synchronization. See also L-Cut, Delay Edit, or Split Edit.

name: Overlay
desc: Keyed insertion of one image into another. Overlay is used for example, to superimpose computer generated text on a video image, for titling purposes. In video, the overlay procedure requires synchronized sources for proper operation.

name: Override cuelist
desc: An ONYX cuelist type that takes priority over standard LTP cuelists when active. Used to create special effects or emergency states that override normal show programming.

name: Oversampled VBI Data
desc: See Raw VBI Data.

name: Oversampling
desc: Sampling Data At A Higher Rate Than Normal To Obtain More Accurate Results Or To Make It Easier To Sample.

name: Overscan
desc: a) Increases scanning amplitudes approximately 20%. Used for tube/yoke set-up and sometimes as a precaution against an edge of picture “raster burn”. b) A video monitor condition in which the raster extends slightly beyond the physical edges of the CRT screen, cutting off the outer edges of the picture.

name: Overshoot
desc: An excessive response to a unidirectional signal change. Sharp overshoots are sometimes referred to as “spikes”.

name: Over Speed Brake
desc: Any Brake That  Is Controlled To Recognize When The Speed Exceeds A Preset Threshold And Then Acts To Stop The Controlled Load.

name: Over The Shoulder
desc: Shot In A Dialog Scene When One Subject Is Photographed In Medium Close With Some Portion Of Another Subject Occupying Some Part Of The Foreground? Maintains The Connection Between The Two Characters. Pov ­ When The Camera Is Setup To Capture The Subject'S Perspective To Show The Audience What He/She Is Looking At Rule Of Thirds The Strategy Of Splitting Your Image Into Three Sections Vertically And Horizontally So You Have A Clear Foreground, Middleground And Background

name: over - the - shoulder( OTS ) shot
desc: a very commonly - used medium camera angle or view in adialogue scene , mostly with alternating shot / reverseshot editing , in which the camera records the action frombehind the shoulder and / or head of one of the characters ,thus framing the image ; the two characters are thus linked orconnected to each other , and their positions are established

name: Over-The-Shoulder Shot
desc: This Type Of Shot Is Used In Many Interviews. It Makes The Audience Feel They Are In The Room Where The Conversation Is Taking Place, Observing Or Eavesdropping Over Someone’S Shoulder.

name: Overtone
desc: A Component Of A Complex Tone Having A Frequency Higher Than The Fundamental.

name: overture
desc: in film terms , a pre - credits or opening credits musicalselection that sets the mood and theme for the upcoming film

name: Overwrite
desc: An edit in which existing video, audio or both is replaced by new material. See also Splice.

name: Overwrite Edit
desc: The addition of a source clip into a record clip, where the record clip edit sequence does not ripple (the duration does not change). The source clip overwrites an equal number of frames on the edit sequence.

name: Oxide Buildup
desc: The accumulation of oxide or, more generally, wear products in the form of deposits on the surface of heads and guides.

name: Oxide Coating
desc: The magnetic material coated on base film.

name: Oxide Loading
desc: A measure of the density with which oxide is packed into a coating. It is usually specified in terms of the weight of oxide per unit volume of the coating.

name: Oxide (Magnetic Oxide)
desc: The magnetizable particle used in the manufacture of magnetic tape.

name: Oxide Shed
desc: The loosening of particles of oxide from the tape coating during use.

name: ozoner
desc: slang term for a drive - in movie theater ; aka passion pit ; seealsohard - top ( indoor movie theater )

name: p.a.
desc: abbreviation for ' personal appearance ' - often required ofmajor stars - to promote or provide PR ( p.r. ) or ' publicrelations ' ( marketing ) for their films

name: pace
desc: the speed / tempo of the dramatic action , which is usuallyenhanced by the soundtrack and the speed of the dialogue ,the type of editing , etc.

name: Pacing (theatre)
desc: How fast or slow the action in a story moves, or the speed of the performance itself. Pacing can apply to individual scenes or the overall show.

name: Pack
desc: A layer in the MPEG system coding syntax for MPEG systems program streams. A pack consists of a pack header followed by zero or more packets. It is a layer in the system coding syntax.

name: package
desc: the marketing elements of a film project , such as script ,signed film stars , director , locations , ' high - concept ' hook , etc.

name: Packed 24-Bit
desc: A compression method where a graphics accelerator transfers more than one bit on each clock cycle, then reassembles the fragmented pixels. For example, some chips can transfer 8, 24-bit pixels in three clocks instead of the four normally required, saving bandwidth.

name: Packed Pixel
desc: Color information for a pixel packed into one word of memory data. For a system with few colors, this packed pixel may require only a part of one word of memory; for very elaborate systems, a packed pixel might be several words long. See Planar

name: Packet
desc: a) A unit of information sent across a (packet-switched) network. A packet generally contains the destination address as well as the data to be sent. b) A packet consists of a header followed by a number of contiguous bytes from an elementary data stream. It is a layer in the system coding syntax.

name: Packet Data
desc: Contiguous bytes of data from an elementary data stream present in the packet.

name: Packet Identifier (PID)
desc: a) MPEG-2 transmits transport stream data in packets of 188 bytes. At the start of each packet is a packet identifier (PID). Since the MPEG-2 data stream might be in multi-program mode, the receiver has to decide which packets are part of the current channel being watched and pass them onto the video decoder for further process- ing. Packets that aren’t part of the current channel are discarded. Four types of PIDs are typically used by receivers. The VPID is for the video stream and the APID is for the audio stream. Usually reference-timing data is embedded into the video stream, though occasionally a PCR (program clock reference) PID is used to synchronize the video and audio packets. The fourth PID is used for data such as the program guide and information about other frequencies that make up the total package. b) A unique integer value used to associate elementary streams of a program in a single- or multi-program transport stream.

name: Packet jitter
desc: The variability over time of packet latency across a network. In real-time applications like VoIP and video, variation in packet arrival rate causes quality degradation.

name: Packet loss
desc: Failure of network packets to reach their destination. Causes: congestion, buffer overflow, hardware failure. In AVoIP: produces visible artifacts, audio glitches, or dropouts. Target: <0.1% for AV streams.

name: Packet loss concealment
desc: Techniques in streaming protocols (SRT, Zixi, RIST) to recover from lost network packets and maintain stream continuity. Uses ARQ, FEC, or both.

name: Packets
desc: A term used in two contexts: in program streams, a packet is a unit that contains one or more presentation units; in transport streams, a packet is a small, fixed size data quantum.

name: Packet Switched Network
desc: Network that transmits data in units called packets. The packets can be routed individually over the best available network connection and reassembled to form a complete message at the destination.

name: Packet Switching
desc: The method of dividing data into individual packets with identification and address, and sending these packets through a switched network.

name: Packet Video
desc: The integration of video coding and channel coding to communicate video over a packetized communication channel. Usually these techniques are designed to work in the presence of high packet jitter and packet loss.

name: Packing Density
desc: The amount of digital information recorded along the length of a tape measured in bit per inch (bpi).

name: Pack Slip
desc: A lateral slip of select tape windings causing high or low spots (when viewed with tape reel laying flat on one side) in an otherwise smooth tape pack. Pack slip can cause subsequent edge damage when the tape is played, as it will unwind unevenly and may make contact with the tape reel flange.

name: Pad
desc: A Switch That Attenuates An Audio Signal At A Fixed Amount.

name: Padding
desc: A method to adjust the average length of an audio frame in time to the duration of the corresponding PCM samples, by continuously adding a slot to the audio frame.

name: Page
desc: Usually a block of 256 addresses. The lower eight bits of an address therefore specify the location within the page, while the upper eight bits specify the page.

name: Page (console)
desc: A layer of playback fader assignments on a console. Changing pages remaps the physical faders and buttons to different stored cues, effectively multiplying the number of playbacks available on the console hardware.

name: Painter’s Algorithm
desc: In traditional painting, paint is applied in layers, and the last paint applied is what is visible. Digitally, the last value placed in a pixel determines its color.

name: Paint Frame
desc: A Rigid Frame, Usually Made Of Wood, To Which Drops And Flats May Be Attached Vertically For Painting. They Are Normally Rigged To Be Raised And Lowered So Painters Can Reach All Areas Of The Frame.

name: Pairing
desc: A partial or complete failure of interlace in which the scanning lines of alternate fields do not fall exactly between one another but tend to fall (in pairs) one on top of the other.

name: PAL
desc: See Phase Alternate Line.

name: PAL 60
desc: This is a NTSC video signal that uses the PAL color subcarrier frequency (about 4.43 MHz) and PAL-type color modulation. It is a further adaptation of NTSC 4.43, modifying the color modulation in addition to changing the color subcarrier frequency. It was developed by JVC in the 1980s for use with their video disc players, hence the early name of “Disk-PAL”. There is a little-used variation, also called PAL 60, which is a PAL video signal that uses the NTSC color subcarrier frequency (about 3.58 MHz), and PAL-type color modulation.

name: PALE
desc: See Phase Alternating Line Encoding.

name: Palette
desc: In 8-Bit Images Or Displays, Only 256 Different Can Be Displayed At Any One Time. This Collection Of 256 Colors Is Called The Palette. In 8-Bit Environments, All Screen Elements Must Be Painted With The Colors Contained In The Palette. The 256-Color Combination Is Not Fixed-- Palettes Can And Do Change Frequently. But At Any One Time, Only 256 Colors Can Be Used To Describe All The Objects On The Screen Or Image.

name: Palette (console)
desc: A stored parameter setting for one or more fixtures (position, color, beam, intensity) that can be referenced in cues. Updating a palette automatically updates all cues that reference it.

name: Palette Flash
desc: A phenomenon caused by simultaneously displaying more than one bitmap or video that do not share the same palette.

name: PAL Format
desc: A color television format having 625 scan lines (rows) of resolution at 25 frames per second (25 Hz). See PAL. Compare NTSC Format.

name: PALplus, PAL+
desc: PALplus (ITU-R BT.1197) is 16:9 aspect ratio version of PAL, and is compatible with standard (B, D, G, H, I) PAL. Normal (B, D, G, H, I) PAL video signals have 576 active scan lines. If a film is broadcast, usually 432 or fewer active scan lines are used. PALplus uses these unused “black” scan lines for additional picture information. The PALplus decoder mixes it with the visible picture, resulting in a 16:9 picture with the full resolution of 576 active scan lines. Widescreen televisions without the PALplus decoder, and standard (B, D, G, H, I) PAL TVs, show a standard picture with about 432 active scan lines. PALplus is compatible with standard studio equipment. The number of pixels of a PALplus picture is the same as in (B, D, G, H, I) PAL, only the aspect ratio is different.

name: Pan
desc: Taken From The Word Panorama, The Camera Moves Horizontally (Left To Right Or Visa-Versa) To Take In A Whole Scene. Pan Slowly, Taking In The Whole Crowd To Search For The Person Who Called Out. Avoid Panning Quickly Unless For Effect. (See Whip Pan)

name: pan and scan
desc: a technique that avoids the ' letterboxing ' of a widescreen filmfor a full - framed 4x3 home video or TV picture , by focusing onthe elements of the picture that are most important to the plotand by adjusting or cropping the image ; when an importantpart of the image drops out of the visible screen , the picture ismechanically panned to the side ( left or right in a ping - pongeffect ) to show the missing part - hence , the term pan - andscan ; approximately 43 % of the visuals are sacrificed orcropped out in the pan - and - scan version , affecting thedirector's original intent and aesthetic sense

name: Pan and Scanner
desc: One Who Pans And Scans, Typically During A Live Event Originating In A Widescreen Format (16:9) But Simulcast In 4:3.

name: Pan and Tilt Head (P/T Head)
desc: A motorized unit permitting vertical and horizontal positioning of a camera and lens combination. Usually 24 V AC motors are used in such P/T heads, but also 110 VAC, i.e., 240 VAC units can be ordered.

name: Panel
desc: 1) A General Term That Can Refer To An Overhead, Butterfly, Or Large Framed Scrim, Diffuser, Or Reflector. 2) An Abridged Version Of Breaker Panel, Circuit Breaker Panel, Electrical Panel, Or Panelboard.

name: Panelboard
desc: A Piece Of Power Distribution Equipment Comprising A Box-Like Metal Enclosure With A Hinged Cover, Accessible Only From One Side, To Allow Access To Internally Mounted Circuit Breakers, Switches, And Fuses.

name: PanelLink
desc: Silicon Image's TMDS all-digital video transmission standard designed to provide bandwidth for digital displays.

name: Panel Memory
desc: See STAR system.

name: Pan (lighting)
desc: The horizontal rotational movement of a moving light fixture's mirror or head, controlled via DMX. Typically the primary horizontal position attribute of a moving fixture.

name: Pan-lock
desc: A tripod adjustment that locks the head to prevent side-to-side movement when panning is not needed.

name: pan( or panning shot ,orpanoramic shot )
desc: abbreviation for panorama shot ; refers to the horizontal scan ,movement , rotation or turning of the camera in one direction( to the right or left ) around a fixed axis while filming ; avariation is theswish pan ( also known as flash pan , flickpan , zip pan , blur pan , or whip pan ) , in which the camera ispurposely panned in either direction at a very fast pace ,creating the impression of a fast - moving horizontal blurring ofimages across the screen ; often confused witha dolly or tracking shot .

name: Pan, Pan Pot
desc: Short For Panoramic Potentiometer. A Pan Pot Is Used To Position (Or Even Dynamically Move) A Monaural Sound Source In A Stereo Mixing Field By Adjusting The Source’S Volume Between The Left And Right Channels. Our Brains Sense Ste_x0002_Reo Position By Hearing This Difference In Loud_x0002_Ness When The Sound Strikes Each Ear, Taking Into Account Time Delay, Spectrum, Ambient Reverberation And Other Cues

name: Pan Pot
desc: An electrical device which distributes a single signal between two or more channels or speakers.

name: Pan Shot
desc: Display Of A Scene While Moving About Any Axis. (See Also Dolly Shot And Track Shot.)

name: Pan Tilt Zoom (PTZ)
desc: A device that can be remotely controlled to provide both vertical and horizontal movement for a camera, with zoom.

name: Pantomime
desc: 1) The technique of expressing emotions and actions by gesture without speech. 2) A British theatrical tradition held around Christmastime featuring popular music, comedy, and audience participation.

name: Pan Unit
desc: A motorized unit permitting horizontal positioning of a camera.

name: Pan Vector
desc: Horizontal offset in video frame center position.

name: Paper
desc: Complimentary (I.E. Free!) Tickets. If Someone Says, "The House Is All Paper Tonight", It Means That Most, If Not All, Of The Audience Have Free Tickets.

name: Paper Edit
desc: Rough edit decision list made by screening original materials, but without actually performing edits.

name: Paper tech
desc: A planning session between the director, designers, and stage manager to walk through the script and establish technical cues on paper before entering the venue.

name: Paper the house
desc: The practice of distributing complimentary tickets to fill an otherwise empty or undersold house, giving the impression of full attendance.

name: PAP (Password Authentication Protocol)
desc: The most basic access control protocol for logging onto a network. A table of usernames and passwords is stored on a server. When users log on, their usernames and passwords are sent to the server for verification.

name: Par
desc: An Abridged Version Of Par Lamp, Par Can, Or Par Light.

name: Parabolic Aluminized Reflector Lamp (Par-Lamp)
desc: An Incandescent Or Tungsten-Halogen Incandescent Lamp With A Hard Glass Bulb And An Interior Reflecting Surface, A Precisely Placed Filament, And A Lens To Control Beam Spread. The Lens Is Hermetically  Sealed To The Reflector. Metal Halide Par-Lamps Are Also Now Available.

name: Parabolic Reflector
desc: A Reflector Designed To Align Light Rays Generally Parallel To The Axis Formed By The Point Source And The Center Of The Reflector, Eventually Resulting In A Cylindrical-To-Wide Beam. The Reflector Has The Shape Of A Paraboloid.

name: Parade
desc: This is a waveform monitor display mode in which the Y and two chrominance components of an analog component video are shown sided by side on the waveform screen.

name: Parallax
desc: The Perceptual Difference In An Object'S Position When Seen From Different Vantage Points .

name: Parallel
desc: One Transmission Path For Each Bit.

name: Parallel cable
desc: A Multi-Conductor Cable Carrying Simultaneous Transmission Of Digital Data Bits. Analogous To The Rows Of A Marching Band Passing A Review Point.

name: Parallel Component Digital
desc: This is the component signal sampling format specified by ITU-R BT.601-2 and the interface specified by ITU-R BT.656.

name: Parallel Composite Digital
desc: This is the composite signal sampling format specified in SMPTE 244M for NTSC. The EBU is working on the PAL standard. The composite signals are sampled at the rate of 4FSC which is 14.4 MHz for NTSC and 17.7 MHz for PAL.

name: Parallel data
desc: Transmission Of Data Bits In Groups Along A Collection Of Wires (Called A Bus). Analogous To The Rows Of A Marching Band Passing A Review Point. A Typical Parallel Bus May Accommodate Transmission Of One 8-, 16-, Or 32-Bit Byte At A Time.

name: Parallel Device
desc: Any hardware device that requires a parallel cable connection to communicate with a workstation.

name: Parallel Digital
desc: A digital video interface which uses twisted pair wiring and 25-pin D connectors to convey the bits of a digital video signal in parallel. There are various component and composite parallel digital video formats.

name: parallel( editing , action ,sound , etc. )
desc: editing that cuts between two sequences taking place atdifferent locations and possibly different times ; parallelaction refers to a narrative device in which two scenes areobserved in parallel by cross - cutting ; parallel sound refers tosound that matches the accompanying image ; aka crosscutting , inter - cutting

name: Parallel HDDR
desc: The recording of multiple PCM data streams which are synchronous to a common clock onto multitrack recorder/reproducers.

name: Parallel Interface
desc: A PC port which receives or transmits data in byte or word form rather than bit form.

name: Parallel Mono
desc: A Mode Of Operation For A Stereo Amplifier That Routes A Single Input To Both Channels, But Combines The Outputs Of Both Channels Into A Single Output By Strapping The Positivie Output Terminals Together, Thereby Providing Twice The Current Of An Individual Output.

name: Parallel Port
desc: An outlet on a workstation to which you connect external parallel devices.

name: Parallel Track Path (PTP)
desc: A variation of DVD dual-layer disc layout where readout begins at the center of the disc for both layers. Designed for separate programs (such as a widescreen and a pan & scan version on the same disc side) or programs with a variation on the second layer. Also most efficient for DVD-ROM random-access application. Contrast with OTP.

name: Parameter
desc: a) A variable which may take one of a large range of values. A variable which can take one of only two values is a flag and not a parameter. b) The values shown in X, Y and Z in each menu, so called because they represent the numerical values assigned to each feature of a video picture, size, aspect ratio, etc. Changing these values, shown in the “X, Y and Z” columns, produces ADO’s visual effects. c) A setting, level, condition or position, i.e., clip level, pattern position, system condi- tion. d) Value passed from one routine to another, either in a register or a memory location.

name: Parametric Audio Decoder
desc: A set of tools for representing and decoding audio (speech) signals coded at bit rates between 2 kbps and 6 kbps.

name: Parametric Eq
desc: A “Fully” Parametric Eq Is An Extremely Power_x0002_Ful Equalizer That Allows Smooth, Continuous, And Independent Control Of Each Of The Three Primary Eq Parameters: Frequency, Gain, And Bandwidth. “Semi” Parametric Eqs Allow Con Trol Of Fewer Parameters, Usually Frequency And Gain (I.E., They Have A Fixed Bandwidth, But Vari Able Center Frequency And Gain).

name: Parametric Equalizer
desc: Frequency Manipulation Device Which Allows 1. Control Over Frequency Levels (Boosting Or Cutting Decibels); 2. Selection Of The Center/Primary Frequency; 3. Manipulation Of Bandwidth/Range (Also Known As Q Or Quotient Of Change) Of Each Frequency

name: Parametric Modeling
desc: This method uses algebraic equations (usually polynomials) to define shapes and surfaces. The user can build and modify complex objects by combining and modifying simple algebraic primitive shapes.

name: Par Can
desc: See The Definition For Par Light.

name: Parental Level
desc: A mechanism that allows control over what viewers may see depending on the settings in the DVD player, the parental code on a DVD and the structure of the material on the DVD. This is especially useful for youthful viewers whose parents wish to exercise a degree of control over what their children can watch.

name: Parental Management
desc: An optional feature of DVD-Video that prohibits programs from being viewed or substitutes different scenes within a pro- gram depending on the parental level set in the player. Parental control requires that parental levels and additional material (if necessary) be encoded on the disc.

name: Parietal  Cortex
desc: An Area Of The Brain Adjacent And Above The Occipital Cortex, Thought To Process Spatial Location And Direction Information.

name: Parity
desc: a) An extra bit appended to a character as an accuracy check. For example, if parity is even, the sum of all 1s in the character should be even. b) Number of 1s in a word, which may be even or odd. When parity is used, an extra bit is used to force the number of 1s in the word (including the parity bit) to be even (even parity) or odd (odd parity).Parity is one of the simplest error detection techniques and will detect a single-bit failure.

name: Parity Clock
desc: A self-checking code employing binary digits in which the total number of 1s (or 0s) in each code expression is always even or always odd. A check may be made for even or odd parity as a means of detecting errors in the system.

name: Park
desc: An Eos function that locks a channel or DMX address at a specific level, preventing it from being changed by cue playback or other console operations. Parked channels are indicated in the display and must be unparked to resume normal control.

name: Park (Avolites)
desc: In Titan, a fixture that has been patched to a handle but has no DMX address assigned. Different from the 'park channel' function on other consoles (which locks a channel at a fixed value).

name: Parking Brake
desc: See “Holding Brake.”

name: Par Lamp
desc: See The Definition For Par Light.

name: Par Light
desc: A Generally Lightweight Luminaire That Utilizes A Par Lamp. The Beam Characteristics Depend On The Type Of Par Lamp Used.

name: parody
desc: a comedy that imitates or makes fun of an existing work ( s ) inan absurd , non - sensical way , and exaggerates itscharacteristics

name: Parsed Audiovisual Objects
desc: See Syntactic Decoded Audiovisual Objects.

name: Parsing
desc: Identifying and extracting syntactic entities related to coded representations from the bit stream and mapping them in semantic entities.

name: Parsing Layer
desc: See Syntactic Decoding Layer.

name: Parsing Script
desc: The description of the parsing procedure.

name: Partial Transport Stream (TS)
desc: Bitstream derived from an MPEG-2 TS by removing those TS packets that are not relevant to one particular selected program, or a number of selected programs.

name: Particle Orientation
desc: The process by which acicular particles are rotated so that their longest dimensions tend to lie parallel to one another.Orientation takes place in magnetic tape by a combination of the sheer force applied during the coating process and the application of a magnetic field to the coating while it is still fluid. Particle orientation increases the residual flux density and hence the output of a tape and improves perform- ance in several other ways.

name: Particles
desc: Refer to such vague objects as clouds, fire, water, sand, or snow that can be rendered using a special program.

name: Particle Shape
desc: The particles of gamma ferric oxide used in conventional magnetic tape are acicular, with a dimensional ratio of about 6:1.

name: Particle Size
desc: The physical dimensions of magnetic particles used in a magnetic tape.

name: Particle Velocity, U
desc: [Lt-1]; M/S---A Fluctuating Velocity Superimposed By The Presence Of Sound On The Other Velocities With The Particles Of The Medium May Have. In Analogy With Alternating Voltage Its Magnitude Can Be Expressed In Several Ways, Such As Instantaneous Particle Velocity Or Peak Particle Velocity. In Air, The Other Velocities Are Those Due To Thermal Agitation And Wind Currents.

name: Partition
desc: A subdivision of the total capacity of a storage disk that creates two or more virtual disks from a single physical disk. In the case of disk arrays, a partition is a virtual array within the whole array.

name: Part of Title (PTT)
desc: In DVD-Video, a division of a Title representing a scene. Also called a chapter. Parts of titles are numbered 1 to 99 in a One_Sequential_PGC Title and 1 to 999 in a Multi_PGC Title.

name: PASC (Precision Adaptive Sub-Band Coding)
desc: The PASC is very close to the Layer 1 subset in the MPEG audio specification. The algorithm, which is used in the DCC system from Phillips, provides a 384 kbit/s data stream.

name: Passive
desc: An Audio Device That Does Not Use Active Electronic Circuits.

name: Passive Absorber
desc: A Sound Absorber That Dissipates Sound Energy As Heat.

name: Passive crossover
desc: A crossover network dividing audio frequencies using only resistors, capacitors, and inductors — without active amplification or buffering.

name: Passive Infrared (Type Of Motion Detector)
desc: Passive Infrared Motion Detectors Sense The Motion Of Infrared Energy (Heat) Within A Space. A  Detector Is Located Behind An Infrared-Transmitting Lens, Which Is Usually Vertically Segmented With Multiple Smaller Lenses Etched Within Each Segment. This Lens Design Results In Horizontal And Vertical “Fan” Pattern Detection Zones. When A Passive Infrared Sensor Detects Motion From One Zone To Another, It Activates Whatever Device It Controls (Usually An Alarm System Or One Or More Luminaires).  See Also Motion Detector.

name: Passive Marker
desc: A Physical Reference For Tracking Purposes Such As A Retroreflective Spheres . Fiducial Passivemarkers Are Printed Glyphs Used For Image - Based Tracking .

name: Passive Optical Network (PON)
desc: A fiber optic network comprising singlemode fiber, passive splitters, and passive couplers for delivering fiber to home (FTTH) or building (FTTB).

name: Password
desc: A combination of letters and/or numbers that only the user knows. If you specify a password for your account or if you are assigned a password by the system administrator, you must type it after you type your login name before the system lets you access files and directories.

name: Past Reference Picture
desc: A past reference picture is a reference picture that occurs at an earlier time than the current picture in display order.

name: Pa System
desc: Abbreviation For Public Address System; Refers To An Audio System Used For Amplifying Music, Speech And Other Sounds For An Audience To Hear.

name: Patch
desc: To Make Electrical Connections On A Patch Panel, I.E., Hard Patching, Or, To Assign Dimmers To Channels On A Control Console, I.E., Soft Patching.

name: Patch Bay
desc: See The Definition For Patch Panel.

name: Patch cord
desc: A predetermined length of cable with connectors at both ends. Also known as a cable assembly, patch cable, or jumper.

name: Patch display (Eos)
desc: The Eos view showing all patched channels in the system with their DMX addresses, fixture types, and interface assignments. Accessed by double-pressing [Address/Patch] or through the Displays menu.

name: Patching
desc: The routing of audio or video from one channel or track in the sequence to another.

name: Patch (lighting)
desc: The assignment of DMX addresses to fixtures in a lighting console. Patch defines which DMX channel(s) a fixture responds to in a given universe, stored in the console's patch database.

name: Patch Panel
desc: A Large, Metal Cabinet That Comprises A Plurality Of Female Connectors Electrically Connected To Dimmers, And A Plurality Of Patch Cords For The Purpose Of Changing Around The Load(S) That Are Connected To The Dimmer(S). Some Patch Panels Use Parallel Bus Bars Electrically Connected To Dimmers, And Another Set Of Parallel Bus Bars Mounted 90° To The First Set And Electrically Connected To The Loads, And Slidable Connectors That Electrically Connect Any Bus Bar From One Set To Any Bus Bar From The Other Set.

name: Patch Panel (or Bay, Board, Rack)
