Wire, Electric Cable, And Other Electrical Such As Circuit Breakers, Terminal Blocks, Connectors, Etc., Are Some Of The Items Employed By Power Distribution Equipment.

name: Power Down
desc: To turn off the power switches on the workstation chassis and the monitor.

name: Powered Ethernet
desc: IEEE 802.3af standard providing power to network devices via existing Ethernet connection, eliminating need for external power supplies.

name: PowerLift™
desc: A J.R. Clancy Product. See “Zero Fleet Angle Winch.”

name: Power meter
desc: A device measuring the loss of optical power in a fiber optic connector, cable, or system.

name: Power-On Diagnostics
desc: A series of tests that automatically check hardware components of a system each time it is turned on.

name: Power (P)
desc: A General Term That Can Mean Heat, Candlepower, And/Or Wattage.

name: Power Rating
desc: The Electrical Output Of The Amplifier Module Based On A Set Of Test Parameters. There Are Many Different Kinds Of Power Ratings For Amplifiers And Speakers, Which Can Make Comparisons Difficult Unless You Identify The Ratings That Use The Same Test Parameters.

name: Power Reduction Factor
desc: A Factor Used In The Economics Chapter That Accounts For The Reduction In Power That Is Drawn By Lamps When They Are Dimmed To A Specified Level (Expressed As A Fraction Of Full Power), Or When They Are Operated By Multiple-Level Switching. The Power Reduction Factor Also Accounts For The Use Of Multiple-Level Lamps (E.G., 50-100-150  Watts) At Various Levels.

name: Power Spectral Density
desc: Power Spectral Density, Or Psd, Is A Method Of Scaling The Amplitude Axis Of Spectra Of Random Rather Than Deterministic Signals. Because A Random Signal Has Energy Spread Out Over A Frequency Band Rather Than Having Energy Concentrated At Specific Frequencies, It Is Not Meaningful To Speak Of Its Rms Value At Any Specific Frequency. It Only Makes Sense To Consider Its Amplitude Within A Fixed Frequency Band, Usually 1 Hz. Psd Is Defined In Terms Of Amplitude Squared Per Hz, And Is Thus Proportional To The Power Delivered By The Signal In A One-Hz Band.

name: Power Strip
desc: An Electrical Distribution Device That Has Multiple Edison Outlets And A Simple Fuse.

name: Power Supply
desc: A Unit Designed To Convert Alternating Current To The Direct Current Necessary To Power An Electronic Circuit Or Device.

name: Power Up
desc: To turn on the power switches on the workstation chassis and the monitor.

name: Power-Up Reset
desc: Initialization process whereby storage elements within a system are preset to defined conditions when power is first applied.

name: Power!Video
desc: This is an intra-frame video compression algorithm from Horizons Technology, Inc., dedicated to desktop computers, and providing playback without additional hardware. The Power!Video Pro version provides additional controls and settings.

name: PP
desc: See Peak to Peak.

name: PPI
desc: Pixels per Inch. A common unit of measurement for pixel density.

name: P-Picture (Predictive-Coded Picture)
desc: One of the three types of digital pictures in an MPEG data stream. A picture that is coded using motion compensated prediction from past reference pictures. The motion compen- sation is causal, that is, only based on preceding pictures, which can be I-pictures or P-pictures. This type of picture generally has more data than B-picture types.

name: PPM
desc: Peak Programme Meter. An audio level meter with fast ballistics that accurately captures signal peaks. Standard in European broadcast (EBU PPM) for preventing digital clipping. Faster response than VU meters.

name: PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)
desc: The most popular method for transport- ing IP packets over a serial link between the user and the ISP. Developed in 1994 by the IETF and superseding the SLIP protocol, PPP establishes the session between the user’s computer and the ISP using its own Link Control Protocol (LCP). PPP supports PAP, CHAP and other authentication protocols as well as compression and encryption.

name: PPS (Pulse Per Second)
desc: The basic repetition rate chosen as the common time reference for all instrumentation (usually 1 pulse per second (pps)).

name: PPT (PDH Path Termination) PPV
desc: See Pay-Per-View.

name: PQ
desc: Perceptual Quantizer. A video display EOTF optimized for HDR with maximum luminance of 10,000 nits. Developed by Dolby and standardized as SMPTE ST 2084.

name: PQ Information
desc: Information on the disc (or tape) that determines track start points, control bits, timing information, etc.

name: Practical
desc: Adjective Used To Describe Properties Or Scenery Which Have To Work As In Real Life When Used; E.G. A Practical Ceiling Light Must Actually Light Up When Switched On By An Actor.

name: PRBS
desc: See Pseudo Random Binary Sequence.

name: Preamp
desc: Preamplifier; Device That Amplifies The Weak Electrical Signals From Microphones And Pickups Used To Record Voices And Musical Instruments To Professional Line Level.

name: Preamplifier
desc: Also called a preamp. An electronic device that boosts extremely weak signal voltages from microphones or other sources to a usable level.

name: Pre - Cap
desc: A Pre - Production Motion Capture Session Used For Motion Studies And To Help Guide Previs .

name: Precision Time Protocol ( PTP )
desc: A Form Of Timecode With Sub - Microsecond Timing Accuracy . See Also Timecode .

name: pre - Code
desc: refers to the four - five years ( 1930-1934 ) before theenforcement of the Hays Production Code in Hollywood , torigidly sanitize and censor films . In film plots from mid - 1935and lasting about the next 30 years , adultery and promiscuitywere prohibited ( unless they ended in a miserable downfall ) ,and all crimes ( and their criminals ) had to be punished .

name: Pre-Command
desc: In DVD-Video a navigation command to be executed before the presentation of a Program Chain (PGC) has been started.

name: Precomputed Media
desc: A computed effect stored in a file and referenced by a composition or sequence. Applications can precompute effects that they cannot create during playback.

name: Predicted Pictures (P-Pictures or P-Frames)
desc: Pictures that are coded with respect to the nearest previous I- or P-picture. This technique is termed forward prediction. P-pictures provide more compression than I-pictures and serve as a reference for future P-pictures or B-pictures. P-pictures can propagate coding errors when P-pictures (or B-pictures) are predicted from prior P-pictures where the prediction is flawed.

name: Prediction
desc: a) The use of a predictor to provide an estimate of the pel/sample value or data element currently being decoded. b) Prediction of a picture (P or B) with indication of a motion vector.

name: Prediction Error
desc: The difference between the actual value of a pel/sample or data element and its predictor.

name: Predictive-Coded Picture
desc: A picture that is coded using motion compensated prediction from past reference pictures.

name: Predictive Coding
desc: Estimation of the sample currently being decoded from other previous (or future) samples.

name: Predictor
desc: A linear combination of previously decoded pel/sample values or data elements.

name: Preemphasis (Predistortion)
desc: A change in level of some frequency components of the signal with respect to the other frequency components at the input to a transmission system. The high frequency portion of the band is usually transmitted at a higher level than the low frequency portion of the band.

name: Preenhancement
desc: In many situations, video losses can be anticipated, allowing signal precompensation in a way that partially corrects for the losses. See Line Compensation.

name: Pre-Eq
desc: Refers To A Signal That Has Been Picked Off Before The Eq, But After The Preamp And High Pass Filter.

name: Pre-Fader
desc: On A Mixing Console, An Insert Or Send That Occurs Before The Fader In The Audio Signal Path.

name: Pre-fader send
desc: An aux send that taps the signal before the channel fader, so the send level is independent of the fader position. Used for monitor mixes (IEM, wedges) so the monitor mix is unaffected when the FOH engineer adjusts channel levels.

name: Pre-Focus Base
desc: A Cylindrical Shaped Lamp Base With A Flange Around The Top To Hold It Into Its Socket. The Flange Fits Into The Socket Only One Particular Way Such That The Filament Ends Up In A Predetermined Orientation, I.E., It Ends Up Pre-Focused. It Has One Contact On The Bottom And The Flange Acts As The Second Contact.

name: Preheat
desc: An Eos cue flag that causes incandescent or tungsten lamp fixtures to glow dimly (typically 5–10%) before being faded up to full, extending lamp life by reducing thermal shock from cold starts.

name: Prelay
desc: The phase of audio post-production during which music, sound effects, dialog replacement and announce tracks are added to the master multitrack before the final mix.

name: Pre - Light
desc: The Process Of Lighting A Scene Before The Main Production Unit Arrives In Order To Facilitatecomplex Setups And Maximize The Full Crew'S Efficiency . Can Apply To Physical Production Orto Prelighting Virtual Environments As They Are Developed .

name: Preload (console)
desc: A console function that pre-positions fixture LTP parameters (position, color, gobo) to their values in an upcoming cue while the fixtures are still dark (at zero intensity), preventing visible pre-show moves.

name: Premastering
desc: The process of formatting data into the exact form that will appear on a DVD, including file structure and file locations. A premas- tered product is ready to be mastered and replicated.

name: premiere
desc: the first official public screening of a movie , marking the kickoff , opening or opening night ; a ' red carpet ' premiere is onewith greater publicity and hoopla ( sensationalpromotion ) , ballyhoo , or hype ; aka a bow , debut , or preem

name: premise
desc: the main idea of a movie , usually explainable in a fewsentences

name: Preprocessing
desc: The video signal processing that occurs before MPEG encoding. Noise reduction, downsampling, cut-edit identification, and 3:2 pull-down identification are examples of preprocessing.

name: Pre - Production
desc: Any Planning , Testing , Visualization , Or Design Done Before Actual Production Begins .

name: prequel
desc: the second or third film in a series of films that presentscharacters and / or events that are chronologicallyset before the time frame of the original movie ; contrastto sequel

name: Pre-read
desc: See:  Read Before Write.

name: Prerecorded Tape
desc: A commercially available recorded tape.

name: Pre-Render
desc: When the computer runs content along a defined sequence that cannot be changed on the fly, like with real-time rendering. Since the content is pre-rendered, it will generally be of higher realism and detail.

name: Pre-Rigged Truss
desc: A Truss Section, Usually Provided With Wheels, That Has Lamp Bars Installed.

name: Pre-Roll
desc: a) The number of frames (or seconds and frames) between the cue point and the edit point which allows ACE to synchronize transports prior to an edit. b) The process of rewinding videotapes to a predetermined cue point (for example, six seconds) so the tapes are stabilized and up to speed when they reach the selected edit point (during recording or digitizing of source material from a video deck).

name: pre - screen
desc: to view / watch / see a movie before it is released for the public( at thepremiere )

name: Presence
desc: References The Enhanced High-Frequency Content Of A Sound, Allowing That Sound To Feel More Present In A Mix Without Necessarily Turning It Up.

name: Presentation Control Information (PCI)
desc: A DVD-Video data stream containing details of the timing and presentation of a program (aspect ratio, angle change, menu highlight and selection information, and so on). PCI and DSI together make up an overhead of about 1 Mbps.

name: Presentation Data
desc: Information, such as video or audio samples, which are presented at a specified time.

name: Presentation device
desc: A general term for a video projector, plasma display, presentation monitor, or other large format data display device.

name: Presentation Layer
desc: This MPEG-4 terminal layer encompasses the rendering layer and user interaction.

name: Presentation Time Stamp (PTS)
desc: a) A field that may be present in a PES packet header that indicates the time that a presentation unit is pre- sented in the system target decoder. b) An information unit whose seman- tic is the time at which the information should begin to be presented.

name: Presentation Unit (PU)
desc: A decoded audio access unit or a decoded picture.

name: Present Pattern
desc: a) An effect selected by the PST PTN push-button where a wipe pattern is used. The characteristics of the pattern are set using the pattern controls. If the effect is wiped on air over an existing on-air background, the wipe pattern will only move as far as the limit set by the vertical and horizontal limit controls. This is sometimes called a preset wipe or a wipe to a pattern limit. If the effect is mixed on-air, it is called a mix to a pattern limit. b) The ability to set both horizontal and vertical limits to the size a pattern will grow to when the fader is moved to the B bus. Ampex switchers can wipe to a preset size, mix in a pattern already at a preset size, and mix or wipe in keys with preset limits. Mixing in a key using preset patterns allows portions of the key to be masked off, and this is the mask key feature on Ampex switchers.

name: Preset
desc: 1) An Abridged Version Of Scene Preset. 2) To Have Something On A Control Console Set Up In Advance Of Need.

name: Preset Background Bus
desc: A row of crosspoint push-buttons used to select the video input that will be placed on-air during the next DSM background transition.

name: Preset Bus
desc: The line of push button switches on the control panel which select and indicate the next video that will appear when the DSK fader is pulled (AVC series in flip or flip-flop mode only). The idea behind the name is that this is a bus that allows one to pre-select (or preset) the next video.

name: Preset checklist
desc: A document used by stage management and technical teams to verify that all elements of the production are correctly set and in starting position before the performance begins.

name: Preset (Eos)
desc: An Eos data storage target that can contain any combination of intensity, focus, color, and beam data across multiple channels. Unlike palettes, presets are not category-specific and can store complete channel states. Referenced in cues.

name: Preset/Key Bus
desc: The line of push button switches on the control panel which select and indicate the preview output, and represents the next video that will appear when the DSK fader is pulled. It can also select and indicate key sources to other keyers due to the fact that it is a “split” bus. That is, reentries can be selected for the next video as well as bus inputs for a key source, both at the same time. This type of bus is exclusive to 4100 series switchers.

name: Preset (MA)
desc: In grandMA, a preset is a stored parameter value (color, position, beam, gobo) for one or more fixtures. Referenced in cues so that updating the preset automatically updates all cues that use it.

name: Preset Positioning
desc: A function of a pan and tilt unit, including the zoom lens, where a number of certain viewing positions can be stored in the system’s memory (usually this is in the PTZ site driver) and recalled when required, either upon an alarm trigger, programmed or manual recall.

name: Preset Wipe
desc: See Preset Pattern.

name: Pre-show
desc: The period before a performance begins when audiences enter and find their seats. Often includes music, a preset, or on-stage activity.

name: Presidential Teleprompter
desc: A teleprompter system with screens on the ground near a podium and angled glass plates in front of the speaker, making the script invisible to cameras.

name: PreSonus StudioLive
desc: PreSonus's digital mixing console range (StudioLive 16, 24, 32, 64S). Features AVB networking, built-in FLEX DSP effects, Fat Channel processing, and integration with Capture recording software and Studio One DAW.

name: Pressure Pad
desc: A device that forces tape into intimate contact with the head gap, usually by direct pressure at the head assembly.

name: Pressure Zone
desc: As Sound Waves Strike A Solid Surface, The Particle Velocity Is Zero At The Surface And The Pressure Is High, Thus Creating A High-Pressure Layer Near The Surface.

name: Pressure Zone Microphone (PZM)
desc: A microphone consisting of a metal plate and a small microphone element. The PZM collects and processes all sound waves that strike the metal plate.

name: preview
desc: a short film , usually with excerpts from a future film , intendedas an advertisement ; a sneak preview refers to anunadvertised , often surprise showing of an entire film beforeits general release or announced premiere , often to gaugeaudience reaction ; aka trailer

name: Preview Bus
desc: A processor function allowing the operator to select any incoming video source for viewing prior to actual use. Typically, each signal can be previewed on its own monitor. This is an effective method to check work before going “on the air”. The Digital Video Mixer includes a separate preview output which can be used to preview all four of its video input signals on-screen simultaneously.

name: Preview Code
desc: An additional reference numbering system, like key numbers, supported by film composer for comparing digital sequences with evolving work print versions using change lists.

name: Preview Key
desc: The ability to see how a key will appear, and the effect of all adjustments on that key, without having to put the key “on-air”.

name: Preview Monitor
desc: A video monitor which displays the picture from a video source. It is used to evaluate a video source before selecting it.

name: Preview Output
desc: The output of the switcher which allows you to observe an effect before it is placed on-air. Also called Look Ahead Preview. This includes previewing keys.

name: Previews
desc: Performances held before a show's official opening night, used to refine the production with a live audience. Ticket prices are often reduced for previews.

name: Previsualization
desc: A Collaborative Process That Generates Preliminary Versions Of Shots Or Sequences Using Avirtual Environment . It Enables Filmmakers To Visually Explore Creative Ideas , Plan Technicalsolutions , And Communicate A Shared Vision For Efficient Production . Also Known As Previs .

name: Primary Color Correction
desc: Color correction that applies to every part of a video image, or to every part of a video image that falls within a defined luminance range. See also Secondary Color Correction.

name: Primary Colors
desc: Colors In Terms Of Which All Other Colors May Be Described, Or From Which All Other Colors May Be Evolved By Mixtures. In Light, The Primary Colors Are Red, Green, And Blue. Combined In Pairs, Two Primary Colors Give The Complementary Color Of The Third. All Three Colors Combine To Form White Light.

name: Primary Distribution
desc: The links that feed the signals to the transmission sites, such as terrestrial transmitters, cable head-ends and satellite up- links, from the studio or “Play-Out Center”, often via a switching center.

name: Primary Inputs
desc: The eight video inputs applied to the Key, Program Background, and Preset Background buses.

name: Primary Matrix
desc: That portion of the crosspoint electronics associated with bus rows accessible from the switcher console. That is, the rows of buttons on the left side of a switcher which select the video inputs to the M/Es and including the program, preset (or line A/B) and PST/key bus row push buttons.

name: Primary Rate
desc: Primary rate (PRI) operates at 1.544 Mbps and consists of twenty-three 64 kbps B-channels and one 64 kbps D-channel. It is the ISDN equivalent of T1.

name: Primitives
desc: Refer to the most basic three-dimensional shapes, for exam- ple cubes, cylinders, cones, and spheres. From these you can build more complex 3D objects.

name: principal photography
desc: refers to the filming of major and significant portions of a filmproduction that involves the main / lead actors / actresses ;contrast tosecond - unit photography

name: Principal Point
desc: One of the two points that each lens has along the optical axis. The principal point closer to the imaging device (CCD chip in our case) is used as a reference point when measuring the focal length of a lens.

name: principals
desc: refers to the main characters in a play or film ( usually thosethat have dialogue ) ; contrasted to protagonists or antagonists ,orextras .

name: print
desc: refers to a positive copy of a film

name: Print Manager
desc: A tool accessed through either the System Toolchest or the System Manager that is used to set up printer software and monitor jobs that are sent to the printer.

name: Print-Thru
desc: The effect of signals being magnetically impressed on adja- cent portions of tape. This is the effect of magnetic induction and its cause can be excessive spooling or heat. Factors affecting spurious sprinting are principally heat, tape thickness and recording level and, to a lesser extent, time. Print-thru increases linearly with the logarithm of the time of contact, other factors being constant.

name: Print-to-Tape
desc: Outputting a digital video file for recording onto a videotape.

name: Print-to-Video
desc: A feature of Adobe Premiere that enables you to play a clip or the timeline centered on a monitor. If the clip or timeline is smaller than the full screen, it will play alone or on a black background. Print-to- video is useful for previewing the program in the timeline, for viewing source clips or individual files, or for video playback because it allows you to play a quarter screen video at full screen size. Some capture cards do not support print-to-video.

name: Priority
desc: Number assigned to an event or device that determines the order in which it will receive service if more than one request is made simultaneously.

name: Prism (Avolites)
desc: Avolites' media server platform integrated with Titan. Available as Prism, Prism Zero, and Prism Player for video playback, pixel mapping, and real-time media control synchronized with Titan lighting control.

name: Prism Convex
desc: Another Name For A Pebble Convex Spotlight.

name: Prism (lighting)
desc: An optical element in a moving light that splits the beam into multiple parallel beams for aerial effects. Available in different shapes (3-facet, 5-facet, linear) with DMX rotation control.

name: prison film
desc: a very popular sub - genre with the film's plot usually set withinthe walls of an institutional prison ; themes involveimprisonment and / or escape , the effects on the charactersinvolved and interactions between officers and inmates , andissues of justice / injustice ; the prison flick sub - genre can befound in any major genre ( animated , drama , comedy , musical ,science fiction , sexploitation , etc. )

name: Private Network
desc: A communication network owned by one or more firms for their exclusive use.

name: Proc Amp
desc: See Video Processing Amplifier.

name: Processed External Key
desc: Synonym for Isolated Key.

name: Processing Amplifier (or Proc Amp)
desc: A set of electronic circuitry used to insure that the video output signal of a switcher (or other video equip- ment) maintains proper levels and relationships and that sync and burst are clean and useable. The AVC series switcher comes with a limited proc amp as a standard feature. This proc amp can pass the video signal as it appears at the input, or strip the old sync and add a new sync pulse. It can also strip both sync and burst and add new sync and burst prior to the output of the switcher.

name: Process Objects
desc: A subclass of MPEG-4 objects that models processing operations (like decoding process, linear transformation, prediction, filtering) that can be applied on other MPEG-4 objects. These objects have an apply method taking parameters like AV objects or stream objects. They define processing operations used to modify other MPEG-4 objects.

name: Processor
desc: See System Processor

name: process( projection or shot )
desc: a technique that shoots live action in front of a screen onwhich the background view is projected ; a process shot refersto a shot of live action in front of a process projection

name: Process Shot
desc: A shot photographed specifically to be part of a special effects composite.

name: Producer
desc: In Amateur Companies Usually Synonymous With The Director, But In The Professional Theatre The Person Who Makes All The Necessary Arrangements For The Production To Be Put On: Finding The Finance, Smoothing The Way For The Director. Almost The Equivalent Of The Business Manager. The Best Producers (The Best Known Is Probably Cameron Mackintosh, Who Produced Les Miserables, Miss Saigon And Many Other Great Hits) Has The Genius To Recognise A Potentially Great Piece Of Theatre As Well As Find The Finance For It To Be Put On.

name: producer ( film )
desc: the chief of a movie production in all logistical matters ( i.e. ,scheduling , financing , budgeting ) save the creative efforts ofthe director ; raises funding and financing , acquires ordevelops a story , finalizes the script , hires key personnel forcast , crew , and director , and arranges for distributors of thefilm to theaters ; serves as the liaison between the financiersand the film - makers , while managing the production from startto finish .

name: Producer (theatre)
desc: The person or entity responsible for financing, organizing, and overseeing the creation of a theatrical production.

name: production
desc: the general process of putting a film together , includingcasting , set construction , costuming , rehearsals , andshooting ; also refers to themiddle stage of production which ispreceded by pre - productionand followed by post - production

name: production design
desc: refers to a film's overall design , continuity , visual look andcomposition ( colors , sets , costumes , scenery , props ,locations , etc. ) that are the responsibility of the productiondesigner ; the art department refers to the people in variousroles ( e.g. , matte painters , set designers and decorators ,illustrators , title designers , scenic artists ,and storyboard artists ) who work under the productiondesigner's supervision ; the art director is responsible for thefilm's physical settings ( specifically refers to the interiors ,landscapes , buildings , etc. )

name: Production Electrician
desc: The member of the electrics team responsible for technical preparation of the lighting rig — generating paperwork (circuit hookups, gel lists), ordering equipment, and preparing the rig for focusing.

name: Production, Electronic
desc: Performing one or more of the steps in the production sequence with the image information encoded in the electronic mode.

name: Production, Electronic, Digital
desc: The SMPTE Working Group on Digital Picture (H19.16) with initial focus upon non real-time digital representation of images, has been formed to develop standards and recommended practices with emphasis upon the production process. The SMPTE Task Force on Digital Image Architecture (ST13.20) has been formed to define further requirements for the exchange of digital pictures at various resolutions and across the interfaces with a variety of video, computer, and data media.

name: Production Manager
desc: In the UK, the person responsible for technical preparations including budgeting and scheduling of productions. Equivalent to the Technical Director role in the US.

name: Production Schedule
desc: A detailed plan of activities for a live event, film project, or service, including load-in details, rehearsals, and primary activities.

name: Production sound engineer
desc: Works alongside the Sound Designer to translate artistic ideas into equipment and install the audio system for a production.

name: Production Switcher
desc: A device that allows transitions between different video pictures. Also allows keying and matting (compositing). See Video Switcher.

name: Production System HDTV
desc: Production system HDTV is the analog of studio standard, HDTV, and addresses only a small part of what the  SMPTE Committee on Hybrid Technology (H19) considers production, and in fact only a small part of what they consider electronic production. Thus, in the context of SMPTE 240M, Television Signal Parameters 1125/60 High-Definition Production System, production has a much more restrictive definition that that employed by CCIR, or the SMPTE Committee on Hybrid Technology (H19).To illustrate by example from SMPTE 240M, the scope explains, this standard defines the basic characteristics of the video signals associated with origination equipment operating at the 1125/60 high-defi- nition television production system. It is, therefore, directed to the equip- ment that first encodes the image information into this electronic format, for example, the studio camera and its associated electronics.

name: production ( value )
desc: production refers to an entire movie project ; preproduction refers to the stage at which a film is prepared to gointo production ; post - production refers to the stage at whichediting , scoring and effects are executed on a motionpicture ; production value refers to the overall quality of a film ,based not on the script , acting , or director , but on criteria suchas costumes , sets , design , etc.

name: product placement
desc: refers to how companies buy advertising space within a filmfor their products , as a way for a producer to fund some filmproduction costs

name: Profile
desc: a) A defined subset of the syntax of a specification. b) Sub- division of video coding into different resolutions. c) Defines the amount of functions and compression processes involved. It is in other words a defined subset of the entire syntax, and limits the number of facilities, that may be used. For instance, a profile specifies the allowed scalability features.

name: Profile Alignment System (PAS)
desc: A fusion splicing technique using a CCD camera for precisely aligning the cores of two optical fibers.

name: Program
desc: PGM. The on-air output of the video system — what is currently being broadcast or displayed.

name: Program Access
desc: Prohibition on exclusive programming contracts between cable operators and program services controlled by cable operators, designed to give alternative multichannel distributors (such as wireless cable and DBS) the opportunity to bid for established cable services (such as CNN or Nickelodeon). The rule expired in 2002.

name: Program Background Bus
desc: A row of crosspoint push-buttons used to select the on-air background output of the switcher.

name: Program Bus
desc: a) Similar to the preview bus in concept except that the resulting output is the final signal which goes “on the air”. b) The line of push button switches on the control panel which select and indicate the video source of the switcher output on a flip or flip-flop style switcher.

name: Program Chain Information (PGCI)
desc: Data describing a chain of cells (grouped into programs) and their sector locations, thus composing a sequential program. PGCI data is contained in the PCI stream.

name: Program Chain (PGC)
desc: In DVD-Video, a collection of programs, or groups of cells, linked together to create a sequential presentation.

name: Program Clock Reference (PCR)
desc: A time stamp in the transport stream from which decoder timing is derived.

name: Program Counter (PC)
desc: Register in the CPU that holds the address of the next program word to be read. Branching requires loading of the jump address into the program counter. Otherwise, the PC is incremented after each word is read.

name: Program Delivery Control
desc: Information sent during the vertical blanking interval using teletext to control VCRs in Europe.

name: Program Element
desc: A generic term for one of the elementary streams or other data streams that may be included in the program.

name: Programmer (console)
desc: The area of a lighting console where show data is created and edited before being recorded into cues. The programmer takes priority over all active playbacks — whatever is in the programmer overrides the output.

name: Program Microphone
desc: A Microphone Mounted In A Venue To Capture Sound Of A Performance, Which Is Then Relayed Backstage For Performers To Monitor, Shared With Assisted Listening Devices, And/Or Recorded.

name: Programming Language
desc: A means of specifying an ordered group of instructions that a computer will execute.

name: Program Monitor
desc: The window in the Above Premiere interface that displays the edited program.

name: Program Nonduplication
desc: Under FCC rules, a cable system must black-out the programming of a distant television station it carries, when the system would duplicate a local station’s programming, on the request of the local station.

name: Program Output
desc: The on-air or final output of the switcher as selected on the program or line A/B bus and as keyed, mixed, or faded with the DSK.

name: Program (PGM)
desc: a) Procedure for solving a problem, coded into a form suitable for use by a computer. Frequently referred to as software. b) A collection of program elements. Program elements may be elementary streams. Program elements need not have any defined time base; those that do have a common time base and are intended for synchronized presentation. c) A concatenation of one or more events under the control  of a broadcaster, e.g., news show, entertainment show.

name: Program Specific Information (PSI)
desc: Normative data which is necessary for the demultiplexing of transport streams and the successful regeneration of programs.

name: Program Stream
desc: a) A bit stream containing compressed video, audio, and timing information. b) Multiplex of several audio and video PES using the same clock. c) Combines one or more packetized elementary streams (PES), which have a common time base into a single stream. The program stream was designed for use in relatively error-free environments, and is suitable for applications which may involve software processing. Program stream packets may be of variable length.

name: Progressive
desc: Short For Progressive Scanning. A System Of Video Scanning Whereby Lines Of A Picture Are Transmitted Consecutively, Such As In The Computer World.

name: Progressive Media
desc: Media composed of single frames, each of which is vertically scanned as one pass.

name: Progressive Picture
desc: Represents sequential scanning of all the lines in the picture. Also called Noninterlaced Picture.

name: Progressive Scan
desc: See Noninterlaced Scan.

name: Progressive Sequence
desc: Sequence of pictures, that all are frame pictures with frame DCT coding.

name: Project
desc: A data device used to organize the work done on a program or series of programs. Bins, rundowns and settings are organized in the project window. The project bins contain all your clips, sequences, effects and media file pointers.

name: Projecting Lens
desc: The Lens In An Optical System That Converges Light Rays Sufficiently To Bring Them To A Clear, Defined Focus.

name: Projection
desc: When a database is visualized, it is “projected” from 3D into 2D (the screen). Two kinds of projection are used, projection and orthogonal.

name: Projection design
desc: The art of creating and implementing projected imagery, video content, and multimedia elements as a scenic or atmospheric element in theatrical production.

name: Projection Lens
desc: See The Definition For Projecting Lens.

name: Projection Mapping
desc: A Method For Warping And Conforming Content Onto A Surface . Projection Mapping Can Beused To Map Content Onto The Geometry Of An Led Volume . Especially For Complex Shapeslike Curves And Multiple Surfaces .

name: Projection (technical)
desc: In technical theatre, throwing an image onto a stage surface by means of a projector for optical or design effect.

name: Projection (voice)
desc: The technique of making the voice louder and more resonant so it can be heard clearly throughout an auditorium without electronic amplification.

name: projector
desc: the machine that rapidly puts ( ' projects ' ) a succession ofmotion picture images ( individual frames ) up onto a screen ,using the principle of illusion of motion

name: Project Preset
desc: A predetermined list of settings for a project. Certified capture cards usually include presets that work with Adobe Premiere.

name: Project Settings
desc: All the items needed for Adobe Premiere to work properly  with video and audio clips.

name: prologue
desc: a speech , preface , introduction , or brief scene preceding thethe main action or plot of a film ; contrast to epilogue .

name: PROM
desc: Programmable Read Only Memory. Non-volatile electronic memory that can be reprogrammed using a special PROM programmer.

name: PROM Monitor
desc: The interface used to communicate with the system after it is powered up, but before it is booted up and running IRIX.

name: promo
desc: slang term for sales promotion

name: PROM (Programmable Read-Only Memory)
desc: Integrated circuit memory that is manufactured with a pattern of all logical 0s and 1s and has a specified pattern written into it by a special hardware programmer.

name: Prompt
desc: A character or word that the system displays that indicates the system is ready to accept commands.

name: Prompt book
desc: The master copy of the script annotated with all technical, lighting, sound, and fly cues. The definitive record of how the show is run. Also called the book.

name: Prompt Copy
desc: See Book. The Copy Of The Script N Which All Notes, Moves, Cues Etc. Are Noted.

name: Prompt corner
desc: The position, usually stage left, from which the DSM or Stage Manager calls the show during a performance. Contains the prompt book, headset comms, and cue lights.

name: Prompter
desc: Amateur Companies Almost Always Have A Prompter, Someone Who Sits In The Wings (Qv) And Prompts The Actors If They Forget Their Lines.There Is No Such Position In The Professional Theatre - Pros Should Not Forget Lines! Neither Should Amateurs, For That Matter, But It Happens - So, If A Prompt Is Needed, It Is Given By Whoever Is "In The Corner" (Qv).

name: Prompt (Side)
desc: The Left Side Of The Stage, As You Face The Audience.

name: Prop
desc: A Physical Or Virtual Item Which Can Be Interacted With .

name: Propagation Delay
desc: The time it takes for a signal to travel through a circuit, piece of equipment, or a length of cable. When the luminance and color information of a video signal are separated for processing, then reunited at the output of a device, it is critical that the propagation delay for each signal component is equal or distortion similar to ghosting will result. Propagation delay is most noticeable in color-under VHS players. Propagation delay is also a problem when routing computer data and clock signals around a chip or circuit board. The faster the clock, the more critical the path delays.

name: Properties
desc: Small Items (A Sword In An Historical Play, For Instance, Or A Briefcase) Which Actors Carry Onto Or Around The Stage. Also Used Loosely For "Set Dressing" (Qv). Usually Abbreviated To Props.

name: Property Master
desc: (Or Mistress) Responsible For The Obtaining And/Or Construction Of The Properties (Qv).

name: Proportion
desc: An Eos mathematical modifier for recorded intensity levels, set numerically from 0% to 200%. At 100%, output equals the recorded level. At 50%, output is half the recorded level. Used for scaling entire cue looks up or down.

name: Props
desc: Short for properties. Furnishings, set dressings, and all items that cannot be classified as scenery, electrics, or wardrobe. Hand props are handled by actors; personal props are kept in an actor's costume.

name: props( or property )
desc: abbreviation for properties - refers to the furnishings , fixtures ,hand - held objects , decorations , or any other moveable itemsthat are seen or used on a film ( or stage ) set but that are nota structural part of the set ; usually the responsibility ofthe prop man or property master .

name: Proscenium
desc: The Architectural Opening On A Stage Through Which The Audience Views The Performance.

name: Proscenium Arch
desc: The Opening In The Proscenium Through Which The Audience Views A Performance.

name: Proscenium theatre
desc: The most common theatre configuration, with the audience facing a framed stage opening (the proscenium arch). The stage is separated from the audience by the fourth wall.

name: Proshare
desc: A video conferencing video system by Intel which adapts PCs using added circuit boards, to video conferencing. The Proshare system is based on H.320 recommendations for audio and video teleconferencing.

name: Protagone
desc: The protagonist or main character of a dramatic work — the character whose story drives the narrative.

name: protagonist
desc: the lead or main character in a film ; also knownashero / heroine ; contrast to antagonist .

name: Protection Layer Entity (PL Entity)
desc: An instance of the MPEG-4 systems resource that processes PL-PDUs associated to a single TransMux channel.

name: Protection Layer (PL)
desc: A logical sub-layer of the TransMux Layer to adapt FlexMux stream data for insertion into TransMux-PDUs. One Protection Layer provides for tools such as error protection tools and error detection tools, automatic retransmission tools and framing tools.

name: Protection Layer Protocol Data Unit Header (PL-PDU Header)
desc: Optional information preceding the PL-PDU payload. It is used for error detection, error correction, framing of the PL-PDUs payload. The format of the PL-PDU header is determined when opening the associated TransMux channel.

name: Protection Layer Protocol Data Unit Payload (PL-PDU Payload)
desc: The data field of the PL-PDU.

name: Protection Layer Protocol Data Unit (PL-PDU)
desc: The smallest protocol unit exchanges between peer PL entities. It consists of PL-PDU header and PL-PDU payload. PL-PDUs with data from one or more FlexMux streams form the payload of TransMux-PDUs.

name: Protection Layer Service Data Unit (PL-SDU)
desc: A logical information unit whose integrity is preserved in transfer from one Protection Layer user to the peer Protection Layer user.

name: Protection Layer User (PL User)
desc: An MPEG-4 systems entity that makes use of the services of the Protection Layer, typically a FlexMux entity.

name: Protection Master
desc: A copy (dub) of a master tape, usually made immediately after the master has been recorded. It is used as a backup if the master is damaged.

name: Protective Master
desc: A master positive from which a dupe negative can be made if the original is damaged.

name: Protocol
desc: a) Set of syntax rules defining exchange of data including items such as timing, format, sequencing, error checking, etc. b) A specific set of rules, procedures or conventions relating to format and timing of data transmission between two devices. A standard procedure that two data devices must accept and use to be able to understand each other.The protocols for data communications cover such things as framing, error handling, transparency and line control.

name: Protocol Data Unit (PDU)
desc: A unit of information exchanged between peer Protocol Layer entities.

name: Protocol Independent Multicasting
desc: A set of multicast routing protocols for IP networks facilitating one-to-many and many-to-many data distribution over LAN, WAN, or the Internet.

name: Provider
desc: A software layer that provides services to other layers. A provider may or may not involve dedicated hardware.

name: Proximity Effect
desc: The Property Of Many Directional Microphones To Accentuate Their Bass Response When The Source-To-Mic Distance Is Small, Typically Three Inches Or Less. Singers Generally Like This Effect Even More Than Singing In The Shower.

name: Proxy
desc: A scaled-down version of an image used to display clips. It includes controls that mimic a VTR.

name: PS
desc: See Program Stream.

name: PSD
desc: Playback Status Display. Also called the Cue List. On Eos Family consoles, the display showing the current cue list with cue numbers, names, timing, and status flags (Block, Assert, Mark, Preheat, Live moves, Dark moves).

name: Pseudo-Color
desc: A color relationship scheme in which a color table contains available color values, and an index into this table is used to refer to a color. If a desired color is not found in the table, it may be matched to the closest available entry or an existing entry may be overwritten.

name: Pseudo-Instruction
desc: Instruction that is used in an assembly language program but is an instruction for the assembler. Pseudo-instructions have no direct correspondence to machine language.

name: Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS)
desc: A random sequence of bits which repeat after 2n -1.

name: Pseudorandom noise
desc: Noise satisfying standard statistical randomness tests. Appears random but consists of a pulse sequence that eventually repeats.

name: Pseudo-Random Sequences/Patterns
desc: Certain systems described in these standards employ feedback shift registers to modify sequences or patterns of bits in a predetermined manner or to restore such modified bit patterns to their original sequence. With outputs of suitably selected stages added modulo-2 and applied to its feedback loop, an n-stage feedback shift register will generate a bit sequence or pattern (2n-1) bits long before repeating. Because such repeating sequences exhibit many of the statisti- cal properties of uniformly distributed random number sequences (e.g., their probability density and autocorrelation functions satisfy appropriate conditions),they are called pseudo-random.

name: PSIP
desc: Program And System Information Protocol. A Part Of The Atsc Digital Television Specification That Enables A Dtv Receiver To Identify Program Information From The Station And Use It To Create Easy-To-Recognize Electronic Program Guides For The Viewer At Home. The Psip Generator Insert Data Related To Channel Selection And Electronic Program Guides Into The Atsc Mpeg Transport Stream.See Also: Electronic Program Guide.

name: PSIP (Program and System Information Protocol)
desc: A part of the ATSC digital television specification that enables a DTV receiver to identify program information from the station and use it to create easy-to-recog- nize electronic program guides for the viewer at home. The PSIP generator inserts data related to channel selection and electronic program guides into the ATSC MPEG transport stream.

name: PSI (Program Specific Information)
desc: a) Information that keeps track of the different programs in an MPEG transport stream and in the elementary streams in each program. PSI includes: PAT, PMT, NIT, CAT, ECM, and  EMM. b) Normative data necessary for the demultiplexing of transport streams and the regeneration of programs.

name: PSI/SI
desc: A general term for combined MPEG PSI and DVB-SI.

name: PSK (Phase Shift Keying)
desc: Phase shift keying (PSK) is a method of transmitting and receiving digital signals in which the phase of a transmit- ted signal is varied to convey information.

name: PSNR (Peak Signal to Noise Ratio)
desc: A measure for image quality.

name: PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network)
desc: The worldwide voice telephone network. Once only an analog system, the heart of most tele- phone networks today is all digital. In the U.S., most of the remaining analog lines are the ones from your house or office to the telephone company’s central office (CO). Also know as POTS.

name: PSW (Pan and Scan Window)
desc: For automatic pan and scan mode, the video is unsqueezed to 16:9 and a portion of the image is shown at full height on a 4:3 screen by following a ‘center of interest’ offset that's encoded in the video stream according to the preferences of the people who transferred the film to video. The pan and scan window is 75% of the full width, which reduces the horizontal pixels from 720 to 540.

name: Psycho-Acoustic Model
desc: A mathematical model for the masking effects of the human auditory system.

name: Psychoacoustics
desc: The Study Of The Interaction Of The Auditory System And Acoustics.

name: PTP
desc: Precision Time Protocol (IEEE 1588). Synchronizes clocks across a network to sub-microsecond accuracy. Essential for AES67 audio and SMPTE ST 2110 video to ensure sample-accurate synchronization across all endpoints.

name: PTS (Presentation Time Stamp)
desc: a) The time at which a presentation unit is to be available to the viewer. b) Time stamp for vision and sound integrated into PES, transmitted at least once every 0.7 sec.

name: PTT Menu
desc: In DVD-Video, a menu used to access specific Part of Title (PTT) in a Video Title Set (VTS). Usually referred to as a Chapter Menu.

name: PTV
desc: See Public Television.

name: PTZ Camera
desc: Pan, tilt and zoom camera.

name: PTZ Site Driver (or Receiver or Decoder)
desc: An electronic device, usually a part of a video matrix switcher, which receives digital, encoded control signals in order to operate pan, tilt, zoom and focus functions.

name: Public Access
desc: To ensure that divergent community opinion is aired on cable television, FCC rules require systems in the top 100 markets to set aside one public access channel along with the education and government channels. The public access channel is free and available at all times on a first-come, first-served basis for noncommercial use by the general public.

name: Public Network
desc: A network established and operated by a telecommunications provider for public data transmission services. The Internet is a public network.

name: Public Television
desc: Television stations and networks that operate as non-commercial ventures.

name: Puck
desc: Another name for a capstan idler.

name: PUH (Pickup Head)
desc: The assembly of optics and electronics that reads data from a disc.

name: Pull
desc: A camera director's instruction to slowly zoom the camera out for a widening effect.

name: Pull-Cord
desc: A String Or Chain That Is Attached To A Switch That Is Mounted In A Luminaire. Pull Cords Typically Are Used To Control Individual Ceiling- Mounted Luminaires, As In An Attic Or Basement.

name: Pull-Data Model
desc: Flexible architecture. A scene is described by a Java class, which is responsible for retrieving bits from the bitstream. See Push-Data Model.

name: Pull-Down
desc: Technique the eliminates redundant frames when converting film material (24 fps) into NTSC (30 fps).

name: Pull-Down Phase
desc: In a project based on an NTSC 24-fps to 30 fps transfer, the video frame at which a master clip starts: A, B, X, C or D. The pull-down phase represents the pull-down to timecode relationship.

name: Pull-In
desc: An Avid term that combines two words – Pull-Down and IN Point. The pull-in is the column where the user logs the pull-down phase of the start timecode as either A, B, X, C or D. The user can modify this field before or after digitizing or recording

name: Pull-Out
desc: An Avid term that combines two words – Pull-Down and OUT Point. The pull-out is the column where the user logs the pull-down relationship at the sync point of the OUT point (end timecode) as either A, B, C or D. This field cannot be modified by the user and is calculated by the system based on the pull-in and the duration of the clip.

name: Pull (theatre)
desc: To take items out of storage (stock) for use in a production, with or without alterations. Pulling from stock refers to scenery, props, or costumes.

name: Pull-Up Resistor
desc: Used to provide the source current for open-collector and three-state logic gates or a termination for unused inputs. Pulls the voltage level up when no other device is driving the line.

name: Pulse
desc: A current or voltage that changes abruptly from one value to another and back to the original value in a finite length of time. Used to describe one particular variation in a series of wave motions.

name: Pulse-Bar Inequality
desc: Kpulse/bar=1/4 | (barpulse)/pulse | X 100%

name: Pulse broadening
desc: An increase in the duration of a pulse.

name: Pulse code modulation
desc: A method converting analog signal to digital data by taking 8-bit samples at 8000 times/second over a 4 kHz bandwidth, yielding 16K data/second.

name: Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)
desc: a) Coding where analog input signal is represented by a given number of fixed-width digital samples per second. Often used for the coding employed in the telephone network. b) A technical term for an analog source waveform, for example, audio or video signals, expressed as periodic, numerical samples. PCM is an uncompressed digital signal. c) This is a form of the digital audio signal used for both CD and laserdisc. It is a serial data stream that is coded for transmission or recording. PCM is also used for many other types of serial data communications.

name: Pulse Distribution Amplifier
desc: An amplifier that boosts sync strength and other control signals to the correct level required for distribution to multiple cameras, special effects generators or other equipment.

name: Pulser
desc: See Logic Pulser.

name: Pulse spreading
desc: The dispersion of an optical signal as it traverses an optical fiber. Also called Pulse Dispersion.

name: Pulse-to-Bar Ratio
desc: (pulse/bar) X 100%

name: Pulse to Bar Ratios
desc: The amplitude ratio between a 2T pulse and a line bar is sometimes used as an indication of short time distortion. The results of this measurement can be described in units of K-2T or K-PB.

name: Pulse width
desc: The time during which a source, such as a laser, is in an ON state.

name: Pulse width modulation
desc: A technique for controlling analog circuits with digital outputs. Used in high-power switching amplifiers to reduce system costs and power consumption.

name: Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)
desc: PWM is a way of digitally encoding analog signal levels, which allows for digital control of analog circuits. This, in turn, helps to reduce cost, size, heat, and power consumption in devices such as consumer audio hardware.

name: Punch Light
desc: A High Intensity Luminaire That Floods An Area With Light Whose Color Temperature Is Approximately That Of Daylight.

name: punchline
desc: a funny , witty line that culminates a story , joke or scene ;contrast with payoff and one - liner

name: PU (Presentation Unit)
desc: a) One compressed picture or block of audio. b) Decoded AAU or a decoded picture.

name: Purchase Line
desc: See “Hand Line.”

name: PURE3 Codec
desc: A codec encoding and streaming both video and computer graphic inputs at various resolutions. Balances low latency, low bandwidth, and high image quality. Optimized for lossy IP networks with built-in error concealment.

name: Pure Tone
desc: A Tone With No Harmonics. All Energy Is Concentrated At A Single Frequency.

name: Push
desc: Operation of adding a word to the stack.

name: Push-Data Model
desc: Non-flexible, bitstream driven architecture. The decoder reacts to explicit “instructions” in the bitstream. See Pull-Data Model.

name: Push-Down Stack
desc: See Stack.

name: PVC
desc: Poly Vinyl Chloride. Used for insulation of wires and jacketing of most non-plenum cables.

name: P-vop (Predictive-coded VOP)
desc: A picture that is coded using motion compensated prediction from the past vop.

name: PVW
desc: See Preview.

name: PX64
desc: Similar to MPEG, but adapted to slower bit rates. Typically used for video conferencing over one or more ISDN lines.

name: Pyropot
desc: A Safe Container Into Which A Pyro (See Pyrotechnics) Charge Is Plugged For Firing.

name: Pyrotechnics
desc: Usually Abbreviated To "Pyro". The Use Of Explosions, Flashes, Smoke, Etc. On-Stage.

name: Pzm
desc: Abbreviation For Pressure Zone Microphone; A Type Of Condenser Microphone That Uses A Small Capsule Which Is Mounted Close To A Backing Plate. Pzms Are Often Placed Face Down Onto A Hard Surface Which Transmits The Vibrations It Picks Up.

name: Q
desc: A Way Of Stating The Bandwidth Of A Filter Or Equalizer Section. An Eq With A Q Of .75 Is Broad And Smooth, While A Q Of 10 Gives A Nar_x0002_Row, Pointed Response Curve. To Calculate The Value Of Q, You Must Know The Center Fre_x0002_Quency Of The Eq Section And The Frequencies At Which The Upper And Lower Skirts Fall 3 Db Below The Level Of The Center Frequency. Q Equals The Center Frequency Divided By The Dif_x0002_Ference Between The Upper And Lower 3 Db_x0002_Down Frequencies. A Peaking Eq Centered At 10Khz Whose -3 Db Points Are 7.5 Khz And 12.5 Khz Has A Q Of 2.

name: Q-1
desc: See Inverse Quantization.

name: Q.2931
desc: An ITU signaling protocol for access to B-ISDN/ATM.

name: QAM
desc: See Quadrature Amplitude Modulation.

name: QCIF
desc: See Quarter Common Interface Format.

name: QEF (Quasi Error Free)
desc: Less than one uncorrected error per hour at the input of the MPEG-2 decoder.

name: QE (Quadrature Error)
desc: Quadrature error is the phase error when the specified phase relationship between any two channels is nominally 90 electrical degrees.

name: QEV (Quadrature Error Vector) QoS
desc: See Quality of Service.

name: Q-factor
desc: Quality factor. A measure of the quality of a resonant system. Resonant systems respond to frequencies close to the natural frequency more strongly. On a response vs. frequency graph, bandwidth is defined as the 3 dB change besides the center frequency.

name: QoS
desc: Quality of Service. Network mechanisms prioritizing time-sensitive traffic (AV streams, VoIP) over best-effort data. Implemented via DiffServ (DSCP marking) at Layer 3 and 802.1p at Layer 2. Critical for reliable AVoIP.

name: QoS (Quality of Service)
desc: a) Bandwidth and management process to meet an application’s requirements for time sensitive and correct delivery of information. b) The performance that an elementary stream requests from the delivery channel through which it is transported, characterized by a set of parameters (e.g., bit rate, delay jitter, bit error rate).

name: QPSK
desc: Quadrature Phase Shift Keying. Qpsk Is A Digital Frequency Modulation Technique Used For Sending Data Over Coaxial Cable Networks. Since It'S Both Easy To Implement And Fairly Resistant To Noise, Qpsk Is Used Primarily For Sending Data From The Cable Subscriber Upstream To The Internet.

name: QPSK (Quaternary Phase Shift Keying)
desc: Type of modulation for digital signals (DVB-S). The digital, serial signal components I and Q directly control phase shift keying. The constellation diagram with its four discrete states is obtained by representing the signal components using the I and Q signals as coordinate axes. Due to the high nonlinear distortion in the satellite channel, this type of modulation is used for satellite transmission. The four discrete states all have the same amplitude that is why nonlinear amplitude distortions have no effect.

name: Q rating
desc: refers to an ad research rating that gauges how easilya celebrityis recognized -- and how well the celebrity is liked

name: QSC
desc: A manufacturer of amplifiers, DSPs, video networking, and control systems for professional AV. Known for Q-SYS, their integrated audio, video, and control platform.

name: QS-FPC
desc: QuickSwitch Front Panel Controller. A front panel controller on Extron matrix switchers allowing easy setup and control through a series of buttons.

name: QSIF
desc: See Quarter Square Interface Format.

name: Q-SYS
desc: QSC's software-defined audio, video, and control platform. Q-SYS Designer is the drag-and-drop design software for creating audio routing, video switching, and control logic on Q-SYS Core processors.

name: Q-SYS Core
desc: The hardware processor running Q-SYS software. Available in multiple models (Core 110f, Core 8 Flex, Core Nano) scaled to system size. Acts as DSP, AV router, and control processor simultaneously.

name: Q-SYS Designer
desc: The design and configuration software for Q-SYS systems using a schematic-based drag-and-drop interface for audio routing, processing, video switching, control scripting, and UCI design.

name: Quad-Box
desc: A Piece Of Power Distribution Equipment Comprising A Small, Metal Enclosure Housing Four, Flush, Female Connectors, And A Permanently Installed Power Cord Whose Conductors Are Electrically Connected To The Female Connectors.

name: Quad Chroma
desc: This is another name for 4FSC because the pixel clock is four times the frequency of the chroma burst. For (M) NTSC the pixel clock is 14.32 MHz (4 x 3.579545 MHz), and 17.73 MHz (4 x 4.43361875 MHz) in (B, D, G, H, I) PAL systems.

name: Quad Compressor
desc: Equipment that simultaneously displays parts or more than one image on a single monitor. It usually refers to four quad- rants display. Also called Split Screen Unit.

name: Quadrature Amplitude Modulation
desc: a) A process that allows two signals to modulate a single carrier frequency. the two signals of interest Amplitude Modulate carrier signals which are the same frequency but differ in phase by 90 degrees (hence the Quadrature notation). The two resultant signals can be added together, and both signals recovered at  the other end, if they are also demodulated 90 degrees apart. b) Type of modulation for digital signals (DVB-C). Two signal components I and Q are quantized and modulated onto two orthogonal carriers as appropriate for the QAM level (4, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256). The constellation diagram is obtained by plotting the signal components with I and Q as the coordinate axes. Therefore, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 or 8 bits of a data stream are transmitted with one symbol, depending on the QAM level (4, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256). This type of modulation is used in cable systems and for coding the COFDM single carriers. c) Method for modulating two carriers. The carriers can be analog or digital.

name: Quadrature Distortion
desc: Distortion results from the asymmetry of sidebands used in vestigial-sideband television transmission. Quadrature distortion appears when envelope detection is used, but can be eliminated by using a synchronous demodulator.

name: Quadrature Modulation
desc: The modulation of two carrier components, which are 90 degrees apart in phase.

name: Quad Select
desc: The matrix and its control that select the video sources feeding each of the four quadrants of a quad split. This is a separate option on the 4100 but has been integrated into the quad split on the AVC.

name: Quad Split
desc: The visual effect of dividing a picture into four segments, each of which may display video from a separate source. Also the name of the switcher panel module which controls this effect.

name: Quad standard
desc: A term for video products compatible with four standards: NTSC 3.58, NTSC 4.43, SECAM, and PAL.

name: Qualified Person
desc: The Esta/Ansi Series E1 Standards Definition Is A Person Who By Possession Of A Recognized Degree Or Certificate Of Professional Standing, Or Who By Extensive Knowledge, Training, And Experience, Has Successfully Demonstrated The Ability To Solve Or Resolve Problems Relating To The Subject Matter And Work.

name: Quality Assessment
desc: The (subjective) process in measuring the quality of an image or video sequence as it appears to humans. Humans find certain types of errors (image distortions) to be more acceptable than others. In video coding, one is often trying to maximize the subjective quality of the video produced by the coding algorithm, which is often quite different than the mathematical quality (measured, for example, by the peak signal to noise ratio or PSNR).

name: Quality Assurance
desc: The Process Of Searching For Errors , Flaws , And Imperfections Within An Environment Or Asset ,Often Abbreviated As Qa Or As Quality Control ( Qc ) .

name: Quantization
desc: The Process Of Sampling An Analog Waveform To Convert Its Voltage Levels Into Digital Data.

name: Quantization Error
desc: The amount that the digital quantity differs from the analog quantity.

name: Quantization Levels
desc: The predetermined levels at which an analog signal can be sampled as determined by the resolution of the analog-to- digital converter (in bits per sample); or the number of bits stored for the sampled signal.

name: Quantization Matrix
desc: A set of sixty-four 8-bit values used by the dequantizer.

name: Quantization Noise
desc: Inaccurate digital representations of an analog signal that occurs during the analog-to-digital signal processing. Typically, the digital interpretation of video resolution is limited through the digital sampling of the analog video input signal.

name: Quantization (Q)
desc: a) The process of converting a continuous analog input into a set of discrete output levels. b) A process in which the continuous range of values of an input signal is divided into non-overlap- ping subranges, and to each subrange a discrete value of the output is uniquely assigned. Whenever the signal value falls within a given subrange, the output has the corresponding discrete value.

name: Quantization Range
desc: Determines the number of distinct colors used in an image. 'Full' range uses values 0–255; 'Limited' range uses 16–235 per ITU-R BT.601 and BT.709. Values 0–15 and 236–255 remain intact but reserved for special use.

name: Quantized DCT Coefficients
desc: DCT coefficients before Dequantization. A variable length coded representation of quantized DCT coefficients is stored as part of the compressed video bit stream.

name: Quantizer
desc: A processing step which intentionally reduces the precision of DCT coefficients.

name: Quantizer Scale
desc: A scale factor coded in the bit stream and used by the decoding process to scale the dequantization.

name: Quantizing
desc: The Process Of Converting The Voltage Level Of A Signal Into Digital Data Before Or After The Signal Has Been Sampled.

name: Quantizing error
desc: Inaccuracies In The Digital Representation Of An Analog Signal. These Errors Occur Because Of Limitations In The Resolution Of The Digitizing Process.

name: Quantizing noise
desc: The Noise (Deviation Of A Signal From Its Original Or Correct Value) Which Results From The Quantization Process. In Serial Digital Video, A Granular Type Of Noise That Occurs Only In The Presence Of A Signal.

name: Quantizing (Quantization) Noise
desc: The noise (devia-tion of a signal from its original or correct value) which results from the quantization process. In serial digital, a granular type of noise only present in the presence of a signal.

name: Quarter Common Interface Format (QCIF)
desc: This video format is often used in low cost video phones. This format has a luminance resolution of 176 x 144 active pixels per line, a refresh rate of 29.97 frames per second at uncompressed bit rate of 9.115 Mbits/s.

name: Quarter Square Interface Format (QSIF)
desc: a) Defines square pixels used in computer applications. b) The computer industry, which uses square pixels, has defined QSIF to be 160 x 120 active pixels, with a refresh rate of whatever the computer is capable of supporting.

name: Quarter-Track
desc: See Four-Track.

name: Quartz
desc: An Abridged Version Of Fused Quartz.

name: Quartz-Halogen Lamp
desc: See Halogen Incandescent Lamp.

name: Quick Compressor
desc: A compressor compatible with Indeo video interac- tive that handles data more quickly than the offline compressor. Videos that compress in hours can take minutes using the quick compressor. Compare Offline Encoder.

name: Quick palette
desc: In Avolites Titan, if a palette is applied with no fixtures selected, the palette is applied to all fixtures that have programming for it. Called a Quick Palette.

name: QuickQ
desc: ChamSys's entry-level lighting console and software range, designed for smaller venues, schools, clubs, and simple installations. Simpler interface than MagicQ while sharing the same core lighting control principles.

name: QuickTime
desc: Apple Computer'S System-Level Software Architecture Supporting Time-Based Media, Giving A Seamless Integration Of Video, Sound, And Animation. For Macintosh And Windows Computers.

name: QuickTime for Windows
desc: Apple’s multimedia playback environment for Microsoft’ Windows operating system. You use QuickTime for Windows by installing several drivers and libraries on your hard disk.

name: Quiet Line
desc: A horizontal quiet line in the vertical interval is sometimes used to evaluate the amount of noise introduced in a certain part of the transmission path. A line is reinserted (and is therefore relatively noise-free) at one end of the transmission path of interest. This ensures that any noise measured on that line at the other end was introduced in that part of the path.

name: Quit
desc: To stop running an application.

name: QUXGA
desc: Quad UXGA. A computer resolution of 3200 x 2400 pixels (four times 1600 x 1200).

name: QXGA
desc: A video graphics resolution of 2048 x 1536.

name: Rack
desc: An Abridged Version Of Dimmer Rack Or Power Distribution Rack, Or An Apparatus That Is A Combination Of The Two.

name: Rack Ears
desc: Brackets That Extend From The Front Panel Of A Piece Of Rackmount Gear That Are Used To Screw It Into Place Within The Rack Unit/Shelf.

name: Rack Focus
desc: Purposely shifting focus during a shot from one subject to another for a cinematic effect.

name: rack focusing
desc: refers to an on - screen film technique of focus change thatblurs the focal planes in sequence , forcing the viewer's eye totravel to those areas of an image that remain in sharp focus ;the focus changes from an object in the foreground to anobject in the background or vice versa , to direct , shift , andsteer the attention of the viewer forcibly from one subject toanother ; also known asselective focusing or pull focus

name: Rack unit
desc: As defined by EIA: 1.75 inches or 44 mm. The universal measurement for product rack height. 1U = 1.75", 2U = 3.5", etc.

name: Radio Common Carrier
desc: Common carriers whose major businesses include radio paging and mobile telephone services.

name: Radio Frequency Interference
desc: A Disturbance Generated By An External Source On An Electrical Circuit Via Electromagnetic Induction.    Synonym: Rf, Rfi

name: Radio Frequency Interference (Rfi)
desc: Direct Radiation From Lamps Or Wiring, Or Conducted Interference  Through Wiring, That Can Affec* The Operation Of Other Electrical Devices.

name: Radiosity
desc: A Diffuse Illumination Calculation System For Graphics Based On Energy Balancing That Takes Into Account Multiple Reflections Off Many Walls.

name: Radix
desc: Total number of distinct characters or numbers used in a numbering system. Same as Base.

name: RAID
desc: Redundant Array Of Independent Disks. A Grouping Of Standard Disk Drives Together With A Raid Controller To Create Storage That Acts As One Disk To Provide Performance Beyond That Available From Individual Drives. Primarily Designed For Operation With Computers Raids Can Offer Very High Capacities, Fast Data Transfer Rates And Much-Increased Security Of Data. The Latter Is Achieved Through Disk Redundancy So That Disk Errors Or Failures Can Be Detected And Corrected. A Series Of Raid Configurations Is Defined By Levels And, Being Designed By Computer People, They Start Counting From Zero. Different Levels Are Suited To Different Applications.

name: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks)
desc:
a) Using more than one drive to achieve either higher throughput, security or both. New technology has made it possible to create EIDE RAID systems that give excellent performance at a very low cost. b) A grouping of standard disk drives together with a RAID controller to create storage that acts as one disk to provide performance beyond that available from individual drives. Primarily designed for operation with computers, RAIDs can offer very high capacities, fast data transfer rates and much increased security of data. The latter is achieved through disk redundancy so that disk errors or failures can be detected and corrected. A series of RAID configurations is defined by levels and, being designed by computer people, they start counting from zero. Different levels are suited to different applications.
Level 0: No redundancy, benefits only of speed and capacity, generated by combining a number of disks. Also known as "striping".
Level 1: Complete mirror system, two sets of disks both reading and writing the same data. This has the benefits of Level 0 plus the security of full redundancy, but at twice the cost. Some performance advantage can be gained in read because only one copy need be read, so two reads can occur simultaneously.
Level 2: An array of nine disks. Each byte is recorded with one bit on each of eight disks and a parity bit recorded to the ninth. This level is rarely, if ever, used.
Level 3: An array of n+1 disks recording 512 byte sectors on each of the n disks to create n x 512 “super sectors” + 1 x 512 parity sector on the additional disk which is used to check the data. The minimum unit of transfer is a whole superblock. This is most suitable for systems in which large amounts of sequential data are transferred, such as for audio and video. For these, it is the most efficient RAID level since it is never necessary to read/modify/write the parity block. It is less suitable for database types of access in which small amounts of data need to be transferred at random.
Level 4: The same as Level 3 but individual blocks can be transferred. When data is written it is necessary to read the old data and parity blocks before writing the new data as well as the updated parity block, which reduces performance.
Level 5: The same as Level 4 but the role of the parity disk is rotated for each block. In Level 4, the parity disk receives excessive load for writes and no load for reads. In Level 5 the load is balanced across the disks.

name: Rails
desc: Slang For The Barricades Used To Separate The Audience From The Performers.

name: Rain Light
desc: A Pinspot Generally Hung Overhead With Its Beam Aiming Downward.

name: Rake
desc: Many Stage Floors, Usually In Theatres Built For Dance Or Variety, Are Higher At The Back Than At The Front, To Give The Audience A Better View. These Stages Are Said To Be "Raked", And The "Rake" Is The Angle Of Slope From Back To Front. In Most Modern Theatres It Is The Audience Seating That Is Raked, Not The Stage.

name: Raked Stage
desc: A Sloped Platform That  Is Lower Near The Audience For Better Visibility And Higher At The Rear, Providing The Illusion Of Distance. This Is The Source For The Terms “Down Stage” And “Upstage.”

name: Ram
desc: Random Access Memory Is A Type Of Computer Memory That Can Be Read From And Written To.

name: RAMbo Drive
desc: A DVD-RAM drive capable of reading and writing CD-R and CD-RW media. (A play on the word “combo.”)

name: Ramped Color
desc: Color intensity extracted from a “smooth” set of predetermined values varying from an initial to a final intensity.

name: RAM (Random Access Memory)
desc: a) The chips in a computer that contain its working memory. b) Usually used to mean semiconductor read/write memory. Strictly speaking, ROMs are also RAMs. See also Random Access. c) This term has come to mean any semiconductor memory whose write access time is approximately the same as its read access time. This is typically taken to include SRAMs (Static RAMs) and DRAMs (Dynamic RAMs). This definition specifically eliminates memories that cannot be altered at all and memories that require a special fixture for erasing (such as EPROMs).

name: Random Access
desc: a) The process of beginning to read and decode the coded bit stream at an arbitrary point. b) Access method in which each word can be retrieved in the same amount of time (i.e., the memory locations can be accessed in any order).

name: Random Error
desc: Errors in measurement leading to inconsistent measured values when repeated measures of a constant attribute are taken.

name: Random Interlace
desc: a) Obsolete form of inexpensive 525 scanning-line system with such poor interlace that line pairing was the norm rather than

name: Random Noise
desc: A Noise Signal, Commonly Used In Measurements, Which Has Constantly Shifting Amplitude, Phase, And A Uniform Spectral Distribution Of Energy.

name: Range
desc: A Term That Usually Means Throw Distance, But Sometimes Refers To Beam Diameter Or Field Diameter.

name: Range (attribute)
desc: In Avolites Titan, a range is a zone within a fixture parameter's DMX range associated with a specific function — for example, DMX values 128–191 on a gobo wheel may correspond to clockwise rotation at varying speeds.

name: Rare-Earth Phosphors
desc: A Group Of Phosphors Containing Rare-Earth Elements. Rare-Earth Phosphors Are Used In Fluorescent Lamps To Achieve Higher Efficacy And Better Color Rendering Than Can Be Achieved With Halophosphates. Rare- Earth Phosphors Each Produce Light In A Very Narrow Wavelength Band. Although Collectively They Are More Efficacious Than Halophosphates, They Are More Expensive. Thus, To Reduce Manufacturing Costs, Lamps Often Are Coated First With Halophosphates And Then With A Thin Layer Of Rare-Earth Phosphors. Re Designates A Lamp Containing Rare-Earth Phosphors.

name: Rarefaction
desc: The Portion Of A Sound Wave In Which Molecules Are Spread Apart, Forming A Region With Lower-Than-Normal Atmospheric Pressure. The Opposite Of Compression.

name: Raster
desc: The series of scan lines making up a TV picture or computer display. All scan lines forming a video frame make up a raster.

name: Raster Graphics
desc: Images defined as a set of pixels or dots in a column- often most associated with Interactive Digital Television. See Back Channel. and-row format. Also called Bitmapped Graphics.

name: Raster Scan Lines
desc: Visible Distortion Which Can Appear As Lines Or Wave Patterns On Camera Such As Whencapturing An Led Panel Without Proper Genlock Or A Camera Shutter Out Of Phase With Thedisplay'S Timing .

name: Rasti
desc: Rapid Speech Transmission Index Expressed In A Decimal Range Of 0.2 For "Bad" To 1.00 For "Excellent"

name: Ratchet Strap
desc: Also known as a truck strap. Used to secure cargo during transport or to secure objects like scrims and signage to truss.

name: Rate conversion
desc: 1. The Process Of Converting From One Digital Sample Rate To Another. The Digital Sample Rate For The Component Digital Video Format Is 13.5 Mhz. For The Composite Digital Video Format, It Is Either 14.3 Mhz For Ntsc Or 17.7 Mhz For Pal. 2. Often Used Incorrectly To Indicate Both Resampling Of Digital Rates And Encoding/Decoding.

name: Rated Lamplife
desc: The Total Length Of Time That A Lamp Should Operate Effectively, As Set By The Manufacturer.

name: Rated Life
desc: See Average Rated Life. Receptacle: An Electrical Outlet.

name: Rated Load
desc: See “Recommended Working Load.”

name: ratingsystem ( s ) or ratings
