desc: Settling time is the time it takes the output analog signal of a DAC to attain the value of the input data signal. This time (usually measured in nanoseconds) is measured from the 50% point of full-scale transition to within +/- 1 LSB of the final value.

name: Set Top Box
desc: A set top device that is a digital receiver. It receives, decompresses, decrypts and converts satellite, cable, terrestrial transmitted digital media signals for playback on a TV or monitor.

name: Set-top box (STB)
desc: These Receivers (Named Because They Typically Sit On Top Of A Television Set) Convert And Display Broadcasts From One Frequency Or Type--Analog Cable, Digital Cable, Or Digital Television) To A Standard Frequency (Typically Channel 3 Or 4) For Display On A Standard Analog Television Set.

name: Set/Trim In, Set/Trim Out
desc: Function of entering edit in- and out-points in the time-code format. Preceding the numeric entry with a + or – adds to or subtracts from already existing edit points.

name: set - up
desc: the place or position where the director and the director ofphotography put the camera ( and lighting ) when shooting ascene ; a scene is usually shot with multiple setups and withmultiple takes from each setup ; aka angle .

name: Setup Files
desc: Customized menus, filters, settings, etc., that you create and can save in UNIX to reuse during work sessions.

name: Setup Mode
desc: The functional level in which you can program the system’s baud rate, parity, and bus address to match the communications standards of an external editor.

name: set - up( screenplay )
desc: in screenplay terms , set - up refers to the first act in which thecharacters , situation , and the setting are established .

name: Set-Up Time
desc: Time that data must be stable prior to a write signal.

name: sex comedy
desc: a humorous , light - hearted film with an improbable plot aboutsexual relationships and extra - marital affairs , with variouspairings between numerous characters , often characterizedby slamming doors ; aka sex farce or bedroom farce .

name: sexploitation
desc: refers to non - pornographic , non - explicit , soft - core films thatfeature sexual themes or explicit sexual material and nudityoften in an apparently crude , immature , leering way ; thesefilms exploited the concept of sex without violating longstanding cultural and legal taboos against showing it all on thescreen ; often with lurid titles ; aka skin flick

name: S/F
desc: Sound over film, meaning the film is silent and sound will come.

name: SFDMA (Synchronous Frequency Division Multiple Access)
desc: For the direct channel from the broadcaster to the user, the European COFDM standard was used, while the return channel was realized using an innova- tive technique called SFDMA (synchronous frequency division multiple access). This technique uses a combination of time division multiple access (TDMA) and frequency division multiple access (FDMA) to transmit users and MAC data to the broadcasting station.

name: SFF 8090
desc: Specification number 8090 of the Small Form Factor Committee, an ad hoc group formed to promptly address disk industry needs and to develop recommendations to be passed on to standards organizations. SFF 8090 (also known as the Mt. Fuji specification), defines a command set for CD-ROM- and DVD-ROM-type devices, including implementation notes for ATAPI and SCSI.

name: SFN (Single Frequency Network)
desc: A TV transmitter network in which all the transmitters use the same frequency. The coverage areas overlap. Reflections are minimized by guard intervals. The transmitters are separat- ed by up to 60 km. The special feature of these networks is efficient frequency utilization.

name: SFP
desc: Small Form-factor Pluggable. Hot-swappable transceiver module for fiber optic or Ethernet connections. SFP: 1 Gbps. SFP+: 10 Gbps. QSFP: 40 Gbps. Allows flexible connectivity selection on switches and AV devices.

name: SFP (Societe Francaise de Production et de Creation Audiovisuelles)
desc: Drafter of the French proposals.

name: SFTP
desc: Secure File Transfer Protocol. A network protocol using SSH certificates and encryption for secure file transfer.

name: Shackle
desc: A U-Shaped Device With Holes At Each End To Accommodate A Pin Or Bolt; Used To Connect A Rope, Cable, Or Chain To Another Device Or A Hanging Point.

name: Shade
desc: A Device On A Luminaire That Is Used To Prevent Glare (By Hiding The Light Source From Direct View), Control Light Distribution,  And Sometimes Diffuse (And Perhaps Color) The Light Emitted.

name: Shader
desc: The person who adjusts iris levels and color balance on cameras in the control room during a live production.

name: Shading
desc: In order to look solid, a polygon must be “shaded” with color. This happens when the polygon is rendered. There are several ways to shade a polygon. These have varying degrees of realism and cost. A polygon’s shading depends on its surface properties, the properties and location of the lights with which it is lit. The shading methods (types) available on PictureMaker are constant, flat, Gouraud, and Phong. The latter two are “smooth” shading types.

name: Shadow
desc: A type of key border effect. A shadow key with a character generator appears as if the letters have been raised off the surface slightly and a light is shining from the upper left; a shadow appears to the right and bottom of the characters.

name: Shadow Chroma Key
desc: The ability to key a subject as a regular chroma key, while using the border channel of the keyer to mix in the low luminance portions of the key signal. This allows a true shadow effect where any shadow in the key video appears as if it is in the background. All Ampex switchers have this feature, with variable shadow levels and densities (bdr adj and bdr lum respectively)..

name: Shadow Mask
desc: A perforated metal plate which is mounted close to the inside of a color CRT display surface. This plate causes the red, green and blue electron beams to hit the proper R, G, or B phosphor dots.

name: Shannon’s Theorem
desc: A criterion for estimating the theoretical limit to the rate of transmission and correct reception of information with a given bandwidth and signal-to-noise ratio.

name: Shape generator (Avolites)
desc: A built-in Titan feature providing pre-programmed repeating effects (circles, figure-8s, rainbow, pulsing, etc.) that can be applied to fixture parameters. Shapes can be customized for size, speed, and phase across multiple fixtures.

name: Shared palette
desc: In Avolites Titan, a palette where data is stored for only one fixture but is made available to all fixtures of the same type. Reduces show file size when all fixtures of a type share the same position or color.

name: Shared Volume Segmentation
desc: See Chunking.

name: Shared  Worlds
desc: Virtual Environments That Are Shared By Multiple Participants.

name: Sharkstooth gauze
desc: The most opaque type of theatrical gauze/scrim fabric. Ideal for transformation scenes — appears solid when lit from the front, disappears when lit from behind.

name: Sharktooth
desc: A Material Used For Fabric Scrims Used In The Theater Industry.

name: Sharpness
desc: a) Apparent image resolution. High sharpness may be the result of high resolution, or it might be an optical illusion caused by image enhancement or by visible edges in a display, such as the vertical stripes  of an aperture grille CRT (e.g., Trinitron). Visible scanning lines can actually increase perceived sharpness. This may be one reason why, in some subjective ATV tests, some viewers have expressed a preference for NTSC pictures over ATV. b) Sharpness is the casual, subjective evaluation of detail clarity in an image. It is often assumed that sharpness and resolution are directly related, in that images possessed of greater sharpness are assumed to have greater resolution. An increase in subjective sharpness is usually reported when objects are more clearly delineated from each other and from background having hard, sharply-defined edges. A major contri- bution to subjective sharpness is this high contrast at edge transitions, as is emphasized by both edge enhancement and aperture correction, for example. In many practical systems, increasing the contrast at edge transi- tions is often accompanied by a reduction in fine detail, and under these conditions sharpness and resolution may describe opposite characteristics.

name: Sheath
desc: Also known as a cable jacket. The outer protective covering of wire or fiber optic cable.

name: Sheave
desc: A Component With A Groove Around Its Circumference To Support And Contain A Rope Or Cable And A Bearing At Its Center To Permit Rotation About A Shaft.

name: Shedding
desc: A tape’s giving off of oxide or other particles from its coating or backing, usually causing contamination of the tape transport and, by redeposit, on the tape itself.

name: Shelf
desc: The effect produced by a shelving equalizer in which the response curve for a certain range of the frequency spectrum (high or low frequency, for example) flattens out or “shelves” at the limits of the audio spectrum. In audio equalization, adjustments to the shelf affect all frequencies within the range of the response curve.

name: Shell
desc: a) The command interpreter between the user and the computer system. b) A window into which you type IRIX, UNIX or DOS commands.

name: Shelving
desc: A Type Of Eq Used To Cut Or Boost A Signal Above Or Below A Cutoff Frequency (Versus Both Sides Of A Selected Frequency).

name: SHF (Super High Frequency)
desc: The band of frequencies ranging from 3 GHz to 30 GHz, currently including all communications satellite signals and most microwave transmissions. SHF has been suggested as a band to be used for terrestrial ATV transmission channels.

name: Shield
desc: In Cabling, A Braided Copper Enclosure Wrapped Around The Wire Conductors,Protecting The Signal From Magnetic Fields, Which Could Result In Unwanted Noise Being Introduced.

name: Shielded Cable
desc: A cable with a conductive covering which reduces the possibility of interference with radio, television, and other devices.

name: Shielding
desc: A Shielded Cable Is An Electrical Cable Of One Or More Insulated Conductors Enclosed By A Common Conductive Layer. The Shield May Be Composed Of Braided Strands Of Metal, A Non-Braided Spiral Winding Of Copper Tape, Or A Layer Of Conducting Polymer. Usually, This Shield Is Covered With A Jacket. The Shield Reduces Electrical Noise And Interference. In Shielded Signal Cables The Shield May Act As The Return Path For The Signal, Or May Act As Screening Only.

name: Shift
desc: To move the characters of a unit of information right or left. For a binary number, this is equivalent to multiplying or dividing by two for each shift.

name: Shin Buster
desc: 1) A Luminaire Placed As Close To The Stage Floor As Possible. It Is Focused Such That No Light Shines On The Floor, Thus Giving The Illusion That The Subject Is Floating. 2) Generally, Any Luminaire Mounted Close To The Stage Floor.

name: SHN
desc: In broadcast and streaming contexts, Seriously Huge News — but in theatre, SHN (Shorenstein Hays Nederlander) is a major San Francisco theatre presenting organization.

name: Shock Load
desc: Loads Generated By The Rapid Application Of A Force Or Motion To An Object Or By The Collision Of Moving Bodies.

name: Shockmount
desc: A Device Designed To Isolate A Microphone From Its Stand To Eliminate The Transmission Of Vibrations.

name: shoot
desc: the process of filming or photographing any aspect of amotion picture with a camera ; the plan for a shoot is termedashooting schedule .

name: Shoot and Protect
desc: A concept of aspect ratio accommodation central to the selection of the 16:9 aspect ratio for the SMPTE HDEP standard. In a shoot and protect system, in production the action is confined to certain bounds (the shoot range) but a larger area (the protect range) is kept free of microphone booms, lights, and other distracting elements. Shoot and protect has been used for years in film, where the shoot aspect ratio is the 1.85:1 used in NTSC. The 16:9 aspect ratio was selected mathematically as the one requiring the least area to protect both 1.33:1 television and 2.35:1 widescreen film. In such a system, both the shoot and the protect aspect ratios would be 16:9. A rectangle of shoot width and protect height would be 1.33:1 (12:9); a rectangle of shoot height and protect width would be 2.35:1 (about 21:9). The concept of 3-perf film conflicts strongly with 1..85:1 shoot and protect.

name: short subject( shorts orshort films )
desc: a film that is shorter than around 30 or 45 minutes ; in thesilent film era , most films were shorts , such as those shown innickelodeons ; then , during the early film era , the price of amovie ticket included not only the weekly feature but also" selected short subjects , " as they were usually billed ; contrastto features .

name: Short Throw
desc: A Term Used To Describe A Luminaire That Has An Effective Intensity At A Relatively Short Distance. This Term Is Very Subjective And Dependent On The Type Of Luminaire Used.

name: Short Time Linear Distortions
desc: These distortions cause amplitude changes, ringing, overshoot and undershoot in fast rise times and 2T pulses. The affected signal components range in duration from 125 nsec to 1 µsec. A 1T pulse must be used to test for these distortions. See the discussion on Linear Distortions. Errors are expressed in “percent-SD”. The presence of distortions in short time domain can also be determined by measuring K2T or Kpulse/bar. See the discussion on K Factor. Picture effects include fuzzy vertical edges. Ringing will sometimes generate chrominance artifacts near vertical edges.

name: Shortwave
desc: Transmissions on frequencies of 6-25 MHz.

name: shot
desc: the basic building block or unit of film narrative ; refers to asingle , constant take made by a motion picture camerauninterrupted by editing , interruptions or cuts , in which alength of film is exposed by turning the camera on , recording ,and then turning the camera off ; it can also refer to a singlefilm frame ( such as a still image ) ; a follow - shot is when thecamera moves to follow the action ; a pull - back shot refers toa tracking shot or zoom that moves back from the subject toreveal the context of the scene ; seealso scene andsequence ; shot analysis refers to theexamination of individual shots ; a one - shot , a two - shot , anda three - shotrefers to common names for shooting just one ,two , or three people in a shot

name: Shotgun Mic
desc: Highly Directional Microphone Often Attached To A Boom Pole

name: Shotgun Mic (Boom Mic)
desc: A Highly Directional Microphone That'S Pointed Straight At Its Target Sound Source For Recording

name: Shotgun Microphone
desc: A Microphone With A Very Narrow Polar Pattern, Which Rejects Sound Dramatically From The Sides And Rear. Commonly Used For Broadcast/Tv Production, And For Capturing Audience Noise For The Performers' Iems.

name: Shot List
desc: An Extensive Log Of All The Shots You Want To Include In Your Video

name: Shot Log
desc: A listing of information about a roll or film or a reel of videotape, usually in chronological order.

name: shot , scene ,sequenceand
desc: a shot , scene , and sequence together make up the largerdramatic narrative of film ; scenes are composed of shots ,sequences are composed of scenes , and films are composedof sequences .

name: Shoulder
desc: On the characteristic curve for a photographic material (the plot of density vs. log exposure) that portion representing nonlinear response at the higher densities. For the electronic relationship of a positive video image to the shoulder of photographic negatives.

name: Show control
desc: Integration of audio, lighting, video, and automation systems using a show control system that triggers events across multiple systems simultaneously via MIDI, OSC, timecode, or proprietary protocols.

name: Show file
desc: The complete saved state of all console settings on a digital mixer including scenes, channel settings, library presets, and system configuration. Stored on internal memory or USB drive.

name: Show Quality
desc: Virtual Content Determined To Be Of Sufficient Visual Quality To Be Suitable For Final Pixel , Incamera Visual Effects .

name: Show relay
desc: A secondary audio feed of the performance broadcast to backstage areas, dressing rooms, and production offices so cast and crew can monitor the show from offstage.

name: Showscan
desc: A film process utilizing 70 mm (65 mm in the camera) film at 60 frames per second. It seems an ideal film production format (expense considerations aside) for transfer to ATV and has been demon- strated as such.

name: Shut Down
desc: To safely close all files, log out, and bring the workstation to a state where you can safely power it down.

name: Shutter
desc: Metal plates inside a Leko or ellipsoidal light instrument used to block and shape the light beam in specific patterns.

name: Shutter Blade
desc: A Single Framing Shutter.

name: Shutter Glasses
desc: Glasses That Alternately Block Out The Left And Right Eye Views In Synchrony With The Computer Display Of Left And Right Eye Images To Provide Stereoscopic Images On The Computer Screen. {{Photo}}

name: Shutters
desc: 1) An Abridged Version Of Framing Shutters. 2) A Rectangular, Metal Apparatus That Resembles A Venetian Blind In Form And Function, Generally Used As A Mechanical Dimmer Or Blackout Mechanism On Large Spotlights.

name: Shutter Speed
desc: The length of time a single camera frame is exposed. Slower speeds allow more light but create motion blur.

name: Shuttle
desc: A variable-rate search, forward or reverse, of a videotape using a video deck or VCR capable of such an operation.

name: Shuttling
desc: The viewing of footage at speeds greater than real time.

name: Sibilance
desc: The Energy Produced When Someone Is Using "S" Sounds In Speech Or Singing.This Can Be Undesirable, And Is Typically Controlled With A De-Esser, Which Compresses Only Those High Frequencies.

name: Side Arm
desc: 1) In The Film And Video Industries, Any Apparatus With A Pipe Or Rod That Attaches To Another Pipe Or Stand Via Some Type Of Clamp On One End, And Has A Stud Or Receiver On The Other End For Mounting A Luminaire Or Grip Equipment. 2) In The Theater Industry, A Length Of Narrow Pipe, Containing One Or More Sliding Tees, With A Pipe Clamp Attached To One End And A Rivet Through The Other End To Retain The Sliding Tees. It Is Used To Extend The Mounting Position Of A Luminaire(S).

name: Sideband
desc: A signal that is a consequence of some forms of modulation. When modulation forms two sidebands, one can sometimes be filtered out to increase efficiency without sacrificing information.

name: Side-chain
desc: A secondary signal input to a dynamics processor that controls the processing without being part of the main audio path. Used for de-essing (filtering the sidechain to react to sibilance), ducking (radio on bass), and keyed gating.

name: Side converting
desc: The Process Which Changes The Number Of Pixels And/Or Frame Rate And/Or Scanning Format Used To Represent An Image By Interpolating Existing Pixels To Create New Ones At Closer Spacing Or By Removing Pixels. Side Converting Is Done From Standard Resolution To Standard Resolution And High Definition To High Definition.See Also: Down Converting, Up Converting.

name: Sidefill
desc: Large loudspeakers placed at the sides of the stage providing a wide stereo or mono monitor mix for performers who need to hear the full band across the stage width.

name: Side Information
desc: Information in the bit stream necessary for controlling the decoder.

name: Side Line Pulley
desc: A Light Duty Block That Mounts By Its Side To A Surface. They Are Normally Used To Support Fire Lines And Operating Cords For Curtain Tracks.

name: Side panels
desc: Image Of A Standard 4:3 Picture On A Widescreen 16:9 Aspect Ratio Television Screen, Typically With Black Bars On The Side. Used To Maintain The Original Aspect Ratio Of The Source Material. See Also: Letterbox, Pillarbox.

name: Sides (Submenu)
desc: Under Source, the function that enables each side of the video image to be cropped.

name: SIF (Standard or Source Interchange Format)
desc: A half-resolution input signal used by MPEG-1. See Standard Input Format.

name: Sifting
desc: The displaying of clips that meet specific criteria in a bin.

name: SIGGRAPH
desc: The Association Of Computing Machinery (Acm)'S Special Interest Group On Computer Graphics (Siggraph). Internet :  Www.Siggraph.Org.

name: sight gag
desc: aka visual gag ; an image that conveys humor visually , usuallynon - verbally ; often used in silent film comedy , or in films withvery little dialogue .

name: Sight Line
desc: The Edge Or Line Of View, Of What Can Be Seen On Stage From The Location Of The Audience.

name: Sight Lines
desc: Imaginary Lines Drawn From The Most Extreme Seats In The House To The Performing Area To Determine What Portions Of The Performing Area Will Be Visible To All Of The Audience.

name: Signal
desc: An Electrical Representation Of An Audio Event, Which Can Be Processed And Mixed With Other Signals By The Audio System.

name: Signal Amplitude
desc: The nominal video signal amplitude shall be 1.0 volt peak-to-peak (140 IRE units).

name: Signal, Chrominance
desc: Video: The color-difference signal(s) and the equation(s) for their derivation. Color Television: The sidebands of the modulated chrominance subcarrier that are added to the luminance signal to convey color information.

name: Signaling rate
desc: The Bandwidth Of A Digital Transmission System Expressed In Terms Of The Maximum Number Of Bits That Can Be Transported Over A Given Period Of Time. The Signaling Rate Is Typically Much Higher Than The Average Data Transfer Rate For The System Due To Software Overhead For Network Control, Packet Overhead, Etc.

name: Signaling Reference Voltage (Dbu And Dbv)
desc: See Entries For Dbu And Dbv.

name: Signal loss
desc: A video problem showing up as a faint picture due to lack of video information.

name: Signal, Luminance
desc: Video: The signal that describes the distribution of luminance levels within the image and the equation for deriving that infor- mation from the camera output. Television, Composite Color: A signal that has major control of the luminance. Note: The signal is a linear combina- tion of gamma-corrected primary color signals.

name: Signal Noise
desc: A random fluctuation in an electrical signal, a characteristic of all electronic circuits.

name: Signal Path
desc: The Route Taken By A Signal From The Input Of A System To Its Final Output.

name: Signal Path/Chain
desc: Path Taken By A Signal. This Can Either Be From The Input To The Output Of One Device, Or The Path Taken Through Many Different Devices (E.G., From Microphone To Mixing Desk / Signal Processing Devices Then Amplifier(S), Speakers, Etc.)

name: Signal Polarity
desc: The polarity of the signal shall be positive, i.e., so that black-to-white transition are positive going.

name: Signal Processing
desc: See Dsp (Digital Signal Processing)

name: Signal-To-Noise Ratio
desc: The Ratio Of Maximum Signal Level To The Residual Noise Floor,Expressed In Decibels.

name: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)
desc: a) The ratio of signal to noise expressed in dB. In general, the higher the signal to noise ratio the better. If there is a low signal-to-noise ratio, the picture can appear grainy, snowy and sparkles of color maybe noticeable. Equipment will not be able to synchro- nize to extremely noisy signals. b) It may not be possible to directly com- pare SNRs for ATV and for NTSC as the eye’s sensitivity to noise varies with the detail of the noise. c) The measurement of the dynamic range of a piece of equipment, measuring from the noise floor (internally generated noise) to the normal operating level or the level prior to limiting.

name: Signal-To-Noise (Sn) Ratio
desc: The Range Or Distance Between The Noise Floor (The Noise Level Of The Equipment Itself) And The Music Signal.

name: Signal Voltage
desc: The Effective Voltage Value Of The Signal That Leaves Or Feeds An Audio Device.

name: Signature
desc: Four-digit value generated by a signature analyzer, which is used to characterize data activity present on a logic node during a specific period of time.

name: Signature Analysis
desc: Technique used to facilitate the troubleshooting of digital circuits. Nodes of the circuit, stimulated during a test mode, produce “signatures” as the result of the data compression process performed by the signature analyzer. When a node signature is compared to a known good documented signature, faulty nodes can be identified.

name: Signature Analyzer
desc: Instrument used to convert the long, complex serial data streams present on microprocessor system nodes into four-digit signatures.

name: Silence
desc: Blank (black) space in the audio tracks in a timeline that contains no audio material.

name: silent film( or silents )
desc: the term for motion pictures without sound ( spoken dialogueor synchronized soundtrack ) , although they were oftenaccompanied by live commentary , piano - music , soundeffects , and / or orchestration ; the period from about 1895 to1927 ( when " talkies " were introduced ) ; contrast with talkies .

name: Silent Radio
desc: A service that feeds data that is often seen in hotels and nightclubs. It's usually a large red sign that shows current news, events, scores, etc. It is present on NTSC lines 10- 11 and 273-274, and uses encoding similar to EIA-608.

name: Silhouette
desc: In a boundary rep system, the typical method for creating a solid begins by drawing a silhouette outline of it; a plan view (in architec- tural terminology).

name: Silicon
desc: The material of which modern semiconductor devices are made.

name: silver bullet
desc: aka " magic bullet " - a solution that completely solves thecomplicated dramatic problem within a film ; the term wasderived from European folklore in which only a silver bulletcould kill a werewolf .

name: SIMM Removal Tool
desc: An L-shaped metal tool used to loosen SIMMs that are installed in the SIMM socket.

name: SIMM (Single In-Line Memory Module)
desc: A small printed circuit board with several chips that contain additional megabytes of random-access memory (RAM).

name: SIMM Socket
desc: A long, thin, female connector located on the CPU board into which you insert a SIMM.

name: SIMPL+
desc: A proprietary C-like programming language by Crestron for programming their control processors. Used to write custom logic, event handlers, and device drivers.

name: Simple Payback
desc: A Term Used In The Economics Chapter To Define The Time Required To Save Enough In Operating Costs By Using Design 2, Compared To Design 1, To Pay Back The Incremental Cost Of Design 2.

name: Simple profile
desc: Mpeg Image Streams Using Only I And P Frames Is Less Efficient Than Coding With B Frames. This Profile, However, Requires Less Buffer Memory For Decoding.

name: Simple Profile (SP)
desc: a) MPEG image streams using only I and P frames is less efficient than coding with B frames. This profile, however, requires less buffer memory for decoding. b) A subset of the syntax of the MPEG-2 video standard designed for simple and inexpensive applications such as software. SP does not allow B pictures.

name: Simple Scalable Visual Profile
desc: Adds support for coding of temporal and spatial scalable objects to the Simple Visual Profile. It is useful for applications which provide services at more than one level of quality due to bit rate or decoder resource limitations, such as Internet use and soft- ware decoding. analyzer or an automatic measurement set. Very little information is discernible in a time domain display. The waveform is shown in the figure to the right. his signal is used for Frequency Response measurements. Refer to the Frequency Response discussion.

name: Simplex cable
desc: A cable comprising a single optical fiber.

name: SIMPL Windows
desc: Crestron's primary programming environment using a graphical signal-flow interface to connect logic modules, device symbols, and user interface elements for control system design.

name: Simulation
desc: A technique for trying an ATV scheme inside a computer

name: Simulation VR
desc: A Type Of Virtual  Reality  That Simulates A User’S Active Involvement In A Scene Or Scenario. Cf. Cinematic  Vr.

name: Simulator   Sickness
desc: Various Disturbances, Ranging In Degree From A Feeling Of Unpleasantness, Disorientation, And Headaches To Extreme Nausea, Caused By Various Aspects Of A Simulator. Possible Factors Include Sensory Distortions Such As Abnormal Movement Of Arms And Heads Because Of The Weight Of Equipment; Long Delays Or Lags In Feedback, And Missing Visual Cues From Convergence And Accommodation.

name: Simulcam
desc: The Live Compositing Of Virtual Elements With Live - Action . Used For Previewing Virtualcharacters And Environments During Live - Action Cinematography . See Also Augmented Reality .

name: Simulcast
desc: To Broadcast The Same Program Over Two Different Transmission Systems. Currently, Some Am And Fm Stations Simulcast The Same Program For Part Of The Day, And Some Radio Stations Simulcast The Audio From Televised Music Events.Although Not Initially Required By The Fcc, It Is Believed That Most Television Stations Will Simulcast Their Dtv And Ntsc Signal. Simulcasting Will Be Required Towards The End Of The Dtv Transition Period To Protect The Public Interest.

name: Simulcast (Simultaneous Broadcast)
desc: Prior to the advent of multi-

name: Simultaneous Localization And Mapping ( SLAM )
desc: A Method Of Tracking Which Analyzes Physical Features Of The Real - World To Compute Positionand Translation In Real - Time .

name: Sine-Squared Pulses
desc: Sine-squared pulses are bandwidth limited and are useful for testing bandwidth-limited television systems. Fast rise  time square waves cannot be used for testing bandwidth-limited systems because attenuation and phase shift of out-of-band components will cause ringing in the output pulse. These out-of-band distortions can obscure the in-band distortions that are of interest. Description of the Pulse: Sine-squared pulses look like one cycle of a sine wave as shown.Mathematically, a sine-squared wave is obtained by squaring a half-cycle of a sine wave. Physically, the pulse is generated by passing an impulse through a sine-squared shaping filter. T Intervals: Sine-squared pulses are specified in terms of half amplitude duration (HAD) which is the pulse width measured at the 50% pulse amplitude points. Pulses with HADs which are multiples of the time interval T are used to test bandwidth limited systems. T, 2T and 12.5T pulses are common examples. T is the Nyquist interval, or 1/2 fc where fc is the cutoff frequency of the system to be measured. For NTSC, fc is take to be 4 MHz, thus T is 125 nsec. T Steps: The rise times of transitions to a constant luminance level (such as the white bar) are also specified in terms of T. A T step has a 10% to 90% rise time of nominally 125 nsec, while a 2T step has a rise time of 250 nsec. Refer to the figure at the right. Energy Distribution: Sine- squared pulses possess negligible energy at frequencies above f=1/HAD. The amplitude of the envelope of the frequency spectrum at 1/(2 HAD) is one-half of the amplitude at zero frequency.

name: Sine Wave
desc: A Periodic Wave Related To Simple Harmonic Motion.

name: Single Channel
desc: Channel-compatible, an ATV scheme fitting into 6 MHz of bandwidth.

name: Single-Domain Particle
desc: All ferromagnetic materials are composed of permanently magnetized regions in which the magnetic moments of the atoms are ordered. These domains have a size determined by energy consideration. When a particle is small enough, it cannot support more than one domain and is called a single domain particle.

name: Single-ended
desc: An unbalanced circuit where one side is grounded. Single-ended audio is unbalanced audio.

name: Single Ended Lamp
desc: A Lamp That Has Only One Base And All Of Its Contacts On The Base.

name: Single-Ended SCSI
desc: An Electrical Signal Configuration Where Information Is Sent Through One Wire In A Cable. Information Is Interpreted By The Change In The Voltage Of The Signal. Single- Ended Interfaces Permit Cable Lengths Up To 18 Feet (6 Meters).

name: Single-Forked
desc: A MooV file whose resources have been moved into the data fork, creating a file that can be played on a PC. See MooV.

name: Single-Line Display
desc: In audio, a visual representation of changing frequencies

name: Single-Link DVI
desc: A DVI connection using one TMDS link with three data channels and one clock signal. Maximum 165 MHz video frequency, supporting resolutions up to 1920×1200.

name: Single-Mode Fiber
desc: An optical glass fiber that consists of a core of  very small diameter. A typical single-mode fiber used in CCTV has a 9 mm core and a 125 mm outer diameter. Single-mode fiber has less attenuation and therefore transmits signals at longer distances (up to 70 km). Such fibers are normally used only with laser sources because of their very small acceptance cone.

name: Singlemode Fiber (SMF)
desc: An optical fiber with a small core through which only a single mode can propagate.

name: Single-Page Mapping
desc: Refers to always using Offset Register 0 (GR9) as the window into display memory. The mode is selected when GRB(0) is programmed to “0”.

name: Single Pass Transform
desc: A transformation process making only one examination of a data set, required to maintain low delay.

name: Single-Perf Film
desc: Film stock that is perforated along one edge only.

name: Single Phase
desc: 1) A Term For An Alternating Current Electrical Supply That Has One Hot Leg And A Neutral Leg, Or, Two Different Hot Legs Whose Phases Are 120° Apart, With Or Without A Neutral Leg. 2) A Term Used To Describe Something That Requires A Single Phase Electrical Supply To Operate.

name: Single-Pole Switch
desc: Single-Location On-Off  Switch That Controls One Luminaire, Or Group Of Luminaires.

name: Single Program Transport Stream (SPTS)
desc: An MPEG-2 transport stream that contains one unique program.

name: Single Purchase
desc: A Rope Or Cable Passing From A Lifting Device (Arbor, Winch, Or Person) Over A Block, Or Series Of Blocks, To A Load Is Single Purchased. Force Must Be Exerted Equal To The Load To Be Held Or Raised.

name: Single-Step
desc: Process of executing a program one instruction or machine cycle at a time.

name: Single-Strand Editing
desc: See A-Roll.

name: Sink Current
desc: Current input capability of a device.

name: Sinusoidal (Sine) Wave
desc: An Oscillation Whose Waveform Is That Of A Sine Curve, E.G., A Sound Wave Or And Electrical Wave. Audio Signals Are Sine Waves.

name: SIP
desc: Solo In Place. A solo mode that mutes all other channels while the soloed channel plays through the main outputs at its normal level in the mix context. Used for referencing how a channel sounds within the mix.

name: SIS
desc: Simple Instruction Set. A set of commands from Extron for easy RS-232 control of Extron products using minimal characters.

name: SI (Service Information)
desc: SI provides information on services and events carried by different multiplexes, and even other networks. SI is structured as six tables (PAT, NIT, CAT, SDT, EIT, and BAT). The applications are only concerned with NIT, BAT, SDT, and EIT.

name: SI (Systéme International d’Unites)
desc: The French version of the International System of Units. A complete system of standardized units and prefixes for fundamental quantities of length, time, volume, mass, and so on. SI is roughly equivalent to the metric system.

name: Sitzprobe
desc: In musical theatre, a seated rehearsal where singers perform with the full orchestra for the first time, focusing on music without staging or movement.

name: Six Degree of Freedom (6Dof)
desc: Refers to the ability of a user to move around in space. With six degrees of freedom, users are able to move forward, backward, up, down, left, and right in a virtual reality experience. Tethered headsets like HTC Vive and Oculus Rift enable six degrees of freedom.

name: Six Degrees Of Freedom (6Dof)
desc: Ability To Move In Three Spatial Directions And Orient About Three Axes Passing Through The Center Of The Body. Thus The Location And Orientation Are Specified By Six Coordinates.

name: size
desc: refers to a film element , used by the film - maker to indicate acharacter's or object's relative strength compared to otherthings or persons

name: Sizing
desc: The operation of shrinking or stretching video data between a system’s input and display. Normally, a combination of scaling and zooming.

name: Sketchfab
desc: A Web-Based Platform For Hosting 3D  Models. Models Can Be Made Available For Free Or Paid Download, Making Sketchfab Similar To An Asset  Store In Functionality.

name: Sketchup
desc: A 3D  Modeling  Software Program Produced By Trimble Inc. Sketchup Is Primarily Designed For Architectural, Engineering, And Design Applications. Both Free And Subscription-Based Versions Are Available.

name: Skew
desc: a) Passage of tape over a head in a direction other than perpendi- cular to the height of the gap. b) Term used for an ADO action whereby rectangles become trapezoids.

name: Skew-Free
desc: A reference to special twisted pair cable where length differences between cables are minimized to reduce skew.

name: Skin Effect
desc: The tendency of alternating current to travel only on the surface of a conductor as its frequency increases.

name: Skip Frame
desc: An optical printing effect eliminating selected frames of the original scene to speed up the action.

name: Skipped Macroblock
desc: – A macroblock for which no data is encoded. Slapback – Discrete repeats created by either digital or tape delay. Slate – a) To label with a take number by recording a voice on the tape. b) Term used for a frame of video text usually recorded after bars prior to countdown sequence at the top of a commercial or program containing information on date recorded, ad agency, direction, etc.

name: Skycloth
desc: Alternative Name For A Cyclorama (Qv).

name: Skydrop
desc: A light-value (sky blue, grey, or white) curtain hanging upstage to suggest sky. Not painted. When wide enough to wrap three sides of the playing space it is called a cyclorama or cyc.

name: Sky Glow
desc: The Brightening Of The Night Sky Caused By Artificial Lighting.

name: Skylight
desc: A Clear Or Translucent Panel Set Into A Roof To Admit Daylight Into A Building.

name: SLA
desc: Service Level Agreement. An agreement between a network service provider and the user defining bandwidth, QoS, and service response time metrics.

name: Slack Line
desc: A Cable That Droops Or Leaves The Sheave Or Drum Groove Because It Lacks Tension In The Line.

name: Slap Back
desc: A Discrete Reflection From A Nearby Surface.

name: slapstick ( comedy )
desc: a broad form of comedy in which the humor comes fromphysical acts or pantomime , frequently harmless violence andpratfalls intended to produce laughter . The name was derivedfrom a device called a slapstick , two boards that slappedtogether with a loud crack when used to strike something orsomeone ; prevalent during the silent era and in early talkies ,with its primary motif being pie - throwing .

name: slasher film
desc: usually a cheaply - made sub - genre film ( usually in the horrorgenre ) designed for the teenage audience ( teen movie ) ,deliberately made to contain gory , blood - splattering , explicitdeaths without any build - up , style or suspense , oftencommitted by an unstoppable serial killer , with a sharp bladedweapon ; most slasher films are created to generate sequelsand repetitive boredom ; aka splatter films ; see also trashfilm , grindhouse film , schlock film , B - film and Z - film

name: Slate
desc: a) To label with a take number by recording a voice on the tape. b) Term used for a frame of video text usually recorded after bars prior to countdown sequence at the top of a commercial or program containing information on date recorded, ad agency, direction, etc.

name: slate ( board )
desc: refers to the digital board held in front of the camera thatidentifies shot number , director , camera - person , studio andtitle ; the slate has the clap sticks on top and the scenenumber , take and production name or title usually written onit , and the person operating the slate will say " mark " and clapthe sticks for picture and sound sync purposes ; originally thedata was written with chalk on a slate board ; the footage ofthe slate at the beginning of each shot or take is used in thelaboratory and editing room to identify the shot ; seealsoclapboard

name: sleeper
desc: a movie that is released with little publicity or pre - releasebuzz ,often directed by and starring relatively unknown people , thateventually becomes popular ( as a cult film ) or financiallysuccessful beyond expectations , usually due to positive wordof - mouth ; the term is sometimes used incorrectly to describeunpopular movies that the critics love

name: Slewing
desc: The synchronizing of decks in computerized editing systems.

name: Slew rate
desc: The ability of audio equipment to reproduce fast amplitude changes. Measured in volts per microsecond. Higher slew rate = tighter, more dynamic sound.

name: Slice
desc: A Series Of Macroblocks. A Slice Is The Basic Synchronizing Unit For Reconstruction Of The Image Data And Typically Consists Of All The Blocks In One Horizontal Picture Interval--Typically 16 Lines Of The Picture.

name: Sliced VBI Data
desc: A technique where a VBI decoder samples the VBI data (such as teletext and captioning data), locks to the timing information, and converts it to binary 0’s and 1’s. DC offsets, amplitude variations,

name: Slide
desc: An editing feature that adjusts the OUT Point of the previous clip, and the IN Point of the next clip without affecting the clip being slid or the program duration.

name: Slider
desc: An Attachment That Typically Mounts On A Tripod, Utilizing A Track To Allow Smooth Camera Movements Laterally, Front To Back, Or Diagonally

name: Slide Timing
desc: The outgoing (A-side) and incoming (B-side) frames change because the clip remains fixed while the footage before and after it is trimmed.

name: Sling Configurations
desc: On any given day a load can be rigged and moved in a variety of ways. At left are a group of hitch and bridle types which represent good, poor, and unacceptable rigging methods (46).

name: Slip
desc: An editing feature that adjusts the In and Out points of a clip without affecting the adjacent clips or program duration.

name: Slip Trimming
desc: The head and tail frames of the clip change because only the contents of the clip are adjusted. The frames that precede and follow the clip are not affected.

name: Slope
desc: A Parameter Of An Eq Or Filter Referring The Rate Of Attenuation, Given In Db/Octave.

name: Slot
desc: A discrete step in a fixture parameter's range, such as a specific position on a gobo wheel or color wheel. Each slot corresponds to a specific DMX value range.

name: Slow-In/Slow-Out
desc: In real life, when an object at rest begins to move, it starts slowly. Similarly, when an object changes its speed, or direction, it rarely does so instantaneously, but rather makes the change gradually (that is one reason we use splines to describe motion paths in computer animation). In order to create satisfying animation, it is important to be sensitive to the rate at which objects change their direction and speed; these factors are the most expressive component of path animation, like tempo and dynamics in music. In particular, the term slow-in/slow-out refers to an object at rest which gradually accelerates, reaches a final velocity, then slows and stops.

name: Slow Motion
desc: Also called overcranking. Shooting at a faster frame rate than playback so the subject appears to move in slow motion.

name: slow - motion( or slo - motion )
desc: refers to an effect resulting from running film through acamera at faster - than - normal speed ( shooting faster than 24frames per second ) , and then projecting it at standard speed ;if a camera runs at 60 frames per second , and captures a onesecond - long event , a 24 - frame playback will slow that eventto two and a half seconds long ; overcrank ( ing ) means tospeed up the camera , thereby making the action appearslower when projected - the term dates back to the old daysof physically hand - cranking film through a camera ; this filmictechnique is usually employed to fully capture a ' moment intime ' or to produce a dramatic ( or romantic feeling ) ; contrastto fast - motion ( or accelerated motion , achievedby undercranking ) or time compression

name: Slow Scan
desc: The transmission of a series of frozen images by means of analog or digital signals over limited bandwidth media, usually telephone.

name: SLSC (Split-Luminance, Split-Chrominance)
desc: A family of ATV schemes proposed by Bell Labs and IIT. SLSC is a receiver-compatible, non-channel compatible ATV scheme utilizing a high line rate camera and prefiltering with receiver line doubling to increase vertical resolution and additional bandwidth to increase horizontal resolution and help reduce NTSC artifacts. Aspect ratio is increased by blanking stuffing in the HBI. SLSC schemes have been proposed with at least two types of chroma encoding and three types of widescreen panel transmission.

name: S-MAC (Studio MAC)
desc: a) A MAC standard proposed for studio intracon_x0002_nection by the SMPTE working group on CAV standards. The S-MAC system uses time compression and time domain multiplexing techniques to convey (Y, CR, CB) video signals – a version of (Y, R-Y, B-Y). b) A MAC designed for single transmission of CAV signals in a television facility or between facilities. See also MAC

name: Small Scale Integration (SSI)
desc: Technology of less complexity than medium scale integration. Usually means less than ten gate functions in the IC.

name: Smart Slate
desc: See Slate.

name: Smart Stage
desc: A Stage Purpose - Built For Virtual Production Which Might Include Led Walls , Tracking Systems ,Real - Time Animation , Performance Capture , And Vr Capabilities .

name: Smart Video Recorder Pro
desc: Intel’s PC video capture card that can capture and even compress video in real-time, using Indeo technology.

name: smash - cut( or shock cut )
desc: a cinematic term that refers to an abrupt , jarring andunexpected change in the scene or film's image ( and theaudio ) , in order to surprise the viewing audience ; seealsotransition

name: SMATV-DTM
desc: SMATV system based on digital trans-modulation.

name: SMATV-IF
desc: SMATV system based on distribution at IF (Intermediate Frequency).

name: SMATV-S
desc: SMATV system based on distribution at extended super band.

name: SMATV (Satellite Master Antenna Television)
desc: Transmission of televi- sion programming to a Satellite Master Antenna installed on top of an apartment building, a hotel, or at another central location from where it serves a private group of viewers. The transmission usually is done in C-band to 1.5 or 2 meter dishes.

name: Smear
desc: A term used to describe a picture condition in which objects appear to be extended horizontally beyond their normal boundaries in a blurred or “smeared” manner.

name: Smearing
desc: A video problem where horizontal bars or objects extend past their boundaries. Also called over-peaking.

name: SMIL (Synchronized Multimedia Integration Language)
desc: Enables simple authoring of interactive audiovisual presentations. SMIL is typically used for "rich media"/multimedia presentations which integrate streaming audio and video with images, text or any other media type. SMIL is an easy-to-learn HTML-like language, and many SMIL presentations are written using a simple text-editor.

name: Smoke Developed
desc: Measure Of Smoke Density Developed By A Material When Compared With Red Oak, Which Has A Smoke Density Index (Sdi) Of 100 In Accordance With Astm E 84. The Infill Of Iac Sound Absorptive Metal Panels Has An Sdi Of 20.

name: Smoke Pocket
desc: A Slot, Usually Of Fabricated Steel That Supports A Guide System At The Edges Of A Fire Safety Curtain And That Helps To Prevent Smoke Passing Around The Edges Of The Curtain.

name: Smoke Seal
desc: A Fabric Flap That Mounts On The Proscenium Wall And Contacts The Fire Curtain In Order To Form A Barrier  That Reduces The Passage Of Air And Smoke Between The Stage And Auditorium.

name: Smooth Shading
desc: Even though an object may be represented by poly- gons, with smooth shading, the facets can be made to appear to blend into each other, making the object look smooth. Smooth shading also makes possible the simulation of “highlights”.

name: SMPTE
desc: 1. Society Of Motion Picture And Television Engineers. A Professional Organization That Sets Standards For American Television. 595 W. Hartsdale Ave., White Plains, Ny, 10607-1824.Tel: 914-761-1100. Fax: 914-761-3115. Email: Smpte@Smpte.Org Internet: Www.Smpte.Org. 2. An Informal Name For A Color Difference Video Format That Uses A Variation Of The Y, R-Y, And B Y Signal Set.

name: SMPTE 120M
desc: NTSC color specification.

name: SMPTE 125M
desc: – SMPTE standard for Bit-Parallel Digital Interface – Component Video Signal 4:2:2. SMPTE 125M (formerly RP-125) defines the parameters required to generate and distribute component video signals on a parallel interface

name: SMPTE 125M (formerly RP-125)
desc: The Smpte Standard For A Bit Parallel Digital Interface For 55- Line Interlace Component Video Signals. Smpte 125M Defines The Parameters Required To Generate And Distribute Component Video Signals On A Parallel Interface.

name: SMPTE 12M
desc: Defines the longitudinal (LTC) and vertical interval (VITC) timecode for NTSC and PAL video systems. LTC requires an entire field time to store timecode information, using a separate track. VITC uses one scan line each field during the vertical blanking interval.

name: SMPTE 170M
desc: sampled at four times color subcarrier frequency. Sampling parameters for the digital representation of encoded video signals, the relationship between sampling phase and color subcarrier, and the digital levels of the video signal are defined.

name: SMPTE 240M
desc: – SMPTE standard for Television – Signal Parameters – 1125/60 High-Definition Production System. This standard defines the basic characteristics of the video signals associated with origination equip_x0002_ment operating in the 1125/60 high-definition television production sys_x0002_tem. As this standard deals with basic system characteristics, all parame_x0002_ters are untoleranced.

name: SMPTE 244M
desc: Proposed SMPTE standard for Television System M/NTSC Composite Video Signals Bit-Parallel Digital Interface. This standard describes a bit-parallel composite video digital interface for systems oper- ating according to the 525-line, 59.94 Hz NESC standard 35 described by SMPTE 170M, sampled at four times color subcarrier frequency. Sampling parameters for the digital representation of encoded video signals, the relationship between sampling phase and color subcarrier, and the digital levels of the video signal are defined.

name: SMPTE 253M
desc: Analog RGB video interface specification for pro-video SDTV systems.

name: SMPTE 259M
desc: The Smpte Standard For Standard Definition Serial Digital Component And Composite Interfaces.

name: SMPTE 260M
desc: Standard for high definition digital 1125/60.

name: SMPTE 266M
desc: Defines the digital vertical interval timecode (DVITC).

name: SMPTE 267
desc: Defines the serial digital signal format for 16:9 aspect ratio television. The signal rate is 360 Mbps.

name: SMPTE 267M
desc: Standard for component digital video with a 16:9 aspect ratio that uses both 13.5 MHz and 18 MHz sampling.

name: SMPTE 272M
desc: The Smpte Standard For Formatting Aes/Ebu Audio And Auxiliary Data Into Digital Video Ancillary Data Space.

name: SMPTE 274M
desc: 1920 x 1080 Scanning And Interface.

name: SMPTE 276M
desc: Transmission of AES/EBU digital audio and auxiliary data over coaxial cable.

name: SMPTE 291M
desc: Ancillary data packet and space formatting.

name: SMPTE 292M
desc: The SMPTE recommended practice for bit-serial digital interface for high definition television systems.

name: SMPTE 293M
desc: The Smpte Standard Defining The Data Representation Of The 720X483 Progressive Signal At 59.94 Hz.

name: SMPTE 294M
desc: The Smpte Standard Defining The Serial Interfaces For Both 4:2:2P (Progressive) On Two-Smpte 259M Links And 4:2:0P (Progressive) On A Single Smpte 259M Link (At 360Mbps).

name: SMPTE 295M
desc: 1920 x 1080 50 Hz Scanning And Interfaces.

name: SMPTE 296M
desc: 1280 x 720 Scanning, Analog And Digital Representation And Analog Interface.

name: SMPTE 297M
desc: Serial Digital Fiber Transmission For SMPTE 295M Signals.

name: SMPTE 298M
desc: Universal Labels For Unique Identification Of Digital Data.

name: SMPTE 299M
desc: The Smpte Standard For 24-Bit Digital Audio Format For Hdtv Bit-Serial Interface. Allows Eight Embedded Aes/Ebu Audio Channel Pairs.

name: SMPTE 305M
desc: The Smpte Standard For Serial Digital Transport Interface (Sdti).

name: SMPTE 308M
desc: Television – MPEG-2 4:2:2 Profile At High Level

name: SMPTE 310
desc: A broadcast standard for transmitting one or more DTV channels and ancillary content as a single data stream.

name: SMPTE 310M
desc: The Smpte Standard For Synchronous Serial Interface (Ssi) For Mpeg-2 Digital Transport Streams; Used As The "Standard" For The Output From The Atsc Systems Multiplexer And The Input To Dtv Transmitters.

name: SMPTE 312M
desc: Television – Splice Points For MPEG-2 Transport Streams.

name: SMPTE 314M
desc: – Television – Data Structure For DV Based Audio, Data And Compressed Video – 25 Mbps and 50 Mbps.

name: SMPTE 318M
desc: Reference Signals For The Synchronization Of 59.95 Hz Related Video And Audio Systems In Analog And Digital Areas (Replaces RP 154). This standard reference is used to synchronize multi-format systems, with a field frequency of 59.94 Hz. In order to synchronize with equipment operating at 23.97 Hz (24/1.001) or 48 kHz, the black burst signal may carry an optional 10-field sequence for identification of the signal as speci- fied in SMPTE 318M. The timing reference synchronizing line is inserted on lines 15 and 278 of a NTSE 525 59.94 Hz signal. The first pulse (1) is always present at the start of the 10-field identification sequence. Pulses (2-5) which are between 0 and 4-frame count pulses follow this. The end pulse (6) is always absent on line 15 and always present on line 278.

name: SMPTE 318M Data Line
desc: SMPTE 318M data line is used for the follow- ing purposes: 1) Synchronization of digital audio operating at 48 kHz within a 525 59.94 Hz system; 2) Synchronization of 23.97 film rate material to 525 59.94 Hz system. Inserted on line 15 and 278 of NTSC 525 59.94 Hz signal.

name: SMPTE 322M
desc: Data stream format for the exchange of DV-based audio, data and compressed video over a Serial Data Transport Interface (SDTI or SMPTE 305M).

name: SMPTE 344M
desc: Defines a 540 Mbps serial digital interface for pro-video applications.

name: SMPTE 348M
desc: High data-rate serial data transport interface (HD-SDTI). This is a 1.485 Gbps serial interface based on SMPTE 292M that can be used to transfer almost any type of digital data, including MPEG-2 program streams, MPEG-2 transport streams, DV bit streams, etc. You cannot exchange material between devices that use different data types. Material that is created in one data type can only be transported to other devices that support the same data type. There are separate map documents that format each data type into the 348M transport.

name: SMPTE 372M
desc: Defines 4:4:4 RGB color space at up to 2.97 Gbps, used with dual-link HD-SDI on two coaxial cables. Supports 1080/60p and 1080/24Psf video.

name: SMPTE 424M
desc: Defines 4:4:4 RGB at up to 2.97 Gbps on a single coaxial cable. Known as 3G-SDI. Supports HDTV 1080p at 50 or 60 fps.

name: SMPTE/DCI P3
desc: A color space from the Digital Cinema Initiatives for digital cinema. Wider gamut than BT.709/sRGB but narrower than BT.2020.

name: SMPTE Format
desc: – In component television, these terms refer to the SMPTE standards for parallel component analog video interconnection. The SMPTE has standardized both an RGB system and a (Y, PR, PB) color difference system – a version of (Y, R-Y, B-Y).

name: SMPTE pattern
desc: A video test pattern with color, black, and white bands used as a standard for setting up video equipment.

name: SMPTE RP 154
desc: Standard that defines reference synchronizing signals for analog or digital 525 line systems including recommendations for black burst.

name: SMPTE RP 155
desc: Standard for digital audio reference levels for digital VTRs. This is being revised into a studio standard.

name: SMPTE RP 160
desc: Analog RGB and YPbPr video interface specification for pro-video HDTV systems.

name: SMPTE RP 165
desc: Standard for error detection and handling in serial digital component and composite systems.

name: SMPTE RP 168
desc: Standard for vertical interval switching points for 525/625 systems.

name: SMPTE RP 219
desc: High definition/standard definition compatible color bar signal (SMPTE color bars) and is a series of television test signals, original- ly developed as multi-format color bars of ARIB STD-B28 (ARIB color bars), and was proposed to SMPTE by ARIB. Users are able to select 1 of 3 types of stripe width, transient characteristics (rise/fall time) and a combination of I and Q axis segments.

name: SMPTE (Society of Motion Picture and Television Engineers)
desc: American standardizing body. SMPTE 240M is the first SMPTE HDEP standard, calling for 1125 scanning lines, 2:1 interlace, a 16:9 aspect ratio, and 60 fields per second, among other characteristics. It is identical to the HDEP standard approved by ATSC. It need not be SMPTE’s only HDEP standard, however. The Society has current standards for more  than ten different videotape recording formats, with more pending. There are indications that members of SMPTE’s WG-HDEP are interested in a progressively-scanned HDEP system, an evolution of the 1125-line interlace standard.

name: SMPTE ST 2059
desc: SMPTE's standard for synchronization of IP-based broadcast media using IEEE 1588 PTP. Defines timing requirements for aligning multiple audio and video streams in IP production environments.

name: SMPTE ST-2081
desc: Defines 4:4:4 RGB at up to 6 Gbps on a single coaxial cable. Known as 6G-SDI. Supports 2160p at 30 fps or 1080p at 120 fps.

name: SMPTE ST-2082
desc: Defines 4:4:4 RGB at up to 12 Gbps on a single coaxial cable. Known as 12G-SDI. Supports 2160p at 60 fps.

name: SMPTE ST 2110
desc: Suite of SMPTE standards for professional media over IP. ST 2110-20: uncompressed video; ST 2110-30: AES67 audio; ST 2110-40: ancillary data; ST 2110-22: JPEG-XS compressed video. Uses RTP/UDP transport and PTP synchronization.

name: SMPTE ST 2110-20
desc: The SMPTE standard defining transport of uncompressed professional video over IP networks, specifying RTP packetization of video essence, sampling formats, and timing.

name: SMPTE ST 2110-21
desc: The SMPTE standard defining traffic shaping and delivery timing for uncompressed IP video streams, ensuring consistent packet pacing to prevent buffer overflow at receivers.

name: SMPTE ST 2110-30
desc: The SMPTE standard defining transport of linear PCM audio over IP networks, based on AES67. Supports multiple channels at 48 kHz with precise PTP timing synchronization.

name: SMPTE ST 2110-40
desc: The SMPTE standard defining transport of ancillary data (captions, timecode, metadata) over IP networks alongside ST 2110-20 video streams.

name: SMPTE Standard
desc: See the SMPTE format discussion.

name: SMPTE Time Code
desc: An 80-bit standardized edit time adopted by SMPTE. A binary time code denoting hours, minutes, seconds and video frames. See also Time Code.

name: SMPTE-VITC
desc: SMPTE’s vertical interval time code (VITC) format standard. The term VITC, used alone, usually refers to SMPTE-VITC.

name: SMS (Subscriber Management System)
desc: A combination of hardware and software as well as human activities that help organize and operate the company business. The SMS is a part of a technical chain, referred to as the Entitlement Control Chain. The SMS contains all customer relevant information and is responsible for keeping track of placed orders, credit limits, invoicing and payments, as well as the generation of reports and statistics.

name: SMTP
desc: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol. Internet standard for email exchange across systems and networks.

name: SNA
desc: Systems Network Architecture entered the market in 1974 as a hierarchical, single-host network structure. Since then, SNA has developed steadily in two directions. The first direction involved tying together mainframes and unintelligent terminals in a master-to-slave relationship. The second direction transformed the SNA architecture to support a cooperative-processing environment, whereby remote terminals link up  with mainframes as well as each other in a peer-to-peer relationship (termed Low Entry Networking (LEN) by IBM). LEN depends on the imple- mentation of two protocols: Logical Unit 6.2, also known as APPC, and Physical Unit 2.1 which affords point-to-point connectivity between peer nodes without requiring host computer control. The SNA model is con- cerned with both logical and physical units. Logical units (LUs) serve as points of access by which users can utilize the network. LUs can be viewed as terminals that provide users access to application programs and other services on the network. Physical units (PUs) like LUs are not defined  within SNA architecture, but instead, are representations of the devices  and communication links of the network.

name: Snake
desc: Thick Cable Containing A Combination Of Many Individual Audio Cables Inside A Common, Sturdy Outer Jacket.

name: SnakeSys
desc: ChamSys's range of network-to-DMX interface nodes for MagicQ systems. SnakeSys devices convert Art-Net or sACN signals from the console's Ethernet output to physical DMX512 connections for connecting to fixtures.

name: Snapshot
desc: See Scene. A saved state of all or selected console parameters that can be recalled at any time. Used in theatre and broadcast to automate mix changes between scenes or songs.

name: SNAP (Subnetwork Access Protocol)
desc: Internet protocol that operates between a network entity in the subnetwork and a network entity in the end system. SNAP specifies a standard method of encapsulating IP datagrams and ARP messages on IEEE networks. The SNAP entity in the end system makes use of the services of the subnetwork and performs three key func- tions: data transfer, connection management, and QoS selection.

name: Snap Zoom
desc: A quick manual zoom of a subject, usually performed by rapidly adjusting the focal ring.

name: SNG (Satellite News Gathering)
desc: The temporary and occasional trans- mission with short notice of television or sound for broadcasting purposes, using highly portable or transportable uplink earth stations operating in the framework of the fixed-satellite service.

name: SNMP
desc: Simple Network Management Protocol. Standard protocol for collecting device status information and triggering alerts from network-connected equipment. Used for monitoring AV device health, generating alerts, and remote management.

name: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol)
desc: A widely used net- work monitoring and control protocol. Data is passed from SNMP agents, which are hardware and/or software processes reporting activity in each network device (hub, router, bridge, etc.) to the workstation console used to oversee the network. The agents return information contained in a MIB (Management Information Base), which is a data structure that defines what is obtainable from the device and what can be controlled (turned off, on, etc.). Originating in the UNIX community, SNMP has become widely used on all major platforms.

name: Snoot
desc: A Metal Tube, Available In Various Sizes And Shapes, That Mounts On The Front Of Some Luminaires To Control Light Spill. Some Snoots Used In The Film And Video Industries Have A Means To Install Circular Rings, I.E., Apertures, To The Front In Order To Change The Size Of The Opening.

name: Snow
desc: a) Heavy random noise. b) White flashes appearing in the video image caused by random noise and/or loss of magnetic particles.

name: SNR
desc: See Signal-to-Noise Ratio.

name: SNR Scalability
desc: A type of scalability where the Enhancement Layer(s) contain only coded refinement data for the DCT coefficients of the base layer. SNR scalability is aimed at transmission in noisy environments, and offers a form of graceful degradation. Under poor reception conditions, only the lower layer (which is covered by the highest error protection) is decod- ed. The picture quality is then not the best, of course, but at least a picture is available. The alternative is a total loss of picture (the “brick wall” effect) below a certain SNR. The lower layer and the Enhancement Layer operate with the same resolution, but the Enhancement Layer may contain the higher frequencies of the picture.

name: S/N (Signal/Noise)
desc: As in “s/n ratio”.

name: snub
desc: during nominations or awards proceedings , when aprominent , leading , or favored performer / director / crewmember or film is inexplicably excluded or denied an award ornomination

name: SOC
desc: Security Operations Center. A location where staff monitors security tasks including live camera feeds, access control, lighting, alarms, and vehicle barriers.

name: So Cable
desc: A Type Of Hard Service, Oil Resistant Electric Cable Rated For Extra Hard Usage.

name: Socapex
desc: Heavy-Duty, 19-Conductor Cable That Is Typically Used To Carry 6 Circuits Of Power For Multiple Lighting Fixtures. Can Also Be Used For Speaker-Level Connections.

name: Social VR
desc: A Way People Can Interact With One Another In Simulated Worlds Within Virtual Reality.

name: Socket
desc: 1) Derived From "Sockett", See The Definition For Lampholder 2) A Female Connector. 3) A Hollow, Cylindrical Shaped Mounting Item Used To Accept Studs, Generally Equipped With A Tee-Handle Or Bolt For Setting Into The Stud. This Prevents The Receiver-Stud Combination From Unintentionally Uncoupling, And Can Also Prevent The Stud From Rotating Within. 4) A Relay Holding Device That Comprises Terminals For Making Electrical Connections To The Socket, And Contacts That Make The Electrical Connections To The Relay. 5) A Miniature Hole With Two Internal Contacts On A Diode Pin Matrix For The Insertion And Electrical Connection Of Diode Pins. 6) In General, Any Threaded, Round Opening. 7) The Part Of A Carbon Arc Luminaire That Holds The Carbon Rods.

name: Soffit Luminaire
desc: An Architectural Luminaire That Directs Light Downward From The Cornice Or Soffit Between The Wall And Ceiling To Light The Wall Surface Below.

name: SOF (Sound On Film)
desc: The sound track is on the film itself.

name: Soft
desc: a) The opposite of “hard”. b)  As applied to a photographic emulsion or developer, having a low contrast. c) As applied to the lighting of a set, diffuse, giving a flat scene in which the brightness difference between highlights and shadows is small.

name: Soft Border
desc: a) The quality of diffusion between adjacent visual areas in a picture around a pattern. b) A wipe pattern border which is missing between the “A” bus video and “B” bus video on the edges to give a soft effect. This has no matte color added.

name: Soft Edge
desc: An edge between two video signals in which the signals are mixed together for a soft transition effect, used on both patterns and keys.

name: Soft Edit
desc: An electronic edit that maintains source clips in memory and tracking processes so that edits can be modified without starting from scratch.

name: soft - focus
desc: a cinematographic effect in which a filter , vaseline or gauzelike substance placed over the camera lens reduces theclarity or sharpness of focus , blurs the image , and produces adiffused , hazy light ; often used to enhance romantic ordreamy scenes , or to remove wrinkle lines from an actor'sface

name: Soft goods
desc: All fabric curtains used on stage, including blacks, cyclorama, backdrop, legs, borders, and any other drapery.

name: Soft Key
desc: a) A selector on the display that changes state or initiates an action when you touch it on screen. You use soft keys to select test signals or a sub-window of functions or to enter a file name. b) A soft key’s func- tion changes to match the block above it, in the bottom line of the screen. c) The softening of a key edge by reducing the gain of the keyer.

name: Soft Light
desc: 1) Illumination That Produces Shadows With A Soft Edge. 2) A Luminaire That Provides Such Illumination.

name: Soft Lighting
desc: Lighting That Tends To “Wrap” Around Objects, Casting Gentle Shadows And Creating Soft Edges; Occurs When A Light Source Is Large Relative To The Subject

name: Softness
desc: A blending or mixing along lines or edges in an image.

name: Soft Patch
desc: A Term Used To Describe A Patch System Whereby The Dimmers Can Be Interchangeably Assigned To Any One Of Any Number Of Channels. This Type Of Patch System Is Usually Found On Memory Boards.

name: Soft Patching
desc: The Routing Of A Signal Within A Digital Device, Often The Software Of A Digital Mixing Board, Without Physically Changing Any Connections.

name: Soft RAID
desc: A Raid System Implemented By Low Level Software In The Host System Instead Of A Dedicated Raid Controller. While Saving On Hardware, Operation Consumes Some Of The Host'S Power.

name: Soft Room
desc: Room With Highly Sound Absorptive Surfaces.

name: Software
desc: Operating instructions loaded into computer memory from disk that controls how system hardware will execute its operation. See Programs.

name: Software Decoder
desc: Software providing a means to decode audio/video streams without requiring a dedicated hardware appliance. Used on PCs via browser, media player, or special application.

name: Software-defined AV
desc: An AV distribution architecture where routing, processing, and control are managed by software on commodity hardware or in the cloud, rather than purpose-built AV matrix hardware.

name: Software Effect
desc: An effect that must be rendered by an editing application before it can be played back.  Contrast with Real-Time

name: Software Option
desc: Any software product that you buy other than the standard system software that comes on your system disk.

name: Soft Wipe
desc: A split screen or wipe effect with a soft border or edge where the two images join.

name: SOG
desc: Sync On Green. A video format where the composite sync signal (horizontal and vertical) is superimposed on the green channel of an RGB video signal.

name: Solarization
desc: Special effect in which the lightest and darkest values of a picture are made dark while the middle tones become light. An ADO effect. Also a photo-optic process.

name: Solder Bridge
desc: Glob of excess solder that shorts two conductors. A common problem on production PC boards.

name: Solid
desc: Polygons meshed together to create closed volumes. It is a compact set of contiguous points in three-dimensional space.

name: Solid State
desc: 1) A General Term Used To Describe An Electronic Component That Uses Immobile Solids, Usually Semiconductors, To Do What Moving Parts, Liquids Or Gases Once Did. Transistors, Thyristors, And Diodes Are Examples Of Solid State Components. 2) A Term Used To Describe An Apparatus That Uses These Components.

name: Solid State Drive ( SSD )
desc: A Hard Drive With No Moving Parts , Which Improves Performance And Reliability ; M2 Ssd'S Arefaster , Enabling Optimal Virtual Production Capabilities . See Also Hdd .

name: Solid State Relay (Ssr)
desc: A Relay That Uses Thyristors In Lieu Of An Electromagnetically Operated Switch. These Items Are Usually Available In A Small, Cube Shaped, Low Profile Package With Terminals For Making The Electrical Connections.

name: soliloquy
desc: a dramatic monologue delivered by a single actor with no oneelse onstage ; sometimes expressed as a ' thinking aloud 'dialogue of inner reflections ; delivered by a character to himor herself , or directly to the audience ; contrast to an aside .

name: Solo
desc: A Button On A Mixing Console That Sends That Channel To The Headphone Buss,Allowing The Listener To Hear That Channel (Plus Additionally Soloed Channels) In Isolation.

name: Solo Safe
desc: A Function Found On Many Large Format Mixing Consoles To Prevent Certain Channels From Being Muted When Others Are Soloed. This Would Typically Be Used To Maintain The Signals From Fx Returns, For Example, So That A Soloed Channel Can Be Auditioned With Its Related Effects.

name: Son
desc: The metal disc produced from a mother disc in the replication process. Fathers or sons are used in molds to stamp discs.

name: Sone
desc: The Unit Of Measurement For Subjective Loudness.

name: Sonet
desc: Synchronous Optical Network. A Set Of Standards For The Digital Transmission Of Information Over Fiber Optics. Based On Increments Of 51 Mbps. It Was Developed To Cost Effectively Support Broadband Services And Multi-Vendor Internetworking.

name: SONET (Synchronous Optical Network)
desc: A fibre optic standard with data rates ranging from 51.84 Mbps up to Gbps.

name: Sonic Welded Assembly
desc: Refers to the joining of the two plastic parts of a cassette by the use of a sonic weld, actually melting the plastic at the point of joining.

name: Sony
desc: First company to sell an HDEP recorder, making HDEP practical. Also the strongest HDEP proponent, spending a great deal to promote it and going so far as to display the products of some of its competitors, as long as they complied with the 1125 scanning-line system.

name: Sorting
desc: The arranging of clips in a bin column in numerical or alphabetical order, depending on the column the user selects.

name: Sound
desc: Sound Is Vibrational Disturbance, Exciting Hearing Mechanisms, Transmitted In A Predictable Manner Determined By The Medium Through Which It Propagates. To Be Audible The Disturbance Must Fall Within The Frequency Range 20Hz To 20,000Hz.

name: Sound Absorption
desc: (1) The Process Of Dissipating Sound Energy. (2) The Property Of Possessed By Materials, Objects And Structures Such As Rooms Of Absorbing Sound Energy. (3) A: [L2]; Metric Sabin---In A Specified Frequency Band, The Measure Of The Magnitude Of The Absorptive Property Of A Material, An Object, Or A Structure Such As A Room.

name: Sound Absorption Coefficient, *
desc: [Dimensionless]; Metric Sabin/M* ---Of A Surface, In A Specified Frequency Band, The Measure Of The Absorptive Property Of A Material As Approximated By The Method Of Test Method C423. Ideally, The Fraction Of The Randomly Incident Sound Power Absorbed Or Otherwise Not Reflected.

name: Sound Attenuation
desc: The Reduction Of The Intensity Of Sound As It Travels From The Source To A Receiving Location. Sound Absorption Is Often Involved As, For Instance, In A Lined Duct. Spherical Spreading And Scattering Are Other Attenuation Mechanisms.

name: Soundboard
desc: See Mixing Console

name: Sound Booth
desc: Term for a small acoustically dead room from which an announcer will record voice overs.

name: Sound Card
desc: A Dedicated Interface To Transfer Audio Signals In And Out Of A Computer;Can Be Installed Internally, Or Connected Externally And Are Available In A Wide Range Of Formats.

name: Soundcheck
desc: The process of testing and setting up audio levels, EQ, and monitor mixes for performers before a show. Includes individual instrument checks, monitor mix setup, and overall system balance.

name: Soundcraft Si series
desc: Soundcraft's professional digital live sound console range (Si Impact, Si Expression, Si Performer). Mid-range consoles popular in regional touring, houses of worship, and installed sound.

name: Soundcraft Vi series
desc: Soundcraft's high-end professional touring console range (Vi1000, Vi3000, Vi5000, Vi7000). Harman brand with extensive connectivity and built-in Studer preamps.

name: Sound Designer
desc: Individual Responsible For All Creative And Technical Aspects Of Sound For A Production.

name: Sound Designer II
desc: A trademark of Avid Technology, Inc.  An audio file format used for the import and export of digital audio tracks.

name: Sound Digitizer
desc: A device that records sounds and stores them as computer files.

name: Sound Energy Density, D
desc: [Ml-1T-2]; J/M---The Quotient Obtained When The Sound Energy In A Region Is Divided By The Volume Of The Region. The Sound Energy Density At A Point Is The Limit Of That Quotient As The Volume That Contains The Point Approaches Zero.

name: Sound Energy, E
desc: [Ml2T-2]; J-Energy Added To An Elastic Medium By The Presence Of Sound, Consisting Of Potential Energy In The Form Of Deviations From Static Pressure And Of Kinetic Energy In The Form Of Particle Velocity.

name: Sound Insulation
desc: The Capacity Of A Structure To Prevent Sound From Reaching A Receiving Location. Sound Energy Is Not Necessarily Absorbed; Impedance Mismatch, Or Reflection Back Toward The Source, Is Often The Principal Mechanism.

name: Sound Intensity, I
desc: [Mt-3]; W/M2 The Quotient Obtained When The Average Rate Of Energy Flow In A Specified Direction And Sense Is Divided By The Area, Perpendicular To That Direction, Through Or Toward Which It Flows. The Intensity At A Point Is The Limit Of That Quotient S The Area That Includes The Point Approaches Zero.

name: Sound Isolation
desc: The Degree Of Acoustical Separation Between Two Locations, Especially Adjacent Rooms.

name: Sound Level
desc: Of Airborne Sound, A Sound Pressure Level Obtained Using A Signal To Which A Standard Frequency- Weighting Has Been Applied.

name: Sound level meter
desc: An instrument designed to measure sound pressure level.

name: Sound-on-Sound
desc: A method by which material previously recorded on one track of a tape may be rerecorded on another track while simultane- ously adding new material to it.

name: Sound Power Level, Lp
desc: Of Airborne Sound, Ten Times The Common Logarithm Of The Ratio Of The Sound Power Under Consideration Of The Standard Reference Power Of 1 Pw. The Quantity So Obtained Is Expressed In Decibels.

name: Sound Power, W
desc: [Ml2T-3]; W---In A Specified Frequency Band, The Rate At Which Acoustic Energy Is Radiated From A Source. In General, The Rate Of Flow Of Sound Energy, Whether From A Source, Through An Area, Or Into An Absorber.

name: Sound Pressure Level (Db Spl)
desc: The Loudness Of An Acoustic Wave Stated In Db.

name: Sound Pressure Level (Spl)
desc: Given In Decibels (Db) Is An Expression Of Loudness Or Volume. A 10Db Increase In Spl Represents A Doubling In Volume. Live Orchestral Music Reaches Brief Peaks In The 105Db Range And Live Rock Easily Goes Over 120Db.

name: Sound Pressure Levels (SPL)
