desc: a) A measure of the sound pressure created by a sound, usually in the units of dB referred to 0.0002 microbar of pressure. b) A measure of acoustic wave force. The force that sound can exert against an object; our ear drums are an example. It is measured in dB and is “0” referenced to 1 dyne per square centimeter.

name: Sound Pressure, P
desc: [Ml-1T-2]; Pa-A Fluctuating Pressure Superimposed On The Static Pressure By The Presence Of Sound. In Analogy With Alternating Voltage Its Magnitude Can Be Expressed In Several Ways, Such As Instantaneous Sound Pressure Or Peak Sound Pressure, But The Unqualified Term Means Root-Mean-Square Sound Pressure. In Air, The Static Pressure Is Barometric Pressure.

name: Sound Receiver
desc: One Or More Observation Points At Which Sound Is Evaluated Or Measured. The Effect Of Sound On An Individual Receiver Is Usually Evaluated By Measurements Near The Ear Or Close To The Boy.

name: Sound Reinforcement
desc: The Process Of Amplifying A Sound With A Pa System.

name: Sound Spectrograph
desc: An Instrument That Displays The Time, Level, And Frequency Of A Signal.

name: Soundstage
desc: A Listening Term That Refers To The Placement Of A Stereo Image In A Fashion That Replicates The Original Performance. A Realistic Soundstage Has Proportional Width, Depth And Height.

name: soundstage( or stage )
desc: a large , soundproof area / room in a studio used in filmproduction , where elaborate sets are constructed , to allowfilm - makers greater control over climate , lighting , and sound ,security , and spectators .

name: Sound Supervisor
desc: Department Head For Sound At A Theatre Company.

name: soundtrack
desc: technically , this term refers to the audio component of amovie , including the dialogue , musical score , narration , andsound effects , that accompany the visual components .Popularly , it refers to a collection of songs heard during themovie , and often sold as an album .

name: Sound Transmission Class, Stc
desc: A Single-Number Rating, Calculated In Accordance With Classification E413 Using Values Of Sound Transmission Loss. It Provides An Estimate Of The Performance Of A Partition In Certain Common Sound Insulation Problems.

name: Sound Transmission Coefficient, R
desc: [Dimensionless]---Of A Partition, In A Specified Frequency Band, The Fraction Of The Airborne Sound Power Incident On The Partition That Is Transmitted By The Partition And Radiated On The Other Side.

name: Sound Transmission Loss, Tl
desc: Of A Partition, In A Specified Frequency Band, Ten Times The Common Logarithm Of The Ratio Of The Airborne Sound Power Incident On The Partition To The Sound Power Transmitted By The Partition And Radiated On The Other Side. The Quantity So Obtained Is Expressed In Decibels.

name: Sound Waves
desc: Sound Waves Can Be Thought Of Like The Waves In Water. Frequency Determines The Length Of The Waves; Amplitude Or Volume Determines The Height Of The Waves. At 20Hz, The Wavelength Is 56 Feet Long! These Long Waves Give Bass Its Penetrating Ability, (Why You Can Hear Car Boomers Blocks Away).

name: Source
desc: Video producing equipment such as cameras, tape recorders or character generators.

name: Source Clip
desc: One of the lowest level building blocks of a sequence composition.  See also Clip, Master Clip, Subclip.

name: Source Code
desc: Program written in other than machine language. May be assembly language or a high-level language.

name: Source Coding
desc: Coding that uses a model of the source from which parameters are extracted and transmitted to the decoder. When used particularly for voice, the coders are called vocoders.

name: Source Current
desc: Current output capability of a device.

name: Source Four
desc: ETC's iconic ellipsoidal reflector spotlight (profile/Leko), first introduced in 1992. Known for its efficient HPL lamp, interchangeable lens tubes, four-blade shutters, and gobo slot. The Source Four LED Series 3 is the current LED version.

name: Source Input Format (SIF)
desc: The luminance component is defined as 352 pixels x 240 lines at 30 Hz for NTSC or 352 pixels x 288 lines at 25 Hz for PAL and SECAM. Defined such that the data rates are the same for field rates for 60 HZ and 50 Hz.

name: Source Intensity
desc: This Applies To Each Source In The Potentially Obtrusive Direction, Outside Of The Area Being Lit.

name: Source (Menu)
desc: The function that changes the aspect ratio and size of the image. The word Source refers to the image generated by the input video, which occupies “Source Space” on the screen. a) Source Aspect uses the X and Y axis b) Source Size uses the Z axis. The image does not move.

name: Source Mode
desc: A method of assembly that determines in what order the edit controller reads the edit decision list (EDL) and assembles the final tape. There are five different types of source mode: A-Mode, B-Mode, C-Mode, D-Mode and E-Mode.

name: Source Monitor
desc: The interface window of Adobe Premiere that displays clips to be edited.

name: Source Room
desc: In Architectural Acoustical Measurements, The Room That Contains The Noise Source Or Sources.

name: Source Side
desc: In color correction, the first of two available levels of color adjustment. Corrections made on the source side typically seek to restore the original color characteristics of a clip or achieve basic clip-to-clip color consistency among the clips in a sequence. See also Program Side.

name: Source Stream
desc: A single, nonmultiplexed stream of samples before compression coding.

name: Source Synchronizing Generator
desc: A synchronizing pulse generator used to drive a specific piece of source equipment. It is referenced to a master reference synchronizing generator.

name: Source/Tape Switch
desc: A control found on control amplifiers with tape monitor jacks, and on recorders with monitor heads; allows comparison of the signal being fed to the tape (source) with the signal just recorded.

name: Source Timing Modules
desc: A synchronizing generator on a module that is used to adjust the timing of a specific piece of source equipment. It is kept in time by a reference sync pulse generator.

name: Southwestern Bell
desc: A Baby Bell, and the first organization to transmit HDHEP 9 as opposed to some bandwidth-reduced form of ATV) a long distance, via optical fiber.

name: Space
desc: The reflective area of a writable optical disc. Equivalent to a land.

name: spaghetti western
desc: a western , low - budget B - movie filmed in Italy ( or Spain )during the 60s , usually characterized by low productionvalues , sparse dialogue .

name: Span
desc: A rigging term identifying the distance between two hang points or ground-supported points.

name: Spanning Tree
desc: IEEE 802.1d. A protocol preventing network loops and multiple paths between source and destination. Prevents multicast video traffic from flooding networks.

name: Spanset
desc: A round rigging sling made of nylon strands inside a polyester cover for hanging heavy loads. Similar to a Gac Flex but uses nylon instead of steel cable.

name: Sparkle
desc: An ADO DigiTrail effect.

name: Spatial
desc: Relating to the area of an image. Video can be defined by its spatial characteristics (information from the horizontal plane and vertical plane) and its temporal characteristics (information at different instances of time).

name: Spatial Compression
desc: A compression method that reduces the data contained within a single video frame by identifying areas of similar color and eliminating the redundancy. See also Codec.

name: Spatial Domain
desc: Waveforms are two dimensional functions of location in space, f (x,y).

name: Spatial Encoding
desc: The process of compressing a video signal by eliminating redundancy between adjacent pixels in a frame.

name: Spatial  Navigation
desc: Self Orientation And Locomotion In Virtual Worlds.

name: Spatial Prediction
desc: Prediction derived from a decoded frame of the lower layer decoder used in spatial scalability.

name: Spatial resolution
desc: The Number Of Pixels Horizontally And Vertically In A Digital Image.

name: Spatial Sampling
desc: Where an image changes a given number of times per unit distance and is sampled at some other number of times per unit distance as opposed to temporal sampling where the input changes with respect to time at some frequency and is sampled at some other frequency.

name: Spatial Scalability
desc: A type of scalability where an Enhancement Layer also uses predictions from pel data derived from a lower layer without using motion vectors. The layers can have different frame sizes, frame rates or chroma formats. Spatial scalability offers a layering of the picture resolution, suitable for HDTV transmissions, for instance. By decoding of the lower layer, a “normal” picture is obtained, and by decoding of the Enhancement Layer, the HDTV picture may be constructed.

name: Spatio-Temporal Filtering
desc: Filtering in both space and time.

name: Spatio-Temporal Spectrum
desc: A three-dimensional representation of the energy distribution of a television signal. The three dimensions are horizontal, vertical, and time.

name: S/PDIF
desc: Sony/Philips Digital Interconnect Format. A protocol for compressed or uncompressed digital audio found in products using 75-ohm coaxial or TOSLINK fiber optic connections. Common in CD and DVD players.

name: SPDIF (Sony/Philips Digital Interface)
desc: This is a consumer interface used to transfer digital audio. A serial, self-clocking scheme is used, based on a coax or fiber interconnect. The audio samples may be 16-24 bits each. 16 different sampling rates are supported, with 32, 44.1, and 48 kHz being the most common. IEC 60958 now fully defines this interface for consumer and professional applications.

name: S/PDIF (Sony/Philips Digital Interface Format)
desc: A consumer version of the AES/EBU digital audio interconnection standard. The format uses a 75-ohm coaxial cable with RCA connectors and has a nominal peak- to-peak value of 0.5V. The frame ordering differs slightly than that of AES/EBU, specifically in the channel status information. Refer to AES/EBU interface.

name: Speaker
desc: A Transducer Used To Convert An Electrical Signal Into Acoustic Energy.

name: Speaker (conical) coverage
desc: Ceiling speakers project audio in a conical pattern. The higher the mounting, the larger the coverage circle, requiring more wattage to maintain the same SPL.

name: Speaker Level
desc: Signals Stronger Than Line Level That Are Used To Drive Headphones And Loudspeaker. Produced By Amplifiers.

name: Speaker polarity
desc: Positive polarity: cone moves forward when positive voltage applied. Speakers must be wired in phase so all cones move in the same direction simultaneously.

name: Speakon
desc: A Type Of Cable Connector Mostly Used In Professional Audio Systems For Connecting Loudspeakers To Amplifiers. Speakon Connectors Are A Higher Current-Carrying Alternative To Other Connectors For Loudspeakers. Nl4 Is A Type Of Speakon Connector That Has Four Electrical Connections.

name: Special
desc: Lighting Term: A Lantern (Qv) - Usually A Spotlight (Qv) - Not Used For General Illumination But For A Special Effect, Such A Lighting A Single Actor In One Place.

name: Special Effects
desc: Artistic effects added to a video production in order to enhance the production by creating drama, enhancing the mood or furthering the story. Special effects may vary from the limited addition of patterns or the mixing of several video images together, to sophisticated digital effects such as picture compression, page flipping and three- dimensional effects. Special effects are usually created using SEGs such as those included in the Video Equalizer, Video TitleMaker 2000 and Digital Video Mixer.

name: Special Effects Generator
desc: A video component that processes video signal and has the ability to manipulate the signal with a variety of wipes and distortions.

name: special effects( or F / X , SFX , SPFX ,or EFX )
desc: a broad , wide - ranging term used by the film industry meaningto create fantastic visual and audio illusions that cannot beaccomplished by normal means , such as travel into space .Many visual ( photographic ) or mechanical ( physical ) filmictechniques or processes are used to produce specialillusionary effects , such as optical and digital effects , CGI , incamera effects , the use of miniatures / models , mattes , rearcamera projections , stop - motion animation , bluescreens , fullscale mockups , pyrotechnics ( squibs ( miniature explosions ,i.e. a gunshot ) ) , stunt men , animatronics ( electronic puppets ) ,rain / snow / wind machines , etc .; F / X are coordinated by thevisual effects and the special effects supervisors ; knownnegatively as trick photography ; see also visual effects - asub - category of special effects . See this site's Milestones inSpecial / Visual Effects in Film History .

name: Special Magnetic Moment
desc: The value of the saturation moment per unit weight of a magnetic material expressed in emu/gm. The specific magnetic moment is the most convenient quantity in which to express the saturation magnetization of fine particle materials.

name: Specific Airflow Resistance, R
desc: [Ml-2T-1]. Mks Rayl (Pa-S/M)---The Product Of The Airflow Resistance Of A Specimen And Its Area. This Is Equivalent To The Quotient Of The Air Pressure Difference Across The Specimen Divided By The Linear Velocity, Measured Outside The Specimen, Of Airflow Through The Specimen.

name: Specification-Grade Luminaire
desc: A Luminaire That Is Produced With High-Quality Construction And Materials. See Also Commodity-Grade Luminaire.

name: Spectra Key
desc: An enhancement to a standard RGB chroma key, employing a patented chroma nulling circuitry, thereby removing any color from the background video. This enables keys to be performed through glass or smoke or with shadows. This would otherwise not be possible without the blue or green fringing effect typical of standard RGB keyers.

name: Spectral Analysis
desc: a) Determination of the monochromatic components of the luminance considered. b) Objective detailed specification of a white reference, of a color, or of the transmission function, with respect to wavelength and intensity.

name: Spectral Balance
desc: Balance Across The Entire Frequency Spectrum Of The Audio Range.

name: Spectral Response
desc: The Portion Of The Color Spectrum A Given Light Source Emits . Led Panels , Due To Their Use Ofrgb Led Bulbs , Have A Reduced Spectral Response Compared To Full - Spectrum Cinema Lights .

name: Spectral Sensitivity
desc: Quotient of the detector output dY(lambda) by the monochromatic detector output dXc(lambda)=Xclambda(lambda)dlambda in the wavelength interval dlambda as a function of the wavelength lambda.

name: Spectrograph
desc: A visual display showing frequency content of audio over time, used for identifying problematic frequencies, feedback, and sound quality issues.

name: Spectrophotometric Match
desc: Spectrophotometry determines the spectral transmittance and the spectral reflectance of objects … to compare at each wavelength the radiant flux leaving the object with that incident upon it. A spectrophotometric match thus occurs only when the two objects being compared are identical in their color structure. Such a match will be maintained regardless of viewing conditions. Spectrophotometric matches are seldom encountered and rarely necessary; in practice, the usual objective is to achieve a metameric match. Metameric matches, however, appear identical only under one set of specified viewing conditions.

name: Spectrum
desc: a) In electromagnetics, spectrum refers to the description of a signal’s amplitude versus its frequency components. b) In optics, spectrum refers to the light frequencies composing the white light which can be seen as rainbow colors.

name: Spectrum Allocation
desc: Designation of certain bandwidths at certain fre- quencies for certain purposes. For example, channel 2 has been allocated 6 MHz of bandwidth from 54 MHz to 60 MHz for television broadcasting. All ATV transmission schemes require some form or another of spectrum allocation. See also Frequency Allocation Table.

name: Spectrum Analyzer
desc: An electronic device that can show the spectrum of an electric signal.

name: Spectrum Management
desc: The Process Of Managing All Of The Various Wireless Hardware , Including Non - Visible Infraredtracking , Etc , On A Stage Or Volume To Avoid Crosstalk And Interference .

name: Specular
desc: An intense highlight caused when light reflects off an object in an image. A specular is not used as the basis for determining the true white point for an image.

name: Specular Reflections
desc: Mirrorlike Reflections Of Sound (Angle Of Incidence Equals Angle Of Reflection) From A Flat Surface. Reflections That Do Not Spread Out.

name: Specular Surface
desc: A Surface From Which The Reflection Is Predominantly Directional. Specular Surfaces Are Mirror-Like Or Shiny, As Opposed To Diffuse.

name: Speech Intelligibility
desc: A Measure Of Sound Clarity That Indicates The Ease Of Understanding Speech. It Is A Complex Function Of Psychoacoustics, Signal-To-Noise Ratio Of The Sound Source, And Direct-To-Reverberant Energy Within The Listening Environment.

name: Speed
desc: The point at which videotape playback reaches a stable speed, all servos are locked, and there is enough pre-roll time for editing, recording, or digitizing.

name: Speed of light
desc: 2.998 × 10⁸ meters per second.

name: SPG (Sync Pulse Generator)
desc: A source of synchronization pulses.

name: Spherical Divergence
desc: Sound Diverges Spherically From A Point Source In Free Space.

name: Spike
desc: To Mark With Tape Where Performers Equipment And Monitors Are On Stage, In Case They Need To Move Between Soundcheck And The Show.

name: Spike Tape
desc: The Tape Used To Mark Equipment Positions On Stage, Usually About ½” Wide And Brightly Colored.

name: Spill Ring
desc: A Metal Plate Placed Around The Lamp Socket Base Of Some Luminaires To Prevent Light Leak.

name: spin - off
desc: refers to a derivative work ( film or TV ) , either a sequel or aprequel which includes characters from the previous original product ; contrast to a prequel , followup , serial , series , sequel or remake

name: SPI (Synchronous Parallel Interface) Spike
desc: See Overshoot.

name: Spl
desc: Abbreviation For Sound Pressure Level; A Measure Of The Intensity Of An Acoustic Sound Wave. Normally Specified In Terms Of Pascals For An Absolute Value Or Relative To The Typical Sensitivity Of Human Hearing.

name: Splaying
desc: Walls Are Splayed When They Are Constructed Somewhat "Off Square," I.E., A Few Degrees From The Normal Rectilinear Form.

name: Splice
desc: A physical join between pieces of tape or film. An edit in which the material already on the video or audio track is lengthened by the addition of new material spliced in at any point in the sequence. See also Overwrite.

name: Splice closure
desc: A housing protecting fiber optic splices from damage and sealing them from the environment.

name: Splice organizer
desc: A device facilitating the splicing and permanent storage of optical fibers.

name: Splice panel
desc: A rack or wall-mounted panel for organizing and splicing fiber optic cables, holding splice trays and slack storage.

name: Splice protector
desc: A fiber optics device providing protection and mechanical strength to a fusion splice for handling and storage in a splice tray.

name: Splice tray
desc: A container securing, organizing, and protecting individual spliced optical fibers.

name: Splicing
desc: Concatenation of, or switching between, two different streams of compressed data.

name: Splicing Tape
desc: A special pressure-sensitive, non-magnetic tape used for joining two lengths of magnetic tape.

name: Spline
desc:
a) A type of mathematical model used to represent curves. They are usually displayed as polylines with a large number of very small sides. The importance of splines is that they give very smooth curves for a rela- tively small number of points. b) In wooden ships, the curved skeleton of a hull is built by attaching bendable strips of wood to small, fixed, and angled blocks of wood. The strips are splines. In computer graphic splines, the blocks of wood are called control points. In computer graphics, curved lines are always visualized by drawing many short vectors. However, since each vector requires a fair amount of storage, curves are often stored in terms of their control points; whenever the curve is needed, the spline is recreated. Another advantage of storing splines as curves is the ease with which a spline curve is manipulated by moving its control points. Instead of moving the curve’s vectors one at a time, a large section of the curve is moved by dragging its control point. Splines convert discontinuity into smoothness. These properties make splines very useful in animation. When we create a keyframe for path animation, the object’s position becomes a control point for a spline that defines the entire path for all the in-between frames as well. This allows us to get smooth motion between all the keyframes, and avoid instantaneous (single frame) changes of direction.
Such changes would be highly unrealistic and could never yield satisfying animation. Another tremendous advantage of splines is that they are reso- lution independent. Magnifying and then redrawing a shape that is repre- sented by a spline does not reveal the short vectors that represent the curve on the screen, because these vectors are recalculated to take into account the new magnification. Spline represented objects can also be easily rotated or skewed in 3D, again with no loss in clarity. So called “vec- tor-based” systems make use of these features by representing fonts and shapes with splines, rather than the traditional bitmap. Bitmap systems, on the other hand, cannot represent or manipulate shapes nearly as handily.

name: Split Cross Fader
desc: A Pair Of Sliders On A Control Console That Performs A Cross Fade When Moved Side By Side, Or Can Be Moved Independently To Adjust Two Separate Control Settings At Different Rates.

name: Split Edit
desc: Type of edit transition where either the video or audio of the source is delayed from being recorded for a given time.

name: Split fade
desc: A crossfade where the incoming and outgoing cues have different timing, causing an asymmetrical or dipped transition. Controlled by separate In Time and Out Time values.

name: split - reel
desc: in the silent era , refers to two different short - subject films( each too brief for a separate screening ) that were joinedtogether on one reel for movie - house exhibition

name: split - screen
desc: the combination of two actions filmed separately by copyingthem onto the same negative and having them appear sideby - side within a single frame ( without overlapping ) ; a slightvariation on split - screen is termed multiple image ( differentimages are set alongside each other within a single frame ) ;split - screen is usually intended to signify simultaneous action ;also see bluescreen and matte shot

name: Split Screen Unit
desc: Equipment that simultaneously displays parts or  more than one image on a single monitor. It usually refers to four quadrant displays. Also called Quad Compressor.

name: Split Sync Scrambling
desc: Video scrambling technique, used with horizon- tal blanking inversion, active video inversion, or both. In split sync, the horizontal sync pulse is “split”, with the second half of the pulse at +100 IRE instead of the standard -40 IRE. Depending on the scrambling mode, either the entire horizontal blanking interval is inverted about the +30 IREaxis, the active video (after color burst and until the beginning of front porch blanking) is inverted about the +30 IRE axis, both are inverted, or neither is inverted. By splitting the horizontal sync pulse, a reference of both -40 IRE and +100 IRE is available to the descrambler. Since a portion of the horizontal sync is still at -40 IRE, some sync separators may still lock on the shortened horizontal sync pulses. However, the timing circuits that look for color burst a fixed interval after the beginning of horizontal sync may be confused. In addition, if the active video is inverted, some video information may fall below 0 IRE, possibly confusing sync detector circuits. The burst is always present at the correct frequency and timing, however, the phase is shifted 180 degrees when the horizontal blanking interval is inverted.

name: Splitter
desc: 1) Generally, Any Connector That Is Electrically Connected To Two Or More Other Connectors, All Constructed As A Single Unit. 2) A Twofer Or A Threefer.

name: Spl Meter
desc: A Device That Measure And Indicates The Sound Pressure Level.

name: SPL (Sound Pressure Level)
desc: The SPL of a sound is equal to twenty times the logarithm (base 10) of the ratio of the root-mean-square sound pressure to the reference sound pressure. As a point of reference, 0 dB-SPL equals the threshold of hearing, while 140 dB-SPL produces irreparable hearing damage.

name: spoiler
desc: information about the plot or ending of a film that maydamage or impair the enjoyment of the film if known ahead oftime ; usually , critics or reviewers warn readers with a ' spoileralert ' , or avoid revealing spoilers altogether . See Greatest PlotTwists and Spoilers

name: Spoking
desc: A form of buckling in which the tape pack is deformed into a shape which approximates a polygon.

name: spoof
desc: usually a comedic film that pays tribute to an earlier film in ahumorous way .

name: Spot
desc: Term used for a commercial.

name: Spot Block
desc: Any Block That Is Designed For Temporary, And Easily Movable Connection To A Gridiron Or Other Theatre Structure.

name: Spot Color Correction
desc: A color adjustment made to a specific part of a video image that is identified using drawing tools. See also secondary color correction.

name: Spot Lamp
desc: A Lamp That Provides A Relatively Narrow Beam Of Light.

name: Spot Light
desc: Generally, Any Of Several Types Of Luminaires Capable Of Emitting A Beam Pattern That Is Round, Or In Some Instances, Oval In Shape, But More Specifically This Term Refers To Fresnel Spotlights, Ellipsoidal Spotlights, And Follow Spots.

name: Spotline Rigging
desc: A Single Line Rigging System Designed To Be Easily Installed, Relocated, And Removed. They Are Often Rigged With Rope And Are Frequently Motorized.

name: Spreader Plates
desc: A Thin Plate Located On Counter Weight Arbor Rods, Spaced By The User At 2 Foot Intervals Between Counter Weights, To Prevent The Rods From Spreading Apart Under A Sudden Impact Load And Releasing The Counter Weights.

name: Spreadsheet view
desc: A grid-based display on professional consoles (Hog, ETC Eos, grandMA) showing the parameter values of all channels in a tabular format, allowing direct editing of individual channel values.

name: Sprites
desc: In MPEG-4, static background scenes. Sprites can have dimen- sions much larger than what will be seen in any single frame. A coordinate system is provided to position objects in relation to each other and the sprites. MPEG-4’s scene description capabilities are built on concepts  used previously by the Internet community’s Virtual Reality Modeling Language (VRML).

name: Sprocket
desc: A toothed driving wheel used to move film through various machine by engaging with the perforation holes.

name: Sputnik
desc: Nickname For The Tracking Marker Affixed To A Motion Picture Camera For Outside - In Trackingwithin An Led Volume . Also Referred To As The Crown .

name: SPX
desc: Yamaha's proprietary multi-effects processors built into Yamaha digital consoles. Provide reverb, delay, chorus, pitch shifting, and other effects. SPX processors are available as global effects returns in Yamaha CL, QL, and TF series.

name: Squareness
desc: A measure of magnetic behavior expressed as a ratio. 1.00 would be considered perfect and the normal range for magnetic material is 0.7 to 0.9.

name: Square Pixels
desc: Pixels generated in a television system having the same horizontal and vertical resolution. There is some evidence that a large mismatch between horizontal and vertical resolution prevents the higher resolution from being fully perceived by the human visual system. NTSC was created with square pixels with a resolution of approximately 330 by 330 lines.

name: Square Wave
desc: A Waveform Whose Crests And Troughs Form A Squared-Off Shape, As Opposed To The Smooth Curvature Of A Sine Wave.

name: Squeal
desc: Audible tape vibrations, primarily in the longitudinal mode, caused by frictional excitation at heads and guides.

name: Squeeze
desc: A change in aspect ratio. Anamorphic lenses sometimes squeeze a widescreen scene by a factor of two horizontally, so it will fit on a 1.33:1 aspect ratio frame. In projection, another anamorphic lens “expands” the squeeze (squeezes vertically) to restore the original aspect ratio. When a widescreen film is presented on television without being expanded, it is said to be squeezed. An unexpanded film print is said to be a squeeze print (the opposite is “flat”).

name: Squeezed Video
desc: See Anamorphic.

name: Squelch
desc: A Function That Mutes The Audio Output Of A Receiver When There Isn’T A Strong Enough Radio Frequency (Rf) Signal Present At The Antennas. Most Professional Level Wireless Microphones Have Adjustable Squelch.

name: Sr
desc: An Acronym For Sound Reinforcement, Which Refers To The Process (Or A System For) Amplify_x0002_Ing Acoustic And Electronic Sounds From A Per_x0002_Formance Or Speech So That A Large Audience Can Hear Clearly. Or, In Popular Music, So That A Large Audience Can Be Excited, Stunned Or Even Partially Deafened By The Tremendous Amplifica_x0002_Tion. The Term “Sr” Is To “Pa” (Public Address) As The Term “Environmental Cleanup Technolo_x0002_Gist” Is To “Garbage Collector”.

name: SRAM
desc: Static Ram. This Type Of Memory Chip In General Behaves Like Dynamic Ram (Dram) Except That Static Rams Retain Data In A Six-Transistor Cell Needing Only Power To Operate (Drams Require Clocks As Well). Because Of This, Current Available Capacity Is 4 Mbits--Lower Than Dram--And Costs Are Higher, But Speed Is Also Greater.

name: sRGB
desc: A color space widely used in computers, monitors, and the Internet. Incorporates the same color primaries as ITU-R BT.709.

name: SRI (Stanford Research Institute)
desc: SRI International owns DSRC, developer of the ACTV schemes.

name: SRM (Session and Resource Manager) SSA
desc: See Serial Storage Architecture.

name: SRT
desc: Secure Reliable Transport. Open-source protocol for low-latency streaming over unreliable public networks. Uses AES encryption and forward error correction. Developed by Haivision, adopted across broadcast and live streaming.

name: SRT Alliance
desc: An open-source initiative founded by Haivision in 2017 promoting the SRT protocol. Members include major broadcast and streaming companies. The SRT specification is publicly available on GitHub.

name: SRTP
desc: Secure RTP. Provides encryption, message authentication, integrity, and replay attack protection for RTP data in unicast and multicast applications.

name: SSA
desc: See: Serial Storage Architecture.

name: SSCG (Spread-Spectrum Clock Generator) SSI
desc: See Small Scale Integration.

name: SSH
desc: Secure Shell. Cryptographic network protocol for secure remote command-line access and configuration of network devices. Encrypts all communication. Standard for secure AV device management.

name: SSL
desc: Secure Socket Layer. A protocol for transmitting private documents over the Internet using a two-key cryptographic system.

name: SSL Live
desc: Solid State Logic's professional live sound console range (L100, L200, L300, L500, L550). Features SSL's renowned analog-modeled processing, True Solo monitoring, and extensive routing flexibility.

name: SST
desc: Single Stream Transport. One of the two DisplayPort transport modes (SST and MST). SST handles only one downstream device.

name: Stabilization
desc: A specialized form of motion tracking used to eliminate unwanted motion such as camera movement from a clip. Stabilization works by tracking an inherently unmoving object in the clip and reposition- ing each frame or field of video to keep that object stationary.

name: Stabilize
desc: a) Remove motion jitter and unwanted camera movement from a clip. b) To track an image in a clip.

name: Stack
desc: Block of successive memory locations that is accessible from one end on a last-in-first-out basis (LIFO). For most processors, the stack may be an block of successive locations in the read/write memory.

name: Stack Pointer
desc: Contains the address of the top of the stack. In general, the stack pointer is decremented immediately following the storage in the stack of each byte of information. Conversely, the stack pointer is incremented immediately before retrieving each byte of information from the stack.

name: Stage
desc: A Platform On Which Performances Are Given.

name: Stage areas
desc: The named zones of a stage from the performer's perspective: downstage (closest to audience), upstage (farthest), stage left, stage right, and center. Used for blocking.

name: Stage Box
desc: A Connection Box Terminating A Multicore Cable (See Snake), Usually Placed On A Stage For Easy Connections Of Multiple Microphone Cables To A Console.

name: Stagecraft
desc: Any technical aspect of theatre production, sometimes also referring to film and TV production. Covers all behind-the-scenes technical disciplines.

name: Stage crew
desc: The backstage workers who operate and maintain the technical elements of a production during performances — stagehands, fly operators, followspot operators, and spot operators.

name: Stage Directions
desc: This Refers To Specific Parts Of The Stage. Stage Left Is To The Performers'Left When Facing The Audience, And Stage Right Is To The Performers' Right. Down Stage Is Closer To The Audience, And Up Stage Is Behind The Performers. Center Stage Is Directly In The Middle. These Can Also Be Combined, Such As Up Stage Left, Or Down Stage Right.

name: Stagehand
desc: Part Of The Production Team, Also Called Hands, Whose Duties Include Moving Gear, Unpacking Cases And Setting Production Elements In Place Around The Venue, And Packing Production Gear Onto Trucks At The End Of The Show. In Many Cities, The Larger Venues Employ Union Stagehands, Who Use Collective Bargaining To Negotiate Rates And Benefits.

name: Stage Left
desc: See Stage Directions

name: Stage Left/Right
desc: The Left And Right Sides Of A Stage As Seen By An Actor Standing On Stage Facing The Audience.

name: Stage Lift
desc: A Section Of The Stage Floor That May Be Raised Or Lowered To Different Levels Above And Below The Stage By Some Mechanical (Typically Motorized) Means.

name: Stage Light
desc: A Luminaire Intended To Illuminate Any Portion Of, Or Anything On, A Stage Or Similar Performing Area, Exclusive Of Practical Lights And Work Lights.

name: Stage Manager
desc: (Sm) In Charge Of Everything That Happens Backstage: All Other Backstage Peronnel, Including Heads Of Departments, Report To Him. In The Professional Theatre, Once The Show Starts Its Run, He Takes Complete Control (Including Taking Any Rehearsals For Understudies Etc.), As The Director'S Job Is Finished Once He Has Given His Notes After The Final Dress Rehearsal.

name: Stage Monitor
desc: A Speaker Used By A Performer To Hear Audio Signal On Stage; Also Called A Wedge.

name: Stage Plug
desc: 1) A Male Pin Connector. 2) A Male Slip Connector.

name: Stage Pocket
desc: 1) In The Theatre Industry, A Piece Of Flush Mounted Power Distribution Equipment Comprising A Metal Box With A Hinged, Protective Cover, Notches In The Cover To Allow For Stage Cable To Enter The Cover In The Closed Position, And One Or More Female Connectors Mounted Internally For The Purpose Of Supplying Electricity To Luminaires, Or Other Electrical Apparatuses. Its Line Side Is Hard Wired. 2) In The Film And Video Industry, A Female Slip Connector.

name: Stage Right
desc: The Right Side Of The Stage When Facing The Audience.

name: Stage Volume
desc: The Level Of Sound Generated By The Performers And Their Instruments/Equipment On Stage.

name: Stage weights
desc: Part of a fly system. Iron bricks stacked on counterweight arbors to balance loads suspended from battens.

name: Staggered seating
desc: A seating arrangement where seats in alternating rows are offset laterally to improve sightlines between rows.

name: Staircase
desc: A pattern generated by the TV generator, consisting of equal width luminance steps of 0, +20, +40, +60, +80, and +100 IRE units and a constant amplitude chroma signal at color burst phase. Chroma amplitude is selectable at 20 IRE units (low stairs) or 40 IRE units (high stairs). The staircase pattern is useful for checking linearity of luminance and chroma gain, differential gain and differential phase.

name: Stamping
desc: The process of replicating optical discs by injecting liquid plastic into a mold containing a stamper (father or son). Also (inaccurately) called Mastering.

name: Stand Adapter
desc: An Apparatus Used For Converting One Type Of Mounting Hardware Attached To A Stand, Such As A Pin E.G., To Another, Such As A Receiver.

name: Stand-Alone Workstation
desc: A workstation that is not connected to a network.

name: Standard
desc: a) The specific signal configuration, reference pulses, voltage levels, etc., that describe the input/output requirements for a particular tape of equipment. Some standards have been established by professional groups or government bodies (such as SMPTE or EBU). Others are deter- mined by equipment vendors and/or users. b) A set of rules or characteris- tics defining a particular television system. Some standards (such as those contained in FCC rules and regulations) are mandatory. Most (including those of the EIA, IEEE, and SMPTE) are voluntary. The establishment of a standard often freezes development at a certain level but allows users and manufacturers to deal with a much larger array of products than might be available without a standard. There is currently one U.S. HDEP standard, the ATSC/SMPTE 1125 scanning-line system. CCIR system E is an HDTV transmission standard, used in France, calling for 819 scanning lines in a 14 MHz bandwidth.

name: Standard Bodies
desc: Any country having a national group of people con- sisting of experts from industry and universities who develop standards for all kinds of engineering problems.

name: Standard Definition Television
desc: See SDTV.

name: Standard Dynamic Range
desc: Conventional video using an 8-bit gamma curve per sample. The baseline for comparison with HDR formats.

name: Standard Input Format
desc: Video format developed to allow the storage and transmission of digital video. The 625/50 SIF format has a resolution of 352 x 288 active pixels and a refresh rate of 25 frames per second. The 525/59.94 SIF format has a resolution of 352 x 240 active pixels and a refresh rate of 29.97 frames per second. MPEG 1 allows resolutions up to 4095 x 4095 active pixels, however, there is a “constrained subset” of parameters defined as SIF. The computer industry, which uses square pixels, has defined SIF to be 320 x 240 active pixels, with a refresh rate of whatever the computer is capable of supporting.

name: Standards Converter
desc: A device for converting signals from one standard to another. Converting between different color schemes with the same scanning structure is called transcoding. Converting between different scanning structures requires line and field interpolation, which usually introduces artifacts. Standards conversion between 525 scanning line and 625 scanning line signals is performed regularly. Conversion from DHEP to either NTSC or a receiver-compatible ATV system will require standards conversion. It may seem that it is more difficult to convert from 1125 scan- ning lines to 525 than from 1050 to 525, but in a pre-filtering converter the difference, if any, may not be large. For dealing with the field-rate dif- ference (HDEP 60 and NTSC 59.94), some DHEP to NTSC conversions are performed by slowing the HDEP recorders to 59.94-field playback. Others are performed through standards converters that periodically omit fields.

name: Stand By
desc: To Be Prepared To Receive And Hence Execute A New Cue, Or To Have Just Received A Signal That A New Cue Is Imminent.

name: stand - in
desc: a substitute person who is physically similar ( in size andappearance ) to an actor and who takes the actor's placeduring often lengthy preparation of a scene ( the taking of lightmeter readings , camera setup , light adjustment , etc. ) but notduring filming . Not to be confused with a stunt double orabody double .

name: Standing ovation
desc: When audience members rise from their seats to applaud, indicating exceptional appreciation for a performance.

name: Standing Wave
desc: A Sound Wave Whose Size Matches The Size Of A Room, And Appears To Stand Still As Its Reflections Superpose. This Can Cause An Increased Perception Of This Frequency In The Room.

name: Standing Wave Ratio
desc: The ratio of transmitted power to reflected power in transmission lines, antenna systems, connectors, etc.

name: Stapler cleaver
desc: A fiber optic cleaver shaped similar to a stapler.

name: star
desc: the name given to famous , talented , and popular actors orcelebrities , often in lead character roles , who can draw anaudience to a film with their photogenic appearance ,inspirational acting , or some other quality . Historically ,astarlet ( or ingenue ) was an attractive actress promoted by afilm studio in a small role as an up - and - coming star during the40s and the 50s ; also used in the term star quality and starsystem

name: Star drop
desc: A piece of black scenic canvas with a large number of small low-voltage lamps (or fiber optic ends) sewn through it to create a magical starry sky effect when the stage is otherwise dark.

name: Starsight
desc: An electronic program guide that you subscribe to. It allows you to sort the guide by your order of preference and delete stations you never watch. It's a national service, that is regionalized. The decoders in Houston only download data for Houston. Move to Dallas and you only get Dallas. It is present on NTSC lines 14 and 277, and uses encoding similar to EIA-608.

name: star system
desc: refers to the way in which studios " groomed " stars undercontract , and sought star vehicles for them ; studios served asprotectorates for their stars

name: STAR System (Setup Swap, Transfer and Recall Panel Memory System)
desc: This describes the possible operations of this feature, utilizing the concept of a setup as an instantaneous snapshot of a complete switch- er panel, including all button selections, adjustments, positions, and fader values. Setups may be broken down into each of the six major modules on the switcher, with the ability to store or recall them independently into any of eight setup registers. This system also provides the capability of trans- ferring the setup of one M/E to another, or swapping their setups.

name: Start Bit
desc: A bit preceding the group of bits representing a character used to signal the arrival of the character in asynchronous transmission.

name: Start Codes (System and Video)
desc: 32-bit codes embedded in that coded bit stream that are unique. They are used for several purposes including identifying some of the structures in the coding syntax. Start codes consist of a 24-bit prefix (0 x 000001) and an 8-bit stream_id.

name: Starter
desc: An Electrical Device That Consists Of A Contactor, Overload Protection, And Other Control Devices To Power A Motor. Many Starters Have Dual Contactors That Are Mechanically And Electrically Interconnected To Cause The Motor To Run In Either Direction.

name: Start of Active Video (SAV)
desc: Digital data that indicate the start of active video time in serial digital component video systems.

name: Startracker
desc: A Camera Tracking System From Mo-Sys. The System Looks At “Stars” – Reflective Stickers Applied To A Studio Ceiling – Which Allows Startracker To Report The Position And Orientation Of The Studio Camera In Real-  Time To The Rendering Engine. Multiple Cameras In The Studio Can Track Off The Star Map. Once Calibrated, The System Is Fully Automatic. This Is Sometimes Referred To As An ‘Inside Out’ Tracking Solution; The Startracker Camera Is Mounted Inside The Capture Area Looking For The Reflective Stickers That Are Outside The Area.

name: Startup Disk
desc: The disk that contains the operating system files. The computer needs operating system information in order to run.

name: star vehicle
desc: a film expressly made to show off the talents of a performer ,with all other aspects almost secondary ; compare with tour deforce

name: State
desc: A Lighting Term, Referring To The Lanterns (Qv) And Their Dimmer (Qv) Settings, Used In A Particular Cue. We Talk Of A "Full-Up State" When All Lanterns Are Used At Full Brightenss, Or A "Red State", When Only Lanterns With Red Filters Are On. During The Plotting Of The Lighting, The Operator May Be Told To "Go Back To A State Of 2", Which Means To Set The Dimmers As They Were In Cue 2.

name: State (lighting)
desc: A complete lighting look on stage — the combination of all fixtures at their current levels, colors, and positions. Also called a look or scene.

name: Static (Dead) Load
desc: A Load That  Does Not Change Position Or Magnitude Over Time.

name: Static Electricity
desc: Whenever your body comes in physical contact with metal parts (including printed circuit boards) of computer equipment there is the potential for you to feel an electrical shock (electro-static discharge or ESD) which could damage the equipment. To prevent this you must always wear a wrist strap when working with internal parts of a workstation.

name: Static IP
desc: An IP address manually and specifically assigned to a device in a network. Can only be changed manually or by enabling DHCP.

name: Static Memory
desc: Memory devices that do not need clocks or refreshing.

name: Static mesh
desc: A basic de-interlacing process in scalers for video with no movement. Produces sharp images but causes tearing when motion occurs.

name: Static Resolution
desc: Detail in a stationary image. Any amount of band- width is sufficient for the transmission of HDTV images with high static resolution, even a telephone line; the smaller the bandwidth, the longer  it takes to transmit all of the resolution. Therefore, many ATV schemes with reduced bandwidths offer the static resolution of HDEP with limited dynamic resolution, resulting in motion artifacts such as motion surprise.

name: static shot
desc: an unmoving camera shot that is stationary , due to the use ofa tripod

name: Statistical Multiplexing
desc: Increases the overall efficiency of a multi- channel digital television transmission multiplex by varying the bit-rate of each of its channels to take only that share of the total multiplex bit-rate it needs at any one time. The share apportioned to each channel is predicted statistically with reference to its current and recent-past demands.

name: Status
desc: Present condition of the device. Usually indicated by flag flip-flips or special registers. See Flag.

name: Status Monitor
desc: A B/W video output available as an option on AVC series switchers that provides display of all switcher adjustments, pattern menus, and diagnostic tools.

name: STB
desc: See Set Top Box.

name: STC (System Time Clock)
desc: The common clock used to encode video and audio in the same program. A 27 MHz clock regenerated from PCR for a jitter-free readout of MPEG data.

name: Stderr
desc: Standard error file. Error messages sent by programs are displayed on the screen, which is by default the Stdout.

name: Stdin
desc: Standard input file.

name: STD Input Buffer
desc: A first-in, first-out buffer at the input of a system target decoder for storage of compressed data from elementary streams before decoding.

name: Stdout
desc: Standard output file.

name: STD (System Target Decoder)
desc: A hypothetical reference model of a decoding process used to describe the semantics of an MPEG multiplexed system bit stream.

name: Steadicam
desc: A camera stabilization system attached to an operator via a mechanical harness to eliminate camera shake during movement.

name: Steadicam ( shot )
desc: a hand - held camera technique using a stabilizing Steadicam( introduced in the late 70s ) , developed by inventor GarrettBrown , with a special , mechanical harness that allows thecamera operator to take relatively smooth and steady shots ,though hand - held , while moving along with the action ; theresulting images are comparable to normal tracking shots ona wheeled dolly

name: Steady Gate
desc: A pin-registered device manufactured by Steady Film for precise telecine transfer. Provides more stables images than EPR, but does not operate in real time.

name: Steady Shot
desc: A system designed to improve hand-held camera video recording by compensating for camera-shake.

name: Steady-State
desc: A Condition Devoid Of Transient Effects.

name: stealing a scene( or scene - stealing )
desc: usually refers to a supporting actor / actress attracting attentionfrom the lead actor or actress to whom the center of interestlegitimately belongs ; see also ' tour de force ' performance

name: SteamVR
desc: A Virtual  Reality Platform Developed By The Company Valve. Steamvr Is Not Specific To A Particular Headset; Instead, Steamvr Applications Can Be Played On Any One Of A Number Of Supported Headsets, Including Htc  Vive  And Oculus  Models.

name: Step index fiber
desc: A fiber with a uniform refractive index throughout the core, as opposed to graded index fiber where the index varies radially.

name: Stepped Lens
desc: A Lens Consisting Of Tiered, Concentric Rings On One Side That Are Segments Of The Flat Portion Of A Plano-Convex Lens. The Other Side Is Convex. It Controls The Light Similar In Manner As A Plano-Convex Lens.

name: Stepping
desc: a) Unsmooth packing, with transversally mispositioned sections. b) The movement forward or backward one frame at a time. See also Jogging.

name: Step Printer
desc: A printer in which each frame of the negative and raw stock is stationary at the time of exposure.

name: Steradians
desc: Just As A Radian Is An Angular Unit Of Measure In 2-Dimensional Space, So A Steradian Is An Angu_x0002_Lar Unit Of Measure In 3-Dimensional Space (Solid Angle).

name: Stereo
desc: Sound received from two separate sources. Simulates human hearing.

name: Stereo Mixing
desc: Simultaneous processing of both left and right audio channels.

name: Stereo Mode
desc: Two audio channels which form a stereo pair (left and right) are encoded in a single bit stream.

name: Stereophonic, Stereo
desc: Using two or more channels to create a spatial effect.

name: Stereopsis
desc: Binocular Vision Of Images With Different Views By The Two Eyes To Distinguish Depth.

name: stereotyping
desc: the act of portraying a particular character ( or group ) with aformulaic , conforming , exaggerated , and oversimplifiedrepresentation , usually offensive and distorted

name: Steroscopic VR
desc: A form of VR where different images are delivered to each eye to create an experience that has more depth. This type of VR is captured with two lenses to replicate the placement of your eyes. Stereoscopic VR is more immersive than monoscopic VR and leads to the highest sense of presence for the user.

name: STE (System Target Error)
desc: The STE gives a global indication about the overall distortion present on raw received data.

name: Stick Slip
desc: The process in which the tape sticks to the recording head because of high friction; the tape tension builds because the tape is not moving at the head; the tape tension reaches a critical level, causing the tape to release from and briefly slip past the read head at high speed; the tape slows to normal speed and once again sticks to the recording head; this process is repeated indefinitely. Characterized by jittery movement of the tape in the transport and/or audible squealing of the tape.

name: Sticky Shed
desc: a) The gummy deposits left on tape path guides and heads after a sticky tape has been played. The phenomenon whereby a tape binder has deteriorated to such a degree that it lacks sufficient cohesive strength so that the magnetic coating sheds on playback. b) The shedding of particles by the tape as a result of binder deterioration that causes dropouts on VHS tapes.

name: Sticky Tape
desc: Tape characterized by a soft, gummy, or tacky tape surface. Tape that has experienced a significant level of hydrolysis so that the magnetic costing is softer than normal. Tape characterized by resinous or oily deposits on the surface of he magnetic tape.

name: Stiction
desc: A term loosely used to describe the phenomenon of tape adhering to transport components such as heads or guides.

name: still
desc: refers to a single , static image , either ( 1 ) a frame still ( possiblyenlarged ) from a finished film , ( 2 ) a production still taken froman unfinished film , or ( 3 ) a publicity shot ( of an actor orscene ) ; aka photogram .

name: Still Frame
desc: A single frame of video repeated so it appears to have no motion.

name: Still Picture
desc: A coded still picture consists of a video sequence contain- ing exactly one coded picture which is intra coded. This picture has an associated PTS, and the presentation time of succeeding pictures, if any, is later than that of the still picture by at least two picture periods.

name: Still Store
desc: Device for storage of specific frames of video, either in analog or digital form, allowing extremely fast access time.

name: Stinger
desc: Slang For An Edison Cable, Used To Power

name: Stippled
desc: A Term Used To Describe A Surface That Is Dimpled Or Covered With Small Indentations Or Bumps.

name: Sti Speech Transmission Index
desc: A Single Number That Indicates The Effect Of A Transmission System On Speech Intelligibility.

name: Stitching
desc: Combining Segments From Multiple Performances Into One Seamless Performance.

name: STL (Studio Transmitter Link)
desc: System used to connect transmitter to studio if the facilities are located in different areas.

name: Stock
desc: Pre-made music, video, images, or effects purchased from a third-party site with royalty-free use rights.

name: stock character
desc: a minor character whose actions are completely predictable ,stereotypical , or standard for his / her job or profession ;similarly , a stock situation is a basic , recognizable plotsituation ( e.g. , a lover hiding in the closet , twins mistaken foreach other , etc. ) .

name: Stock characters
desc: Stereotypical characters representing a generic, simplified personality type that audiences immediately recognize — such as the villain, the fool, or the ingénue.

name: stock footage( or stock / library shot )
desc: previously - shot footage or film of common elements orscenes , such as canyons or deserts in the American West , ortravelogue shots ( e.g. , skylines , airplane takeoffs / landings ,famous places , etc. ) that are kept in a film archive or libraryand used to fill in portions of a movie in different filmproductions , thereby saving the time of reshooting similarscenes over and over ; a stock shot refers to an unimaginativeor commonplace shot that looks like it could be stock footage

name: STOP
desc: STOP Arm extended, palm down, move am back and forth.

name: Stop Batten (Bumper Angle)
desc: A Member Mounted To The T Or J-Guides That Limits The Travel Of Arbors At Their Top And Bottom Trim. They Are Often Provided With A Wood Or Rubber.

name: stop - motion( animation )
desc: a special - effects animation technique where objects , such assolid 3 - D puppets , figures , or models are shot one frame at atime and moved or repositioned slightly between each frame ,giving the illusion of lifelike motion . Stop - motion was one ofthe earliest special - effects techniques for science - fiction films ,now replaced by CGI and animatronics ; aka stop - framemotion

name: Stopping Brake
desc: A Brake That Is Activated While The Load Is In Motion And Is Used To Decelerate The Load To A Controlled Stop And Hold It.

name: Storage
desc: The Media In Which Assets Are Stored Including Magnetic And Solid State Hard Drives . Storageperformance Is A Critical Factor For Real - Time Workflows .

name: Storage Capacity
desc: Using the ITU-R 601 4:2:2 digital coding standard, each picture occupies a large amount of storage space, especially when related to computer storage devices such as DRAM and disks. So much so that the numbers can become confusing unless a few benchmark statistics are remembered. Fortunately, the units of mega, giga, tera and penta make it easy to express the very large numbers involved. The capacities can all be worked out directly from the 601 standard. Bear in mind that sync words and blanking can be regenerated and added at the output, only the active picture area need be stored.

name: Store
desc: The action of retaining in memory panel parameters (in the case of switchers), edit decision lists (in the case of editors), frames of video (in the case of machines like AVA, ESS and CGs).

name: Story
desc: The Avid term for an edited piece. A story is created by editing clips and sequences together.

name: storyboard
desc: a sequential series of illustrations , stills , rough sketchesand / or captions ( sometimes resembling a comic or cartoonstrip ) of events , as seen through the camera lens , that outlinethe various shots or provide a synopsis for a proposedfilmstory ( or for a complex scene ) with its action andcharacters ; the storyboards are displayed in sequence for thepurpose of visually mapping out and crafting the various shotdivisions and camera movements in an animated or liveaction film ; a blank storyboard is a piece of paper withrectangles drawn on it to represent the camera frame ( foreach successive shot ) ; a sophisticated type of previewstoryboard ( often shot and edited on video , with a soundtrack )is termed an animatic

name: Storyboarding
desc: Visual Representation Of The Script Using Images And Notes To Help Plan Out Shots

name: Stow
desc: To reduce a window to an icon for later use. In Windows®  it is called “minimize”.

name: STP
desc: Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D). Prevents network loops in Ethernet networks by blocking redundant paths. RSTP (Rapid STP) provides faster convergence. Important for AV networks using redundant switch connections.

name: STP (Surface Transfer Process)
desc: A method of producing dual-layer DVDs that sputters the reflective (aluminum) layer onto a temporary substrate of PMMA, then transfers the metalized layer to the already- molded layer 0.

name: Straight Lift Curtain
desc: A Curtain That Can Be Raised (Opened) Without Folding In Any Way.

name: straight man
desc: an actor / actress who serves as a stooge fora comedian ( orfunnyman ) , usually by adopting a seriousstance or reaction to the comic partner ; the straight man oftenfeeds lines to the other irreverent comedian - who replies withwitty comments ; aka second banana or foil

name: Straight Tip (ST)
desc: A popular legacy fiber optic connector with a twist-lock design similar to a BNC. Features a 2.5 mm ferrule.

name: Strain Relief
desc: An Item Mounted To A Piece Of Electrical Equipment Or A Luminaire Designed To Retain A Permanently Installed Power Cord Such That Any Reasonable Pull Or Twist On The Power Cord Will Not Cause The Power Cord To Get Damaged At The Point Of Entry, Fall Out, Or Adversely Affect The Conductors Within The Electrical Equipment, Luminaire, Or Electrical Connector.

name: Streaking
desc: A term used to describe a picture condition in which objects appear to be extended horizontally beyond their normal boundaries. This will be more apparent at vertical edges of objects when there is a large transition from black to white or white to black. The change in luminance is carried beyond the transition, and may be either negative or positive. For example, if the tonal degradation is an opposite shade to the original fig- ure, (white following black), the streaking is called negative; however, if the shade is the same as the original figure, (white following white), the streak- ing is called positive. Streaking is usually expressed as short, medium or long streaking. Long streaking may extend to the right edge of the picture, and in extreme cases of low-frequency distortion, can extend over a whole line interval.

name: Stream
desc: 1. To Transmit Multimedia Files That Begin Playing Upon Arrival Of The First Packets, Without Needing To Wait For All The Data To Arrive. 2. To Send Data In Such A Way As To Simulate Real-Time Delivery Of Multimedia.

name: Streaming
desc: The process of sending video over the Web or other networks to allow playback on the desktop as the video is received, rather than requiring the entire file to be downloaded prior to playback.

name: Streaming media
desc: Multimedia Content--Such As Video, Audio, Text, Or Animation--That Is Displayed By A Client A Client As It Is Received From The Internet, Broadcast Network, Or Local Storage.

name: Streaming Video
desc: Compressed audio and video that is transmitted over the Internet or other network in real time. Typical compression techniques are MPEG-2, MPEG-4, Microsoft WMT, RealNetworks, and Apple's QuickTime. It usually offers "VCR-style" remote control capabilities such as play, pause, fast forward, and reverse.

name: Stream key
desc: A unique identifier string provided by a streaming platform to authenticate and direct an encoder's outgoing stream to the correct channel on the streaming server.

name: Stream Multiplex Interface (SMI)
desc: An interface modeling the exchange of SL-packetized stream data and associated control information between the Sync Layer and the TransMux Layer.

name: Stream Objects
desc: A class in the MPEG-4 class hierarchy that represents the interface of the elementary streams.

name: Stress Testing
desc: Introducing mechanical, electrical, or thermal stress on electrical devices so as to modify their operation and allow intermittent problems and/or failures to be observed.

name: Striations
desc: Stripes Or Bands Of Light In A Beam Pattern, Usually Undesirable.

name: Strike
desc: 1) See The Definition For Ignite. 2) To Remove All Lighting And Related Equipment For Storage And/Or Transport When A Production Is Over.

name: Stringer
desc: A Power Cable With Multiple Outlets At Even Spaces Along The Cable, Typically Used To Provide Stage Power For Backline And The Performers.

name: Strip
desc: Part of a wide roll of manufactured film slit to its final width for motion picture use.

name: Stripe
desc: A narrow band of magnetic coating or developing solution applied to a length of motion picture film.

name: Striped Stock
desc: Film stock to which a narrow stripe of magnetic record- ing material has been applied for the recording of a sound track. See Black and Code.

name: Striping
desc: Preparing a tape for editing by recording continuous control track, timecode, and a video signal (e.g., black). Also known as Black Stripe.

name: Striping a Tape
desc: Preparing a tape for editing by recording continuous control track, time code, and a video signal (such as black or colour bars).

name: Striplight
desc: A Multi-Lamp Luminaire With Its Lamps Mounted In A Straight Row.

name: Stripper
desc: A tool removing the jacket from a cable or wire. In fiber optics, removes the buffer coating from an optical fiber.

name: Strobe
desc: 1) To Cause An Intense Light Source To Turn On And Off Repeatedly At A Relatively Fast Rate. This Is Usually Done In An Area Devoid Of All Other Illumination To Create A Flickering, Slow Motion Effect. 2) An Abridged Version Of Strobe Light.

name: Strobe Light
desc: A Luminaire Specifically Designed To Create A Strobe Effect, Usually Using An Arc Lamp As Its Light Source.

name: Strobe (lighting)
desc: A lighting effect device or mode that produces rapid, repeated flashes at a controllable rate. Used in concerts, nightclubs, and theatrical effects. High-intensity strobes require photosensitivity warnings.

name: Structural Drawing
desc: An Engineering Drawing That Describes The Size, Location, And Attachment Details Of The Building Structure.

name: Structureborne Noise
desc: Generation And Propagation Of Time- Dependent Motions And Forces In Solid Materials Which Result In Unwanted Radiated Sound.

name: Structured Audio
desc: A method of describing synthetic sound effects and music.

name: STS (Synchronization Time Stamp)
desc: The Synchronization Time Stamp (STS) is the number of 100 ns time intervals between the last 1 second clock tick of the common time reference and the occurrence in the MPEG-2 transport stream of the first bit of the MPEG-2 packet sync byte in the header of the distributed transmission packet (DXP) at the output of the distributed transmission adapter (DXA).

name: ST (Stuffing Table)
desc: An optional DVB SI table that authorizes the replacement of complete tables due to invalidation at a delivery system boundary such as a cable headend.

name: STT (System Time Table)
desc: An ATSC PSIP table that carries the current date and time of day. It provides timing information for any application requiring schedule synchronization.

name: Studer
desc: A Harman-owned Swiss brand making high-end broadcast mixing consoles. The Studer Vista series is widely used in broadcast and live television. Known for exceptional preamp quality.

name: Studio Address System
desc: An intercom system that allows communication between control room personnel and personnel working on the studio floor.

name: studio chief
desc: the head or chairperson of a film studio who has the finalauthority for each film project ( gives the green light - orauthorization go - ahead ) , and oversees the many departments( financial , legal , marketing , advertising , distribution , etc. ) ; alsocalled the topper ; in Hollywood's Golden Age , the chief wascalled a mogul

name: Studio Fresnel
desc: A Fresnel Spotlight Used Primarily In Studios For The Film And Video Industries. Because Portability Is Generally Not A Concern, They Tend To Be Larger In Size When Compared To Location Fresnels Of The Same Wattage.

name: studio ( s )
desc: ( 1 ) the for - profit companies that specialize in developing ,financing and distributing most American commercial films ;( 2 ) also refers to the actual site for a film production , withphysical sets , stages , offices , backlots ( located on the outdoorgrounds of a film studio and used for filming exteriors ) , etc ;see also majors and independents , andmogul .

name: Studio Standard, HDTV
desc: a) Approaches to the specification of a studio standard, HDTV have been in the context of present operations in 525/59.94 and 625/50 i.e., operations in the studio conform to the specifications for transmission and broadcast. The studio standard with its implication of no systems transform, therefore, might be described also as one of the distribution standards – expected to be one of the inputs to display, and to be evaluated by subjective judgment of the display. b) As employed by CCIR Rep 801-4 and its annexes, the term studio standard loosely embraces everything from image capture through distribu

name: studio system
desc: refers to the all - powerful control the monopolistic film studioshad over all aspects of assembly - line filmmaking and filmproduction from the 1920s until the late 1950s , when chiefs -moguls ( Mayer , Selznick and Zukor ) ruled ; tactics includedthe ownership of property , control of publicity and marketing ,and iron - clad contracts with star - actors , directors , composers ,cameramen , costume designers , writers , and producers .

name: Stuffing (bits); Stuffing (bytes)
desc: Code-words that may be inserted into the compressed bit stream that are discarded in the decoding process. Their purpose is to increase the bit rate of the stream.

name: stunt double ( s )
desc: a stunt performer ( s ) ( aka stunts ) that take the place of anactor when the scene calls for a dangerous or risky action( car crash , fight , window jump , etc. ) ; doubles usually have thesame build or appearance as the star ; also called stuntperformer , stuntman or stuntwoman ; not to be confused witha stand - in or a body double ; stunts are supervised , conductedand planned by a stunt coordinator

name: Stuntvis
desc: A Form Of Techvis Which Uses 3D Assets And Physical Simulation To Plan Live - Action Stunts . Alsocalled Action Design .

name: STU (Set Top Unit)
desc: See Set Top Box.

name: STV (Subscription Television)
desc: Pay television, in which subscribers, or viewers, pay a monthly fee.

name: stylize ( d )
desc: a term that refers to the artificial exaggeration or elimination ofdetails in order to deliberately create an effect - in otherwords , to make ( or interpret ) a person , a face , a tree , a figure ,or something as ' grotesque , ' ' disturbing , ' or ' overbright ' asopposed to realistic or naturalistic .

name: Sub-band Adaptive Differential Pulse Code Modulation (SB-ADPCM)
desc: The audio frequency band is partitioned into two sub-bands and then each sub-band is encoded using ADPCM.

name: Sub-Band Coding
desc: A pure sub-band coder performs a set of filtering operations on an image to divide it into spectral components. Usually, the result of the analysis phase is a set of sub-images, each of which repre- sents some region in spatial or spatio-temporal frequency space. For example, in a still image, there might be a small sub-image that represents the low-frequency components of the input picture that is directly viewable as either a minified or blurred copy of the original. To this are added suc- cessively higher spectral bands that contain the edge information neces- sary to reproduce the original sharpness of the original at successively larger scales. As with DCT coder, to which it is related, much of the image energy is concentrated in the lowest frequency band. For equal visual qual- ity, each band need not be represented with the same signal-to-noise ratio; this is the basis for sub-band coder compression. In many coders, some bands are eliminated entirely, and others are often compressed with a vector or lattice quantizer. Succeedingly higher frequency bands are more coarsely quantized, analogous to the truncation of the high frequency coefficients of the DCT. A sub-band decomposition can be the intraframe coder in a predictive loop, thus minimizing the basic distinctions between DCT-based hybrid coders and their alternatives.

name: Subcarrier Phase Shifter
desc: Special circuitry that controls the phase relationships of the two parts of the encoded color signal, ensuring the relationship is correct during recording, transmission, and reproduction.

name: Subcarrier (SC)
desc: a) The modulation sidebands of the color subcarrier contain the R-Y and B-Y information. For NTSC, the subcarrier frequency is 3.579545 MHz. For PAL the subcarrier is approximately 4.43 MHz. b) An auxiliary information carrier added to the main baseband signal prior to modulation. The most common example in television is the NTSC color subcarrier. Many ATV schemes propose adding additional subcarriers to NTSC. c) A sine wave which is imposed on the luminance portion of a video signal and modulated to carry color information. Subcarrier is also used to form burst. The frequency of subcarrier is 3.58 MHz in NTSC and PAL-M and 4.43 MHz in PAL. d) The high-frequency signal used for quadrature amplitude modulation of the color difference signals.

name: Sub-Channel
desc: A transmission path within the main transmission path. Subcarriers are examples of sub-channels, but there are others.Quadrature modulation of the picture carrier provides a sub-channel; so does blanking stuffing.

name: Subclip
desc: a) An edited part of a clip. In a sequence, a subclip can be bound by any variation of clip beginnings, endings, and mark points.b) A subclip created by marking IN and OUT points in a clip and by saving the frames between the points. The subclip does not contain pointers to media files. The subclip references the master clip, which alone contains pointers to the media files.

name: Sub-frame compression
desc: Compression carried out on only part of a video frame rather than an entire frame.

name: Subgroup
desc: An Output Buss In A Mixing Console That Carries A Submix, Which Can Then Be Routed To Various Places, Like The Main Output Buss, Or Directly To Output Connectors On The Console.

name: Sub Harmonic
desc: Sub Harmonics Are Synchronous Components In A Spectrum That Are Multiples Of 1/2, 1/3, Or 1/4 Of The Frequency Of The Primary Fundamental. They Are Sometimes Called "Sub-Synchronous" Components. In The Vibration Spectrum Of A Rotating Machine, There Will Normally Be A Component At The Turning Speed Along With Several Harmonics Of Turning Speed. If There Is Sufficient Looseness In The Machine So That Some Parts Are Rattling, The Spectrum Will Usually Contain Sub Harmonics. Harmonics Of One-Half Turning Speed Are Called "One-Half Order Sub Harmonics," Etc.

name: subjectivepoint - of - view ( POV )
desc: a film in which the narrator has a limited point - of - viewregarding the characters , events , action , places , thoughts ,conversations , etc .; a subjective camera is a style of filmingthat allows the viewer to look at events from the POV of eithera character or the author , when the camera position is closeto the line of sight of the character ; contrast to omniscientpoint - of - view

name: Sub Master
desc: A Slider On A Control Console That Controls Groups Of Sliders On The Console, And Perhaps Other Sub Masters. They Can Usually Be Controlled By Masters And The Grand Master If The Console Is So Equipped.

name: Submittal Drawings
desc: Drawings That Are Prepared By The Equipment Supplier Or Installer To Describe The Equipment And Details Of The Installation To The Client. Approval Of The Drawings By The Client Indicates His Acceptance Of The Proposed Equipment, Locations, And Conditions Of The Installation.

name: Submix
desc: A Mix Of Multiple Audio Signals That Is, In Turn, Sent To The Main Output Buss.This Mix Can Be Treated Separately, For Example, You Could Apply Compression Or Eq On That Mix Before Combining It With Other Submixes.

name: Subnet mask
desc: A 32-bit number dividing an IP address into network and host portions. /24 mask (255.255.255.0) = 254 usable host addresses. Used to determine if a destination IP is local (same subnet) or requires routing.

name: Sub-Nyquist Sampling
desc: A scheme for sampling at a frequency lower than that prescribed by the Nyquist sampling theorem.

name: Subpicture
desc: A DVD data type that is used to describe an overlay over video such as is used for menus, subtitles, simple animation.

name: Sub-Picture Information
desc: Captions, subtitles or other text that can be displayed or hidden.

name: Subpicture Menu
desc: Menu used to select a subpicture stream.

name: Sub-Pixel
desc: A spatial resolution smaller than that of a pixel. Although digital images are composed of pixels, it can be very useful to resolve image detail to smaller than pixel size, i.e., sub-pixel. For example, the data for generating a smooth curve on television needs to be created to a finer accuracy than the pixel grid itself, otherwise the curve will look jagged. Again, when tacking an object in a scene or executing a DVE move, the size and position of the manipulated picture must be calculated, and the picture resolved, to a far finer accuracy than the pixels, otherwise the move will appear jerky.

name: subplot
desc: a secondary , subordinate , or auxiliary plotline , oftencomplementary but independent from the main plot ( the Astory ) , and often involving supporting characters ; not thesame as multiple plotlines ; aka the B story or C story

name: Subroutine
desc: Self-contained portion of a program that performs a well-defined task. May be used at place in the same program.

name: Subsampled
desc: Signal that has been sampled at a lower rate than some other signal in the system. A good example of this is the Y’CbCr color space used in component serial video (ITU-R BT.601). For every two luma (Y’) samples, only one Cb and Cr sample is taken causing the Cb and Cr signals to be subsampled.

name: Subsampled Signal
desc: A signal that has been sampled at a lower rate than some other signal in the system.

name: Sub-Sampling
desc: Sampling within samples. For example, dividing an NTSC pixel into three or four sub-pixels is an example of sub-sampling. Some ATV schemes use such pixel subdivision to transmit a high definition image over a sequence of multiple fields or frames, with only one sub-pixel being transmitted per field or frame. The resulting potential artifacts include motion surprise and twinkle.

name: Subscriber Connector (SC)
desc: A popular fiber optic connector with a snap (push-pull) coupling. Being replaced by the LC connector in most applications.

name: Subsidiary Communications Authorizations
desc: Authorizations granted to FM broadcasters for using subcarriers on their channels for other communications services.

name: Substrate
desc: A DVD half-disc. Two substrates, each 0.6 mm thick, are bonded together to form a 1.2 mm thick DVD disc.

name: subtext
desc: the deeper and usually unexpressed " real " meanings of acharacter's spoken lines or actions if the viewer can ' readbetween the lines ' .-

name: Subtitles
desc: Text that is added below or over a picture that usually reflects what is being said, possibly in another language. Open subtitles are trans- mitted as video that already has the subtitles present. Closed subtitles are transmitted during the VBI, and relies on the TV to decode it and position it below or over the picture. Closed captioning is a form of subtitling.

name: Subtractive Color Mixing
desc: The Removal Of Energy From Various Wavelengths Of Light, Usually By Filtering. When Filters Are Superimposed, Each Tends To Remove Energy At The Wavelengths It Would Have If Acting Independently.

name: Subtractive color process
desc: Color process used in printing mixing cyan, magenta, yellow, and black (CMYK) to produce desired colors.

name: Subtractive Color System
desc: Color specification system in which primary colors are subtracted from a reference color to achieve a desired color. Examples include the cyan/magenta/yellow (CMY) and luminance/red – luminance/blue – luminance (Y, R-Y, B-Y) systems.

name: Subwoofer
desc: Speaker Designed To Reproduce Very Low Frequencies; Typically 20-200 Hz.Synonym: Subs

name: Summing amplifier
desc: A device combining left and right channel audio signals into a single mono channel. Used in multi-speaker paging systems.

name: Sundance
desc: short for the influential Sundance Film Festival , known for theexhibition and screening of the best of independent films eachyear in Utah ; also see ( film ) festival

name: Super
desc: See Title.

name: Super 16
desc: The 16mm film stock produced for a special format with an enlarged picture area. Super 16 is designed to be printed to 35 mm film for release.

name: Super Black
