desc: refers to the atmospheric , background sound effect for theindistinct murmurings and buzz of voices in a crowd ; extras incrowd scenes , in older films ( or in radio ) , would be asked tomurmur a phrase ( ' walla walla , ' ' rhubarb , ' ' peas and carrots , 'or ' watermelon , ' etc. ) to create the sound of the crowd and topretend that they were talking ; see also foley artist , dubbing ,and non - synchronized sound

name: Wall Batten
desc: Horizontal Structural Members To Which Guide Tracks  Are Attached.

name: Wall Box
desc: See The Definition For Wall Pocket.

name: Wall Bracket
desc: A Sturdy Metal Arm That Attaches To A Wall And Provides A Means For Attaching A Luminaire.

name: Wall Knee
desc: Bracket That Attaches A Wall Batten To The Building Structure.

name: Wall Pocket
desc: A Stage Pocket Whose Cover Is Flush Mounted With The Wall To Which It Is Mounted.

name: Wall Washing
desc: A  Technique That Lights A Wall Fairly Evenly From Top To Bottom Without Spilling Or Wasting Light Away From The Wall Into The Room.

name: WAN
desc: Wide Area Network. Network spanning large geographic distances using leased telecommunications lines. Cloud-based AV management and remote system monitoring use WAN connectivity.

name: Wandelprobe
desc: A staged rehearsal of an opera or musical with singers and orchestra moving through the blocking, intermediate between a sitzprobe (seated) and a full staging rehearsal.

name: Wander
desc: Long-term timing drift in digital networks. Causes loss of synchronization.

name: WAN (Wide Area Network)
desc: A computer network that covers a large geographic area, such as a state. WANs may use telephone lines, fiber- optic cables, or satellite links for their long-distance connections. Some WANs are created by connecting several smaller LANs.

name: wardrobe
desc: the general name for the costume department , or thecostumes ( and their accessories ) themselves ; seealsocostume

name: Wardrobe Master
desc: (More Usually Wardrobe Mistress) Responsible For The Making (Under The Direction Of The Designer), Repair And Washing Of All Costumes.

name: Warm Color
desc: Generally, A Color That Is In The Yellow-Orange-Red Range.

name: Warmth
desc: A Listening Term. The Opposite Of Cool Or Cold. In Terms Of Frequency, Generally Considered The Range From Approx. 150Hz-400Hz. A System With The "Proper" Warmth Will Sound Natural Within This Range.

name: Warm-up (theatre)
desc: A series of physical and vocal exercises designed to prepare an actor for rehearsal or performance.

name: Warp
desc: A special effect created by ADO to distort (twist) video pictures.

name: Warping
desc: This video effect is related to morphing except that a warp consists of transforming one video image into one of a completely differ- ent type. For example, a scorebox might be twisted on and off a screen containing video action. Some examples of video transitions include fly-ons/offs, slide ons/offs, zoom in or out to/from a pinpoint, shattered glass transition, pixelization where on screen explodes into thousands of pixels and fades out at a controlled rate.

name: Wash
desc: 1) An Even, Overall Illumination Over A Large Area. 2) To Create Such An Illumination.

name: Wash fixture
desc: A moving head or static fixture producing a broad, even, soft-edged wash of light. Used for coloring cycloramas, backdrops, and large areas. LED wash fixtures use RGBW or RGBWW color mixing.

name: Wash Light
desc: A Luminaire Used To Produce A Wash.

name: Wash (lighting)
desc: A broad, even coverage of light over a large area of the stage, providing base illumination without strong shadow.

name: Watermark
desc: Information hidden as “invisible noise” or “inaudible noise” in a video or audio signal.

name: Watt
desc: The Unit Of Electrical Or Acoustical Power.

name: Wattage
desc: Is The Unit Of Power Used To Rate The Output Of Audio Amplifiers. For A Wattage Number To Have Meaning The Distortion Level And Impedance Must Also Be Specified.

name: Wattage (W)
desc: The Measure Of Electrical Power In Watts.

name: Watt-Hour
desc: Unit Of Electric Energy. One Watt- Hour Is The Amount Of Energy Consumed At The Rate Of 1 Watt During A L-Hour Period.

name: Watt (W)
desc: The Unit For Measuring Electrical Power.

name: .Wav
desc: This Is A Uncompressed File Format For Audio That Is Commonly Used In Video Editing

name: Wave
desc: A Regular Variation Of An Electrical Signal Or Acoustical Pressure.

name: Waveform
desc: A Graphic Representation Of Either An Electrical Audio Signal Or Acoustic Sound Wave.

name: Waveform Coding
desc: Coding that aims to reconstruct the waveform of the original (audio) signal as close as possible, independently of the material. Includes linear PCM, differential PCM, adaptive differential PCM, sub-band coding, adaptive transform coding, etc.

name: Waveform Monitor
desc: A piece of test equipment which displays waveforms (analog video signals) at a horizontal and/or vertical rate. A specialized oscilloscope for evaluating television signals. a) DC Restore – A circuit used in picture monitors and waveform monitors to clamp on point of the waveform to a fixed DC level. Typically the tip of the sync pulse or the back porch. This ensures the display does not move vertically with changes in the signal amplitude or average picture level (APL). The DC Restore speed can be set to SLOW or FAST DC. SLOW allows hums and other low fre_x0002_quency distortions to be seen. FAST DC removes the effects of hum from the display so it will not interfere with other measurements. b) AFC/Direct – This selection allows the waveform monitor’s horizontal sweep to trigger on each individual horizontal sync pulse (direct mode). This will allow the user to see any jitter that might be in the signal. Or the waveform monitor can trigger horizontally in the AFC mode which causes the horizontal sweep to trigger on the average value of the horizontal sync pulses. The AFC mode eliminates jitter.

name: Waveguide
desc: An acoustic device in a loudspeaker enclosure improving efficiency by confining sound wave movement over a desired path. Couples loudspeaker cone motion to air motion in a tube-like structure.

name: Waveguide Dispersion
desc: The distortion of an electromagnetic signal or light as it encounters a waveguide and disperses into multiple modes or wavelengths.

name: Wavelength
desc: The Physical Distance Needed To Complete A Full Cycle Of A Sound Wave,Typically Measured In Meters, Feet, Or Inches. 50Hz Is About 22.6 Feet, While 8Hkz Is 1.7 Inches. If The Length Of The Room Is Smaller Than The Wavelength Of The Sound Wave, The Wave Cannot Propagate Fully, Which Can Be Problematic For Low Frequencies.

name: Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)
desc: The combination of two or more optical signals at different wavelengths for transmission within a single optical fiber.

name: Wavelet
desc: a) A transform in the basic function that is not of fixed length but that grows longer as frequency is reduced. b) A compression algorithm that samples the video image based on frequency to encode the informa- tion. This creates a series of bands representing the data at various levels of visual detail. The image is restored by combining bands sampled at low, medium, and high frequencies.

name: Wavelet-based compression
desc: An Asymmetrical Image Compression Technique That Is Scalable And Can Provide High Quality. The Drawback Is That It Becomes More Computationally Expensive As The Picture Resolution And Frame Rates Go Up. The Encode And Decode Are Asymmetrical In That One Side Is A Lot More Expensive Computationally Than The Other. The Immix Cube And Turbocube Used Wavelet-Based Compression.

name: Wavelet Transform
desc: A time-to-frequency conversion which gives a constant bandwidth frequency analysis. It uses short windows at high frequencies and long windows at low frequencies.

name: Waveshape
desc: The shape traced by the varying amplitude of the wave. See Waveform.

name: Waves SoundGrid
desc: Waves Audio's server-based plug-in processing platform allowing Waves plug-ins to run on an external SoundGrid server and integrate with compatible mixing consoles (DiGiCo, Avid, Yamaha). Provides access to hundreds of Waves plug-in processors.

name: Wave Velocity
desc: The propagation velocity of a wave. The time it takes for one point of a waveform to travel a certain distance. Wave velocity is dependent on the medium through which the wave travels and the temperature of the medium.

name: WAV (pronounced wave)
desc: The Windows-Compatible Audio File Format. The Wav File Can Be Recorded At 11 Khz, 22 Khz, And 44 Khz, And In 8- Or 16-Bit Mono And Stereo. See Also: Aif, Au.

name: Way
desc: Another Word For "Channel" (Qv).

name: Wayfinding
desc: An interface connected to Extron Room Scheduling panels providing real-time meeting space availability, status, and location information on a centralized display.

name: WD
desc: See Working Draft.

name: Wear Product
desc: Any material that is detached from the tape during use. The most common wear products are oxide particles or agglomerates, portions of coating and material detached from the edges of the tape.

name: Weave
desc: Periodic sideways movement of the image as a result of mechanical faults in camera, printer or projector.

name: WebAR
desc: Enables a consumer to load AR experiences directly from the web on their browser. WebAR minimizes friction that a consumer may experience when trying to view AR content. This is not the standard today for AR content delivery but is being experimented with in the industry.

name: Webhook
desc: An HTTP callback mechanism where a device sends an automated notification to a specified URL when a specific event occurs. Used in modern AV control for event-driven automation.

name: WebRTC
desc: Web Real-Time Communication. Open standard enabling real-time audio/video/data directly in web browsers without plugins. Used for browser-based conferencing, remote AV monitoring, and IP camera viewing.

name: WebTV
desc: Webtv Networks, Inc. Is A Leading Manufacturer Of Set-Top Boxes Used For Viewing Interactive Television And Regular Television. These Receivers Let Users Access The Internet, Including Use Of Electronic Mail And Online Chats. Webtv Set-Top Boxes Like The Webtv Plus Receiver Connect To A Standard Television And A Phone Line. The Webtv Plus Receiver Supports Tv Crossover Links And Webpip. Webpip Lets Users Simultaneously View Web Pages And Tv Programming On The Same Screen, Without A Special Picture-In-Picture Tv. Webtv Is A Trademark And Service Of The Microsoft Corporation.

name: WebVR
desc: Enables a consumer to load VR experiences directly from the web on their browser. WebVR minimizes friction that a consumer may experience when trying to view VR content. This is not the standard today but is being experimented with in the industry.

name: Wedge
desc: See Stage Monitor

name: Wedge monitor
desc: A floor monitor loudspeaker angled toward the performer, providing stage monitoring for performers who prefer traditional floor wedge monitoring over IEM systems.

name: weenie
desc: refers to the object that motivates the main action ina serial ( e.g . , a lost city , buried treasure , or missing plans , etc )

name: Weighting
desc: A Method Of Measuring Sound Pressure Levels Involving Particular Frequency Curves To More Accurately Reflect Human Perception Of Sound. See A Weighting And C-Weighting.

name: Weighting, ANSI A
desc: The A-curve is a side bandpass filter centered at 2.5 kHz with ~20 dB attenuation at 100 Hz, and ~10 dB attenuation at 20 kHz. Therefore, it tends to heavily roll off the low end, with a more modest effect on high frequencies. It is essentially the inverse of the 30-phon (or 30 dB-SPL) equal-loudness curve of a Fletcher-Munson.

name: Weighting, ANSI B
desc: The B-weighting curve is used for intermediate level sounds and has the same upper corner as the C-weighting, but the lower amplitude corner is 120 Hz.

name: Weighting, ANSI C
desc: The C-curve is basically “flat”, with -3 dB corners of 31.5 Hz and 8 kHz, respectively.

name: Weighting, CCIR 468
desc: This filter was designed to maximize its response to the types of impulsive noise often coupled into audio cables as they pass through telephone switching facilities. The CCIR 468-curve peaks at 6.3 kHz, where it has 12 dB of gain (relative to 1 kHz). From here, it gently rolls off low frequencies at a 6 dB/octave rate, but it quickly attenuates high frequencies at ~30 dB/octave (it is down -22.5 dB at 20 kHz, relative to +12 dB at 6.3 kHz).

name: Weighting, CCIR ARM (or CCIR 2 kHz)
desc: This curve is derived from the CCIR 468-curve. Dolby Laboratories proposed using an average-response meter with the CCIR 468-curve instead of the costly true quasi-peak meters used by the Europeans in specifying their equipment. They further proposed shifting the 0 dB reference point from 1 kHz to 2 kHz (in essence, sliding the curve down 6 dB). This became known as the CCIR ARM (average response meter), as well as the CCIR 2 kHz-weighting curve.

name: Weighting filter
desc: A band-limiting filter used in measuring audio loudness that emphasizes certain frequency bands. A-weighting mimics human hearing (centered at 2.5 kHz); C-weighting is generally flat.

name: Well
desc: Gaps Between Gridiron Members Intended For The Mounting And Support Of Loft Blocks On Boundary Channels And For Allowing The Free Passage Of Cables.

name: WEP
desc: Wired Equivalent Privacy. A Wi-Fi security protocol providing privacy comparable to a wired network.

name: Wet
desc: A Signal With Added Reverberation Or Other Effect Like Echo, Delay Or Chorusing.

name: Wet-Gate Printing
desc: A system of printing in which the original is tem- porarily coated with a layer of liquid at the moment of exposure to reduce the effect of surface faults

name: Wet Signal
desc: The output of an effect device, especially a reverb unit.

name: Wet tech
desc: A full technical rehearsal with both actors and all technical elements (lighting, sound, scenery, costumes). The opposite of a dry tech.

name: WGHDEP
desc: SMPTE Working Group on High-Definition Electronic Production (N15.04). Now reformed as the SMPTE Committee on Hybrid Technology (H19).

name: WG (Working Group)
desc: A WG works a very specific area of technical standards. Usually, WGs develop standards that are scoped by approved NPs. The WGs produce successive WDs, and then CDs, and then the FDIS.

name: What'S Your 20?
desc: "Where Are You Right Now?", Commonly Asked Over Radio.

name: WHDI
desc: A proprietary protocol for wireless video transmission in the 5 GHz radio band.

name: Wheels To The Sky
desc: Refers To Packing A Road Case In A Truck Or In Storage Upside-Down (Wheels Up).

name: Whip
desc: A horizontal picture disturbance at an edit point, usually caused by timing mis-adjustments in the edit system.

name: Whip Pan
desc: A quick movement of the camera from left to right or right to left which creates a blurred image. Also called Swish Pan.

name: Whip Zoom
desc: A very fast zoom in or out, often used as a transition effect. Also called a crash zoom.

name: whistleman
desc: in early film production , an individual on a film set's soundstage who was employed to make sure all noises were" shushed " before filming . The whistleman would blow on awhistle just before the shooting of a take commenced , tomake sure that the set was quiet and that extraneous noisesnear or outside the studio set were silenced .

name: White
desc: The lightest visible surface created by reflection of all colored light.

name: White Balance
desc: An electronic process used in camcorders and video cameras to calibrate the picture for accurate color display in different lighting conditions (i.e., sunlight vs. indoor incandescent). White balancing should be performed prior to any recording, typically by pointing the camera at a white object for reference.

name: White Book
desc: The document from Sony, Philips, and JVC, begun in 1993 that extended the Red Book compact disc format to include digital video in MPEG-1 format. Commonly called Video CD.

name: White Clip
desc: The maximum video signal excursion in the white direction permitted by the system.

name: White Compression
desc: a) Amplitude compression of the signals corre- sponding to the white regions of the picture, thus modifying the tonal gra- dient. b) The reduction in gain applied to a picture signal at those levels corresponding to light areas in the picture, with respect to the gain at the level corresponding to the midrange light value in the picture. Note: The gain referred to in the definition is for a signal amplitude small in compari- son with the total peak-to-peak picture signal involved. A quantitative evaluation of this effect can be obtained by a measurement of differential gain. The overall effect of white compression beyond bandwidth limiting is to reduce contrast in the highlights of the picture as seen on a monitor.

name: White Level
desc: Level which defines white for the video system.

name: White Level Control
desc: This is a name for the contrast or picture control. It describes a function that is otherwise not clearly spelled out in names of controls used on monitors. It is not a term found on a monitor control. (As “black level” clearly defines the brightness control function, “white level” more clearly defines the contrast or picture control function.)

name: White light
desc: A blend of multiple colors of the visible electromagnetic spectrum resulting in light that appears white to the human eye.

name: White Noise
desc: An Audio Signal With Equal Energy Across All Frequencies That Is Used To Test Speaker Systems Or Audio Connections.

name: white ( or color )balance
desc: refers to electronically setting or ' color - correcting ' a camera'swhite balance - or the true color of white , since white doesn'tappear ' white ' with all lighting conditions

name: White Peak
desc: The maximum excursion of the picture signal in the white direction at the time of observation.

name: White Point
desc: That point on the chromaticity diagram having the tristimu- lus of a source appearing white under the viewing conditions; i.e., a spec- trally nonselective sample under the illumination of viewing conditions.

name: White, Reference
desc: a) The light from a nonselective diffuse reflector (in the original scene) that is lighted by the normal illumination of the scene. That white with which the display device stimulates reference white of the original scene. b) In production context, reference white is defined as the luminance of a white card having 90% reflectance and subjected to scene illumination. It is expected that there will be the capability of some discrimination of surface texture or detail within that portion of the transfer function incorporating reference white.

name: whodunit
desc: refers to a mystery / detective film

name: Whole Hog
desc: A family of professional theatrical and concert lighting control consoles originally developed by High End Systems, now owned and maintained by ETC (Electronic Theatre Controls). Known for its intuitive interface, strong tracking engine, and the Hog 4 OS software platform.

name: whoop - whoops
desc: in sound effects , this refers to the extra noises added to asound , e.g. , bells , horns , or whistles to an explosion , to makeit more interesting or exciting

name: Wide-Angle
desc: Refers to camera lenses with short focal length and broad horizontal field of view.

name: wide - angle shot
desc: a shot ( often abbreviated WS ) taken with a lens that is able totake in a wider field or range of view ( to capture more of thescene's elements or objects ) than a regular or normal lens ; awide - angle shot exaggerates the distance , depth or disparitybetween foreground and background planes , thereby creatinggreater depth - of - field and keeping all objects in focus and inperspective ; an extreme or ultra - wide - angle lens giving a 180degree view is called a ' fish - eye ' lens

name: Wideband
desc: Relatively wide in bandwidth.

name: Widescreen
desc: Term Given To Picture Displays That Have A Wider Aspect Ratio Than Normal. For Example, Tv'S Normal Aspect Ratio Is 4:3 And Widescreen Is 16:9. Although This Is The Aspect Ratio Used By Hdtv, Widescreen Is Also Used With Standard Definition Systems.

name: Widescreen Panels
desc: Additional sections of picture information that can be added to a 1.33:1 aspect ratio picture to create a widescreen image.

name: Wide Screen Signaling System (WSS)
desc: It is used on (B, D, G, H, I) PAL line 23 and (M) NTSC lines 20 and 283 to specify the aspect ratio of the program and other information. ITU-R BT.1119 specifies the WSS signal for PAL and SECAM system. EIAJ CPX-1204 specifies the WSS signal for NTSC systems.

name: Wide Shot
desc: A camera framing term showing the whole of the subject or a broad view of the scene.

name: Wide Shot (Ws)
desc: A Shot Taken From A Distance To Show A Landscape, Building, Or Large Crowd, Such As The View Of New York City From The Ellis Island. Wide Shots Are Often Used As Establishing Shots At The Beginning Of A Scene.

name: WiDi
desc: A proprietary protocol by Intel for streaming audio and video between devices over a wireless connection.

name: Width
desc: Refers to the width of recording tape, varying from 0.150” in cassette tape to 2.0” for video, mastering and instrumentation tapes. The size of the picture in a horizontal direction.

name: Width Border
desc: The 4100 series name for a Hard Border.

name: Wi-Fi
desc: Brand name for wireless networking products using IEEE 802.11 standards. Wi-Fi 5 (802.11ac): 3.5 Gbps. Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax): 9.6 Gbps. Wi-Fi 6E: adds 6GHz band. Used in AV for wireless control and increasingly for wireless streaming.

name: WiGig
desc: Implementation of IEEE 802.11ad for wireless LAN in the 60 GHz radio band.

name: wig - wag
desc: a red warning light located above each entrance - exit door ona film set sound stage , designed to flash ( with a buzzersound ) to indicate when shooting commences or ends ; alsoknown as " red - eye "

name: Wild Sound, Wild Track
desc: A recording of sound on either videotape or audiotape made without an accompanying picture.

name: Winch
desc: A Geared Mechanism, Either Hand Operated Or Motorized, For Use In Raising (Vertical) Or Moving (Horizontal) Equipment. The Gearing Produces A Mechanical Advantage In Speed And Load Capacity.

name: Winch Fly System
desc: A fly system using motorized winches to raise and lower battens. Provides precise, programmable control of flying elements. See Fly System.

name: Wind
desc: The way in which tape is wound onto a reel. An A-wind is one in which the tape is wound so that the coated surface faces toward the hub; a B-wind is one in which the coated surface faces away from the hub. A uniform wind, as opposed to an uneven wind, is one giving a flat-sided tape pack free from laterally displaced, protruding layers.

name: Winder/Cleaner
desc: A device designed to wind and clean magnetic tape in order to restore it to a quality that approaches the condition of a new tape, providing the tape has not been physically damaged.

name: Window
desc: 1. Video Containing Information Or Allowing Information Entry, Keyed Into The Video Monitor Output For Viewing On The Monitor Crt. A Window Dub Is A Copy Of A Videotape With Time Code Numbers Keyed Into The Picture. 2. A Video Test Signal Consisting Of A Pulse And Bar. When Viewed On A Monitor, The Window Signal Produces A Large White Square In The Center Of The Picture. 3. A Graphical User Interface That Presents Icons And Tools For Manipulating A Software Application. Most Applications Have Multiple Windows That Serve Different Purposes.

name: Window Dub
desc: Copies of videotape with “burned in” time code display. Hours, minutes, seconds and frames appear on the recorded image.Window dubs are used in off-line editing.

name: Window Function
desc: In digital signal processing, a distortion is caused on the transformed waveform when a window function is applied to it.

name: Windowing
desc: The video display is divided into two or more separate areas to display different material from different sources in each area.

name: Windows, Analog
desc: All analog windowing architectures multiplex graphics and video as analog signals rather than as digital information, but they vary widely in signal manipulation and digital processing capabilities. While they do offer some advantages, analog architectures fail to address certain problems. For example, the graphics pixel-clock frequency becomes the pixel clock for the video image. Therefore, the greater screen resolution,  the smaller the video window. Since enlarging the image means losing graphics resolution, the end user may find himself changing display drivers several times a day to fit the immediate task. The simplest analog architec- ture is the genlocked video overlay. Composite video is decoded into its RGB components. Having no control over the video source, the graphics controller must be genlocked to the video source, operating at a resolution and timing characteristic compatible with the incoming video signal. The graphics signal is switched in and out at appropriate times so that the graphic appears in the desired place in the image. The multiplexed output is then encoded into a new composite signal. The analog multiplexer, cur- rently the most popular architecture, is actually a group of slightly varied architectures. The most popular variation imports the graphics data and pixel clock from the graphics card feature connector across a ribbon cable, where it is fed to a DAC. The video signal is digitized, color-converted, and scaled, then is stored in a frame buffer similar to a FIFO which synchro- nizes the data. When the video data emerges from the frame buffer, it is  fed to a second DAC. The two DACs are connected to an analog multiplexer that is controlled by a set of counters that keep track of the beam position on the graphics display. When the beam enters the video-window area, the mux is switched from the graphics signal to the video signal.

name: Windows CE
desc: Microsoft Windows Ce Is A 32-Bit Real-Time Embedded Operating System (Rtos) Designed From The Ground Up To Empower The Development Of A New Range Of Emerging Computing Appliances, Including Set-Top Boxes, Digital Versatile Disc (Dvd) Drives, Entertainment Consoles, Smart Phones, Highly Portable And Personal Computing Devices Like Handheld Computers, And Home Appliances. Windows Ce Is Modular, Allowing Use Of A Minimum Set Of Software Components Needed To Support Receiver Requirements. This Uses Less Memory And Improves Operating System Performance. Windows Ce Provides A Subset Of The Win32 Application Program Interface (Api) Set, Which Provides An Effective Amount Of Application Source-Code Level Portability And Compatibility And User Interface Consistency With Other Microsoft Windows Operating Systems And Windows Applications.

name: Windows, Digital
desc: Digital windowing offers a distinct advantage over analog. it digitizes the video image immediately, only converting it to analog as it is sent to the CRT. Incoming composite video is digitized and decoded to produce a YUV data stream, which then enters the video-processing pipeline (color-space and format conversion, scaling and/or zooming). After processing, the data is stored in the frame buffer. At the appropriate time, the data moves to the overlay controller, which serves as a digital multi- plexer. Graphics data remains in digital form through the overlay controller; it is not converted to analog until the final DAC that drives the CRT.

name: Window Shades
desc: See Side Panels.

name: Windows Media Player
desc: Delivers The Most Popular Streaming And Local Audio And Video Formats, Including Asf, Wav, Avi, Mpeg, Quick-Time, And More. Windows Media Player Can Play  Anything From Low-Bandwidth Audio To Full-Screen Video.

name: Windscreen
desc: A Device, Often Made Of Foam, Placed Around A Microphone That Helps Alleviate The Unwanted Noises That Stem From Large Air Pressure Variations Like Wind And Other Outdoor Elements.

name: Wing
desc: 1) See Definition #1 For Door. 2) The Areas To The Left And Right Of The Stage Or Performing Area Not Visible To The Audience.

name: Wing and drop
desc: A traditional stage setting using painted flat pieces (wings) at the sides and a painted cloth (drop/backdrop) at the back. The classic form of scenery from the 18th–19th centuries.

name: Wings
desc: The Sides Of The Stage, Out Of Sight Of The Audience, Where Actors Stand Before Making Their Entrance, And Where Props Are Kept, Ready To Be Brought Onto The Stage.

name: wipe
desc: a transitional technique or optical effect / device in which oneshot appears to be " pushed off " or " wiped off " the screen byanother shot replacing it and moving across the existingimage ; also called a push - over ; a flip - over ( or flip ) wipe iswhen one scene rotates or flips - over to the new scene ; wipeswere very commonly used in the 30s

name: Wipeall
desc: An Avolites Titan function that clears the current show from memory and starts fresh, without affecting previously saved show files on disk.

name: Wirecast
desc: A live video streaming production software for macOS and Windows supporting multi-camera production, screen capture, NDI, and simultaneous streaming to multiple destinations.

name: Wireframe
desc: a) An image generated by displaying only the edges of all polygons or surfaces. b) A display option where solid or filled objects are represented by mesh lines and/or curves.

name: Wire Grid
desc: An Open Floor That Supports Lights Or Provides Access To Theatrical Equipment. It Is Formed Of Woven Cables Attached To, And Supported By,A Structural Frame.

name: Wire Guide
desc: Wires Placed To Control The Location And Travel Of Arbors, Clews And Curtains.

name: Wireless
desc: Transmission of information using radio or microwave technologies.

name: WirelessHD
desc: A proprietary protocol for wireless video transmission in the 60 GHz radio band.

name: Wireless Microphone
desc: A Microphone That Uses Radio Frequencies (Rf) To Transmit Audio Without An Xlr Cable. These Can Be Either Analog Or Digital, And Use Various Compression Schemes To Achieve Wireless Transmission.

name: Wireless Microphone System
desc: A microphone system consisting of a microphone, an FM transmitter, and a tuned receiving station that eliminates the need for long runs of microphone cable.

name: Wireless presentation
desc: Technology enabling users to share their screen wirelessly from laptops, tablets, or phones to a room display. Solutions include Barco ClickShare, Mersive Solstice, Crestron AirMedia, and Extron ShareLink.

name: Wire Rope
desc: A Wire Rope Consists Of A Number Of Strands Laid Helically About A Metallic Or Non-Metallic Core. Each Strand Consists Of A Number Wires Also Laid Helically About A Center.

name: Within
desc: A Virtual  Reality  Application Designed For Viewing Cinematic  Vr  Experiences. It Is Free To Download.

name: Witness Camera
desc: Camera ( S ) Placed On Set To Provide Alternate Perspectives On A Shoot And Provide Acomprehensive Understanding Of The Action Within A Scene . Often Used To Facilitate Remotecollaboration , Or To Capture Additional Data For Later Visual Effects Work .

name: Witness Cameras
desc: Witness Cameras Are Placed On Set To Provide Alternate Perspectives On A Shoot. This Provides A Comprehensive Understanding Of The Action Within A Scene.

name: WLAN
desc: Wireless Local Area Network. Uses radio waves to transmit data between nodes instead of cable. Governed by the IEEE 802.11 standard.

name: .Wma
desc: Windows Media Audio

name: WMF (Windows Meta File)
desc: The standard vector-based structure of the Windows operating system. Bitmapped images may be embedded in WMF files.

name: .Wmv
desc: Windows Media Video

name: Woofer
desc: A Loudspeaker Designed For The Reproduction Of Low-Frequency Sounds.

name: Word
desc: Set of characters that occupies one storage location and is treated by the computer circuits as a unit. Ordinarily a word is treated by the con- trol unit as an instruction and by the arithmetic unit as a quantity. See Byte.

name: word of mouth
desc: a term referring to the public discussion or buzz that a filmcan acquire , fueled by sneak previews and advanceadvertising ; word of mouth is an important marketing elementin a film's success or failure - positive word of mouth gives afilm legs , while negative word of mouth may prematurelyclose it down

name: Work For Hire
desc: Work For Hire Is When Your Employer Owns The Copyright For Your Intellectual Copyrights Because You Create The Material At Work

name: Working Draft (WD)
desc: Preliminary stage of a standard but kept internal to MPEG for revision.

name: Working lights
desc: Low-level blue lights kept on backstage during a performance for crew safety. Not under control of the lighting board except during a dead blackout.

name: Working Load Limit (WLL)
desc: See Recommended Working Load (Rwl).

name: Work Print
desc: A film print made from the original negative that is used dur- ing the editing process to produce a cut list or edit decision list for final program assembly. Work prints are typically low-cost, one-light prints that receive heavy wear through repeated handling. See also Answer Print, Print, Release Print.

name: Workspace
desc: The main window for working with icons and customizing your view of the file system. You place files and directories from all over the file system here for easy access; placing them in the Workspace does not change their actual location in the file system.

name: Workspace (console)
desc: A saved arrangement of windows and views on a console's touchscreens. Multiple workspaces can be saved for different tasks (programming, playback, busking) and recalled with a single button press.

name: Workstation
desc: The physical hardware that contains the CPU and graphics boards, a system disk, and a power supply. You connect it to a monitor, keyboard, and mouse to configure a working system. It is also sometimes referred to as the chassis.

name: World - Building
desc: The Process Of Developing A Coherent Virtual World For Use In A Production Whose Qualitiesmay Include History , Geography , And Ecology .

name: World Capture
desc: The Use Of Lidar , Photography , Video And Other References To Translate Real - World Spacesinto Digital Assets . Also Referred To As Reality Capture Or Scene Digitization .

name: World Coordinate System
desc: See World Reference.

name: World In The Hand
desc: A Metaphor For Visualized Tracking Where A Tracker Is Held In The Hand And Is Connected To The Motion Of An Object In A Display.

name: World Reference
desc: The absolute coordinate system which is the root reference and upon which all other references are based. It cannot be animated.

name: World Standard
desc: A television standard accepted in all parts of the world. CCIR recommendation 601 is currently the closest there is to a world standard. It is accepted throughout the world, but can be used with either 525-scanning line or 625-scanning line picture. HDTV 1125/60 Group is attempting to promote its system as a world HDEP standard, but Zenith suggests the same for 3XNTSC, and there are other candidates.

name: World System Teletext (WST)
desc: ITU-R BT.653 525 line and 625 line system B teletext.

name: World Transmission Standards
desc: For a definition of “TV” column codes. See Terrestrial Transmission Standards.

name: WORM
desc: Write Once/Read Many--Describes Storage Devices On Which Data, Once Written, Cannot Be Erased Or Re-Written. Being Optical, Worms Offer Very High Recording Densities And Are Removable, Making Them Very Useful For Archiving.

name: WORM (Write Once, Read Many)
desc: A WORM is an optical drive where the data is recorded once (usually with a laser) but may be read many times. CD ROMs are WORMs.

name: Wow
desc: Slow, periodic variations in the speed of the tape, characterized by its effect on pitch. A measure of non-uniform movement of magnetic tape or other recording parts.

name: Wowza
desc: A streaming media server software and cloud platform supporting RTMP, HLS, DASH, WebRTC, and other protocols. Used for live streaming ingest, transcoding, and delivery at scale.

name: WPA
desc: Wi-Fi Protected Access. A security method providing stronger data encryption and user authentication than WEP.

name: WPA2
desc: Wi-Fi Protected Access 2. Wireless security method providing stronger data protection and network access control than WPA.

name: WPP (Wipe to Preset Pattern)
desc: See Preset Pattern.

name: WQHD
desc: Wide Quad High Definition. A computer resolution of 2560×1440 pixels, equivalent to 4 times 720p.

name: WQXGA
desc: Wide QXGA. A computer resolution of 2560×1600 pixels, a widescreen version of the QXGA format.

name: wrap
desc: refers to the completion of film shooting ( either for the day orfor the entire production or project ) ; in the early days ofcinema , the cameraman would say after filming : " Wind , Reel ,And Print ' - abbreviated as WRAP ; a entirely completed film istermed in the can

name: Wrap-around
desc: A video problem where left picture information displays on the right and right information on the left, separated by a vertical bar.

name: Wrinkle
desc: A physical deformity of the videotape. Any creases or wrinkle in the videotape may produce dropouts or loss of picture information upon playback. See Creasing.

name: Wrist Strap
desc: A coiled cable with a loop for your wrist at one end and an alligator clip at the other. You fasten the clip to a metal part of the workstation and place the loop around your wrist whenever you work with internal components of the workstation to avoid electrical shocks to yourself and the components. See also Static Electricity.

name: Write
desc: a) To transfer information, usually from a processor to memory or from main storage to an output device. b) To record data in a register, location, or other storage device.

name: Write Buffer
desc: A term used to denote the buffer that is logically posi- tioned between the CPU interface and the display memory.

name: writer
desc: refers to the individual who authors the content of the piecefrom pre - existing material or uses an entirely new idea ;usually there are many writers involved with re - writes ,adaptations , character development , etc .; aka screenwriter

name: Write-Through
desc: A strategy where cache data is always written into main memory when data is written by the CPU. The write-through is done through the cache system.

name: WRS (Wireless Relay Station)
desc: The WRS is a cost effective infrastruc- ture building block providing improved or extended coverage in low traffic density applications (both indoor and outdoor). A WRS can be equipped with one directional antenna and one omnidirectional antenna to provide cost efficient public network access to users in remote areas.

name: WSS
desc: See Wide Screen Signaling System.

name: WST
desc: See World System Teletext.

name: WSXGA
desc: Wide-SXGA. A class of SXGA displays with 16:9 aspect ratio. 1920×1080 to 1600×900 pixels.

name: W-VHS (Wide VHS)
desc: A standard proposed by JVC, featuring a high resolution format and an aspect ratio of 16:9.

name: WWW
desc: World Wide Web. An international network of subscriber sites where text and graphics information is made available to computer users.

name: WXGA
desc: Wide-XGA. A class of XGA displays with 16:9 aspect ratio. 1366×768 to 1280×720 pixels.

name: WYSIWYG
desc: What You See Is What You Get--Usually, But Not Always. Referring To The Accuracy Of A Screen Display To Show How The Final Result Will Look. For Example A Word Processor Screen Showing The Final Layout And Typeface That Will Appear From The Printer.

name: WYSIWYG (What You See Is What You Get)
desc: Usually, but not always, referring to the accuracy of a screen display to show how the final result will look. For example, a word processor screen showing the final layout and typeface that will appear from the printer.

name: X.25
desc: A standard networking protocol suite approved by the CCITT and ISO. This protocol suite defines standard physical, link, and networking layers (OSI layers 1 through 3). X.25 networks are in use throughout the world.

name: X.400
desc: The set of CCITT communications standards covering mail services provided by data networks.

name: XA
desc: See CD-ROM XA.

name: X-Axis
desc: The horizontal axis of a graph. When a television signal is examined in one dimension, the x-axis is usually time. When it is examined in three dimensions, the x-axis is usually horizontal resolution.

name: Xdr
desc: Acronym For Extended Dynamic Range. Xdr Is Collection Of Mackie Circuit Design Elements Which Contribute To The Low Noise, High Head_x0002_Room Mic Preamp Circuit Employed On Many Mackie Mixers.

name: XGA
desc: Extended Graphics Array. A screen resolution of 1024x768 pixels.

name: XGA-2
desc: eXtended Graphics Array card, 2nd generation. Capable of scanning from 31 to 68 kHz and resolutions up to 1600 x 1200 pixels. Uses a 15-pin HD VGA-style connector.

name: XGA (Extended Graphics Adapter)
desc: IBM graphics standard that includes VGA and supports higher resolutions, up to 1024 pixels by 768 lines interlaced.

name: XLR
desc: Trade Name (Itt-Cannon); Multi-Pin Locking Connector. Synonyms: 3 Pin, Xlr

name: Xlr Cable/Connector
desc: A Style Of Electrical Connector Most Commonly Associated With Balanced Audio Interconnection.

name: Xlr Connector
desc: See Cannon.

name: XML
desc: Extensible Markup Language. A text-based format for structured data. Used in AV for configuration files, control system programming, and some device APIs.

name: XML (Extensible Markup Language)
desc: A simple, very flexible text format derived from the International Standards Organization’s Standard Generalized Markup Language or SGML (ISO 8879). It was developed for electronic publishing and web page information exchange. Promoted at a method of exchanging metadata because it is easy to print and the printed material can be easily understood by users.

name: XMT (Extensible MPEG-4 Textual Format)
desc: XMT is the use of a textual syntax to represent MPEG-4 3D scene descriptions. XMT was designed to provide content authors the ability to exchange their content with other authors while preserving their intentions in the text format. XMT provides interoperability between MPEG-4, Extensible 3D (X3D), and Synchronized Multimedia Integration Language (SMIL).

name: XR (extended reality)
desc: XR (Extended Reality) Is An Umbrella Term That Covers The Various Forms Of Computer-Altered Reality.It Is A Wide Spectrum Of Hardware And Software, Including Sensory Interfaces, Applications, And Infrastructures, That Enable Content Creation For Virtual Reality (Vr), Mixed Reality (Mr), Augmented Reality (Ar) And Cinematic Reality (Cr).With These Tools, Users Can Generate New Forms Of Reality By Bringing Digital Objects Into The Physical World And Bringing Physical World Objects Into The Digital World.

name: XSENS
desc: A Company That Manufactures Motion Capture Products. Xsens’ Wearable Sensors Register The Movements Of A Performance Artist, And The Tracking Data Is Then Used To Create Digital Characters. Dock10 Uses Xsens’ Mo-Cap Sensors To Create The Ar Robot Character ‘Clogs’ For Bbc Bitesize Daily.

name: XSVCD (Extended Super VideoCD)
desc: See Super VideoCD.

name: XVCD (Extended VideoCD)
desc: See VideoCD.

name: xvYCC
desc: Extended-gamut YCC color space. Can be used in televisions and other video displays to improve image quality of high-definition video signals.

name: XXX Profile Bitstream
desc: A bitstream of a scalable hierarchy with a profile indication corresponding to xxx. Note that this bitstream is only decodable together with all its lower layer bitstreams (unless it is a base layer bitstream).

name: XXX Profile Decoder
desc: Decoder able to decode one or a scalable hierarchy of bitstreams of which the top layer conforms to the specifica- tions of the xxx profile (with xxx being any of the defined profile names).

name: XXX Profile Scalable Hierarchy
desc: Set of bitstreams of which the top layer conforms to the specifications of the xxx profile.

name: XYZ
desc: – A 10-bit word with the two least significant bits set to zero to survive an 8-bit signal path. Contained within the standard definition “xyz” word are bit functions F, V, and H, which have the following values: Bit 8 – (F-bit) 0 for field one and 1 for field two Bit 7 – (V-bit) 1 in vertical blanking interval; 0 during active video lines Bit 6 – (H-bit) 1 indicates the EAV sequence; 0 indicates the SAV

name: Y
desc: Luminance or luma.

name: Yamaha CL series
desc: Yamaha's flagship professional digital live sound console range (CL1, CL3, CL5). Designed for large-scale touring and corporate events. Features Dante networking, built-in SPX effects, and Yamaha's VCM (Virtual Circuitry Modeling) channel processing.

name: Yamaha QL series
desc: Yamaha's mid-range professional digital live sound console range (QL1, QL5). Smaller than the CL series but shares the same Dante networking and processing. Popular for corporate AV, houses of worship, and regional touring.

name: Yamaha TF series
desc: Yamaha's entry-level digital mixing console range (TF1, TF3, TF5). iPad-controllable with touchscreen workflow. Popular for small venues, houses of worship, and corporate installations.

name: Yaw
desc: The Angular Displacement About The Vertical Axis.

name: yawner
desc: a slang term , meaning a boring film

name: Y-Axis
desc: The vertical axis of a graph. When a television signal is examined in one dimension, the y-axis is usually signal strength. When it is examined in three dimensions, the y-axis is usually vertical resolution.

name: Y, B-Y, R-Y
desc: These Are The Analogue Luminance, Y, And Color Difference Signals (R-Y) And (B-Y) Of Component Video. Y Is Pure Luminance Information Whilst The Two Color Difference Signals Together Provide The Color Information. The Latter Are The Difference Between A Color And Luminance: Red – Luminance And Blue – Luminance. The Signals Are Derived From The Original Rgb Source (E.G. A Camera Or Telecine).The Y, (R-Y), (B-Y) Signals Are Fundamental To Much Of Television. For Example In Itu-R Bt.601 It Is These Signals That Are Digitized To Make 4:2:2 Component Digital Video, In The Pal And Ntsc Tv Systems They Are Used To Generate The Final Composite Coded Signal And In Dtv They Are Sampled To Create The Mpeg-2 Video Bitstream.

name: Y/C
desc: The technical description of S-video. Luminance (Y) and chrominance (C) are carried on separate signal/ground pairs, allowing higher bandwidth and eliminating color subcarrier crosstalk.

name: Y, C1, C2
desc: A generalized set of CAV signals: Y is the luminance signal, C1 is the 1st color difference signal and C2 is the 2nd color difference signal.

name: Y-Cable
desc: A Cable With One Input And Two Outputs, Used To Mult A Source To Two Inputs.

name: Y, Cb, Cr
desc:
The international standard ITU-R BT.601-1 specifies eight-bit
digital coding for component video, with black at luma code 16 and white
at luma code 235, and chroma in eight-bit two’s complement form cen tered on 128 with a peak at code 224. This coding has a slightly smaller
excursion for luma than for chroma: luma has 219 risers compared to 224
for Cb and Cr. The notation CbCr distinguishes this set from PbPr where
the luma and chroma excursions are identical. For Rec. 601-1 coding is
eight bits per component.
Y_8b = 16 + 219 * 9
Cb_8b = 128 +112 * (0.5/0.886) * (Bgamma – Y)
Cr_8b = 128 +112 * (0.5/0.701) * (Rgamma – Y)
Some computer applications place black at luma code 0 and white at luma
code 255. In this case, the scaling and offsets above can be changed
accordingly, although broadcast-quality video requires the accommodation
for headroom and footroom provided in the CCIR-601-1 equations. ITU-R
BT.601-1 Rec. calls for two-to-one horizontal subsampling of Cb and Cr,
to achieve 2/3 the data rate of RGB with virtually no perceptible penalty.
This is denoted 4:2:2. A few digital video systems have utilized horizontal
subsampling by a factor of four, denoted 4:1:1. JPEG and MPEG normally
subsample Cb and Cr two-to-one horizontally and also two-to-one vertical ly, to get 1/2 the data rate of RGB. No standard nomenclature has been
adopted to describe vertical subsampling. To get good results using sub sampling you should not just drop and replicate pixels, but implement
proper decimation and interpolation filters. YCbCr coding is employed by
D1 component digital video equipment.

name: YCC (Kodak PhotoCD™)
desc:
Kodak’s Photo YCC color space (for PhotoCD) is similar to YCbCr, except that Y is coded with lots of headroom and no foot- room, and the scaling of Cb and Cr is different from that of Rec. 601-1 in order to accommodate a wider color gamut. The C1 and C2 components are subsequently subsampled by factors of two horizontally and vertically, but that subsampling should be considered a feature of the compression process and not of the color space.
Y_8b = (255/1.402) * Y
C1_8b = 156 + 111.40 * (Bgamma – Y)
C2_8b = 137 + 135.64 * (Rgamma – Y)

name: Y/C Connections
desc: Connections between videotape recorders and between videotape recorders and cameras, monitors, and other devices that keep luminance and chrominance separate and thus avoid cross-color and cross-luminance. See also S-Video.

name: Y/C Delay
desc: A delay between the luminance (Y) and chrominance (C) signals.

name: Y/C (Luminance and Chrominance)
desc: A term used to describe the separation of video signal components used in systems such as Hi-8 and S-VHS. Generically called S-Video, all Videonics video products support the (Y/C) format.

name: Y, Cr, Cb
desc: The Digital Luminance And Color Difference Signals In Itu-R Bt.601 Coding. The Y Luminance Signal Is Sampled At 3.5 Mhz And The Two Color Difference Signals Are Sampled At 6.75 Mhz Co-Sited With One Of The Luminance Samples. Cr Is The Digitized Version Of The Analogue Component (R-Y), Likewise Cb Is The Digitized Version Of (B-Y). For The Hd Smpte 274M Standard, Sampling Rates Are 5.5 Times Greater – 74.25 Mhz For Y And 37.125 Mhz For Cr And Cb.

name: Y/C Separator
desc: a) Decoder used to separate luma and chroma in an (M) NTSC or (B, D, G, H, I) PAL system. b) Used in a video decoder to separate the luma and chroma in a NTSC or PAL system. This is the first thing that any video decoder must do. The composite video signal is fed to a Y/C separator so that the chroma can then be decoded further.

name: Y/C separator (YCS)
desc: A Y/C separator isolates the luma (Y) and chroma (C) components of a composite video signal. The first step necessary in decoding composite video.

name: Y/C Video
desc: a) Shorthand for luma (Y) and chroma (C). b) A component video signal in which the luminance (Y) and chrominance (C) information are separate. S-VHS videocassette recorders use th Y/C video format.

name: Yellow Book
desc: The document produced in 1985 by Sony and Philips that extended the Red Book compact disc format to include digital data for use by a computer. Commonly called CD-ROM.

name: Yellow card (IATSE)
desc: An IATSE scheme where touring companies send labour requirements to upcoming venues in advance to ensure sufficient union crew. Originally physical yellow cards, converted to electronic in 2013.

name: Yield Strength
desc: The minimum force per unit cross-sectional area at which the tape or base film deforms without further increase in the load. Units are pounds per square inch (psi) or pounds per tape sample of given width and base film thickness.

name: Y, I,Q
desc:
The human visual system has less spatial acuity for magenta green transitions than it does for red-cyan. Thus, if signals I and Q are
formed from a 123 degree rotation of U and V respectively, the Q signal
can be more severely filtered than I (to about 600 kHz, compared to about
1.3 MHz) without being perceptible to a viewer at typical TV viewing dis tance. YIQ is equivalent to YUV with a 33 degree rotation and an axis flip 
in the UV plane. The first edition of W.K. Pratt “Digital Image Processing”,
and presumably other authors that follow that bible, has a matrix that 
erroneously omits the axis flip; the second edition corrects the error. Since
an analog NTSC decoder has no way of knowing whether the encoder was
encoding YUV or YIQ, it cannot detect whether the encoder was running at 0 degree or 33 degree phase. In analog usage the terms YUV and YIQ are
often used somewhat interchangeably. YIQ was important in the early days
of NTSC but most broadcasting equipment now encodes equiband U and V.
The D2 composite digital DVTR (and the associated interface standard)
conveys NTSC modulated on the YIQ axes in the 525-line version and PAL
modulated on the YUV axes in the 625-line version. The set of CAV signals
specified for the NTSC system: Y is the luminance signal, I is the 1st color
difference signal and Q is the 2nd color difference signal.

name: Y (Luma or Luminance)
desc: a) This is an abbreviation or symbol for luminance, the black and white information in a television signal. b) Signal which is made up of 0.59G + 0.3R + 0.11B. c) It is the y-axis of the chart of the spectral sensitivity of the human visual system.

name: Yoke
desc: A Sturdy, U-Shaped Metal Bracket That Attaches To Opposite Sides Of A Luminaire, Or, Video And Film Industry Reflectors, Butterflies, Etc., Such That It Allows Either To Tilt Freely. A Locking Mechanism Is Provided To Prevent Slippage When The Desired Position Has Been Achieved. Also Provided At The Center Of The Yoke Is A Hole, Stud, Or Receiver For Mounting The Yoke.

name: Yo-Yo
desc: A Winch Type Used When Space Is Tight And Fleet Angles Are Difficult. The Drum Contains A Narrow Slot(S) Where The Lift Line Piles Up In A Single Layer. The Winch Capacity, Speed,And Distance Traveled Vary With Each Cable Wrap.

name: Yo-yo hoist
desc: A pile-up winch with a narrow-slot drum where lift line piles up in a single layer. Used in tight spaces with difficult fleet angles. Also called a stacking drum hoist.

name: YPbPr
desc: Analog component video color space. Y = luma, Pb = blue-difference, Pr = red-difference. Used in component video connections (three separate cables). Higher quality than composite video.

name: Y, PR, PB
desc: The three nonlinear video signals in which the information has been transformed into a luminance signal and two chrominance signals, each of which has been subject to nonlinear processing, and the chromi- nance signals at least have also been bandlimited. By convention, P’R and P’B represent color-difference signals in analog format, with typical excur- sion between -350 mV and +350 mV.

name: Y, R-Y, B-Y
desc: The general set of CAV signals used in the PAL system  as well as for some encoder and most decoder applications in the NTSC systems. Y is the luminance, R-Y is the 1st color difference signal and B-Y is the 2nd color difference signal.

name: Y signal
desc: The luma signal transmitted in standard color video. In a color picture made up of 0.30 red, 0.59 green, and 0.11 blue. Compatible with a standard monochrome receiver.

name: Y-Split
desc: An Audio Adapter That Takes One Connector, Typically An Xlr, And Splits It To Two Connectors. When Mixing Monitors From Foh, This Lets You Split A Microphone Signal Into Two Channels Of The Mixer, One For Foh And One For Monitors. Or, It Can Be Used For A Million Other Things.

name: Y to C delay
desc: Relative delay or timing of the luminance channel compared to the chrominance channel in a video system.

name: Y, U, V
desc: Luminance and color difference components for PAL systems. Y, U and V are simply new names for Y, R-Y and B-Y. The derivation from RGB is identical. In composite NTSC, PAL or S-Video, it is necessary to scale (B-Y) and (R-Y) so that the composite NTSC or PAL signal (luma plus mod- ulated chroma) is contained within the range -1/3 to +4/3. These limits reflect the capability of composite signal recording or transmission chan- nel. The scale factors are obtained by two simultaneous equations involving both B-Y and R-Y, because the limits of the composite excursion are reached at combinations of B-Y and R-Y that are intermediate to primary colors. The scale factors are as follows: U = 0.493 * (B-Y); V = 0.877 * (R- Y). U and V components are typically modulated into a chroma component: C = U*cos(t) + V*sin(t) where t represents the ~3.58 MHz NTSC color sub- carrier. PAL coding is similar, except that the V component switches Phase on Alternate Lines (+ or -1), and the sub-carrier is at a different frequency, about 4.43 MHz. It is conventional for an NTSC luma signal in a composite environment (NTSC or S-Video) to have 7.5% setup: Y_setup = (3/40) + (37/40) * Y. A PAL signal has zero setup. The two signals Y (or Y_setup) and C can be conveyed separately across an S-Video interface, or Y and C can be combined (encoded) into composite NTSC or PAL: NTSC = Y_setup + C; PAL = Y + C. U and V are only appropriate for composite transmission as 1-wire NTSC or PAL, or 2-wire S-Video. The UV scaling (or the IQ set, described below) is incorrect when the signal is conveyed as three sepa- rate components. Certain component

name: YUV12
desc: Intel’s notation for MPEG-1 4:2:0 YCbCr stored in memory in a planar format. The picture is divided into blocks, with each block compris- ing 2 x 2 samples. For each block, four 8-bit values of Y, one 8-bit value of Cb, and one 8-bit value of Cr are assigned. The result is an average of 12 bits per pixel.

name: YUV2
desc: Intel’s notation for 4:2:2 YCbCr format.

name: YUV9
desc: a) Intel’s notation for compressed Y, U, V format that provides a compression ratio of 3 to 1. b) A bitstream format that does not compress the video signal, but converts it from the RGB into the YUV color model and averages pixel colors so that the signal uses only nine bits per pixel. See Compress, Encode, RGB, YUV. Compare YUV9.

name: YUV9C
desc: A bitstream format that converts the video signal from RGB into the YUV color model, averages pixel colors so that the signal uses only nine bits per pixel, and then compresses the signal slightly. See Compress, Encode, RGB, YUV. Compare YUV9.

name: Z
desc: In electronics and television this is usually a code for impedance.

name: Z-Axis
desc: An axis of a three-dimensional graph, which, when printed on a flat piece of paper, is supposed to be perpendicular to the plane of the paper. When a television signal is examined in three dimensions, the z-axis is usually time.

name: ZCLV (Zoned Constant Linear Velocity)
desc: Concentric rings on a disc within which all sectors are the same size. A combination of CLV and CAV.

name: Zebra Pattern
desc: A camera viewfinder display that places stripes over a part of an image which has reached a pre-determined video level, usually set at about 70 IRE units and used to ensure correct exposure of skin tones.

name: Zebra Stripes
desc: A camera feature overlaying diagonal lines on overexposed areas in the viewfinder as a guide. Does not appear on the recorded image.

name: Zenith
desc: a) The tilt of the head relative to a direction perpendicular to the tape travel. b) Major U.S. consumer electronics manufacturer and proponent of the 3XNTSC ATV scheme, also possibly the first organization to suggest pre-combing for NTSC.

name: Zero Carrier Reference
desc: A 120 IRE pulse in the vertical interval which is produced by the demodulator to provide a reference for evaluating depth of modulation.

name: Zero Duration Dissolve
desc: The method of editing two scenes end-to-end simultaneously.

name: Zero Fleet Angle Winch
desc: A Winch With Cables That Exit The Winch At Fixed Points So That Fleet Angles Do Not Need To Be Considered In The Rigging Layout. This Is Accomplished By Incorporating A Moving Head Block Or By Making The Drum Move In Relation To The Head Block Per Izenour/ Clancy Designs Dating From The Early 1960S.

name: Zero latency
desc: Marketing term for extremely low-latency AV distribution. Uncompressed video over HDMI, SDVoE, or fiber optic has near-zero latency (<1 frame). Compressed AVoIP introduces encoding/decoding latency of 1 frame to several seconds.

name: Zero Modulation Noise
desc: The noise arising when reproducing an erased tape with the erase and record heads energized as they would be in normal operation, but with zero input signal. This noise is usually 3-4 dB higher than the bulk erased noise. The difference between bulk erased and zero modulation noise is sometimes referred to as induced noise.

name: Zero Timing Point
desc: The point at which all video signals must be in synchronization (typically the switcher input).

name: Zetex®
desc: Registered Trade Name For A Woven, High Temperature, Silica Glass Fabric Used In The Making Of Fire Safety Curtains. The Name Is A Trademark Of Newtex Industries, Inc.

name: Z - film orZ - movie
desc: refers to a very low - budgeted , independently - made , nonunion , less than B - film grade movie , usually with first - timedirector and actors ; often quickly - made for the teenagedyouth market and amateurish - looking , but with campy appeal ;with exploitational subject matter that includes surfing films ,motorcycle flicks , cheap horror films , etc .; Z - films becomeprime candidates for cult film status

name: Zig-Zag Scan
desc: Zig-zag scan of quantized DCT coefficient matrix. This gives an efficient run length coding (RLC).

name: Zig-Zag Scanning Order
desc: a) A specific sequential ordering of the DCT coefficients from (approximately) the lowest spatial frequency to the high- est. b) A specific sequential ordering of the 8 x 8 two-dimensional DCT coefficients into a linear array, ordering from the lowest spatial frequency to the highest. Contact Tektronix

name: Zintegrator
desc: A Category Of Markered Props Used For Aligning Physical Sets / Objects To Virtual Counterparts ;Can Be Used To Drive Static Non - Markered Objects Within A Volume .

name: Zipcord
desc: A cable comprising two jacketed wires or optical fibers that are conjoined together and can be separated.

name: Zip Strip
desc: A Compact Striplight That Uses 1 To 4 Groups Of Ten 12 Volt Lamps Wired In A Series Circuit, Manufactured By Altman Stage Lighting, Inc. The Name Zip Strip Is Trademarked.

name: Zixi
desc: A proprietary streaming protocol and platform for reliable broadcast-quality video delivery over the public internet using adaptive forward error correction and network path bonding.

name: Zolly (Push-Pull, Trombone)
desc: A Combination Of The Words Zoom And Dolly, This Is A Special Effects Shot In Which The Camera Zooms Out From The Subject While At The Same Time Dollying Toward It. This Has A Dizzying, Disconcerting Effect On The Viewer. The Classic Example Of This Move Is In The Hitchcock Film “Vertigo” Where It Was First Used.

name: Zoom
desc: The Camera Actually Doesn’T Move In This Shot But The Appearance Of Movement Is Achieved With A Telephoto Lens, Which Allows The Distance Between The Camera And The Object Being Recorded To Change During The Same Shot. The Camera Can Zoom In To Get Closer To An Object, Or It Can Zoom Out By Pulling Back From An Object.

name: Zoom fixture
desc: A lighting fixture with an adjustable lens system allowing the beam angle to be changed remotely via DMX or manually. Moving head zoom fixtures are essential in television and broadcast lighting.

name: Zoom Focus
desc: A Term Used To Describe An Optical System Whereby The Lenses In A Luminaire Adjust Such That A Beam Pattern With A Hard Edge Can Be Attained At Various Sizes At Various Distances Without Sacrificing Beam Lumens.

name: Zooming
desc: The enlarging or minimizing of an image on a computer monitor to facilitate ease of viewing and accurate editing.

name: Zoom Lens
desc: A camera lens that can vary the focal length while keeping the object in focus, giving an impression of coming closer to or going away from an object. It is usually controlled by a keyboard with buttons that are marked zoom-in and zoom-out.

name: Zoom Ratio
desc: A mathematical expression of the two extremes of focal length available on a particular zoom lens.

name: Zoom Rooms
desc: A software-defined conference room solution using Zoom's platform on certified hardware. Provides a touch-panel interface for starting and joining Zoom meetings and room control.

name: Zoom Rooms Controller
desc: Zoom's term for a Zoom Rooms controller (IP or USB enabled).

name: zoom shot
desc: a single shot taken with a lens that has a variable focal length ,thereby permitting the cinematographer to change thedistance between the camera and the object being filmed ,and rapidly move from a wide - angle shot to a telephoto shotin one continuous movement ; this camera technique makesan object in the frame appear larger ; movement towards asubject to magnify it is known as zoom in or forward zoom , orreversed to reduce its size is known as zoomout / back orbackward zoom

name: zoptic special effects
desc: a revolutionary special effects , 3 - D process invented bycameraman Zorian Perisic , incorporating a camera systemand a projector with synchronized zoom lenses , to create theillusion of movement in depth

name: Zorro II/III
desc: Amiga expansion slots. Zorro III, because of its 32-bit design improvements, provides a much faster data rate and therefore is preferred over Zorro II for use with video editing systems.

name: Z - Space
desc: The Distance Of A 3D Object From The Camera , Real Or Virtual .

name: Zulu Set
desc: A Physical Set Or Prop Designed For Actors To Interact With And Stand In As A Proxy For A Virtualasset . Sometimes Built At Different Scales To Accommodate For Major Size Differences In Actorto Character . This Term Was Coined And Still In Use By The Avatar Team ; Also Referred To As Aproxy Set .

name: ZV Port (Zoomed Video Port)
desc: Used on laptops, the ZV Port is a point- to-point unidirectional bus between the PC Card host adapter and the graphics controller, enabling video data to be transferred real-time directly from the PC Card into the graphics frame buffer. The PC Card host adapter has a special multimedia mode configuration. If a non-ZV PC Card is plugged into the slot, the host adapter is not switched into the multimedia mode, and the PC Card behaves as expected. Once a ZV card has been plugged in and the host adapter has been switched to the multimedia mode, the pin assignments change. The PC Card signals A4-A25, SPKR#, INPACK# and I0IS16# are replaced by ZV Port video signals (Y0-Y7,UV0-UV7, HREF, VSYNC, PCLK) and 4-channel audio signals (MCLK, SCLK, LRCK, and SDATA).

name: Zweiton
desc: A technique of implementing stereo or dual-mono audio for NTSC and PAL video. One FM subcarrier transmits a L+R signal, and a second FM subcarrier transmits a R signal (for stereo) or a second L+R signal. It is discussed in BS.707, and is similar to the BTSC technique.

name: Zymurgy
desc: The Science Of Brewing, An Important Part Of Mackie Technology Since The Factory Is Located Less Than A Mile From The Red Hook Brewery. Besides, We Needed Something Other Than Just Plain “Z” To Put In This A-To-Z Glossary.
